<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Explain the Von Neumann and Harvard architectures. State which model the 8086 microprocessor follows.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Von Neumann Architecture:**
- Uses a **single memory** and **single bus** to fetch both instructions and data.
- The CPU fetches an instruction, then fetches its operand — sequentially.
- **Disadvantage:** Von Neumann bottleneck — bus is busy with one transfer at a time, limiting speed.

**Harvard Architecture:**
- Uses **separate memories and separate buses** for instructions and data.
- Allows **simultaneous** access to instructions and data, providing higher throughput.
- Used in DSPs and microcontrollers like 8051.

**8086 Classification:**
- The 8086 uses a **modified Harvard** design at the pin level (separate physical pins for address and data via demultiplexing), but logically uses a unified memory for instructions and data, and is generally classified as a **Von Neumann-style** CISC processor.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**What is the function of the Instruction Queue in 8086? Why is it 6 bytes in size?**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Function:**
- The **6-byte instruction queue** is a FIFO buffer located in the **Bus Interface Unit (BIU)**.
- The BIU **pre-fetches** instruction bytes from memory and stores them in this queue while the **Execution Unit (EU)** is busy decoding and executing the previous instruction.
- This enables **pipelining** — instruction fetch and execution happen in **parallel**, improving overall throughput.

**Why 6 bytes?**
- The 8086 instruction length varies from **1 byte to 6 bytes**.
- A 6-byte queue is the **maximum length** of any single 8086 instruction, so the EU can always pull a complete instruction from the queue without waiting for the BIU.
- It balances **pre-fetch speed** with **on-chip memory cost** (the 8086 was fabricated in HMOS, where on-chip memory was expensive).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 – Module 1]`

**(a)** Draw and explain the **internal block diagram of the 8086 microprocessor**, clearly identifying the BIU and EU with all their sub-blocks. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **physical address generation mechanism** in 8086. Compute the physical address when `CS = 5000H` and `IP = 4321H`. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 8086 Internal Architecture (7 Marks):**

The 8086 CPU is divided into **two independent units** that operate in parallel:

**1. Bus Interface Unit (BIU) — 3.5 Marks:**
- **Segment Registers** (CS, DS, SS, ES) — each 16 bits, hold the base of the four active 64 KB memory segments.
- **Instruction Pointer (IP)** — 16-bit, holds the offset of the next instruction within the code segment.
- **Instruction Queue** — 6-byte FIFO buffer for pre-fetched instructions (key to pipelining).
- **Address Generation Unit (AGU)** — performs the 20-bit physical address calculation: $PA = \text{Seg} \times 16 + \text{Offset}$.
- **Bus Control Logic** — generates control signals $\overline{RD}$, $\overline{WR}$, $\overline{INTA}$, ALE, DEN, DT/$\overline{R}$, M/$\overline{IO}$.

**2. Execution Unit (EU) — 3.5 Marks:**
- **ALU** — 16-bit arithmetic and logic operations.
- **General Purpose Registers** — AX, BX, CX, DX (with 8-bit halves AH/AL, BH/BL, CH/CL, DH/DL).
- **Pointer and Index Registers** — SP, BP, SI, DI (used in addressing and string operations).
- **FLAGS Register** — 9 active flags: CF, PF, AF, ZF, SF, OF (status) and TF, IF, DF (control).
- **Control Unit / Instruction Decoder** — decodes opcode and generates micro-sequences.

**Key advantage:** BIU and EU operate **concurrently** — BIU pre-fetches next instructions into the queue while EU executes the current one. This **overlapped operation** is called pipelining and increases throughput.

**Part (b) — Physical Address Generation (7 Marks):**

**Mechanism (4 Marks):**
- The 8086 has a **20-bit address bus** but **16-bit registers**.
- To access 1 MB memory using 16-bit registers, Intel introduced **segmented addressing**.
- A logical address consists of a **16-bit segment base** and a **16-bit offset**.
- The **AGU shifts the segment base left by 4 bits** (effectively multiplying by 16) and adds the offset to obtain the 20-bit physical address.

**Diagram representation (2 Marks):**

$$
\text{Segment Base} \rightarrow [\text{Left shift by 4}] \rightarrow \text{20-bit Base} \\
\text{20-bit Base} + \text{16-bit Offset} \rightarrow \text{20-bit Physical Address}
$$

**Numerical Computation (1 Mark):**
Given `CS = 5000H` and `IP = 4321H`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PA &= CS \times 16 + IP \\
   &= 5000H \times 10H + 4321H \\
   &= 50000H + 4321H \\
   &= 54321H
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $PA = \mathbf{54321H}$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 – Module 1]`

**(a)** Explain the **register organization of 8086** with a neat diagram. List the special purposes of BX, CX, and SI registers. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare **8086 and 8085 microprocessor architectures** in terms of bus width, memory, registers, pipelining, and addressing capability. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 8086 Register Organization (7 Marks):**

The 8086 has **14 sixteen-bit registers** organized into four groups:

**1. General Purpose Registers (4) — 2 Marks:**
| Register | Special Purpose |
|---|---|
| **AX** (Accumulator) | Used in arithmetic, I/O, multiplication, division |
| **BX** (Base) | Holds base address in memory access |
| **CX** (Counter) | Used as counter in loops and string operations |
| **DX** (Data) | Holds data in multiplication/division, port address in IN/OUT |

Each register can be used as **one 16-bit register** or **two independent 8-bit registers** (e.g., AH and AL).

**2. Segment Registers (4) — 2 Marks:**
- **CS** (Code Segment) — base of instruction stream.
- **DS** (Data Segment) — base of data.
- **SS** (Stack Segment) — base of stack.
- **ES** (Extra Segment) — auxiliary segment for string operations.

**3. Pointer and Index Registers (4) — 2 Marks:**
- **SP** (Stack Pointer), **BP** (Base Pointer) — stack management.
- **SI** (Source Index), **DI** (Destination Index) — string operations.

**4. Special Registers (2) — 1 Mark:**
- **IP** (Instruction Pointer) — equivalent to Program Counter.
- **FLAGS** — 9 active flags.

**Special Purposes (required by question):**
- **BX:** Default base register for memory operands in many addressing modes (e.g., `[BX]`).
- **CX:** Default counter for `LOOP`, `REP`, `SHIFT`/`ROTATE` instructions; also used in string operations as count register.
- **SI:** Default source pointer in string operations; holds offset in indexed addressing.

**Part (b) — 8086 vs 8085 Comparison (7 Marks):**

| Feature | 8085 | 8086 | Marks |
|---|---|---|---|
| Data Bus | 8-bit | 16-bit | 1 |
| Address Bus | 16-bit | 20-bit | 1 |
| Memory | $2^{16} = 64$ KB | $2^{20} = 1$ MB | 1 |
| ALU | 8-bit | 16-bit | 1 |
| Registers | 8-bit GPRs + SP, PC | 14 × 16-bit + Flags | 1 |
| Pipelining | None | 6-byte pre-fetch queue | 1 |
| Memory Model | Linear (single segment) | Segmented (4 active) | 1 |

**Conclusion (1 Mark):** The 8086 represents a major architectural leap over 8085 — 16× larger memory, parallel BIU/EU operation, and richer instruction set suitable for 16-bit computing applications.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting to shift segment by 4 bits** — students often write `PA = Segment + Offset`. The correct formula is $PA = \text{Segment} \times 16 + \text{Offset}$ or equivalently append `0H` to the segment.
> 2. **Confusing SP and IP** — IP is the program counter (instruction offset), SP is stack pointer. Examiners deduct 1 mark for the swap.
> 3. **Missing the 6-byte queue in block diagram** — drawing BIU and EU without the pre-fetch queue loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Stating 8086 is purely Von Neumann or purely Harvard** — the precise answer is *modified Harvard at pin level, Von Neumann-like in execution*. Vague answers get partial marks only.
> 5. **Wrong FLAGS count** — there are **9 active flags** out of 16 bits. Writing "16 flags" is incorrect.
> 6. **Not showing ALE in pin/multiplexing diagrams** — Address Latch Enable signal is essential for demultiplexing AD0–AD15 into A0–A15 and D0–D15.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Microprocessor Architecture (8086)**

- 8086 is a **16-bit microprocessor** with a **20-bit address bus**, accessing **1 MB** of memory.
- Architecture is split into **BIU (Bus Interface Unit)** and **EU (Execution Unit)** that operate **in parallel** — this is the heart of 8086 pipelining.
- **BIU contains:** 4 segment registers (CS, DS, SS, ES), IP, 6-byte instruction queue, AGU, and bus control logic.
- **EU contains:** ALU, 4 GPRs (AX, BX, CX, DX), 4 pointer/index (SP, BP, SI, DI), FLAGS register, and instruction decoder.
- **Physical Address Formula:** $PA = \text{Segment Register} \times 16 + \text{Offset} = (\text{Segment} \ll 4) + \text{Offset}$.
- **Logical address** is 32 bits (16 + 16); **physical address** is 20 bits.
- **Segment size** is fixed at **64 KB**; there are **65,536 possible segment bases** and **16 non-overlapping segments** in 1 MB.
- **FLAGS register** has 9 active flags: CF, PF, AF, ZF, SF, OF (status) and TF, IF, DF (control).
- The 8086 follows a **modified Harvard** architecture — separate pin interfaces for address and data, but unified logical memory.
- **Pre-fetch queue size = 6 bytes** — equals the maximum 8086 instruction length, allowing complete instruction buffering.
- **BIU suspends pre-fetch** when the EU needs a memory operand — this is called the *bus steal* mechanism.
- **Maximum I/O ports** = $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ (isolated I/O space).
- The 8086 has **40 pins** in a DIP package and operates at **5–10 MHz** depending on variant.
- Always remember to **append `0H` to the segment register** (or shift left 4 bits) when computing physical addresses — this is the single most-tested concept in Module 1.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Addressing Modes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCIT404 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Addressing Modes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Addressing Mode (KTU Definition):** An *addressing mode* is the mechanism by which the **Instruction Set Architecture (ISA)** specifies the location of an *operand* (the data on which the instruction operates) and, where applicable, the *target address* of a branch or jump instruction. In the **Intel 8086** architecture, every instruction implicitly or explicitly encodes one of the supported addressing modes, which the **Bus Interface Unit (BIU)** resolves into a 20-bit **Physical Address** using the segment:offset formula.

In the 8086 microprocessor, operands may reside in three physically distinct locations:
- **CPU Registers** (e.g., `AX`, `BX`, `CX`, `DX`)
- **Memory** (Code, Data, Stack, or Extra segments)
- **I/O Ports** (8-bit or 16-bit)

The addressing mode defines *how* the CPU locates these operands.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy: Sending a Letter**
> Imagine you are writing a letter (an **instruction**) and need to tell a postman (the **CPU**) *where* to deliver it or *where* to pick up the content.

- *Register Mode:* "Take the letter from your **left hand** and put it in your **right hand**." (Operands already inside the CPU.)
- *Immediate Mode:* "Read the **text I wrote inside the letter itself**." (The data is part of the instruction.)
- *Direct Mode:* "Deliver it to **House #45, MG Road**." (The exact address is given in the instruction.)
- *Register Indirect Mode:* "The **address is written on this slip of paper** in your pocket." (A register *holds* the address.)
- *Based / Indexed Mode:* "Start from **your home** (base/index) and walk **3 steps forward** (displacement)." (Effective Address = Base + Offset.)

## 1.3 Classification Overview (KTU High-Yield Map)

The 8086 supports **17 logical addressing modes**, grouped as:

| Category | Sub-Modes |
|----------|-----------|
| **Data Addressing Modes** | Register, Immediate, Direct, Register Indirect, Based, Indexed, Based-Indexed, Based-Indexed with Displacement |
| **Program Memory Addressing Modes** | Intrasegment Direct, Intrasegment Indirect, Intersegment Direct, Intersegment Indirect |
| **Stack Memory Addressing Modes** | Stack (PUSH/POP) |
| **String Addressing Modes** | Implicit (uses SI, DI) |
| **I/O Addressing Modes** | Direct (fixed 8-bit port), Indirect (via DX) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physical Address Generation Pipeline

The 8086 is a **16-bit processor** with a **20-bit address bus**. The BIU therefore computes a *Physical Address* by combining a 16-bit **Segment Register** with a 16-bit **Offset (Effective Address)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Effective Address (EA):** The 16-bit offset computed from the addressing mode before segmentation.

The "Why" and "How" of each addressing mode centers on **how the EA is calculated**.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic: How a Mode Resolves

For every data addressing mode, the BIU performs:
1. Fetches the opcode.
2. Decodes the addressing-mode bits (`mod`, `reg`, `r/m`).
3. Computes the **16-bit Effective Address (EA)**.
4. Left-shifts the default segment register by 4 bits (`× 16`).
5. Adds EA to form the **20-bit Physical Address**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Mode | Effective Address Formula | Default Segment | Example Instruction |
|---|------|--------------------------|-----------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Register | No memory access | None | `MOV AX, BX` |
| 2 | Immediate | Data is in instruction | None | `MOV AL, 05H` |
| 3 | Direct | EA = `Displacement` (16-bit) | DS | `MOV AL, [5000H]` |
| 4 | Register Indirect | EA = `[BX] / [SI] / [DI]` or `[BP]` | DS (BP→SS) | `MOV AL, [BX]` |
| 5 | Based Relative | EA = `[BX] + Disp` or `[BP] + Disp` | DS (BP→SS) | `MOV AL, [BX+05H]` |
| 6 | Indexed | EA = `[SI] + Disp` or `[DI] + Disp` | DS | `MOV AL, [SI+05H]` |
| 7 | Based-Indexed | EA = `[BX] + [SI]` or `[BX] + [DI]` | DS | `MOV AL, [BX+SI]` |
| 8 | Based-Indexed + Disp | EA = `[BX] + [SI] + Disp` | DS (BP→SS) | `MOV AL, [BX+SI+05H]` |
| 9 | String | EA = `[SI]` (source), `[DI]` (dest) | DS (source), ES (dest) | `MOVSB` |
| 10 | I/O Direct | 8-bit port address | None | `IN AL, 80H` |
| 11 | I/O Indirect | EA = `[DX]` (16-bit port) | None | `IN AL, DX` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Equation — Physical Address:**
> $$\text{Physical Address} = (\text{Segment Register} \times 16) + \text{Effective Address}$$
> Equivalently, with the segment register shifted left by 4 bits:
> $$\text{PA} = (\text{SR} \ll 4) + \text{EA}$$

**Segment Override Prefix:** Any data addressing mode using a memory operand can be forced to use a non-default segment (CS, SS, ES, FS, GS) by inserting a 1-byte prefix (`26H`, `2EH`, `36H`, `3EH`, `64H`, `65H`).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Based Mode** is used for accessing *struct* fields and stack-frame locals in compiled C code.
> - **Indexed Mode** is used for **array traversal**: `[SI]` auto-increments inside `LOOP`.
> - **Based-Indexed with Displacement** is the workhorse of C compilers for structures of arrays.
> - **String Mode** powers high-speed `REP MOVSB` block copies (used in `memcpy`).
- **I/O Indirect** lets software address up to **65,536 ports** — essential for device drivers in embedded systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — Physical Address Computation

**Problem (KTU-Style):**
Given the 8086 instruction: `MOV AL, [BX + SI + 2000H]`
and the data: `DS = 4000H`, `BX = 1000H`, `SI = 0500H`.
Compute the **20-bit Physical Address** of the source operand.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the addressing mode.**
The mnemonic `[BX + SI + 2000H]` indicates **Based-Indexed with Displacement (8/16-bit)**.

**Step 2 — Apply the Effective Address formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EA} &= [\text{BX}] + [\text{SI}] + \text{Displacement} \\
          &= 1000\text{H} + 0500\text{H} + 2000\text{H} \\
          &= 3500\text{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Determine the default segment.**
Since the offset registers involved are `BX` and `SI` (neither is `BP`), the default data segment is `DS = 4000H`.

**Step 4 — Compute the segment base (left-shift by 4 bits).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Segment Base} &= \text{DS} \times 16 \\
                    &= 4000\text{H} \times 10\text{H} \\
                    &= 40000\text{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Add EA to segment base for the Physical Address.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Physical Address} &= 40000\text{H} + 03500\text{H} \\
                        &= 43500\text{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** The 8086 BIU reads the byte from memory location `43500H`.

## 3.2 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Mode Selection

| Mode | Operand Type | Instruction Size | Use Case | KTU Pitfall |
|------|--------------|------------------|----------|-------------|
| Register | CPU-internal | 2 bytes | Fastest execution | Cannot use **segment registers** (`CS, DS, ES, SS`) as MOV operands in most cases |
| Immediate | Constant | 3–6 bytes | Loading constants, masks | Destination **cannot** be a segment register |
| Direct | Memory | 3 bytes | Accessing fixed global variables | Often confused with "Direct = [addr]" — `[5000H]` is offset, not physical |
| Register Indirect | Memory | 2 bytes | Pointer-based access (e.g., traversing a linked list) | `CX` and `DX` **cannot** hold offsets (only BX, BP, SI, DI) |
| Based | Memory | 3–4 bytes | Stack frames, struct base | Forgetting BP uses **SS**, not DS |
| Indexed | Memory | 3–4 bytes | Arrays | SI/DI cannot be used with BP in 8086 |
| Based-Indexed | Memory | 2 bytes | 2D arrays, double indirection | Mixing BP with SI/DI changes default to **SS** |
| Based-Indexed+Disp | Memory | 4–5 bytes | Complex data structures | Highest flexibility, also largest instruction |

## 3.3 Pitfall: The `[BP]` Exception

> [!WARNING]
> **BP Exception (Frequently Tested):** Although `BP` looks identical to `BX`, it **implicitly uses the Stack Segment (SS)**. Therefore:
> $$\text{PA for [BP]} = (\text{SS} \times 16) + [\text{BP}]$$
> A common error is computing this with `DS`, producing a wrong physical address by up to **64 KB**.

## 3.4 Analytical Derivation — Maximum Memory Reach

**Question:** Why is the maximum physical address `FFFFFH` (1 MB)?

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Max Segment} &= \text{FFFFH} \\
\text{Max Offset}  &= \text{FFFFH} \\
\text{Max PA}      &= \text{FFFFH} \times 10\text{H} + \text{FFFFH} \\
                   &= \text{FFFF0H} + \text{FFFFH} \\
                   &= \text{10FFEFH} \\
                   &\rightarrow \text{Wrapped to } \text{FFFFFH} \text{ (20-bit)} \\
                   &= 1{,}048{,}575 \text{ decimal} = 1 \text{ MB}
\end{aligned}
$$

This **segment overlap** phenomenon is called the *64 KB "window"* — a unique feature of 8086 segmentation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — 8086 Addressing Mode Classification Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[8086 Addressing Modes] --> B[Data Addressing]
    A --> C[Program Memory]
    A --> D[Stack Memory]
    A --> E[String]
    A --> F[I-O Ports]

    B --> B1[Register]
    B --> B2[Immediate]
    B --> B3[Direct]
    B --> B4[Register Indirect]
    B --> B5[Based Relative]
    B --> B6[Indexed]
    B --> B7[Based Indexed]
    B --> B8[Based Indexed with Displacement]

    C --> C1[Intrasegment Direct]
    C --> C2[Intrasegment Indirect]
    C --> C3[Intersegment Direct]
    C --> C4[Intersegment Indirect]

    E --> E1[Source via SI in DS]
    E --> E2[Destination via DI in ES]

    F --> F1[Direct 8-bit port]
    F --> F2[Indirect 16-bit via DX]

    B4 --> B4a[BX or SI or DI uses DS]
    B4 --> B4b[BP uses SS]

    B5 --> B5a[BX plus Disp uses DS]
    B5 --> B5b[BP plus Disp uses SS]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Physical Address Formation Pipeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    OP[Opcode Fetch] --> DEC[Decode mod reg rm bits]
    DEC --> CALCEA[Compute 16-bit Effective Address]
    CALCEA --> EA[EA ready]
    SEG[Segment Register DS or SS] --> SHIFT[Left shift by 4 bits]
    SHIFT --> BASE[20-bit Segment Base]
    BASE --> ADD[20-bit Adder]
    EA --> ADD
    ADD --> PA[20-bit Physical Address]
    PA --> BUS[Address Bus A0 to A19]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Register Indirect Pointer Walk

```mermaid
graph TD
    START[Instruction: MOV AX, BX] --> FETCH[BIU fetches opcode]
    FETCH --> READREG[Read 16-bit value of BX]
    READREG --> EA[Effective Address equals BX]
    EA --> SEG[Default segment DS]
    SEG --> CALC[PA = DS times 16 plus BX]
    CALC --> MEM[Read word from memory at PA]
    MEM --> WRITE[Write to destination register]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Effective Address Composition (Based-Indexed + Disp)

```mermaid
graph LR
    BX[16-bit BX] --> SUM
    SI[16-bit SI] --> SUM
    DISP[8 or 16 bit Displacement] --> SUM
    SUM[EA Adder] --> EAOUT[16 bit Effective Address]
    EAOUT --> PHYS[Add shifted Segment Base]
    PHYS --> PADDR[20 bit Physical Address]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define an addressing mode. List any four data addressing modes of the 8086 microprocessor.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- (1 M) **Definition:** An addressing mode specifies the rule by which the operand(s) of an instruction are located — i.e., whether they reside in a register, in the instruction itself (immediate), or in memory.
- (2 M) Four data addressing modes:
  1. **Register** — `MOV AX, BX`
  2. **Immediate** — `MOV AL, 25H`
  3. **Direct** — `MOV AL, [2000H]`
  4. **Register Indirect** — `MOV AL, [BX]`

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**What is the role of the segment override prefix in 8086 addressing modes?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- (1 M) It is a **1-byte prefix** placed before an instruction.
- (1 M) It forces the BIU to use a **non-default segment** (`CS, SS, ES, FS, GS`) instead of the default `DS` or `SS`.
- (1 M) Example: `MOV AX, ES:[BX]` reads from the extra segment instead of the data segment.

---

## 📗 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### ⭐ **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks**
> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain with neat diagrams the **Based, Indexed, and Based-Indexed** addressing modes of the 8086. Show how the **20-bit physical address** is generated in each case. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Given `DS = 5000H`, `BX = 0200H`, `SI = 0100H`, `DI = 0300H`, compute the **physical addresses** for the following operands:
  1. `[BX + 10H]`
  2. `[SI + 200H]`
  3. `[BX + DI + 50H]`
  4. `[BX + SI]`
**(7 Marks)**

#### 🔹 Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

- **Based Mode (2 M):** EA = `[BX] + 8/16-bit Displacement`. Default segment = `DS`. The base register supplies a *starting reference*, like a struct base address.
  $$\text{PA} = \text{DS} \times 16 + [\text{BX}] + \text{Disp}$$
- **Indexed Mode (2 M):** EA = `[SI] + Disp` or `[DI] + Disp`. The index register is used to step through arrays, often auto-incremented by `LOOP` or string instructions.
  $$\text{PA} = \text{DS} \times 16 + [\text{SI}] + \text{Disp}$$
- **Based-Indexed Mode (2 M):** EA = `[BX] + [SI]` (or `[DI]`). Combines a base for *structure start* with an index for *element offset*. Equivalent to a 2D-array accessor.
  $$\text{PA} = \text{DS} \times 16 + [\text{BX}] + [\text{SI}]$$
- **Diagram Note (1 M):** Reference the EA composition flow shown in Section 4.4.

#### 🔹 Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

Given: `DS = 5000H` → Segment Base = `50000H`

1. **`[BX + 10H]`** — Based Relative
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EA} &= 0200\text{H} + 0010\text{H} = 0210\text{H} \\
\text{PA} &= 50000\text{H} + 0210\text{H} = 50210\text{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

2. **`[SI + 200H]`** — Indexed
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EA} &= 0100\text{H} + 0200\text{H} = 0300\text{H} \\
\text{PA} &= 50000\text{H} + 0300\text{H} = 50300\text{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

3. **`[BX + DI + 50H]`** — Based-Indexed with Disp
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EA} &= 0200\text{H} + 0300\text{H} + 0050\text{H} = 0550\text{H} \\
\text{PA} &= 50000\text{H} + 0550\text{H} = 50550\text{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

4. **`[BX + SI]`** — Based-Indexed
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EA} &= 0200\text{H} + 0100\text{H} = 0300\text{H} \\
\text{PA} &= 50000\text{H} + 0300\text{H} = 50300\text{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### ⭐ **Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks**
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**(a)** With examples, discuss the **Register, Immediate, Direct, and Register Indirect** addressing modes of the 8086. State one limitation of each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An 8086 instruction reads a byte from memory location whose physical address is `40200H` using the `BP` register with a displacement of `1000H`. If `SS = 3000H`, find the value that must be loaded into `BP`. **(7 Marks)**

#### 🔹 Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

| Mode | Example | Operation | Limitation |
|------|---------|-----------|------------|
| **Register** (1.5 M) | `MOV AX, BX` | Operand is a CPU register | Cannot transfer data directly between two memory locations |
| **Immediate** (1.5 M) | `MOV CX, 0A00H` | Constant embedded in instruction | Destination cannot be a segment register or memory |
| **Direct** (2 M) | `MOV AL, [4000H]` | 16-bit offset is part of the instruction | Displacement is **fixed**; cannot dynamically compute offset |
| **Register Indirect** (2 M) | `MOV [BX], AL` | Register holds the offset | Only `BX, BP, SI, DI` can hold offsets (not `AX, CX, DX`) |

#### 🔹 Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Given:** PA = `40200H`, Disp = `1000H`, SS = `3000H`, Mode = `[BP + Disp]`.

**Step 1 — Identify addressing mode:** `BP` is used → default segment is `SS`.

**Step 2 — Apply the PA formula:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PA} &= (\text{SS} \times 16) + [\text{BP}] + \text{Disp} \\
40200\text{H} &= 30000\text{H} + [\text{BP}] + 1000\text{H} \\
40200\text{H} &= 31000\text{H} + [\text{BP}] \\
[\text{BP}] &= 40200\text{H} - 31000\text{H} \\
[\text{BP}] &= 0F200\text{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer (1 M):** `BP = F200H` to access physical address `40200H`.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **BP vs BX trap:** Forgetting that `BP` defaults to `SS` is the **#1 reason for losing 2–3 marks** in PA-computation questions.
> 2. **Segment register confusion:** `MOV CS, AX` is **illegal** — many students mark it correct.
> 3. **Displacement confusion:** "Direct mode" displacement is the **OFFSET**, not the physical address.
> 4. **Register Indirect restriction:** Many write `MOV [CX], AX` — invalid because `CX` cannot hold an offset.
> 5. **Hex addition errors:** Always show the addition in the **derivation steps** to earn full marks; skipping the arithmetic loses 1 mark per sub-step.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🔑 **8 Data Addressing Modes** form the most-tested section; commit them to memory.
- 🔑 **Master Equation:** `PA = (SR × 16) + EA` — applicable to **every** memory access.
- 🔑 **Default Segments:** `BX/SI/DI → DS`; `BP → SS`; `SP → SS`.
- 🔑 **String Mode uses both SI and DI implicitly** with auto-increment/decrement.
- 🔑 **Segment Override Prefix** is just 1 byte but changes the segment silently.
- 🔑 **Register Indirect** is restricted to only `BX, BP, SI, DI`.
- 🔑 **Based-Indexed with Displacement** is the most powerful and the most common in C-compiled code.
- 🔑 **No direct memory-to-memory `MOV`** in 8086 — must go through a register.
- 🔑 **Maximum addressable memory = 1 MB** (`FFFFFH`); 8086 is a **16-bit** CPU with a **20-bit** address bus.
- 🔑 In Part A, definitions carry **1 mark**, lists/tables carry the rest — write **neatly** and use bullet points for clarity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Instruction Set and Assembler Directives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCIT404 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Instruction Set and Assembler Directives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The 8086 Instruction Set — Formal Definition

The **Instruction Set** of the 8086 microprocessor is the complete collection of binary patterns (opcodes) and their corresponding mnemonics that the CPU can decode, interpret, and execute. Each instruction is a contract between the programmer and the silicon — it tells the processor *what* operation to perform, *on what* data, and *where* to put the result.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, the 8086 instruction set is classified into **six functional groups** as defined by Intel:

1. **Data Transfer Instructions** (MOV, PUSH, POP, XCHG, LEA, LDS, LES, IN, OUT, XLAT, etc.)
2. **Arithmetic Instructions** (ADD, ADC, SUB, SBB, INC, DEC, MUL, IMUL, DIV, IDIV, CMP, NEG, etc.)
3. **Logical / Bit Manipulation Instructions** (AND, OR, XOR, NOT, TEST, SHL/SAL, SHR, SAR, ROL, ROR, RCL, RCR)
4. **String Instructions** (MOVS, CMPS, SCAS, LODS, STOS — with REP prefix variants)
5. **Control Transfer / Program Transfer Instructions** (JMP, conditional jumps, CALL, RET, LOOP, INT, IRET)
6. **Processor Control Instructions** (STC, CLC, CMC, STD, CLD, STI, CLI, NOP, HLT, WAIT, ESC, LOCK)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Point:** When asked to "list the instruction groups," write them in this **exact six-group classification**. Examiners award full marks only when the official Intel taxonomy is used.

## 1.2 Assembler Directives — Formal Definition

**Assembler Directives** (also called *pseudo-operations* or *pseudo-ops*) are commands to the **assembler software** (e.g., MASM/TASM), **NOT** to the 8086 microprocessor. They do not generate any machine code. Their job is to:
- Tell the assembler *how* to translate the source program
- Reserve memory locations
- Define data constants
- Set up segments
- Assign names to constants and addresses

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction:** `MOV` is an **instruction** (generates machine code). `MOV` as a directive does NOT exist. `DB` (Define Byte) is a **directive** (no machine code). This distinction is a favorite KTU question.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Recipe Book 🍳

Imagine you are a chef (the **8086 CPU**):

- **Instructions** = Actual cooking commands ("chop onions", "boil water for 5 minutes", "add salt"). These are things the chef physically executes.
- **Assembler Directives** = Notes to your *publisher* writing the recipe book. Statements like "*this recipe serves 4 people*", "*use page 12 for the photo*", "*the ingredient list is on the next page*". The chef never cooks these notes — they are for organizing the *book* (the source program) before it is printed (assembled into machine code).

Without directives, you would have a disorganized pile of recipes. Without instructions, you would have an empty book.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants (Bolded)

- **8086 is a 16-bit microprocessor** with a **16-bit data bus** and **20-bit address bus**.
- The 8086 can address **2²⁰ = 1,048,576 bytes = 1 MB** of physical memory.
- The instruction queue is **6 bytes long** (4 bytes for 8088).
- General-purpose registers are **16-bit wide**: AX, BX, CX, DX (each splittable into 8-bit halves: AH/AL, BH/BL, CH/CL, DH/DL).
- Segment registers are **16-bit wide**: CS, DS, SS, ES.
- Default operands are typically **byte (8-bit)** or **word (16-bit)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Instruction Format — The Anatomy of a 8086 Machine Instruction

A 8086 machine instruction can be **1 to 6 bytes long**. The general layout is:

$$
\text{Instruction Byte} = \underbrace{\text{Opcode}}_{\text{1–2 bytes}} \;\vert\; \underbrace{\text{Mod–R/M}}_{\text{mode, reg, r/m}} \;\vert\; \underbrace{\text{Displacement}}_{\text{0/1/2 bytes}} \;\vert\; \underbrace{\text{Immediate Data}}_{\text{0/1/2 bytes}}
$$

The **Mod–R/M byte** is the most important part to understand. It is a single byte that encodes:

| Bits 7–6 (MOD) | Bit 5 (REG/Opcode extension) | Bits 2–0 (R/M) |
|----------------|------------------------------|----------------|
| Addressing mode (register vs memory, displacement size) | Specifies the second register OR an opcode extension | Specifies the register/memory operand |

### 2.1.1 MOD Field Encoding

| MOD Value | Meaning |
|-----------|---------|
| **00** | Memory mode, **no displacement** (except `[BP]` which defaults to 0) |
| **01** | Memory mode with **8-bit signed displacement** |
| **10** | Memory mode with **16-bit displacement** |
| **11** | **Register-to-register** mode (both operands are registers) |

### 2.1.2 R/M Field Encoding (when MOD = 11)

| R/M | W=0 (byte) | W=1 (word) |
|-----|------------|------------|
| 000 | AL | AX |
| 001 | CL | CX |
| 010 | DL | DX |
| 011 | BL | BX |
| 100 | AH | SP |
| 101 | CH | BP |
| 110 | DH | SI |
| 111 | BH | DI |

> [!NOTE]
> The **W bit** (bit 0 of the opcode byte) decides whether the operation is on 8-bit or 16-bit data. **W = 0 → byte; W = 1 → word.** This is a classic 2-mark KTU question.

## 2.2 Six Instruction Groups — Detailed Classification

### 2.2.1 Group 1: Data Transfer Instructions

| Mnemonic | Operation | Example |
|----------|-----------|---------|
| MOV | Copy data | `MOV AX, BX` |
| PUSH | Push 16-bit to stack (SS:SP) | `PUSH AX` |
| POP | Pop 16-bit from stack | `POP BX` |
| XCHG | Exchange two operands | `XCHG AX, CX` |
| LEA | Load Effective Address | `LEA BX, [SI]` |
| LDS | Load DS:register | `LDS SI, [1000H]` |
| LES | Load ES:register | `LES DI, [2000H]` |
| IN | Input from port | `IN AL, 30H` |
| OUT | Output to port | `OUT 40H, AL` |
| XLAT | Translate using lookup table | `XLAT` |

**Affected flags:** None (except POPF, PUSHF which manipulate FLAGS).

### 2.2.2 Group 2: Arithmetic Instructions

| Mnemonic | Operation | Example |
|----------|-----------|---------|
| ADD | Addition | `ADD AX, BX` |
| ADC | Add with Carry | `ADC AL, 5` |
| SUB | Subtraction | `SUB CX, 10` |
| SBB | Subtract with Borrow | `SBB AX, BX` |
| INC | Increment by 1 | `INC SI` |
| DEC | Decrement by 1 | `DEC BX` |
| MUL | Unsigned Multiply (AX ← AL×r/m8 OR DX:AX ← AX×r/m16) | `MUL BL` |
| IMUL | Signed Multiply | `IMUL BX` |
| DIV | Unsigned Divide | `DIV CL` |
| IDIV | Signed Divide | `IDIV BX` |
| CMP | Compare (SUB without storing) | `CMP AX, BX` |
| NEG | Two's complement (Negate) | `NEG AL` |
| DAA | Decimal Adjust after Addition | `DAA` |
| DAS | Decimal Adjust after Subtraction | `DAS` |
| AAA | ASCII Adjust after Addition | `AAA` |
| AAS | ASCII Adjust after Subtraction | `AAS` |
| AAM | ASCII Adjust after Multiplication | `AAM` |
| AAD | ASCII Adjust before Division | `AAD` |
| CBW | Convert Byte to Word (sign extend AL→AX) | `CBW` |
| CWD | Convert Word to Doubleword (sign extend AX→DX:AX) | `CWD` |

**Affected flags:** AF, CF, OF, PF, SF, ZF (all conditional flags).

### 2.2.3 Group 3: Logical & Bit Manipulation Instructions

| Mnemonic | Operation |
|----------|-----------|
| AND, OR, XOR, NOT, TEST | Boolean operations |
| SHL / SAL | Shift Left (Logical / Arithmetic) |
| SHR | Shift Right (Logical) — fills with 0 |
| SAR | Shift Right (Arithmetic) — sign-extends |
| ROL, ROR | Rotate Left / Right (no carry) |
| RCL, RCR | Rotate Left / Right through Carry |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **`SHR` fills the MSB with 0** (treats data as unsigned). **`SAR` copies the sign bit** (preserves signedness). This is a high-frequency 2-mark KTU question.

### 2.2.4 Group 4: String Instructions

Strings are sequences of bytes or words. Operands use **SI** (source index, addressed via DS) and **DI** (destination index, addressed via ES).

| Mnemonic | Operation |
|----------|-----------|
| MOVSB / MOVSW | Move byte/word from [DS:SI] to [ES:DI] |
| CMPSB / CMPSW | Compare bytes/words |
| SCASB / SCASW | Scan (compare AL/AX with [ES:DI]) |
| LODSB / LODSW | Load byte/word from [DS:SI] into AL/AX |
| STOSB / STOSW | Store AL/AX into [ES:DI] |

**Prefixes** that auto-repeat these instructions:

| Prefix | Meaning | Counter used |
|--------|---------|--------------|
| REP | Repeat while CX ≠ 0 | CX |
| REPE / REPZ | Repeat while equal (ZF=1) and CX ≠ 0 | CX |
| REPNE / REPNZ | Repeat while not equal (ZF=0) and CX ≠ 0 | CX |

After each iteration, **SI and DI are auto-incremented (DF=0) or auto-decremented (DF=1)** by 1 (byte) or 2 (word).

### 2.2.5 Group 5: Control Transfer Instructions

**Unconditional:** JMP, CALL, RET, INT, IRET, LOOP, LOOPE, LOOPZ, LOOPNE, LOOPNZ, JCXZ.

**Conditional jumps** (test flags after CMP/TEST):

| Mnemonic | Condition |
|----------|-----------|
| JE / JZ | Jump if Zero flag set (ZF=1) |
| JNE / JNZ | Jump if Not Zero (ZF=0) |
| JG / JNLE | Jump if Greater (signed) |
| JL / JNGE | Jump if Less (signed) |
| JGE / JNL | Jump if Greater or Equal (signed) |
| JLE / JNG | Jump if Less or Equal (signed) |
| JA / JNBE | Jump if Above (unsigned) |
| JB / JNAE | Jump if Below (unsigned) |
| JAE / JNB | Jump if Above or Equal (unsigned) |
| JBE / JNA | Jump if Below or Equal (unsigned) |
| JS / JNS | Jump if Sign / Not Sign |
| JO / JNO | Jump if Overflow / No Overflow |
| JC / JNC | Jump if Carry / No Carry |
| JP / JNP | Jump if Parity / No Parity |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** "JA" tests *unsigned* `CF=0 AND ZF=0`, while "JG" tests *signed* `ZF=0 AND SF=OF`. Mixing them is a common error.

### 2.2.6 Group 6: Processor Control Instructions

| Mnemonic | Purpose |
|----------|---------|
| STC, CLC, CMC | Set / Clear / Complement Carry flag |
| STD, CLD | Set / Clear Direction flag |
| STI, CLI | Set / Clear Interrupt flag |
| NOP | No Operation (1 byte: 90H), wastes 3 clock cycles |
| HLT | Halt the processor (resumes on interrupt or reset) |
| WAIT | Wait for TEST pin to go low |
| ESC | Escape (for coprocessor access) |
| LOCK | Bus lock prefix (for multiprocessor) |

## 2.3 Assembler Directives — The Complete Catalogue

| Directive | Purpose | Example |
|-----------|---------|---------|
| **SEGMENT** | Declares start of a program segment | `CODE SEGMENT` |
| **ENDS** | Ends a segment | `CODE ENDS` |
| **ASSUME** | Tells assembler which segment register points to which segment | `ASSUME CS:CODE, DS:DATA` |
| **DB** | Define Byte (1 byte) | `MSG DB 'HELLO$'` |
| **DW** | Define Word (2 bytes) | `X DW 1234H, 5678H` |
| **DD** | Define Doubleword (4 bytes) | `Y DD 12345678H` |
| **DQ** | Define Quadword (8 bytes) | `Z DQ ?` |
| **DT** | Define Tenbytes (10 bytes, for BCD) | `BCD_NUM DT 1234567890H` |
| **EQU** | Equate — assigns a constant value or symbol to a name | `MAX EQU 100` |
| **=** | Same as EQU but redefinable | `COUNT = 0` |
| **OFFSET** | Returns the offset of a label/variable | `MOV BX, OFFSET DATA` |
| **PTR** | Specifies the type (BYTE/WORD/DWORD) of operand | `MOV AL, BYTE PTR [BX]` |
| **SEG** | Returns the segment of a label | `MOV AX, SEG DATA` |
| **DUP** | Duplicates a value | `BUF DB 100 DUP(0)` |
| **LENGTH** | Returns the number of elements in a data array | `MOV CX, LENGTH ARRAY` |
| **SIZE** | Returns total bytes (LENGTH × type size) | `MOV CX, SIZE ARRAY` |
| **TYPE** | Returns bytes per element (1, 2, 4, 8, 10) | `MOV BX, TYPE ARRAY` |
| **$** | Current location counter (address) | `JMP $+5` |
| **ORG** | Sets the location counter to a value | `ORG 1000H` |
| **END** | Marks end of source program | `END START` |
| **PROC** | Declares a procedure | `ADDITION PROC NEAR` |
| **ENDP** | Ends a procedure | `ADDITION ENDP` |
| **NEAR / FAR** | Specifies segment reach (intra/inter) | `RET FAR` |
| **PUBLIC** | Declares symbol visible to other modules | `PUBLIC RESULT` |
| **EXTRN** | Declares external symbol | `EXTRN SUBR:FAR` |
| **NAME** | Names a module | `NAME MYPROG` |
| **TITLE** | Title text for listing file | `TITLE 'Sum Program'` |
| **INCLUDE** | Inserts another source file | `INCLUDE MACROS.MAC` |
| **MACRO / ENDM** | Defines a macro | `SHROUT MACRO` ... `ENDM` |

## 2.4 Addressing Modes — The Heart of the 8086

The 8086 supports **8 addressing modes** for operand access:

| # | Mode | Syntax Example | Physical Address Formula |
|---|------|----------------|---------------------------|
| 1 | Register | `MOV AX, BX` | None (in register) |
| 2 | Immediate | `MOV AX, 5000H` | None (in instruction) |
| 3 | Direct | `MOV AX, [2000H]` | EA = 2000H |
| 4 | Register Indirect | `MOV AX, [BX]` | EA = (BX) |
| 5 | Based | `MOV AX, [BX+SI]` | EA = (BX) + (SI) |
| 6 | Indexed | `MOV AX, [SI+5]` | EA = (SI) + 5 |
| 7 | Based-Indexed | `MOV AX, [BX+DI+10H]` | EA = (BX) + (DI) + 10H |
| 8 | String | uses SI/DI implicitly | EA = (SI) or (DI) |

**Physical Address Formula (the most important equation in 8086):**

$$
\text{Physical Address} = (\text{Segment Register}) \times 16 + (\text{Effective Address / Offset})
$$

**Example:** If CS = 2000H and IP = 4500H, then the next instruction is fetched from:

$$
\text{PA} = 2000\text{H} \times 16 + 4500\text{H} = 20000\text{H} + 4500\text{H} = 24500\text{H}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This **left-shift by 4 bits (×16)** is the single most important calculation in 8086 architecture. KTU 2024 examiners love to give 3-mark problems based on it.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---------|----------------|---------------|
| Physical Address | $\text{PA} = \text{Seg} \times 16 + \text{OA}$ | 20-bit result |
| Offset from Segment | Shift left by 4 bits | Append a 0 nibble |
| Max Memory | $2^{20} = 1{,}048{,}576$ bytes = **1 MB** | 8086 limit |
| I/O Space | $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ ports | 16-bit I/O address |
| MUL byte result | AX = AL × r/m8 | 16-bit result |
| MUL word result | DX:AX = AX × r/m16 | 32-bit result (DX=high, AX=low) |
| DIV byte | AL = quotient, AH = remainder | of AX ÷ r/m8 |
| DIV word | AX = quotient, DX = remainder | of DX:AX ÷ r/m16 |
| Instruction Size | 1 to 6 bytes | 8086 minimum: 1 byte (NOP, 90H) |
| Queue Size | **6 bytes** (8086) / **4 bytes** (8088) | Prefetch buffer |
| Stack Growth | SP decrements on PUSH | Word-only (16-bit) |
| Default segment | Data → DS, Stack → SS, Code → CS, Extra → ES | Override possible |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The 8086 instruction set is the **ancestor of the entire x86 family** (80186, 80286, 80386, Pentium, Core, modern 64-bit processors). Every modern PC runs x86-64 code whose roots trace directly to the 8086 ISA. Learning the 8086 instruction set is therefore a **direct foundation for embedded systems, IoT firmware, BIOS programming, reverse engineering, device drivers, OS kernel development, and compiler design**. Assembler directives remain crucial in modern toolchains (GAS, NASM, MASM) for memory layout, linker scripts, and startup code.

# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Physical Address Calculation (KTU-style 3-mark)

**Question:** Given CS = 4052H, DS = 4050H, SS = 4054H, IP = 1020H, SP = 1000H, BX = 0100H, SI = 0050H. Calculate the physical address for the next instruction fetch.

**Step 1 — Identify segments and offsets involved.**
- For **instruction fetch**: CS and IP are used.
- For **stack operations**: SS and SP are used.
- For **data access**: DS and BX/SI are used.

**Step 2 — Apply the Physical Address formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PA}_{\text{next\_inst}} &= (\text{CS} \times 16) + \text{IP} \\
&= 4052\text{H} \times 16 + 1020\text{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Perform the hex multiplication (×16 = shift left by 1 hex digit).**

$$
4052\text{H} \times 16 = 40520\text{H}
$$

**Step 4 — Add the offset.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PA} &= 40520\text{H} + 1020\text{H} \\
&= 41540\text{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer: 41540H**

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Encoding the Instruction `MOV AX, BX` (14-mark favorite)

**Step 1 — Identify the opcode.**
The opcode for `MOV reg16, reg16` is the byte **8B** (binary: `1000 1011`). The **D bit** (bit 1) = 0 because the source is a register. The **W bit** (bit 0) = 1 because the operand is a word.

**Step 2 — Construct the Mod–R/M byte.**

$$
\text{Mod–R/M} = \underbrace{11}_{\text{MOD = register}} \; \underbrace{011}_{\text{REG = BX (source)}} \; \underbrace{000}_{\text{R/M = AX (destination)}}
$$

> **Note:** In `MOV r/m, r` format, **REG = source** and **R/M = destination**.

**Step 3 — Combine into hex.**

$$
\text{8B} = 1000\;1011_2
$$

$$
\text{Mod–R/M} = 11\;011\;000 = 1101\;1000_2 = \text{D8H}
$$

**Step 4 — Concatenate the two bytes.**

$$
\text{Machine code} = \text{8B D8H}
$$

**Step 5 — Size = 2 bytes.** No displacement. No immediate data. ✓

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Encoding `MOV AL, 05H` (immediate-to-register, byte)

**Step 1 — Opcode for `MOV r/m8, imm8` = B0 + REG.** For AL (REG=000): **B0H**.

**Step 2 — Opcode byte encodes the destination register directly.**

$$
\text{Opcode} = \text{B0H}
$$

**Step 3 — Append the immediate byte (8 bits).**

$$
\text{Immediate} = 05\text{H}
$$

**Step 4 — Concatenate.**

$$
\text{Machine code} = \text{B0 05H} \quad (\text{2 bytes total})
$$

## 3.4 Worked Example 4: Program to Add Two 16-bit Numbers

```asm
; Program: Add two 16-bit numbers stored in memory
ASSUME CS:CODE, DS:DATA
DATA SEGMENT
    NUM1 DW 1234H
    NUM2 DW 5678H
    SUM  DW ?
DATA ENDS
CODE SEGMENT
START:  MOV AX, DATA        ; Initialize DS
        MOV DS, AX
        MOV AX, NUM1        ; AX = 1234H
        ADD AX, NUM2        ; AX = 1234H + 5678H = 68ACH
        MOV SUM, AX         ; Store result
        MOV AH, 4CH         ; Exit to DOS
        INT 21H
CODE ENDS
END START
```

**Step-by-step trace:**

| Step | Instruction | AX | After | Flags |
|------|-------------|-----|-------|-------|
| 1 | `MOV AX, 1234H` | 1234H | — | none |
| 2 | `ADD AX, 5678H` | 68ACH | 1234+5678 | CF=0, ZF=0 |
| 3 | `MOV SUM, AX` | 68ACH | 68ACH stored | none |
| 4 | `MOV AH, 4CH` | 4CACH | — | none |
| 5 | `INT 21H` | — | DOS exit | none |

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Directive Behavior Comparison

| Directive | Output to .LST | Machine Code Generated? | Common Mistake |
|-----------|----------------|------------------------|----------------|
| `MOV AX, BX` | Source line | ✅ Yes (8BD8H) | None |
| `EQU MAX 100` | Substituted as 100 | ❌ No | Treating as variable |
| `DB 10` | Allocates 1 byte | ❌ No | Trying to read as word |
| `DW 1234H` | Allocates 2 bytes | ❌ No | Byte-order confusion (little-endian) |
| `DD 12345678H` | Allocates 4 bytes | ❌ No | Same |
| `ASSUME CS:CODE` | Affects segment reg | ❌ No | Forgetting to LOAD DS manually |
| `ORG 1000H` | Shifts location counter | ❌ No | Overlapping segments |
| `PROC NEAR` | Label entry | ❌ No | Using FAR unnecessarily |
| `END START` | Assembly stops | ❌ No | Forgetting execution entry point |
| `DUP(0)` | Allocates N bytes | ❌ No | Very large DUP → memory error |

## 3.6 Step-by-Step: Convert Source → Object → Executable

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Source} &\rightarrow \text{Programmer writes .ASM file} \\
\text{Step 2: Assembler} &\rightarrow \text{Translates .ASM to .OBJ (object code)} \\
\text{Step 3: Linker} &\rightarrow \text{Combines .OBJ + libraries → .EXE} \\
\text{Step 4: Loader} &\rightarrow \text{Loads .EXE into RAM (sets up PSP at offset 0)} \\
\text{Step 5: CPU Executes} &\rightarrow \text{CS:IP jumps to entry point}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Important:** `ASSUME` does NOT load segment registers. It merely *informs* the assembler. You must **manually** `MOV AX, DATA` and `MOV DS, AX`. This is the #1 reason KTU programs fail at runtime.

## 3.7 Divisibility of Memory by 16 (Paragraph Boundary)

Every segment in 8086 must **start at a paragraph boundary** — a memory address divisible by 16. The formula:

$$
\text{Start Address} = \text{Segment Register} \times 16
$$

**Example:** If DS = 5000H, the data segment starts at:

$$
5000\text{H} \times 16 = 50000\text{H}
$$

Which is divisible by 16 (the last hex digit is 0). Valid. ✓

    I --> I1[MOV CX 2000H]
    I --> I2[ADD AL 05H]

    M --> M2[Register Indirect MOV AX open BX close]

## 4.6 Execution Flow: Source to Executable

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Source ASM file] --> B[Assembler MASM TASM]
    B --> C[Object OBJ file]
    C --> D[Linker LINK]
    D --> E[Executable EXE file]
    E --> F[Loader DOS]
    F --> G[RAM Memory]
    G --> H[8086 CPU Executes]
```

## 4.7 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Instruction Execution

```mermaid
graph LR
    BUS[System Bus 20 bit Address 16 bit Data] --> BIU[Bus Interface Unit]
    BIU --> Q[6 byte Instruction Queue]
    Q --> EU[Execution Unit]
    EU --> REG[General Purpose Registers AX BX CX DX]
    EU --> ALU[Arithmetic Logic Unit]
    EU --> FLAG[Flags Register]
    BIU --> SEG[Segment Registers CS DS SS ES]
    SEG --> BIU
    ALU --> BUS
    FLAG --> EU
    REG --> ALU
```

> [!NOTE]
> The BIU prefetches instructions into the queue, while the EU decodes and executes them. This **pipelining** is what gives the 8086 its performance edge over 8085.

## 📌 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: List any six categories of 8086 instruction set with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

The 8086 instruction set is divided into six functional groups:

1. **Data Transfer Instructions** — Example: `MOV AX, BX` (copies content of BX into AX).
2. **Arithmetic Instructions** — Example: `ADD AX, BX` (adds BX to AX, result in AX).
3. **Logical / Bit Manipulation Instructions** — Example: `AND AL, 0FH` (masks upper nibble of AL).
4. **String Instructions** — Example: `MOVSB` (copies byte from [DS:SI] to [ES:DI]).
5. **Control Transfer Instructions** — Example: `JMP 2000H` (unconditional jump to offset 2000H).
6. **Processor Control Instructions** — Example: `STC` (sets the carry flag).

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between an instruction and an assembler directive. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Instruction | Assembler Directive |
|-----------|-------------|---------------------|
| Definition | A command executed by the **CPU** at runtime | A command to the **assembler** at translation time |
| Generates Machine Code? | **Yes** (opcode bytes emitted) | **No** (just controls assembly process) |
| Runtime Effect | Affects registers, memory, flags | No runtime effect |
| Examples | `MOV AX, BX`, `ADD AL, 5`, `JMP LABEL` | `DB 10H`, `EQU COUNT 50`, `ASSUME CS:CODE` |
| Stored in .OBJ? | Yes (as binary) | No (resolved during assembly) |
