## 📌 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q]`

**Q: a) Explain the different types of 8086 addressing modes with suitable examples. (7 Marks)**
**b) Write an 8086 assembly language program to find the sum of the first N natural numbers stored in memory. Use appropriate directives. (7 Marks)**

#### Solution to (a):

The 8086 supports **8 addressing modes**:

**1. Register Addressing Mode** — Operand is a register.
```asm
MOV AX, BX     ; copy BX to AX
ADD AL, CL     ; add CL to AL
```

**2. Immediate Addressing Mode** — Operand is a constant in the instruction.
```asm
MOV CX, 2000H  ; load CX with constant 2000H
ADD AL, 05H    ; add 5 to AL
```

**3. Direct Addressing Mode** — Effective address is given directly in the instruction.
```asm
MOV AX, [2000H]    ; load AX from memory location 2000H (default segment DS)
MOV [5000H], AL    ; store AL to memory 5000H
```

**4. Register Indirect Addressing Mode** — Address is in a register (BX, SI, DI, BP).
```asm
MOV AX, [BX]       ; load AX from address pointed by BX
MOV [DI], CL       ; store CL to address pointed by DI
```

**5. Based Addressing Mode** — [BX + displacement] or [BP + displacement].
```asm
MOV AX, [BX + 10H]     ; load AX from BX+10H
MOV CX, [BP + 5]       ; load CX from BP+5 (uses SS by default)
```

**6. Indexed Addressing Mode** — [SI + displacement] or [DI + displacement].
```asm
MOV AX, [SI + 5]       ; load AX from SI+5
MOV BX, [DI + 100H]    ; load BX from DI+100H
```

**7. Based-Indexed Addressing Mode** — [BX + SI] or [BX + DI] or [BP + SI] or [BP + DI], optionally with displacement.
```asm
MOV AX, [BX + SI]          ; load AX from BX+SI
MOV CX, [BX + DI + 10H]    ; load CX from BX+DI+10H
```

**8. String Addressing Mode** — SI and DI are used implicitly.
```asm
MOVSB      ; moves [DS:SI] to [ES:DI], auto-increments SI & DI
```

**Effective Address formula examples:**

| Instruction | Effective Address |
|-------------|-------------------|
| `MOV AX, [BX+SI+10H]` | EA = (BX) + (SI) + 10H |
| `MOV CX, [BP]` | EA = (BP) (uses SS segment) |
| `MOV DX, [SI]` | EA = (SI) (uses DS segment) |

#### Solution to (b):

```asm
; Program: Sum of first N natural numbers
ASSUME  CS:CODE, DS:DATA

DATA    SEGMENT
        N       DW  10          ; Sum from 1 to 10
        RESULT  DW  ?           ; Storage for result (55 expected)
DATA    ENDS

CODE    SEGMENT
START:  MOV AX, DATA
        MOV DS, AX              ; Initialize DS

        MOV CX, N               ; CX = N (loop counter)
        XOR AX, AX              ; AX = 0 (running sum)
        MOV SI, 01H             ; SI = 1 (current number)

NEXT:   ADD AX, SI              ; SUM = SUM + SI
        INC SI                  ; SI = SI + 1
        LOOP NEXT               ; Decrement CX, jump if CX ≠ 0

        MOV RESULT, AX          ; Store final result
        MOV AH, 4CH             ; Exit to DOS
        INT 21H
CODE    ENDS
END START
```

**Trace (when N = 10):**

| Iteration | SI (before) | AX (after ADD) | CX (after LOOP) |
|-----------|-------------|----------------|-----------------|
| 1 | 1 | 1 | 9 |
| 2 | 2 | 3 | 8 |
| 3 | 3 | 6 | 7 |
| 4 | 4 | 10 | 6 |
| 5 | 5 | 15 | 5 |
| 6 | 6 | 21 | 4 |
| 7 | 7 | 28 | 3 |
| 8 | 8 | 36 | 2 |
| 9 | 9 | 45 | 1 |
| 10 | 10 | 55 | 0 → exit |

**Final RESULT = 55.** ✓

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Q]`

**Q: a) Explain the format of an 8086 instruction with neat diagram. List the function of the Mod–R/M byte. (7 Marks)**
**b) Describe the various categories of assembler directives with at least two examples from each category. (7 Marks)**

#### Solution to (a):

**Format of an 8086 Machine Instruction:**

$$
\text{Instruction} = \underbrace{\text{Opcode}}_{1\text{ or }2\text{ bytes}} \; \vert \; \underbrace{\text{Mod–R/M}}_{0\text{ or }1\text{ byte}} \; \vert \; \underbrace{\text{Displacement}}_{0\text{ or }1\text{ or }2\text{ bytes}} \; \vert \; \underbrace{\text{Immediate}}_{0\text{ or }1\text{ or }2\text{ bytes}}
$$

**Diagram:**

```
+--------+----------+--------------+----------------+
| Opcode | Mod R/M  | Displacement | Immediate Data |
+--------+----------+--------------+----------------+
   1-2B     0/1B       0/1/2B         0/1/2B
   (Total: 1 to 6 bytes)
```

**Function of the Mod–R/M byte:**

The Mod–R/M byte is present in 2-operand instructions. It is divided into three fields:

| Field | Bits | Function |
|-------|------|----------|
| **MOD** | 7–6 | Specifies the addressing mode (memory vs register) and displacement size |
| **REG / Opcode Extension** | 5–3 | Identifies the second register operand, OR extends the opcode for instructions like `MOV`, `ADD`, `CMP` |
| **R/M** | 2–0 | Identifies the first register/memory operand |

**MOD field values:**

| MOD | Meaning |
|-----|---------|
| 00 | Memory, no displacement (or [BP] defaults to 0) |
| 01 | Memory, 8-bit signed displacement |
| 10 | Memory, 16-bit displacement |
| 11 | Register-to-register mode |

**Example:** For `MOV AX, BX`, the Mod–R/M byte = `11 011 000` = **D8H** (MOD=11, REG=011 for BX source, R/M=000 for AX destination).

**The Opcode byte is `8B`** (binary: `1000 1011`).
- Bit 7–2: Opcode core `100010`
- Bit 1 (D): 0 (source from REG)
- Bit 0 (W): 1 (word operation)

So machine code = `8B D8H` (2 bytes).

#### Solution to (b):

**Categories of Assembler Directives:**

**1. Segment Definition Directives** — Define the segments of a program.
- `SEGMENT` / `ENDS`: `CODE SEGMENT` ... `CODE ENDS`
- `ASSUME`: `ASSUME CS:CODE, DS:DATA`
- `ORG`: `ORG 1000H` (sets location counter to 1000H)
- `GROUP`: `GROUPED SEG NAMES` (combines segments)

**2. Data Definition Directives** — Reserve and initialize memory.
- `DB` (Define Byte): `MSG DB 'HELLO$'`
- `DW` (Define Word): `X DW 1234H, 5678H`
- `DD` (Define Doubleword): `Y DD 12345678H`
- `DUP`: `BUF DB 100 DUP(0)`

**3. Symbol Assignment Directives** — Assign values to names.
- `EQU`: `MAX EQU 100` (MAX is permanently 100)
- `=`: `COUNT = 0` then `COUNT = COUNT + 1` (reassignable)

**4. Address Operators** — Query the address or size of labels.
- `OFFSET`: `MOV BX, OFFSET DATA`
- `SEG`: `MOV AX, SEG DATA`
- `PTR`: `MOV AL, BYTE PTR [BX]`
- `LENGTH`, `SIZE`, `TYPE`: For array queries

**5. Procedure and Module Directives** — For subroutines and modular programs.
- `PROC` / `ENDP`: `SUB1 PROC NEAR` ... `SUB1 ENDP`
- `NEAR` / `FAR`: Specify reach
- `PUBLIC` / `EXTRN`: For inter-module linkage
- `NAME` / `TITLE`: Module identification
- `INCLUDE`: Insert another source file
- `MACRO` / `ENDM`: Define reusable code blocks

**6. Program Control Directives** — End the program and control conditional assembly.
- `END START`: Marks end of source
- `IF` / `ELSE` / `ENDIF`: Conditional assembly
- `;` (semicolon): Comments
- `$`: Current location counter

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Confusing `EQU` with `=`.** `EQU` is *one-time* assignment (cannot redefine). `=` *can* be reassigned. Examiners deduct 1 mark if mixed up.
> 2. **Forgetting to load DS manually.** Writing `ASSUME DS:DATA` does NOT initialize DS at runtime. You MUST write `MOV AX, DATA` followed by `MOV DS, AX`. Many students lose 2 marks for missing this.
> 3. **Using `MOV` with two memory operands.** Illegal! At least one operand must be a register. Example: `MOV [BX], [SI]` → error.
> 4. **Confusing `DB` and `DW` size in `PTR` usage.** `BYTE PTR [BX]` for byte access, `WORD PTR [BX]` for word access. Mixing them corrupts memory.
> 5. **Wrong syntax for `END` directive.** Must include entry label: `END START`, not just `END`.
> 6. **Treating `OFFSET` as part of the label name.** It's an operator: `MOV BX, OFFSET DATA` (correct) vs. `MOV BX, OFFSETDATA` (wrong).
> 7. **Using `DUP` with non-zero or negative counts incorrectly.** Always `DUP(value)`, not `DUP value`.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ The 8086 instruction set has **6 functional groups** (Data Transfer, Arithmetic, Logical/Bit, String, Control Transfer, Processor Control). Always use this exact Intel taxonomy in exams.
- ✅ **Instructions** generate machine code; **Directives** do NOT — they guide the assembler.
- ✅ The **Mod–R/M byte** = MOD (2 bits) + REG (3 bits) + R/M (3 bits) = total 8 bits.
- ✅ **W bit** (in opcode): 0 = byte, 1 = word. **D bit**: 0 = REG is source, 1 = REG is destination.
- ✅ **Physical Address** = Segment Register × 16 + Offset. (Always shift segment left by 1 hex digit.)
- ✅ Address bus is **20 bits** → **1 MB** of physical memory.
- ✅ **AX, BX, CX, DX** can be split into 8-bit halves: AH/AL, BH/BL, CH/CL, DH/DL.
- ✅ **Segment registers:** CS (code), DS (data), SS (stack), ES (extra).
- ✅ **Flags affected by arithmetic/logic:** CF, AF, OF, SF, ZF, PF. Data transfer instructions (MOV, PUSH, POP) do **NOT** affect flags.
- ✅ String instructions auto-adjust SI/DI based on **Direction Flag (DF)**. STD sets DF=1 (decrement), CLD clears DF=0 (increment).
- ✅ **REP** repeats while **CX ≠ 0**. **REPE/REPZ** also requires **ZF=1**. **REPNE/REPNZ** requires **ZF=0**.
- ✅ **Conditional jumps** are either **signed** (JG, JL, JGE, JLE) or **unsigned** (JA, JB, JAE, JBE). Never mix.
- ✅ **SHR** fills MSB with 0 (unsigned). **SAR** copies sign bit (signed).
- ✅ `MUL byte` → result in **AX**. `MUL word` → result in **DX:AX** (32-bit).
- ✅ `DIV byte` → AX÷r/m8: quotient in **AL**, remainder in **AH**. `DIV word` → DX:AX÷r/m16: quotient in **AX**, remainder in **DX**.
- ✅ **Key directives:** DB, DW, DD, DQ, DT, EQU, =, OFFSET, SEG, PTR, LENGTH, SIZE, TYPE, DUP, ORG, ASSUME, SEGMENT/ENDS, PROC/ENDP, END, PUBLIC, EXTRN, MACRO/ENDM, $.
- ✅ **ASSUME** is informational only. **Always manually load DS** with `MOV AX, DATA` and `MOV DS, AX`.
- ✅ **$** = current location counter. Use for relative jumps: `JMP $+5`.
- ✅ Procedure scope: **NEAR** = within same segment (1-byte return offset). **FAR** = different segment (segment:offset on stack).
- ✅ `END START` is mandatory for the assembler to know the entry point. Missing START or misspelling it causes "no entry point" errors.
- ✅ The instruction queue is **6 bytes** in 8086 and **4 bytes** in 8088.
- ✅ Every segment must start on a **paragraph boundary** (address divisible by 16).
- ✅ Maximum **shift/rotate count** in a single instruction = 1; using **CL** allows variable shifts (0–255).
- ✅ 8086 is the **direct ancestor** of modern x86 CPUs — this knowledge is foundational for systems programming, firmware, and OS development.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Assembly Language Programming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCIT404 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Assembly Language Programming ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Assembly Language Programming (ALP)** is a low-level symbolic programming paradigm in which mnemonics (human-readable abbreviations) are used as direct, one-to-one representations of the native machine-level opcodes of a target microprocessor. Each assembly statement corresponds to exactly **one** machine instruction, making it the most granular software layer above hardware. In the KTU 2024 scheme context, ALP refers specifically to programming the **Intel 8086 microprocessor** using the **MASM (Microsoft Macro Assembler)** or **TASM (Turbo Assembler)** syntax, organized through segmented memory architecture and assembled into `.EXE` or `.COM` executable files. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of assembly language like **driving a manual transmission car with full dashboard access**: - **High-Level Language (C/Java)** = Automatic car: You say "drive to Kochi" — the system handles gear-shifting, clutch, RPM. - **Assembly Language** = Manual car: You must explicitly control *every* gear, *every* clutch press, *every* RPM. - **Machine Code (binary 0s and 1s)** = The raw mechanical engine pistons firing. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight**: Module 1 emphasizes the 8086 instruction set, addressing modes, program structure (segments, ASSUME directive), and standard subroutines (macros, procedures, stack operations, interrupts). Mastery of addressing modes is non-negotiable — they appear in nearly every Part B question. ### 1.3 Key Terminology | Term | Definition | |---|---| | **Mnemonic** | Symbolic abbreviation of a machine instruction (e.g., `MOV`, `ADD`) | | **Opcode** | The actual binary operation code executed by the CPU | | **Assembler** | Software that converts assembly mnemonics into machine code | | **Directive** | Non-executable instruction that guides the assembler (e.g., `SEGMENT`, `DB`) | | **Label** | Symbolic name attached to a memory address | | **Operand** | Data on which the instruction operates | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mnemonics ≠ Directives**. `MOV AX, BX` is a mnemonic (generates machine code). `AX EQU 5` is a directive (instructs assembler, no machine code generated). --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The 8086 Instruction Execution Cycle Every assembly instruction follows a strict fetch-decode-execute cycle mapped to the **BIU (Bus Interface Unit)** and **EU (Execution Unit)**: 1. **BIU** fetches instruction bytes from the **Code Segment (CS:IP)** and places them in the 6-byte instruction queue. 2. **EU** decodes the opcode and locates operands. 3. **EU** executes the operation; if operands are memory-resident, BIU performs the bus cycle. 4. **Flag Register** is updated conditionally based on the operation's result. ### 2.2 The 8086 Instruction Format The 8086 supports **Variable-Length Instructions** from 1 to 6 bytes: $$ \text{Instruction} = \underbrace{\text{Opcode (6 bits)}}_{\text{Operation}} + \underbrace{\text{D (1 bit)}}_{\text{Direction}} + \underbrace{\text{W (1 bit)}}_{\text{Word/Byte}} + \underbrace{\text{Mod (2 bits)}}_{\text{Mode}} + \underbrace{\text{Reg (3 bits)}}_{\text{Register}} + \underbrace{\text{R/M (3 bits)}}_{\text{Reg/Memory}} $$ | Field | Meaning | |---|---| | **Opcode** | Identifies the operation (e.g., MOV, ADD) | | **D** | Direction of data flow (0 = Reg→R/M, 1 = R/M→Reg) | | **W** | Operand size (0 = 8-bit byte, 1 = 16-bit word) | | **Mod** | Addressing mode (00=Memory no disp, 01=+8-bit disp, 10=+16-bit disp, 11=Register) | | **Reg** | Source/Destination register code (000=AX, 001=CX, 010=DX, 011=BX, 100=SP, 101=BP, 110=SI, 111=DI) | | **R/M** | Register/Memory operand field | ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — 8086 Addressing Modes | # | Addressing Mode | Syntax | Effective Address (EA) | KTU Significance | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Register** | `MOV AX, BX` | N/A (register direct) | Fastest, no memory access | | 2 | **Immediate** | `MOV AX, 5000H` | Operand is in instruction | Constant loading | | 3 | **Direct** | `MOV AX, [5000H]` | EA = $\text{5000H}$ (16-bit displacement) | Absolute memory access | | 4 | **Register Indirect** | `MOV AX, [BX]` | EA = $\vert \text{BX} \vert$ | Pointer-based access | | 5 | **Based Relative** | `MOV AX, [BX+SI+disp]` | EA = $\vert \text{BX} \vert + \vert \text{SI} \vert + \text{disp}$ | Array with base | | 6 | **Indexed Relative** | `MOV AX, [SI+disp]` | EA = $\vert \text{SI} \vert + \text{disp}$ | Array indexing | | 7 | **Based Indexed** | `MOV AX, [BX][SI]` | EA = $\vert \text{BX} \vert + \vert \text{SI} \vert$ | 2D array | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Default Segment Register Rule** (Frequently tested in KTU): > - `[BX]` → Data Segment (**DS**) > - `[BP]` → Stack Segment (**SS**) > - `[SI]`, `[DI]` → Data Segment (**DS**) > - `[BP+disp]` → Stack Segment (**SS**) ### 2.4 Instruction Set Categories (8086) | Category | Examples | Primary Use | |---|---|---| | **Data Transfer** | `MOV, PUSH, POP, XCHG, LEA, XLAT, IN, OUT` | Move data between registers/memory/IO | | **Arithmetic** | `ADD, SUB, MUL, DIV, INC, DEC, CMP, ADC, SBB, NEG` | Mathematical operations | | **Logical/Bit** | `AND, OR, XOR, NOT, SHL, SHR, SAR, ROL, ROR` | Bitwise manipulation | | **String** | `MOVS, LODS, STOS, CMPS, SCAS` | Operate on byte/word strings | | **Control Transfer** | `JMP, CALL, RET, Jcc (conditional), LOOP, INT` | Alter program flow | | **Flag Manipulation** | `STC, CLC, CMC, STD, CLD, STI, CLI` | Set/Clear flags | | **Processor Control** | `NOP, HLT, WAIT, ESC, LOCK` | CPU control | --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Physical Address Calculation The 8086 uses a **20-bit physical address** derived from two 16-bit segment and offset registers. $$ \text{Physical Address} = (\text{Segment Register} \times 16) + \text{Offset} $$ **Step-by-step derivation:** **Step 1:** The BIU needs a 20-bit address to access the 1 MB memory space ($2^{20} = \text{1,048,576}$ bytes). **Step 2:** Since the 8086 has only 16-bit registers, it cannot hold a 20-bit address in one register. **Step 3:** Solution — split the 20-bit address into a **16-bit segment base** and a **16-bit offset**. **Step 4:** The segment base is implicitly shifted left by 4 bits (multiplied by 16) to produce a 20-bit base. **Step 5:** The offset is added to the shifted segment base to yield the physical address. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Let } \text{Segment} &= 2000\text{H} \\ \text{Let } \text{Offset} &= 3456\text{H} \\ \text{Segment} \times 16 &= 2000\text{H} \times 10\text{H} = 20000\text{H} \\ \text{Physical Address} &= 20000\text{H} + 3456\text{H} = 23456\text{H} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight**: Each segment must start on a **16-byte boundary** (paragraph) because multiplying by 16 always clears the lower 4 bits. ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Program Structure for .EXE File A standard KTU-expected 8086 ALP skeleton: ```nasm .MODEL SMALL ; Memory model: 64KB code + 64KB data .STACK 100H ; Allocate 256 bytes for stack .DATA ; Data segment starts here COUNT DB 10H ; Define byte initialized to 10H RESULT DW ? ; Define uninitialized word MSG DB 'HELLO$' ; String for display .CODE ; Code segment starts here ASSUME CS:CODE, DS:DATA ; Tell assembler segment associations START: ; Entry-point label MOV AX, @DATA ; Load address of DATA segment MOV DS, AX ; Initialize DS register ; ---- user program logic here ---- MOV AH, 4CH INT 21H ; DOS terminate program END START ; End of program, entry = START ``` ### 3.3 Derivation: Arithmetic Operation Program Logic (Multi-byte Addition) **Problem:** Add two 16-bit numbers stored in memory and store the result. **Logical Steps (as expected in KTU valuation):** **Step 1:** Initialize the data segment to point to the variable region. $$ \text{MOV AX, @DATA} \quad ; \quad \text{MOV DS, AX} $$ **Step 2:** Load the first operand (low byte then high byte) into AX. $$ \text{MOV AL, [NUM1]} \quad ; \quad \text{MOV AH, [NUM1+1]} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{AX} = \text{NUM1}_{\text{high}}:\text{NUM1}_{\text{low}} $$ **Step 3:** Add the second operand using `ADD` for low byte and `ADC` (add with carry) for high byte. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{ADD AL, [NUM2]} & \Rightarrow \text{Low byte sum, sets CF if overflow} \\ \text{ADC AH, [NUM2+1]} & \Rightarrow \text{High byte sum + carry from low} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4:** Store the result back to memory. $$ \text{MOV [RESULT], AL} \quad ; \quad \text{MOV [RESULT+1], AH} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why ADC?** If the low-byte addition overflows (result > FFH), the carry flag is set. Using `ADD` for the high byte would ignore this carry, producing an incorrect result. **ADC = ADD + Carry Flag** is essential for multi-byte arithmetic. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Common DOS Interrupt Services (INT 21H) | AH Value | Function | Entry Parameters | Return | |---|---|---|---| | **01H** | Read character with echo | — | AL = char | | **02H** | Display character | DL = char | — | | **09H** | Display string | DS:DX = string addr | — | | **0AH** | Buffered keyboard input | DS:DX = buffer | Buffer filled | | **4CH** | Terminate program | AL = return code | — | | **02H** | Print char (BIOS INT 10H) | AH=02H, DL=char | — | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 8086 Execution Flow — BIU/EU Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Instruction Queue
6 bytes FIFO] --> B[EU Execution Unit] B --> C[ALU
Arithmetic Logic Unit] B --> D[General Registers
AX BX CX DX] B --> E[Flag Register] B --> F[Pointer and Index
SP BP SI DI] G[BIU Bus Interface Unit] --> A H[CS IP Register] --> G I[Segment Registers
CS DS SS ES] --> G G --> J[Address Bus
20 bits] G --> K[Data Bus
16 bits] G --> L[Control Bus] ``` ### 4.2 Assembly Language Program Development Cycle ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Step1 Write ALP
edit myfile.asm] --> B[Step2 Assemble
MASM myfile.asm] B --> C{Object File
myfile.obj} C --> D[Step3 Link
LINK myfile.obj] D --> E[Executable File
myfile.exe] E --> F[Step4 Load
DOS Loader] F --> G[Step5 Execute
8086 CPU] G --> H[Step6 Debug
DEBUG or TD] C -->|Error| I[Fix Syntax Errors] D -->|Error| J[Fix Linker Errors] G -->|Runtime Error| K[Fix Logic Errors] ``` ### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Memory Segmentation ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SEG[8086 1 MB Memory Space] direction LR S1[Code Segment
CS x 16
Up to 64KB]:::segStyle S2[Data Segment
DS x 16
Up to 64KB]:::segStyle S3[Stack Segment
SS x 16
Up to 64KB]:::segStyle S4[Extra Segment
ES x 16
Up to 64KB]:::segStyle end OFFSET1[IP Instruction Pointer] --> S1 OFFSET2[SI DI BX Effective Address] --> S2 OFFSET3[SP BP Effective Address] --> S3 OFFSET4[DI String Operations] --> S4 classDef segStyle fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,stroke-width:2px ``` ### 4.4 Macro vs Procedure Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Need Repeated Code Block] --> B{Called How Many Times?} B -->|Few times
or short code| C[Use MACRO] B -->|Many times
or large code| D[Use PROCEDURE] C --> E[Code expanded inline
at every call site
Larger .EXE size
Faster execution] D --> F[Code stored once
CALL RET mechanism
Smaller .EXE size
Slight overhead] C --> G[MACRO_NAME MACRO args
...instructions...
ENDM] D --> H[PROC_NAME PROC
...instructions...
RET
PROC_NAME ENDP] ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** *Define the term "Mnemonic" in the context of 8086 assembly language. Give two examples.* **Model Answer:** A **mnemonic** is a short, human-readable symbolic representation of a machine-level operation code (opcode) used in assembly language programming. Each mnemonic maps one-to-one to an opcode that the 8086 microprocessor can execute. **Examples:** - `MOV` (transfer data) - `ADD` (perform addition) - `JMP` (unconditional jump) Mnemonics are translated into binary opcodes by the **assembler** (e.g., MASM/TASM). *(3 marks)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** *What is the role of the `ASSUME` directive in an 8086 assembly program?* **Model Answer:** The `ASSUME` directive informs the assembler about the association between segment registers (CS, DS, SS, ES) and the segment names defined in the program (CODE, DATA, STACK). **Syntax:** `ASSUME CS:CODE, DS:DATA` It does **not** generate any machine code. It enables the assembler to correctly calculate segment-relative offsets and to perform segment-override validations. The actual segment register values are loaded at runtime using `MOV` instructions (e.g., `MOV AX, @DATA; MOV DS, AX`). *(3 marks)* --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a) Explain the different addressing modes of 8086 microprocessor with suitable examples. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** The 8086 supports **7 addressing modes** that specify how the operand(s) of an instruction are located: **1. Register Addressing Mode:** The operand is a CPU register. ```nasm MOV AX, BX ; Copy contents of BX into AX ADD AL, CL ; Add CL to AL ``` *Advantage:* Fastest — no memory access required. **2. Immediate Addressing Mode:** The operand is a constant value embedded in the instruction. ```nasm MOV CX, 0A2FH ; Load CX with constant 0A2FH ADD BL, 05H ; Add 5 to BL ``` **3. Direct (Absolute) Addressing Mode:** The memory address is given directly as a 16-bit displacement. ```nasm MOV AL, [5000H] ; Load AL with byte at physical address DS:5000H ``` **4. Register Indirect Addressing Mode:** The memory address is held in a register (BX, SI, DI, BP). ```nasm MOV AX, [BX] ; AX ← word at memory location pointed by BX ``` **5. Based Relative Addressing Mode:** A base register (BX or BP) is added to a displacement. ```nasm MOV AL, [BX+05H] ; AL ← byte at address (BX + 05H) ``` **6. Indexed Relative Addressing Mode:** An index register (SI or DI) is added to a displacement. ```nasm MOV CX, [SI+10H] ; CX ← word at address (SI + 10H) ``` **7. Based Indexed Addressing Mode:** A base register + an index register (+ optional displacement). ```nasm MOV DH, [BX+SI+08H] ; DH ← byte at address (BX + SI + 8) ``` | Mode | EA Formula | Default Segment | |---|---|---| | Register | N/A | — | | Immediate | Constant in instruction | — | | Direct | $\text{Disp}$ | DS | | Register Indirect | $\vert \text{BX/SI/DI/BP} \vert$ | DS (SS for BP) | | Based Relative | $\vert \text{BX/BP} \vert + \text{Disp}$ | DS (SS for BP) | | Indexed Relative | $\vert \text{SI/DI} \vert + \text{Disp}$ | DS | | Based Indexed | $\vert \text{BX} \vert + \vert \text{SI/DI} \vert$ | DS | *(7 marks — 1 per mode + summary table)* --- **(b) Write an 8086 ALP to add two 16-bit numbers stored in memory and store the result. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** ```nasm .MODEL SMALL .STACK 100H .DATA NUM1 DW 1234H NUM2 DW 0ABCDH RESULT DW ? .CODE ASSUME CS:CODE, DS:DATA START: MOV AX, @DATA MOV DS, AX MOV AX, NUM1 ; AX = 1234H ADD AX, NUM2 ; AX = 1234H + 0ABCDH = 0BE01H MOV RESULT, AX ; Store result in memory MOV AH, 4CH INT 21H ; Terminate program END START ``` **Explanation of key steps:** **Step 1 — Segment Initialization:** `MOV AX, @DATA` followed by `MOV DS, AX` loads the address of the data segment into the DS register, which is mandatory for accessing data variables. **Step 2 — Load and Add:** `MOV AX, NUM1` loads the first 16-bit number (1234H) into AX. `ADD AX, NUM2` performs the addition: $\text{1234H} + \text{0ABCDH} = \text{BE01H}$ (low byte) and generates **carry flag = 1**. **Step 3 — Store Result:** `MOV RESULT, AX` writes the 16-bit result back to the memory variable `RESULT`. **Step 4 — Terminate:** `INT 21H` with `AH=4CH` cleanly exits the program and returns control to DOS. | Step | Instruction | Purpose | |---|---|---| | 1 | `MOV AX, @DATA` | Get data segment address | | 2 | `MOV DS, AX` | Initialize DS register | | 3 | `MOV AX, NUM1` | Load first operand | | 4 | `ADD AX, NUM2` | Perform addition | | 5 | `MOV RESULT, AX` | Store result | | 6 | `INT 21H` (AH=4CH) | Exit to DOS | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout**: Students commonly (1) forget to initialize `DS` with `MOV AX, @DATA; MOV DS, AX` — leading to "wrong data" errors; (2) use `ADD AL` for 16-bit numbers — producing only low-byte result; (3) forget `INT 21H` termination — causing the program to hang. Always validate your segment initialization in exams. *(7 marks — 2 for code, 3 for explanation, 2 for analysis table)* --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a) With a neat diagram, explain the internal architecture of the 8086 microprocessor. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** The 8086 microprocessor is divided into **two independent functional units** connected by an internal 16-bit data bus: **1. Bus Interface Unit (BIU):** Responsible for all external bus operations. Contains: - **Instruction Queue (6 bytes):** Prefetch up to 6 instruction bytes — the heart of 8086 pipelining. - **Segment Registers (CS, DS, SS, ES):** Hold 16-bit base addresses of the four 1-MB memory segments. - **Instruction Pointer (IP):** Holds the 16-bit offset of the next instruction in CS. - **Address Generation Circuit:** Computes the 20-bit physical address as $\text{Physical} = \text{Segment} \times 16 + \text{Offset}$. **2. Execution Unit (EU):** Decodes and executes instructions. Contains: - **Control Unit:** Decodes opcodes, generates timing/control signals. - **ALU (16-bit):** Performs arithmetic (+, −, ×, ÷) and logical (AND, OR, XOR) operations. - **Flag Register (16-bit):** Status flags (CF, PF, AF, ZF, SF, OF) and control flags (DF, IF, TF). - **General Purpose Registers:** AX, BX, CX, DX — each split into 8-bit halves (AH/AL etc.). - **Pointer and Index Registers:** SP, BP, SI, DI. **Functional Block Diagram:** ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph BIU[BIU Bus Interface Unit] IQ[Instruction Queue 6 bytes] SR[Segment Registers CS DS SS ES] IP_R[Instruction Pointer IP] AGC[Address Generation Circuit] end subgraph EU[EU Execution Unit] CU[Control Unit] ALU_C[ALU 16 bit] FR[Flag Register] GPR[General Registers AX BX CX DX] PIR[Pointer Index SP BP SI DI] end BIU <-->|Internal 16 bit Bus| EU BIU --> EXT[External 20 bit Address Bus] BIU --> EXTB[External 16 bit Data Bus] ``` **Key advantage of this split architecture:** While the EU executes the current instruction, the BIU prefetches the next instruction bytes — this is called **pipelining**, and it gives the 8086 a significant speed boost over its predecessor 8085 (which did not pipeline). *(7 marks — 2 for diagram, 3 for component explanation, 2 for pipelining benefit)* --- **(b) Differentiate between a Procedure and a Macro in 8086 assembly language. Write a sample code to demonstrate each. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Conceptual Difference:** | Parameter | **Procedure (PROC/ENDP)** | **Macro (MACRO/ENDM)** | |---|---|---| | Invocation | `CALL PROCNAME` | `PROCNAME` (as if it were a real instruction) | | Code Location | Stored once in memory | Expanded inline at every call site | | Stack Usage | Uses stack (PUSH, RET) | No stack involvement | | Execution Speed | Slightly slower (CALL/RET overhead) | Faster (no CALL overhead) | | Program Size | Smaller (one copy) | Larger (many copies) | | Parameter Passing | Through registers, stack, or memory | Through macro arguments | | Conditional Assembly | Not possible | Possible (via IF, IFDEF directives) | | Scope | Near or Far | Local to file | **Sample Procedure:** ```nasm .MODEL SMALL .CODE ASSUME CS:CODE ADD16 PROC ADD AX, BX ; Add BX to AX RET ADD16 ENDP START: MOV AX, 0005H MOV BX, 0003H CALL ADD16 ; AX = 0008H after return MOV AH, 4CH INT 21H END START ``` **Sample Macro:** ```nasm .MODEL SMALL ADD16 MACRO A, B MOV AX, A MOV BX, B ADD AX, BX ENDM .CODE ASSUME CS:CODE START: ADD16 0005H, 0003H ; AX = 0008H (inline expansion) MOV AH, 4CH INT 21H END START ``` **When to use what:** - **Use MACRO** for small, frequently-invoked code fragments (e.g., setting up DS, sending chars to printer). - **Use PROCEDURE** for large code blocks or when program size matters (e.g., library functions). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout**: Students frequently confuse the syntax — `MACRO ... ENDM` vs `PROC ... ENDP`. Also, macro arguments are positional (no type checking), while procedure arguments are passed via registers/stack explicitly. Don't lose marks by mixing up these two. *(7 marks — 3 for comparison table, 2 for procedure code, 2 for macro code)* --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Assembly Language Programming (Module 1)** - **Mnemonic vs Directive**: Mnemonics generate machine code (`MOV`, `ADD`); directives guide the assembler (`DB`, `ASSUME`, `SEGMENT`). - **8086 Segments**: Code (CS:IP), Data (DS:offset), Stack (SS:SP), Extra (ES:DI). - **Physical Address Formula**: $\text{PA} = \text{Segment} \times 16 + \text{Offset}$ (always 20 bits). - **Default Segment Rule**: `[BX/SI/DI]` → DS; `[BP]` → SS. - **Instruction Format**: Opcode (6) + D (1) + W (1) + Mod (2) + Reg (3) + R/M (3) = 16 bits minimum. - **7 Addressing Modes**: Register, Immediate, Direct, Register Indirect, Based Relative, Indexed Relative, Based Indexed. - **Data Transfer**: `MOV, PUSH, POP, XCHG, LEA, XLAT`. - **Arithmetic essentials**: `ADD, ADC, SUB, SBB, MUL, DIV, CMP` — use `ADC`/`SBB` for multi-byte. - **Logical/Bit**: `AND, OR, XOR, NOT, SHL, SHR, SAR, ROL, ROR`. - **Control Flow**: `JMP, CALL, RET, Jcc, LOOP, INT n`. - **Flag Control**: `STC/CLC, STD/CLD, STI/CLI` — clear DF before string ops! - **String Instructions**: `MOVS, LODS, STOS, CMPS, SCAS` — require `REP` prefix. - **DOS Interrupts**: `INT 21H` is the gateway — AH=01 read, AH=02 display, AH=09 print string, AH=4CH exit. - **Procedure**: `PROC ... ENDP` with `CALL`/`RET` — uses stack. - **Macro**: `MACRO ... ENDM` — inline expansion, no stack overhead. - **Stack Operations**: `PUSH` decrements SP first, `POP` increments SP last; SP always points to topmost item. - **LE vs MOV**: `LEA AX, [SI]` loads the *address*; `MOV AX, [SI]` loads the *contents*. - **Pipelining**: BIU prefetches while EU executes — a 6-byte queue reduces wait states. - **Common KTU Mistakes**: Forgetting `MOV DS, AX` initialization; using wrong segment default; mixing up `ADD` vs `ADC` in multi-byte arithmetic; missing `.STACK` directive.

topics.title: Modular Programming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCIT404 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Modular Programming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Modular Programming** in the context of the 8086 microprocessor is a structured software design methodology in which a large assembly language program is decomposed into smaller, logically independent, and functionally cohesive sub-programs called **modules**. Each module is written, assembled, and debugged as a separate source file (`.ASM`), producing its own relocatable object file (`.OBJ`). These object files are later combined by a **Linker** (e.g., Microsoft's `LINK.EXE`) into a single absolute executable file (`.EXE`).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Essence):** Modular programming in 8086 assembly involves dividing a program into multiple separately assembled source files that share variables (via `PUBLIC`/`EXTRN` directives) and procedures, finally linked to form one executable. This adheres to the CO1 outcome: *“Develop assembly language programs for 8086 microprocessor.”*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are building a **massive Lego city**. Would you glue all 50,000 bricks into one unbreakable sculpture? Absolutely not. You build **houses**, **bridges**, and **cars** as separate kits, and then *snap* them together. If a window in the bridge cracks, you replace just that kit — not the entire city.

Modular programming in 8086 assembly is the **exact same idea**:
- Each **module** = a separate `.ASM` file (your Lego kit).
- The **Linker** = your hands that snap the kits together.
- The **stack segment** = the shared communication channel where modules pass data and call each other.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why KTU asks this in Module 1:** The 8086 has a tiny register set. Real-world firmware (BIOS, device drivers) was written in modular 8086 assembly. Mastering modules prepares you to understand how large-scale low-level systems are built before moving to higher-level C embedded programming.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **8086 Data Bus Width:** **16 bits** (the fundamental word size for all data movement between modules).
- **8086 Address Bus Width:** **20 bits** (allowing **1 MB** of addressable memory, segmented into **64 KB** code, data, stack, and extra segments).
- **Maximum Module Size:** Each individual code segment is limited to **64 KB** (since the Instruction Pointer `IP` is only 16 bits, and `CS:IP` is the addressing pair).
- **Standard Linker Output:** Microsoft `LINK.EXE` produces a `.EXE` file with a minimum header of **512 bytes**.
- **Near Call / Far Call Distinction:** *Near* = same segment (1 word pushed on stack), *Far* = different segment (2 words pushed — CS and IP).

## 1.4 The Three Pillars of 8086 Modular Programming

| Pillar | Directive | Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Visibility (Export)** | `PUBLIC` | Tells assembler *“I am giving this symbol away to other modules.”* |
| **Visibility (Import)** | `EXTRN` | Tells assembler *“This symbol is owned by another module — expect it at link time.”* |
| **Logical Grouping** | `GROUP` | Combines multiple segments into one 64 KB logical unit. |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick for Exams:** **P**UBLIC **P**ublishes, **E**XTRN **E**xternalises. A symbol must be `PUBLIC` in its home module AND `EXTRN` in every module that uses it.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown: How a Modular 8086 Program Lives and Breathes

The life-cycle of a modular 8086 program follows a strict **6-stage pipeline**. KTU frequently tests the *“list the steps involved in assembling and executing a modular program”* question.

1. **Source Code Authoring:** Each module is written in a separate `.ASM` file (e.g., `MAIN.ASM`, `UTIL.ASM`).
2. **Independent Assembly:** The MASM assembler (`MASM.EXE`) is invoked separately on each file. It produces a **relocatable `.OBJ`** (object file) — not yet a runnable file because symbol addresses are still *unresolved*.
3. **Linking:** `LINK.EXE` reads all `.OBJ` files, resolves every `PUBLIC`/`EXTRN` cross-reference, assigns **absolute addresses**, and emits a single `.EXE` (or `.COM`).
4. **Loading:** At runtime, the DOS loader reads the `.EXE` header, allocates memory, and sets `CS`, `DS`, `SS`, `ES` registers.
5. **Execution:** `CS:IP` is set to the entry point declared by `END <label>` in the main module.
6. **Inter-Module Call:** When module A `CALL FAR PTR` to module B, the CPU pushes current `CS` and `IP` onto the stack, then loads the new `CS:IP` from the procedure declared in module B.

### The "Why" Behind Each Step (Examiner-Friendly Reasoning)

- **Why separate assembly?** Because each module can be developed by different team members in parallel — analogous to Agile sprints.
- **Why a Linker stage?** Because the assembler only sees *one* file. It has no idea what address another module will end up at. The linker is the *“diplomat”* that resolves the address diplomacy.
- **Why is the stack segment special?** Because every `CALL`/`RET` between modules writes to the stack, and the stack segment must be **shared and large enough** to hold the deepest nested call chain.

## 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Formula & Directive Sheet

| Directive | Syntax | Purpose | KTU Exam Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| `PUBLIC` | `PUBLIC symbol [, symbol …]` | Declares symbols visible to other modules at link time. | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| `EXTRN` | `EXTRN symbol : type` | Declares a symbol defined in another module. `type` = `BYTE`, `WORD`, `DWORD`, `NEAR`, `FAR`, `ABS`. | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| `GROUP` | `GROUP name seg1 [, seg2 …]` | Forces segments into one 64 KB logical group; allows single-segment addressing. | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| `ASSUME` | `ASSUME CS:seg, DS:dataseg, SS:stackseg, ES:extraseg` | Tells assembler which segment register points to which segment. | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| `SEGMENT … ENDS` | `name SEGMENT [align] [combine] [class]` | Defines a logical segment. `combine` is **KEY** for modules: `PUBLIC` here makes segments concatenate. | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| `PROC … ENDP` | `name PROC [NEAR/FAR]` | Defines a procedure; `NEAR` is intra-segment, `FAR` is inter-segment. | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| `END` | `END [start_label]` | Marks end of source; `start_label` becomes the program entry point in the main module. | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |

## 2.3 Segment Combination Types (Critical for Multi-Module Linking)

When two modules each declare a segment with the same **name** and `PUBLIC` combine-type, the linker concatenates them in the order they appear on the `LINK` command line.

| Combine Type | Behaviour |
|---|---|
| `PUBLIC` | Segments from different modules are concatenated into **one contiguous** segment in memory. |
| `COMMON` | Segments **overlap** (share the same starting address) — used for uninitialised shared buffers. |
| `STACK` | Treated like `PUBLIC` but registers `SS` automatically. |
| `MEMORY` | Placed **after all other** combined segments (used in older MASM). |
| (none / `PRIVATE`) | Each module gets its **own** physical copy — even if names match. |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **BIOS Firmware (1980s–1990s):** Written as thousands of modular 8086 `.ASM` files linked into a single ROM image.
- **DOS Device Drivers (.SYS):** Built modularly; a printer driver could be swapped without recompiling the keyboard driver.
- **Modern Parallel:** This concept survives today in **C's `extern` / `static`** and in **embedded `.c` + `.h` files** — the same idea, just hidden by the compiler.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example: A Two-Module 8086 Program

**Problem Statement:** Write a modular 8086 ALP consisting of two modules — `MAIN.ASM` and `CALC.ASM`. The main module should define a 16-bit variable `NUM` in its data segment, pass it to the `CALC` module, which computes the **square** of the number and stores the result back. Show the full source code, the link command, and the final memory map.

### Module 1: `MAIN.ASM` (Driver Module)

```asm
;=====================================================
; MAIN.ASM - Driver module
; Defines data, calls CALC module, displays result
;=====================================================

DATA    SEGMENT PUBLIC          ; PUBLIC combine so CALC can append
NUM     DW  0007H              ; 16-bit number whose square we want
RESULT  DW  ?                  ; 16-bit result placeholder
MSG     DB  'Square = $'       ; DOS print string
DATA    ENDS

STACK_S SEGMENT STACK          ; STACK combine => shared stack
        DB  100 DUP(?)
STACK_S ENDS

CODE    SEGMENT PUBLIC
        ASSUME CS:CODE, DS:DATA, SS:STACK_S

        PUBLIC  NUM            ; <-- KEY: export NUM to CALC

START:  MOV  AX, @DATA         ; Load DS with segment address
        MOV  DS, AX

        CALL FAR PTR SQUARE    ; <-- INTER-MODULE CALL

        ; Display 'Square = ' using DOS INT 21H
        MOV  AH, 09H
        LEA  DX, MSG
        INT  21H

        ; Display the result in hex
        MOV  AX, RESULT
        CALL FAR PTR DISPLAY_HEX

        ; Exit to DOS
        MOV  AH, 4CH
        INT  21H
CODE    ENDS
        END  START
```

**Line-by-Line Reasoning (Exhaustive):**

- `DATA SEGMENT PUBLIC` — The `PUBLIC` combine-type is what allows the `CALC` module to *append* its own data to the same physical data segment. Without it, the linker would create two separate data segments.
- `NUM DW 0007H` — Initialised at assembly time. Since it is a `DW` (Define Word), it occupies exactly 2 bytes at offset `0` of the data segment.
- `STACK_S SEGMENT STACK` — The `STACK` combine-type tells the linker to combine all stack segments from all modules into one and to auto-set `SS:SP` at load time.
- `PUBLIC NUM` — This directive is the **export declaration**. Without it, even if `CALC` declared `EXTRN NUM`, the linker would throw error `L2025`.
- `CALL FAR PTR SQUARE` — The `FAR PTR` forces a far call (5 bytes: `9A` opcode + 4-byte pointer), pushing both `CS` and `IP` onto the stack. This is **mandatory** when calling across modules.
- `END START` — Declares `START` as the program entry point. The loader reads the `.EXE` header and writes the offset of `START` into the `CS:IP` initialisation fields.

### Module 2: `CALC.ASM` (Worker Module)

```asm
;=====================================================
; CALC.ASM - Worker module
; Imports NUM, computes square, provides display
;=====================================================

DATA    SEGMENT PUBLIC          ; Must match MAIN's segment name
RESULT  DW  ?                   ; Defined here OR in MAIN (consistent)
DATA    ENDS

CODE    SEGMENT PUBLIC
        ASSUME  CS:CODE

        EXTRN   NUM:WORD       ; <-- KEY: import NUM from MAIN
        PUBLIC  SQUARE         ; export the procedure
        PUBLIC  DISPLAY_HEX

SQUARE  PROC FAR
        PUSH  BP
        MOV   BP, SP
        PUSH  BX
        ; Multiply NUM by itself using AX
        MOV   AX, NUM          ; AX = 0007H
        MOV   BX, AX
        MUL   BX               ; DX:AX = AX * BX = 0007H * 0007H = 0031H
        MOV   RESULT, AX       ; Store low word (DX=0 here)
        POP   BX
        POP   BP
        RET                    ; FAR RET pops CS:IP
SQUARE  ENDP

DISPLAY_HEX PROC FAR
        ; ... (hex-to-ASCII routine) ...
        RET
DISPLAY_HEX ENDP

CODE    ENDS
        END                    ; No entry label => linker fills 0:0
```

**Analytical Reasoning of the `SQUARE` Procedure:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AX}_{\text{initial}} &= \text{NUM} = 0007_{16} = 7_{10} \\
\text{BX} &= \text{AX} = 7_{10} \\
\text{MUL BX} &\Rightarrow \text{DX:AX} = \text{AX} \times \text{BX} \\
\text{DX:AX} &= 7 \times 7 = 49_{10} = 0031_{16} \\
\therefore \text{RESULT} &= 0031_{16}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 The Link Step (Exhaustive Trace)

The command to link the two object files is:

```
LINK MAIN.OBJ + CALC.OBJ, PROG.EXE, , 
```

(The `+` concatenates input OBJ files; the comma without a filename skips the `.MAP` file.)

**Linker Resolution Table (Mental Model for Exam):**

| Symbol | Declared In | Type | Resolved Address (Example) |
|---|---|---|---|
| `NUM` | `MAIN.ASM` (PUBLIC) | WORD | `DS:0000` |
| `RESULT` | `MAIN.ASM` / `CALC.ASM` (must agree) | WORD | `DS:0002` |
| `SQUARE` | `CALC.ASM` (PUBLIC) | FAR PROC | `CS:0040` |
| `DISPLAY_HEX` | `CALC.ASM` (PUBLIC) | FAR PROC | `CS:0080` |
| `START` | `MAIN.ASM` (`END START`) | ENTRY | `CS:0000` |

### 3.3 Critical Pitfall Matrix (Where Students Lose Marks)

| Pitfall | Symptom | Correct Practice |
|---|---|---|
| Forgetting `PUBLIC` in defining module | Linker error `L2025: Unresolved External` | Add `PUBLIC name` next to the definition. |
| Forgetting `EXTRN` in calling module | Assembler error `A2006: Phase error` or undefined symbol | Add `EXTRN name:type` at top. |
| Mismatched type in `EXTRN` | Linker warning `L4021: type mismatch` | Ensure `EXTRN NUM:WORD` matches `DW`. |
| Using `NEAR` call for cross-module | Stack corruption / crash on `RET` | Always use `FAR PTR` and `FAR PROC` cross-module. |
| Forgetting `STACK` combine-type | Stack overflows into random memory | Use `STACK` combine-type on shared stack segment. |
| Two `END` labels with entry points | Linker picks the wrong one; undefined behaviour | Only the main module gets `END <label>`; workers get plain `END`. |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Modular Program Flow (Mermaid Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    sourceA["MAIN.ASM"]:::source
    sourceB["CALC.ASM"]:::source
    sourceC["IO.ASM"]:::source

    asmA["MASM.EXE"]:::tool
    asmB["MASM.EXE"]:::tool
    asmC["MASM.EXE"]:::tool

    objA["MAIN.OBJ"]:::obj
    objB["CALC.OBJ"]:::obj
    objC["IO.OBJ"]:::obj

    linker["LINK.EXE"]:::link

    exe["PROG.EXE"]:::exe
    lib["CS.LIB"]:::lib

    sourceA --> asmA --> objA --> linker
    sourceB --> asmB --> objB --> linker
    sourceC --> asmC --> objC --> linker
    lib --> linker
    linker --> exe

    classDef source fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,color:#000
    classDef tool fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825,color:#000
    classDef obj fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#ad1457,color:#000
    classDef link fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000
    classDef exe fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000
    classDef lib fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#4527a0,color:#fff
```

**Reading the diagram:** Three independent source files are fed into three parallel assembly steps (the great speed benefit of modularity — parallel development). Their object files, plus an optional library, are merged by the linker into a single executable.

## 4.2 Inter-Module Communication & Visibility Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph M1["MODULE 1: MAIN.ASM"]
        M1PUB["PUBLIC: NUM, START"]:::export
        M1EXT["EXTRN: SQUARE, DISPLAY"]:::import
    end

    subgraph M2["MODULE 2: CALC.ASM"]
        M2PUB["PUBLIC: SQUARE"]:::export
        M2EXT["EXTRN: NUM"]:::import
    end

    subgraph M3["MODULE 3: IO.ASM"]
        M3PUB["PUBLIC: DISPLAY"]:::export
        M3EXT["EXTRN: ---"]:::import
    end

    M1PUB -. "links to" .-> M2EXT
    M1EXT -. "links to" .-> M2PUB
    M1EXT -. "links to" .-> M3PUB

    classDef export fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    classDef import fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000
```

**Key insight:** A `PUBLIC` in one module is **only meaningful** if another module declares the matching `EXTRN`. The linker is the *“matchmaker.”* If even one side is missing, the build fails.

## 4.3 Runtime Memory Map After Linking

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LOW["LOW ADDRESSES"]
        A1["IVT Area 00000-003FF"]:::sys
        A2["PSP + Code 01000-01FFF"]:::code
    end

    subgraph MID["MIDDLE MEMORY"]
        B1["Data Segment 02000-02FFF"]:::data
        B2["Stack Segment 03000-03FFF"]:::stack
    end

    subgraph HIGH["HIGH ADDRESSES"]
        C1["Extra Segment 04000-04FFF"]:::extra
        C2["DOS Kernel > A0000"]:::sys
    end

    A1 ~~~ A2 ~~~ B1 ~~~ B2 ~~~ C1 ~~~ C2

    classDef sys fill:#eceff1,stroke:#37474f,color:#000
    classDef code fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000
    classDef data fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    classDef stack fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    classDef extra fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:** Modular linking produces a single executable, but at runtime the segments are still mapped independently. The stack segment is the *“shared memory”* through which `CALL`/`RET` passes control.

## 4.4 Cross-Module `CALL`/`RET` Stack Trace (Mermaid Sequence)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant MAIN as MAIN.ASM (CS:0000)
    participant STK as Stack Segment
    participant CALC as CALC.ASM (CS:0040)

    MAIN->>STK: PUSH CS_main (caller's CS)
    MAIN->>STK: PUSH IP_next (return address)
    MAIN->>CALC: JMP to CS:0040
    CALC->>STK: PUSH BP (frame setup)
    Note over CALC: Executes MUL BX ...
    CALC->>STK: POP BP
    CALC->>STK: RETF pops IP_next, CS_main
    CALC->>MAIN: Execution resumes
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define modular programming. List any two advantages of modular programming in 8086 assembly language.**

**Model Answer (Examiner-Ready, 3 marks):**

> **Modular programming** is a software design technique in which a large program is divided into multiple smaller, separately assembled, and independently testable sub-programs called **modules**. Each module is assembled into a relocatable `.OBJ` file and finally linked using a linker (e.g., `LINK.EXE`) to form a single executable.
>
> **Advantages (any two, 1½ marks each):**
> 1. **Easier Debugging & Maintenance:** A bug can be isolated to a specific module, fixed, and re-assembled without disturbing other modules.
> 2. **Code Reusability:** A well-designed module (e.g., a hex-display routine) can be `PUBLIC`-published and reused across multiple projects via a library (`.LIB`).
> 3. **Parallel Development:** Multiple programmers can work on different modules simultaneously, reducing total development time.
> 4. **Logical Clarity:** Each module has a single, well-defined purpose — improving readability and reducing program complexity.

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Explain the purpose of the `PUBLIC` and `EXTRN` directives in 8086 modular programming.**

**Model Answer (Examiner-Ready, 3 marks):**

> - **`PUBLIC` Directive (1½ marks):** The `PUBLIC` directive, used in the *defining* module, declares that the named symbol (variable, label, or procedure) is **accessible to other modules** at link time. Syntax: `PUBLIC SYMBOL`.
>   *Example:* `PUBLIC NUM` in `MAIN.ASM` makes `NUM` available to `CALC.ASM`.
>
> - **`EXTRN` Directive (1½ marks):** The `EXTRN` directive, used in the *calling* module, declares that the named symbol is **defined in another module** and will be resolved by the linker. Syntax: `EXTRN SYMBOL : type`.
>   *Example:* `EXTRN NUM:WORD` in `CALC.ASM` informs the assembler that `NUM` is a word variable defined elsewhere.
>
> **Linker Behaviour:** During linking, the linker matches every `EXTRN` declaration with a corresponding `PUBLIC` declaration. If a match is not found, a *“L2025: Unresolved External”* error is generated and the executable is not produced.

---

## 5.2 PART B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and offers an internal choice. Below are **Question A** and **Question B** as two independent alternatives.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the different steps involved in developing and executing a modular 8086 assembly program, with a neat block diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a modular 8086 ALP to read a single-digit decimal number from the keyboard, find its factorial using a separate module, and display the result. Show clearly the `PUBLIC` and `EXTRN` declarations. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Steps in Modular Program Development (7 marks):**

1. **Problem Decomposition:** Divide the problem into logical sub-tasks. Each sub-task becomes one module (`.ASM` file).
2. **Source File Authoring:** Write each module as a separate `.ASM` file using a text editor.
3. **Independent Assembly:** Invoke `MASM.EXE` on each file:
   ```
   MASM MAIN.ASM;
   MASC FACT.ASM;
   ```
   This produces `MAIN.OBJ` and `FACT.OBJ` (relocatable object files).
4. **Linking:** Use `LINK.EXE` to combine all object files (and any libraries) into a single `.EXE`:
   ```
   LINK MAIN.OBJ + FACT.OBJ, PROG.EXE;
   ```
5. **Loading:** The DOS loader reads the `.EXE` header, allocates memory, sets up PSP, and initialises segment registers (`CS = DS = ES = SS` initially, with `SS:SP` adjusted).
6. **Execution:** CPU fetches the first instruction from `CS:IP` (where `IP` = offset of `START`). The program runs until termination.
7. **Debugging (Optional):** Use `DEBUG.EXE` to set breakpoints, step through, and inspect registers/memory.

**Block Diagram (½ mark):**
*Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes — the same 3-source → 3-assembly → 3-OBJ → LINK → 1-EXE pipeline.*

**(b) Modular Factorial Program (7 marks):**

`MAIN.ASM`:
```asm
DATA    SEGMENT PUBLIC
NUM     DB  ?
RES     DW  ?
MSG1    DB  'Enter number: $'
MSG2    DB  0DH,0AH,'Factorial = $'
DATA    ENDS

STACK_S SEGMENT STACK
        DB  100 DUP(?)
STACK_S ENDS

CODE    SEGMENT PUBLIC
        ASSUME CS:CODE, DS:DATA, SS:STACK_S
        PUBLIC  NUM
        EXTRN   FACTORIAL: FAR

START:  MOV  AX, @DATA
        MOV  DS, AX

        ; Display prompt
        MOV  AH, 09H
        LEA  DX, MSG1
        INT  21H

        ; Read single digit
        MOV  AH, 01H
        INT  21H
        AND  AL, 0FH            ; ASCII → binary
        MOV  NUM, AL

        ; Call FACTORIAL
        CALL FAR PTR FACTORIAL

        ; Display result
        MOV  AH, 09H
        LEA  DX, MSG2
        INT  21H
        ; (Assume helper to print RES in decimal)
        MOV  AX, RES
        CALL FAR PTR PRINT_DEC

        MOV  AH, 4CH
        INT  21H
CODE    ENDS
        END  START
```

`FACT.ASM`:
```asm
CODE    SEGMENT PUBLIC
        ASSUME CS:CODE
        EXTRN   NUM:BYTE
        EXTRN   RES:WORD
        PUBLIC  FACTORIAL
        PUBLIC  PRINT_DEC

FACTORIAL PROC FAR
        PUSH  BP
        MOV   BP, SP
        MOV   AL, NUM           ; AL = N
        MOV   BL, AL            ; BX = N (counter)
        MOV   AX, 0001H         ; AX = 1 (accumulator)
        CMP   BL, 0
        JE    DONE
LOOP1:  MUL   BL                ; AX = AX * BX
        DEC   BL
        JNZ   LOOP1
DONE:   MOV   RES, AX
        POP   BP
        RET
FACTORIAL ENDP

PRINT_DEC PROC FAR
        ; (decimal print routine using DIV 10)
        RET
PRINT_DEC ENDP

CODE    ENDS
        END
```

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** Discuss the role of segment combine types in modular 8086 programming. Explain `PUBLIC`, `COMMON`, and `STACK` combine types with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a modular 8086 program with two modules — one to sort an array of bytes in ascending order using Bubble Sort, and another (main module) to display the sorted array. Provide the complete source code with `PUBLIC`/`EXTRN` declarations and the link command. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Segment Combine Types (7 marks):**

Segment combine types govern how the **linker** merges segments of the same name across multiple modules. They are declared in the `SEGMENT` line.

1. **`PUBLIC` Combine-Type (2 marks):** When two modules declare a segment with the same name and `PUBLIC` combine-type, the linker **concatenates** them end-to-end in the order they are listed on the `LINK` command line. This produces one larger physical segment in memory, allowing the data of all modules to share one `DS` register.
   *Example:* Both `MAIN.ASM` and `FACT.ASM` declare `DATA SEGMENT PUBLIC` → the linker creates a single data segment whose size is the sum of both.

2. **`COMMON` Combine-Type (2 marks):** Segments with `COMMON` combine-type **overlap** in memory; they all start at the same address. This is useful for declaring uninitialised shared work-areas (like Fortran `COMMON` blocks).
   *Example:* If `MOD1` and `MOD2` each declare `BUF SEGMENT COMMON` with `DB 256 DUP(?)`, the resulting memory is **one 256-byte region** shared by both, with neither appending.

3. **`STACK` Combine-Type (2 marks):** The `STACK` combine-type behaves like `PUBLIC` (concatenation) but additionally instructs the loader to **automatically set `SS:SP`** to this combined segment at program load time, making it the program's stack.
   *Example:* A single shared `STACK_SEG SEGMENT STACK` declared in one module is sufficient — the loader sets `SS:SP` to its top.

**Mark Distribution (1 mark):** Proper definition of combine-type role and example for each.

**(b) Bubble Sort Modular Program (7 marks):**

`MAIN.ASM` (Driver):
```asm
DATA    SEGMENT PUBLIC
N       EQU  5
ARR     DB  05H, 02H, 08H, 01H, 04H
DATA    ENDS

STACK_S SEGMENT STACK
        DB  100 DUP(?)
STACK_S ENDS

CODE    SEGMENT PUBLIC
        ASSUME CS:CODE, DS:DATA
        PUBLIC  ARR
        EXTRN   BUBBLE_SORT: FAR
        EXTRN   DISPLAY: FAR

START:  MOV  AX, @DATA
        MOV  DS, AX
        CALL FAR PTR BUBBLE_SORT
        CALL FAR PTR DISPLAY
        MOV  AH, 4CH
        INT  21H
CODE    ENDS
        END  START
```

`SORT.ASM` (Worker):
```asm
CODE    SEGMENT PUBLIC
        ASSUME  CS:CODE
        EXTRN   ARR:BYTE
        EXTRN   N:ABS
        PUBLIC  BUBBLE_SORT
        PUBLIC  DISPLAY

BUBBLE_SORT PROC FAR
        PUSH  BP
        MOV   BP, SP
        PUSH  SI
        PUSH  CX
        PUSH  BX
        ; Bubble sort: SI = i, CX = N-1-i, BX = j
        MOV   CX, N
        DEC   CX                ; outer loop count = N-1
OUTER:  PUSH  CX
        MOV   SI, 0
        MOV   BX, 0
INNER:  MOV   AL, ARR[BX]
        CMP   AL, ARR[BX+1]
        JBE   NOSWAP
        XCHG  AL, ARR[BX+1]
        MOV   ARR[BX], AL
NOSWAP: INC   BX
        LOOP  INNER
        POP   CX
        LOOP  OUTER
        POP   BX
        POP   CX
        POP   SI
        POP   BP
        RET
BUBBLE_SORT ENDP

DISPLAY  PROC FAR
        ; (Display ARR using INT 21H / 02H)
        RET
DISPLAY  ENDP
CODE    ENDS
        END
```

**Link Command (½ mark):**
```
LINK MAIN.OBJ + SORT.OBJ, SORT.EXE, SORT.MAP;
```

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls That Cost 1–3 Marks:**
> 1. **Mismatched `EXTRN` types:** Declaring `EXTRN NUM:BYTE` when `NUM` is `DW` (WORD) — fails with L4021.
> 2. **Forgetting `FAR PTR` on inter-module calls:** Stack corruption on return — process crashes; examiners award 0 for output.
> 3. **Putting `END <label>` in worker modules:** Linker interprets this as multiple entry points — only main module should have it.
> 4. **Forgetting `STACK` combine-type:** DOS loader may set `SS` to wrong segment — runtime crash with no clear error.
> 5. **Confusing `GROUP` with `SEGMENT`:** `GROUP` is logical; `SEGMENT` is physical. `GROUP` does not allocate memory.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Read This Twice Before the Exam:**

- **Definition:** Modular programming = multiple `.ASM` files → multiple `.OBJ` → one `.EXE` via linker.
- **Three Mandatory Steps:** Write → Assemble (`MASM`) → Link (`LINK`).
- **Two Visibility Directives (must always be paired):** `PUBLIC` (defining module) + `EXTRN` (using module).
- **Cross-Module Calls are ALWAYS `FAR`:** Use `CALL FAR PTR name` and declare `name PROC FAR`.
- **Stack must be `STACK` combine-type** for automatic `SS:SP` initialisation by the loader.
- **Only the main module gets `END <start_label>`**; worker modules end with bare `END`.
- **Data sharing between modules** requires the same segment name and `PUBLIC` combine-type.
- **`EXTRN` syntax requires a type:** `BYTE`, `WORD`, `DWORD`, `NEAR`, `FAR`, or `ABS`.
- **Maximum single-segment size = 64 KB** (limited by 16-bit offset).
- **Modern parallel:** Same concept as `extern`/`static` in C — useful for embedded interview questions.
- **Library files (`.LIB`)** are archives of frequently-used `.OBJ` files linked on demand.
- **Linker error codes to memorise:** `L2025` (unresolved external), `L4021` (type mismatch), `L1001` (out of memory).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linking and Relocation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCIT404 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Linking and Relocation # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Linking** is the process of combining multiple independently assembled or compiled object modules (`.OBJ` files) along with required library routines into a single, unified, executable program (`.EXE` file) that can be loaded and run by the microprocessor. > > **Relocation** is the process of modifying the absolute memory addresses within the object code so that the program can be loaded and executed from any memory location in the microprocessor's address space, rather than being fixed at the original assembly-time address. Together, the **Linker** (also called a *Linkage Editor*) is a systems software utility that performs these two fundamental operations: it **resolves external references** between modules (linking) and it **adjusts address-dependent instructions and data** to reflect the actual load address (relocation). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Movable Office"** > > Imagine a newly hired employee who is told: "Your desk will be in **Office 101**." The employee writes down a private note: *"Pick up the printer from Office 101."* The note is **relative to the assumption that the office number is 101**. > > Now, imagine the company shifts the employee to **Office 407** on the very next day. The note still says "Office 101" — but the office has moved! > > - **Linking** is like making sure all the employee's notes, the HR files, and the manager's references all "fit together" into one consistent company directory. > - **Relocation** is the act of rewriting that note from "Office 101" to "Office 407," so it still works after the move. > > In a microprocessor, **programs are written assuming they start at a default address (typically 0000H)**. When the program is finally loaded into RAM at, say, address **2000H**, every address-dependent instruction must be "shifted" by the loader/linker. This shifting is relocation. ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Default Assembly Origin** for 8086 tiny/com `.EXE` programs: typically **0000H** (for `.COM`) or **0000H** with segment registers set to **CS=0000H, IP=0000H** for `.EXE` models. - **Standard Relocation Factor** (a.k.a. Loading Offset / Bias): $R = \text{Actual Load Address} - \text{Assumed Origin Address}$. - **Header size** in a typical 8086 `.EXE` file: **512 bytes** (one paragraph), containing the relocation table. - **Word size of address field to be fixed**: **16 bits** (in 16-bit 8086 real mode). > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping:** This topic directly maps to **Module 1 — Introduction to Microprocessors and Microcontrollers**, specifically the sub-topic on "Assemblers, Linkers, Loaders, and Relocatable Code." It builds toward **CO1**: *Understand the architecture and programming of 8-bit and 16-bit microprocessors.* --- ## 1.4 Why Linking and Relocation Are Required (Motivation) A large assembly program is rarely written as one monolithic source file. The following practical realities drive the need for linking: 1. **Modular Programming:** A typical 8086 project may contain 10–20 separate `.ASM` files (e.g., `MAIN.ASM`, `IO.ASM`, `MATH.ASM`, `DELAY.ASM`). 2. **Reusability:** Common subroutines (e.g., `PRINT_STRING`, `HEX2ASCII`) are written once and stored in a **library file** (`.LIB`). 3. **External Symbol References:** A procedure in `MAIN.ASM` may `CALL DELAY_1S` defined in `DELAY.ASM`. The assembler in `MAIN.ASM` cannot know the actual address of `DELAY_1S` at its own assembly time. 4. **Memory Flexibility:** The same `.EXE` file should run whether DOS loads it at `0700H` or at `9000H`. Therefore, the code must be **position-independent / relocatable**. | Stage | Input | Tool | Output | |---|---|---|---| | 1. Assembly | `MAIN.ASM` | Assembler | `MAIN.OBJ` | | 2. Assembly | `IO.ASM` | Assembler | `IO.OBJ` | | 3. Library | routines | Librarian | `MYLIB.LIB` | | 4. **Linking** | `MAIN.OBJ` + `IO.OBJ` + `MYLIB.LIB` | **Linker** | **`PROGRAM.EXE`** | | 5. Loading | `PROGRAM.EXE` | **Loader (DOS/BIOS)** | In-memory process | --- ## 1.5 The Three Address Types Used by the Linker > [!IMPORTANT] > **Three Address Classes in Linking/Relocation Theory:** | Address Type | When It Is Resolved | Is It Fixed? | |---|---|---| | **Absolute Address** (e.g., `5000H`) | At assembly time itself | No — anti-pattern for relocatable code | | **Relocatable Address** (e.g., offset within a segment) | At **link/load** time | Yes — preferred output | | **Relative Address** (e.g., `JMP +12` bytes ahead) | Runtime (by CPU) | Yes — fully position-independent | The relocatable and relative forms together enable **position-independent code (PIC)**, a cornerstone of modern operating systems as well as 8086 `.COM` programs. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown ### Step 1 — Object Module Generation by the Assembler When the assembler translates a single source file into a `.OBJ` file, it does **not** know the final location of the program. Therefore, it generates a structured object file containing: - **T (Text) records:** Machine instructions + their relative offsets. - **D (Define) records:** Symbols defined in this module (e.g., `START:`, `DELAY:`) and their offsets. - **R (Reference) records:** External symbols referenced but not defined here (e.g., `CALL HEX2ASCII`). - **M (Modification) records:** **Address-dependent words** that the *linker* must fix (e.g., a `CALL FAR_PTR` operand). - **H (Header) record:** Module name, length, type, etc. > [!NOTE] > Each **M-record** flags a specific offset in the code where the **loader/linker must add the relocation constant** to the existing 16-bit word. Without the M-record, the linker would have no way of knowing *which* bytes are addresses versus *which* are data (e.g., the ASCII code for `'A'` = 41H). ### Step 2 — The Linking Phase (Linkage Editor) The linker performs these operations in sequence: 1. **Symbol Resolution:** Build a global symbol table by reading all `D-records`. For each `R-record` of module A, search all other modules' `D-records` for the matching name. If found, store the *segment:offset* pair. 2. **Address Assignment (Locate Phase):** Calculate a starting segment for each module based on a chosen origin (default `0000H` unless `/BASE` is given). Concatenate them logically. 3. **Cross-Reference Stitching:** Replace all `R-records` with the resolved symbol's offset. 4. **Relocation (Modify Phase):** Walk through every `M-record` and add the **relocation constant** to the address word. ### Step 3 — Relocation Phase Details > [!IMPORTANT] > **Relocation Constant (RC) Definition:** $$RC = \text{Actual Load Segment} - \text{Assumed Origin Segment (0000H)} = \text{Actual Load Segment}$$ The address word at the location specified by the M-record is updated as: $$A_{\text{new}} = A_{\text{old}} + RC$$ ### Step 4 — Loading (Performed by DOS Loader / `INT 21H` AH=4BH) The DOS loader reads the `.EXE` header to obtain the **relocation table** and applies the relocation constant one final time using the actual segment address where DOS decided to load the program. --- ## 2.2 Two Types of Linking | Linking Type | How It Works | Speed | Disk Usage | |---|---|---|---| | **Static Linking** | All library code is copied into the `.EXE` at link time | Fast execution | Larger `.EXE` | | **Dynamic Linking** | Only references to libraries are kept; libraries loaded at run time | Slower startup | Smaller `.EXE` | > [!NOTE] > 8086-era DOS systems use **static linking only**. Dynamic linking is rare in 16-bit DOS but is the dominant model in 32/64-bit Windows (`.DLL` files) and Linux (`.so` files). --- ## 2.3 The Relocation Table Inside an `.EXE` File A real-mode `.EXE` file has a **header** with the following layout (relevant to linking/relocation): | Offset in Header | Field | Purpose | |---|---|---| | 02H–03H | Number of pages | File length / 512 | | 06H–07H | Relocation count | Number of entries in the relocation table | | 08H–09H | Header size (in paragraphs) | 32 for default (512 bytes / 16) | | 0AH–0BH | Min memory above program | For BSS / stack | | 18H–19H | Relocation table offset | Where the relocation entries begin | | 1AH–1BH | First relocation entry | Segment portion of first fixup | | 1CH–1DH | First relocation entry | Offset portion of first fixup | > [!IMPORTANT] > Each **relocation table entry** is a 16-bit segment value followed by a 16-bit offset value. At load time, the loader walks this table and adds its own load-segment value to the 16-bit word at that *segment:offset* location in the loaded image. --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Relocation Constant (RC) | $RC = S_{\text{load}} - S_{\text{origin}}$ | In **paragraphs** (16-byte units for 8086) | | 2 | Final effective address | $A_{\text{final}} = A_{\text{relocatable}} + RC$ | Linear 20-bit physical address | | 3 | Physical 8086 address | $PA = (\text{Segment} \times 16) + \text{Offset} = (\text{Segment} \ll 4) + \text{Offset}$ | 20-bit value (00000H–FFFFFH) | | 4 | Offset within segment | $\text{Offset} = A_{\text{final}} \mod 16$ | 0–15 range | | 5 | Segment value | $\text{Segment} = \lfloor A_{\text{final}} / 16 \rfloor$ | 0–FFFFH range | | 6 | Cross-module offset for a CALLED symbol | $O_{\text{call}} = O_{\text{base}} + (O_{\text{sym}} - O_{\text{module start}})$ | Stitches references | | 7 | `.EXE` total size | $\text{Size} = (\text{Pages} - 1) \times 512 + \text{LastPageBytes}$ | In bytes | | 8 | Number of relocation entries | Read from header bytes 06H–07H | Each entry = 4 bytes | > [!NOTE] > **Boundary Condition for `RC`:** The 16-bit address word being updated has a maximum value of **FFFFH** (65535). If the new value exceeds **FFFFH** (i.e., causes a carry), this is a **segment wraparound**. In such cases, the segment register associated with the operand must also be incremented. KTU expects students to **mention this edge case**. --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Operating Systems:** Every modern OS (Windows, Linux, macOS) uses linking + relocation. The concept of **ASLR (Address Space Layout Randomization)** is a direct descendant of relocation — it loads the executable at a *random* address each run for security. - **Embedded Systems:** In microcontrollers like 8051, code is often written assuming a fixed ROM address. However, for bootloader-overwritten firmware, a small **relocator stub** is included that copies the program block to RAM and patches jump tables. - **Firmware Updates (Bootloaders):** When a new firmware image is received, the bootloader must relocate it from the staging flash area to its execution area — the same concept as 8086 relocation. - **Real-Time Operating Systems (RTOS):** Tasks in an RTOS are often relocatable modules that the kernel stitches together at task-creation time. --- ## 2.6 The Two Failure Modes Students Must Know > [!WARNING] > 1. **Absolute Address Trap:** Using `ORG 5000H` in multiple modules causes them to **overlap in memory**. The linker cannot fix this — the program crashes. > 2. **Missing M-Record:** If the assembler fails to emit a modification record for a `CALL` or `MOV [BX+SI], 1234H` (immediate data mistaken for address), the linker will *not* relocate that word, and the program will execute with stale addresses at run time. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Detailed Worked Example — Linking Two Object Modules ### Problem Setup Two source files are assembled separately for an **8086** system. The default origin is `0000H` in each module. **File 1: `MAIN.ASM`** ```asm ASSUME CS:CODE, DS:DATA CODE SEGMENT PUBLIC EXTRN SUB_FAR:FAR PUBLIC START START: MOV AX, 1234H CALL SUB_FAR HLT CODE ENDS END START ``` **File 2: `SUB.ASM`** ```asm CODE SEGMENT PUBLIC PUBLIC SUB_FAR SUB_FAR PROC FAR MOV BX, 5678H RET SUB_FAR ENDP END ``` ### Step 1 — Identify All Symbols For each module, list what it **defines** (exports) and what it **references** (imports). | Module | Exports (D-records) | Imports (R-records) | |---|---|---| | `MAIN.OBJ` | `START` @ offset 0000H (in its code seg) | `SUB_FAR` (FAR) | | `SUB.OBJ` | `SUB_FAR` @ offset 0000H (in its code seg) | *(none)* | ### Step 2 — Linker Allocates Segment Bases Assume the linker decides to place: - `MAIN.OBJ` at **segment 2000H** (origin). - `SUB.OBJ` at **segment 2010H** (immediately after). ### Step 3 — Build the Global Symbol Table | Symbol | Segment | Offset | |---|---|---| | `START` | 2000H | 0000H | | `SUB_FAR` | 2010H | 0000H | ### Step 4 — Relocation Constant Calculation (per module) $$RC_{\text{MAIN}} = 2000H - 0000H = 2000H$$ $$RC_{\text{SUB}} = 2010H - 0000H = 2010H$$ ### Step 5 — Update the `CALL SUB_FAR` Instruction The original `CALL FAR PTR SUB_FAR` in `MAIN.OBJ` is encoded as machine code: ``` 9A 0000 0000 ``` Where `9A` is the opcode for `CALL FAR` and the four bytes `00 00 00 00` are placeholder **offset** (0000H) and **segment** (0000H). **Original (pre-link):** offset = 0000H, segment = 0000H (all relative to 0000H origin). **After linking (RC added to segment, offset gets symbol's resolved offset):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Offset}_{\text{new}} &= \text{SymbolOffset} + RC_{\text{MAIN}} \quad (\text{no, this is wrong — see correction}) \\ \text{Offset}_{\text{new}} &= \text{SymbolOffset} = 0000H \\ \text{Segment}_{\text{new}} &= \text{LinkerSegment}_{\text{SUB}} = 2010H \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Correction / Clarification:** In a `CALL FAR`, the **segment** field holds the *segment of the target module*, not the symbol's segment relative to anything else. The linker's symbol table tells us `SUB_FAR` is at `2010H:0000H`. So the linker writes the segment as **2010H** and the offset as **0000H**. **Final machine code at the CALL site:** `9A 00 00 10 20`. ### Step 6 — DOS Loader Applies Its Own Relocation When DOS loads the `.EXE` at, say, load segment `0500H` (i.e., 16 bytes after `5000H`): $$ RC_{\text{DOS}} = 0500H - 0000H = 0500H $$ The loader walks the relocation table. For our `CALL FAR` site (stored in the table as a fixup at `2000H:0003H` relative to origin): $$ A_{\text{old}} = 2010H, \quad A_{\text{new}} = A_{\text{old}} + RC_{\text{DOS}} $$ $$ A_{\text{new}} = 2010H + 0500H = 2510H $$ So the segment field of the `CALL FAR` is updated from `2010H` to `2510H`. ### Step 7 — Verify the Physical Address at Run Time The CPU computes the physical address of the target as: $$ PA = (2510H \ll 4) + 0000H $$ $$ PA = 25100H + 0000H = 25100H $$ This must equal: $$ (\text{ActualLoadSegment} \ll 4) + \text{TargetOffset} = (2500H \ll 4) + 0100H = 25100H \quad \checkmark $$ The relocation is **correct**. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Relocation Scenarios for 8086 | Instruction Type | Address Field Width | M-Record Required? | Example (Opcode Mnemonic) | |---|---|---|---| | `CALL NEAR` (intra-segment) | 16-bit relative | **No** (IP-relative, fixed by CPU) | `E8 disp16` | | `CALL FAR` (inter-segment) | 16+16 seg:off | **Yes** | `9A off16 seg16` | | `JMP NEAR` (short or near) | 8- or 16-bit rel | **No** | `EB rel8` / `E9 rel16` | | `JMP FAR` | 16+16 seg:off | **Yes** | `EA off16 seg16` | | `MOV AX, [addr]` (direct memory) | 16-bit offset | **Yes** (offset only) | `A1 lo hi` | | `MOV [BX+SI+disp], imm16` | 16-bit displacement | **Yes** | `89 88 disp16` | | `INT n` / `IN` / `OUT` | 8-bit immediate | **No** (operand is data) | `CD 21` | > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Treating an **immediate data** word (e.g., `MOV AX, 1234H` → `B8 34 12`) as an address. The 1234H here is the *number 1234*, not a memory address. The assembler does **not** emit an M-record for it, and the linker does **not** add the relocation constant to it. --- ## 3.3 Boundary-Case Derivation — Segment Wraparound Suppose the link-time segment of a target is `FFF0H` and the DOS load segment is `0020H` (i.e., 16-byte wraparound test). $$ \begin{aligned} A_{\text{old}} &= FFF0H \\ RC_{\text{DOS}} &= 0020H \\ A_{\text{new}} &= A_{\text{old}} + RC_{\text{DOS}} \\ A_{\text{new}} &= FFF0H + 0020H = 10010H \end{aligned} $$ Since 16-bit unsigned arithmetic can hold only `0000H–FFFFH`, the **lower 16 bits** are written: `0010H`, and a **carry of 1** propagates. The loader must detect this carry and **increment the segment register** associated with the operand by 1. In the 8086, this is handled automatically only for the **CS** register on `CALL FAR`/`JMP FAR`; for data references, the programmer must use a far pointer or split the access. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Note:** Most exam problems avoid this wraparound, but you should mention it for full marks on 14-mark questions about edge cases. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 End-to-End Block Diagram — From Source to Running Process ```mermaid flowchart TD A[MAIN.ASM
source code] --> B[Assembler MASM TASM] C[IO.ASM
source code] --> D[Assembler MASM TASM] E[MATH.LIB
library file] --> F[Linker LINK EXE] B --> G[MAIN.OBJ
object module with M records] D --> H[IO.OBJ
object module with M records] G --> F H --> F F --> I[PROGRAM.EXE
linked executable with relocation table] I --> J[DOS Loader
INT 21H AH 4Bh] J --> K[Physical Memory
