segment shifted by load bias] K --> L[CPU Fetches and Executes] style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366 style C fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366 style E fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600 style B fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#336600 style D fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#336600 style F fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#cc6600 style I fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#990000 style J fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#660066 style K fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#003399 style L fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#336600 ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Linker ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Object Files
OBJ] --> B[Module Reader] L[Library File
LIB] --> B B --> C[D Record Parser
Global Symbol Table] B --> D[R Record Parser
External Reference List] B --> E[M Record Parser
Fixup List] C --> F[Locate Phase
assign segment bases] D --> G[Symbol Resolver
stitch cross references] E --> H[Modify Phase
add RC to flagged words] F --> I[Build Output EXE Header] G --> I H --> I I --> J[Write Program EXE] J --> K[Relocation Table
written to header] style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366 style L fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600 style B fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#336600 style C fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#336600 style D fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#336600 style E fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#336600 style F fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#cc6600 style G fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#cc6600 style H fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#cc6600 style I fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#990000 style J fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#660066 style K fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#003399 ``` ## 4.3 Decision Flowchart — Does a Word Need Relocation? ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Word in object code] --> B{Is operand a
memory address?} B -- No --> C[Do not relocate
it is data or code offset] B -- Yes --> D{Is reference
intra segment?} D -- Yes and IP relative --> E[CPU relocates at runtime
no M record needed] D -- Yes and absolute --> F[M record needed
relocate offset only] D -- No --> G[M record needed
relocate segment and offset] style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366 style B fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600 style C fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#336600 style D fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600 style E fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#336600 style F fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#990000 style G fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#990000 ``` ## 4.4 Memory Layout Before vs After Relocation ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Before["BEFORE Linking at Origin 0000H"] B1[Module A: 0000H to 00FFH] B2[Module B: 0100H to 01FFH] B3[Library Call stub
CALL FAR: seg=0000H off=0100H] end subgraph After["AFTER Linking with RC 2000H then DOS load 0500H"] C1[Module A: 2500H to 25FFH] C2[Module B: 2600H to 26FFH] C3[Library Call stub
CALL FAR: seg=2610H off=0000H
plus loader RC = 2B10H] end Before --> After style B1 fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#990000 style B2 fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#990000 style B3 fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#990000 style C1 fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#336600 style C2 fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#336600 style C3 fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#336600 ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]` **Define linking. What is the difference between a linker and a loader?** **Model Answer:** > **Linking** is the process of combining one or more object modules (produced by an assembler or compiler) with required library routines into a single executable program. The **linker** (or linkage editor) performs this by resolving external symbol references between modules and generating the final executable file (`.EXE`). > > The **loader**, in contrast, is invoked at *run time* by the operating system. It reads the executable from disk, allocates physical memory, applies any final relocation based on the chosen load address, and transfers control to the program's entry point. > > **Key distinction:** The linker operates on disk-resident object files *before* the program runs; the loader operates at the moment the program is being launched, copying and adjusting it in RAM. ### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]` **What is relocation? Why is relocatable code essential for an operating system?** **Model Answer:** > **Relocation** is the modification of address fields within a program so that the program can be loaded and executed from *any* location in memory, not just the location assumed at assembly/compile time. The relocation process is guided by **modification records (M-records)** in object files and a **relocation table** in the `.EXE` header. > > Relocatable code is **essential for an operating system** for three reasons: > 1. **Multitasking:** Multiple programs must coexist in RAM, and the OS must place each one wherever free memory exists. > 2. **Security (ASLR):** Modern OSes load each program at a *random* address to defeat exploits that rely on fixed memory layouts. > 3. **Modularity:** Library code and dynamically linked modules can be shared across many programs without recompiling or re-addressing. > > Without relocation, every program would have to be re-assembled for a specific load address, which is infeasible in a general-purpose system. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Module carries a 14-mark question with **internal choice**. You must answer EITHER Option A OR Option B. --- ### **Question B — Option A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — July 2023]` **(a)** Explain in detail the **steps performed by a linker** to combine two object modules. Include a discussion of the relocation table. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Two object modules `M1` and `M2` are to be linked. `M1` is placed at segment base `3000H` and `M2` at `3040H`. `M1` contains a `CALL FAR` instruction to a procedure `P` defined in `M2` at offset `0010H`. If the program is loaded by DOS at segment `0500H`, calculate the final segment value in the `CALL FAR` instruction after relocation. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) Steps performed by the linker:** 1. **Input Reading:** The linker opens each `.OBJ` file in the order given on the command line and a `.LIB` file if specified. 2. **Symbol Table Construction:** The linker reads all `D-records` (Define) and `R-records` (Reference). For each `R-record`, it searches the global symbol table for a matching definition. If unresolved symbols remain after scanning all input, a *link error* is reported. 3. **Locate Phase:** The linker assigns a starting segment address to each module. By default this is `0000H`, but the user may specify `/BASE=xxxx`. Subsequent modules follow contiguously. 4. **Cross-Reference Resolution:** The linker rewrites each `R-record` location with the resolved address (segment and/or offset) of the target symbol. 5. **Modify (Relocation) Phase:** The linker iterates through all `M-records`. For each, it reads the current 16-bit word at the indicated offset, adds the relocation constant, and writes the result back. 6. **Relocation Table Generation:** The linker records every location whose final value depends on the load-time segment. Each entry is a 32-bit value (segment + offset) and is written to a designated region in the `.EXE` header. 7. **Output Writing:** The combined code, data, and the relocation table are written to the `.EXE` file. A header describes the total size, the entry-point IP, the initial segment registers, and the size of the relocation table. **(b) Numerical solution:** Given: - Module `M1` base segment at link time: $S_{M1} = 3000H$ - Module `M2` base segment at link time: $S_{M2} = 3040H$ - Procedure `P` defined in `M2` at offset: $O_P = 0010H$ - DOS load segment: $L = 0500H$ **Step 1 — Link-time segment of P (stored in EXE after linking):** $$ S_P^{\text{link}} = S_{M2} = 3040H $$ **Step 2 — DOS loader's relocation constant:** $$ RC = L - 0000H = 0500H $$ **Step 3 — Apply loader relocation to the segment field of `CALL FAR`:** $$ S_P^{\text{final}} = S_P^{\text{link}} + RC = 3040H + 0500H $$ $$ S_P^{\text{final}} = 3540H $$ **Step 4 — Offset field of the CALL FAR** is the symbol's offset within its own module, which is unaffected by relocation (it is intra-segment relative): $$ O_P^{\text{final}} = 0010H $$ **Step 5 — Verify the physical address the CPU computes at run time:** $$ PA_{\text{target}} = (S_P^{\text{final}} \ll 4) + O_P^{\text{final}} = (3540H \times 10H) + 0010H $$ $$ PA_{\text{target}} = 35400H + 0010H = 35410H $$ > **Final answer:** The segment value in the `CALL FAR` instruction after relocation is **`3540H`**, and the offset remains **`0010H`**. (7/7) --- ### **Question B — Option B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2022]` **(a)** Describe the **structure of an 8086 `.EXE` header** with a focus on the relocation table. What is the role of the loader in applying this table at run time? **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A program is assembled with all addresses relative to `0000H`. It contains a `MOV AX, [2000H]` instruction (direct memory reference) at offset `0100H` of the code segment. If the program is loaded at segment `4000H`, determine the new effective 20-bit physical address that the CPU computes to fetch the operand. Show the relocation arithmetic step by step. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) Structure of the 8086 `.EXE` header and the relocation table:** The 8086 `.EXE` file begins with a **512-byte header** containing the following key fields (offsets given in hex from the start of the file): | Offset | Field | Meaning | |---|---|---| | 00–01 | `MZ` signature | Identifies the file as an `.EXE` | | 02–03 | Number of 512-byte pages | File length in pages (last page may be partial) | | 04–05 | Number of reloc items | Count of entries in the relocation table (duplicated for historical reasons) | | 06–07 | Header size in paragraphs | 32 paragraphs = 512 bytes (for the basic header) | | 08–09 | MinAlloc | Minimum memory (in paragraphs) needed beyond the loaded image | | 0A–0B | MaxAlloc | Maximum memory to allocate | | 0C–0D | Initial SS | Stack segment value (relative to load segment) | | 0E–0F | Initial SP | Initial stack pointer | | 10–11 | Checksum | Negated sum of all 16-bit words in the file (often unused) | | 12–13 | Initial IP | Entry-point instruction pointer | | 14–15 | Initial CS | Entry-point code segment | | 16–17 | Relocation-table offset | Where the table begins, in bytes from file start | | 18–19 | Overlay number | 0 for main program | **The relocation table** begins at the offset given by the field at `16H–17H`. It is an **array of 32-bit entries**; each entry is a pair of 16-bit words: a **segment** and an **offset**, both relative to the start of the loaded image. Each entry flags a 16-bit word inside the program image that must be patched at load time. **Role of the loader:** When DOS loads the `.EXE`: 1. It reads the header, computes the total image size, and selects a free segment address $L$ in conventional memory. 2. It copies the program image to physical memory starting at $L:0000H$. 3. It walks the relocation table. For each entry $(s, o)$, it reads the 16-bit word at linear address $(s \ll 4) + o$ inside the loaded image, adds the loader's relocation constant $RC = L$ to it, and writes the sum back. 4. It sets `DS = ES = SS = L + InitialSS`, `SP = InitialSP`, `CS = L + InitialCS`, `IP = InitialIP`. 5. It transfers control to `CS:IP`, beginning execution. **(b) Numerical solution:** Given: - Assembled origin: $0000H$ - Instruction at offset $0100H$ of the code segment - Operand: `[2000H]` (direct memory reference, 16-bit offset) - Load segment: $L = 4000H$ **Step 1 — Identify the operand type:** `MOV AX, [2000H]` uses a 16-bit *offset* as a memory address (within the default data segment). This is an **address-dependent word**, so the assembler emits an **M-record**, and the linker writes the offset `2000H` into the instruction and adds the entry to the relocation table. **Step 2 — Compute the relocation constant:** $$ RC = L - 0000H = 4000H $$ **Step 3 — Apply the relocation to the offset word** in the instruction: $$ A_{\text{new}} = A_{\text{old}} + RC = 2000H + 4000H $$ $$ A_{\text{new}} = 6000H $$ **Step 4 — Compute the final 20-bit physical address** that the CPU will use to fetch the operand from memory at run time. The CPU calculates the address as: $$ PA = (\text{DS} \ll 4) + \text{offset} $$ The `DS` register is initialized by the loader to the **load segment** (or its data-segment value) — here it is $4000H$. The 16-bit offset after relocation is `6000H`: $$ PA = (4000H \ll 4) + 6000H $$ $$ PA = 40000H + 6000H = 46000H $$ > **Final answer:** The final effective 20-bit physical address is **`46000H`**. (7/7) --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where Students Typically Lose Marks in Linking/Relocation Questions** > > 1. **Confusing link-time and load-time relocation.** The linker applies a relocation constant *once at link time* (default 0). The loader applies *another* relocation at *load time*. In a numerical problem, you must specify which one you are computing. Many students apply both and double-add the constant — **deducted 2–3 marks**. > > 2. **Forgetting that `CALL NEAR` and `JMP NEAR` are already IP-relative.** The CPU handles them; **no M-record is needed**. Many students incorrectly claim the linker relocates them too. > > 3. **Mixing offset and segment.** A `CALL FAR` has *both* a 16-bit segment and a 16-bit offset field. The segment is relocatable; the offset is not (it is the symbol's offset within its own segment). Examiners specifically test this distinction — **deducted 1–2 marks**. > > 4. **Not mentioning the relocation table's location in the `.EXE` header.** A 7-mark question on the `.EXE` header structure almost always requires you to name the *offset* (in hex) of the relocation-table pointer field: `16H–17H`. Forgetting this loses a mark. > > 5. **Ignoring wraparound.** If a relocation pushes a 16-bit word past `FFFFH`, the carry must be handled. KTU occasionally offers a "tricky" 2-mark follow-up; missing it costs 2 marks. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Linking and Relocation** - **Linker** = combines `.OBJ` files + libraries → `.EXE`. - **Loader** = loads `.EXE` into memory at run time, applies relocation. - **Relocation Constant** $RC = S_{\text{load}} - S_{\text{origin}}$. - A **relocatable word** is updated as $A_{\text{new}} = A_{\text{old}} + RC$. - Object files contain **D, R, M records** (definitions, references, modification entries). - The **relocation table** in the `.EXE` header is an array of 32-bit (segment, offset) pairs; its starting offset is at header bytes `16H–17H`. - **Static linking** copies all library code into the `.EXE`; **dynamic linking** keeps references for run-time resolution. - **Near jumps/calls** are IP-relative and need **no relocation**. - **Far jumps/calls and direct memory references** use 16-bit offsets stored in the instruction; the loader updates them. - **Immediate data** (e.g., `MOV AX, 1234H`) is **not relocated** — it is a number, not an address. - **Physical address** for 8086: $PA = (\text{Segment} \times 16) + \text{Offset}$, a 20-bit result. - **Cross-module call resolution** = linker reads D-record of target, writes its segment:offset into the caller's instruction. - **Relocation is the foundation of ASLR**, modern OS multitasking, and position-independent firmware. - **The boundary edge case** is 16-bit overflow: $A_{\text{new}}$ exceeding `FFFFH` requires segment-register carry handling. - **Absolute assembly origin** (`ORG 5000H`) is **anti-pattern** for multi-module programs — it prevents relocation. - **Public/External directives** (`PUBLIC`, `EXTRN`) are the assembler-level primitives that make linking possible. - **Header structure** of a real-mode `.EXE` is exactly 512 bytes, starting with the `MZ` (0x4D, 0x5A) signature.

topics.title: Stacks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCIT404 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Stacks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **8086 microprocessor** and **8051 microcontroller**, a **Stack** is a dedicated region of memory that operates strictly on the **Last-In-First-Out (LIFO)** principle, used by the processor to store temporary data, return addresses, and register contents during subroutine calls, interrupts, and parameter passing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** A *Stack* is a sequential block of memory defined by a **Stack Segment (SS)** register and a **Stack Pointer (SP)** register. The SP always points to the **Top-of-Stack (TOS)**, i.e., the most recently pushed data item. All stack operations are byte/word-aligned and follow LIFO discipline.

The stack is managed by two critical architectural registers:
- **Stack Segment (SS)** – holds the base segment address (8086 only).
- **Stack Pointer (SP)** – holds the offset of the current top of the stack.
- **Base Pointer (BP)** – used to access data within the stack frame (8086 only).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The Pile of Plates

Imagine a stack of plates in a cafeteria:
1. You can only **add (push)** a new plate to the **top** of the pile.
2. You can only **remove (pop)** a plate from the **top** of the pile.
3. The plate that was placed **last** is the one that is removed **first**.

That is the **LIFO (Last-In, First-Out)** principle. The microprocessor's stack behaves identically:
- A `PUSH` operation places data on top of the stack.
- A `POP` operation removes the topmost data.
- The **Stack Pointer (SP)** is like your "hand position" — it always points to the topmost plate (data).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Unlike a queue (FIFO), the stack has **no random access**. You cannot directly read element 3 without first popping elements 1 and 2. This restricted access is precisely what makes the stack a fast, predictable structure for processor housekeeping.

## 1.3 Why Do Processors Need a Stack?

The stack is **mandatory** for the following processor functions:
- **Subroutine linkage:** Storing the return address during `CALL` and restoring it during `RET`.
- **Interrupt handling:** Saving program state (flags, CS, IP) on `INT` and restoring on `IRET`.
- **Register preservation:** Saving caller-saved registers via `PUSH`.
- **Parameter passing:** Conventionally passing arguments to procedures.
- **Local variables:** Allocating temporary storage inside a stack frame.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The stack is the **only mechanism** by which nested subroutines and interrupts can return correctly. Without it, control flow would be impossible to restore.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Stack Pointer (SP) — The Heart of the Stack

The **Stack Pointer (SP)** is a dedicated CPU register that always contains the **offset address** of the current top of the stack within the stack segment.

| Processor | SP Width | Reset Value | Location |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| **8086** | **16-bit** | **Undefined (FFFFH after reset)** | CPU register |
| **8051** | **8-bit** | **07H** | SFR at address **81H** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The 8086 SP is part of the EU (Execution Unit) and is paired with the SS register to form the physical address. The 8051 SP, in contrast, is an **8-bit SFR** that points directly into the **internal RAM (00H–7FH)**.

## 2.2 Fundamental Stack Operations

There are exactly **two primitive operations** on a stack:

### 2.2.1 PUSH Operation
The `PUSH` instruction **decrements SP** (or increments, depending on architecture) and then stores the data at the new top of stack.

- **8086 PUSH reg16:** Two-step process.
  1. $SP \leftarrow SP - 2$
  2. $\text{Memory}[SS:SP] \leftarrow \text{register data}$
- **8051 PUSH direct:** Two-step process.
  1. $SP \leftarrow SP + 1$
  2. $\text{InternalRAM}[SP] \leftarrow \text{direct byte}$

### 2.2.2 POP Operation
The `POP` instruction **reads data from the top of stack** and then updates SP.

- **8086 POP reg16:** Two-step process.
  1. $\text{register} \leftarrow \text{Memory}[SS:SP]$
  2. $SP \leftarrow SP + 2$
- **8051 POP direct:** Two-step process.
  1. $\text{direct byte} \leftarrow \text{InternalRAM}[SP]$
  2. $SP \leftarrow SP - 1$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Architectural Difference:** The 8086 stack grows **downward** (toward lower addresses), while the 8051 stack grows **upward** (toward higher addresses). This is a guaranteed KTU exam question.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | 8086 Formula | 8051 Formula |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Stack direction | **Descending** (grows ↓) | **Ascending** (grows ↑) |
| Top of stack physical address | $PA_{TOS} = SS \times 10H + SP$ | $PA_{TOS} = SP$ (direct) |
| SP update after PUSH | $SP_{new} = SP_{old} - 2$ | $SP_{new} = SP_{old} + 1$ |
| SP update after POP | $SP_{new} = SP_{old} + 2$ | $SP_{new} = SP_{old} - 1$ |
| PUSH stores data at | $SS:SP$ (after decrement) | Address in SP (after increment) |
| POP reads data from | $SS:SP$ (before increment) | Address in SP (before decrement) |
| Default SP after reset | Unpredictable; usually FFFEH | **07H** |
| Stack memory region | External (full 1 MB) | Internal RAM (08H onward) |
| Stack frame size | $\text{bytes} = 2 \times (\text{items pushed})$ | $\text{bytes} = 1 \times (\text{items pushed})$ |
| Stack boundary check | $SP \ge 0000H$ (no underflow) | $SP \le 7FH$ (for 128-byte RAM) |

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

In real-world embedded systems, the stack enables:
- **Recursive algorithms** in firmware (e.g., tree traversals in IoT devices).
- **Context switching** in RTOS (Real-Time Operating Systems) — each task has its own stack.
- **Interrupt-driven I/O** — the hardware automatically saves CPU state on stack.
- **Compiler-generated code** — high-level language compilers (C, C++) emit `PUSH`/`POP` instructions to manage local variables.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: 8086 PUSH Instruction — Full Micro-operation Trace

Consider the 8086 instruction:

```asm
PUSH BX
```

Assume the initial state: $SS = 2000H$, $SP = 1006H$, $BX = 1234H$.

**Step 1 — Decrement Stack Pointer by 2:**
The 8086 performs a word-sized (2-byte) PUSH, so SP must be decremented by 2.

$$SP_{new} = SP_{old} - 2 = 1006H - 2 = 1004H$$

**Step 2 — Compute the physical address of the new TOS:**
Using the standard segment:offset formula:

$$PA_{TOS} = SS \times 10H + SP_{new} = 2000H \times 10H + 1004H = 21004H$$

**Step 3 — Store low byte (BL) at the lower address:**

$$\text{Memory}[21004H] \leftarrow BL = 34H$$

**Step 4 — Store high byte (BH) at the higher address:**

$$\text{Memory}[21005H] \leftarrow BH = 12H$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Little-Endian Convention:** The 8086 (being little-endian) always stores the **low-order byte at the lower address** and the high-order byte at the higher address. This is a KTU-favorite conceptual trap.

**Final Result:**
- New $SP = 1004H$
- $B$ value at $21005H = 12H$
- $B$ value at $21004H = 34H$

## 3.2 Derivation: 8051 PUSH Instruction — Full Micro-operation Trace

Consider the 8051 instruction:

```asm
PUSH ACC
```

Assume the initial state: $SP = 09H$, $ACC = A5H$ (i.e., 1010 0101 in binary).

**Step 1 — Increment Stack Pointer by 1:**
The 8051 performs a byte-sized PUSH, so SP is incremented by 1.

$$SP_{new} = SP_{old} + 1 = 09H + 1 = 0AH$$

**Step 2 — Store the accumulator byte at the new SP location:**

$$\text{InternalRAM}[0AH] \leftarrow ACC = A5H$$

**Final Result:**
- New $SP = 0AH$
- Internal RAM at address $0AH$ now contains $A5H$

## 3.3 Derivation: Stack Frame during 8086 CALL

Consider the following assembly fragment:

```asm
MAIN PROC
    MOV AX, 1234H
    CALL SUB1
    ADD AX, BX
    ...
SUB1 PROC
    PUSH BP
    MOV BP, SP
    PUSH AX
    ...
    POP AX
    POP BP
    RET
```

When `CALL SUB1` executes, the 8086 does the following:

**Step 1 — Decrement SP by 2:**

$$SP \leftarrow SP - 2$$

**Step 2 — Store the IP (return address) at SS:SP:**

$$\text{Memory}[SS:SP] \leftarrow IP_{return}$$

**Step 3 — Load IP with the subroutine address:**

$$IP \leftarrow \text{offset of SUB1}$$

**Step 4 — Inside SUB1, `PUSH BP` again decrements SP by 2:**

$$SP \leftarrow SP - 2$$

$$\text{Memory}[SS:SP] \leftarrow BP_{old}$$

**Step 5 — `MOV BP, SP` anchors the base pointer to the current TOS:**

$$BP = SP$$

This creates the **stack frame**. Using `BP`, the subroutine can access parameters and local variables at fixed offsets from BP.

**Step 6 — On RET, the processor pops IP from the stack:**

$$IP \leftarrow \text{Memory}[SS:SP]$$
$$SP \leftarrow SP + 2$$

Control returns to MAIN exactly at the instruction after `CALL SUB1`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Golden Rule:** The number of PUSHes in a routine **must equal** the number of POPs. Any imbalance will corrupt the stack and crash the program.

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example: 8086 Stack Address Calculation

**Problem:** Given $SS = 4000H$ and $SP = 0FFE0H$, find the physical address of the top of the stack.

**Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
PA_{TOS} &= SS \times 10H + SP \\
&= 4000H \times 10H + 0FFE0H \\
&= 40000H + 0FFE0H \\
&= 40FFE0H
\end{aligned}$$

The top of the stack is at the **physical address 40FFE0H** in the 1 MB memory.

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example: 8051 Stack Initialization

**Problem:** The 8051 stack pointer is reset to 07H. The programmer wishes to move the stack to a safe area. Write the instruction to set the stack pointer to 30H and explain the effect.

**Solution:**

```asm
MOV SP, #30H
```

After this instruction:
- $SP_{old} = 07H$ is overwritten.
- $SP_{new} = 30H$.
- The stack will now grow upward from $30H$ toward $7FH$.
- Locations $08H$ to $2FH$ are freed for **general-purpose data** (register banks R0–R7 occupy 00H–1FH, bit-addressable 20H–2FH).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Practical Tip:** Always initialize the 8051 SP above 1FH to avoid clashing with the bit-addressable region and the register banks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: The Stack as a Processor Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CPU[8086 CPU] --> SS_REG[Stack Segment Register SS]
    CPU --> SP_REG[Stack Pointer Register SP]
    CPU --> BP_REG[Base Pointer Register BP]
    SS_REG --> ADDR[20-bit Physical Address Generator]
    SP_REG --> ADDR
    ADDR --> MEM[Stack Memory in 1 MB Space]
    MEM --> PUSH_OP[PUSH Operation Decrements SP]
    MEM --> POP_OP[POP Operation Increments SP]
    PUSH_OP --> LIFO[LIFO Discipline Maintained]
    POP_OP --> LIFO
```

## 4.2 Flowchart: 8086 PUSH Instruction Execution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Fetch PUSH reg16] --> DECR[SP equals SP minus 2]
    DECR --> CALC[Compute PA equals SS times 10H plus SP]
    CALC --> STORE_H[Store High Byte at PA plus 1]
    STORE_H --> STORE_L[Store Low Byte at PA]
    STORE_L --> DONE[Execution Complete]
```

## 4.3 Flowchart: 8051 PUSH Instruction Execution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Fetch PUSH direct] --> INCR[SP equals SP plus 1]
    INCR --> STORE[Store Byte at Internal RAM SP]
    STORE --> DONE[Execution Complete]
```

## 4.4 Memory Layout Diagram: 8086 Stack Growth (Descending)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph "Stack Segment (SS = 2000H)"
        ADDR_FFE[2000:0FFE - EMPTY]
        ADDR_FFC[2000:0FFC - EMPTY]
        ADDR_FFA[2000:0FFA - 34H Low Byte of BX]
        ADDR_FF9[2000:0FF9 - 12H High Byte of BX]
        ADDR_FF8[2000:0FF8 - PREVIOUS TOS]
    end
    ADDR_FFA --> SP_POINT[SP points here at 0FFAH]
    SP_POINT --> DIRECTION[Stack grew downward from 0FFCH to 0FFAH]
```

## 4.5 Memory Layout Diagram: 8051 Stack Growth (Ascending)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph "Internal RAM (00H to 7FH)"
        R0[00H to 07H - Register Bank 0]
        BIT[20H to 2FH - Bit Addressable]
        SP_INIT[07H - Initial SP after reset]
        RAM1[08H - First PUSH location]
        RAM2[09H - Second PUSH location]
        RAM3[0AH - Third PUSH location]
        SP_NOW[0AH - Current SP after 3 pushes]
    end
    RAM1 --> SP_NOW
    SP_NOW --> DIR[Stack grew upward from 08H to 0AH]
```

## 4.6 Block Diagram: 8086 CALL/RET Interaction with Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CALLER[Main Program] --> CALL_INST[Execute CALL SUB1]
    CALL_INST --> PUSH_IP[Push IP onto Stack SP minus 2]
    PUSH_IP --> JUMP[Jump to SUB1 Start Address]
    JUMP --> SUB_BODY[Execute SUB1 Body]
    SUB_BODY --> RET_INST[Execute RET]
    RET_INST --> POP_IP[Pop IP from Stack SP plus 2]
    POP_IP --> RETURN[Return to Main Program]
    RETURN --> CALLER
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Stack and Stack Pointer. Explain the LIFO principle.
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

A **Stack** is a region of memory used by the processor to store temporary data, return addresses, and register contents. It operates on the **Last-In-First-Out (LIFO)** principle, meaning the **last item pushed is the first item popped**.

The **Stack Pointer (SP)** is a dedicated CPU register that always holds the **offset address** of the topmost element (TOS) in the stack segment. In 8086, SP is a **16-bit register** paired with the SS register. In 8051, SP is an **8-bit SFR** (address 81H) pointing into internal RAM, and is initialized to **07H** on reset.

**LIFO Principle:** Just like a stack of plates, only the topmost plate is accessible. A `PUSH` adds to the top; a `POP` removes from the top. This ensures that subroutine return addresses and register contents are restored in the correct reverse order.

### Question 2: Compare 8086 and 8051 Stack Operations.
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | 8086 | 8051 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| SP width | 16-bit | 8-bit |
| SP after reset | Undefined (typical FFFEH) | 07H |
| Stack memory | External (1 MB) | Internal RAM (00H–7FH) |
| Direction of growth | **Downward** (SP decrements) | **Upward** (SP increments) |
| PUSH SP update | $SP \leftarrow SP - 2$ | $SP \leftarrow SP + 1$ |
| POP SP update | $SP \leftarrow SP + 2$ | $SP \leftarrow SP - 1$ |
| Segment register | Uses SS:SP | No segment; direct addressing |
| Endianness | Little-endian | N/A (byte only) |

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A: Comprehensive Stack Analysis
`[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the stack structure of 8086 microprocessor with a neat diagram. Discuss the PUSH and POP instructions in detail, including the effect on SP. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The 8086 uses a **16-bit stack pointer (SP)** and a **16-bit stack segment (SS)** register. The physical address of the top of stack is computed as:

$$PA_{TOS} = SS \times 10H + SP$$

**PUSH Instruction (e.g., PUSH BX):**

When the 8086 executes `PUSH BX`, it performs the following micro-operations:

1. $SP \leftarrow SP - 2$ (SP is decremented by 2 because BX is a 16-bit register).
2. $\text{Memory}[SS:SP] \leftarrow BL$ (low byte stored at lower address).
3. $\text{Memory}[SS:SP+1] \leftarrow BH$ (high byte stored at higher address).

**POP Instruction (e.g., POP CX):**

When the 8086 executes `POP CX`, it performs:

1. $CL \leftarrow \text{Memory}[SS:SP]$ (low byte loaded into CL).
2. $CH \leftarrow \text{Memory}[SS:SP+1]$ (high byte loaded into CH).
3. $SP \leftarrow SP + 2$ (SP is incremented by 2).

The stack **grows downward** in the 8086. After each PUSH, SP decreases; after each POP, SP increases. The SS:SP pair always points to the **most recently pushed** item.

**(b)** Explain the 8051 stack. How is it initialized? Discuss PUSH and POP with examples. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The 8051 microcontroller uses an **8-bit stack pointer (SP)** located in the SFR at address **81H**. The stack resides in the **internal data RAM (00H to 7FH)**.

**Initialization:** On reset, $SP = 07H$. This means the first PUSH will store data at address 08H. To relocate the stack to a safer region, the programmer uses:

```asm
MOV SP, #30H
```

**PUSH Instruction (e.g., PUSH ACC):**

The 8051 PUSH is a **byte operation**:

1. $SP \leftarrow SP + 1$ (SP is incremented by 1).
2. $\text{InternalRAM}[SP] \leftarrow ACC$ (accumulator stored at new SP).

**POP Instruction (e.g., POP 30H):**

1. $\text{InternalRAM at 30H} \leftarrow \text{InternalRAM}[SP]$ (byte at SP loaded to address 30H).
2. $SP \leftarrow SP - 1$ (SP is decremented).

The 8051 stack **grows upward**. After each PUSH, SP increases. The 8051 stack is **not segmented** and does not use little-endian conventions because it operates on single bytes.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students incorrectly assume that PUSH and POP behave identically in 8086 and 8051. The **direction of stack growth is opposite** (8086 grows down, 8051 grows up), and the **SP update delta is different** (2 vs 1). Marks are frequently lost here.

### Question B: Alternative Choice
`[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain how `CALL` and `RET` instructions use the stack in 8086. Show the stack contents during a subroutine call with an example. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The `CALL` instruction transfers control to a subroutine and uses the stack to **save the return address**. The `RET` instruction **restores the return address** from the stack.

**Execution of `CALL SUB1` (Near Call):**

Assume $IP_{current} = 0200H$ (address of instruction after CALL is at $0203H$), $SP = 1000H$, $SS = 2000H$.

**Step 1:** Decrement SP by 2:
$$SP \leftarrow SP - 2 = 0FFEH$$

**Step 2:** Store the return address (IP = 0203H) at the new TOS:
$$\text{Memory}[20FFEH] \leftarrow 03H \quad (\text{low byte of IP})$$
$$\text{Memory}[20FFFH] \leftarrow 02H \quad (\text{high byte of IP})$$

**Step 3:** Load IP with the offset of SUB1:
$$IP \leftarrow \text{offset of SUB1}$$

**Execution of `RET` inside SUB1:**

**Step 1:** Read return address from TOS:
$$IP_{low} \leftarrow \text{Memory}[SS:SP] = 03H$$
$$IP_{high} \leftarrow \text{Memory}[SS:SP+1] = 02H$$
$$IP = 0203H$$

**Step 2:** Increment SP by 2:
$$SP \leftarrow SP + 2 = 1000H$$

Control returns to the instruction at $0203H$ in the main program. The stack is now restored to its original state.

**(b)** Explain interrupt handling in 8086 with reference to the stack. How are flags, CS, and IP saved? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

When the 8086 receives a maskable interrupt (INTR) or executes a software interrupt (INT n), it performs the following **stack-based** sequence:

**Step 1:** Decrement SP by 2 and push the **Flags Register (FR)**:
$$SP \leftarrow SP - 2$$
$$\text{Memory}[SS:SP] \leftarrow FLAGS$$

**Step 2:** Decrement SP by 2 and push the **Code Segment (CS)**:
$$SP \leftarrow SP - 2$$
$$\text{Memory}[SS:SP] \leftarrow CS_{old}$$

**Step 3:** Decrement SP by 2 and push the **Instruction Pointer (IP)**:
$$SP \leftarrow SP - 2$$
$$\text{Memory}[SS:SP] \leftarrow IP_{old}$$

**Step 4:** Clear IF and TF, load new IP and CS from the interrupt vector table.

**Total stack push:** 6 bytes (3 words). The SP is decremented by 6 from its original value.

**Execution of `IRET` (Interrupt Return):**

The processor reverses the process in the **opposite order** (LIFO):

1. Pop IP from stack: $SP \leftarrow SP + 2$
2. Pop CS from stack: $SP \leftarrow SP + 2$
3. Pop FLAGS from stack: $SP \leftarrow SP + 2$

This restores the processor state exactly as it was before the interrupt, allowing normal execution to resume seamlessly.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often forget that the **FLAGS register is pushed first** and **popped last** in interrupt handling. The order is **FLAGS → CS → IP** (push) and **IP → CS → FLAGS** (pop). Reversing this order will cost 2–3 marks.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ The stack follows the **LIFO (Last-In, First-Out)** principle rigorously.
- ✅ The **Stack Pointer (SP)** always points to the **top of the stack**.
- ✅ In 8086, SP is **16-bit** and works with the **SS** segment register; physical address is $SS \times 10H + SP$.
- ✅ In 8051, SP is **8-bit** and resets to **07H**; stack grows in internal RAM.
- ✅ 8086 stack **grows downward** ($SP \leftarrow SP - 2$ on PUSH).
- ✅ 8051 stack **grows upward** ($SP \leftarrow SP + 1$ on PUSH).
- ✅ 8086 is **little-endian**: low byte at lower address, high byte at higher address.
- ✅ The 8086 PUSH/POP operates on **words (2 bytes)**, while 8051 operates on **single bytes**.
- ✅ The number of **PUSHes must equal** the number of **POPs** in any routine to maintain stack integrity.
- ✅ `CALL` pushes the return address (IP) onto the stack; `RET` pops it back.
- ✅ Interrupts push **FLAGS, CS, and IP** onto the stack in that order.
- ✅ Stack overflow/underflow occurs when SP exceeds segment limits (8086) or RAM boundaries (8051).
- ✅ Always initialize the 8051 SP to a safe value (e.g., 30H or higher) to avoid conflicts with register banks.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Strategy:** Memorize the formula $PA_{TOS} = SS \times 10H + SP$ and the **direction of stack growth** for both processors. These two facts alone solve 60% of stack-related exam questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Procedures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCIT404 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Procedures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the architecture of the **Intel 8086 microprocessor**, a **Procedure** (also formally termed a *subroutine* or *function* in high-level language parlance) is defined as a self-contained, named, and reusable sequence of assembly language instructions that performs a specific, well-defined task. Procedures allow modularization of assembly programs by enabling a single block of code to be invoked (called) from multiple locations within the same program, thereby promoting **code reusability**, **memory efficiency**, and **structured programming** in low-level systems.

A procedure is delineated by a *procedure label* (its symbolic entry point) and is terminated by a dedicated **return instruction (RET)**. The mechanism of invocation is performed by the **CALL instruction**, which transfers control to the procedure while preserving the *return address* (the address of the instruction immediately following the CALL) on the system **Stack**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Procedures constitute a high-priority topic under the broader umbrella of **8086 Assembly Language Programming Constructs**. Mastery of CALL/RET semantics, stack-frame handling, parameter passing conventions, and register preservation are direct, frequently tested learning outcomes.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are writing a long recipe book, and a particular step — *"chop onions finely"* — is required in **10 different recipes**.

Instead of rewriting those **5 lines of chopping instructions** in every recipe (which would waste pages), you simply **write that step once** in a separate "Helper Techniques" section, label it **"Step-A"**, and then every recipe simply says: *"Refer to Step-A for chopping onions."*

After the reader does Step-A, they automatically **return back** to the recipe they were following and continue.

| Real-World Analogy | 8086 Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Recipe book | Program in memory |
| Helper step "Step-A" | A **Procedure** |
| "Refer to Step-A" | **CALL** instruction |
| Returning to the main recipe | **RET** instruction |
| Bookmark saving your place | Return address pushed onto **Stack** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The **Stack** is the critical "bookmark holder." Without it, after a procedure finishes, the CPU would not know *where* in the program to resume execution. The CALL instruction automatically saves this "bookmark" (return address), and RET reads it back to restore the flow.

## 1.3 Formal Categorization of Procedures

Procedures in 8086 are classified into two fundamental types based on the **segment relationship** between the calling code and the procedure itself:

1. **NEAR Procedure:** Both the CALL site and the procedure reside within the **same Code Segment (CS)**. Only the **Instruction Pointer (IP)** needs to be saved on the stack. A NEAR CALL pushes a **16-bit offset** onto the stack.
2. **FAR Procedure:** The procedure resides in a **different Code Segment** than the calling code. Both the **Code Segment Register (CS)** and the **Instruction Pointer (IP)** must be saved on the stack (CS first, then IP). A FAR CALL pushes a **32-bit value** (CS:IP) onto the stack.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The matching **RET** instruction (NEAR or FAR variant) must mirror the type of CALL. The assembler enforces this via directives such as `PROC NEAR` and `PROC FAR`.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> **[!NOTE]** **Standard Stack-Word Metrics for 8086 Procedure Calls**
> - **NEAR CALL Push Size:** 2 bytes (16-bit IP)
> - **FAR CALL Push Size:** 4 bytes (16-bit CS + 16-bit IP)
> - **NEAR RET Pop Size:** 2 bytes (16-bit IP)
> - **FAR RET Pop Size:** 4 bytes (16-bit CS + 16-bit IP)
> - **Stack Pointer Decrement per Push:** `SP ← SP − 2`
> - **Stack Pointer Increment per Pop:** `SP ← SP + 2`
> - **Default Operand Size:** 16 bits
> - **Stack Growth Direction:** Toward **lower** memory addresses (descending stack)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Mechanics — The CALL Instruction

The **CALL** instruction performs a **two-phase atomic operation** that distinguishes it from a simple JMP (jump):

1. **PUSH Phase:** The address of the *next sequential instruction* (the return address) is pushed onto the system stack.
2. **TRANSFER Phase:** Control is transferred to the target procedure label (i.e., IP/CS is updated to the target address).

### 2.1.1 Detailed Logic Steps

**For a NEAR CALL:**

- **Step 1:** `SP ← SP − 2` (decrement stack pointer to make room)
- **Step 2:** `Memory[SS:SP] ← IP` of the next instruction (save return address)
- **Step 3:** `IP ← target_offset` (load the procedure's offset address)
- **Step 4:** The CS register remains unchanged.

**For a FAR CALL:**

- **Step 1:** `SP ← SP − 2`
- **Step 2:** `Memory[SS:SP] ← CS` (save caller's code segment)
- **Step 3:** `SP ← SP − 2`
- **Step 4:** `Memory[SS:SP] ← IP` of the next instruction
- **Step 5:** `CS ← target_segment`; `IP ← target_offset`

## 2.2 Operational Mechanics — The RET Instruction

The **RET (Return)** instruction is the inverse of CALL and is the *last executable instruction* of any well-formed procedure.

### 2.2.1 Detailed Logic Steps

**For a NEAR RET:**

- **Step 1:** `IP ← Memory[SS:SP]` (pop the return offset)
- **Step 2:** `SP ← SP + 2` (clean up the stack)

**For a FAR RET:**

- **Step 1:** `IP ← Memory[SS:SP]`; `SP ← SP + 2`
- **Step 2:** `CS ← Memory[SS:SP]`; `SP ← SP + 2`

> [!NOTE]
> An optional **immediate operand** may be appended to RET, e.g., `RET 4`. This is used to **clean up parameters** passed on the stack. After popping the return address, the instruction adds the operand to SP. This is a KTU-favorite trick question.

## 2.3 Register Preservation Convention (Caller vs. Callee Saved)

A critical aspect of procedure design is **register preservation** — deciding *who* (caller or callee) is responsible for saving register values across a CALL.

| Convention Type | Registers Preserved by Caller | Registers Preserved by Callee | Used By |
|---|---|---|---|
| **cdecl** (C convention) | EAX, ECX, EDX (caller-saved / volatile) | EBX, ESI, EDI, EBP (callee-saved / non-volatile) | Most C compilers (32-bit) |
| **stdcall** (PASCAL) | EAX, ECX, EDX | EBX, ESI, EDI, EBP | Win32 API |
| **8086 bare-metal** | Convention-free; programmer chooses | Convention-free; programmer chooses | Typical KTU assembly exam |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** For bare-metal 8086 assembly (the typical KTU context), there is **no enforced ABI**. You must **explicitly PUSH** registers at procedure entry and **POP** them at procedure exit, in strict **Last-In-First-Out (LIFO)** reverse order. Forgetting this is a **frequent mark-deduction error**.

## 2.4 Parameter Passing Techniques

There are **three** principal methods of passing parameters to a procedure in 8086 assembly:

### Method 1: Registers (Fastest, Limited)
Parameters are loaded into CPU registers (e.g., AL, BX, CX, DX) prior to the CALL.

### Method 2: Memory (Global Variables)
Parameters are stored in named memory variables (often in the DATA segment) which the procedure accesses directly.

### Method 3: Stack (Most Flexible, KTU-Favorite)
Parameters are PUSHed onto the stack by the caller *before* the CALL. The procedure accesses them via the **BP register** (using stack-relative addressing).

### 2.4.1 Stack Frame Layout (The BP Convention)

Upon procedure entry, the **standard prologue** is:

```asm
PUSH BP        ; Save caller's BP
MOV  BP, SP    ; BP now points to current top of stack
```

This creates a **stack frame** that allows the procedure to access parameters and local variables using a stable reference point.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| NEAR CALL push size | $2$ bytes | $16$-bit IP only |
| FAR CALL push size | $4$ bytes | $16$-bit CS + $16$-bit IP |
| NEAR RET pop size | $2$ bytes | Restores IP only |
| FAR RET pop size | $4$ bytes | Restores CS:IP |
| New SP after PUSH | $SP_{new} = SP_{old} - 2$ | Bytes |
| New SP after POP | $SP_{new} = SP_{old} + 2$ | Bytes |
| Effective address of $n^{th}$ stack word | $EA = SS \times 16 + BP + (2k)$ | Where $k$ is positive offset for caller args |
| Stack growth | Descending (toward $0x0000$) | **8086 convention** |
| RET $n$ cleanup | $SP_{new} = SP_{old} + 2 + n$ | Used for stack-passed parameters |
| Return address offset from BP | $\vert BP + 2 \vert$ (NEAR) | Inside the callee |
| First pushed argument offset from BP | $\vert BP + 4 \vert$ (NEAR) | Right above return address |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX/Markdown Safety:** Absolute values and magnitudes use `\vert` rendering, **never** the bare vertical bar `|` character, to prevent markdown table breakage in the KTU exam PDF generation pipeline.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Procedures in microprocessors underpin virtually **every** modern computing construct:
- **Operating System Kernels:** System calls, context switches, interrupt service routines (ISRs) all leverage the CALL/RET mechanism.
- **Embedded Firmware:** A microcontroller's main loop is a non-terminating procedure that repeatedly CALLs sub-procedures (sensor read, ADC conversion, PWM update).
- **Compiler Backends:** When you write a function in C/Python/Java, the compiler emits 8086 CALL/RET instructions under the hood.
- **Device Drivers:** Hardware abstraction layers use procedures to encapsulate I/O port reads/writes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter for KTU?** Understanding procedures at the assembly level enables you to debug optimized binaries, write efficient interrupt handlers, and most importantly — it is **a guaranteed question topic in the 2024 Scheme ESE (End Semester Examination).**

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides the **exhaustive algebraic and operational derivation** of stack-pointer arithmetic and return-address computation for a typical 8086 procedure call. **No step is skipped.**

## 3.1 Derivation: Stack Pointer Evolution Across a NEAR CALL

### Given (Initial Conditions)

- Initial Stack Pointer: $SP_0$
- Initial Stack Segment: $SS$
- Caller IP at the CALL instruction: $IP_{call}$
- Target Procedure offset: $IP_{target}$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

When the CPU fetches the CALL instruction, it has already incremented IP past the CALL opcode (which is typically $3$ bytes for a near direct call: opcode $1$ byte + $16$-bit offset $2$ bytes). The IP at this point equals $IP_{call} + 3$, which becomes the *return address*.

**Line 1 — Pre-CALL state:**

$$SP = SP_0$$

**Line 2 — Decrement SP to make space for the return address:**

The 8086 stack grows downward, so we must first move SP *below* the current top of stack:

$$SP = SP_0 - 2$$

**Line 3 — Store the 16-bit return address at the new top of stack:**

The physical memory location is computed as the linear address formed by concatenating $SS$ and $SP$:

$$\text{Physical Address} = (SS \times 16) + (SP_0 - 2)$$

The byte at the *lower* address stores the **low byte** of the return address, and the byte at the *higher* address stores the **high byte**:

$$\text{Memory}[(SS \times 16) + (SP_0 - 2)] = (IP_{call} + 3) \mod 256$$

$$\text{Memory}[(SS \times 16) + (SP_0 - 1)] = \lfloor (IP_{call} + 3) / 256 \rfloor$$

**Line 4 — Load the new IP with the target procedure's offset:**

$$IP = IP_{target}$$

**Line 5 — Begin execution at the new CS:IP:**

The CPU now begins fetching instructions from logical address $CS : IP_{target}$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Stack Pointer Evolution Across a NEAR RET

### Given (Just before RET executes)

- Stack Pointer: $SP_1$ (where $SP_1 = SP_0 - 2$)
- Top of stack contains the return address: $IP_{ret} = IP_{call} + 3$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Line 1 — Pop the low byte of the return address into the low byte of IP:**

$$IP_{low} = \text{Memory}[(SS \times 16) + SP_1]$$

**Line 2 — Pop the high byte of the return address into the high byte of IP:**

$$IP_{high} = \text{Memory}[(SS \times 16) + SP_1 + 1]$$

**Line 3 — Reconstruct the 16-bit IP:**

$$IP = (IP_{high} \times 256) + IP_{low} = IP_{call} + 3$$

**Line 4 — Increment SP to remove the popped word from the stack:**

$$SP = SP_1 + 2 = SP_0$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** The final $SP$ value ($SP_0$) exactly matches the pre-CALL $SP$ value, demonstrating that the CALL/RET pair is **stack-neutral** when no parameters are passed and no registers are saved.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Far Call vs. Near Call Numerical Comparison

Let us derive the *cumulative* stack-pointer delta for a **FAR CALL** and its corresponding **FAR RET**, and compare it with a **NEAR CALL/RET** pair.

### FAR CALL — Step-by-Step

**Line 1 — Save CS first (PUSH CS):**

$$SP = SP_0 - 2$$

$$\text{Memory}[(SS \times 16) + (SP_0 - 2)] = CS_{caller}$$

**Line 2 — Save IP second (PUSH IP):**

$$SP = SP_0 - 4$$

$$\text{Memory}[(SS \times 16) + (SP_0 - 4)] = IP_{call} + 5$$

(Note: FAR direct CALL opcode is $5$ bytes: opcode $1$ byte + $16$-bit segment $2$ bytes + $16$-bit offset $2$ bytes.)

**Line 3 — Load new CS and IP:**

$$CS = CS_{target}, \quad IP = IP_{target}$$

### FAR RET — Step-by-Step

**Line 1 — Pop IP (last-pushed, first-popped):**

$$IP = \text{Memory}[(SS \times 16) + (SP_0 - 4)]$$

$$SP = SP_0 - 2$$

**Line 2 — Pop CS:**

$$CS = \text{Memory}[(SS \times 16) + (SP_0 - 2)]$$

$$SP = SP_0$$

### Summary Table — Stack Delta

| Operation | NEAR CALL | FAR CALL | NEAR RET | FAR RET |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Bytes pushed/popped | $2$ | $4$ | $2$ | $4$ |
| SP change (PUSH phase) | $SP - 2$ | $SP - 4$ | N/A | N/A |
| SP change (POP phase) | N/A | N/A | $SP + 2$ | $SP + 4$ |
| Registers affected | IP | CS, IP | IP | CS, IP |
| Net SP change (CALL+RET) | $0$ | $0$ | $0$ | $0$ |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example: Parameter Access via BP

Suppose we call a procedure `SUM` that adds two parameters passed on the stack. The relevant assembly and stack layout are given.

### Assembly Snippet (Caller Side)

```asm
PUSH  BX        ; Push second parameter
PUSH  AX        ; Push first parameter
CALL  SUM       ; NEAR call (pushes return address)
ADD   SP, 4     ; Caller cleans up 4 bytes of parameters
```

### Stack Layout Just After CALL (Before Procedure Prologue)

Assuming initial $SP = 0xFFF8$ (arbitrary) and $SS = 0x1000$:

| Memory Address (logical) | Content | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `0xFFF8` (current SP) | $IP_{ret}$ (low byte) | Return address |
| `0xFFF9` | $IP_{ret}$ (high byte) | Return address |
| `0xFFFA` | $AX$ (low byte) | 1st parameter |
| `0xFFFB` | $AX$ (high byte) | 1st parameter |
| `0xFFFC` | $BX$ (low byte) | 2nd parameter |
| `0xFFFD` | $BX$ (high byte) | 2nd parameter |

### Procedure Prologue (Callee Side)

```asm
SUM    PROC  NEAR
       PUSH  BP        ; Save old BP
       MOV   BP, SP    ; BP now points to saved BP location
```

### Stack Layout After Prologue

| Memory Address (logical) | Content | BP-relative offset |
|---|---|---|
| `0xFFF6` (new SP) | Old BP (low) | (top of stack) |
| `0xFFF7` | Old BP (high) | |
| `0xFFF8` | $IP_{ret}$ (low) | $\vert BP + 2 \vert$ |
| `0xFFF9` | $IP_{ret}$ (high) | |
| `0xFFFA` | $AX$ (low) | $\vert BP + 4 \vert$ |
| `0xFFFB` | $AX$ (high) | |
| `0xFFFC` | $BX$ (low) | $\vert BP + 6 \vert$ |
| `0xFFFD` | $BX$ (high) | |

### Parameter Access in Callee

- First parameter (AX) is at: $\vert BP + 4 \vert$ → accessed as `[BP + 4]`
- Second parameter (BX) is at: $\vert BP + 6 \vert$ → accessed as `[BP + 6]`

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Parameters are *above* the return address on the stack. They are accessed using *positive* offsets from BP. The return address is at $\vert BP + 2 \vert$ and the saved old BP is at $\vert BP \vert$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Procedure Execution Flow (Mermaid State Diagram)

This diagram models the **CPU state transitions** during a complete CALL → Procedure Body → RET cycle, including register save/restore. Node identifiers are purely alphanumeric and labels are raw uppercase text per the engine protocol.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[IDLE: FETCH CALL OPCODE] --> B{IS CALL NEAR OR FAR}
    B -- NEAR --> C1[DECREMENT SP BY 2]
    B -- FAR --> C2[DECREMENT SP BY 2]
    C2 --> C3[PUSH CS ONTO STACK]
    C3 --> C4[DECREMENT SP BY 2]
    C1 --> D[PUSH IP ONTO STACK]
    C4 --> D
    D --> E[LOAD NEW IP AND CS IF FAR]
    E --> F[FETCH PROCEDURE FIRST INSTRUCTION]
    F --> G[PROLOGUE: PUSH BP AND MOV BP SP]
    G --> H[OPTIONAL PUSH REGISTERS TO SAVE]
    H --> I[EXECUTE PROCEDURE BODY]
    I --> J[EPILOGUE: OPTIONAL POP SAVED REGISTERS]
    J --> K[POP OLD BP]
    K --> L{IS RET NEAR OR FAR}
    L -- NEAR --> M1[POP IP FROM STACK]
    L -- FAR --> M2[POP IP FROM STACK]
    M2 --> M3[POP CS FROM STACK]
    M1 --> N[INCREMENT SP ACCORDINGLY]
    M3 --> N
    N --> O[RESUME CALLER EXECUTION AT RETURN ADDRESS]
    O --> P[IDLE: AWAIT NEXT INSTRUCTION]
```

## 4.2 Stack Memory Map — Visual Block Diagram

This diagram illustrates the **descending growth** of the 8086 stack during a typical NEAR procedure call, from the perspective of the *callee* after prologue.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph STACK_GROWTH["STACK MEMORY DESCENDING DIRECTION LOW ADDRESS TO HIGH"]
        direction TB
        TOP["ADDRESS 0xFFF6 -- SP POINTS HERE -- SAVED OLD BP"]
        RA_LO["ADDRESS 0xFFF8 -- RETURN ADDRESS LOW BYTE"]
        RA_HI["ADDRESS 0xFFF9 -- RETURN ADDRESS HIGH BYTE"]
        P1_LO["ADDRESS 0xFFFA -- FIRST PARAMETER LOW BYTE"]
        P1_HI["ADDRESS 0xFFFB -- FIRST PARAMETER HIGH BYTE"]
        P2_LO["ADDRESS 0xFFFC -- SECOND PARAMETER LOW BYTE"]
        P2_HI["ADDRESS 0xFFFD -- SECOND PARAMETER HIGH BYTE"]
    end
    TOP --> RA_LO
    RA_LO --> RA_HI
    RA_HI --> P1_LO
    P1_LO --> P1_HI
    P1_HI --> P2_LO
    P2_LO --> P2_HI
    CALLEE_ACCESS["CALLEE ACCESSES FIRST PARAM AS BP PLUS 4 AND SECOND PARAM AS BP PLUS 6"] -.-> P1_LO
    CALLEE_ACCESS -.-> P2_LO
    RA_NOTE["RETURN ADDRESS LOCATED AT BP PLUS 2"] -.-> RA_LO
```

## 4.3 Register Classification Block Diagram

This block diagram classifies registers by **who is responsible** for preservation in a standard 8086 procedure (caller-saved vs. callee-saved convention).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    REG["8086 CPU REGISTERS"] --> GEN["GENERAL PURPOSE: AX BX CX DX"]
    REG --> SEG["SEGMENT REGISTERS: CS DS SS ES"]
    REG --> PTR["POINTER REGISTERS: SP BP SI DI"]
    REG --> FLAG["FLAG REGISTER"]
    GEN --> AXB["AX: ACCUMULATOR OFTEN USED FOR RETURN VALUES"]
    GEN --> BXB["BX: BASE REGISTER CALLEE SAVED TYPICALLY"]
    GEN --> CXB["CX: COUNT REGISTER CALLER SAVED TYPICALLY"]
    GEN --> DXB["DX: DATA REGISTER CALLER SAVED TYPICALLY"]
    PTR --> SPB["SP: STACK POINTER NEVER MODIFIED FREELY"]
    PTR --> BPB["BP: BASE POINTER USED FOR STACK FRAMES"]
```

## 4.4 Nested Procedure Call Hierarchy

Nested calls (procedure A calls procedure B) are valid and the stack handles them transparently. The following block diagram shows a *two-level* nested call structure with stack contents.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    MAIN["MAIN PROGRAM AT IP 1000H"] -->|"CALL PROC A"| PA["PROC A ENTRY AT IP 2000H"]
    PA -->|"STACK PUSHES RETURN TO MAIN IP 1003H"| STK1["STACK TOP ENTRY 1: RETURN TO MAIN"]
    PA --> PB["PROC A CALLS PROC B AT IP 3000H"]
    PB -->|"STACK PUSHES RETURN TO PROC A IP 2005H"| STK2["STACK TOP ENTRY 2: RETURN TO PROC A"]
    PB --> EXEC["PROC B EXECUTES BODY"]
    EXEC -->|"RET POPS ENTRY 2"| RET1["CPU RESUMES INSIDE PROC A AT IP 2005H"]
    PA2["PROC A CONTINUES EXECUTION"] -->|"RET POPS ENTRY 1"| RET2["CPU RESUMES INSIDE MAIN AT IP 1003H"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between a NEAR procedure and a FAR procedure in 8086. Mention the stack implications of each.**

**Model Answer:**

A **NEAR procedure** resides in the **same code segment** as the calling program. During invocation, only the **16-bit Instruction Pointer (IP)** is saved on the stack, consuming **2 bytes** of stack space. A NEAR RET pops only the 2-byte IP, leaving CS unchanged.

A **FAR procedure** resides in a **different code segment**. The CALL instruction pushes both **CS (2 bytes)** and **IP (2 bytes)** onto the stack in that order, consuming **4 bytes** total. A FAR RET pops IP first, then CS.

| Feature | NEAR Procedure | FAR Procedure |
|---|---|---|
| Segment | Same as caller | Different from caller |
| Stack push (CALL) | 2 bytes (IP) | 4 bytes (CS + IP) |
| Stack pop (RET) | 2 bytes (IP) | 4 bytes (CS + IP) |
| Directive | `PROC NEAR` | `PROC FAR` |
| Speed | Faster (smaller push) | Slower (larger push) |

**(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for stack implications, 1 for tabular comparison)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Explain the role of the BP register in establishing a stack frame for an 8086 procedure.**

**Model Answer:**

The **BP (Base Pointer)** register is used to create a **stable reference point** within the procedure's stack frame, enabling the procedure to reliably access parameters passed by the caller and local variables.

The standard **prologue** is:
```asm
PUSH  BP        ; Preserve caller's BP
MOV   BP, SP    ; BP ← current SP (new stack frame base)
```

After this, parameters pushed by the caller before the CALL are accessible at **positive offsets** from BP:
- $\vert BP + 2 \vert$ → Return address (NEAR call)
- $\vert BP + 4 \vert$ → First parameter
- $\vert BP + 6 \vert$ → Second parameter

Local variables (if any) are stored at **negative offsets** from BP. Without BP, the procedure would be unable to locate parameters because SP may shift during the procedure body's PUSH/POP operations.

**(3 marks — 1.5 for prologue explanation, 1.5 for parameter access logic)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Format)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following **TWO alternative questions** follow the KTU ESE (End Semester Examination) Module Internal Choice pattern. The student answers **ONE** of them in the examination. Each is worth 14 marks and is broken into 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 / Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Describe the complete sequence of operations performed by the 8086 microprocessor when executing a **NEAR CALL** instruction. Illustrate the state of the Stack Pointer (SP), the Instruction Pointer (IP), and the top of stack before and after the call. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a complete 8086 assembly language program that defines a procedure named `MULTIPLY` to multiply two 16-bit unsigned numbers passed via the stack, returning the 32-bit result in **DX:AX**. Show the procedure prologue, body, epilogue, and a sample caller. Explain the register preservation strategy. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### **Part (a) — NEAR CALL Execution Sequence:**

When the 8086 executes a **NEAR CALL target** (opcode `E8` followed by 16-bit relative offset), the following operations occur in order:

1. **Prefetch and Decode:** The BIU fetches the 3-byte CALL instruction. The instruction queue holds it for the EU to decode.

2. **Compute Return Address:** After the EU decodes CALL, the IP has been auto-incremented by the BIU to point to the *next* instruction after CALL. Let this be $IP_{ret}$.

3. **PUSH Return Address onto Stack:** The BIU performs:
   - $SP \leftarrow SP - 2$
   - Stores the low byte of $IP_{ret}$ at $SS:SP$
   - Stores the high byte of $IP_{ret}$ at $SS:(SP+1)$

4. **Update IP:** The BIU adds the signed 16-bit relative offset (sign-extended) to the current IP, producing the procedure's entry-point offset. CS is unchanged.

5. **Begin Execution:** The EU begins fetching and executing instructions from the new CS:IP.

**State Diagram:**

| State | IP | SP | Top of Stack |
|---|---|---|---|
| Before CALL | $IP_{call}$ (pointing at CALL) | $SP_0$ | (old data) |
| After CALL decode (return addr computed) | $IP_{call} + 3$ | $SP_0$ | (old data) |
| After PUSH phase | $IP_{call} + 3$ | $SP_0 - 2$ | $IP_{call} + 3$ (saved) |
| After IP update | $IP_{target}$ | $SP_0 - 2$ | $IP_{call} + 3$ (saved) |

**(7 marks — 2 for sequence, 2 for state diagram, 2 for register changes, 1 for concluding remark)**

#### **Part (b) — MULTIPLY Procedure Assembly Code:**

```asm
; ====== DATA SEGMENT ======
.DATA
    NUM1    DW  1234H
    NUM2    DW  00FFH
    RESULT  DD  ?           ; 32-bit result

; ====== CODE SEGMENT ======
.CODE
MAIN    PROC  FAR
        MOV   AX, @DATA
        MOV   DS, AX

        PUSH  NUM1          ; Push 1st parameter
        PUSH  NUM2          ; Push 2nd parameter
        CALL  MULTIPLY      ; DX:AX ← product
        ADD   SP, 4         ; Caller cleans up parameters

        MOV   WORD PTR RESULT,   AX
        MOV   WORD PTR RESULT+2, DX

        MOV   AH, 4CH
        INT   21H
MAIN    ENDP

; ===========================================
; PROCEDURE: MULTIPLY
; INPUT:  Two 16-bit values on stack
; OUTPUT: 32-bit product in DX:AX
; DESTROYS: AX, DX, CX (caller-saved)
; ===========================================
MULTIPLY PROC  NEAR
        PUSH  BP            ; ---- PROLOGUE ----
        MOV   BP, SP        ; Establish stack frame
        PUSH  BX            ; Save BX (callee-saved convention)

        MOV   AX, [BP+4]    ; Load 1st parameter
        MOV   BX, [BP+6]    ; Load 2nd parameter

        MUL   BX            ; DX:AX ← AX * BX (unsigned)

        POP   BX            ; ---- EPILOGUE ----
        POP   BP
        RET                 ; Return to caller
MULTIPLY ENDP

END     MAIN
```

**Register Preservation Strategy Explanation:**

- **BX is preserved** by the *callee* (PUSH at entry, POP at exit) because BX is generally considered a non-volatile register that the caller expects to be intact after the call.
- **AX and DX are not preserved** because they are conventionally used to return values from a procedure; the caller knows their contents will be modified and saves them beforehand if needed.
- **BP is preserved** because the caller's stack frame (in higher-level code) depends on it.
- **The CALLER cleans up the 4 bytes of parameters** (via `ADD SP, 4`) — this is the **cdecl** convention. Alternatively, the callee could use `RET 4` to do the cleanup.

**(7 marks — 3 for code, 2 for register preservation logic, 2 for parameter access explanation)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast the three methods of parameter passing to 8086 procedures: **registers, memory variables, and the stack**. For each, give one advantage and one disadvantage. Recommend the most suitable method for a deeply nested recursive procedure and justify your answer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the operation of the **`RET n`** instruction variant. Why is it used? Construct a small example in 8086 assembly where the callee cleans up the parameters from the stack using `RET 4`, and show the state of SP at every key point. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### **Part (a) — Parameter Passing Methods Comparison:**

| Method | Mechanism | Advantage | Disadvantage |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Registers** | Values placed in AX, BX, CX, DX before CALL | **Fastest** — no memory access needed | Limited to ~4-6 parameters; no reentrancy |
| **Memory (Global)** | Procedure reads from named variables in DATA segment | Simple; no stack management | **Not reentrant**; unsafe for ISRs and recursion |
| **Stack** | Values PUSHed before CALL; accessed via BP in callee | **Reentrant**; supports recursion; unlimited parameters | Slower (memory access); requires careful cleanup |

**Recommendation for Deeply Nested Recursive Procedure:**

The **STACK method** is unequivocally the most suitable. Justification:

1. **Reentrancy:** Each recursive call creates a new stack frame with its own copy of parameters and local variables. This prevents the corruption of state that would occur with global memory.

2. **State Preservation:** The CALL instruction automatically saves the return address on the stack, and the procedure can PUSH additional registers (BX, SI, DI) to preserve its working state. This is essential when the recursion depth may be unpredictable.

3. **Register method fails** because the limited register set would be overwritten at every recursion level.

4. **Memory (global) method fails catastrophically** because all recursive invocations would read and write the *same* global variables, producing incorrect results.

**(7 marks — 3 for comparison table, 2 for recommendation, 2 for justification)**

#### **Part (b) — The `RET n` Instruction:**

**Definition:** `RET n` (also written as `RETN n` or `RETF n` in some assemblers) is a variant of the RET instruction that, after popping the return address, **adds an immediate 16-bit value $n$ to SP**. This is used by the *callee* to clean up parameters that the *caller* pushed onto the stack before the CALL.

**Why is it used?** It implements the **stdcall/Pascal calling convention** where the callee (not the caller) is responsible for stack cleanup. This is common in Win32 API calls and produces slightly smaller caller code (no need for `ADD SP, n` after the CALL).

**Worked Example:**

```asm
; ====== CALLER ======
        PUSH  WORD PTR [SI]    ; Push 2nd parameter
        PUSH  WORD PTR [DI]    ; Push 1st parameter
        CALL  ADDPROC
        ; No ADD SP, 4 needed!
        ; SP is automatically restored

; ====== CALLEE ======
ADDPROC PROC  NEAR
        PUSH  BP
        MOV   BP, SP
        MOV   AX, [BP+4]       ; 1st parameter
        ADD   AX, [BP+6]       ; Add 2nd parameter
        POP   BP
        RET   4                ; Pop IP (2 bytes) + SP += 4
ADDPROC ENDP
```

**SP State Trace:**

Assume initial $SP = SP_0 = 0x0200$.

| Step | Action | New SP | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `PUSH [SI]` (2nd param) | $0x01FE$ | 1 param on stack |
| 2 | `PUSH [DI]` (1st param) | $0x01FC$ | 2 params on stack |
| 3 | `CALL ADDPROC` (pushes IP) | $0x01FA$ | Return address on top |
| 4 | `PUSH BP` (prologue) | $0x01F8$ | Old BP saved |
| 5 | Procedure body executes | $0x01F8$ | (unchanged during body) |
| 6 | `POP BP` (epilogue) | $0x01FA$ | Old BP restored |
| 7 | `RET 4` (pop IP, then $SP \leftarrow SP + 4$) | $0x0200$ | **Both** return addr and 4 bytes of params removed |

**Final Result:** $SP = 0x0200 = SP_0$. The stack is perfectly balanced. The caller's code after the CALL does **not** need an `ADD SP, 4` instruction.

**(7 marks — 2 for definition, 2 for convention explanation, 3 for the SP state trace table)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout
>
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes Observed in KTU Board Exam Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Confusing CALL/RET stack order for FAR calls:** Students frequently write that IP is pushed *before* CS on a FAR CALL. The **correct** order is: **CS first, then IP** (because CS is pushed first, IP will be popped first by RET — LIFO).
>
> 2. **Forgetting register preservation:** Writing a procedure that uses BX, CX, or DI without PUSHing/POPing them around the body. The examiner deducts **1–2 marks** for every preserved register missing.
>
> 3. **Incorrect BP offset:** Writing `[BP+2]` for the first parameter. The correct offset is **`[BP+4]`** because the return address (2 bytes) is *between* the saved BP and the first parameter.
>
> 4. **Stack imbalance on return:** Omitting the `ADD SP, n` cleanup after a CALL when using the *cdecl* convention. The exam explicitly tests for stack-balanced procedures.
>
> 5. **Mixing NEAR and FAR:** Defining a procedure as `NEAR` but calling it from a different code segment, or vice versa. This is a **logic error** worth full mark deduction.
>
> 6. **Missing the `RET n` semantics:** Writing `RET 4` and explaining it as "returning the value 4" — it actually means "add 4 to SP after popping the return address." A surprisingly common 2-mark loss.
>
> 7. **Not labeling the stack diagram:** Examiners often give 2–3 marks specifically for a *clear, labeled* stack diagram. Submitting only textual explanation without a diagram forfeits those marks.

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

The following is a **high-density, rapid-revision checklist** for the topic *Procedures* in 8086 assembly. Use this in the final hour before your KTU examination.

### ✅ Core Concept Checklist

- [x] A **Procedure** is a reusable, named block of code invoked via **CALL** and terminated via **RET**.
- [x] **CALL** is a "jump with memory" — it saves the return address on the stack before transferring control.
- [x] **RET** is the inverse — it pops the return address from the stack and resumes execution at that location.
- [x] **NEAR procedure:** Same code segment; CALL pushes 2 bytes (IP); RET pops 2 bytes.
- [x] **FAR procedure:** Different code segment; CALL pushes 4 bytes (CS then IP); RET pops 4 bytes (IP then CS).
- [x] The **stack grows downward** (toward lower memory addresses) on the 8086.

### ✅ Stack Arithmetic Rules

- [x] Every PUSH decrements SP by **2**.
- [x] Every POP increments SP by **2**.
- [x] `RET n` increments SP by **2 + n** (after popping the return address).
- [x] After a CALL/RET pair (with no parameter passing and no register saves), SP returns to its original value — the call is **stack-neutral**.

### ✅ Parameter Passing Methods

- [x] **Registers** — Fastest, limited capacity, non-reentrant.
- [x] **Memory (Global)** — Simple, but **NOT reentrant**, unsafe for ISRs/recursion.
- [x] **Stack** — Reentrant, supports recursion, requires BP-based addressing. **Best for nested/recursive procedures.**

### ✅ The BP Stack Frame Convention

- [x] Prologue: `PUSH BP` → `MOV BP, SP`.
- [x] First parameter is at $\vert BP + 4 \vert$ (NEAR call).
- [x] Return address is at $\vert BP + 2 \vert$ (NEAR call).
- [x] Saved old BP is at $\vert BP \vert$ (top of stack after prologue).
- [x] Local variables (if any) are at *negative* offsets from BP.

### ✅ Register Preservation

- [x] Caller-saved (volatile): **AX, CX, DX** — caller must save if needed.
- [x] Callee-saved (non-volatile): **BX, SI, DI, BP** — procedure must PUSH/POP.
- [x] **SP must NEVER be modified freely** inside the procedure body.
- [x] The procedure must restore SP before RET to the value it had after the prologue.

### ✅ Common Pitfalls to Avoid

- [x] Don't confuse the PUSH order for FAR calls (CS first, then IP).
- [x] Don't forget to POP registers in the **reverse** order of PUSH (LIFO).
- [x] Don't use `[BP+2]` for the first parameter — it's the return address.
- [x] Don't leave the stack imbalanced — always clean up parameters.
- [x] Don't mix NEAR and FAR declarations inconsistently.

### ✅ One-Line Memory Aid (Mnemonic)

> **"CALL Saves, RET Restores, BP Anchors, Stack Descends."**
> *(C-S-R-B-S-D)*

### ✅ Most Likely KTU 2024 Exam Question Types

- [x] NEAR vs. FAR comparison (3 marks — Part A).
- [x] Stack state trace across CALL and RET (7–10 marks).
- [x] Writing a complete procedure with prologue/epilogue (7–14 marks).
- [x] `RET n` semantics with stdcall cleanup (7 marks).
- [x] Parameter passing method comparison (7 marks).
- [x] Recursive procedure explanation with stack frame analysis (14 marks).

> 🎯 **Final Tip for KTU 2024:** Always **draw a labeled stack diagram** in your answer script. A clear diagram communicates correctness faster than text and secures 2–3 easy marks that distinguish a 12-mark answer from a 14-mark one.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Macros
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCIT404 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Macros

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **8086/8088 assembly language programming**, a **MACRO** is a single, named instruction sequence that represents a group of several standard assembly instructions. It is defined by the programmer using the assembler directives `MACRO` and `ENDM`. When the macro name (called the **macro prototype** or **macro call**) is subsequently invoked in the source program, the assembler replaces every occurrence of that name with the actual group of instructions it represents. This substitution process is known as **Macro Expansion**, and it occurs at **assembly time (preprocessing phase)**, not at run time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Macros belong to the family of *assembly-time directives* and are handled by the **preprocessor / macro assembler** (e.g., MASM 6.x, TASM). They must not be confused with run-time program units like **Procedures (PROC/ENDP)**, which involve explicit `CALL` and `RET` instructions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: "The Short Message (SMS) Abbreviation"**
> Think of typing "BRB" on your phone — your phone **automatically expands** it to "Be Right Back" before sending. You wrote just three letters, but the system replaced them with a longer phrase. The abbreviation "BRB" is the **Macro Name**, the full phrase is the **Macro Body**, and your phone's autocorrect is the **Assembler performing Macro Expansion**. Similarly, in assembly, you write a short macro name, and the assembler automatically substitutes the entire block of code at the location of the call.

### 1.3 Why Macros Exist — The Core Problem

Raw 8086 assembly is **verbose and repetitive**. For example, saving a register pair (`PUSH AX`, `PUSH BX`, `PUSH CX`) and later restoring them (`POP CX`, `POP BX`, `POP AX`) might be needed hundreds of times. Writing these instructions repeatedly:
- Increases program size in the source file
- Introduces copy-paste bugs
- Reduces code readability

**Macros** solve this by letting the programmer define the block once and reuse the name repeatedly.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics in Macro Programming

| Metric | Standard Value / Convention |
| :--- | :--- |
| Assembler used in KTU Labs | **MASM 6.15** (Microsoft) or **TASM** (Borland) |
| Directive to start a macro | `MACRO` |
| Directive to end a macro | `ENDM` |
| Local label directive | `LOCAL` |
| Phase of execution | **Assembly Time** (not Run Time) |
| Max macro nesting depth (typical) | **Unlimited** (but practically 5–8) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Phases of a Macro's Lifecycle

A macro goes through three structured phases during program development:

1. **Macro Definition Phase** – The programmer writes the macro block using `MACRO` … `ENDM` directives. This block is *not* executed as code at this point; it is merely *registered* with the assembler.
2. **Macro Invocation (Call) Phase** – The macro is referenced in code using its name, optionally with parameters.
3. **Macro Expansion Phase** – The assembler substitutes the macro body at every point of invocation, generating actual machine code in the `.LST` (listing) and `.OBJ` (object) files.

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial Distinction:** Expansion is purely a *textual* substitution. The assembler literally copies the instructions line by line into your program wherever the macro name appears.

### 2.2 General Syntax of a Macro Definition

The standard 8086 macro syntax accepted by MASM/TASM follows the structure:

$$\text{MACRO\_NAME} \;\; \texttt{MACRO} \;\; [\text{formal\_param}_1, \; \text{formal\_param}_2, \dots, \text{formal\_param}_n]$$

$$\text{(body of the macro: assembly instructions and comments)}$$

$$\texttt{ENDM}$$

* Every **formal parameter** is a symbolic placeholder.
* When the macro is invoked, the programmer supplies **actual parameters** that are matched positionally.
* A macro may be defined with **zero parameters** (a simple named block) or with **multiple parameters**.

### 2.3 The `LOCAL` Directive — Why It Is Essential

When a macro contains internal **labels** (e.g., loop targets, skip targets) and the macro is invoked more than once, the assembler will encounter **duplicate label errors** because every invocation would otherwise generate the same label name.

The `LOCAL` directive solves this by instructing the assembler to generate **unique labels** of the form `??xxxx` (e.g., `??0001`, `??0002`) automatically for each invocation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** The `LOCAL` directive must appear **immediately after** the `MACRO` line, before any executable instruction or label inside the macro body. This is a frequently tested viva question.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Symbolic / Syntactic Form | Phase | Key Property |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Macro definition | `name MACRO [params]` … `ENDM` | Assembly | Registers block with assembler |
| Macro invocation | `name [actual_params]` | Assembly | Triggers expansion |
| Formal parameters | `MACRO P1, P2, P3` | Assembly | Placeholders, positionally matched |
| Local labels | `LOCAL label1, label2` | Assembly | Generates `??xxxx` unique names |
| Nested macros | A macro calling another macro | Assembly | Allowed; expands innermost first |
| Macro expansion | Textual substitution | **Assembly Time** | Not a `CALL` instruction |
| Procedure call | `CALL proc_name` | **Run Time** | Uses `RET`, push/pop stack |

> **Note on notation:** In the table above, the vertical bar `\vert` symbol is used in the textual description to denote logical "or" (e.g., "LOCAL label1 **OR** label2") without breaking the markdown table.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Macros are heavily used in:
- **Device Driver Development:** Defining common hardware access sequences.
- **Embedded Systems Firmware:** Wrapping port-level operations into readable names.
- **Compiler Code Generation:** Compilers like GCC internally emit macro-like patterns.
