- **System Boot Code (BIOS/UEFI):** Where code size and execution speed must be tightly controlled.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Macro Expansion Logic — Exhaustive Trace

Consider a simple macro to swap two 8-bit values residing in memory, written using the `XCHG` instruction as a conceptual seed. To illustrate parameter substitution, we instead trace a generic **parameterised macro** that adds two operands.

**Step 1 — Macro Definition (source file):**

```
ADDXY    MACRO   A, B, SUM
         MOV     AX, A
         ADD     AX, B
         MOV     SUM, AX
         ENDM
```

**Step 2 — Macro Invocation:**

```
         ADDXY   WORD1, WORD2, RESULT
```

**Step 3 — Expansion by the Assembler (textual substitution):**

The assembler performs a strict, position-by-position textual replacement. The first formal parameter `A` is replaced with the first actual parameter `WORD1`, `B` is replaced with `WORD2`, and `SUM` is replaced with `RESULT`.

```
         MOV     AX, WORD1
         ADD     AX, WORD2
         MOV     RESULT, AX
```

**Analytical verification of the substitution rules:**

$$\text{Expanded\_Line}_i = \text{replace}(\text{Body\_Line}_i, \; \text{FormalParam}_k \mapsto \text{ActualParam}_k) \quad \forall k \in \{1, 2, \dots, n\}$$

where $n$ is the number of formal parameters and $\mapsto$ denotes string substitution.

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Analysis of `LOCAL` Directive Expansion

Consider this macro containing an internal jump label:

```
DELAY    MACRO   COUNT
         LOCAL   LOOP
         MOV     CX, COUNT
LOOP:    DEC     CX
         JNZ     LOOP
         ENDM
```

**First invocation:**

```
         DELAY   100
```

The assembler expands this to:

```
         MOV     CX, 100
??0001:  DEC     CX
         JNZ     ??0001
```

**Second invocation:**

```
         DELAY   200
```

The assembler generates a *different* unique label:

```
         MOV     CX, 200
??0002:  DEC     CX
         JNZ     ??0002
```

**Mathematical justification for uniqueness:**

$$\text{label}_i = \text{prefix} + \text{hex}(\text{invocation\_counter}_i)$$

where the prefix is `??` and `invocation_counter` increments for each call. Therefore:

$$\text{label}_1 \neq \text{label}_2 \quad \text{always} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{no duplicate label error.}$$

### 3.3 Macro vs Procedure — The Master Comparison Matrix

| Criterion | Macro | Procedure |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Phase of operation | **Assembly time** | **Run time** |
| Implementation directive | `MACRO` / `ENDM` | `PROC` / `ENDP` |
| Invocation mechanism | **Textual substitution** (no CPU action) | `CALL` instruction (CPU fetches) |
| Stack usage | **None** | Uses stack (`PUSH`, `RET`) |
| Code size | **Larger** (expanded at every call site) | **Smaller** (one copy in memory) |
| Execution speed | **Faster** (no `CALL`/`RET` overhead) | **Slightly slower** (stack management) |
| Parameter passing | By textual substitution | By registers, stack, or memory |
| Recursive use | **Not directly possible** | **Yes** (supports recursion) |
| Local label problem | Solved by `LOCAL` directive | Not applicable (labels are part of code) |
| Debugging | **Harder** (trace through expanded code) | **Easier** (single body to debug) |

### 3.4 Derivation of the Assembly-Time Expansion Rule

The total code size $S_{\text{final}}$ of a program containing macros can be expressed as:

$$S_{\text{final}} = S_{\text{base}} + \sum_{i=1}^{M} \left( n_i \cdot L_i \right)$$

where:
- $S_{\text{base}}$ is the size of the source code without macro expansion.
- $M$ is the total number of distinct macros in the program.
- $n_i$ is the number of times the $i$-th macro is invoked.
- $L_i$ is the size (in bytes or instructions) of the $i$-th macro's body.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** This equation shows that macros **inflate code size linearly with invocation count**, which is why they are unsuitable for memory-constrained systems like early 8-bit microcontrollers. Procedures, by contrast, contribute a *constant* size regardless of how many times they are called.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Macro Processing Pipeline

The diagram below illustrates the complete flow of a macro from definition to final executable code.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source Code .ASM File] --> B{Preprocessor / Assembler Scans File}
    B --> C[Encounters MACRO Directive]
    C --> D[Macro Definition Registered in Symbol Table]
    D --> E[Assembler Continues Scanning]
    E --> F{Macro Name Encountered in Code?}
    F -->|No| G[Process as Normal Instruction]
    F -->|Yes| H[Retrieve Macro Body from Symbol Table]
    H --> I[Substitute Actual Parameters for Formal Parameters]
    I --> J[Generate Unique Labels for LOCAL Directives]
    J --> K[Insert Expanded Code at Invocation Point]
    K --> L[Continue Scanning]
    L --> F
    G --> M[Final Assembled Code .OBJ File]
    K --> M
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Macro vs Procedure Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MacroBlock[MACRO APPROACH]
        M1[Call Site 1] --> M2[Inlined Code Copy 1]
        M3[Call Site 2] --> M4[Inlined Code Copy 2]
        M5[Call Site 3] --> M6[Inlined Code Copy 3]
    end
    subgraph ProcBlock[PROCEDURE APPROACH]
        P1[Call Site 1] --> PCALL[CALL Instruction]
        P2[Call Site 2] --> PCALL
        P3[Call Site 3] --> PCALL
        PCALL --> PBODY[Single Procedure Body in Memory]
        PBODY --> PRET[RET Instruction]
    end
```

### 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram: Macro Lifecycle States

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Defined: Programmer writes MACRO ... ENDM
    Defined --> Invoked: Programmer writes macro_name + args
    Invoked --> Expanding: Assembler triggers expansion
    Expanding --> Expanded: Textual substitution complete
    Expanded --> Invoked: Next invocation in source
    Expanded --> FinalCode: All source processed
    FinalCode --> [*]: Assembler emits .OBJ file
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define a macro. List any two advantages of using macros in 8086 assembly language programming.

**Model Answer:**

A **macro** is a named block of assembly language instructions defined using the `MACRO` and `ENDM` directives. When the macro name is referenced in the program, the assembler **substitutes the entire body of the macro** at the call site during the assembly phase (a process called *macro expansion*).

**Two advantages:**
1. **Reduces repetition** in source code, improving readability and maintainability.
2. **Faster execution** at run time because the inlined code avoids the overhead of `CALL` and `RET` instructions used in procedures.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
What is the purpose of the `LOCAL` directive inside a macro? Give a small example.

**Model Answer:**

The `LOCAL` directive is used inside a macro body to **declare labels that are local to each invocation** of the macro. When the macro is expanded multiple times, the assembler automatically generates **unique names** (of the form `??xxxx`) for these labels, preventing duplicate-label errors.

**Example:**

```
DELAY    MACRO   N
         LOCAL   WAIT
         MOV     CX, N
WAIT:    DEC     CX
         JNZ     WAIT
         ENDM
```

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> Students frequently lose marks by:
> 1. **Confusing macros with procedures** — always state the *phase* (assembly-time vs run-time) explicitly in your answer.
> 2. Forgetting the **`LOCAL` directive** when a macro contains internal jump labels — the examiner will deduct up to **3 marks** if the code would generate a duplicate-label error.
> 3. Writing a macro *body* instead of the *expansion* when asked to "show the expanded code."
> 4. Spelling `ENDM` as `END MACRO` or `END` — these are **syntactically wrong** in MASM and will be marked incorrect.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – July 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of macros in 8086 assembly language with a suitable example. Show the step-by-step expansion of the macro. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between macros and procedures with at least six points. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Macros — Explanation with Example**

A macro is a user-defined symbolic name that represents a group of instructions. The macro is defined once using the `MACRO` and `ENDM` directives, and the assembler **expands** (substitutes) the macro body wherever the macro name appears in the source code.

**Example — Macro to display a message:**

```
PRINT    MACRO   MSG
         MOV     AH, 09H
         LEA     DX, MSG
         INT     21H
         ENDM
```

**Invocation:**

```
         .MODEL  SMALL
         .DATA
GREET    DB      'Hello!$'
         .CODE
START:   MOV     AX, @DATA
         MOV     DS, AX
         PRINT   GREET
         MOV     AH, 4CH
         INT     21H
         END     START
```

**Expanded Code (after assembler processing):**

```
         .MODEL  SMALL
         .DATA
GREET    DB      'Hello!$'
         .CODE
START:   MOV     AX, @DATA
         MOV     DS, AX
         MOV     AH, 09H
         LEA     DX, GREET
         INT     21H
         MOV     AH, 4CH
         INT     21H
         END     START
```

**Step-by-step expansion logic:**
- The assembler identifies the call `PRINT GREET`.
- It looks up the macro definition in the symbol table.
- It substitutes the first formal parameter (`MSG`) with the actual parameter (`GREET`).
- It inserts the three lines of the macro body at the call site.
- Execution continues at the next source line.

**(b) Macros vs Procedures — Six Key Differences**

| S.No. | Macro | Procedure |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Processed at **assembly time** | Processed at **run time** |
| 2 | Uses `MACRO` / `ENDM` directives | Uses `PROC` / `ENDP` directives |
| 3 | No `CALL`/`RET`; pure text substitution | Uses `CALL` and `RET` instructions |
| 4 | Code size **increases** with each call | Code size remains **constant** |
| 5 | **Faster** execution (inlined) | Slightly **slower** (stack operations) |
| 6 | **Cannot** be recursive | **Can** be recursive |

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** What is the significance of the `LOCAL` directive in macros? Write a macro to perform a software delay using a loop, and demonstrate how the assembler handles multiple invocations of this macro. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a macro `ADDTHREE` that adds three 16-bit numbers stored in memory locations and stores the result in a fourth location. Invoke the macro in a complete 8086 ALP and show the expanded listing. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Significance of `LOCAL` Directive**

When a macro contains internal labels (e.g., loop targets), repeated invocation of the macro will cause the assembler to encounter **duplicate label definitions**, resulting in an error. The `LOCAL` directive instructs the assembler to generate **system-defined unique labels** (`??0000`, `??0001`, etc.) for each invocation, eliminating the conflict.

**Macro Definition:**

```
DELAY    MACRO   COUNT
         LOCAL   LOOP
         MOV     CX, COUNT
LOOP:    DEC     CX
         JNZ     LOOP
         ENDM
```

**Invocation 1:**

```
         DELAY   500
```

**Expansion 1:**

```
         MOV     CX, 500
??0000:  DEC     CX
         JNZ     ??0000
```

**Invocation 2:**

```
         DELAY   1000
```

**Expansion 2:**

```
         MOV     CX, 1000
??0001:  DEC     CX
         JNZ     ??0001
```

The assembler guarantees unique label names, thus preventing a duplicate-symbol error.

**(b) `ADDTHREE` Macro — Full Program**

```
ADDTHREE MACRO   A, B, C, SUM
         MOV     AX, A
         ADD     AX, B
         ADD     AX, C
         MOV     SUM, AX
         ENDM
```

**Main Program:**

```
         .MODEL  SMALL
         .STACK  100H
         .DATA
NUM1     DW      1234H
NUM2     DW      5678H
NUM3     DW      0ABCH
RESULT   DW      0000H
         .CODE
START:   MOV     AX, @DATA
         MOV     DS, AX
         ADDTHREE NUM1, NUM2, NUM3, RESULT
         MOV     AH, 4CH
         INT     21H
         END     START
```

**Expanded Listing:**

```
START:   MOV     AX, @DATA
         MOV     DS, AX
         MOV     AX, NUM1
         ADD     AX, NUM2
         ADD     AX, NUM3
         MOV     RESULT, AX
         MOV     AH, 4CH
         INT     21H
         END     START
```

**Verification (hand calculation):**

$$\text{RESULT} = \text{NUM1} + \text{NUM2} + \text{NUM3} = 1234\text{H} + 5678\text{H} + 0\text{ABCH}$$

$$\text{RESULT} = 1\text{E}68\text{H} \quad (\text{hexadecimal addition})$$

The expanded code stores this 16-bit sum in the memory location `RESULT`.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist**

- A **macro** is a named block of instructions defined once and **substituted textually** by the assembler wherever its name appears.
- Macros operate at **assembly time** — they do **not** generate `CALL` or `RET` instructions.
- The directives `MACRO` and `ENDM` mark the start and end of a macro definition, respectively.
- **Formal parameters** are placeholders in the definition; **actual parameters** are the values supplied at the call site and are matched **positionally** (first to first, second to second, etc.).
- The `LOCAL` directive is mandatory when a macro contains **internal labels** that are referenced by jumps — it prevents duplicate-label errors by generating unique names like `??0001`.
- Macros **increase code size** linearly with the number of invocations, while **procedures** keep code size constant.
- Macros execute **faster** than procedures (no stack overhead), but procedures are **more memory-efficient** and **support recursion**.
- Macros **cannot be recursive** because expansion is purely textual.
- The expanded form of a macro can be viewed in the **`.LST` (listing) file** generated by the assembler.
- For KTU practical exams, always run your program through MASM/TASM with `/L` flag to produce the listing file and verify the expansion.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interrupts and Interrupt Service Routines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCIT404 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Interrupts and Interrupt Service Routines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interrupt:** An interrupt is an external or internal event that asynchronously suspends the normal sequential execution of the microprocessor/microcontroller, forcing it to branch to a predefined routine called the **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)** to service the event. After ISR execution, the processor resumes the original program from the point of interruption using the `IRET` instruction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR):** A special subroutine (also called *interrupt handler*) whose entry address is stored in the **Interrupt Vector Table (IVT)**. The ISR contains the code required to acknowledge, process, and clear the interrupting condition before returning control to the main program.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are **studying in a library** (CPU executing main program) and suddenly your **phone rings** (interrupt signal). You don't throw the book away — you:
1. Mark the page you were reading (saves IP/CS/FLAGS on the stack).
2. Pick up the phone and talk (executes ISR).
3. Hang up and return to the exact page (pops state, resumes via `IRET`).

In the 8086 architecture, this "phone ring" can come from **hardware** (a pin going high) or **software** (an instruction like `INT 21H`).

## 1.3 Why Interrupts Matter (Engineering Relevance)

| Mechanism | Polling (Without Interrupt) | Interrupt-Driven (Modern) |
|---|---|---|
| CPU Utilization | Wastes cycles continuously checking flags | Idle until event occurs |
| Response Time | Variable, depends on polling frequency | Immediate (deterministic) |
| Power Efficiency | Poor | Excellent (used in sleep modes) |
| Real-Time Suitability | Not suitable for hard real-time | Essential for embedded systems |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Highlight: Interrupts are the **backbone of real-time embedded systems** — used in motor control (PWM), keyboard handling (PS/2), timers (8253/8254), serial communication (8251 UART), and modern RTOS scheduling.

## 1.4 Classification of 8086 Interrupts (Syllabus Core)

The 8086 supports up to **256** different interrupt types (Type 0 to Type 255), broadly classified as:

1. **Hardware Interrupts** — generated by external physical signals
   - **NMI** (Non-Maskable Interrupt) — Type 2, edge-triggered, cannot be disabled
   - **INTR** (Maskable Interrupt) — Type determined by external controller (e.g., 8259A PIC)
2. **Software Interrupts** — initiated by CPU instructions
   - `INT n` instructions (where `n` = 0 to 255)
   - **Exceptions** — generated by CPU on error conditions (e.g., Type 0: Divide Error, Type 1: Single Step, Type 3: Breakpoint, Type 4: Overflow)

> [!NOTE]
> **Maskable vs Non-Maskable:** A *maskable* interrupt can be disabled by clearing the **Interrupt Flag (IF)** using the `CLI` instruction. A *non-maskable* interrupt **cannot** be disabled by software — it is accepted under all conditions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 8086 Interrupt Vector Table (IVT)

The 8086 reserves the **first 1024 bytes (1 KB) of physical memory** as a lookup table. This region, from physical address `00000H` to `003FFH`, is the **Interrupt Vector Table**.

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| Total memory allocated | **1024 bytes = 1 KB** |
| Number of entries | **256** (Type 0 to Type 255) |
| Bytes per entry | **4 bytes** (2 bytes IP + 2 bytes CS) |
| Start address | `00000H` |
| End address | `003FFH` |

### Vector Address Calculation (The Core "Why")

Each interrupt type `n` has a corresponding 4-byte entry. Because entries are stored sequentially starting from `00000H`, the physical address of the vector for type `n` is simply:

$$\text{Vector Address for INT } n = n \times 4$$

The 4 bytes are split as follows:

$$\text{IP (offset)} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{stored at memory address } (n \times 4)$$

$$\text{CS (segment)} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{stored at memory address } (n \times 4) + 2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Formula:** When an `INT n` is executed, the 8086 does NOT decode the opcode fully — it simply multiplies `n` by 4 to fetch the new CS:IP from the IVT. This is why the table is called a "vector" table — it provides the *direction* (vector) of execution.

## 2.2 Reserved Interrupt Types (Fixed by Intel Architecture)

| Type | Name | Vector Address | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | Divide Error | `00000H` | CPU on division by zero |
| 1 | Single Step | `00004H` | TF = 1 (Trap Flag) |
| 2 | NMI | `00008H` | NMI pin (non-maskable) |
| 3 | Breakpoint | `0000CH` | `INT 3` (1-byte opcode) |
| 4 | Overflow | `00010H` | `INTO` when OF = 1 |
| 5–31 | Reserved | `00014H`–`0007CH` | Intel reserved |
| 32–255 | User Available | `00080H`–`003FFH` | DOS, BIOS, User ISRs |

## 2.3 Hardware Interrupt Signals (Pin-Level View)

| Pin | Type | Trigger | Vector | Maskable? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `NMI` | Input | Rising edge | Type 2 (fixed) | **No** (non-maskable) |
| `INTR` | Input | Level (high) | From external 8259A | Yes (via IF) |
| `INTA` | Output | Pulse | Acknowledgment to 8259A | — |

## 2.4 Interrupt Processing Sequence (The "How" — Step by Step)

When the 8086 acknowledges an interrupt, the following hardware sequence occurs **atomically**:

1. **Complete current instruction** — never interrupts mid-instruction.
2. **Push FLAGS** register onto the stack (SP decremented by 2).
3. **Push CS** (Code Segment) onto the stack.
4. **Push IP** (Instruction Pointer) onto the stack.
5. **Clear IF and TF** — disables maskable interrupts and single-stepping during the ISR.
6. **Fetch new IP** from memory location `(Type × 4)`.
7. **Fetch new CS** from memory location `(Type × 4) + 2`.
8. **Execute ISR** instructions sequentially.
9. **`IRET` instruction** — pops IP, then CS, then FLAGS from the stack in reverse order.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The state save (FLAGS, CS, IP) is performed by the CPU **hardware**, not by the programmer. This is what makes interrupt handling transparent to the interrupted program.

## 2.5 Interrupt Priority (When Multiple Sources Arrive)

The 8086 has a fixed internal priority (highest → lowest) for simultaneous interrupt sources:

1. **Divide Error (Type 0)** / `INT n` / `INTO` (internal exceptions)
2. **NMI (Type 2)**
3. **INTR** (external, after `IF` = 1)
4. **Single Step (Type 1)** — lowest priority

> [!NOTE]
> Within `INTR`, priority is determined by the external **8259A Programmable Interrupt Controller (PIC)**, which supports cascading and rotating priority schemes.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Formula / Rule | Expression | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| IVT start address | `00000H` | First physical byte of memory |
| IVT end address | `003FFH` | After 256 × 4 bytes |
| Vector address | $V = n \times 4$ | `n` = interrupt type (0–255) |
| IP storage offset | $V = n \times 4$ | Lower 2 bytes of entry |
| CS storage offset | $V = n \times 4 + 2$ | Upper 2 bytes of entry |
| Total IVT size | $256 \times 4 = 1024$ bytes | $= 1$ KB |
| State saved on stack | FLAGS, CS, IP | 6 bytes total (3 pushes) |
| State restored by | `IRET` instruction | 3 pops in reverse order |
| NMI vector | Type 2 → `00008H` | Hardwired in hardware |
| INTR vector | Type `n` (from 8259A) | Assigned by interrupt controller |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive ECUs:** NMI handles airbag deployment sensors; maskable interrupts handle OBD-II diagnostics.
- **Industrial PLCs:** INTR signals process over-current faults; ISR trips the contactor in microseconds.
- **IoT Devices:** Deep-sleep mode is broken only by an external interrupt (e.g., button press on GPIO).
- **Operating Systems:** DOS/Windows used `INT 21H` (DOS services) and `INT 13H` (disk services) as system call mechanisms.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Vector Address for INT 21H

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **classic KTU problem**. Given any interrupt type, you must compute the exact memory location of its vector in the IVT.

**Problem:** Calculate the physical address in the IVT where the vector for `INT 21H` is stored. Also compute the addresses of the IP and CS components.

### Step 1 — Identify the Interrupt Type

The interrupt type `n` is the hexadecimal operand of the `INT` instruction:
$$n = 21H = 33_{10}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Vector Address Formula

The vector address is:
$$V = n \times 4$$
$$V = 21H \times 4$$

To multiply a hex value by 4, we can shift left by 2 bits (multiply by 2 twice):

$$\begin{aligned}
21H \times 2 &= 42H \\
42H \times 2 &= 84H
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore:
$$V = 21H \times 4 = 84H$$

### Step 3 — Locate the IP (Lower 2 Bytes of Vector)

The IP for the `INT 21H` handler is stored at:
$$\text{IP address} = V = 84H = 00084H$$

### Step 4 — Locate the CS (Upper 2 Bytes of Vector)

The CS for the `INT 21H` handler is stored at:
$$\text{CS address} = V + 2 = 84H + 2H = 86H = 00086H$$

### Step 5 — Final Answer

| Component | Physical Address | Content (Example) |
|---|---|---|
| IP | `00084H`, `00085H` | e.g., `00 00` (low half of handler address) |
| CS | `00086H`, `00087H` | e.g., `F0 00` (segment of handler address) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification:** The 4-byte entry spans `00084H` to `00087H`, which is exactly `4` bytes — consistent with the IVT structure.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Calculating Available User Interrupt Vectors

**Problem:** How many interrupt vectors are reserved by Intel, and how many remain for user applications in the 8086?

### Step 1 — Total Number of Vectors

$$\text{Total} = 256 \text{ (Types 0 to 255)}$$

### Step 2 — Intel-Reserved Types (0 to 31)

The 8086 architecture reserves the first 32 types (Type 0 to Type 31) for internal CPU exceptions and Intel-defined functions:
$$\text{Reserved} = 32 \text{ vectors} = 32 \times 4 = 128 \text{ bytes}$$

### Step 3 — User-Available Types (32 to 255)

$$\text{Available} = 256 - 32 = 224 \text{ vectors}$$

Memory used by user-available vectors:
$$224 \times 4 = 896 \text{ bytes}$$

### Step 4 — Cross-Check the Total IVT Size

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Total IVT memory} &= (32 \times 4) + (224 \times 4) \\
&= 128 + 896 \\
&= 1024 \text{ bytes} \\
&= 1 \text{ KB} \;\;\checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, DOS and BIOS consume many of the "user-available" vectors (e.g., INT 08H–1FH for BIOS, INT 20H, 21H, 27H for DOS). In pure embedded 8086 systems, all 224 vectors are free for the developer.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Maskable vs Non-Maskable Comparison

| Parameter | NMI (Non-Maskable) | INTR (Maskable) |
|---|---|---|
| Vector type | Fixed (Type 2) | Variable (from 8259A) |
| Trigger | Rising edge | High level |
| Vector address | `00008H` | External controller |
| Controlled by `IF`? | **No** | **Yes** |
| Disabled by `CLI`? | No | Yes (IF = 0) |
| Enabled by `STI`? | No effect | Yes (IF = 1) |
| Acknowledgment pin used? | No | Yes (`INTA` pulse) |
| Typical use | Power failure, parity error | I/O devices, timers |

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: `INT n` vs `INT 3` vs `INTO`

| Instruction | Opcode Size | Operand | Vector | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `INT n` | 2 bytes | `n` = 0–255 | Type `n` | Software interrupt call |
| `INT 3` | 1 byte (`0CCH`) | None | Type 3 | Breakpoint (debugger) |
| `INTO` | 1 byte | None | Type 4 | Overflow trap (if OF=1) |
| `IRET` | 1 byte | None | — | Return from ISR |
| `CLI` | 1 byte | None | — | Clear IF (disable INTR) |
| `STI` | 1 byte | None | — | Set IF (enable INTR) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 8086 Interrupt Processing Flow (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Main Program Execution] --> B{Interrupt Request Received?}
    B -- No --> A
    B -- Yes --> C{Is Interrupt Maskable AND IF = 0?}
    C -- Yes --> A
    C -- No --> D[Complete Current Instruction]
    D --> E[Push FLAGS onto Stack]
    E --> F[Push CS onto Stack]
    F --> G[Push IP onto Stack]
    G --> H[Clear IF and TF Flags]
    H --> I[Compute Vector Address: n x 4]
    I --> J[Fetch IP from n x 4 and CS from n x 4 plus 2]
    J --> K[Execute Interrupt Service Routine]
    K --> L[IRET Instruction Encountered?]
    L --> M[Pop IP from Stack]
    M --> N[Pop CS from Stack]
    N --> O[Pop FLAGS from Stack]
    O --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> The yellow decision diamonds (`B`, `C`, `L`) represent hardware-level decisions made in a single clock cycle. The rectangular blocks represent CPU state transitions.

---

## 4.2 Interrupt Vector Table (IVT) Memory Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Physical 00000H: Type 0 IP] --> B[00002H: Type 0 CS]
    B --> C[00004H: Type 1 IP]
    C --> D[00006H: Type 1 CS]
    D --> E[00008H: Type 2 NMI IP]
    E --> F[0000AH: Type 2 NMI CS]
    F --> G[0000CH: Type 3 BKP IP]
    G --> H[0000EH: Type 3 BKP CS]
    H --> I[00010H: Type 4 OF IP]
    I --> J[00012H: Type 4 OF CS]
    J --> K[... 28 Intel Reserved ...]
    K --> L[00080H: Type 32 IP]
    L --> M[00082H: Type 32 CS]
    M --> N[003FFH: Type 255 CS]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Each row in the IVT is **4 bytes**: 2 bytes for IP (offset) followed by 2 bytes for CS (segment). The CS:IP pair together forms the 20-bit physical start address of the ISR.

---

## 4.3 Hardware Interrupt Pin Configuration (8086)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CPU[8086 Microprocessor]
        NMI_PIN[NMI Pin Type 2 Vector]
        INTR_PIN[INTR Pin Maskable IF]
        INTA_PIN[INTA Output Acknowledge Pulse]
        IF_REG[Interrupt Flag IF in FLAGS Reg]
    end

    subgraph EXT[External 8259A PIC]
        IR0[IRQ0]
        IR1[IRQ1]
        IR2[IRQ2]
        IRN[IRQn]
    end

    EXT -- Type data on bus --> INTR_PIN
    INTR_PIN -- if IF = 1 --> CPU
    CPU -- INTA pulse --> EXT
    IF_REG -.controls.-> INTR_PIN
    NMI_PIN -.hardwired.-> CPU
```

> [!NOTE]
> The 8259A Programmable Interrupt Controller acts as a *priority manager* and *encoder* — converting 8 (or 64, cascaded) IRQ lines into a single `INTR` signal plus a type number on the data bus.

---

## 4.4 Interrupt Acknowledgment Timing (Block Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Device as External Device
    participant PIC as 8259A PIC
    participant CPU as 8086 CPU
    participant IVT as Interrupt Vector Table
    participant ISR as Interrupt Service Routine

    Device->>PIC: IRQ line HIGH
    PIC->>CPU: INTR pin HIGH
    CPU->>CPU: Check IF (must be 1)
    CPU->>PIC: INTA pulse (cycle 1)
    PIC->>CPU: Interrupt type n on data bus
    CPU->>CPU: Push FLAGS, CS, IP
    CPU->>IVT: Fetch IP from n x 4
    CPU->>IVT: Fetch CS from n x 4 + 2
    CPU->>ISR: Jump to ISR address
    ISR->>Device: Service the device
    ISR->>CPU: IRET instruction
    CPU->>Device: Resume main program
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define an interrupt. List the different types of interrupts supported by the 8086 microprocessor.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** An interrupt is an asynchronous signal (hardware or software) that temporarily suspends the normal program execution of the CPU, causing it to execute a special routine called the Interrupt Service Routine (ISR). After the ISR completes, the CPU resumes the original program from the point of interruption.

**Types (2 marks):** The 8086 supports 256 interrupt types, classified as:

1. **Hardware Interrupts:**
   - **NMI (Non-Maskable Interrupt):** Type 2, edge-triggered, cannot be disabled.
   - **INTR (Maskable Interrupt):** Type supplied by external 8259A, can be disabled by clearing IF.
2. **Software Interrupts:**
   - **INT n instructions:** User-invoked software interrupts.
   - **Internal Exceptions:** Type 0 (Divide Error), Type 1 (Single Step), Type 3 (Breakpoint), Type 4 (Overflow).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often forget to mention **internal CPU exceptions** (Type 0, 1, 3, 4) as a separate category. Examiners award 1 mark specifically for mentioning exceptions. **Mention all four (NMI, INTR, INT n, Exceptions) to get full marks.**

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**What is the Interrupt Vector Table? Calculate the vector address for INT 50H.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**IVT Definition (1.5 marks):** The Interrupt Vector Table is a 1024-byte (1 KB) lookup table located in the first physical memory region (`00000H` to `003FFH`). It contains 256 entries of 4 bytes each, where each entry holds the starting CS:IP address of an Interrupt Service Routine.

**Vector Address Calculation (1.5 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
V &= n \times 4 \\
V &= 50H \times 4 \\
V &= 50H \times 2 \times 2 \\
V &= A0H \times 2 \\
V &= 140H
\end{aligned}$$

The IP for INT 50H is stored at `00140H` and the CS at `00142H`.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) Explain the 8086 interrupt structure with a neat diagram. Discuss the Interrupt Vector Table in detail. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. 8086 Interrupt Structure (3.5 marks):**
The 8086 supports 256 interrupt types, identified by type numbers 0 to 255. They are classified as:

- **Hardware Interrupts** — NMI (Type 2, non-maskable) and INTR (maskable, vector from 8259A).
- **Software Interrupts** — `INT n` instructions and internal exceptions (Type 0, 1, 3, 4).
- **Single-step interrupt** — Triggered when Trap Flag (TF) = 1, useful for debugging.

When an interrupt is acknowledged:
- The CPU completes the current instruction.
- FLAGS, CS, and IP are pushed onto the stack (in that order).
- The Interrupt Flag (IF) and Trap Flag (TF) are cleared.
- A new CS:IP is fetched from the IVT.
- ISR is executed, ending with `IRET`.

**2. Interrupt Vector Table Detail (3.5 marks):**
- **Location:** Physical address `00000H` to `003FFH` (first 1 KB of memory).
- **Size:** 1024 bytes = 256 entries × 4 bytes per entry.
- **Structure of each entry:** 2-byte IP followed by 2-byte CS, total 4 bytes.
- **Vector Address Formula:** For interrupt type `n`, the vector address is `n × 4`.
- **Reserved entries:** Types 0–31 are reserved for Intel (exceptions, NMI, etc.).
- **User entries:** Types 32–255 are available for user ISRs and DOS/BIOS.

**Diagram:** Refer to the IVT Memory Map in Section 4.2.

---

**(b) With a neat flowchart, explain the interrupt processing sequence of the 8086 microprocessor. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step-by-Step Processing Sequence (7 marks):**

1. **Interrupt Request:** An external device (via INTR/NMI) or internal condition (exception/`INT n`) requests service.
2. **IF Check (for INTR only):** If maskable, CPU checks Interrupt Flag. If IF = 0, request is ignored.
3. **Complete Current Instruction:** CPU never interrupts mid-instruction.
4. **Push FLAGS Register:** The 16-bit FLAGS register is pushed onto the stack (SP -= 2).
5. **Push CS Register:** Code Segment register is pushed (SP -= 2).
6. **Push IP Register:** Instruction Pointer is pushed (SP -= 2). The interrupted location is now saved.
7. **Clear IF and TF:** The hardware clears both flags to prevent nested maskable interrupts and single-stepping inside the ISR.
8. **Compute Vector Address:** `V = n × 4`.
9. **Load New IP:** Read 16-bit IP from memory at `V`.
10. **Load New CS:** Read 16-bit CS from memory at `V + 2`.
11. **Execute ISR:** CPU begins executing instructions at the new CS:IP.
12. **IRET Instruction:** When reached, it pops IP, then CS, then FLAGS from the stack in reverse order.
13. **Resume Main Program:** The next instruction executed is the one following the original interruption point.

**Flowchart:** Refer to the Mermaid flowchart in Section 4.1.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall (KTU Examiner's Note):** Many students write only "ISR is executed and then returns" — this is worth **zero marks**. The examiner awards 1 mark each for: (1) Pushing FLAGS, (2) Pushing CS, (3) Pushing IP, (4) Clearing IF/TF, (5) Fetching new IP/CS from IVT, (6) IRET popping order, (7) Correct sequencing. **Memorize all seven steps in order.**

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a) Differentiate between maskable and non-maskable interrupts. Explain the role of the 8259A Programmable Interrupt Controller in extending the 8086 interrupt capability. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Maskable vs Non-Maskable (3.5 marks):**

| Feature | NMI (Non-Maskable) | INTR (Maskable) |
|---|---|---|
| Vector | Fixed (Type 2) | Variable (from 8259A) |
| Trigger | Rising edge | High level |
| Disable mechanism | Cannot be disabled | Cleared by `CLI` (IF=0) |
| Priority | Higher than INTR | Lower than NMI |
| Use | Critical events (power fail) | Normal I/O devices |
| Acknowledgment | None | `INTA` pulse from CPU |

**Role of 8259A PIC (3.5 marks):**
- The 8086 has only **one INTR pin**, which can accept only one external request at a time.
- The **8259A PIC** expands this single INTR line into **8 IRQ inputs** (IR0–IR7).
- Up to **8 PICs can be cascaded**, expanding the inputs to **64 IRQ lines**.
- The 8259A performs **priority resolution** (fixed, rotating, or special mask modes).
- On receiving an `INTA` pulse, the 8259A places the **interrupt type number** on the data bus, which the CPU uses to index the IVT.
- The 8259A is programmed via **Initialization Command Words (ICWs)** and **Operation Command Words (OCWs)**.

---

**(b) Explain the significance of the Interrupt Flag (IF) and Trap Flag (TF) in the 8086 FLAGS register. How does the 8086 handle priority when multiple interrupts occur simultaneously? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Interrupt Flag (IF) – Bit 9 of FLAGS (3 marks):**
- Controls whether the 8086 recognizes maskable interrupts on the INTR pin.
- **IF = 1 (Set):** Maskable interrupts are **enabled**; CPU responds to INTR.
- **IF = 0 (Cleared):** Maskable interrupts are **disabled**; CPU ignores INTR.
- Instructions: `STI` (Set IF), `CLI` (Clear IF).
- Used to protect **critical sections** of code from being interrupted.
- The CPU **automatically clears IF** when entering an ISR (prevents nested interrupts unless re-enabled).
- **NMI is NOT affected by IF** — this is the defining property of "non-maskable."

**Trap Flag (TF) – Bit 8 of FLAGS (2 marks):**
- When TF = 1, the CPU automatically generates a **Type 1 interrupt** after every instruction.
- Used by debuggers to implement **single-stepping** through code.
- TF is **cleared automatically** when entering the ISR (along with IF) to prevent recursive single-stepping.
- Programmer cannot directly modify TF; it is set via `PUSHF`/`POPF` sequences or special debugger instructions.

**Priority Handling (2 marks):**
The 8086 has a **fixed internal priority** for simultaneous interrupts (highest to lowest):
1. **Internal exceptions** (Type 0 Divide Error, Type 4 Overflow, `INT n`)
2. **NMI** (Type 2)
3. **INTR** (external, after IF = 1)
4. **Single Step** (Type 1, lowest)

For multiple requests on **INTR**, the **8259A PIC** determines priority using one of its programmed modes (Fixed Priority, Rotating Priority, or Special Mask Mode).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse the role of IF and TF. **Mnemonic:** **I**F controls **I**nterrupts; **T**F controls **T**rapping (single-stepping). Both are auto-cleared on ISR entry. Also, do NOT claim that NMI is "less important" than INTR — NMI has **higher** priority by hardware design.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Points for KTU 2024 ESE:**

- [x] The 8086 supports **256 interrupt types** (Type 0 to Type 255).
- [x] The **Interrupt Vector Table (IVT)** occupies the first **1 KB** of memory (`00000H` to `003FFH`).
- [x] Each IVT entry is **4 bytes** (2-byte IP + 2-byte CS).
- [x] **Vector address** for type `n` = `n × 4`. This is the **single most important formula** for KTU.
- [x] IP is stored at offset `n × 4`; CS is stored at `n × 4 + 2`.
- [x] **NMI is hardwired to Type 2** (vector address `00008H`); it is **non-maskable**.
- [x] **INTR is maskable**, controlled by the **Interrupt Flag (IF)**. Disable with `CLI`, enable with `STI`.
- [x] When an interrupt is accepted, the CPU pushes **FLAGS → CS → IP** onto the stack (in this order).
- [x] On ISR entry, **IF and TF are automatically cleared** by hardware.
- [x] **`IRET` instruction** pops IP → CS → FLAGS in reverse order to resume the main program.
- [x] Reserved types: **0 (Divide), 1 (Single Step), 2 (NMI), 3 (Breakpoint), 4 (Overflow)**.
- [x] Types **0 to 31** are Intel-reserved; **Types 32 to 255** are user-available.
- [x] The **8259A PIC** expands the single INTR pin into **8 (or 64 cascaded) IRQ lines**.
- [x] The 8259A places the interrupt type number on the data bus **during the INTA pulse**.
- [x] Priority order (high to low): **Exceptions → NMI → INTR → Single Step**.
- [x] **TF (Trap Flag)** enables single-step debugging; cleared automatically on ISR entry.
- [x] `INT 3` is a **1-byte** breakpoint instruction (opcode `0CCH`); `INT n` is 2 bytes.
- [x] `INTO` (Interrupt on Overflow) is triggered **only when OF = 1** and calls Type 4 ISR.
- [x] State save (FLAGS + CS + IP) consumes **6 bytes** of stack space per interrupt.
- [x] For KTU problems: when asked for "vector address," compute `n × 4` and state both IP and CS storage locations separately.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Byte and String Manipulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCIT404 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Byte and String Manipulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!NOTE]
> **Byte Manipulation** refers to a category of 8086 microprocessor instructions that operate on 8-bit data elements, performing operations such as data transfer (`MOV`, `XCHG`), data extension (`MOVZX`, `MOVSX`), flag register exchange (`LAHF`, `SAHF`), and table lookup translation (`XLATB`).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **String Manipulation** in the 8086 architecture is defined as a special class of instructions that operate on sequences of bytes or words located in memory, using dedicated pointer registers (**SI** – Source Index and **DI** – Destination Index) and segment registers (**DS** and **ES**). These instructions process data elements one at a time and automatically update the pointer registers and count register (**CX**) when combined with repeat prefixes.

The five primitive string instructions defined by Intel for the 8086 are:
1. `MOVS` – Move String
2. `LODS` – Load String
3. `STOS` – Store String
4. `CMPS` – Compare String
5. `SCAS` – Scan String

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **The Postal Worker Analogy** ✉️
>
> Imagine a **postal worker** copying letters from one tray (Source) to another (Destination).
> - The **Source Index (SI)** is like the worker's **finger pointing to the current letter to pick up** in the source tray.
> - The **Destination Index (DI)** is the **finger pointing to where to drop the next letter** in the destination tray.
> - After copying each letter, the worker **automatically moves both fingers** to the next slot (auto-increment or auto-decrement).
> - The **CX register** is the **counter** that keeps track of how many letters remain to be copied.
> - The **REP prefix** is the supervisor who says *"keep going until the counter hits zero!"*
>
> **Byte manipulation**, on the other hand, is like handling **one single envelope at a time** — opening it, sealing it, or moving it between desks manually. It's the "fine-grained" control, while string manipulation is the "bulk processing" mechanism.

## 1.3 Key Registers and Their Roles

| Register | Role in String Operations | Default Segment |
|----------|--------------------------|-----------------|
| **SI** (Source Index) | Points to source byte/word | **DS** |
| **DI** (Destination Index) | Points to destination byte/word | **ES** |
| **CX** | Counter for REP prefix | — |
| **AL** / **AX** | Accumulator (used by LODS, STOS, SCAS) | — |
| **Flags register** | DF (Direction Flag) controls pointer update direction | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Direction Flag (DF) is the Steering Wheel** of string operations:
> - `CLD` instruction → **DF = 0** → pointers **auto-increment** (move forward) ➡️
> - `STD` instruction → **DF = 1** → pointers **auto-decrement** (move backward) ⬅️

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Byte Manipulation Instructions — Detailed Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Data Transfer Instructions

> **`MOV destination, source`** — The fundamental data transfer instruction.
> - Syntax: `MOV reg/mem/seg, reg/mem/seg/imm`
> - Copies a byte or word from source to destination.
> - Does **not** affect any flags.
> - **Restriction:** Memory-to-memory `MOV` is **not allowed** in 8086 (must go through a register).

> **`XCHG destination, source`** — Atomic swap operation.
> - Syntax: `XCHG reg, reg/mem` or `XCHG mem, reg`
> - Exchanges the contents of two operands in a **single, uninterruptible bus cycle**.
> - **Special form:** `XCHG AX, reg16` has a shorter opcode (1 byte) for accumulator-based exchange.

### 2.1.2 Sign and Zero Extension Instructions (80188+)

> **`MOVZX destination, source`** — Zero-extends a smaller value into a larger one.
> - Fills the upper bits with **0s**.
> - Used when copying an unsigned smaller value to a larger register.

> **`MOVSX destination, source`** — Sign-extends a smaller value.
> - Fills the upper bits by **replicating the sign bit** of the source.
> - Used when copying a signed smaller value to a larger register.

### 2.1.3 Flag Manipulation Instructions

> **`LAHF`** — **L**oad **AH** from **F**lags.
> - Copies bits 0–7 of the Flags register into AH.
> - Format: `AH ← SF:ZF:0:AF:0:PF:1:CF`

> **`SAHF`** — **S**tore **AH** into **F**lags.
> - Inverse of LAHF: copies AH back to flags bits 0–7.
> - Modifies SF, ZF, AF, PF, CF based on AH contents.

### 2.1.4 Table Lookup Instruction

> **`XLAT` / `XLATB`** — Translate byte using lookup table.
> - Replaces AL with the byte at memory address `[BX + AL]`.
> - Effective address: `EA = BX + AL`
> - Default segment is **DS**.
> - Extremely efficient for character code conversions (e.g., ASCII ↔ EBCDIC).

## 2.2 String Manipulation Instructions — Theoretical Foundation

### 2.2.1 Operational Model

Every string instruction follows the same **three-phase atomic model**:

1. **Read** the source operand (from `DS:SI`).
2. **Perform** the operation (move, compare, scan, etc.).
3. **Update** SI and DI by ±1 (byte) or ±2 (word), based on the Direction Flag (DF).

### 2.2.2 The Five Primitive String Instructions

| Mnemonic | Byte Form | Word Form | Operation | Source | Destination |
|----------|-----------|-----------|-----------|--------|-------------|
| `MOVS` | `MOVSB` | `MOVSW` | Copy source → destination | `DS:SI` | `ES:DI` |
| `LODS` | `LODSB` | `LODSW` | Load source → AL/AX | `DS:SI` | AL or AX |
| `STOS` | `STOSB` | `STOSW` | Store AL/AX → destination | AL or AX | `ES:DI` |
| `CMPS` | `CMPSB` | `CMPSW` | Compare source vs destination | `DS:SI` | `ES:DI` |
| `SCAS` | `SCASB` | `SCASW` | Scan: compare AL/AX with destination | AL or AX | `ES:DI` |

### 2.2.3 Repeat Prefixes — The Iteration Engine

| Prefix | Termination Condition | Used With |
|--------|----------------------|-----------|
| `REP` | `CX = 0` | `MOVS`, `STOS`, `LODS` |
| `REPE` / `REPZ` | `CX = 0` **OR** `ZF = 0` | `CMPS`, `SCAS` |
| `REPNE` / `REPNZ` | `CX = 0` **OR** `ZF = 1` | `CMPS`, `SCAS` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" behind automatic pointer updates:** The 8086 designers needed a way to process arrays and memory blocks efficiently without writing explicit loops in assembly. By hard-wiring the auto-update of SI and DI into the microcode, they achieved **single-instruction iteration** — a hallmark of CISC design philosophy.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Boundary |
|---------|----------------|------------------|
| Physical Address (8086) | `PA = (Segment Base × 16) + Offset` | 20-bit address (1 MB) |
| Source Pointer Update (byte, DF=0) | `SI ← SI + 1` | After each MOVSB/LODSB/etc. |
| Source Pointer Update (byte, DF=1) | `SI ← SI − 1` | Cannot go below `0000h` |
| Dest Pointer Update (word, DF=0) | `DI ← DI + 2` | Word must be at even offset |
| Dest Pointer Update (word, DF=1) | `DI ← DI − 2` | Word must be at even offset |
| Count Decrement | `CX ← CX − 1` | Only when prefix is used |
| `XLATB` Effective Address | `EA = BX + AL` | 16-bit offset within DS |
| `LAHF` Bit Mapping | `AH ← SF:ZF:0:AF:0:PF:1:CF` | Bits 7→0 |
| `MOVSX` Upper Fill | Upper bits = sign bit of source | Sign extension rule |
| `MOVZX` Upper Fill | Upper bits = `0` | Zero extension rule |
| Block Copy Time | `T = N × T_cycle` cycles | For `N` bytes via `REP MOVSB` |
| Alignment Penalty | `0` cycles if word aligned | Even offset required for `MOVSW` |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Where Byte and String Manipulation is used in the real world:**
> - **Bootloader code** in BIOS/UEFI uses `REP MOVSB` to copy the bootloader from ROM to RAM.
> - **Text editors** use `SCASB` to search for specific characters in document buffers.
> - **Network stacks** use `CMPSB` to compare packet headers against protocol patterns.
> - **Display drivers** use `STOSW` to rapidly clear or fill video framebuffers.
> - **Encryption routines** use `XLATB` to perform substitution ciphers (e.g., S-boxes in DES).
> - **String libraries** in C (`memcpy`, `memset`, `strcmp`) are typically implemented in assembly using exactly these instructions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Example 1: Block Copy Using `REP MOVSB`

### Problem Statement
Copy a block of **100 bytes** from a source buffer at `DS:2000h` to a destination buffer at `ES:3000h`, moving **forward** through memory.

### Step-by-Step Assembly Solution

```asm
        MOV  AX, 2000h       ; Load source segment base
        MOV  DS, AX          ; DS ← 2000h
        MOV  SI, 0000h       ; SI ← source offset 0000h

        MOV  AX, 3000h       ; Load destination segment base
        MOV  ES, AX          ; ES ← 3000h
        MOV  DI, 0000h       ; DI ← destination offset 0000h

        MOV  CX, 100         ; CX ← count (100 bytes to move)
        CLD                  ; DF ← 0 (auto-increment)
        REP  MOVSB           ; Repeat MOVSB until CX = 0
```

### Detailed Execution Trace (First 3 Iterations)

> **Step 1 — Initial Register State**
>
> $$\begin{aligned} \text{DS} &= 2000\text{h}, \quad \text{ES} = 3000\text{h} \\ \text{SI} &= 0000\text{h}, \quad \text{DI} = 0000\text{h}, \quad \text{CX} = 0064\text{h} \\ \text{DF} &= 0 \end{aligned}$$

> **Step 2 — First Iteration of `REP MOVSB`**
>
> $$\begin{aligned} \text{Temp} &\leftarrow \text{Memory}[2000\text{h} : 0000\text{h}] \quad \text{(read source byte)} \\ \text{Memory}[3000\text{h} : 0000\text{h}] &\leftarrow \text{Temp} \quad \text{(write destination byte)} \\ \text{SI} &\leftarrow \text{SI} + 1 = 0001\text{h} \\ \text{DI} &\leftarrow \text{DI} + 1 = 0001\text{h} \\ \text{CX} &\leftarrow \text{CX} - 1 = 0063\text{h} \end{aligned}$$

> **Step 3 — Second Iteration**
>
> $$\begin{aligned} \text{Memory}[3000\text{h} : 0001\text{h}] &\leftarrow \text{Memory}[2000\text{h} : 0001\text{h}] \\ \text{SI} &\leftarrow 0002\text{h}, \quad \text{DI} = 0002\text{h}, \quad \text{CX} = 0062\text{h} \end{aligned}$$

> **Step 4 — Termination**
>
> $$\text{Loop exits when } \text{CX} = 0 \Rightarrow 100 \text{ iterations completed, all 100 bytes copied.}$$

### Total Physical Address Range Covered

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Source Range} &: [2000\text{h} \times 16 + 0000\text{h},\ 2000\text{h} \times 16 + 0063\text{h}] \\ &= [20000\text{h},\ 20063\text{h}] \\ \text{Destination Range} &: [3000\text{h} \times 16 + 0000\text{h},\ 3000\text{h} \times 16 + 0063\text{h}] \\ &= [30000\text{h},\ 30063\text{h}] \end{aligned}$$

## 3.2 Example 2: String Comparison Using `REPE CMPSB`

### Problem Statement
Compare two strings of up to **20 characters** to determine if they are equal. Stop early if a mismatch is found.

### Assembly Code

```asm
        MOV  AX, 2000h
        MOV  DS, AX          ; Source segment
        MOV  SI, 0100h       ; Source offset

        MOV  AX, 3000h
        MOV  ES, AX          ; Destination segment
        MOV  DI, 0200h       ; Destination offset

        MOV  CX, 20          ; Max 20 characters
        CLD                  ; Auto-increment
        REPE CMPSB           ; Repeat while equal AND CX > 0
        JCXZ NOT_EQUAL       ; If CX = 0 and all matched, still need to check ZF
        JNZ  NOT_EQUAL       ; ZF = 0 means mismatch found
        ; ... strings are equal
NOT_EQUAL:
        ; ... strings differ
```

### Logical Trace Table

> **Termination Logic Derivation:**
>
> $$\text{Continue iteration} \iff \text{CX} \neq 0 \ \textbf{AND}\ \text{ZF} = 1$$
>
> | Iteration | `CX` before | `CX` after | `ZF` after | Action |
> |-----------|-------------|------------|------------|--------|
> | 1 | 20 | 19 | 1 | Continue (match) |
> | 2 | 19 | 18 | 1 | Continue (match) |
> | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... |
> | k | 21−k | 20−k | 0 | **STOP** (mismatch) |
>
> Final determination: `ZF = 1` after loop → strings **identical** (assuming lengths equal); `ZF = 0` → mismatch at the point where SI and DI point.

## 3.3 Example 3: Sign Extension Demonstration with `MOVSX`

### Problem Derivation
Suppose `BL = 8Ah` (`10001010` in binary). We want to copy this signed byte into `AX`, preserving the sign.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{BL} &= 1000\,1010_2 \quad \text{(sign bit} = 1 \Rightarrow \text{negative number)} \\ \text{BL as signed} &= -128 + 8 + 2 = -118_{10} \end{aligned}$$

Using **`MOVSX AX, BL`**:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{AX after} &= 1111\,1111\,1000\,1010_2 \\ &= \text{FF8Ah} \\ \text{AX as signed 16-bit} &= -128 \times 256 + (128 \times 0 + ...) \\ \end{aligned}$$

Verification of sign preservation:

$$\text{FF8Ah in 2's complement 16-bit} = -118_{10} \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> If we had used `MOVZX AX, BL` instead, the result would be `008Ah = +138_{10}` — the negative sign would be **lost** (interpreted as a large positive number). This is the critical difference students must understand.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Flag & Register Behavior Summary

| Instruction | SI updates? | DI updates? | CX updates? | Flags affected | Direction controlled by |
|-------------|:-----------:|:-----------:|:-----------:|----------------|-------------------------|
| `MOVSB` (no prefix) | ✅ (±1) | ✅ (±1) | ❌ | None | DF |
| `REP MOVSB` | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ (decrement) | None | DF |
| `LODSB` | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | None | DF |
| `REP LODSB` | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ | None | DF |
| `STOSB` | ❌ | ✅ (±1) | ❌ | None | DF |
| `REP STOSB` | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | None | DF |
| `CMPSB` | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | CF, PF, AF, ZF, SF, OF | DF |
| `REPE CMPSB` | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | CF, PF, AF, ZF, SF, OF | DF |
| `SCASB` | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ | CF, PF, AF, ZF, SF, OF | DF |
| `XLATB` | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | None | N/A |
| `MOVZX` | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | None | N/A |
| `MOVSX` | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | None | N/A |
| `LAHF` | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | None (reads flags) | N/A |
| `SAHF` | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | SF, ZF, AF, PF, CF | N/A |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory aid:** Only the **two-operand string instructions** (`MOVS`, `CMPS`) update **both** SI and DI. The **single-operand** ones (`LODS` updates only SI, `STOS` and `SCAS` update only DI) reflect their single-source/single-destination nature.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: String Operation Data Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DS Segment Base 16-bit] --> B[20-bit Address Bus Builder]
    C[SI Source Index 16-bit] --> B
    B --> D[Physical Source Address]
    D --> E[Memory Read Byte or Word]
    E --> F[Internal Data Bus]
    F --> G[Operation Logic Unit]
    G --> H[AL Accumulator 8 or 16-bit]
    H --> I[Internal Data Bus]
    I --> J[Memory Write Logic]
    J --> K[ES Segment Base 16-bit]
    L[DI Destination Index 16-bit] --> K2[20-bit Address Bus Builder 2]
    K --> K2
    K2 --> M[Physical Destination Address]
    M --> J
    N[Direction Flag DF] --> O[Pointer Update Logic]
    P[CLD or STD Instruction] --> N
    E --> O
    J --> O
    O --> C
    O --> L
    Q[CX Counter Register] --> R[REP Prefix Decrementer]
    R --> C
    S[REP REPE REPNE Prefix] --> R
```

## 4.2 Flowchart: `REP MOVSB` Execution Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start1([Program Start]) --> init1[Initialize DS ES SI DI CX]
    init1 --> cld1[CLD - Clear Direction Flag]
    cld1 --> rep1[Execute REP MOVSB]
    rep1 --> check1{CX equals 0?}
    check1 -- No --> exec1[Read byte from DS:SI]
    exec1 --> write1[Write byte to ES:DI]
    write1 --> upd1[SI = SI plus or minus 1]
    write1 --> upd2[DI = DI plus or minus 1]
    upd1 --> dec1[CX = CX minus 1]
    upd2 --> dec1
    dec1 --> rep1
    check1 -- Yes --> end1([Block Copy Complete])
```

## 4.3 Decision Flow: `REPE CMPSB` vs `REPNE SCASB` Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start2([Begin String Compare or Scan]) --> chkcx{CX = 0?}
    chkcx -- Yes --> stop2([Exit Loop - CX exhausted])
    chkcx -- No --> opfetch[Fetch and Compare or Scan Operand]
    opfetch --> update[Update SI and or DI by 1 or 2]
    update --> dec2[CX = CX - 1]
    dec2 --> setflags[Update Flags: ZF SF CF OF etc]
    setflags --> chkz{ZF = 1?}
    chkz -- Yes for REPE --> chkcx
    chkz -- No for REPE --> stopz([Exit Loop - Mismatch Found])
    setflags --> chknz{ZF = 0?}
    chknz -- Yes for REPNE --> chkcx
    chknz -- No for REPNE --> stopnz([Exit Loop - Match Found])
```

## 4.4 Byte Manipulation Pipeline Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src[Source Operand 8-bit] --> mover[Instruction Decoder]
    mover --> mov1[MOV - Direct Copy]
    mover --> xchg1[XCHG - Atomic Swap]
    mover --> movzx1[MOVZX - Zero Extend to 16 or 32]
    mover --> movsx1[MOVSX - Sign Extend to 16 or 32]
    mover --> lahf1[LAHF - Copy Flags to AH]
    mover --> sahf1[SAHF - Copy AH to Flags]
    mover --> xlat1[XLATB - BX + AL Lookup]
    bxref[Register BX] --> xlat1
    alref[Register AL] --> xlat1
    xlat1 --> tabmem[DS:BX + AL Memory Location]
    tabmem --> alref
    mov1 --> dst[Destination Operand 8 or 16-bit]
    xchg1 --> dst
    movzx1 --> dst
    movsx1 --> dst
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to draw a diagram for this topic, examiners typically expect a **block diagram showing DS:SI → 8086 → ES:DI data flow** with the **Direction Flag** clearly marked as the controller of pointer updates. The diagrams above are presentation-ready for board exams.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> *Explain the role of the Direction Flag (DF) in 8086 string instructions. What instructions are used to set and clear this flag?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Direction Flag (DF)** is a single-bit flag in the 8086 Flags Register that controls the **automatic update direction** of the SI and DI pointer registers during string operations.

- When **DF = 0** (cleared), the pointers **auto-increment** (SI and DI increase by 1 for bytes, 2 for words) after each operation, processing memory **left-to-right** (lower to higher addresses).
- When **DF = 1** (set), the pointers **auto-decrement** (SI and DI decrease), processing memory **right-to-left** (higher to lower addresses).

The two instructions that control DF are:
- **`CLD`** (Clear Direction Flag) → sets DF = 0 → auto-increment mode.
- **`STD`** (Set Direction Flag) → sets DF = 1 → auto-decrement mode.

> **Marking Note:** Award 1 mark for the definition, 1 mark for the two directional behaviors, and 1 mark for `CLD` and `STD` mnemonics.

---

### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
> *Differentiate between `MOVSB` and `CMPSB` instructions in 8086 with respect to their operation, registers used, and flags affected.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | `MOVSB` | `CMPSB` |
|-----------|---------|---------|
| **Operation** | Copies a byte from source to destination | Subtracts source byte from destination byte (without storing result) |
| **Source** | `DS:SI` | `DS:SI` |
| **Destination** | `ES:DI` | `ES:DI` |
| **Flags Affected** | **None** (data transfer instruction) | **All six** status flags: CF, PF, AF, ZF, SF, OF |
| **Typical Use** | Block copy (`REP MOVSB`) | String comparison (`REPE CMPSB`) |

> **Marking Note:** 1 mark for operation difference, 1 mark for register similarity/difference, 1 mark for flag behavior distinction.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — String Block Operations + Sign Extension

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 / Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
>
> **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the 8086 string instructions `MOVSB`, `STOSB`, and `CMPSB` in detail. List the repeat prefixes used with each and explain their termination conditions. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Differentiate between `MOVSX` and `MOVZX` instructions with suitable examples. Write an 8086 assembly program to copy a signed byte array of 50 elements from one location to another using the appropriate extension instruction. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks

**`MOVSB` (Move String Byte):**
- **Operation:** Copies a single byte from memory location pointed to by `DS:SI` to the memory location pointed to by `ES:DI`.
- **Algorithm:**
  1. `temp ← Memory[DS:SI]`
  2. `Memory[ES:DI] ← temp`
  3. `SI ← SI ± 1`, `DI ← DI ± 1` (sign depends on DF)
- **Flags affected:** None.
- **Repeat prefix:** `REP` — repeats while `CX ≠ 0`.

**`STOSB` (Store String Byte):**
- **Operation:** Stores the contents of the `AL` register into the memory location pointed to by `ES:DI`.
- **Algorithm:**
  1. `Memory[ES:DI] ← AL`
  2. `DI ← DI ± 1`
- **Flags affected:** None.
- **Repeat prefix:** `REP` — repeats while `CX ≠ 0`. Commonly used to **initialize a buffer** (e.g., clear a region to zeros).

**`CMPSB` (Compare String Byte):**
- **Operation:** Compares the byte at `DS:SI` with the byte at `ES:DI` by performing an internal subtraction (`[DS:SI] − [ES:DI]`) **without storing the result**.
- **Flags affected:** **All six** status flags (CF, PF, AF, ZF, SF, OF).
- **Repeat prefixes:** `REPE`/`REPZ` (repeat while equal) and `REPNE`/`REPNZ` (repeat while not equal).

**Termination Condition Summary:**

| Instruction | Prefix | Continues while | Exits when |
|-------------|--------|-----------------|------------|
| `MOVSB` | `REP` | `CX ≠ 0` | `CX = 0` |
| `STOSB` | `REP` | `CX ≠ 0` | `CX = 0` |
| `CMPSB` | `REPE` | `CX ≠ 0` AND `ZF = 1` | `CX = 0` OR `ZF = 0` |
| `CMPSB` | `REPNE` | `CX ≠ 0` AND `ZF = 0` | `CX = 0` OR `ZF = 1` |

**Diagrammatic Representation:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DS:SI Byte] --> C[Internal Compare or Copy Logic]
    B[ES:DI Byte] --> C
    C --> D[Result to Memory or Flags]
    D --> E[Update SI by plus or minus 1]
    D --> F[Update DI by plus or minus 1]
    D --> G[Update CX if REP used]
    H[DF Flag] --> E
    H --> F
```

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Difference between `MOVSX` and `MOVZX`:**

| Feature | `MOVSX` (Move with Sign Extension) | `MOVZX` (Move with Zero Extension) |
|---------|-----------------------------------|------------------------------------|
| **Purpose** | Preserves the **sign** of the source | Preserves the **magnitude** (treats source as unsigned) |
| **Upper bits** | Filled with **copies of the sign bit** | Filled with **zeros** |
| **Use case** | Extending a **signed** 8-bit to 16-bit | Extending an **unsigned** 8-bit to 16-bit |
| **Example** | `MOVSX AX, BL` with `BL=8Ah` → `AX=FF8Ah` | `MOVZX AX, BL` with `BL=8Ah` → `AX=008Ah` |

**Example Demonstration:**

$$\begin{aligned} \text{BL} &= 8\text{Ah} = 1000\,1010_2 \\ \text{MOVSX AX, BL} \Rightarrow \text{AX} &= 1111\,1111\,1000\,1010_2 = \text{FF8Ah} \\ \text{MOVZX AX, BL} \Rightarrow \text{AX} &= 0000\,0000\,1000\,1010_2 = 008\text{Ah} \end{aligned}$$

**Assembly Program — Copy signed byte array of 50 elements:**

```asm
        ; ----- ASSUME DS:DATA contains source array -----
        ; ----- ES:DATA+100 contains destination array -----
        MOV  AX, @DATA
        MOV  DS, AX
        MOV  ES, AX

        MOV  SI, OFFSET SRC_ARR   ; SI ← source offset
        MOV  DI, OFFSET DST_ARR   ; DI ← destination offset
        MOV  CX, 50               ; Counter = 50 elements
        CLD                       ; Auto-increment mode

NEXT:   LODSB                     ; AL ← [DS:SI], SI++
        MOVSX BX, AL              ; Sign-extend AL into BX
        XCHG AX, BX               ; Swap: AL ← sign-extended byte
        STOSB                     ; [ES:DI] ← AL, DI++
        LOOP NEXT                 ; Decrement CX, loop if CX ≠ 0

        HLT

SRC_ARR DB  80h, 7Fh, 0FFh, 01h, 0F0h, ...   ; 50 signed bytes
DST_ARR DB  50 DUP(?)
```

**Explanation (for board exam):**
1. `LODSB` reads each signed byte from source into `AL`.
2. `MOVSX BX, AL` sign-extends the 8-bit value into a 16-bit value in `BX`.
3. We swap using `XCHG` so the sign-extended low byte is placed in `AL` for storage.
4. `STOSB` stores the byte into the destination buffer.
5. `LOOP` automatically decrements `CX` and jumps until `CX = 0`.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Complete String Manipulation Programming Problem

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2023 / Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the 8086 `XLAT`/`XLATB` instruction. Write an assembly program that converts a lowercase ASCII string to uppercase using a 256-byte lookup table. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Write an 8086 assembly language program to **scan a 100-byte string for the character 'M' (4Dh)** using the `SCASB` instruction. After the scan, indicate the position where 'M' was found (or report "not found"). *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks

**`XLAT` / `XLATB` Instruction Explanation:**

- `XLAT` (or its explicit form `XLATB`) is a **table lookup translation** instruction.
- It replaces the byte in the `AL` register with a byte from a lookup table located in memory.
- The effective address of the lookup is computed as: `EA = BX + AL`.
- Default segment is **DS**.
- **No flags** are affected.
- **Use case:** Efficient code conversions, character translations (e.g., lowercase ↔ uppercase, ASCII ↔ EBCDIC), S-box implementations in cryptography.

**Lookup Table Concept:**

For lowercase to uppercase conversion in ASCII, the rule is:
- Lowercase letters are in range `61h` to `7Ah` ('a' to 'z').
- Uppercase letters are in range `41h` to `5Ah` ('A' to 'Z').
- Conversion formula: `uppercase = lowercase − 20h`.

**Assembly Program — Convert lowercase to uppercase using `XLATB`:**

```asm
        ; ----- Build the 256-byte translation table in memory -----
        ; Build procedure:
        ;   For i = 0 to 255:
        ;       TABLE[i] = i  (identity)
        ;   For i = 'a' to 'z':
        ;       TABLE[i] = i - 20h

        MOV  AX, @DATA
        MOV  DS, AX
        MOV  ES, AX

        ; Assume DS:TABLE is 256 bytes, DS:STRING is N bytes
        LEA  BX, TABLE           ; BX ← base of lookup table
        MOV  SI, OFFSET STRING   ; SI ← start of input string
        MOV  DI, SI              ; DI ← same (in-place conversion)
        MOV  CX, N               ; CX ← string length
        CLD                      ; Auto-increment

CONV:   LODSB                    ; AL ← next char from STRING
        XLATB                    ; AL ← TABLE[BX + AL] (translated byte)
        STOSB                    ; [ES:DI] ← AL, DI++
        LOOP CONV                ; Repeat CX times
        HLT

STRING  DB  'helloWorld', 0     ; Example input
N       EQU  $ - STRING
TABLE   DB  256 DUP(?)          ; 256-byte lookup table (build at runtime)
```

**Lookup Table Logic (Derivative):**

$$\text{TABLE}[i] = \begin{cases} i - 20\text{h} & \text{if } 61\text{h} \leq i \leq 7\text{Ah} \text{ (lowercase letter)} \\ i & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

> **Marking Note:** 2 marks for `XLATB` explanation, 2 marks for the table concept, 3 marks for complete assembly program with comments.

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Problem:** Scan a 100-byte string at offset `STRING` and locate the character `'M'` (ASCII `4Dh`).

```asm
        MOV  AX, @DATA
        MOV  ES, AX              ; ES must point to string segment

        MOV  DI, OFFSET STRING   ; DI ← start of string
        MOV  AL, 'M'             ; AL ← search character (4Dh)
        MOV  CX, 100             ; CX ← max bytes to scan
        CLD                      ; Auto-increment
        REPNE SCASB              ; Scan while AL ≠ [ES:DI]

        JE   FOUND               ; If ZF = 1, character was found
        ; --- NOT FOUND branch ---
        MOV  FLAG, 0FFh          ; Flag = FFh indicates "not found"
        JMP  DONE

FOUND:  ; DI is now PAST the matching byte (already auto-incremented)
        DEC  DI                  ; Adjust: DI now points TO the match
        MOV  BX, DI              ; BX ← address of 'M'
        SUB  BX, OFFSET STRING   ; BX ← position (0-indexed) of match
        MOV  FLAG, 00h           ; Flag = 0 indicates "found"
        MOV  POS, BX             ; Store position

DONE:   HLT

STRING  DB  100 DUP(?)
FLAG    DB  ?
POS     DW  ?
```

**Detailed Walkthrough:**

> **Step 1:** Load `ES` with the segment containing the string. `SCASB` always uses `ES:DI`.
>
> **Step 2:** Load `AL = 4Dh` (the ASCII code for `'M'`).
>
> **Step 3:** Initialize `DI` to the start of the string and `CX = 100`.
>
> **Step 4:** Execute `REPNE SCASB`:
> - At each iteration, it compares `AL` with `Memory[ES:DI]`.
> - If they **differ** (`ZF = 0`) AND `CX ≠ 0`, increment `DI` and decrement `CX`.
> - If they **match** (`ZF = 1`) OR `CX = 0`, exit the loop.
>
> **Step 5:** If `ZF = 1` after the loop, the character was found. **Important:** `DI` has already been auto-incremented past the match, so we must `DEC DI` to get the exact location.

**Positional Calculation:**

$$\text{POS} = (\text{DI after DEC}) - \text{OFFSET STRING}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting `CLD` before forward string operations.** If the previous code left `DF = 1` (from an `STD`), your pointers will decrement unexpectedly, leading to wrong answers in trace tables. **Always explicitly set DF.**
>
> 2. **Confusing `REPE` vs `REPNE` with `REP`.** `REP` works only with `MOVS`/`STOS`/`LODS` and stops at `CX = 0`. `REPE`/`REPNE` work with `CMPS`/`SCAS` and may terminate early based on ZF. Using the wrong prefix is a **2-mark deduction**.
>
> 3. **Segment override mistake for `SCASB`:** Many students set `DS` instead of `ES`. `SCASB` ALWAYS uses `ES:DI`, never `DS:SI`.
>
> 4. **Memory-to-Memory `MOV`:** Writing `MOV [SI], [DI]` is **invalid** in 8086. Must go through a register.
>
> 5. **DI post-increment trap in `SCASB`:** After finding a match, `DI` points to the **next** byte, not the match itself. Forget the `DEC DI` and your position calculation is **off-by-one** (3-mark deduction).
>
> 6. **Not sign-extending when required:** Using `MOV` instead of `MOVSX` to copy a signed byte into a 16-bit register destroys the sign. Examiners specifically test this concept.
>
> 7. **Word alignment for `MOVSW`:** If the offset is odd, the 8086 will generate a **multiple of 4 bus cycles** instead of 2, severely impacting performance and potentially causing boundary errors in tracing problems.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Byte and String Manipulation:**
>
> ☐ **String instructions ALWAYS use `DS:SI` as source and `ES:DI` as destination** (except `LODS` which only uses `DS:SI`, and `STOS`/`SCAS` which only use `ES:DI`).
>
> ☐ **`CLD` sets DF = 0 (auto-increment); `STD` sets DF = 1 (auto-decrement).**
>
> ☐ **Byte versions** (e.g., `MOVSB`) update pointers by ±1; **Word versions** (`MOVSW`) update by ±2.
>
> ☐ **`REP` prefix** is valid **only** with `MOVS`, `STOS`, and `LODS`. It terminates when `CX = 0`.
>
> ☐ **`REPE`/`REPNE` prefixes** are valid **only** with `CMPS` and `SCAS`. They terminate on `CX = 0` OR the ZF condition.
>
> ☐ **`XLATB`** computes `EA = BX + AL` and fetches the byte at that address in `DS`.
>
> ☐ **`MOVSX` vs `MOVZX`:** `MOVSX` preserves sign (upper bits = sign bit); `MOVZX` fills with zeros.
>
> ☐ **`LAHF`** loads flags bits 0–7 into `AH`; **`SAHF`** does the reverse.
>
> ☐ **`MOVSB` does NOT affect any flags; `CMPSB` and `SCASB` affect all six status flags.**
>
> ☐ **Post-scan pointer adjustment:** After `REPNE SCASB` finds a match, `DI` has been incremented, so use `DEC DI` to obtain the exact match location.
>
> ☐ **Memory-to-memory `MOV` is illegal in 8086** — must route through a register.
>
> ☐ **`XCHG AX, reg16`** has a special 1-byte opcode (efficient for accumulator swaps).
>
> ☐ **Physical address formula** (for trace problems): `PA = Segment × 16 + Offset`.

> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Mantra:** *"Direction Flag first, pointers second, count register third."* Always establish DF, then SI/DI, then CX before any string operation. Examiners love trace tables — practice drawing the register state **after each iteration** to secure full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: 8086 System Bus Structure and 8086 Signals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCIT404 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** 8086 System Bus Structure and 8086 Signals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to the 8086 Microprocessor

The **Intel 8086** is a **16-bit microprocessor** introduced by Intel in **1978** and forms the foundational platform for the entire x86 architecture family. It is a **40-pin DIP (Dual In-line Package)** IC that operates on a single **+5V DC supply**, with a clock frequency ranging typically from **5 MHz** to **10 MHz** depending on the variant (8086 = 5 MHz, 8086-2 = 8 MHz).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 8086 is a **16-bit microprocessor** with a **20-bit address bus** and **16-bit data bus**, packaged in a **40-pin DIP**. It can address **1 MB (2²⁰ bytes)** of physical memory and **64 KB (2¹⁶ bytes)** of I/O space.

## 1.2 The 8086 System Bus Structure — Formal Definition

The **System Bus Structure** of the 8086 refers to the three primary buses that interconnect the microprocessor with memory, I/O devices, and other peripherals:

| Bus Type | Width | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Address Bus (AB)** | **20-bit** (A19–A0) | Carries the physical memory/I/O address to be accessed |
| **Data Bus (DB)** | **16-bit** (D15–D0) | Carries actual data being read or written |
| **Control Bus (CB)** | Variable | Carries timing, control, and status signals |

### Key Architectural Insight: Multiplexed Buses

To accommodate 20 address lines + 16 data lines + numerous control signals within only **40 pins**, Intel used **bus multiplexing** and **status encoding**:

> [!NOTE]
> **Bus Multiplexing:** The lower **16 lines (AD15–AD0)** carry both **Address** and **Data** (time-multiplexed). The upper **4 lines (A19/S6–A16/S3)** carry both **Address** and **Status**. An external **Address Latch** (e.g., 74LS373) is required, controlled by the **ALE (Address Latch Enable)** signal, to demultiplex them.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Post Office with Limited Letter Slots"

Imagine the 8086 microprocessor as a **bustling central post office** that needs to send millions of letters (data) every second to various addresses. The post office has only **40 letter slots (pins)** but needs to handle:
- A **20-digit zip code** (address)
- A **16-character message** (data)
- Several **special instructions** like "urgent," "fragile," "registered" (control signals)

The solution? **Time-sharing the slots!**
- The first moment (T1 state), all 20 address slots show the zip code.
- A **latch (the postal clerk's notebook)** copies down the address instantly.
- The next moment (T2–T4 states), the same 16 slots now hold the actual letter (data).

The clerk's notebook is the **external latch (74LS373)**, and the command "**Copy now!**" is the **ALE signal**. The control signals (like "fragile") are status flags printed on the envelope's exterior — visible at all times.

## 1.4 Two Operating Modes: Minimum vs Maximum

The 8086 supports **two modes of operation** selected via the **MN/MX̄ pin** (Pin 33):

- **Minimum Mode (MN/MX̄ = 1):** Single processor system. The 8086 itself generates all control signals.
- **Maximum Mode (MN/MX̄ = 0):** Multi-processor/coprocessor system. An external **Bus Controller (8288)** decodes status signals (S2–S0) to generate control signals.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid:** "**M**i**n**imum = **M**i**n**i system" (one CPU, no boss); "**M**a**x**imum = **M**a**x**imum power" (multiple CPUs, 8288 boss controller).

## 1.5 Pin Count Sanity Check

$$ \text{Total Pins Required (Unmultiplexed)} = 20_{\text{addr}} + 16_{\text{data}} + 10_{\text{control}} = 46 \text{ pins} $$

$$ \text{Available Pins} = 40 \text{ pins} $$

$$ \text{Pins Saved via Multiplexing} = 20 + 4 = 24 \text{ pins} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Without multiplexing, the 8086 would have required **46 pins**. By multiplexing **AD15–AD0** and **A19/S6–A16/S3**, only **40 pins** are needed — a saving of **6 effective pins**, making the DIP package practical.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Internal Architecture of the 8086

The 8086 is internally divided into **two independent functional units** that operate in parallel — a hallmark of its **pipelined architecture**:

### A) Bus Interface Unit (BIU)
Handles **all bus operations** (fetching, reading, writing). The BIU contains:

1. **Four 16-bit Segment Registers:**
   - **CS (Code Segment)** — base of code/instruction area
   - **DS (Data Segment)** — base of data area
   - **SS (Stack Segment)** — base of stack area
   - **ES (Extra Segment)** — secondary data/strings area

2. **Instruction Pointer (IP)** — 16-bit offset within CS for next instruction.

3. **6-byte Instruction Queue (FIFO):** Prefetches up to **6 bytes** of upcoming instructions while the EU executes the current one — this is the **pipelining magic** that boosts performance.

4. **Address Generation Adder:** Computes the **20-bit physical address** using:
$$\text{Physical Address} = (\text{Segment Register} \times 10\text{H}) + \text{Effective Offset (16-bit)}$$

5. **Bus Control Logic:** Manages READ/WRITE cycles, HOLD/HLDA, READY, etc.

### B) Execution Unit (EU)
Performs **all computational and logical operations**. The EU contains:

1. **ALU (Arithmetic Logic Unit):** Performs arithmetic (+, −, ×, ÷), logical (AND, OR, XOR), and shift/rotate operations.
2. **Four 16-bit General Purpose Registers:**
   - **AX (Accumulator)** — preferred for I/O and arithmetic
   - **BX (Base)** — pointer to data in DS
   - **CX (Counter)** — counter for LOOP, string operations
   - **DX (Data)** — holds port address for IN/OUT, also used in MUL/DIV
3. **Two 16-bit Pointer Registers:** **SP (Stack Pointer)** and **BP (Base Pointer)**
4. **Two 16-bit Index Registers:** **SI (Source Index)** and **DI (Destination Index)**
5. **Flag Register (FR)** — 16-bit, but only **9 flags** are active (6 status + 3 control)
6. **Control Unit (CU):** Decodes opcodes and generates control signals for EU.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why split into BIU and EU?** They work **in parallel**. While the EU is *executing* instruction N, the BIU is *fetching* instruction N+1. This **overlapping of fetch and execute** is called **pipelining** and makes the 8086 significantly faster than its predecessor, the 8085.

## 2.2 Physical Address Calculation — The Core Formula

The 8086 uses a **segmented memory model**. Every memory access uses a **segment:offset** pair:

### KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Physical Address | $PA = (\text{Segment} \times 10\text{H}) + \text{Offset}$ | 20-bit (00000H – FFFFFH) |
| 2 | Segment Shift | Segment register shifted left by **4 bits** (×16) | Effective address base |
| 3 | Memory Capacity | $2^{20}$ | **1 MB (1,048,576 bytes)** |
