| 4 | I/O Capacity | $2^{16}$ | **64 KB (65,536 ports)** |
| 5 | Segment Size | $2^{16}$ | **64 KB per segment** |
| 6 | Total Segments | $2^{20} / 2^{16} = 2^{4}$ | **16 overlapping segments** |
| 7 | Overlap | New segment starts every **16 bytes** | **Paragraph boundary** |
| 8 | Reset Vector | $PA = CS \times 10\text{H} + IP = \text{FFFF0H}$ | Always **physical FFFF0H** |
| 9 | Stack Operation | $PA = SS \times 10\text{H} + SP$ | SS:SP pair |
| 10 | Default Access | Code→CS:IP, Data→DS, Stack→SS:SP | Implicit registers |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Rule:** Both **Segment** and **Offset** are **16-bit unsigned values**. The multiplication by **10H (16 in decimal)** is equivalent to a **left shift by 4 bits** (binary).

### Worked Example (KTU Board Pattern):
> Given: **CS = 2000H**, **IP = 0400H**. Find the physical address of the next instruction.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PA} &= (\text{CS} \times 10\text{H}) + \text{IP} \\
&= (2000\text{H} \times 10\text{H}) + 0400\text{H} \\
&= 20000\text{H} + 0400\text{H} \\
&= 20400\text{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Answer: 20400H** ✓

## 2.3 The 8086 Signal Groupings — Complete Reference

The 8086 has **40 pins** grouped functionally as follows:

### Group 1: Power & Clock (3 pins)
- **Pin 40:** Vcc = **+5V DC**
- **Pin 1, 20:** GND (two grounds for noise reduction)
- **Pin 19:** **CLK** — Clock input, 33% duty cycle (low:high = 1:2 or 1:3)

### Group 2: Address/Data Bus Multiplexed (20 pins)
- **Pins 2–16, 39:** **AD15–AD0** — Multiplexed Address/Data
- **Pins 35–38:** **A19/S6, A18/S5, A17/S4, A16/S3** — Multiplexed Address/Status

### Group 3: Address/Control (3 pins)
- **Pin 34:** **BHĒ/S7** — Bus High Enable / Status (active LOW)
- **Pin 32:** **RD̄** — Read (active LOW)
- **Pin 21 (Max Mode):** **RQ̄/ḠT̄0**, replaced by **HOLD** in Min Mode (Pin 31)
- **Pin 30 (Max Mode):** **RQ̄/ḠT̄1**, replaced by **HLDA** in Min Mode (Pin 30)

### Group 4: Control Signals (Common & Mode-dependent)

| Signal | Pin | Min Mode | Max Mode | Active Level |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **M/IŌ** | 28 | Memory/IO select | M/IŌ | High = Memory, Low = I/O |
| **WR̄** | 29 | Write | (via 8288) | LOW |
| **ALE** | 25 | Address Latch Enable | ALE | HIGH pulse |
| **DEN̄** | 26 | Data Enable | (via 8288) | LOW |
| **DT/R̄** | 27 | Data Transmit/Receive | (via 8288) | High = CPU sends, Low = CPU receives |
| **S2̄, S1̄, S0̄** | 28, 27, 26 | (Not used) | Status to 8288 | Encoded |
| **LOCK̄** | 29 | (Not used) | Bus lock | LOW |
| **QS1, QS0** | 24, 25 | (Not used) | Queue Status | Output to 8087 |
| **READY** | 22 | Wait state insert | Wait state insert | HIGH = ready |

### Group 5: Interrupt Signals (4 pins)
- **Pin 18:** **INTR** — Maskable Interrupt Request (level-sensitive)
- **Pin 17:** **NMI** — Non-Maskable Interrupt (edge-triggered, vector 2)
- **Pin 24:** **INTĀ** — Interrupt Acknowledge (active LOW, output)
- **Pin 23:** **TEST̄** — Wait for coprocessor (active LOW, input)

### Group 6: Reset & Mode
- **Pin 21:** **RESET** — Resets CPU (must be HIGH for ≥4 CLK cycles)
- **Pin 33:** **MN/MX̄** — Mode Select (HIGH = Min, LOW = Max)

### Group 7: DMA (Min Mode only)
- **Pin 31:** **HOLD** — DMA request (input, HIGH)
- **Pin 30:** **HLDA** — Hold Acknowledge (output, HIGH)

## 2.4 The BHĒ Signal and Odd/Even Banking

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **BHĒ (Bus High Enable, Pin 34, Active LOW):** This signal enables the **upper 8 bits (D15–D8)** of the data bus. Combined with **A0**, it allows the 8086 to access **8-bit and 16-bit** devices efficiently:

| BHĒ | A0 | Operation | Bus Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | **Word (16-bit)** access — aligned | D15–D0 |
| 0 | 1 | **Odd byte** (upper bank) | D15–D8 |
| 1 | 0 | **Even byte** (lower bank) | D7–D0 |
| 1 | 1 | **Idle** (no bank selected) | — |

This gives the 8086 a clever **memory banking scheme**:
- **Even bank** = connected to D7–D0, selected by A0 = 0
- **Odd bank** = connected to D15–D8, selected by BHĒ = 0

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Embedded System Design:** The 8086's bus structure is the grandparent of every modern CPU's system bus (PCIe, AXI, etc.).
- **Industrial Control:** Understanding multiplexing helps design **bus demultiplexing circuits** using latches.
- **Memory Interfacing:** BHĒ/A0 banking is critical when interfacing **8-bit EPROMs** to a 16-bit bus.
- **Multiprocessor Systems:** RQ/GT signals enable **tightly-coupled multiprocessing** (8086 + 8087 NDP + 8089 IOP).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Why the Physical Address is 20-bit

The 8086 has 20 address lines. To compute the 20-bit physical address from two 16-bit values (segment and offset), the 8086 uses a dedicated **20-bit adder** inside the BIU.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Let Segment} &= S_{15}S_{14} \dots S_1 S_0 \quad (16 \text{-bit binary}) \\
\text{Shift left by 4 bits:} \quad S \times 2^4 &= S_{15}S_{14} \dots S_0 0000 \\
\text{Add the 16-bit offset:} \quad O_{15}O_{14} \dots O_0 \\
\hline
\text{Physical Address} &= S \times 16 + O \\
&= S \times 10\text{H} + O
\end{aligned}
$$

This is implemented using a **20-bit binary adder** that takes:
- The segment register shifted left by 4 (gains 4 zero bits at LSB, becoming 20-bit)
- The 16-bit offset (zero-extended to 20-bit)

**Result:** 20-bit PA capable of addressing **2²⁰ = 1,048,576 bytes = 1 MB**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Why Multiply by 10H, Not 16H?

This is a classic **KTU question**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Segment value} &= 2000\text{H} \\
\text{If we shifted by 4 bits (multiplied by 2⁴ = 16 = 10H):} \\
2000\text{H} \times 10\text{H} &= 20000\text{H} \\
\text{If we mistakenly multiplied by 16H:} \\
2000\text{H} \times 16\text{H} &= 32000\text{H} \quad \text{(WRONG!)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write $\times 16\text{H}$ instead of $\times 10\text{H}$. The correct multiplier is **10H (= 16 decimal = 2⁴)**, representing a **4-bit left shift** in hexadecimal.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Reset Vector Address

When **RESET = 1** for ≥ 4 clock cycles:
- **CS = FFFFH**
- **IP = 0000H**
- **DS = SS = ES = 0000H**
- **Instruction Queue is flushed**
- The 8086 resumes execution from:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Reset Vector PA} &= (\text{CS} \times 10\text{H}) + \text{IP} \\
&= (\text{FFFFH} \times 10\text{H}) + 0000\text{H} \\
&= \text{FFFF0H}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The CPU always begins fetching from physical address **FFFF0H**, which is in the **top 16 bytes** of the 1 MB memory — typically a **ROM** containing the system's jump-to-bootstrap code.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Memory Banking Address Derivation

The 8086 splits its 1 MB memory into **two 512 KB banks**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Even Bank} &: \text{Connected to } D_7 - D_0, \text{ enabled by } A_0 = 0 \\
\text{Odd Bank} &: \text{Connected to } D_{15} - D_8, \text{ enabled by } \overline{BHE} = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Word access at even address (e.g., 0400H):**
- $A_0 = 0$ → Even bank enabled
- $\overline{BHE} = 0$ → Odd bank enabled
- Both banks active → **16-bit word** read in **1 bus cycle**

**Word access at odd address (e.g., 0401H):**
- 8086 performs **2 bus cycles**:
  - Cycle 1: Reads byte at 0401H from odd bank
  - Cycle 2: Reads byte at 0400H from even bank
- The 8086 internally rearranges bytes to form the 16-bit word

> [!TIP]
> **Performance Tip:** Always align 16-bit data on **even addresses** to avoid this 2-cycle penalty!

| **A19/S6 – A16/S3** | 35–38 | Output | Both | — | Multiplexed Address/Status | T1: Address; T2–T4: Status |

## 3.6 Minimum Mode vs Maximum Mode — Side-by-Side

| Aspect | Minimum Mode (MN/MX̄ = 1) | Maximum Mode (MN/MX̄ = 0) |
|---|---|---|
| **Pins reused** | HOLD/HLDA + control pins | RQ/GT0/RQ/GT1 + status pins |
| **Bus Controller** | **Not required** (CPU generates all) | **8288 required** to decode status |
| **Multiprocessing** | Not supported (single CPU) | Supported (8086 + 8087 + 8089) |
| **Control Signals** | Direct from 8086 (RD, WR, M/IO, etc.) | Generated by 8288 from S2–S0 |
| **Interrupts** | INTA directly from 8086 | INTA from 8288 |
| **Cost / Complexity** | Low — single master | Higher — 8288 + coprocessors |
| **Use Case** | Simple standalone systems | High-performance multiprocessor |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**
**Explain the bus interface unit (BIU) of 8086. List its components.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Bus Interface Unit (BIU)** is responsible for **all external bus operations** of the 8086, including instruction fetch, memory read/write, and I/O operations. It acts as the interface between the CPU's internal execution unit and the external system bus.

**Components of BIU:**
1. **Four 16-bit Segment Registers:** CS, DS, SS, ES — hold the base addresses of the four memory segments.
2. **16-bit Instruction Pointer (IP):** Holds the offset of the next instruction within the current code segment.
3. **6-byte Instruction Queue:** A FIFO buffer that **prefetches up to 6 bytes** of instructions while the EU executes the current one, enabling pipelining.
4. **20-bit Address Generation Adder:** Computes the **physical address** using: $PA = (Segment \times 10\text{H}) + \text{Offset}$.
5. **Bus Control Logic:** Generates and manages control signals (RD̄, WR̄, ALE, DEN̄, DT/R̄, etc.) and handles handshaking (READY, HOLD/HLDA).

**Key Function:** The BIU allows **fetch–execute overlap** (pipelining), making the 8086 significantly faster than the 8085. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**
**What is the function of the BHĒ signal in 8086? Explain with a truth table.**

**Model Answer:**

The **BHĒ (Bus High Enable)** signal, on **Pin 34**, is an **active-low output** that enables the **upper 8 bits (D15–D8)** of the 16-bit data bus. It is used together with **A0** to access even/odd memory banks:

| BHĒ | A0 | Type of Access | Active Bank(s) | Bus Used |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **0** | **0** | **Word (16-bit)** | Both banks | D15–D0 |
| **0** | **1** | **Odd byte** (upper) | Odd bank only | D15–D8 |
| **1** | **0** | **Even byte** (lower) | Even bank only | D7–D0 |
| **1** | **1** | **Idle** | None | — |

> This allows the 8086 to interface with both **8-bit and 16-bit** memory devices efficiently. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module 2 Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — 8086 Architecture & Bus Structure**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]**

#### (a) Draw and explain the internal architecture of 8086 microprocessor. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

The 8086 is internally divided into **two independent units** operating in parallel:

**1. Bus Interface Unit (BIU)** — Manages all external bus activities:
- **Segment Registers (CS, DS, SS, ES):** 16-bit registers holding base addresses.
- **Instruction Pointer (IP):** 16-bit offset within CS.
- **6-byte Instruction Queue:** Prefetches instructions.
- **20-bit Address Adder:** Computes $PA = (Seg \times 10\text{H}) + \text{Offset}$.
- **Bus Control Logic:** Generates RD̄, WR̄, ALE, DEN̄, DT/R̄.

**2. Execution Unit (EU)** — Performs all computations:
- **ALU:** 16-bit arithmetic, logical, shift, and rotate operations.
- **General Purpose Registers:** AX, BX, CX, DX (each divisible into two 8-bit halves: AH/AL, BH/BL, CH/CL, DH/DL).
- **Pointer Registers:** SP, BP.
- **Index Registers:** SI, DI.
- **Flag Register:** 16-bit (9 active flags — 6 status: CF, PF, AF, ZF, SF, OF; 3 control: TF, IF, DF).
- **Control Unit:** Decodes opcodes and generates timing signals.

**Pipelining Operation:**
- While EU **executes** instruction N, BIU **fetches** instruction N+1 into the queue.
- This **overlap** improves throughput and reduces average instruction time.

**Block Diagram (textual representation):**

```
        ┌─────────────── BIU ───────────────┐
   CS ─┐                                   ┌─→ AD15-AD0
   DS ─┤   ┌──────┐   ┌──────┐   ┌──────┐  │  A19-A16
   SS ─┼──→│ 20-bit│   │ 6-byte│  │ Bus  │  │  BHE, RD, WR
   ES ─┤   │ Adder│   │ Queue │  │ Ctrl │──┘  M/IO, ALE
   IP ─┘   └──────┘   └──────┘   └──────┘
                  ↕ Internal Bus ↕
        ┌─────────────── EU ────────────────┐
   AX ─┐    ┌──────┐    ┌────────────────┐
   BX ─┤    │      │    │ Flag Register  │
   CX ─┼───→│ ALU  │←───│ Control Unit   │
   DX ─┤    │      │    │                │
   SP ─┤    └──────┘    └────────────────┘
   BP ─┤         ↑
   SI ─┤         └─ Operands & Results
   DI ─┘
```

**(7 marks — full marks requires labeled diagram + explanation of pipelining)**

#### (b) Explain the physical address calculation in 8086 with a suitable example. Show how the 20-bit address is generated. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

The 8086 has a **20-bit address bus** but its registers are only **16-bit** wide. To address the full **1 MB** memory, the 8086 uses a **segment:offset** scheme:

**Physical Address Formula:**
$$\text{PA} = (\text{Segment Register} \times 10\text{H}) + \text{16-bit Offset}$$

**Mechanism:**
1. The **16-bit segment value** is shifted **left by 4 bits** (equivalent to multiplying by **10H or 16 decimal**).
2. This produces a **20-bit value** with the last 4 bits as zeros.
3. The **16-bit offset** is added to this base using a 20-bit adder.

**Worked Example:**
Let **DS = 2000H** and an instruction uses **BX = 0430H** as an effective address. Find the physical address of the data byte.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PA} &= (DS \times 10\text{H}) + BX \\
&= (2000\text{H} \times 10\text{H}) + 0430\text{H} \\
&= 20000\text{H} + 0430\text{H} \\
&= 20430\text{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Binary Verification:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DS} &= 0010\,0000\,0000\,0000_2 \\
\text{DS} \times 2^4 &= 0010\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000_2 = 20000\text{H} \\
\text{BX} &= 0000\,0100\,0011\,0000_2 = 0430\text{H} \\
\text{Sum} &= 0010\,0000\,0100\,0011\,0000_2 = 20430\text{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

> The 20-bit address **20430H** uniquely identifies the byte in the 1 MB memory. **(7 marks — formula + numerical example + binary conversion)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — 8086 Signals (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Alternative]**

#### (a) List and explain the various signals of 8086 in maximum mode. Mention the role of 8288 bus controller. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

In **maximum mode** (MN/MX̄ = 0, Pin 33 grounded), the 8086 is configured for **multiprocessor systems**. Several pins change function and an external **8288 Bus Controller** is mandatory.

**Maximum Mode Signals:**

1. **S2̄, S1̄, S0̄ (Pins 28, 27, 26) — Status Signals:**
   - These 3 output lines encode the current bus cycle type and are sent to the **8288 Bus Controller**.

| S2̄ | S1̄ | S0̄ | Cycle Type | 8288 Output |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | **Interrupt Acknowledge** | INTA |
| 0 | 0 | 1 | **I/O Read** | IORC |
| 0 | 1 | 0 | **I/O Write** | IOWC, AIOWC |
| 0 | 1 | 1 | **Halt** | — |
| 1 | 0 | 0 | **Instruction Fetch** | MRDC |
| 1 | 0 | 1 | **Memory Read** | MRDC |
| 1 | 1 | 0 | **Memory Write** | MWTC, AMWC |
| 1 | 1 | 1 | **Passive (no bus cycle)** | — |

2. **LOCK̄ (Pin 29) — Bus Lock:** Active LOW signal that prevents other processors from gaining bus control during critical operations (e.g., XCHG, INTA).

3. **QS1, QS0 (Pins 24, 25) — Queue Status:** 2 outputs that report the state of the internal 6-byte instruction queue to the **8087 NDP** (Numeric Data Processor) for synchronization.

4. **RQ̄/GT̄0 (Pin 31) and RQ̄/GT̄1 (Pin 30) — Request/Grant:** Bidirectional signals for **bus arbitration** with other processors. RQ̄/GT̄0 has **higher priority**. They use a 3-pulse handshake.

5. **Pin 21:** **RESET** (same as minimum mode).
6. **Pin 22:** **READY** (same as minimum mode).

**Role of the 8288 Bus Controller:**
- **Decodes S2–S0** status and generates the **actual control signals** (MRDC, MWTC, IORC, IOWC, AMWC, AIOWC, INTA).
- Generates **DEN** and **DT/R** for transceiver control.
- Generates **MCE/PDEN** for cascade mode in 8259A.
- Allows **8086 + 8087 + 8089** multiprocessor configurations to share the bus safely.

**(7 marks — table + 8288 explanation + multiprocessor role)**

#### (b) Differentiate between minimum and maximum mode of 8086. Explain the signals HOLD and HLDA. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Minimum vs Maximum Mode Comparison:**

| Feature | Minimum Mode (MN/MX̄ = 1) | Maximum Mode (MN/MX̄ = 0) |
|---|---|---|
| **Configuration** | Single processor system | Multi-processor system |
| **Bus Controller** | **Not required** | **8288 required** |
| **Control Signals** | Generated directly by 8086 (RD̄, WR̄, M/IŌ, ALE, DEN̄, DT/R̄) | Generated by 8288 from S2–S0 status |
| **DMA Support** | HOLD + HLDA (Pin 31, 30) | RQ̄/GT̄0, RQ̄/GT̄1 (Pin 31, 30) |
| **Interrupts** | INTA directly from 8086 (Pin 24) | INTA via 8288 |
| **Multiprocessing** | **Not supported** | **Supported** (8086 + 8087 + 8089) |
| **LOCK̄** | Not available | Available (Pin 29) |
| **Queue Status (QS1, QS0)** | Not available | Available (Pins 24, 25) |
| **Application** | Simple embedded/standalone systems | High-performance, numeric-intensive systems |

**HOLD and HLDA Signals (Minimum Mode):**

- **HOLD (Pin 31) — Input, Active HIGH:**
  - Used by external **DMA controllers** (e.g., 8237) or other masters to request **control of the system bus**.
  - When HOLD = 1, the 8086 **completes the current bus cycle**, then **releases the bus** (tristates AD, RD̄, WR̄, etc.).

- **HLDA (Hold Acknowledge, Pin 30) — Output, Active HIGH:**
  - Sent by the 8086 to **acknowledge** the HOLD request.
  - When HLDA = 1, the 8086 has **relinquished bus control** to the requesting master.
  - The 8086 **continues executing internally** (EU) using the instruction queue, but cannot access memory/IO until HOLD is removed.

**Timing Sequence:**
1. DMA controller drives HOLD = 1.
2. 8086 finishes current bus cycle.
3. 8086 sets HLDA = 1 and tristates buses.
4. DMA controller takes control of the bus.
5. When DMA is done, HOLD is de-asserted.
6. 8086 removes HLDA and resumes bus activity.

**(7 marks — table + HOLD/HLDA explanation + sequence)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Address Calculation:** Writing $2000\text{H} \times 16\text{H}$ instead of $2000\text{H} \times 10\text{H}$ — wrong by 4 bits. Always multiply by **10H** (= 16 decimal = 2⁴).
> 2. **Mode Confusion:** Saying "WR̄ is a status signal in max mode" — WR̄ is a control signal in **min mode only**; in max mode it's replaced by 8288 outputs (MWTC, IOWC).
> 3. **Pin Number Mix-ups:** HOLD = Pin 31, HLDA = Pin 30. RQ̄/GT̄0 = Pin 31, RQ̄/GT̄1 = Pin 30. Same physical pins, different modes — **HIGH-PRIORITY exam question type**.
> 4. **ALE Polarity:** ALE is **active HIGH** (not LOW). It is HIGH only during T1 of each bus cycle.
> 5. **BHĒ for Odd Address:** At an odd address, BHĒ = 0 and A0 = 1. Don't confuse this with word access (both BHĒ and A0 = 0).
> 6. **Segment × 10H vs 16H confusion:** This single mistake can cost **3–4 marks** in any address-calculation problem.
> 7. **Forgetting "MN/MX̄"** is active-LOW for maximum mode. Mnemonic: "**MAX has a bar (line on top)** because the pin is LOW to enable maximum."

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

**Architecture:**
- 8086 is **16-bit CPU** with **20-bit address bus** and **16-bit data bus** in a **40-pin DIP**.
- Internally split into **BIU** (bus + queue + address adder) and **EU** (ALU + registers + control).
- **6-byte instruction queue** enables **pipelining** (fetch–execute overlap).

**Buses:**
- **Address Bus:** 20 bits (A19–A0) → **1 MB** memory.
- **Data Bus:** 16 bits (D15–D0).
- **Control Bus:** RD̄, WR̄, M/IŌ, ALE, DEN̄, DT/R̄, BHĒ, READY, RESET, INTR, NMI, INTĀ, TEST̄, HOLD/HLDA or RQ̄/GT̄.

**Bus Multiplexing:**
- **AD15–AD0** and **A19/S6–A16/S3** are multiplexed.
- **ALE** pulse in T1 latches address into **74LS373** latches.
- **DEN̄** + **DT/R̄** control external **bidirectional transceivers** (74LS245).

**Address Calculation:**
- $PA = (\text{Seg} \times 10\text{H}) + \text{Offset}$
- **Reset Vector = FFFF0H** (CS = FFFFH, IP = 0000H).

**Modes:**
- **Minimum Mode:** MN/MX̄ = 1. Single CPU. HOLD/HLDA. Direct control signals.
- **Maximum Mode:** MN/MX̄ = 0. Multi-CPU. RQ̄/GT̄. 8288 Bus Controller required. LOCK̄, S2–S0, QS1–QS0 available.

**BHĒ and A0 Banking:**
- **BHĒ + A0 = 00** → Word access (D15–D0, 1 cycle).
- **BHĒ + A0 = 01** → Odd byte (D15–D8).
- **BHĒ + A0 = 10** → Even byte (D7–D0).
- **BHĒ + A0 = 11** → Idle.

**Key Pin Numbers (HIGH-YIELD for KTU):**
- Pin **1, 20:** GND
- Pin **19:** CLK (33% duty cycle)
- Pin **21:** RESET
- Pin **22:** READY
- Pin **23:** TEST̄
- Pin **24:** INTĀ (Min) / QS1 (Max)
- Pin **25:** ALE (Min) / QS0 (Max)
- Pin **26:** DEN̄ (Min) / S0̄ (Max)
- Pin **27:** DT/R̄ (Min) / S1̄ (Max)
- Pin **28:** M/IŌ (Min) / S2̄ (Max)
- Pin **29:** WR̄ (Min) / LOCK̄ (Max)
- Pin **30:** HLDA (Min) / RQ̄/GT̄1 (Max)
- Pin **31:** HOLD (Min) / RQ̄/GT̄0 (Max)
- Pin **32:** RD̄
- Pin **33:** MN/MX̄
- Pin **34:** BHĒ/S7
- Pin **35–38:** A19/S6 – A16/S3
- Pin **40:** Vcc (+5V)

**Must-Remember Constants:**
- **Memory:** 2²⁰ = **1,048,576 bytes = 1 MB**
- **I/O:** 2¹⁶ = **65,536 ports = 64 KB**
- **Segment size:** 2¹⁶ = **64 KB**
- **Segments in 1 MB:** 2⁴ = **16**
- **Duty cycle of CLK:** **33% (low:high = 1:2)**
- **Queue size:** **6 bytes**
- **Reset time:** **≥ 4 clock cycles**
- **Pin count:** **40 pins, DIP**
- **Power:** **+5V DC**

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** KTU frequently asks for **physical address calculation** (3–4 marks), **differences between min/max mode** (table format, 7 marks), and **BHĒ/A0 truth table** (3 marks). Master these three patterns and you cover 60%+ of Module 2 marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Configurations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCIT404 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Basic Configurations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the architecture of the **8086 microprocessor**, the term **Basic Configurations** refers to the two distinct operating modes determined by the logic level applied to the **MN/MX̄ (Minimum/Maximum) pin** (Pin 33) of the CPU. These configurations dictate how the processor generates its bus control signals, manages its system bus, and interfaces with external support chips.

> [!IMPORTANT] 
> The **8086 has only ONE physical 40-pin package**, but it behaves as two different microprocessors depending on the MN/MX̄ pin:
> - **Minimum Mode (Uniprocessor System)**: MN/MX̄ = **1 (Logic High / +5V)**
> - **Maximum Mode (Multiprocessor System)**: MN/MX̄ = **0 (Logic Low / GND)**

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the 8086 as a **company's Chief Executive Officer (CEO)**:
- In **Minimum Mode**, the CEO (8086) personally makes every decision and directly issues all orders (control signals like RD̄, WR̄, M/IŌ, INTĀ) to the workers (memory and I/O). This works for a small company (uniprocessor system) with a simple hierarchy.
- In **Maximum Mode**, the CEO delegates control-signal generation to a **dedicated Manager** (the **8288 Bus Controller**). This frees the CEO to coordinate with other senior executives like a **CFO (8087 Math Co-Processor)** and **Logistics Head (8089 I/O Processor)**. The Manager interprets status signals (S0̄, S1̄, S2̄) from the CEO and issues precise control commands.

> [!NOTE] 
> **Pin Remapping**: The same physical pins (Pin 24 through Pin 31) carry different meanings in the two modes. In Minimum Mode, they are direct control outputs. In Maximum Mode, they become status inputs to the external 8288 bus controller.

### 1.3 Why Two Configurations Exist

| Aspect | Minimum Mode | Maximum Mode |
|---|---|---|
| System Type | Uniprocessor | Multiprocessor |
| CPU Workload | High (CPU issues all signals) | Reduced (8288 issues signals) |
| Co-Processor Support | Not directly supported | Supports 8087, 8089 |
| Design Complexity | Simpler | More complex but more powerful |
| Typical Use | Embedded control, learning kits | High-performance desktops, servers |

### 1.4 The Support Chip Family (KTU High-Yield Chip IDs)

The following Intel peripheral chips are the standard companions to the 8086:

- **8284A**: Clock Generator + Ready Synchronizer + Reset Generator
- **8282/74LS373**: 8-bit Address Latch (used to demultiplex AD0–AD15)
- **8283**: Inverting Address Latch
- **8286/74LS245**: 8-bit Bidirectional Data Bus Buffer/Transceiver
- **8287**: Inverting Data Buffer
- **8288**: Bus Controller (used **only in Maximum Mode**)
- **8087**: Numeric Data Co-Processor (NDP)
- **8089**: I/O Processor

> [!IMPORTANT] 
> **KTU Exam Trivia**: The 8086 has a **20-bit address bus** (1 MB addressable) and a **16-bit data bus**. The lower 16 bits of the address are multiplexed with the 16-bit data bus on pins AD0–AD15, requiring external latching.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Minimum Mode Configuration (MN/MX̄ = 1)

In Minimum Mode, the 8086 itself generates all bus control signals. The pins 24–31 are configured as follows:

- **Pin 29 (LOCK̄)**: Active LOW output; prevents other bus masters from gaining control of the bus. Activated automatically during **XCHG, IN, OUT, MOV to segment register, INTA, and any instruction prefixed with LOCK**.
- **Pin 30 (RQ̄/GT̄0)** and **Pin 31 (RQ̄/GT̄1)**: Request/Grant pins. Used in **multiprocessor minimum mode** to arbitrate bus access between two co-processors. The sequence is: Request (pulse LOW) → Grant (pulse LOW) → Release (pulse LOW).

**Direct Control Signals Issued by 8086 in Minimum Mode**:
1. **M/IŌ** (Pin 28): HIGH = Memory access, LOW = I/O access
2. **RD̄** (Pin 32): Active LOW read strobe
3. **WR̄** (Pin 29 in maximum, but redefined): Active LOW write strobe
4. **INTĀ** (Pin 24): Interrupt Acknowledge output
5. **ALE** (Pin 25): Address Latch Enable – HIGH pulse to latch AD0–AD15 into 8282
6. **DEN̄** (Pin 26): Data Enable – LOW enables 8286 transceivers
7. **DT/R̄** (Pin 27): Data Transmit/Receive – HIGH = CPU writing, LOW = CPU reading
8. **BHĒ/S7** (Pin 34): Bus High Enable, used to access upper byte of 16-bit data bus

### 2.2 Maximum Mode Configuration (MN/MX̄ = 0)

When MN/MX̄ = 0, pins 24–31 take on new identities:

- **S0̄, S1̄, S2̄** (Pins 26, 27, 28): **Status signals** – sent to the 8288 bus controller
- **LOCK̄** (Pin 29): Same as minimum mode
- **RQ̄/GT̄0** and **RQ̄/GT̄1** (Pins 30, 31): Same function as minimum mode
- **QS0, QS1** (Pins 24, 25): **Queue Status** – indicate the state of the internal 6-byte instruction queue for co-processors (8087)

**The 8288 Bus Controller** decodes the 3-bit status (S0̄, S1̄, S2̄) and generates the actual control signals:

| S2̄ | S1̄ | S0̄ | Operation (8288 Output) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | INTA Cycle (INTĀ generated) |
| 0 | 0 | 1 | I/O Read (IORC̄) |
| 0 | 1 | 0 | I/O Write (IOWC̄) |
| 0 | 1 | 1 | Halt / None |
| 1 | 0 | 0 | Opcode Fetch (MRDC̄) |
| 1 | 0 | 1 | Memory Read (MRDC̄) |
| 1 | 1 | 0 | Memory Write (MWTC̄) |
| 1 | 1 | 1 | Passive / None |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact**: The 8288 generates **separate read and write signals** for memory and I/O (MRDC̄, MWTC̄, IORC̄, IOWC̄, INTĀ), enabling simultaneous memory and I/O operations when combined with the AEN (Address Enable) and CEN (Command Enable) inputs. The 8086 alone in minimum mode can only generate one RD̄ and one WR̄, which must be externally demultiplexed using M/IŌ.

### 2.3 Address Demultiplexing – The 8282/74LS373 Story

Because pins **AD0–AD15** carry both address and data at different times of the bus cycle, the system needs a way to **separate** them. This is done using an **8-bit transparent latch (8282 or 74LS373)**:

- **T1 state**: ALE = HIGH → Latches are transparent → AD lines pass through to address bus
- **T2 state onwards**: ALE = LOW → Latches hold the address → AD lines now carry data

Three 8282 latches are required:
- One for **AD0–AD7** (lower address byte + lower data byte)
- One for **AD8–AD15** (upper address byte + upper data byte)
- One for **A16/S3, A17/S4, A18/S5, A19/S6, BHĒ/S7** (these are the upper address bits multiplexed with status)

The **BHĒ (Bus High Enable)** is latched separately because it must be valid throughout the bus cycle to select the upper byte bank of memory.

### 2.4 Data Bus Buffering – The 8286/74LS245

For systems with multiple devices on the data bus, a **bidirectional transceiver (8286)** is used:
- **DT/R̄ = 1**: 8286 buffers data from CPU to memory/I/O (CPU writing)
- **DT/R̄ = 0**: 8286 buffers data from memory/I/O to CPU (CPU reading)
- **DEN̄ = 0**: Enables the 8286 transceivers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The direction control DT/R̄ must be set up **before DEN̄ goes low**, otherwise bus contention can occur. In KTU problems, students often forget this timing requirement.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Value / Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Physical Address (PA) | $PA = (\text{Segment Register}) \times 16 + \text{Offset} = (\text{Segment}) \times 10H + \text{Offset}$ | 20-bit result | Canonical 8086 addressing formula |
| Memory Address Space | $2^{20}$ bytes | **1,048,576 bytes = 1 MB** | 20-bit address bus |
| I/O Address Space | $2^{16}$ bytes | **65,536 bytes = 64 KB** | 16-bit I/O address bus |
| Data Bus Width | 16 bits | 2 bytes per transfer | |
| Segment Size | $2^{16}$ bytes | **65,536 bytes = 64 KB** | One segment register covers 64 KB |
| Number of Segments | $2^{20} / 2^{16} = 2^4$ | **4 overlapping segments** at any instant | CS, DS, SS, ES |
| Clock Frequency (8086) | $f_{CLK}$ | **5 MHz** (standard 8086) | 8086-2 supports 8 MHz |
| Clock Period (T-state) | $T = 1 / f_{CLK}$ | **200 ns** (at 5 MHz) | Basic timing unit |
| Wait States Calculation | $T_w = 2N - 1$ | Clock cycles | $N$ = count loaded into 8284 |
| Bus Cycle (Read) | $4T$ | **800 ns** (4 clock cycles) | T1, T2, T3, T4 |
| Bus Cycle (Write) | $4T$ | **800 ns** | T1, T2, T3, T4 |
| Instruction Prefetch Queue | 6 bytes | FIFO queue | Filled during bus idle |
| Min/Max Pin | Pin 33 | $1 =$ Min, $0 =$ Max | Decides entire pin mapping |
| Status Decode Width | $\lceil \log_2(8) \rceil = 3$ bits | $\overline{S_0}, \overline{S_1}, \overline{S_2}$ | Only in Maximum Mode |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units in KTU problems** are almost always in **ns, µs, MHz, bytes, KB, or MB**. Always show units in your final answer; marks are frequently deducted for missing units.

### 2.6 Real-World Utility

The Minimum/Maximum configuration scheme is the conceptual ancestor of modern multiprocessor architectures. Today's **multi-core CPUs, SoCs (System-on-Chip), and Northbridge/Southbridge chip architectures** all evolved from this principle of **separating CPU control logic from external bus control**, allowing multiple specialized processors to share a common system bus. The 8288 bus controller is conceptually a "predecessor" of the modern **Memory Controller Hub (MCH)**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Why 8086 has 1 MB Memory Space

**Given**: Address bus lines $A_0$ through $A_{19}$ (20 lines total).

**Step 1: Each address line can carry one bit, giving a unique combination per address.**

$$n = 20 \text{ address lines}$$

**Step 2: Total unique addresses = $2^n$.**

$$N_{\text{addresses}} = 2^{20}$$

**Step 3: Evaluate $2^{20}$.**

$$2^{20} = (2^{10})^2 = 1024^2 = 1{,}048{,}576$$

**Step 4: Convert to MB ($1 \text{ MB} = 2^{20} \text{ bytes}$).**

$$N_{\text{memory}} = \frac{1{,}048{,}576 \text{ bytes}}{1{,}048{,}576 \text{ bytes/MB}} = \mathbf{1 \text{ MB}}$$

> **Conclusion**: The 8086 can directly access **1,048,576 bytes (1 MB)** of memory.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Physical Address Calculation

**Problem**: A code segment has CS = 0x2050 and the instruction pointer IP = 0x0034. Find the physical address of the next instruction.

**Step 1: Recall the canonical 8086 physical address formula.**

$$PA = (\text{Segment} \times 16) + \text{Offset} = (\text{Segment} \times 10H) + \text{Offset}$$

**Step 2: Shift segment register left by 4 bits (multiply by 16).**

$$\text{CS shifted} = 0x2050 \times 16 = 0x20500$$

**Explanation of the shift**: Multiplying a hexadecimal number by 16 (which is $2^4$) is equivalent to a 4-bit left shift, which appends a 0 to the hexadecimal representation. So $0x2050 \rightarrow 0x20500$.

**Step 3: Add the offset.**

$$PA = 0x20500 + 0x00034 = 0x20534$$

**Step 4: Convert result to binary or decimal for verification.**

$$0x20534 = (2 \times 16^4) + (0 \times 16^3) + (5 \times 16^2) + (3 \times 16^1) + (4 \times 16^0)$$

$$= 131072 + 0 + 1280 + 48 + 4 = \mathbf{132{,}404}$$

> **Conclusion**: The next instruction is fetched from physical address **0x20534 (132,404 in decimal)**.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Wait State Timing Calculation

**Problem**: A slow memory chip requires the 8086 (5 MHz) to insert 2 wait states. Calculate the total time for one read bus cycle.

**Step 1: Determine the clock period T.**

$$T = \frac{1}{f_{CLK}} = \frac{1}{5 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}} = 200 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 2: A normal bus cycle = 4T (states T1, T2, T3, T4).**

$$T_{\text{cycle}} = 4 \times 200 \text{ ns} = 800 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 3: Wait states are inserted between T3 and T4. With $N$ wait states, the cycle becomes $4T + N \times T$.**

$$T_{\text{total}} = (4 + N) \times T = (4 + 2) \times 200 \text{ ns} = 6 \times 200 \text{ ns} = \mathbf{1200 \text{ ns}}$$

**Alternative formula for wait state from 8284 counter:**

$$T_{\text{wait}} = (2N - 1) \times T$$

Where $N$ is the count loaded into the 8284. If $N = 2$ (loaded into 8284), the actual wait duration is $(2 \times 2 - 1) \times 200 = 600$ ns, but the 8084 counter is decremented at half the clock rate, so the effective wait states inserted = $N$ clock cycles.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Address Latch and BHE Generation

**Problem**: A 16-bit data word at offset 0x2001 in DS = 0x4000 is to be read. Show how BHĒ and A0 control byte selection.

**Step 1: Compute physical address.**

$$PA = 0x4000 \times 16 + 0x2001 = 0x42001$$

**Step 2: Determine the address bus value.**

$$A_{19} \dots A_0 = 0000 \ 0100 \ 0010 \ 0000 \ 0000 \ 0001 = 0x42001$$

**Step 3: Latch the lower 16 address bits using 8282.**

The lower 16 bits = $A_0 \dots A_{15} = 0x2001$.

**Step 4: BHĒ decoding for 16-bit aligned and unaligned access.**

For a **16-bit read starting at an odd address (0x2001)**, the 8086 must perform **two consecutive 8-bit bus cycles**:
- **Cycle 1**: A0 = 1, BHĒ = 0 → reads the lower byte from address 0x42001 (upper bank selected)
- **Cycle 2**: A0 = 0, BHĒ = 1 → reads the upper byte from address 0x42002 (lower bank selected)

> **Conclusion**: Misaligned 16-bit accesses take **2 bus cycles = 1600 ns**, while aligned 16-bit accesses (even address) take **1 bus cycle = 800 ns**. This is a common KTU question on memory timing.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Minimum Mode vs Maximum Mode

| Feature | Minimum Mode | Maximum Mode |
|---|---|---|
| MN/MX̄ Pin Level | **HIGH (+5V)** | **LOW (GND)** |
| System Type | Uniprocessor | Multiprocessor |
| Pin 24 | INTĀ (output) | QS0 (output) |
| Pin 25 | ALE (output) | QS1 (output) |
| Pin 26 | DEN̄ (output) | S0̄ (status output) |
| Pin 27 | DT/R̄ (output) | S1̄ (status output) |
| Pin 28 | M/IŌ (output) | S2̄ (status output) |
| Pin 29 | WR̄ (output) | LOCK̄ (output) |
| Pin 30 | HLDA (output) | RQ̄/GT̄0 (bidirectional) |
| Pin 31 | HOLD (input) | RQ̄/GT̄1 (bidirectional) |
| Control Signal Generation | By 8086 directly | By 8288 Bus Controller |
| Read/Write Strobes | RD̄, WR̄ (2 signals) | MRDC̄, MWTC̄, IORC̄, IOWC̄, INTĀ (5 signals) |
| Co-Processor Support | Limited (only HOLD/HLDA) | Full (8087 NDP, 8089 IOP) |
| Queue Status | Not accessible | QS0, QS1 available |
| Lock Functionality | Available | Available |
| Bus Arbitration | HOLD/HLDA only | RQ̄/GT̄ protocol (3-pulse) |
| External Hardware | 8284, 8282×3, 8286×2 | 8284, 8282×3, 8286×2, **8288** |
| Power Consumption | Lower | Slightly higher |
| Cost of Design | Lower | Higher |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Minimum Mode 8086 System Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TB
    cpuA["8086 CPU MN MXbar tied HIGH"]:::main
    clkGen["8284A Clock Generator"]:::support
    latch1["8282 Latch AD0 to AD7"]:::latch
    latch2["8282 Latch AD8 to AD15"]:::latch
    latch3["8282 Latch A16 to A19 plus BHEbar"]:::latch
    buf1["8286 Transceiver D0 to D7"]:::buffer
    buf2["8286 Transceiver D8 to D15"]:::buffer
    mem["Memory Banks Lower and Upper"]:::memory
    ioDev["I/O Devices"]:::io

    cpuA -- "AD0-AD7" --> latch1
    cpuA -- "AD8-AD15" --> latch2
    cpuA -- "A16-A19, BHEbar" --> latch3
    cpuA -- "ALE pulse" --> latch1
    cpuA -- "ALE pulse" --> latch2
    cpuA -- "ALE pulse" --> latch3
    latch1 -- "A0-A7 Address" --> mem
    latch2 -- "A8-A15 Address" --> mem
    latch3 -- "A16-A19, BHEbar" --> mem
    cpuA -- "D0-D7" --> buf1
    cpuA -- "D8-D15" --> buf2
    buf1 -- "Bidirectional Data" --> mem
    buf2 -- "Bidirectional Data" --> mem
    cpuA -- "M/IObar, RDbar, WRbar" --> mem
    cpuA -- "M/IObar, RDbar, WRbar" --> ioDev
    clkGen -- "CLK, RESET, READY" --> cpuA

    classDef main fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#0d1b2a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef support fill:#2d6a4f,stroke:#1b4332,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef latch fill:#9d4edd,stroke:#5a189a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef buffer fill:#e76f51,stroke:#9d0208,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef memory fill:#f4a261,stroke:#e85d04,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef io fill:#264653,stroke:#003049,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.2 Maximum Mode 8086 System Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TB
    cpuB["8086 CPU MN MXbar tied LOW"]:::main
    clkGenB["8284A Clock Generator"]:::support
    busCtrl["8288 Bus Controller"]:::ctrl
    latch1B["8282 Latch AD0 to AD7"]:::latch
    latch2B["8282 Latch AD8 to AD15"]:::latch
    latch3B["8282 Latch A16 to A19 plus BHEbar"]:::latch
    buf1B["8286 Transceiver D0 to D7"]:::buffer
    buf2B["8286 Transceiver D8 to D15"]:::buffer
    memB["Memory Banks"]:::memory
    ioB["I/O Devices"]:::io
    coproc1["8087 Math Co-Processor"]:::coproc
    coproc2["8089 I/O Processor"]:::coproc

    cpuB -- "Status S0bar S1bar S2bar" --> busCtrl
    busCtrl -- "MRDC MWTC IORC IOWC INTAb" --> memB
    busCtrl -- "MRDC MWTC IORC IOWC INTAb" --> ioB
    cpuB -- "Queue QS0 QS1" --> coproc1
    cpuB -- "RQbar GTbar bus arbitration" --> coproc2
    cpuB -- "AD0-AD7" --> latch1B
    cpuB -- "AD8-AD15" --> latch2B
    cpuB -- "A16-A19, BHEbar" --> latch3B
    cpuB -- "ALE pulse" --> latch1B
    cpuB -- "ALE pulse" --> latch2B
    cpuB -- "ALE pulse" --> latch3B
    cpuB -- "D0-D7" --> buf1B
    cpuB -- "D8-D15" --> buf2B
    busCtrl -- "DENbar DT/Rbar ALE" --> buf1B
    busCtrl -- "DENbar DT/Rbar ALE" --> buf2B
    busCtrl -- "DENbar DT/Rbar ALE" --> latch1B
    busCtrl -- "DENbar DT/Rbar ALE" --> latch2B
    busCtrl -- "DENbar DT/Rbar ALE" --> latch3B
    clkGenB -- "CLK RESET READY" --> cpuB
    coproc1 -- "Shared bus via RQbar GTbar" --> cpuB
    coproc2 -- "Shared bus via RQbar GTbar" --> cpuB

    classDef main fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#0d1b2a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef support fill:#2d6a4f,stroke:#1b4332,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef ctrl fill:#d00000,stroke:#370617,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef latch fill:#9d4edd,stroke:#5a189a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef buffer fill:#e76f51,stroke:#9d0208,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef memory fill:#f4a261,stroke:#e85d04,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef io fill:#264653,stroke:#003049,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef coproc fill:#ffb703,stroke:#fb8500,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

### 4.3 Bus Cycle Timing Flow (Read Operation)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start(["Start of Bus Cycle"]) --> t1["T1: M/IObar valid, Address driven on AD0-AD15, ALE high, BHEbar valid"]
    t1 --> t2["T2: AD lines go to high impedance, RDbar goes low, DENbar goes low for 8286 enable"]
    t2 --> t3["T3: Data sampled by 8086, READY sampled for wait states"]
    t3 --> waitCheck{"READY HIGH?"}
    waitCheck -- "No" --> tw["Insert Tw: Wait state, sampled READY each cycle"]
    tw --> waitCheck
    waitCheck -- "Yes" --> t4["T4: RDbar goes high, Data read complete, Cycle ends"]
    t4 --> idle["Idle State Ti: Bus free for DMA or HOLD"]
    idle --> endNode(["Next Bus Cycle or Instruction Fetch"])

    classDef state fill:#003049,stroke:#0d1b2a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef decision fill:#d00000,stroke:#370617,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef endNode fill:#2d6a4f,stroke:#1b4332,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff

    class t1,t2,t3,t4,tw,idle state
    class waitCheck decision
    class start,endNode endNode
```

### 4.4 8288 Status Decode Truth Table Visual

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sInput["S2bar S1bar S0bar from 8086"]:::inputNode
    sDecoder["8288 Bus Controller Decoder"]:::decoder
    out1["INTAbar Interrupt Acknowledge"]:::out
    out2["IORCbar I/O Read"]:::out
    out3["IOWCbar I/O Write"]:::out
    out4["MRDCbar Memory Read"]:::out
    out5["MWTCbar Memory Write"]:::out
    out6["None Passive State"]:::out

    sInput --> sDecoder
    sDecoder -- "000" --> out1
    sDecoder -- "001" --> out2
    sDecoder -- "010" --> out3
    sDecoder -- "100" --> out4
    sDecoder -- "101" --> out5
    sDecoder -- "011 110 111" --> out6

    classDef inputNode fill:#003049,stroke:#0d1b2a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef decoder fill:#d00000,stroke:#370617,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef out fill:#2d6a4f,stroke:#1b4332,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff

    class sInput inputNode
    class sDecoder decoder
    class out1,out2,out3,out4,out5,out6 out
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the role of the MN/MX̄ pin in the 8086 microprocessor. What happens when this pin is connected to +5V and to GND?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **MN/MX̄ (Minimum/Maximum)** is pin 33 of the 8086 microprocessor. It determines the **operating mode** of the processor by reconfiguring pins 24–31.

1. **When MN/MX̄ = +5V (Logic HIGH) – Minimum Mode:**
   The 8086 operates in **uniprocessor mode**. It directly generates all bus control signals (RD̄, WR̄, M/IŌ, ALE, DEN̄, DT/R̄, INTĀ). Pin 30 becomes HLDA and pin 31 becomes HOLD input for DMA handshaking. Used in simple, single-CPU systems.

2. **When MN/MX̄ = GND (Logic LOW) – Maximum Mode:**
   The 8086 operates in **multiprocessor mode**. Pins 24–31 become QS0, QS1, S0̄, S1̄, S2̄, LOCK̄, RQ̄/GT̄0, RQ̄/GT̄1. The status signals (S0̄, S1̄, S2̄) are sent to the external **8288 bus controller**, which generates the actual control signals. Supports co-processors like 8087 and 8089.

*(Total: 3 marks – 1 for pin role, 1 for minimum mode, 1 for maximum mode.)*

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**List the support chips used in a minimum mode 8086 system and state the function of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Chip | Function |
|---|---|
| **8284A** | Clock generator (provides CLK, PCLK, OSC); generates READY synchronization and RESET signal. |
| **8282** (×3) | 8-bit transparent latch used to demultiplex the address/data bus (AD0–AD15, A16/S3–A19/S6, BHĒ/S7). |
| **8286** (×2) | 8-bit bidirectional bus transceiver used to buffer and drive the 16-bit data bus (D0–D7 and D8–D15). |

*(Total: 3 marks – 1 mark per chip with correct function.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B: Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Draw and explain the block diagram of the **8086 in minimum mode configuration**. List all the support chips used and explain the function of each chip. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the function of the **8288 bus controller** with a neat block diagram. Decode the 3 status inputs and generate the appropriate control signals. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) – Minimum Mode Block Diagram (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark): Introduction.**
In minimum mode, the MN/MX̄ pin (Pin 33) is tied to **+5V (Logic HIGH)**. The 8086 generates all bus control signals directly, making it suitable for **uniprocessor systems**.

**Step 2 (2 marks): Block Diagram Description.**
The system comprises:
- **8086 CPU** as the master
- **8284A** clock generator providing 5 MHz CLK (200 ns T-state), RESET, and READY synchronization
- **Three 8282 latches** that demultiplex the address/data bus:
  - Latch 1: captures AD0–AD7
  - Latch 2: captures AD8–AD15
  - Latch 3: captures A16/S3 – A19/S6 and BHĒ/S7
- **Two 8286 transceivers** that buffer the 16-bit data bus (D0–D7 and D8–D15)
- **Memory banks** (lower bank D0–D7 selected by A0, upper bank D8–D15 selected by BHĒ)
- **I/O devices** controlled by M/IŌ

**Step 3 (2 marks): Signal Flow Explanation.**
- During **T1**, the 8086 drives addresses on AD0–AD15, A16–A19, and BHĒ. A HIGH pulse on **ALE (Pin 25)** causes the 8282 latches to capture the address. DT/R̄ is set HIGH (write) or LOW (read) during T1.
- During **T2**, AD lines are released. RD̄ or WR̄ goes LOW. **DEN̄ goes LOW** enabling the 8286 transceivers.
- During **T3**, data is transferred; READY is sampled.
- During **T4**, the bus cycle ends; RD̄/WR̄ goes HIGH; DEN̄ goes HIGH.

**Step 4 (2 marks): Role of each support chip.**
- **8284A**: Generates the 33% duty cycle CLK from a 15 MHz crystal (3× multiplier); synchronizes external READY input; generates power-on RESET.
- **8282**: 8-bit D-type transparent latch; when STB (strobe) = HIGH, output follows input; when STB = LOW, output is latched. Used here with ALE as the strobe.
- **8286**: 8-bit bidirectional transceiver; OĒ (Output Enable) connects to DEN̄; T (direction) connects to DT/R̄. Provides **high drive current** (~32 mA) for the system data bus.

**Part (b) – 8288 Bus Controller (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark): Purpose.**
The 8288 is used **only in maximum mode** because the 8086 pins 26, 27, 28 are reconfigured as status outputs S0̄, S1̄, S2̄. The 8288 decodes these status bits and generates the actual control signals.

**Step 2 (2 marks): Block Diagram of 8288.**
The 8288 has:
- **Status Inputs**: S0̄, S1̄, S2̄ from 8086
- **Clock Input**: CLK from 8284
- **Command Outputs**: MRDC̄, MWTC̄, IORC̄, IOWC̄, INTĀ
- **Control Outputs**: DEN, DT/R̄, ALE, MCE/PDEN
- **Control Inputs**: AEN (Address Enable), CEN (Command Enable), IOB (I/O Bus Mode)

**Step 3 (3 marks): Status Decoding Table.**

| S2̄ | S1̄ | S0̄ | 8288 Output | Cycle Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | INTĀ | Interrupt Acknowledge |
| 0 | 0 | 1 | IORC̄ | I/O Read |
| 0 | 1 | 0 | IOWC̄ | I/O Write |
| 0 | 1 | 1 | None | Halt / None |
| 1 | 0 | 0 | MRDC̄ | Opcode Fetch |
| 1 | 0 | 1 | MRDC̄ | Memory Read |
| 1 | 1 | 0 | MWTC̄ | Memory Write |
| 1 | 1 | 1 | None | Passive |

**Step 4 (1 mark): AEN and CEN functions.**
- **AEN**: When LOW, command outputs are enabled (used for system bus vs. local bus arbitration).
- **CEN**: When LOW, command outputs are enabled; used in multiple 8288 systems.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks – Alternative)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain **address demultiplexing in 8086** using 8282 latches. Why is demultiplexing necessary? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **RQ̄/GT̄ protocol** used in 8086 maximum mode for bus arbitration. Draw the timing diagram and explain each pulse. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) – Address Demultiplexing (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark): Problem statement.**
The 8086 has only **40 pins** but needs 20 address lines + 16 data lines = 36 lines just for address/data, plus control signals. To fit into 40 pins, pins **AD0–AD15** carry **address during T1** and **data during T2, T3, T4** of the bus cycle. This is called **multiplexing**.

**Step 2 (2 marks): Why demultiplexing is necessary.**
Memory and I/O devices need the address to be **stable throughout the bus cycle**, not just during T1. They cannot latch the address themselves quickly enough. So an external latch is needed to capture the address during T1 and hold it for the rest of the cycle.

**Step 3 (2 marks): 8282 Latch Operation.**
- The 8282 is an **8-bit transparent latch** with 8 input pins (D0–D7) and 8 output pins (O0–O7).
- **STB (Strobe) pin**: When HIGH, the latch is **transparent** (output = input). When LOW, the latch holds the last value.
- **OĒ (Output Enable)**: When LOW, outputs are enabled; when HIGH, outputs go to high-impedance.
- Connection: D inputs ← AD lines; STB ← ALE (8086); OĒ ← GND (always enabled).

**Step 4 (2 marks): Three latches required.**
- Latch 1: AD0–AD7 → A0–A7 (lower address byte)
- Latch 2: AD8–AD15 → A8–A15 (upper address byte)
- Latch 3: A16/S3, A17/S4, A18/S5, A19/S6, BHĒ/S7 → A16–A19, BHĒ (upper address bits and bus high enable)

During **T1**, the 8086 activates **ALE = HIGH** for ~1.5 × T = 300 ns. All 24 address bits pass through the latches onto the physical address bus. At the end of T1, ALE goes LOW, and the latches hold the address. The AD lines then become the data bus.

**Part (b) – RQ̄/GT̄ Protocol (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark): Introduction.**
The **RQ̄/GT̄ (Request/Grant)** is a 3-pulse handshaking protocol used in **8086 maximum mode** (and also available in minimum mode) for bus arbitration between the CPU and co-processors like 8087 and 8089. There are two such pins: RQ̄/GT̄0 and RQ̄/GT̄1, with RQ̄/GT̄0 having higher priority.

**Step 2 (2 marks): Three Pulses Explained.**
- **Pulse 1 (Request)**: The co-processor sends a LOW pulse to the 8086's RQ̄/GT̄ pin, requesting the bus.
- **Pulse 2 (Grant)**: The 8086, on detecting the request, floats its address, data, and control lines and sends a LOW pulse back to the co-processor as a grant. The 8086 enters **HOLD Acknowledge** state internally.
- **Pulse 3 (Release)**: After completing its bus operation, the co-processor sends a final LOW pulse to the 8086, releasing the bus. The 8086 resumes execution from where it left off.

**Step 3 (2 marks): Timing Diagram (described).**

```
CPU 8086   ___      _________________           ____________
              |    |                                      |
              |    |  (Float bus, internal HOLD)         |
              |    |                                      |
Coproc      __|____|__________________________________|____
                |  |                                      |
                |  |<- Pulse 1: Request                   |
                |__|                                      |
                   |<- Pulse 2: Grant (from CPU)         _|
                                                          |
                   |<- Pulse 3: Release (from coproc)-->|
                   |                                      |
```

**Step 4 (2 marks): Key rules and conditions.**
- RQ̄/GT̄0 has **higher priority** over RQ̄/GT̄1.
- The CPU samples the RQ̄/GT̄ line during the **T4 or Ti (idle) state** of each bus cycle, not during T1, T2, T3.
- The bus is **released between instruction boundaries** (not in the middle of an instruction) unless a LOCK̄ prefix is used.
- The requesting co-processor must wait at least **one CLK cycle** after request before assuming the bus is granted.
- After release, the CPU begins a new bus cycle from the **next instruction fetch**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Callout**
> 
> 1. **Confusing RQ̄/GT̄ with HOLD/HLDA**: RQ̄/GT̄ is **3-pulse bidirectional** on the same pin, used in maximum mode. HOLD/HLDA is **2-signal separate direction** used in minimum mode. Marks lost if these are swapped.
> 2. **Forgetting the 8288 in maximum mode**: Many students draw a maximum mode block diagram but **omit the 8288**. The 8288 is mandatory in maximum mode — without it, no control signals reach memory/I/O.
> 3. **ALE timing**: ALE goes HIGH only during T1 (~300 ns at 5 MHz). It is NOT a continuous signal. Marks lost if this is shown wrong.
> 4. **BHĒ vs A0**: For an **even address (A0 = 0)**, BHĒ = 0 selects upper byte. For an **odd address (A0 = 1)**, BHĒ = 0 selects lower byte (misaligned access). Memorize this 8086 byte-selection truth table.
> 5. **Wait state placement**: Wait states go between T3 and T4, NOT between T1 and T2. Many KTU answer sheets get this wrong.
> 6. **Segment boundary rule**: A segment can start every **16 bytes (paragraph boundary)**. So CS = 0x2050 means the code segment starts at physical address 0x20500, not 0x2050.
> 7. **Physical address is 20 bits but maximum address is 0xFFFF0 + 0xFFFF = 0x10FFEF**. This wraps around (called **address wrap-around**) because the 8086 has no A20 line to disable wrap. This is the famous bug that became "the A20 gate" in later PCs.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **MN/MX̄ pin (Pin 33) is the master switch**: HIGH = Minimum, LOW = Maximum. **Memorize this first.**
- **Minimum mode** = CPU generates all control signals (RD̄, WR̄, M/IŌ, INTĀ, ALE, DEN̄, DT/R̄).
- **Maximum mode** = CPU generates 3 status bits (S0̄, S1̄, S2̄); the **8288 bus controller** decodes them into control signals.
- **Address bus = 20 bits → 1 MB memory** ($2^{20}$ bytes).
- **Data bus = 16 bits → 2 bytes per transfer**, multiplexed on AD0–AD15.
- **I/O bus = 16 bits → 64 KB I/O space** ($2^{16}$ bytes), separate from memory.
- **Physical Address Formula**: $PA = (\text{Segment} \times 16) + \text{Offset} = (\text{Segment} \times 10H) + \text{Offset}$.
- **Segment registers**: CS, DS, SS, ES — only **4 are visible at a time**, but up to 65,536 segments exist.
- **Address demultiplexing is mandatory** using 8282 (or 74LS373) latches, controlled by the **ALE** pulse during T1.
- **Data buffering is optional but recommended** for large systems using 8286 (or 74LS245) transceivers, controlled by DEN̄ and DT/R̄.
- **Clock generator 8284A** is mandatory; provides CLK (5 MHz for 8086, 200 ns T-state), PCLK, OSC, READY synchronization, and RESET.
- **Wait states** are inserted by the 8284A based on the slow memory's READY signal; inserted between T3 and T4.
- **Bus cycle** = 4 T-states (T1, T2, T3, T4) = **800 ns** at 5 MHz with zero wait states.
- **HOLD/HLDA** = minimum mode DMA arbitration (2 separate pins).
- **RQ̄/GT̄0 / RQ̄/GT̄1** = maximum mode bus arbitration (3-pulse protocol on each pin).
- **LOCK̄** pin = prevents DMA/co-processor from accessing bus during critical instructions (XCHG, IN, OUT, INTA, segment register MOV, and any instruction prefixed with LOCK).
- **QS0, QS1** = queue status output in maximum mode; lets the 8087 track the 8086's prefetch queue.
- **Co-processor support** = only in **maximum mode** via 8087 (math), 8089 (I/O).
- **8086 has 6-byte prefetch queue**, 8088 has 4-byte queue. The '6' in 8086 refers to the queue size, NOT the data bus width.
- **BHĒ** (Bus High Enable, Pin 34) = combined with A0 to select which byte(s) of the 16-bit data bus are active:
  - A0 = 0, BHĒ = 0 → both bytes (16-bit aligned access)
  - A0 = 0, BHĒ = 1 → lower byte only
  - A0 = 1, BHĒ = 0 → upper byte only (misaligned 16-bit access takes 2 cycles)
- **Status signals S3, S4, S5, S6** are multiplexed on A16–A19 during T2–T4; only S7 (on BHĒ/S7) is undefined.
- **Test pin (T̄EST, Pin 23)**: Used by the **WAIT instruction**; CPU suspends until T̄EST = HIGH.
- **NMI (Pin 17)** = Non-Maskable Interrupt; edge-triggered; cannot be disabled by CLI.
- **INTR (Pin 18)** = Maskable Interrupt; can be disabled by CLI; acknowledged by INTĀ.
- **RESET (Pin 21)** must be held HIGH for at least **4 CLK cycles** to reset the 8086; on reset, CS = 0xFFFF, IP = 0x0000, so first instruction is fetched from **0xFFFF0** (the highest 16-byte region of memory).

> [!TIP]
> **Final 30-second Exam Tip**: If a KTU question asks "Explain basic configurations of 8086," your answer should always be structured as: (1) MN/MX̄ pin role, (2) Minimum mode signals and block diagram, (3) Maximum mode signals, 8288 bus controller, and status decoding, (4) Brief note on address latching and data buffering. End with one or two KTU exam-style timing facts (bus cycle = 4T, ALE in T1, wait states in T3–T4).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: System Bus Timing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCIT404 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** System Bus Timing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> **System Bus Timing** in the 8086 microprocessor refers to the precise chronological sequencing and synchronization of signals on the multiplexed address/data bus, the address/status bus, and the control bus during a single bus cycle. It defines *when* each signal becomes valid, transitions, or returns to its inactive state relative to the system clock (CLK) edges.

### Intuitive Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a perfectly choreographed **orchestra performance**. The conductor (the system clock, **CLK**) raises and lowers the baton at a fixed rhythm. Each musician (control signal like **RD#, WR#, ALE, DEN#**) knows exactly **which beat (T-state)** to start playing and **which beat** to stop. If a slow musician (a slow memory chip) needs more time, the conductor pauses extra beats (**Wait States, Tw**) just for that instrument — but the rest of the orchestra continues marching in rhythm. This is precisely how the 8086 orchestrates communication with memory and I/O devices.

### Why System Bus Timing is Critical
- The 8086 uses a **multiplexed bus** (AD0–AD15 carries both address and data at different times). Without strict timing rules, the system would not know whether the lines currently hold an *address* or *data*.
- Timing diagrams are used to determine the maximum **memory access time** a peripheral can have without requiring wait states.
- It forms the basis for interfacing slow peripherals (like 8255, 8253, EPROM) with the high-speed 8086.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core T-State Definitions (Must Memorize)**
> - **T-State (T)**: One clock period, the smallest unit of time.
> - **Bus Cycle**: A complete operation (read or write) lasting **4 T-states (T1, T2, T3, T4)** in the standard 8086.
> - **Wait State (Tw)**: Extra clock periods inserted between T3 and T4 when the peripheral is not READY.
> - **Idle State (Ti)**: Clock periods between bus cycles when no bus activity is required.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Canonical T-States of an 8086 Bus Cycle

The 8086 basic bus cycle consists of **four T-states**: **T1, T2, T3, and T4**.

#### **T1 — The Address Phase**
- The 8086 drives the **20-bit address** onto:
  - **AD0–AD15** (lower 16 address bits on the multiplexed Address/Data bus)
  - **A16/S3, A17/S4, A18/S5, A19/S6** (upper 4 address bits multiplexed with status)
- The signal **BHE#/S7** (Bus High Enable) is also driven, indicating whether the upper byte of the data bus is active.
- The control signal **M/IO#** indicates whether the cycle targets memory (HIGH) or I/O (LOW).
- **ALE (Address Latch Enable)** pulses HIGH in T1, allowing external latches (like 74LS373) to capture and hold the address for the rest of the cycle.
- **DT/R# (Data Transmit/Receive)** is set HIGH for a write or LOW for a read (for use by external bus transceivers like 74LS245).

#### **T2 — The Status & Activation Phase**
- The lower 16 bits of the address bus (AD0–AD15) are released (go into **high-impedance**) so they can later carry data.
- The upper address lines transition to **status signals S3–S6**.
- **DEN# (Data Enable)** goes LOW to enable the external data bus transceiver.
- **RD#** or **WR#** becomes **ACTIVE** (LOW), signaling the addressed peripheral that a read or write is commencing.
- For **write cycles**, the data is also driven onto AD0–AD15 by the 8086.

#### **T3 — The Data Phase**
- For a **read cycle**: the addressed peripheral places data on AD0–AD15. The 8086 samples this data at the **trailing (rising) edge of T3**.
- For a **write cycle**: the data already placed on the bus in T2 is held valid; the peripheral reads it.
- The 8086 checks the **READY** input at the start of T3. If READY = LOW, the processor inserts **Wait States (Tw)** instead of proceeding to T4.

#### **T4 — The Termination Phase**
- The 8086 latches the data (read) or completes the write.
- **RD#** or **WR#** returns HIGH (inactive).
- **DEN#** goes HIGH, disabling the bus transceiver.
- **ALE** is already LOW. The bus cycle ends.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Concept — The Role of READY**
> The READY input is sampled at the **end of T2** and again at the **end of every Tw**. If READY = 1, the processor exits wait states and enters T4. If READY = 0, it inserts another Tw. This is the fundamental mechanism for synchronizing the 8086 with slower memory or I/O devices.

### 2.2 Read Cycle vs. Write Cycle Timing — The Critical Differences

| Aspect | Read Cycle | Write Cycle |
|---|---|---|
| Data Direction | From peripheral → 8086 | From 8086 → peripheral |
| Data Valid On Bus | In **T3** (driven by peripheral) | In **T2** (driven by 8086) |
| Active Control Signal | **RD# = LOW** during T2, T3 (and Tw) | **WR# = LOW** during T2, T3 (and Tw) |
| DT/R# State | LOW (Receive) | HIGH (Transmit) |
| Data Sampled By | 8086 (at end of T3 or last Tw) | Peripheral (during WR# = LOW) |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use `\vert` notation instead of `|` to preserve markdown table integrity.**

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Clock Period | $T = \dfrac{1}{f_{CLK}}$ | Duration of one T-state, where $f_{CLK}$ is the system clock frequency | $\mu s$ or $ns$ |
| Basic Bus Cycle Duration | $T_{BC} = 4T$ | Standard read/write cycle with zero wait states | $\mu s$ or $ns$ |
| Cycle with $n$ Wait States | $T_{BC}(n) = (4 + n)T$ | Total cycle time when $n$ wait states are inserted | $\mu s$ or $ns$ |
| Data Sampling Time (Read) | $t_{sample} = 2T$ (or $(2 + n)T$) | Time available for memory to provide data after address is valid | $ns$ |
| Memory Access Time Bound | $t_{acc} \leq 2T - t_{CO} - t_{DT}$ | Maximum allowed memory access time without wait states | $ns$ |
| $t_{CO}$ (Clock-to-Output delay) | $\approx 30$ to $70$ | 8086 internal delay between CLK edge and output change | $ns$ |
| $t_{DT}$ (Data Transit time) | $\approx 30$ to $50$ | Delay in data propagation through address/data bus | $ns$ |
| Wait State Insertion Condition | $READY = 0$ at end of T2 | Wait states are inserted as long as READY remains LOW | — |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Motherboard Design**: Modern Intel/AMD CPUs use sophisticated bus timing protocols (HyperTransport, QuickPath, PCIe) that are direct descendants of the 8086's T1–T4 paradigm.
- **Embedded Systems**: The 8086 timing model is fundamental to designing glue logic, address latches, and wait-state generators for legacy industrial controllers.
- **Memory Interfacing**: The formula $t_{acc} \leq 2T - 140$ ns is the cornerstone calculation for selecting compatible memory chips (e.g., 2716, 2764 EPROMs) for 8086-based systems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Memory Access Time Requirement for a 0-Wait-State Read

**Problem Statement**: For an 8086 system with a clock frequency of **$f_{CLK} = 5$ MHz**, determine the maximum permissible memory access time such that **no wait states are required** during a read cycle.

#### **Step 1 — Compute the Clock Period**
We know that the clock period is the reciprocal of the clock frequency.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \frac{1}{f_{CLK}} \\
T &= \frac{1}{5 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}} \\
T &= 0.2 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} \\
T &= 200 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

This means one T-state lasts **200 ns**. A basic 4-T bus cycle therefore takes **4 × 200 = 800 ns**.

#### **Step 2 — Identify the Critical Timing Path**
In a read cycle, the address becomes valid at the **start of T1**. The 8086 samples the data at the **trailing edge of T3**. The number of T-states elapsed between these two events (excluding T1 itself, since the address is already valid by its end) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Time available from address valid to data sampled} &= T2 + T3 \\
&= T + T \\
&= 2T
\end{aligned}
$$

#### **Step 3 — Account for Internal Delays**
However, the address is **not immediately valid** at the start of T1. There is an internal propagation delay from the clock edge to when the address appears on the pins. This is called the **clock-to-output delay**, denoted $t_{CO}$, typically around **30 to 70 ns** for the 8086 (we take a representative value).

Additionally, the data sampled at the 8086 input must travel through the external address/data bus, incurring a **data transit time** $t_{DT}$, typically around **25 to 50 ns** (we take a representative value).

#### **Step 4 — Apply the Standard Memory Access Time Formula**
The memory access time is the time from when the address is valid at the memory's pins to when it places valid data on the bus. For the 8086 to sample this data correctly at the end of T3:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{acc} + t_{CO} + t_{DT} &\leq 2T \\
t_{acc} &\leq 2T - t_{CO} - t_{DT}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### **Step 5 — Substitute Numerical Values**
Using $T = 200$ ns, $t_{CO} = 70$ ns, and $t_{DT} = 30$ ns as standard reference values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{acc} &\leq 2(200) - 70 - 30 \\
t_{acc} &\leq 400 - 100 \\
t_{acc} &\leq 300 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result**: The memory chip must have an access time of **$\leq 300$ ns** for a 0-wait-state read at 5 MHz. The 2716 EPROM (450 ns access time) would therefore require **wait states**; the 2732A (200 ns) would not.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Number of Wait States Required for a Given Memory

**Problem Statement**: An 8086 system runs at **$f_{CLK} = 10$ MHz** and uses a memory chip with an access time of **$t_{acc} = 450$ ns**. How many wait states must be inserted?

#### **Step 1 — Compute Clock Period**
$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \frac{1}{f_{CLK}} = \frac{1}{10 \times 10^6} = 100 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### **Step 2 — Compute the Time Available in 0 Wait States**
$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{available}(0) &= 2T - t_{CO} - t_{DT} \\
&= 200 - 70 - 30 \\
&= 100 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is far less than 450 ns — wait states are mandatory.

#### **Step 3 — Generalize to $n$ Wait States**
With $n$ wait states, the data sampling occurs at the end of T3 + $n$ extra clocks, so:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{available}(n) &= (2 + n)T - t_{CO} - t_{DT} \\
&= (2 + n)(100) - 100 \\
&= 200 + 100n - 100 \\
&= 100 + 100n
\end{aligned}
$$

#### **Step 4 — Solve for Minimum $n$**
We need $t_{available}(n) \geq t_{acc} = 450$ ns:

$$
\begin{aligned}
100 + 100n &\geq 450 \\
100n &\geq 350 \\
n &\geq 3.5
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $n$ must be an integer, we round up: **$n = 4$ wait states**.

#### **Step 5 — Verify**
With $n = 4$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{available}(4) &= 100 + 100(4) = 500 \text{ ns} \geq 450 \text{ ns} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **General Engineering Heuristic**: Memory access time must always satisfy $t_{acc} \leq (2 + n)T - t_{CO} - t_{DT}$. Always round $n$ **up**, never down, to ensure correct sampling.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Signal Activity in Each T-State

| Signal | T1 | T2 | T3 | T4 | During Tw |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| CLK | Pulsing | Pulsing | Pulsing | Pulsing | Pulsing |
| AD15–AD0 | **Address A0–A15** | High-Impedance | **Data (read)** or held (write) | Float / End | Data held (read) |
| A19/S6–A16/S3 | **Address A16–A19** | **Status S3–S6** | Status S3–S6 | Status | Status |
| BHE#/S7 | **BHE#** | S7 | S7 | S7 | S7 |
| M/IO# | Valid | Valid | Valid | Ends | Held valid |
| ALE | **HIGH (latch pulse)** | LOW | LOW | LOW | LOW |
| WR# | HIGH | **LOW (write)** | **LOW** | HIGH (ends) | **LOW** |
| RD# | HIGH | **LOW (read)** | **LOW** | HIGH (ends) | **LOW** |
| DEN# | HIGH | **LOW (enable)** | LOW | HIGH (ends) | LOW |
| DT/R# | Set (R/W) | Held | Held | Ends | Held |
| READY (input) | X (don't care) | Sampled here | If LOW → Tw | If HIGH → exit | Must be HIGH to exit |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid State Diagram — The 8086 Bus Cycle State Machine

This diagram captures the complete flow of an 8086 bus cycle, including wait-state insertion and idle-state transitions.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> T1
    T1 : T1 Address Phase
    T1 : Output 20-bit Address
    T1 : Pulse ALE HIGH
    T1 : Set M/IO# and BHE#
    T1 --> T2
    T2 : T2 Status Activation
    T2 : AD bus goes to Hi-Z
    T2 : Activate RD# or WR#
    T2 : DEN# goes LOW
    T2 --> CheckReady
    CheckReady : Sample READY pin
    CheckReady --> T3 : READY is HIGH
    CheckReady --> Tw : READY is LOW
    Tw : Tw Wait State
    Tw : Bus signals held steady
    Tw --> CheckReady : Re-sample READY
    T3 : T3 Data Phase
    T3 : Peripheral drives data (Read)
    T3 : Sample data at trailing edge
    T3 --> T4
    T4 : T4 Termination
    T4 : RD# or WR# goes HIGH
    T4 : DEN# goes HIGH
    T4 : Bus cycle ends
    T4 --> Ti
    Ti : Ti Idle State
    Ti : Bus in Hi-Z
    Ti : No control signals active
    Ti --> T1 : Next bus cycle begins
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Read Cycle Signal Interaction

This sequence diagram depicts the temporal relationship between the major signals in a read cycle with **one wait state** (Tw).

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CLK as System Clock
    participant AD as AD15-AD0 Bus
    participant ALE as ALE Signal
    participant RD as RD# Signal
    participant DEN as DEN# Signal
    participant MEM as Memory / IO Device
    participant RDY as READY Input

    Note over CLK,RDY: Read Cycle with 1 Wait State (n=1)

    CLK->>AD: T1 - Address A0-A15 placed on bus
    CLK->>ALE: T1 - ALE goes HIGH (latch address)
    CLK->>RD: T2 - RD# goes LOW
    CLK->>AD: T2 - AD bus enters Hi-Z
    CLK->>DEN: T2 - DEN# goes LOW (enables transceiver)
    CLK->>RDY: T2 - Sample READY
    RDY-->>CLK: T2 - READY = LOW (request wait)
    Note over CLK,RDY: Insert Tw (Wait State)
    CLK->>MEM: Tw - Memory still preparing data
    CLK->>RDY: Tw - Re-sample READY
    RDY-->>CLK: Tw - READY = HIGH now
    CLK->>MEM: T3 - Memory drives valid data on bus
    MEM->>AD: T3 - Place data on AD0-AD15
    CLK->>CLK: T3 - Sample data at trailing edge
    CLK->>RD: T4 - RD# goes HIGH (cycle ends)
    CLK->>DEN: T4 - DEN# goes HIGH (transceiver off)
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — System Bus Timing Architecture

This block-level functional architecture diagram illustrates the conceptual flow of signals during a bus cycle, mapping how the CPU, address latch, data transceiver, and external devices interact.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CPU8086[8086 Microprocessor]
    LATCH[Address Latch 74LS373]
    XCVR[Bus Transceiver 74LS245]
    MEM[Memory Chip]
    IO[I/O Device]
    RDYGEN[Wait State Generator]

    CPU8086 -- Multiplexed AD Bus AD0-AD15 --> LATCH
    CPU8086 -- A16 to A19 with Status --> LATCH
    CPU8086 -- Control ALE --> LATCH
    LATCH -- Latched Address A0-A19 --> MEM
    LATCH -- Latched Address A0-A15 --> IO

    CPU8086 -- Data Bus AD0-AD15 --> XCVR
    CPU8086 -- Control DEN# --> XCVR
    CPU8086 -- Control DT/R# --> XCVR
    XCVR -- Buffered Data --> MEM
    XCVR -- Buffered Data --> IO

    CPU8086 -- Control RD# --> MEM
    CPU8086 -- Control RD# --> IO
    CPU8086 -- Control WR# --> MEM
    CPU8086 -- Control WR# --> IO
    CPU8086 -- Control M/IO# --> MEM
    CPU8086 -- Control M/IO# --> IO

    MEM -- READY signal --> RDYGEN
    IO -- READY signal --> RDYGEN
    RDYGEN -- READY pin --> CPU8086
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read the architecture diagram**: The 8086 CPU connects to the address latch via the **multiplexed bus** (which carries both address and data, separated in time by the **ALE** signal). The latched address is then sent as a clean, demultiplexed address to memory and I/O. The transceiver isolates the CPU's data bus from external loads. The wait-state generator monitors peripheral speed and drives the **READY** line back to the CPU.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define a bus cycle. Draw the timing diagram of an 8086 read cycle and explain the function of each T-state.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **bus cycle** is the time taken by the microprocessor to fetch an instruction or to read/write data from/to memory or an I/O device. The 8086 basic bus cycle consists of **four T-states (T1, T2, T3, T4)**.

- **T1 (Address Phase)**: The 20-bit physical address is placed on AD0–AD15 and A16–A19. ALE pulses HIGH to latch the address. M/IO# and BHE# are also driven.
- **T2 (Status Phase)**: The lower address bus enters high-impedance. Status signals S3–S6 become valid on the upper lines. RD# goes LOW for a read. DEN# goes LOW to enable the bus transceiver.
- **T3 (Data Phase)**: The peripheral drives valid data on the data bus. The 8086 samples this data at the trailing (rising) edge of T3.
- **T4 (Termination Phase)**: RD# returns HIGH, DEN# returns HIGH, and the bus cycle ends.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for diagram/sequence, 1 mark for T-state explanation.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
**What is the function of the READY signal in the 8086? How does it relate to wait states?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **READY** signal is an input to the 8086 that indicates whether the addressed peripheral is ready for data transfer.

- It is sampled at the **end of T2** and again at the **end of every Tw**.
- If READY = 1 (HIGH), the bus cycle proceeds normally from T3 to T4.
- If READY = 0 (LOW), the 8086 inserts **wait states (Tw)** between T3 and T4, extending the cycle by one clock period per wait state. This allows slow memory or I/O devices to complete their operation before the 8086 samples the data (read) or terminates the write.

*(Award 1 mark for READY definition, 1 mark for sampling instants, 1 mark for wait-state insertion logic.)*

---

### PART B — Extended Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE Pattern**: Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and is split into sub-parts **(a) = 7 marks** and **(b) = 7 marks**. Students must answer exactly **one full question** with both sub-parts.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 — Module 2]**

**Q. (a) Draw and explain the bus timing diagram of the 8086 microprocessor in MINIMUM mode during a memory read operation. Explain the role of the READY signal and wait state insertion. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

In minimum mode, the 8086 itself generates all control signals (RD#, WR#, M/IO#, ALE, DEN#, DT/R#) without an external 8288 bus controller.

**Memory Read Cycle — T-State by T-State Analysis:**

**T1 State:**
- The 20-bit physical address is placed on the bus. A0–A15 appear on AD0–AD15; A16–A19 appear on A16/S3–A19/S6.
- **M/IO# = HIGH** (indicating a memory operation).
- **BHE# = 0** if reading the upper byte of the data bus.
- **ALE pulses HIGH** → external latch (74LS373) captures the address.
- **DT/R# = LOW** (CPU is receiving data).

**T2 State:**
- AD0–AD15 enters **high-impedance** (no longer driving the bus).
- Status signals S3–S6 become valid on A16/S3–A19/S6.
- **RD# goes LOW** → tells the addressed memory chip to drive its data onto the bus.
- **DEN# goes LOW** → enables the data bus transceiver (74LS245).

**T3 State:**
- The addressed memory chip drives the requested data onto AD0–AD15.
- The 8086 samples this data at the **rising edge of T3**.
- If the memory cannot respond in time, the external **READY logic** holds READY LOW at the sampling instant.

**T4 State:**
- The 8086 completes the data read.
- **RD# goes HIGH**, **DEN# goes HIGH**, the bus cycle ends.

**Role of READY and Wait State Insertion:**
- The 8086 samples **READY at the end of T2**.
- If **READY = LOW**, the CPU inserts one or more **wait states (Tw)** between T3 and T4.
- During each Tw, all bus signals (RD#, DEN#, address) are **held constant**, giving the slow memory more time.
- The CPU **re-samples READY at the end of every Tw**. When READY finally goes HIGH, the CPU exits the wait loop and enters T4.

**Memory Access Time Constraint:**
For correct sampling at the trailing edge of T3 (or last Tw), the memory access time must satisfy:
$$t_{acc} \leq (2 + n)T - t_{CO} - t_{DT}$$

**Timing Diagram (Schematic Representation):**

| T-State | AD Bus | ALE | RD# | DEN# | DT/R# | READY |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| T1 | Address | HIGH | HIGH | HIGH | LOW | X |
| T2 | Hi-Z | LOW | **LOW** | **LOW** | LOW | Sampled |
| T3 | Data (from mem) | LOW | LOW | LOW | LOW | X |
| T4 | Float | LOW | HIGH | HIGH | LOW | X |

*(Mark Distribution: 2 marks for T1–T2 explanation, 2 marks for T3–T4 explanation, 2 marks for READY/wait states, 1 mark for timing diagram.)*

---

**Q. (b) An 8086 system operates at a clock frequency of 8 MHz. The address/data bus path introduces a delay of 30 ns. The clock-to-output delay of the 8086 is 60 ns. Calculate: (i) the maximum memory access time allowed for 0 wait states, (ii) the number of wait states required if a memory chip of access time 600 ns is used. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Given Data:**
- $f_{CLK} = 8$ MHz
- $t_{DT} = 30$ ns (data transit time)
- $t_{CO} = 60$ ns (clock-to-output delay)
- $t_{acc} = 600$ ns (for part ii)

**Step 1: Compute the Clock Period**
$$T = \frac{1}{f_{CLK}} = \frac{1}{8 \times 10^6} = 125 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 2: Maximum Memory Access Time for 0 Wait States ($n = 0$)**
Applying the access time formula:
$$t_{acc, max} = 2T - t_{CO} - t_{DT}$$
$$t_{acc, max} = 2(125) - 60 - 30$$
$$t_{acc, max} = 250 - 90 = 160 \text{ ns}$$

> Therefore, for 0 wait states, the memory must have $t_{acc} \leq \mathbf{160}$ **ns**.

**Step 3: Wait States Required for $t_{acc} = 600$ ns**
Using the generalized formula:
$$t_{acc} \leq (2 + n)T - t_{CO} - t_{DT}$$
$$600 \leq (2 + n)(125) - 60 - 30$$
$$600 \leq 250 + 125n - 90$$
$$600 \leq 160 + 125n$$
$$125n \geq 440$$
$$n \geq 3.52$$

Rounding up, $n = 4$ wait states are required.

**Step 4: Verification**
$$t_{available}(n=4) = (2+4)(125) - 90 = 750 - 90 = 660 \text{ ns} \geq 600 \text{ ns} \quad \checkmark$$

> **Final Answer**: (i) 160 ns; (ii) **4 wait states**.

*(Mark Distribution: 1 mark for T calculation, 2 marks for part (i), 3 marks for part (ii) with full derivation, 1 mark for verification.)*

---
