> **[Example 1]** A system has a Degree of Multiprogramming of **4**, and each job spends **40%** of its time waiting for I/O. Compute (a) the CPU utilisation, and (b) the throughput if the average job requires **20 seconds** of pure CPU time and the total observed wall-clock duration is **100 seconds**.

**Given:** $N = 4$, $p = 0.40$, $T_{\text{CPU per job}} = 20\,\text{s}$, $T_{\text{total}} = 100\,\text{s}$.

**Part (a) — CPU Utilisation:**

$$U = 1 - p^{\,N}$$

$$U = 1 - (0.40)^{4}$$

$$(0.40)^{4} = 0.40 \times 0.40 \times 0.40 \times 0.40 = 0.0256$$

$$U = 1 - 0.0256 = 0.9744$$

Therefore the CPU is busy **97.44%** of the time.

**Part (b) — Throughput:**

In 100 seconds with 97.44% utilisation, the total CPU work delivered is:

$$T_{\text{CPU delivered}} = U \times T_{\text{total}}$$

$$T_{\text{CPU delivered}} = 0.9744 \times 100 = 97.44 \text{ seconds}$$

Since each job consumes 20 s of CPU time, the number of jobs completed is:

$$N_{\text{completed}} = \dfrac{T_{\text{CPU delivered}}}{T_{\text{CPU per job}}}$$

$$N_{\text{completed}} = \dfrac{97.44}{20} = 4.872$$

Rounding down (only whole jobs can complete), throughput is:

$$Th = 4 \text{ jobs} / 100 \text{ s} = 0.04 \text{ jobs/s} = 2.4 \text{ jobs/min}$$

---

### 3.3 Application of Little's Law

> **[Example 2]** In a multiprogrammed system, the average Turnaround Time is observed to be **50 ms**, and the throughput is **20 jobs/second**. Determine the average number of jobs resident in memory (DMP).

**Given:** $TAT = 50\,\text{ms} = 0.05\,\text{s}$, $Th = 20\,\text{jobs/s}$.

**By Little's Law:**

$$N = Th \times TAT$$

$$N = 20 \times 0.05 = 1$$

**Interpretation:** On average, **1 job** is resident in the system. This indicates an extremely *low* degree of multiprogramming — a counter-intuitive result showing that throughput and residency are not the same concept.

---

### 3.4 The Trade-off Matrix (Analytical Summary)

| $N$ (DMP) | $p$ | $U = 1-p^N$ | Comment |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | 0.5 | 0.500 | Uni-programmed — half the CPU wasted. |
| 2 | 0.5 | 0.750 | Significant gain. |
| 4 | 0.5 | 0.9375 | Near-peak. |
| 10 | 0.5 | 0.9990 | Diminishing returns; memory pressure rises. |
| 100 | 0.5 | $\approx 1$ | Thrashing risk if memory overcommitted. |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Multiprogrammed System

The Mermaid diagram below maps the *functional interactions* between the major components of a multiprogrammed microcomputer (8086-class) — the Job Scheduler, Ready Queue, CPU, I/O Devices, and the Process Control Blocks stored in memory.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Job Pool on Disk] -->|Admit| B[Job Scheduler Long Term]
    B -->|Allocate Memory| C[Main Memory RAM]
    C -->|Insert PCB| D[Ready Queue]
    D -->|Dispatch| E[CPU 8086]
    E -->|I/O Request| F[I/O Device Subsystem]
    F -->|Interrupt| G[Interrupt Handler]
    G -->|Save State to PCB| C
    F -->|Completion Signal| G
    G -->|Move PCB to Ready| D
    E -->|Exit Syscall| H[Termination Handler]
    H -->|Free Memory| C
    E -->|Time Slice Expired| I[Preemption Logic]
    I -->|Save State| C
    C -->|Load Next PCB| E
    subgraph ProtectionLayer [Hardware Protection Layer 80286 onward]
    J[Descriptor Tables GDT LDT]
    K[Privilege Rings 0 to 3]
    end
    C -.->|Validated by| J
    E -.->|Executes under| K
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read the diagram:** The flowchart traces the *life-cycle of a job* in a multiprogrammed system. Arrows labelled "Save State to PCB" represent **Context Save** operations, while "Load Next PCB" represents **Context Restore**. The `ProtectionLayer` subgraph illustrates the *hardware features* added in the 80286/80386 to make multiprogramming *safe* (memory isolation and privilege enforcement).

---

### 4.2 State-Transition Diagram of a Single Process

This Mermaid diagram represents the five classical states a process can occupy: New, Ready, Running, Blocked, and Exit.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> NewState
    NewState --> ReadyQ : Admitted by Job Scheduler
    ReadyQ --> Running : Dispatched by CPU Scheduler
    Running --> ReadyQ : Preemption Timeout or Interrupt
    Running --> Blocked : I/O Request or Wait Event
    Blocked --> ReadyQ : I/O Completion Interrupt
    Running --> ExitState : Exit or Normal Termination
    ExitState --> [*]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- A job enters the system in the **New** state, gets admitted to memory, and is placed in the **Ready Queue**.
- The **CPU Scheduler** dispatches it to the **Running** state.
- From Running, two transitions are possible: (a) it gets *preempted* back to Ready, or (b) it issues an I/O request and enters the **Blocked** state.
- Once the I/O device raises an interrupt, the process returns to **Ready** and waits for its next CPU turn.
- Finally, it transitions to the **Exit** state and is deallocated.

---

### 4.3 Comparative Diagram: Uni-programming vs Multiprogramming

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Uniprog [Uniprogrammed System Timeline]
    U1[Job A CPU] --> U2[Job A I O Wait Idle CPU] --> U3[Job B CPU]
    end
    subgraph Multiprog [Multiprogrammed System Timeline]
    M1[Job A CPU] --> M2[Job A I O Wait]
    M2 --> M3[Job B CPU]
    M3 --> M4[Job A I O Done Ready]
    M4 --> M5[Job A CPU Resume]
    M5 --> M6[Job B I O Wait]
    M6 --> M7[Job C CPU]
    M7 --> M8[Job B I O Done Ready]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Observation:** In the uniprogrammed timeline, the CPU is *idle* during Job A's I/O wait. In the multiprogrammed timeline, Job B executes on the CPU *exactly when* Job A is waiting. The dark blocks in the Multiprog subgraph represent **CPU busy time** — visually, the multiprogrammed line is *much denser* with CPU work.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A1] [3 Marks]**
> **Q:** Define *multiprogramming*. How does it differ from *multiprocessing*?

**Model Answer:**
Multiprogramming is a technique in which more than one independent program is held in main memory simultaneously, and the single CPU switches between them whenever the currently running program performs an I/O operation, thereby reducing CPU idle time. *Multiprocessing*, in contrast, involves the simultaneous execution of multiple processes on **two or more physical processors / cores**, not merely the time-sliced interleaving of processes on one CPU. The key distinction is *physical concurrency* (multiprocessing) versus *logical concurrency achieved through time-sharing* (multiprogramming).

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A2] [3 Marks]**
> **Q:** What is *Degree of Multiprogramming* (DMP)? Mention any two factors limiting it.

**Model Answer:**
The Degree of Multiprogramming is the maximum number of processes that can be kept resident in the main memory at any instant. Two factors that limit the DMP are:
1. **Total available physical memory** — each process needs its own address space; when memory is full, no more processes can be admitted.
2. **CPU scheduling overhead** — beyond a threshold, context-switching cost dominates the gains, leading to *thrashing*.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** A common reason students lose 3–4 marks in this module is *confusing multiprogramming with multitasking*. Multiprogramming is about **memory residency and I/O overlap**; multitasking (or time-sharing) is about **rapid CPU switching for user-perceived simultaneity**. Examiners explicitly look for the word *overlap of I/O and CPU* in the answer. Also, **always state the assumptions** (job independence, uniform $p$) before applying the $1 - p^N$ formula — failure to do so costs 2 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

> **Q (a)** [7 Marks] Explain the concept of multiprogramming with a neat block diagram. Discuss its advantages and limitations.
>
> **Q (b)** [7 Marks] In a multiprogrammed system, the degree of multiprogramming is 5. The probability that any single job is waiting for I/O is 0.3. Calculate the CPU utilisation. If throughput is observed as 4 jobs/second, find the average turnaround time using Little's Law.

**Model Solution to Part (a):**
Multiprogramming is a CPU-management strategy in which multiple independent programs reside in main memory concurrently. The CPU is allocated to whichever job is in the *ready* state; when the current job enters the I/O-wait state, a **context switch** occurs and the CPU is given to another ready job. This overlapping of the I/O phase of one job with the CPU phase of another maximises CPU utilisation.

(Refer to **Section 4.1** of these notes for the required block diagram. The student should reproduce the Job Scheduler, Ready Queue, CPU, I/O, and PCB flow, and explicitly label the *context switch* arrows.)

**Advantages:**
- Maximises CPU utilisation (theoretically up to 100%).
- Increases system throughput.
- Better utilisation of I/O devices via overlap.

**Limitations:**
- Memory management becomes complex (requires segmentation/paging).
- Requires hardware memory protection (provided in 80286 Protected Mode).
- Context-switching overhead consumes CPU cycles.
- Risk of deadlock and thrashing at high DMP.

**[7 marks: 3 for definition + diagram, 2 for advantages, 2 for limitations]**

---

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

**Step 1 — CPU Utilisation:**

$$U = 1 - p^{\,N}$$

$$U = 1 - (0.3)^{5}$$

$$(0.3)^{5} = 0.3 \times 0.3 \times 0.3 \times 0.3 \times 0.3 = 0.00243$$

$$U = 1 - 0.00243 = 0.99757 \approx 99.76\%$$

**Step 2 — Turnaround Time using Little's Law:**

$$N = Th \times TAT$$

For a stable system, the average number of resident jobs $N$ equals the DMP under equilibrium. Hence $N = 5$:

$$5 = 4 \times TAT$$

$$TAT = \dfrac{5}{4} = 1.25 \text{ seconds}$$

**Final Answer:** CPU Utilisation = **99.76%**, Average Turnaround Time = **1.25 s**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Alternative Choice]

> **Q (a)** [7 Marks] With the help of a process state-transition diagram, explain the role of context switching in multiprogramming.
>
> **Q (b)** [7 Marks] Compare uniprogramming, multiprogramming, and multiprocessing in a tabular form. State one real-world example of each.

**Model Solution to Part (a):**
(Refer to **Section 4.2** for the state diagram.) A process moves through five states: **New → Ready → Running → Blocked → Exit**. Context switching is the act of saving the running process's state (all registers, PC, flags, segment registers) into its PCB and loading the next process's PCB into the CPU. This is the *fundamental enabler* of multiprogramming — without context switching, the CPU could never yield to a different job. The 80286's **TSS (Task State Segment)** and the hardware `IRET` / `JMP` task-switch instructions were specifically designed to accelerate this operation.

**Key points (3 marks each, totalling 7):**
- 2 marks: Correct diagram with all 5 states and valid transitions.
- 2 marks: Explanation of *what* is saved in the PCB.
- 2 marks: Explanation of *why* this enables multiprogramming.
- 1 mark: Mention of 8086/80286 hardware support.

---

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

| Parameter | Uniprogramming | Multiprogramming | Multiprocessing |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Number of CPUs** | One | One | Two or more |
| **Programs in memory** | One | Multiple | Multiple (one per CPU) |
| **CPU idle time** | High (during I/O) | Low (overlapped) | Very low |
| **Throughput** | Lowest | Higher | Highest |
| **Hardware support** | Minimal | Segmentation + Protection | Multi-core / SMP |
| **Real-world example** | MS-DOS (early 1980s) | Windows 95 / Linux | Modern multi-core smartphones |
| **Degree of parallelism** | None | Logical only | True physical |
| **Failure mode** | Single point of failure | Same | Graceful degradation possible |

**[7 marks: 1 per comparison row + 1 for examples — allocate marks per correct row]**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (must memorise before the exam):**

- **Definition:** Multiprogramming = multiple programs resident in memory of a *single* CPU, overlapping I/O of one with CPU work of another.
- **Master Formula:** $\boxed{U = 1 - p^{N}}$ — derive from complement rule; **state assumptions** before using.
- **Little's Law:** $N = Th \times TAT$ — connects residency, throughput, and turnaround.
- **Hardware Enablers in 8086/80286 family:** Segmentation, GDT/LDT, Privilege Rings, TSS, hardware task switching.
- **DMP (Degree of Multiprogramming):** Limited by physical RAM, CPU scheduling overhead, and I/O bandwidth.
- **Context Switch cost:** $T_{\text{cs}} = T_{\text{save}} + T_{\text{load}}$ — must be added to TAT.
- **Five Process States:** New, Ready, Running, Blocked, Exit — draw and label arrows correctly.
- **Differentiate cleanly:** Multiprogramming ≠ Multiprocessing ≠ Multitasking (the first is memory-based, the second is CPU-based, the third is time-slice-based).
- **Thrashing:** When DMP is too high, the system spends more time swapping than computing — recognise and mention as a limitation.
- **Key terms to define confidently:** PCB, Throughput, Turnaround Time, Waiting Time, CPU Burst, I/O Burst.
- **One-line exam answer for "What is multiprogramming?":** *"It is a CPU-management technique in which multiple jobs reside in main memory simultaneously so that the CPU is kept busy by switching to another ready job whenever the current job enters I/O wait."*
- **Numerical trap:** When asked for throughput from a wall-clock observation, multiply by $U$ first, then divide by per-job CPU time.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: System Bus Structure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCIT404 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** System Bus Structure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **System Bus** is a set of common electrical conducting pathways (wires/traces) that interconnect the major components of a microprocessor-based system — namely the **CPU (Microprocessor)**, **Memory (RAM/ROM)**, and **I/O Devices** — to facilitate the transfer of **address information**, **data**, and **control signals** between them. It acts as the central communication backbone of the computer system.

In the context of the **8086 microprocessor** (the KTU reference architecture), the system bus is logically partitioned into three functionally independent sub-buses, each carrying a specific category of information.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** "A bus is a set of common lines (wires) that carry information between different parts of the system. The system bus of a microprocessor is the combination of the address bus, data bus, and control bus."

### 1.2 The Three Sub-Buses (Logical Partitioning)

The system bus is universally divided into **three functional groups**:

| Sub-Bus | Full Name | Direction | Carries | Width (8086) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **AB** | Address Bus | Unidirectional (CPU → Memory/I/O) | Memory & I/O locations | **20 bits** (A0–A19) |
| **DB** | Data Bus | Bidirectional (CPU ↔ Memory/I/O) | Actual data being transferred | **16 bits** (D0–D15) |
| **CB** | Control Bus | Bidirectional (mixed) | Read/Write strobes, interrupts, clock, reset | Variable |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Highway System" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a **multi-lane superhighway** connecting three cities: **CPU City**, **Memory City**, and **I/O City**.
>
> - **Address Bus** → The **Green Direction Signboards** at every junction. They are *one-way* (CPU → others) and they tell the driver "**WHERE** to go" (i.e., which memory location). The bus is *unidirectional* because only the CPU decides destinations.
> - **Data Bus** → The **Cargo Trucks** traveling on the road. Trucks can move **northbound or southbound** (data going to memory OR coming from memory). The bus is *bidirectional* because data flows both ways.
> - **Control Bus** → The **Traffic Signal Lights, Police Hand Signals, and Horns**. They *coordinate* who moves, when, and how — equivalent to **Read/Write strobes, Clock pulses, Reset, Interrupts**.
>
> The **width of the road** (number of lanes = number of parallel wires) determines **how much cargo can move per trip**. Wider bus = more information transferred in parallel = faster system.

### 1.4 Why the System Bus Matters (Engineering Significance)

- **Performance bottleneck**: The bus width and clock speed directly determine system throughput.
- **Modularity**: A standardized bus allows different vendors to build compatible CPUs, memory, and peripherals.
- **Scalability**: Bus standards (e.g., ISA, PCI, PCIe) evolve to support more devices at higher speeds.
- **Cost vs. performance trade-off**: Wider buses cost more (more pins, more PCB traces) but deliver higher bandwidth.

> [!TIP]
> **Golden Rule to Remember:** *Address Bus width* → defines **maximum addressable memory**. *Data Bus width* → defines **maximum data throughput per cycle*.

---

### 1.5 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **8086 Address Bus Width:** **20 bits** → Addressable memory = $2^{20} = \mathbf{1,048,576}$ locations = **1 MB**
> - **8086 Data Bus Width:** **16 bits** → Maximum parallel data = **2 bytes** per bus cycle
> - **8086 Control Bus Width:** Approximately **16–17 active control lines** ($\overline{RD}$, $\overline{WR}$, $\overline{BHE}$, $M/\overline{IO}$, etc.)
> - **Bus Bandwidth (theoretical):** $\text{Bandwidth} = \text{Data Bus Width} \times \text{Clock Frequency}$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Address Bus (AB) — "The Locator"

**Operational Logic Steps:**

1. The CPU places the **target memory or I/O address** on the address bus.
2. The address is **decoded by external decoder circuits** (e.g., 74LS138 3-to-8 decoder) to *select exactly one* memory chip or I/O port.
3. Because only the **CPU originates addresses**, the address bus is **strictly unidirectional** — peripherals never drive the address lines.
4. The **width of the address bus directly limits the maximum addressable memory** of the processor.

**Why is it unidirectional?**
→ In a von Neumann / Harvard architecture, *the CPU is the only "master" that knows which location to access next*. Peripherals respond to addresses, they do not generate them. Therefore, no reverse path is needed.

**Real-world utility:** Address decoders built using the address bus determine memory-mapped I/O layouts, chip select logic, and memory banking schemes (e.g., odd/even bank selection in 8086 using $\overline{BHE}$).

---

### 2.2 The Data Bus (DB) — "The Carrier"

**Operational Logic Steps:**

1. During a **Read** operation → memory/I/O **drives data onto** the data bus → CPU reads it.
2. During a **Write** operation → CPU **drives data onto** the data bus → memory/I/O stores it.
3. Because data flows in **both directions** depending on the operation, the data bus is **bidirectional**.
4. The bus uses **three-state buffers (tristate logic)** so that only one device drives the bus at a time — preventing electrical contention (short circuits).

**Why tristate buffers?**
→ If two devices drive the bus simultaneously, you get **bus contention** — a condition where outputs fight each other, causing excessive current draw, heating, and possible chip damage. Tristate buffers allow outputs to be in **HIGH-Z (high impedance)** state, effectively disconnecting from the bus.

> [!NOTE]
> **Tristate Logic:** A logic level that is *neither HIGH (1) nor LOW (0)* — it's a **floating / disconnected** state. Represented as **'Z'** in HDLs and timing diagrams.

**Real-world utility:** Data bus bandwidth is the primary determinant of CPU-memory throughput. Doubling the data bus width (e.g., 8086's 16-bit vs 8088's 8-bit) approximately doubles the maximum memory transfer rate at the same clock.

---

### 2.3 The Control Bus (CB) — "The Coordinator"

The control bus is a **collection of individual control and timing lines**, each with a specific synchronization or management function.

**Major Categories of Control Signals:**

**(a) Read/Write Strobe Signals:**
- $\overline{RD}$ (Read) — Asserted by CPU to request data from memory/I/O.
- $\overline{WR}$ (Write) — Asserted by CPU to send data to memory/I/O.

**(b) Memory/IO Select:**
- $M/\overline{IO}$ — HIGH means memory access; LOW means I/O access (in 8086).

**(c) Bus Arbitration Signals:**
- $\overline{BHE}/S7$ (Bus High Enable) — 8086 specific: enables the upper 8 bits (D8–D15) of the data bus. Used for accessing the **high byte bank** of memory.
- $IO/\overline{M}$ — alternate polarity (8088).
- $\overline{DEN}$ (Data Enable) — activates external data bus transceivers.
- $DT/\overline{R}$ (Data Transmit/Receive) — indicates data direction (write/read).

**(d) Interrupt & Status Signals:**
- $INTR$ (Interrupt Request), $\overline{INTA}$ (Interrupt Acknowledge)
- $NMI$ (Non-Maskable Interrupt)
- $READY$ — inserted by memory/I/O to request **wait states** if slow.

**(e) System-Level:**
- $CLK$ (System Clock), $RESET$, $HOLD$, $HLDA$ (Direct Memory Access control)

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Rule | Expression | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Maximum Addressable Memory** | $\text{Memory} = 2^n$ bytes, where $n$ = address bus width | $n$ = number of address lines |
| 2 | **Maximum I/O Ports (I/O-mapped)** | $\text{Ports} = 2^m$, where $m$ = address lines used for I/O | 8086 uses **16-bit** I/O address → $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ ports |
| 3 | **Data Throughput per Cycle** | $\text{Data/cycle} = \text{Data bus width} \div 8$ | Bytes transferred per bus cycle |
| 4 | **Bus Bandwidth** | $\text{BBW} = \text{Data Width (bits)} \times \text{Clock (Hz)}$ | bits/sec |
| 5 | **8086 Memory Capacity** | $2^{20} = 1{,}048{,}576 = 1\,\text{MB}$ | 20 address lines |
| 6 | **Even Address Access** | $A0 = 0$ → Lower byte (D0–D7) enabled via $\overline{BLE}$ | Word alignment rule |
| 7 | **Odd Address Access** | $A0 = 1$, $\overline{BHE} = 0$ → Upper byte (D8–D15) enabled | Single-byte access |
| 8 | **Aligned Word Transfer Time** | 1 bus cycle (4 clock ticks) | $\overline{BHE}=0, A0=0$ |
| 9 | **Misaligned Word Transfer Time** | 2 bus cycles (8 clock ticks) | $A0=1$ requires two byte reads |
| 10 | **Tristate Condition** | Output = 'Z' when $\overline{OE} = 1$ | Disconnect from bus |

### 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **CPU-Memory Interfacing**: Address bus + decoders determine memory maps; data bus determines transfer size; control bus handles timing.
- **Direct Memory Access (DMA)**: External DMA controller uses control bus signals ($HOLD$, $HLDA$) to seize the bus.
- **Multiprocessor Systems**: Bus arbitration logic prevents multiple CPUs from driving the bus simultaneously.
- **Modern Extensions**: Today's processors use internal point-to-point buses (e.g., Intel's QPI, AMD's Infinity Fabric) instead of shared parallel buses to overcome bottlenecks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Addressable Memory of the 8086

**Problem:** Given the 8086 has a 20-bit address bus, derive the maximum directly addressable memory.

**Step 1 — Identify the bus width.**

The 8086 microprocessor provides address lines labeled $A_0$ through $A_{19}$.

$$
n = 19 - 0 + 1 = 20 \text{ address lines}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the binary addressing principle.**

Each address line can be in one of **two states** — either logic 0 (LOW) or logic 1 (HIGH). For $n$ independent binary lines, the total number of unique combinations is:

$$
N_{\text{unique}} = 2^n
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $n = 20$.**

$$
N_{\text{unique}} = 2^{20}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the power of 2.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
2^{10} &= 1024 \\
2^{20} &= 2^{10} \times 2^{10} = 1024 \times 1024 \\
2^{20} &= 1{,}048{,}576
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to standard memory units.**

$$
1{,}048{,}576 \text{ bytes} = \frac{1{,}048{,}576}{1024 \times 1024} \text{ MB} = 1 \text{ MB}
$$

**Result:**

$$
\boxed{\text{Maximum Addressable Memory of 8086} = 1\,\text{MB} = 1{,}048{,}576 \text{ bytes}}
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: 8086 Bus Bandwidth at 5 MHz Clock

**Problem:** Compute the theoretical maximum data transfer rate of the 8086 system bus when running at **5 MHz** with a **16-bit** data bus.

**Step 1 — Recall the bandwidth formula.**

$$
\text{Bandwidth (bits/sec)} = \text{Data Bus Width (bits)} \times \text{Clock Frequency (Hz)}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute known values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Data Width} &= 16 \text{ bits} \\
\text{Clock Frequency} &= 5\,\text{MHz} = 5 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute.**

$$
\text{Bandwidth} = 16 \times 5 \times 10^6 = 80 \times 10^6 \text{ bits/sec}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to bytes and megabytes per second.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{In bytes/sec} &= \frac{80 \times 10^6}{8} = 10 \times 10^6 = 10\,\text{MB/s} \\
\text{In MiB/s} &= \frac{10 \times 10^6}{1024 \times 1024} \approx 9.54\,\text{MiB/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:**

$$
\boxed{\text{Bus Bandwidth} = 80\,\text{Mbps} = 10\,\text{MB/s}}
$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — 8086 Bus Read vs Write Cycle

| Phase / Signal | $\overline{RD}$ (CPU Read) | $\overline{WR}$ (CPU Write) |
|---|---|---|
| **T1 (Initial)** | HIGH (inactive) | HIGH (inactive) |
| **T2 (Address Valid)** | HIGH, address placed on AB | HIGH, address placed on AB |
| **T3 (Active Strobe)** | **LOW** (asserted) | **LOW** (asserted) |
| **T4 (Data Transfer)** | Memory/IO **drives DB** → CPU latches | CPU **drives DB** → Memory/IO latches |
| **Bus Direction** | Memory/IO → CPU | CPU → Memory/IO |
| **Tristate State After** | DB → HIGH-Z | DB → HIGH-Z |

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Aligned vs Misaligned Word Access in 8086

| Access Type | $A_0$ | $\overline{BHE}$ | Bytes Used | Bus Cycles Required | Time @ 5 MHz |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Aligned Word (Even Address)** | 0 | 0 | D0–D15 (both banks) | **1 cycle** | **4 T-states (800 ns)** |
| **Misaligned Word (Odd Address)** | 1 | 0 | D8–D15 first, then D0–D7 | **2 cycles** | **8 T-states (1600 ns)** |
| **Even Byte Only** | 0 | 1 | D0–D7 (lower bank) | 1 cycle | 4 T-states |
| **Odd Byte Only** | 1 | 0 | D8–D15 (upper bank) | 1 cycle | 4 T-states |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is misaligned access slower?** The 8086 cannot read both banks simultaneously when $A_0 = 1$ (because the lower bank is disabled). It must perform **two sequential byte reads** to assemble a 16-bit word, effectively halving throughput for misaligned data.

### 3.5 Derivation: I/O Port Addressing Capacity of 8086

**Step 1 — Identify the I/O address bus width.**

The 8086 uses a **16-bit I/O address** (lower 16 lines, $A_0$–$A_{15}$).

**Step 2 — Apply the formula.**

$$
\text{I/O Ports} = 2^{16}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate.**

$$
2^{16} = 65{,}536
$$

**Result:**

$$
\boxed{\text{Maximum I/O Ports} = 65{,}536 = 64\,\text{K ports}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Note: I/O address space (64 KB) is **independent** of memory address space (1 MB). The $M/\overline{IO}$ control line distinguishes between them.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 System Bus Architecture — High-Level Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CPU1["CPU 8086"]
    MEM1["MEMORY RAM ROM"]
    IO1["I O DEVICES"]
    DEC1["ADDRESS DECODER 74LS138"]

    CPU1 -- "ADDRESS BUS 20 LINES A0 A19" --> DEC1
    DEC1 -- "CHIP SELECT LINES" --> MEM1
    DEC1 -- "CHIP SELECT LINES" --> IO1
    CPU1 -- "DATA BUS 16 LINES D0 D15 BIDIRECTIONAL" --> MEM1
    CPU1 -- "DATA BUS 16 LINES D0 D15 BIDIRECTIONAL" --> IO1
    CPU1 -- "CONTROL BUS RD WR BHE M IO READY" --> MEM1
    CPU1 -- "CONTROL BUS RD WR BHE M IO READY" --> IO1
```

### 4.2 8086 Internal Bus Structure (Detailed)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BIU["BIU BUS INTERFACE UNIT"]
        AX["AX Accumulator 16 bit"]
        BX["BX Base 16 bit"]
        CX["CX Count 16 bit"]
        DX["DX Data 16 bit"]
        SI["SI Source Index"]
        DI["DI Destination Index"]
        BP["BP Base Pointer"]
        SP["SP Stack Pointer"]
        CS["CS Code Segment"]
        DS["DS Data Segment"]
        SS["SS Stack Segment"]
        ES["ES Extra Segment"]
        IP["IP Instruction Pointer"]
        Q1["INSTRUCTION QUEUE 6 BYTES"]
    end

    subgraph EU["EU EXECUTION UNIT"]
        ALU["ALU 16 BIT"]
        FL["FLAG REGISTER"]
        CT1["CONTROL CIRCUITRY"]
    end

    subgraph EXT["EXTERNAL SYSTEM BUS"]
        AB["ADDRESS BUS 20 BIT"]
        DB["DATA BUS 16 BIT"]
        CB["CONTROL BUS"]
    end

    Q1 -- "FETCH" --> AB
    EU -- "REQUEST DATA" --> DB
    BIU -- "READ WRITE" --> CB
    AB --> EXT
    DB --> EXT
    CB --> EXT
```

### 4.3 Signal Flow During a Memory Read Operation

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CPU as CPU 8086
    participant AB as Address Bus
    participant DEC as Decoder
    participant MEM as Memory
    participant DB as Data Bus
    participant CB as Control Bus

    Note over CPU,MEM: T1 ADDRESS PHASE
    CPU->>AB: Place address on A0 A19
    CPU->>CB: Assert M over IO HIGH
    Note over CPU,MEM: T2 T3 COMMAND PHASE
    CPU->>CB: Assert RD LOW
    AB->>DEC: Decoder activates CS of target chip
    Note over CPU,MEM: T3 DATA PHASE
    MEM->>DB: Drive data onto D0 D15
    CPU->>CB: READY sampled HIGH
    Note over CPU,MEM: T4 COMPLETION
    CPU->>CB: Deassert RD HIGH
    CPU->>DB: Latch data into internal register
    MEM->>DB: Release bus to HIGH Z
```

### 4.4 Physical Pin Grouping of 8086 (Bus-Centric View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PIN["8086 40 PIN DIP"]
        P1["PINS 2 TO 16 9 TO 16 ADDRESS and DATA multiplexed AD0 AD15"]
        P2["PINS 35 TO 38 17 TO 19 UPPER ADDRESS A16 A19 plus STATUS"]
        P3["PINS 32 TO 34 22 25 27 28 21 CONTROL PINS RD WR M IO BHE DEN DT R"]
        P4["PINS 30 31 CLK READY RESET"]
        P5["PINS 18 19 21 INTR NMI TEST"]
        P6["PINS 23 24 25 26 HOLD HLDA"]
    end

    subgraph BUS_OUT["EXTERNAL BUS SIGNALS"]
        ADD["ADDRESS BUS A0 A19 20 LINES"]
        DAT["DATA BUS D0 D15 16 LINES"]
        CTL["CONTROL BUS STROBES and STATUS"]
    end

    P1 --> DAT
    P1 --> ADD
    P2 --> ADD
    P3 --> CTL
    P4 --> CTL
    P5 --> CTL
    P6 --> CTL
```

### 4.5 Data Flow on Multiplexed Address/Data Bus

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LATCH["ADDRESS LATCH 74LS373"]
    CPU2["8086 CPU"]
    MEM2["MEMORY"]
    CPU2 -- "T1 AD0 AD15 carries ADDRESS" --> LATCH
    CPU2 -- "T2 T3 T4 AD0 AD15 carries DATA" --> MEM2
    LATCH -- "Latched A0 A15 sent to memory" --> MEM2
    CPU2 -- "A16 A19 S3 S4 S5 S6 directly to memory" --> MEM2
    Note1["ALE signal latches address in T1"]
    CPU2 -. "ALE Address Latch Enable" .-> LATCH
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define the term "System Bus". List and briefly explain its three main components.**

**Model Answer:**

A *system bus* is a set of common electrical pathways used to transfer data, addresses, and control signals between the microprocessor, memory, and I/O devices of a computer system.

The three main components are:

1. **Address Bus (AB):** A unidirectional bus that carries the memory or I/O address from the CPU to memory/I/O. In 8086, it is 20 bits wide, allowing access to **1 MB** of memory.

2. **Data Bus (DB):** A bidirectional bus that carries the actual data being read from or written to memory/I/O. In 8086, it is 16 bits wide.

3. **Control Bus (CB):** A set of control and timing lines such as $\overline{RD}$, $\overline{WR}$, $M/\overline{IO}$, $\overline{BHE}$, and $READY$ that coordinate and synchronize bus operations.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Why is the address bus unidirectional while the data bus is bidirectional? Explain in the context of 8086.**

**Model Answer:**

- The **address bus is unidirectional** because *only the CPU generates addresses*. Memory and I/O devices never originate addresses — they only *respond* to addresses placed by the CPU. Hence, there is no need for a reverse data path, and the address lines are always driven by the CPU.

- The **data bus is bidirectional** because data must flow in *both directions*: from memory/IO to the CPU during a **Read** operation, and from the CPU to memory/IO during a **Write** operation. Bidirectionality is implemented using **tristate buffers** that allow either the CPU or the peripheral (but not both simultaneously) to drive the bus.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse the term "bidirectional" with "simultaneous two-way." The data bus is bidirectional *in time* — only one device drives it at any given moment, controlled by $\overline{RD}$ / $\overline{WR}$ signals.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice Format

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the system bus structure of the 8086 microprocessor. Clearly identify the address bus, data bus, and control bus. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Introduction:**

The 8086 microprocessor has a **16-bit external data bus** and a **20-bit address bus**, giving it the ability to access 1 MB of memory and 64 KB of I/O space.

**Step 2 — Address Bus (20 bits, A0–A19):**

- The lower 16 lines (A0–A15) are **multiplexed** with the data bus (AD0–AD15) and must be latched externally using the **ALE (Address Latch Enable)** signal and a latch like the 74LS373.
- The upper 4 lines (A16–A19) are **non-multiplexed** and directly drive the address bus along with status signals (S3–S6).
- The address bus is **unidirectional** — driven only by the BIU.

**Step 3 — Data Bus (16 bits, D0–D15):**

- Shares physical pins with the lower address bus (multiplexed).
- During T1 of the bus cycle, these pins carry the **address**; from T2 to T4, they carry **data**.
- Bidirectional — controlled by $DT/\overline{R}$ (Data Transmit/Receive) and $\overline{DEN}$ (Data Enable) signals through external transceivers like 74LS245.
- $\overline{BHE}$ (Bus High Enable) controls whether the upper 8 bits (D8–D15) are active.

**Step 4 — Control Bus:**

Key control signals include:

| Signal | Function |
|---|---|
| $\overline{RD}$ | Active-low read strobe |
| $\overline{WR}$ | Active-low write strobe |
| $M/\overline{IO}$ | Selects memory (HIGH) or I/O (LOW) access |
| $\overline{BHE}/S7$ | Enables upper data byte (D8–D15) |
| $\overline{DEN}$ | Enables external data bus transceiver |
| $DT/\overline{R}$ | Indicates data flow direction |
| $READY$ | Wait-state request from slow devices |
| $CLK$, $RESET$ | System clock and reset |
| $INTR$, $\overline{INTA}$, $NMI$ | Interrupt signals |
| $HOLD$, $HLDA$ | DMA control signals |

**Step 5 — Block Diagram (already shown in Section 4.1 & 4.2).**

---

**(b)** Explain the concept of *multiplexed address/data bus* in 8086. How is address demultiplexing achieved externally? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Why Multiplexing?**

The 8086 comes in a **40-pin DIP package**. To accommodate 20 address bits, 16 data bits, and ~17 control bits, Intel used pin multiplexing — the lower 16 address lines (A0–A15) share the same physical pins as the data lines (D0–D15). These are labeled **AD0–AD15** and are time-multiplexed.

**Step 2 — Bus Cycle Phases:**

| T-State | Function of AD0–AD15 |
|---|---|
| **T1** | Act as **address lines** (A0–A15) |
| **T2, T3, T4** | Act as **data lines** (D0–D15) |
| **Tw (Wait)** | Floating (HIGH-Z) |

**Step 3 — Demultiplexing Process:**

A **latch** (typically 74LS373, an 8-bit transparent latch) is used:

1. During T1, the CPU outputs the lower 16-bit address on AD0–AD15.
2. The CPU also asserts **ALE (Address Latch Enable) = HIGH**.
3. The 74LS373 latches the address on the **falling edge** of ALE.
4. The latch's outputs (A0–A15) drive the memory/IO address inputs throughout the rest of the cycle.
5. The AD0–AD15 pins are now free to carry data.

**Step 4 — System Diagram:**

```
   8086 AD0-AD15  ---->  74LS373  ---->  A0-A15 (latched, to memory)
       |
       |---ALE---->  G (enable) of 74LS373
```

**Step 5 — Upper Address & Status:**

A16–A19 and status bits S3–S6 are similarly latched using a second 74LS373 controlled by ALE.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Differentiate between the address bus, data bus, and control bus of 8086 in terms of width, direction, and function. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Address Bus | Data Bus | Control Bus |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Width (8086)** | 20 bits (A0–A19) | 16 bits (D0–D15) | ~17 active lines |
| **Direction** | Unidirectional (CPU → Mem/IO) | Bidirectional (CPU ↔ Mem/IO) | Mixed (mostly CPU → others, but some input) |
| **Function** | Carries the address of memory/IO location to be accessed | Carries the actual data being transferred | Carries timing, read/write strobes, and status signals |
| **Driven by** | CPU only (BIU) | CPU (write) or Memory/IO (read) | CPU for outputs; Memory/IO for inputs like READY, HOLD |
| **Multiplexed?** | Lower 16 bits shared with data | Lower 16 bits shared with address | No, mostly dedicated pins |
| **Buffer needed?** | Latch (74LS373) for demultiplexing | Transceiver (74LS245) for buffering | Buffers/driver chips |
| **Example signals** | A0–A19 | D0–D15 | $\overline{RD}$, $\overline{WR}$, $M/\overline{IO}$, $\overline{BHE}$ |

**Conclusion:** The three sub-buses together constitute the system bus. The address bus selects the *location*, the data bus carries the *payload*, and the control bus *orchestrates* the transfer.

---

**(b)** With neat timing diagrams, explain the 8086 memory read and write bus cycles. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Common 4 T-State Bus Cycle:**

A standard 8086 bus cycle consists of **4 T-states** (T1, T2, T3, T4), each typically 200 ns at 5 MHz.

**Step 2 — Memory Read Cycle:**

| T-State | AB (A0–A19) | $\overline{RD}$ | DB (D0–D15) | $M/\overline{IO}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| T1 | Address valid | HIGH (1) | — | HIGH |
| T2 | Address held | Goes LOW (0) | Tri-state (Z) | HIGH |
| T3 | Address held | LOW (0) | Memory drives data | HIGH |
| T4 | Address held | Goes HIGH (1) | Data latched by CPU | HIGH |

**Key points:**
- Address is placed on the bus during T1 and latched externally using ALE.
- $\overline{RD}$ is asserted in T2 and remains active through T3.
- Memory places data on the bus during T3, sampled by CPU on the rising edge of T4's clock.
- $M/\overline{IO}$ is HIGH to indicate a memory (not I/O) operation.

**Step 3 — Memory Write Cycle:**

| T-State | AB (A0–A19) | $\overline{WR}$ | DB (D0–D15) | $M/\overline{IO}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| T1 | Address valid | HIGH (1) | Data placed (early) | HIGH |
| T2 | Address held | Goes LOW (0) | Data valid | HIGH |
| T3 | Address held | LOW (0) | Data valid | HIGH |
| T4 | Address held | Goes HIGH (1) | Tri-state | HIGH |

**Key points:**
- Data is placed on the bus *early* (in T1) and remains valid through T4.
- $\overline{WR}$ is asserted in T2 and latched by memory on its rising edge in T4.
- The memory writes the data on the **rising edge of $\overline{WR}$**.

**Step 4 — Wait States:**

If memory is slow, it can deassert READY before T3, forcing the CPU to insert **wait states (Tw)** between T3 and T4. Each Tw is one additional clock period.

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**

Read and write cycles differ in (a) the strobe signal used ($\overline{RD}$ vs $\overline{WR}$), (b) the direction of data flow on DB, and (c) when data is sampled (CPU samples on $\overline{RD}$ rising edge; memory samples on $\overline{WR}$ rising edge).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing width and address space:** Students write "8086 has 20-bit data bus" — this is **wrong**; 8086 has a *16-bit* data bus and *20-bit* address bus. Always verify before writing.
> 2. **Forgetting demultiplexing:** In block diagrams, students often draw A0–A15 and D0–D15 as *separate* physical buses without showing the 74LS373 latch. This loses 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Misaligned word access:** Many students believe an 8086 word read always takes 1 cycle. They forget that misaligned (odd-address) word access takes **2 cycles** — this is a favorite KTU question.
> 4. **Direction confusion in Read/Write:** Students often swap the data direction — saying "data flows from memory to CPU during Write." This is the opposite of the truth.
> 5. **ALE description:** Some students describe ALE as a "clock" or "enable" without mentioning its role in **latching the multiplexed address**. KTU specifically tests this.
> 6. **Skipping units in calculations:** Writing "$2^{20} = 1{,}048{,}576$ bytes" without converting to **1 MB** may cost a half-mark for incomplete final answer.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ The **system bus** = **Address Bus + Data Bus + Control Bus**.
- ✅ **Address Bus** is **unidirectional**; **Data Bus** is **bidirectional**; **Control Bus** is **mixed**.
- ✅ **8086 Address Bus** = 20 bits → **1 MB** addressable memory; **Data Bus** = 16 bits → **2 bytes/cycle**.
- ✅ The lower 16 address lines of 8086 are **multiplexed with the data bus** (AD0–AD15) and need **external latching using 74LS373 + ALE**.
- ✅ $\overline{BHE}$ (Bus High Enable) controls access to the **upper byte** of the data bus.
- ✅ **Aligned** word access ($A_0 = 0$, $\overline{BHE} = 0$) takes **1 bus cycle**; **misaligned** word access ($A_0 = 1$) takes **2 bus cycles**.
- ✅ **Tristate buffers** prevent bus contention by allowing devices to electrically disconnect (state **'Z'**).
- ✅ Read cycle uses $\overline{RD}$; Write cycle uses $\overline{WR}$; $M/\overline{IO}$ selects memory vs I/O.
- ✅ **READY** signal is used to insert **wait states** for slow memory/peripherals.
- ✅ $HOLD$ / $HLDA$ signals enable **DMA** operations by releasing bus control to an external master.
- ✅ Bus bandwidth formula: $\text{BW} = \text{Data Width} \times \text{Clock Frequency}$.
- ✅ Maximum I/O ports of 8086 = $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ (independent of memory space).
- ✅ Status signals S3–S6 are multiplexed with A16–A19 in 8086 (upper address + status).
- ✅ Modern CPUs use **point-to-point high-speed serial buses** (PCIe, QPI) instead of shared parallel buses for higher bandwidth.
- ✅ KTU favorite question type: *Block diagram + description of three buses* — practice drawing the bus structure with proper labels.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multiprocessor Configurations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCIT404 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Multiprocessor Configurations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Multiprocessor Configuration (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> A multiprocessor configuration is a system architecture in which **two or more processors** (each capable of executing its own instruction stream) are interconnected to share a common set of resources — such as **system bus, memory, and I/O devices** — and cooperate to solve a single computational problem or improve system throughput. In the context of the 8086 family, multiprocessor configurations are classified into three principal categories defined by Intel: **Coprocessor (8087 NDP), I/O Processor (8089 IOP), and Multiprocessing using Multiple 8086 CPUs**, all coordinated through a shared system bus and a dedicated **Bus Arbiter (8289)**.

The 8086 microprocessor is the **Master** in such configurations, while the auxiliary processors act as **Slaves/Co-processors**. Each device in the configuration possesses a unique **Request (REQ)** and **Grant (GRANT)** pin pair to interface with the **8289 Bus Arbiter**, which serializes bus access using a strict priority scheme.

## 1.2 Classification of Multiprocessor Systems

```
Multiprocessor Configurations
│
├── 1. Coprocessor Configuration (8086 + 8087)
│        └── Same physical/IO address space
│
├── 2. I/O Processor Configuration (8086 + 8089)
│        └── Independent IO address space
│
├── 3. Closely-Coupled (Tightly-Coupled) Configuration
│        └── Shared common memory & bus, multiple 8086s
│
└── 4. Loosely-Coupled Configuration
         └── Each CPU has private memory, system bus shared
```

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Hospital Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy (The Hospital):**
> Imagine a large hospital. The **Main Doctor (8086)** is highly skilled at diagnosis and treatment planning. However, the doctor spends too much time on repetitive tasks such as filing reports, taking X-rays, and writing prescriptions.
>
> To optimize, the hospital hires:
> - **A Specialist (8087 Math Coprocessor):** Shares the same office and patient files (same address space). The specialist only handles complex mathematical prescriptions.
> - **A Nurse (8089 IOP):** Manages a separate storage room of patient reports (separate I/O address space), brings them in only when needed, and frees the doctor from paperwork.
> - **Another Doctor (Second 8086):** Works in a separate wing but shares the central records department (shared memory) for complex cases.
>
> An **Air Traffic Controller (8289 Bus Arbiter)** ensures that only one specialist enters the main records room at a time, preventing file collisions. This is exactly how multiprocessor configurations function in the 8086 family.

## 1.4 Need for Multiprocessor Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a Single 8086 is Insufficient for Advanced Tasks:**
> 1. **Speed Bottleneck:** A single 8086 cannot simultaneously compute transcendental functions (sin, cos, log) and manage I/O. The 8087 accelerates math by **100× to 1000×**.
> 2. **Bus Saturation:** When I/O dominates (e.g., disk transfers), the CPU is locked out. The 8089 IOP offloads I/O, allowing parallel execution.
> 3. **Parallelism:** Complex tasks (real-time control + signal processing + communication) can be partitioned across specialized processors.
> 4. **Reliability:** Failure of one coprocessor can be isolated; the master 8086 can continue basic operation.

## 1.5 The Two Fundamental Bus Coordination Modes

- **MULTI-MASTER mode:** Multiple processors (e.g., 8086 + 8086) request the shared bus through the 8289 Bus Arbiter.
- **SLAVE mode:** Coprocessors (8087) and IOPs (8089) translate the host 8086's commands into specialized actions; they do not arbitrate in a competing sense.

## 1.6 Key Physical Constants and Performance Metrics

- **Maximum Bus Arbitration Resolution Time:** **~ 200 ns** for the 8289 in parallel priority resolution.
- **8087 Performance Gain:** Up to **100×** for floating-point operations compared to software emulation on the 8086.
- **Number of Masters in a 8289 Cascade:** Up to **3** arbiters can be cascaded to support up to **8 masters** in parallel priority.
- **Common System Bus Clock:** **5 MHz, 8 MHz, or 10 MHz** depending on the 8086 variant.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Coprocessor Configuration — 8086 with 8087 NDP

### 2.1.1 The Numeric Data Processor (8087) Architecture

The **8087 NDP (Numeric Data Processor)** is a specialized co-processor that extends the 8086's instruction set with **~60 new floating-point instructions** starting with the **ESC (Escape) opcode prefix (11011xxx)**. The 8086 fetches all instructions; if the opcode is an ESC, the 8087 intercepts and executes it in parallel.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The 8086 and 8087 run **instruction-level parallelism**. The 8086 fetches and decodes; the 8087 performs floating-point math. They **do not** compete for the bus simultaneously because the 8087 only takes the bus when the 8086 initiates an ESC instruction.

### 2.1.2 Operational Steps in Coprocessor Mode

1. The 8086 fetches an instruction from memory.
2. The 8086 decodes the opcode. If it is an ESC instruction (D8–DF), it is forwarded to the 8087 via the **QSO/QS1 (Queue Status) pins**.
3. The 8086 computes the operand address and reads the operand from memory.
4. The 8086 then performs a dummy read cycle (or write) so that the 8087 can latch the operand into its internal register stack.
5. The 8087 executes the floating-point operation in parallel while the 8086 continues to the next instruction.
6. The 8087 signals completion via the **TEST pin** (active low) to the 8086.

### 2.1.3 Shared vs. Separate Address Space

| Property | Coprocessor (8087) | I/O Processor (8089) |
|----------|-------------------|----------------------|
| Memory Address Space | **Same as 8086** | **Separate I/O Space** |
| BHE/S7 Pin usage | Yes | No |
| S0, S1, S2 Status | Yes (from 8087) | No |
| Operating Mode | **SLAVE** | **Master-like (IO mode)** |
| Typical use | Floating point math | DMA-like I/O channel |

## 2.2 I/O Processor Configuration — 8086 with 8089 IOP

The **8089 IOP (I/O Processor)** is a **Direct Memory Access (DMA) controller with intelligence**. It has its own instruction set (Channel Program) stored in memory. The 8086 sets up a "channel" — a DMA channel that executes autonomously.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **8089 IOP Highlights:**
> - Two independent **I/O channels** per 8089.
> - Each channel has its own register set: **GA, GB, GC, TP, IX, BC, CC, DP, SP, PSW, CS, DS, SS, ES**.
> - **Transfer rate:** Up to **1.25 MBytes/sec** (for 5 MHz 8089).
> - Modes of operation: **Local mode** and **Remote mode**.

### 2.2.1 Remote vs. Local Mode of 8089

- **Local Mode:** The 8089 and 8086 share the **same system bus** and memory. Easier to implement, but bus contention must be managed by the 8289.
- **Remote Mode:** The 8089 has a **dedicated local I/O bus** and **local memory**. The 8089 fetches its channel program from local memory and only uses the system bus for I/O transfers. **Higher throughput, no arbitration for I/O cycles**.

## 2.3 Closely-Coupled (Tightly-Coupled) Configuration

### 2.3.1 Architecture

In a **closely-coupled configuration**, two (or more) **8086 microprocessors** share:
- A **common system bus**
- A **common shared memory**
- **Common I/O devices**

Each 8086 also has access to a **private local memory** and **private local I/O** for dedicated operations.

```
Master 8086  ─── Local Mem & I/O
    │
    ├──┬── System Bus (shared)
    │  │
Master 8086' ── Local Mem & I/O
    │
    ├── Bus Arbiter (8289) ── decides which master owns the bus
    │
    └── Shared Memory & I/O
```

### 2.3.2 Bus Arbitration Logic

The **8289 Bus Arbiter** operates in one of three modes:
1. **Single Bus Mode (SBM):** All masters compete for a single system bus.
2. **Multiple Bus Mode (MBM):** Each master has a local bus; they arbitrate only for shared resources.
3. **Parallel Priority Mode (PPM):** A parallel priority resolver assigns bus ownership in **O(1) time** without daisy-chaining.

## 2.4 Loosely-Coupled Configuration

### 2.4.1 Architecture

In a **loosely-coupled configuration**, each 8086 has:
- A **private local bus**
- **Private local memory**
- **Private local I/O**
- A shared **global system bus** and **global shared memory**

The processors communicate **only through the shared global memory** — this is the most scalable architecture.

| Feature | Closely-Coupled | Loosely-Coupled |
|---------|----------------|-----------------|
| Coupling | Tight | Loose |
| Communication via | Shared bus + shared memory | Global memory only |
| Scalability | 2–3 processors | Many processors |
| Bus contention | High | Low |
| Use case | Real-time embedded | Distributed systems |

## 2.5 The 8289 Bus Arbiter — The Heart of Coordination

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **8289 Bus Arbiter Pins:**
> - **AEN, AEN0, AEN1:** Address Enable outputs to the bus controller (8288).
> - **BREQ:** Bus Request to the CPU.
> - **BUSY:** Common signal to all arbiters (wired-OR).
> - **CBRQ:** Common Bus Request (active when a master is willing to release the bus).
> - **INIT:** Reset input.
> - **S0, S1, S2:** Status inputs from the 8086.
> - **SYSB/RESB:** System Bus / Resident Bus mode select.
> - **IOB:** I/O Bus mode select.

### 2.5.1 Priority Resolution Modes

- **Parallel Priority (PPM):** Uses a **74LS148 priority encoder** to give hard priorities. **Fastest** — **O(1)** arbitration.
- **Serial Priority (Daisy Chain):** Priority determined by physical chain position. **Slower** — **O(N)** arbitration.
- **Rotating Priority:** Time-multiplexed fair access. **No starvation**.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter / Concept | Formula / Value | Unit / Note |
|---------------------|-----------------|-------------|
| 8087 speedup over software emulation | $\approx 100\times$ | Floating-point operations |
| 8089 I/O transfer rate | $f_{CLK} \times 1 \text{ word per } 4 \text{ clocks}$ | Max 1.25 MByte/s @ 5 MHz |
| Bus arbitration time (parallel) | $T_{ARB} \approx 50 \text{ ns}$ | Per 8289 |
| Max number of masters (parallel) | $2^{N} = 8$ | N = 3 cascaded 8289s |
| Queue Status propagation | $T_{QS} \approx 25 \text{ ns}$ | 8086 $\rightarrow$ 8087 |
| TEST pin synchronization delay | 4–5 clock cycles | 8086 wait states |
| Memory access time (Coprocessor) | Same as 8086 | Shared address space |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CNC Machines:** A coprocessor (8087) + master (8086) controls high-precision motion. The 8087 handles complex trigonometric path calculations.
- **Robotics:** Multiprocessor configurations allow simultaneous servo control, vision processing, and communication.
- **Real-Time Data Acquisition:** 8089 IOP offloads disk streaming while the 8086 processes the buffered data.
- **Spacecraft Avionics:** NASA-era designs used loosely-coupled 8086 systems for redundancy and reliability.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Bus Arbitration Delay in Parallel Priority Mode

We derive the worst-case bus arbitration latency when **N** masters compete through **k** cascaded 8289 arbiters in **Parallel Priority Mode (PPM)**.

### 3.1.1 Step 1: Number of Masters Supported

Each 8289 arbiter has **2** priority input pins (PRI0, PRI1) plus the **SYSB/RESB** and **IOB** encoding, supporting effectively **2** masters in the simplest case. When cascaded through a 74LS148 priority encoder:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Number of Masters} &= 2^{k} \\
\text{where } k &= \text{number of cascaded 8289s}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.2 Step 2: Per-Arbiter Propagation Delay

The 8289 has an internal propagation delay $T_{p_{8289}} \approx 30 \text{ ns}$. The 74LS148 priority encoder has $T_{p_{74LS148}} \approx 18 \text{ ns}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{p,\text{single stage}} &= T_{p_{8289}} + T_{p_{74LS148}} \\
&= 30 \text{ ns} + 18 \text{ ns} \\
&= 48 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.3 Step 3: Total Arbitration Latency

For cascaded stages, the total delay grows linearly with the number of arbiter stages, but the resolution of priority is still **O(1) per master** because each stage is independent:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{ARB,\text{total}} &= T_{p,\text{single stage}} \times k \\
\text{For } k=3: \quad T_{ARB,\text{total}} &= 48 \text{ ns} \times 3 = 144 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.4 Step 4: Total Wait Time Including 8288 Bus Controller

The 8288 bus controller introduces an additional **40 ns** of address enable delay:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{WAIT,\text{total}} &= T_{ARB,\text{total}} + T_{p_{8288}} \\
T_{WAIT,\text{total}} &= 144 \text{ ns} + 40 \text{ ns} \\
T_{WAIT,\text{total}} &= 184 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the worst-case time a master must wait after asserting a bus request before being granted ownership.

## 3.2 Derivation: Effective Throughput of 8089 IOP Channel

### 3.2.1 Step 1: Channel Program Execution Time

The 8089 executes channel programs stored in memory. Let $N_{inst}$ be the number of instructions in a typical channel program. Each instruction takes **4 clock cycles** on average (fetch + execute) for a memory reference instruction, or **2 clock cycles** for a register-only instruction.

Let the ratio of memory-reference to register-only instructions be $r$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{channel} &= 4 \cdot r \cdot N_{inst} \cdot T_{CLK} + 2 \cdot (1-r) \cdot N_{inst} \cdot T_{CLK} \\
T_{channel} &= N_{inst} \cdot T_{CLK} \cdot [2 + 2r]
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.2 Step 2: Data Transfer Time

For a DMA transfer of $D$ bytes between memory and I/O, with each transfer taking **4 clock cycles** (in synchronized transfer mode):

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{transfer} &= 4 \cdot D \cdot T_{CLK}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.3 Step 3: Total Time

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{total} &= T_{channel} + T_{transfer} \\
T_{total} &= N_{inst} \cdot T_{CLK} \cdot [2 + 2r] + 4 \cdot D \cdot T_{CLK} \\
T_{total} &= T_{CLK} \cdot \left[ N_{inst}(2+2r) + 4D \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.4 Step 4: Throughput in Bytes/Second

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Throughput} &= \frac{D}{T_{total}} \\
\text{Throughput} &= \frac{D}{T_{CLK} \cdot [N_{inst}(2+2r) + 4D]} \\
\text{As } D \to \infty: \quad \text{Throughput}_{\max} &= \frac{1}{4 \cdot T_{CLK}} \\
\text{For } f_{CLK} = 5 \text{ MHz}: \quad \text{Throughput}_{\max} &= \frac{1}{4 \times 200 \text{ ns}} = 1.25 \text{ MByte/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Decision Table for Configuration Selection

| Requirement | Recommended Configuration | Justification |
|-------------|---------------------------|---------------|
| High-speed floating-point math | 8086 + 8087 Coprocessor | Up to 100× speedup, shared address space |
| High-throughput I/O (disk/tape) | 8086 + 8089 IOP | DMA-like channel, offloads 8086 |
| Real-time control + parallel compute | Closely-coupled (2 × 8086) | Low communication latency, shared memory |
| Large-scale distributed system | Loosely-coupled (multi 8086) | Scalable, low bus contention |
| System with custom I/O controller | 8089 in Remote mode | Dedicated I/O bus, isolation |
| Backward compatibility with 8086 | Any of the above | Pin-compatible family |

## 3.4 Step-by-Step: How the 8086 and 8087 Coordinate an FADD Instruction

Consider the source code fragment:
```
FADD DWORD PTR [SI]   ; Add single-precision float from memory to ST(0)
```

| Step | 8086 Action | 8087 Action | Bus State |
|------|-------------|-------------|-----------|
| 1 | Fetches opcode `D8 04` (ESC + FADD) | Idle | 8086 reads instruction |
| 2 | Decodes — recognizes ESC | Reads QS0, QS1 = 10 (first ESC byte) | Idle |
| 3 | Computes EA = DS:[SI] | Latches opcode in instruction queue | 8086 reads EA |
| 4 | Dummy read from memory[SI] | Latches operand (4 bytes) | 8086 performs read |
| 5 | Moves to next instruction | Begins FADD computation | 8086 free to continue |
| 6 | Continues next instruction | Performs IEEE 754 add (80-bit) | 8087 internal |
| 7 | Reaches WAIT (or FWAIT) instruction | Asserts TEST pin LOW when done | Synchronization |
| 8 | Reads TEST = 0, proceeds | Returns to idle | 8086 continues |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** The **WAIT (9B)** or **FWAIT** instruction is **mandatory** before using the floating-point result. Without it, the 8086 will read stale data from memory or the stack.

## 3.5 Algorithm: Bus Arbitration in Multi-Master System

The 8289 Bus Arbiter follows this hardware algorithm:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Input: } & \overline{S_0}, \overline{S_1}, \overline{S_2} \text{ from master}, \text{ CBRQ, BUSY status} \\
\text{Output: } & \text{AEN (Address Enable to 8288)} \\
\text{Step 1: } & \text{If } \overline{S_0}=\overline{S_1}=\overline{S_2}=1, \text{ then T-state = Idle, no bus request.} \\
\text{Step 2: } & \text{If any } \overline{S_i} = 0, \text{ master requests bus. Check priority via PRI0, PRI1.} \\
\text{Step 3: } & \text{If } \overline{BUSY} = 1 \text{ (bus free) AND this master has highest priority:} \\
              & \quad \text{Assert AEN LOW; pull BUSY LOW.} \\
\text{Step 4: } & \text{If BUSY = 0 (bus occupied), wait for it to go high, then re-evaluate priority.} \\
\text{Step 5: } & \text{If CBRQ = 0 (current master willing to release) AND this master needs bus:} \\
              & \quad \text{Current master releases on next idle cycle.}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 4.5 Diagram: Bus Arbitration State Machine (8289)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Request : Master asserts S0-S2 not all 1
    Request --> PriorityCheck : Sample PRI0, PRI1
    PriorityCheck --> Granted : Highest priority AND BUSY=1
    PriorityCheck --> Waiting : BUSY=0 (bus occupied)
    Waiting --> PriorityCheck : BUSY goes high
    Granted --> AENActive : Pull AEN LOW
    AENActive --> Transfer : Master performs bus cycle
    Transfer --> Idle : Cycle complete, release bus
    Idle --> [*]
```

## 4.6 Diagram: Functional Flow of an FADD in Coprocessor Mode

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["8086 Fetches Opcode"] --> B{"Opcode = ESC?"}
    B -- No --> Z["8086 Executes Normally"]
    B -- Yes --> C["8087 Latches ESC via QS0/QS1"]
    C --> D["8086 Computes Operand Address"]
    D --> E["8086 Reads Operand from Memory"]
    E --> F["8087 Latches Operand (dummy bus cycle)"]
    F --> G["8087 Executes FADD in Parallel"]
    G --> H["8086 Continues to Next Instruction"]
    H --> I{"Reaches WAIT?"}
    I -- No --> J["8086 May Use Stale Data (BUG!)"]
    I -- Yes --> K["8086 Polls TEST Pin"]
    K --> L{"TEST = 0?"}
    L -- No --> M["Wait State"]
    L -- Yes --> N["8086 Reads Result from Stack"]
```
