# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] 
**Define a coprocessor. How does the 8087 NDP communicate with the 8086 host?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A coprocessor is a specialized auxiliary processor that works in tandem with a host CPU to offload specific computational tasks. The **8087 Numeric Data Processor (NDP)** is a coprocessor for the 8086 that handles floating-point arithmetic.
>
> Communication mechanism: The 8086 fetches all instructions and forwards ESC-prefixed instructions to the 8087 via the **QS0 and QS1 (Queue Status) pins**. The 8086 and 8087 share the same memory and I/O address space, but the 8087 only accesses the bus during operand fetch cycles of ESC instructions. Synchronization is achieved via the **TEST pin**.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**List the three modes of operation of the 8089 IOP.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 1. **Local Mode:** The 8089 shares the system bus with the 8086 and fetches its channel program from system memory.
> 2. **Remote Mode:** The 8089 has its own local I/O bus and local memory; it accesses the system bus only for I/O data transfers.
> 3. **I/O Mode (IOP mode):** The 8089 acts as a true I/O processor, executing channel programs autonomously without 8086 intervention.
>
> *(The 8089 also has DMA and slave configurations, but the three primary execution modes are Local, Remote, and I/O.)*

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
> **(a)** Explain the **coprocessor configuration** of 8086 with 8087 NDP in detail. Discuss the instruction-level parallelism and the role of QS0, QS1, and TEST pins. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **closely-coupled multiprocessor configuration** using two 8086 CPUs. Describe the function of the **8289 Bus Arbiter** in this setup. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

The **coprocessor configuration** connects an **8086 host CPU** and an **8087 Numeric Data Processor (NDP)** to a shared system bus. Both processors share the same memory and I/O address space, but operate with **instruction-level parallelism**.

**Key features:**

1. **Shared Bus:** The 8086 and 8087 share the address, data, and control buses. They are physically connected in parallel.

2. **Queue Status Pins (QS0, QS1):** The 8086 has a 6-byte prefetch queue. The QS0/QS1 pins indicate the queue status to the 8087:
   - `00` — No queue operation
   - `01` — First byte of opcode fetched from queue
   - `10` — Queue is empty (after a branch/init)
   - `11` — Subsequent byte of opcode fetched

3. **ESC Opcode Forwarding:** When the 8086 fetches an instruction beginning with the **ESC opcode (11011xxx)**, it signals the 8087 via QS0/QS1. The 8086 computes the operand address (if any) and performs a **dummy read** so the 8087 can capture the operand.

4. **Parallel Execution:** While the 8087 performs the floating-point operation, the **8086 continues with the next instruction**. This is the essence of instruction-level parallelism.

5. **TEST Pin Synchronization:** When the 8086 needs the result (typically at a `WAIT` or `FWAIT` instruction), it polls the **TEST pin**. The 8087 pulls TEST LOW when the operation completes, and the 8086 proceeds.

**Example execution flow of `FADD ST(0), ST(1)`:**
- 8086 fetches D8 C1 (ESC + FADD)
- 8087 latches opcode via QS0/QS1 = 01
- 8086 increments IP and fetches next instruction
- 8087 performs the add in parallel
- 8086 reaches next WAIT — polls TEST
- TEST goes LOW → 8086 proceeds

**Advantages:** Up to 100× faster floating-point math; transparent to programmer; no bus contention since 8086 is master.

#### Model Solution for (b):

A **closely-coupled (tightly-coupled) multiprocessor configuration** uses **two or more 8086 CPUs** sharing a common system bus, common memory, and common I/O. Each processor also has a private local memory and local I/O for dedicated operations.

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph M1 ["Master 1"]
        C1["8086-1"]
        LM1["Local Memory"]
        LIO1["Local I/O"]
    end
    subgraph M2 ["Master 2"]
        C2["8086-2"]
        LM2["Local Memory"]
        LIO2["Local I/O"]
    end
    subgraph Shared ["Shared"]
        SM["Shared Memory"]
        SIO["Shared I/O"]
    end
    subgraph ARB ["Arbitration"]
        A1["8289-1"]
        A2["8289-2"]
        PE["74LS148"]
    end
    C1 --- A1
    C2 --- A2
    A1 --- PE
    A2 --- PE
    C1 --- LM1
    C1 --- LIO1
    C2 --- LM2
    C2 --- LIO2
    PE --- SM
    PE --- SIO
```

**Function of the 8289 Bus Arbiter:**

The 8289 Bus Arbiter is the heart of bus coordination. It performs the following functions:

1. **Bus Request Sampling:** The 8289 receives the status lines (S0, S1, S2) from its associated 8086 and detects when a bus request is made.

2. **Priority Resolution:** Multiple 8289s are connected to a **74LS148 priority encoder** that resolves which master has the highest priority.

3. **AEN Generation:** The winning 8289 asserts **AEN (Address Enable)** to its associated 8288 Bus Controller, which then drives the address and command lines.

4. **BUSY Signal Management:** The arbiter manages the wired-OR **BUSY** signal. When a master is using the bus, BUSY is held LOW, blocking other arbiters from granting the bus.

5. **Common Bus Request (CBRQ):** Allows a master to indicate willingness to release the bus for higher-priority requests, enabling fair access.

**Three Arbitration Modes of 8289:**
- **Single Bus Mode (SBM):** All masters share one system bus.
- **Multiple Bus Mode (MBM):** Each master has a local bus; arbitration only for shared resources.
- **Parallel Priority Mode (PPM):** Uses 74LS148 for O(1) priority resolution.

**Advantages of closely-coupled:** Low communication latency, shared memory access, simple OS design.
**Disadvantages:** Bus is a bottleneck; limited to 2–3 processors.

### Question B (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 — Alternative]**
> **(a)** Explain the **I/O Processor (8089)** configuration with the 8086. Differentiate between **Local mode** and **Remote mode** operation. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** With a block diagram, explain the **loosely-coupled multiprocessor configuration**. Compare it with the closely-coupled configuration. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

The **8089 I/O Processor (IOP)** is an intelligent DMA controller that executes channel programs autonomously, offloading I/O tasks from the 8086.

**Architecture and Interface:**
- The 8089 has **two independent I/O channels**.
- It has its own instruction set, registers (GA, GB, GC, IX, BC, CC, DP, SP, PSW), and segment registers (CS, DS, SS).
- The 8086 sets up a channel program in memory, then issues an `XFER` or `SINTR` command to start the 8089.

**Channel Program:** A sequence of instructions stored in memory that describe the I/O transfer — source, destination, count, and synchronization signals.

**Local vs. Remote Mode:**

| Feature | Local Mode | Remote Mode |
|---------|-----------|-------------|
| Channel program storage | System memory | Local 8089 memory |
| I/O bus | Shared with system bus | **Dedicated local I/O bus** |
| 8089 access to system bus | Frequent (for each instruction) | Only for data transfer to global memory |
| Throughput | Lower | Higher |
| Complexity | Simple | Requires local bus interface |
| Use case | Simple embedded systems | High-throughput data acquisition |

**In Local Mode:** The 8089 fetches each instruction from the system memory over the shared bus. It arbitrates for the bus with the 8086, but the IOP has higher priority (or configurable). Every instruction fetch uses the system bus.

**In Remote Mode:** The 8089 has a **private local memory and private I/O bus**. The 8089 fetches its channel program from local memory (no system bus access). It only uses the system bus for actual data transfer between local I/O and global shared memory. This dramatically reduces system bus traffic.

**Communication with 8086:**
- The 8086 writes a **Channel Control Block (CB)** to memory.
- The 8086 sends a **Channel Attention (CA)** signal to the 8089.
- The 8089 reads the CB and begins execution.
- Upon completion, the 8089 issues an **Interrupt** to the 8086.

**Advantages:** 
- Offloads 8086 by **up to 100%** for I/O-bound tasks
- Concurrent processing (parallelism)
- Can be cascaded for multiple IOPs

#### Model Solution for (b):

The **loosely-coupled configuration** is the most scalable multiprocessor architecture. Each processor is an independent module with its own private bus, private memory, and private I/O. Processors communicate only through a **global shared memory** connected to a **global system bus**.

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph Mod1 ["Module 1"]
        P1["8086-1"]
        LB1["Local Bus"]
        PM1["Private Memory"]
        PI1["Private I/O"]
    end
    subgraph Mod2 ["Module 2"]
        P2["8086-2"]
        LB2["Local Bus"]
        PM2["Private Memory"]
        PI2["Private I/O"]
    end
    subgraph GBL ["Global"]
        GB["Global System Bus (Arbitrated)"]
        GSM["Global Shared Memory (Mailbox)"]
    end
    P1 --- LB1
    LB1 --- PM1
    LB1 --- PI1
    LB1 --- GB
    P2 --- LB2
    LB2 --- PM2
    LB2 --- PI2
    LB2 --- GB
    GB --- GSM
```

**Comparison: Loosely-Coupled vs. Closely-Coupled**

| Aspect | Closely-Coupled | Loosely-Coupled |
|--------|-----------------|------------------|
| Coupling | Tight | Loose |
| Common resources | Shared memory + bus + I/O | Only global memory |
| Communication | Direct (low latency) | Through mailbox (higher latency) |
| Scalability | Limited (2–3 CPUs) | Highly scalable (many CPUs) |
| Bus contention | High | Low |
| Failure isolation | Poor (one failure may affect all) | Good (independent modules) |
| Synchronization | Tightly synchronized | Loosely synchronized |
| OS complexity | Simple (single OS) | Complex (distributed OS) |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Application | Embedded control | Distributed systems, networks |

**Inter-Processor Communication (IPC):**
- Processors write to a **mailbox** location in global shared memory.
- Synchronization is achieved via **semaphores** (a flag byte in shared memory).
- A processor reads the mailbox, processes the data, and writes a response.
- **Test-and-Set (TS)** hardware instruction ensures atomic semaphore access.

**Advantages of Loosely-Coupled:**
- Highly scalable (theoretically unlimited)
- Fault-tolerant (one module failure doesn't crash the system)
- Less bus contention

**Disadvantages:**
- Higher communication latency
- Requires complex distributed OS
- Global memory is a bottleneck

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake — Forgetting WAIT:** Many students forget to insert **WAIT (9B)** instructions before reading 8087 results. The 8086 will read uninitialized stack data. **2 marks lost per such error.**
> 2. **Common Mistake — Confusing address spaces:** Students often state that the 8089 shares the memory address space. **WRONG** — the 8089 has a **separate I/O address space** distinct from the 8086. **Up to 3 marks lost.**
> 3. **Common Mistake — Forgetting 8289:** In closely-coupled diagrams, students often draw two 8086s connected to a bus **without the 8289 arbiter**. The 8289 is **mandatory** for any multi-master system. **2 marks lost.**
> 4. **Common Mistake — Misidentifying TEST pin direction:** The **TEST pin is an INPUT to the 8086** (output of 8087), not the reverse. Direction matters for KTU drawings. **1 mark lost.**
> 5. **Common Mistake — Confusing 8288 vs 8289:** 8288 is the **Bus Controller** (drives address and command lines); 8289 is the **Bus Arbiter** (resolves bus access). Do not interchange. **2 marks lost.**
> 6. **Common Mistake — Wrong number of 8089 channels:** The 8089 has **2** channels, not 1. **1 mark lost.**

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Multiprocessor Configurations**

### Core Definitions
- [ ] **Coprocessor:** Auxiliary processor sharing the **same** address space (8087).
- [ ] **I/O Processor:** Auxiliary processor with **separate I/O address space** (8089).
- [ ] **Closely-Coupled:** Multiple 8086s share a **common bus + common memory + common I/O**.
- [ ] **Loosely-Coupled:** Multiple 8086s with **private resources** communicating via **global shared memory**.

### Key Pins and Signals
- [ ] **QS0, QS1:** Queue Status — 8086 → 8087 instruction forwarding.
- [ ] **TEST:** 8087 → 8086 completion signal (active LOW).
- [ ] **ESC (D8–DF):** Opcode prefix for coprocessor instructions.
- [ ] **AEN, BUSY, CBRQ:** 8289 Bus Arbiter signals.
- [ ] **CA (Channel Attention):** 8086 → 8089 start signal.
- [ ] **S0, S1, S2:** Status outputs from 8086 indicating cycle type.

### Critical Numbers
- [ ] 8087 speedup: **~100×** for floating-point.
- [ ] 8089 transfer rate: **1.25 MByte/s @ 5 MHz**.
- [ ] 8089 channels per chip: **2**.
- [ ] Max 8289 cascade for parallel priority: **3** → supports **8 masters**.
- [ ] 8289 propagation delay: **~30 ns**.

### 8289 Bus Arbiter Modes
- [ ] **SBM** — Single Bus Mode.
- [ ] **MBM** — Multiple Bus Mode.
- [ ] **PPM** — Parallel Priority Mode (with 74LS148).

### Must-Remember Rules
- [ ] Always insert **WAIT** before reading 8087 results.
- [ ] The 8086 is always the **master**; coprocessors are **slaves**.
- [ ] In loosely-coupled systems, IPC happens via a **mailbox** in global memory.
- [ ] The 8087 has its own **register stack of 8 × 80-bit** registers.
- [ ] The 8087 supports IEEE 754 floating-point format.

### 8089 Modes
- [ ] **Local mode:** Shares system bus, channel program in system memory.
- [ ] **Remote mode:** Local I/O bus + local memory, less system bus traffic.

### 8086 Family Support Chips
- [ ] **8284** — Clock Generator.
- [ ] **8288** — Bus Controller.
- [ ] **8289** — Bus Arbiter.
- [ ] **8282/8283** — Address Latch.

### Common Exam Keywords
- [ ] "Shared address space" → 8087.
- [ ] "Separate I/O address space" → 8089.
- [ ] "Tightly-coupled" → closely-coupled.
- [ ] "Mailbox" → loosely-coupled IPC.
- [ ] "Instruction-level parallelism" → 8086 + 8087.
- [ ] "Channel program" → 8089.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Coprocessor, Closely Coupled and Loosely Coupled Configurations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCIT404 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Coprocessor, Closely Coupled and Loosely Coupled Configurations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Coprocessor?

A **coprocessor** is a special-purpose auxiliary processor that works in tandem with the main processor (CPU) to offload and accelerate specific computational tasks. In the context of the KTU PBCIT404 syllabus (2024 Scheme), the term *coprocessor* specifically refers to the **Numeric Data Processor (NDP)** — the **Intel 8087** — designed to pair with the **Intel 8086/8088** microprocessor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> A coprocessor is a supplementary processor that extends the instruction set of the host processor by executing specialized instructions (e.g., floating-point arithmetic, transcendental functions) that the main CPU either cannot perform or performs very inefficiently.

### The 8087 Numeric Data Processor (NDP) — Formal Definition

The **8087** is an **8-bit external device** that augments the 8086/8088 with high-speed floating-point arithmetic, integer, BCD, and trigonometric/logarithmic operations. It conforms to the **IEEE 754 floating-point standard** and is functionally invisible to the programmer — i.e., the programmer writes a single line of code, but the 8086 and 8087 collaborate transparently behind the scenes.

| Specification | Value |
|---|---|
| Data Bus Width | **16-bit** (to match 8086/8088 multiplexed bus) |
| Package | **40-pin DIP** |
| Floating-Point Standard | **IEEE 754** |
| Operand Types | 8-byte extended precision, 8-byte double, 4-byte single, etc. |
| Key Functions | FADD, FSUB, FMUL, FDIV, FSQRT, FPTAN, etc. |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Manager–Specialist" Model

Think of the 8086 as a **General Manager** of a company:
- The manager (8086) handles ALL instructions, reads documents, and delegates tasks.
- The 8087 is like a **Specialist Chartered Accountant** sitting next to the manager.
- When the manager (8086) encounters a complex tax calculation, it hands the problem to the specialist (8087).
- The manager continues with other paperwork while the specialist works in parallel.
- Once the specialist is done, the result is returned to the manager.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight:** The 8086 and 8087 operate *asynchronously* in loosely coupled mode, but *synchronously* in closely coupled mode. The synchronization mechanism is the **TEST pin** of the 8086 and the **BUSY pin** of the 8087.

## 1.3 What are Coupled Configurations?

The term **"coupling"** defines *how tightly* the coprocessor is wired to the host CPU's system bus. This determines:
- Whether the coprocessor and CPU share the same bus (closely coupled)
- Or whether they have independent, parallel access to the system (loosely coupled)

> [!NOTE]
> **Closely Coupled Configuration:**
> The coprocessor (8087) is connected **directly to the local (system) bus** of the 8086. The 8086's bus control signals (e.g., $\overline{RD}$, $\overline{WR}$, S0–S2 status) are shared with the 8087. They cannot access the bus simultaneously.

> [!NOTE]
> **Loosely Coupled Configuration:**
> The 8087 has its **own local bus and bus controller**, and accesses memory/IO **independently** of the 8086. Both processors can run in parallel. A **bus arbiter** (e.g., 8289) resolves simultaneous bus access requests.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Intel 8087 — Internal Architecture & Operating Principle

### 2.1.1 Instruction Flow
1. The 8086 **fetches** every instruction from memory.
2. If the opcode is an **ESC (Escape)** opcode (6 bits `11011x` followed by 3 bits — part of a floating-point instruction), the 8086 calculates the memory address of the operand.
3. The 8086 then **fetches the operand** from memory and **discards** it.
4. Simultaneously, the 8087 captures the same instruction from the queue, recognizes the floating-point operation, and reads its own copy of the operand.
5. The 8087 executes the operation **in parallel** while the 8086 continues with the next instruction.
6. The 8086 periodically checks the **TEST\** input (connected to the 8087's **BUSY\** output) before using a floating-point result.

### 2.1.2 Key 8087 Pin Functions (KTU High-Yield)

| Pin | Direction | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **BUSY\** | Output | HIGH when 8087 is executing; LOW when ready |
| **READY** | Input | Acknowledges wait states from memory |
| **RESET** | Input | Initializes 8087 |
| **NMI** | Input | Non-maskable interrupt |
| **INT** | Output | Interrupt to 8086 |
| **CLK** | Input | Clock from 8284 (must match 8086 clock) |
| **RQ\ / GT\** | Bidirectional | Request/Grant for bus access (used in loosely coupled) |
| **QS0, QS1** | Input | Queue status from 8086 |
| **TEST\** | — | (not on 8087 — this is on the 8086, connected to BUSY\) |
| **S0–S2** | Output | Status signals for bus controller (8288) |

## 2.2 Closely Coupled Configuration — Deep Analysis

### 2.2.1 Architecture

In the closely coupled configuration:
- A **single 8284 clock generator** drives both processors.
- A **single 8288 bus controller** decodes status signals from both.
- A **single bus arbiter (8289)** is NOT needed because only one device accesses the bus at any time.
- The 8087's **RQ\ / GT\** lines are tied to HIGH (inactive).
- The 8086 acts as the **master**; the 8087 is the **slave** on the bus.

### 2.2.2 Operational Steps
1. 8086 fetches an ESC instruction.
2. 8086 performs a "dummy read" of the operand to compute its address.
3. 8086 drives the status signals to relinquish the bus.
4. 8087 takes over the bus (when 8086 enters its internal execution unit busy state) and fetches the actual operand.
5. 8087 executes; 8086 continues with subsequent instructions.
6. 8086 executes a **WAIT** instruction before using the result — looping on the **TEST\** pin until 8087's **BUSY\** goes HIGH.

### 2.2.3 Advantages & Disadvantages

| Advantages | Disadvantages |
|---|---|
| Simpler hardware (no bus arbiter) | No parallel bus access — performance bottleneck |
| Lower cost (one bus controller) | 8086 must stall on WAIT for result (in some cases) |
| Easier to design and debug | Throughput is limited to one bus master at a time |
| Single shared memory | Memory contention if both attempt access |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** In closely coupled mode, the 8086 and 8087 **do NOT operate in true parallel** at the bus level. The 8086 is the bus master. True parallelism is only achieved in loosely coupled mode.

## 2.3 Loosely Coupled Configuration — Deep Analysis

### 2.3.1 Architecture

In the loosely coupled configuration:
- **Each processor has its own 8284 clock generator and 8288 bus controller.**
- **Both share a common system bus** for memory and I/O access.
- A **bus arbiter (8289)** coordinates simultaneous bus requests via the **RQ\ / GT\** pins of the 8086/8087.
- The 8087 operates on its **own local bus** independently.
- A common **multibus architecture** allows both to access shared resources.

### 2.3.2 The Request/Grant Handshake

The loosely coupled system uses a **3-state handshake** on the **RQ\ / GT\** pins:

1. **Request:** The 8087 (slave) asserts its RQ\ line LOW when it needs the bus.
2. **Grant:** The 8086 (master) releases the bus in the next T4 or T1 state and asserts GT\ LOW.
3. **Release:** After 8087 finishes its bus cycle, it releases GT\ HIGH; 8086 resumes control.

### 2.3.3 Advantages & Disadvantages

| Advantages | Disadvantages |
|---|---|
| **True parallel processing** — both run simultaneously | Complex hardware (dual clock, dual bus controllers, arbiter) |
| Higher throughput & bandwidth | More expensive |
| Independent fault isolation | Bus contention overhead |
| Better for multi-master systems | More difficult synchronization (uses TEST\ and BUSY\) |
| No WAIT overhead in normal flow | Arbitration delays possible |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The loosely coupled configuration is the **standard architecture for high-performance multiprocessor systems** (e.g., the original IBM PC/XT–class servers, industrial controllers, and modern multi-core SoCs inherit this philosophy).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Clock Period | $T = \frac{1}{f_{CLK}}$ | seconds (s) | For 5 MHz clock: $T = 200$ ns |
| One Bus Cycle | $N \cdot T$ | seconds | $N$ = number of clock T-states |
| Memory Access Time | $t_{ACC}$ | ns | Must satisfy $t_{ACC} \leq N \cdot T$ |
| Wait States Required | $W = \left\lceil \frac{t_{ACC} - N \cdot T}{T} \right\rceil$ | integer | If $t_{ACC} > N \cdot T$, wait states needed |
| Effective Cycle Time | $T_{eff} = (N + W) \cdot T$ | seconds | Includes wait state penalty |
| Throughput | $\theta = \frac{1}{T_{eff}}$ | operations/s | Higher throughput = better performance |
| Bus Bandwidth | $B = \frac{\text{Data Width}}{T_{eff}}$ | bytes/s | For 16-bit bus: $B = \frac{2}{T_{eff}}$ |
| Speedup (Loosely Coupled) | $S = \frac{T_{1\text{processor}}}{T_{2\text{processor}}}$ | dimensionless | Loosely coupled $S > 1$ in best case |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KRITICAL NOTATION RULE:** In KTU exam scripts, use $\vert x \vert$ written as `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` to denote absolute value — never the literal `|` character inside a table cell, as it breaks markdown rendering in the KTU digital evaluation portal.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CAD/CAM systems** — tightly coupled coprocessors accelerate 3D matrix transformations.
- **Industrial process control** — loosely coupled systems allow independent fault handling for redundancy.
- **DSP acceleration** — modern GPUs are essentially specialized coprocessors following the same architectural model.
- **Aerospace & defense** — multiprocessor systems with NUMA-like architectures trace their lineage to the 8086/8087 loosely coupled model.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Wait State Calculation for 8086 System

### Problem Statement
An 8086 system runs at **5 MHz**. The memory access time is **460 ns**. The standard bus cycle uses 4 clock T-states. Compute the number of wait states required and the effective cycle time.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Calculate the clock period.**

$$
T = \frac{1}{f_{CLK}} = \frac{1}{5 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz}} = 200 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate the duration of one bus cycle without wait states.**

$$
T_{\text{cycle}} = N \cdot T = 4 \times 200 \text{ ns} = 800 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 3: Compare the cycle time with memory access time.**

Since $T_{\text{cycle}} = 800$ ns $\gg t_{ACC} = 460$ ns, the bus cycle is *faster* than memory. Wait states are needed because the processor would complete its bus cycle before memory is ready.

Wait — correction. The 8086 standard bus cycle is **4 T-states** (T1, T2, T3, T4). The actual data is read/written during T2–T3. Memory must be ready *by the end of T3*. So:

$$
T_{\text{data-ready}} = 3 \cdot T = 3 \times 200 = 600 \text{ ns}
$$

Since $t_{ACC} = 460$ ns $< 600$ ns, **no wait states are required**. But if memory were slower, e.g., $t_{ACC} = 800$ ns, then:

**Step 4: General wait state formula.**

$$
W = \left\lceil \frac{t_{ACC} - 3T}{T} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{800 - 600}{200} \right\rceil = \lceil 1 \rceil = 1 \text{ wait state}
$$

**Step 5: Effective cycle time with 1 wait state.**

$$
T_{eff} = (N + W) \cdot T = (4 + 1) \times 200 \text{ ns} = 1000 \text{ ns} = 1 \text{ \mu s}
$$

**Step 6: Throughput calculation.**

$$
\theta = \frac{1}{T_{eff}} = \frac{1}{1 \times 10^{-6}} = 1 \times 10^{6} \text{ bus cycles/s} = 1 \text{ MIPS equivalent}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Performance Comparison — Closely vs Loosely Coupled

### Problem Statement
A system executes a program of **1000 floating-point operations**. The 8086 takes **10 µs per FP operation** (emulated). The 8087 takes **2 µs per FP operation**. Overhead for synchronization in closely coupled mode is **1 µs per operation**. Overhead in loosely coupled mode is **0.2 µs per operation**. Calculate the total execution time and speedup for both configurations.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Total time for closely coupled configuration.**

For each floating-point operation, the 8086 must:
- Fetch and decode instruction: included in 10 µs emulated time.
- Issue ESC; 8087 takes over bus.
- 8087 executes in 2 µs.
- Synchronization overhead: 1 µs (waits on TEST\).

Total per operation:

$$
T_{CC} = 2 \text{ \mu s} + 1 \text{ \mu s} = 3 \text{ \mu s per operation}
$$

Total execution time:

$$
T_{\text{CC,total}} = 1000 \times 3 \text{ \mu s} = 3000 \text{ \mu s} = 3 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2: Total time for loosely coupled configuration.**

In loosely coupled mode, the 8086 fetches instructions while 8087 executes. Synchronization overhead is lower (0.2 µs per op).

$$
T_{LC} = 2 \text{ \mu s} + 0.2 \text{ \mu s} = 2.2 \text{ \mu s per operation}
$$

Total execution time:

$$
T_{\text{LC,total}} = 1000 \times 2.2 \text{ \mu s} = 2200 \text{ \mu s} = 2.2 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3: Speedup calculation.**

$$
S = \frac{T_{\text{CC,total}}}{T_{\text{LC,total}}} = \frac{3 \text{ ms}}{2.2 \text{ ms}} \approx 1.36
$$

**Step 4: Percentage improvement.**

$$
\text{Improvement} = \frac{T_{\text{CC}} - T_{\text{LC}}}{T_{\text{CC}}} \times 100\% = \frac{3 - 2.2}{3} \times 100\% \approx 26.67\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Insight:** The closely coupled configuration is **slower per operation** due to bus contention and synchronization overhead. The loosely coupled configuration's advantage grows as the proportion of floating-point operations in a program increases.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Bus Arbitration Delay in Loosely Coupled System

### Problem Statement
In a loosely coupled system, the 8087 requests the bus via the **RQ\ / GT\** pin. The 8289 bus arbiter resolves requests in **3 clock cycles**. The clock frequency is **5 MHz**. Compute the arbitration delay and its impact on effective throughput for a 1000-bus-request workload.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Arbitration delay per request.**

$$
T_{\text{arb}} = N_{\text{arb}} \times T = 3 \times 200 \text{ ns} = 600 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 2: For 1000 bus requests, total arbitration time.**

$$
T_{\text{arb,total}} = 1000 \times 600 \text{ ns} = 600{,}000 \text{ ns} = 600 \text{ \mu s} = 0.6 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3: Impact on overall execution.**

If the actual data transfer takes 2 µs per operation (as in Derivation 2):

$$
T_{\text{effective}} = T_{\text{LC,total}} + T_{\text{arb,total}} = 2.2 \text{ ms} + 0.6 \text{ ms} = 2.8 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4: Recomputed speedup.**

$$
S' = \frac{3 \text{ ms}}{2.8 \text{ ms}} \approx 1.07
$$

This shows that the **arbitration overhead reduces the theoretical speedup**, but the loosely coupled system is still faster in practice for high-FP workloads.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — KTU Comparison Table

| Parameter | Closely Coupled | Loosely Coupled |
|---|---|---|
| Bus Controllers (8288) | **1** (shared) | **2** (one per processor) |
| Clock Generators (8284) | **1** | **2** |
| Bus Arbiter (8289) | **Not required** | **Required** |
| 8087 RQ\ / GT\ pins | **Tied HIGH** (inactive) | **Used for arbitration** |
| Parallel bus access | **No** | **Yes** |
| Hardware complexity | **Low** | **High** |
| Cost | **Low** | **High** |
| Throughput | **Lower** | **Higher** |
| Application | Embedded, cost-sensitive | High-performance, multi-master |
| Memory architecture | Single shared memory | Multi-port memory / dual access |
| 8086 role | **Master** | **Master (or peer)** |
| 8087 role | **Slave** | **Slave with independent bus** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Closely Coupled Configuration — Block Diagram

The diagram below shows the **closely coupled configuration** where the 8086 and 8087 share a single system bus, clock, and bus controller.

```mermaid
graph TB
    CLKGEN["8284 CLOCK GENERATOR"]
    BUSCTL["8288 BUS CONTROLLER"]
    CPU["8086 CPU MASTER"]
    NDP["8087 NDP SLAVE"]
    LATCH["ADDRESS LATCH 8282"]
    MEM["SHARED MEMORY"]
    IO["SHARED I/O DEVICES"]
    SYNC["TEST-BUSY SYNC LINK"]

    CLKGEN -- "CLK" --> CPU
    CLKGEN -- "CLK" --> NDP
    CLKGEN -- "CLK" --> BUSCTL

    CPU -- "S0-S2 STATUS" --> BUSCTL
    NDP -- "S0-S2 STATUS" --> BUSCTL

    BUSCTL -- "RD, WR, DEN, DT/R" --> LATCH
    CPU -- "AD0-AD15, A16-A19" --> LATCH
    LATCH -- "ADDRESS BUS" --> MEM
    LATCH -- "ADDRESS BUS" --> IO

    CPU -- "DATA BUS" --> MEM
    NDP -- "DATA BUS" --> MEM
    CPU -- "DATA BUS" --> IO
    NDP -- "DATA BUS" --> IO

    NDP -- "BUSY SIGNAL" --> SYNC
    SYNC -- "TEST INPUT" --> CPU
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:**
> - The 8086 is the bus master; 8087 is the slave.
> - Both share the address latches and the data bus.
> - The 8087's **BUSY\** output feeds the 8086's **TEST\** input to synchronize result availability.
> - Only **one** 8284, **one** 8288, and **no** 8289 arbiter.

## 4.2 Loosely Coupled Configuration — Block Diagram

The diagram below illustrates the **loosely coupled configuration** with dual bus controllers, a dedicated arbiter, and independent local buses for each processor.

```mermaid
graph TB
    CLK1["8284 CLOCK A"]
    CLK2["8284 CLOCK B"]
    CTL1["8288 BUS CONTROLLER A"]
    CTL2["8288 BUS CONTROLLER B"]
    CPU["8086 PROCESSOR A"]
    NDP["8087 PROCESSOR B"]
    ARB["8289 BUS ARBITER"]
    SHARED["MULTIBUS SYSTEM BUS"]
    MEM["COMMON MEMORY"]
    IO["COMMON I/O"]

    CLK1 -- "CLK" --> CPU
    CLK2 -- "CLK" --> NDP
    CLK1 -- "CLK" --> CTL1
    CLK2 -- "CLK" --> CTL2

    CPU -- "S0-S2" --> CTL1
    NDP -- "S0-S2" --> CTL2
    CTL1 -- "BUS CMD" --> SHARED
    CTL2 -- "BUS CMD" --> SHARED

    CPU -- "RQ/GT" --> ARB
    NDP -- "RQ/GT" --> ARB
    ARB -- "GRANT" --> CPU
    ARB -- "GRANT" --> NDP

    SHARED -- "ADDRESS-DATA" --> MEM
    SHARED -- "ADDRESS-DATA" --> IO

    CPU -. "TEST-BUSY SYNC" .-> NDP
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Visual Cues:**
> - Each processor has its **own** 8284 + 8288 (independent local bus).
> - The **8289 bus arbiter** mediates simultaneous bus requests.
> - The shared **MULTIBUS** connects to common memory and I/O.
> - The dashed line between CPU and NDP represents the **TEST\–BUSY\** synchronization link.

## 4.3 Instruction Flow — Synchronization Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant M as 8086 MASTER
    participant N as 8087 SLAVE
    participant BUS as SYSTEM BUS
    participant MEM as MEMORY

    M->>M: Fetch ESC instruction
    M->>BUS: Issue memory read (dummy)
    BUS->>MEM: Read operand address
    MEM-->>M: Returns operand (discarded by M)
    M->>N: ESC opcode captured via queue
    M->>M: Continue with next instruction
    par Parallel Execution
        N->>BUS: Request bus via RQ/GT
        BUS-->>N: Grant access
        N->>MEM: Fetch real operand
        MEM-->>N: Returns operand
        N->>N: Execute floating-point operation
        N->>N: Assert BUSY = LOW
    end
    M->>M: Execute WAIT instruction
    M->>N: Poll TEST input
    N-->>M: BUSY goes HIGH (done)
    M->>M: Use the floating-point result
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
**Q: Define a coprocessor. List any two advantages of using the 8087 coprocessor with the 8086 microprocessor.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **coprocessor** is an auxiliary processor designed to work alongside a main processor (CPU) to handle specialized computational tasks that the main processor either cannot execute or executes inefficiently.

*Advantages of the 8087:*
1. **High-speed floating-point arithmetic** — executes transcendental functions (sin, cos, log, exp) 50–100× faster than software emulation on the 8086.
2. **IEEE 754 compliance** — ensures portability of floating-point results across platforms, critical for scientific and engineering applications.
3. *Extended precision* (80-bit internal) provides higher numerical accuracy than the host CPU's software routines.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Many students write "8087 is faster" without specifying **why** (parallel execution, dedicated hardware multiplier, hardware microcode for transcendentals). Examiners expect specific technical justification for full marks.

---

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`**
**Q: Differentiate between closely coupled and loosely coupled multiprocessor configurations.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Closely Coupled | Loosely Coupled |
|---|---|---|
| Bus access | Shared, sequential | Independent, parallel |
| Bus arbiter | Not required | Required (8289) |
| Hardware cost | Low | High |
| Performance | Limited | High |

In a **closely coupled** configuration, the 8087 is tied directly to the 8086's local bus, with both sharing one 8284 clock generator and one 8288 bus controller. The 8086 acts as the master and the 8087 as the slave. They cannot access the bus simultaneously, so true parallel bus access is not possible.

In a **loosely coupled** configuration, each processor has its own 8284, 8288, and local bus. A **8289 bus arbiter** coordinates simultaneous bus requests via the RQ\ / GT\ pins. Both processors can access the common memory and I/O in parallel, giving higher throughput.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 2 Internal Choice]`**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **closely coupled configuration** of the 8086 and 8087. List its **advantages and disadvantages**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **pin connections** between the 8086 and 8087 in closely coupled mode, specifically explaining the role of **TEST\** and **BUSY\** signals in synchronization. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a) [7 marks]

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**
Refer to **Section 4.1** of these notes for the Mermaid block diagram. In text form:

- The 8086 and 8087 share a **common 8284 clock generator**, **common 8288 bus controller**, and **common address latches** (8282).
- The 8087's **RQ\ / GT\** pins are **tied HIGH** (inactive) since bus arbitration is unnecessary.
- The 8086 controls the address and data bus directly.
- The 8087's **BUSY\** output is connected to the 8086's **TEST\** input for synchronization.
- The 8086 is the **bus master**; the 8087 is the **bus slave**.

**Description (2 marks):**
When the 8086 encounters an ESC (Escape) instruction (the first 5 bits being `11011`), it:
1. Performs a dummy memory read to compute the operand address.
2. Continues to the next instruction; the 8087 simultaneously decodes the floating-point instruction.
3. The 8087 requests the bus (via internal logic) and reads the actual operand.
4. The 8087 executes the floating-point operation.
5. The 8086, before using the result, executes a **WAIT** instruction and polls the **TEST\** pin until the 8087's **BUSY\** signal goes HIGH (inactive).

**Advantages (1 mark):**
- Simple hardware design — no bus arbiter required.
- Low cost (single 8284 + 8288).
- Suitable for cost-sensitive embedded applications.

**Disadvantages (1 mark):**
- No true parallel bus access — performance bottleneck.
- 8086 and 8087 cannot fetch/execute simultaneously on the bus.

#### Model Solution — (b) [7 marks]

**Key Pin Connections in Closely Coupled Mode (3 marks):**

| 8086 Pin | 8087 Pin | Connection Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **TEST\** (input) | **BUSY\** (output) | Synchronization of result readiness |
| **CLK** | **CLK** | Common clock from 8284 |
| **S0, S1, S2** | (not used by 8087) | 8086 drives bus controller status |
| **QS0, QS1** | **QS0, QS1** (input) | 8086 queue status sent to 8087 for instruction tracking |
| **RQ\ / GT\** (8086) | (8087's tied HIGH) | No arbitration needed |

**Role of TEST\ and BUSY\ (4 marks):**

- The **8086** uses the **WAIT** instruction to test its **TEST\** pin.
- The **8087** keeps its **BUSY\** output **LOW** while executing a floating-point instruction.
- The connection is: `8087's BUSY\  →  8086's TEST\`.
- When the 8086 executes a **WAIT** instruction:
  - It samples the **TEST\** pin every 5 clock cycles (5T).
  - If **TEST\** = 1 (i.e., **BUSY\** has gone HIGH), the floating-point operation is complete.
  - The 8086 proceeds to use the result.
- If **TEST\** = 0 (8087 still busy), the 8086 re-enters the wait loop.

**Synchronization Sequence (textual):**
1. 8086 issues ESC; 8087 starts execution.
2. 8086 encounters next floating-point variable use → executes **WAIT**.
3. 8086 polls TEST\ every 5T until HIGH.
4. 8087 finishes → raises BUSY\ HIGH.
5. 8086 reads result from memory/8087 register and continues.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert (KTU 2024):**
> Many students incorrectly write that the 8086 *waits continuously* on the TEST\ pin. The actual KTU board-accepted answer: **the 8086 polls the TEST\ input once every 5 clock cycles** (5T), not every T-state. Also, ensure you state that the WAIT instruction is *inserted by the assembler automatically* — the programmer does not write it explicitly in MASM (Microsoft Assembler). This is a common 1-mark loss.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**`[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 2 Internal Choice]`**

**(a)** Explain the **loosely coupled configuration** with a block diagram. Discuss the function of the **bus arbiter (8289)**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **RQ\ / GT\** handshake protocol used in loosely coupled multiprocessor systems. How does the 8087 request and release the bus? Compare the throughput with the closely coupled configuration. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a) [7 marks]

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**
Refer to **Section 4.2** of these notes for the Mermaid block diagram. In text form:

- The 8086 and 8087 each have their **own 8284 clock generator** and **own 8288 bus controller**.
- Both connect to a **common system bus** (the *MULTIBUS* in Intel's terminology).
- An **8289 bus arbiter** is connected between the two processors' **RQ\ / GT\** pins.
- The 8087's local bus is independent of the 8086's local bus.
- Shared memory and I/O are connected to the common system bus.

**Function of the 8289 Bus Arbiter (4 marks):**

The 8289 resolves simultaneous bus requests from multiple processors by implementing a **priority-based arbitration scheme**. Its main functions are:

1. **Request detection** — monitors the RQ\ line from each processor.
2. **Priority resolution** — assigns priority (e.g., 8086 = master, 8087 = slave) using the **CBRQ** (Common Bus Request) input and **SYSB** (System Bus) modes.
3. **Grant issuance** — when the higher-priority processor releases the bus, the arbiter grants access to the requesting processor via the GT\ line.
4. **Bus lock support** — supports the **LOCK\** prefix for indivisible read-modify-write operations.
5. **Multi-arbiter cascade** — multiple 8289s can be cascaded for more than 2 processors.

**Operating modes of 8289:**
- **SINGLE** — for single-master systems.
- **MULTI** — for multi-master systems (the loosely coupled case).
- **I/O** — for I/O-bound peripherals.

#### Model Solution — (b) [7 marks]

**RQ\ / GT\ Handshake Protocol (4 marks):**

The 8086 and 8087 use a **3-pin bidirectional request/grant** mechanism on their **RQ\ / GT\** pins. The protocol has **three states**:

1. **Request (State 1):** The 8087 asserts **RQ\** (active LOW) when it needs the system bus. This occurs in the **T4 or T1** state of its current cycle.

2. **Grant (State 2):** The 8086 (master) releases the bus by:
   - Floating its address, data, and control outputs (entering high-impedance).
   - Asserting **GT\** LOW in the next T4/T1 state.
   - The 8087 detects this grant and prepares to take over.

3. **Release (State 3):** After the 8087 completes its bus cycle(s):
   - It releases **GT\** HIGH.
   - The 8086 regains bus mastership and continues execution.
   - If the 8087 needs more bus cycles, it re-asserts RQ\.

**Throughput Comparison (3 marks):**

| Metric | Closely Coupled | Loosely Coupled |
|---|---|---|
| Bus utilization | One master at a time | Two masters in parallel |
| FP operation time | ~3 µs | ~2.2 µs |
| Synchronization overhead | 1 µs (WAIT loops) | 0.2 µs (RQ/GT handshake) |
| Effective speedup (typical) | 1× (baseline) | **1.3× – 1.5×** for FP-heavy code |

The loosely coupled configuration achieves **higher throughput** because the 8086 and 8087 fetch/execute in parallel, eliminating the WAIT-state bottleneck inherent in the closely coupled design.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall — KTU Board Examiner's Note:**
> A common mistake is to claim the 8087 *automatically* takes the bus in loosely coupled mode. **It does not.** The 8087 must *request* the bus via RQ\ and *wait* for GT\ from the 8086 (or from the 8289 arbiter). Writing "the 8087 directly accesses the bus" will cost 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2**

- ⭐ **Coprocessor** = auxiliary processor offloading specialized tasks; for 8086, this is the **8087 NDP**.
- ⭐ The 8087 supports **IEEE 754** floating-point formats: 32-bit, 64-bit, 80-bit extended.
- ⭐ The 8086 fetches ALL instructions; the 8087 only processes **ESC** (Escape) opcodes (first 5 bits = `11011`).
- ⭐ The 8086 and 8087 communicate via the **TEST\–BUSY\** link.
- ⭐ **Closely coupled** = single 8284, single 8288, no 8289, 8087's RQ\ / GT\ tied HIGH, 8086 is bus master, 8087 is slave, no true parallel bus access.
- ⭐ **Loosely coupled** = dual 8284, dual 8288, 8289 arbiter present, both processors have independent local buses, true parallel execution possible.
- ⭐ The 8086's **WAIT** instruction is *inserted automatically by MASM* before floating-point result usage; it polls TEST\ every **5 clock cycles**.
- ⭐ The **RQ\ / GT\** handshake has 3 states: Request, Grant, Release.
- ⭐ Wait state formula: $W = \lceil (t_{ACC} - N \cdot T) / T \rceil$ for slow memory.
- ⭐ For 5 MHz clock, $T = 200$ ns; standard 4T bus cycle = 800 ns.
- ⭐ Throughput $\theta = 1 / T_{eff}$; Bandwidth $B = \text{Data Width} / T_{eff}$.
- ⭐ **Key comparison metric**: Loosely coupled is faster for FP-heavy workloads; closely coupled is cheaper and simpler.
- ⭐ The 8087 has **8 × 80-bit** internal register stack (ST(0)–ST(7)).
- ⭐ Status pin **S0–S2** of 8086/8087 drive the 8288 bus controller.
- ⭐ **QS0/QS1** queue status lines from 8086 help the 8087 track instruction flow.

**Mnemonic for KTU Viva:**
> **"C**oupled **C**onfigurations: **C**losely = **C**heap, **C**ontended (sequential bus). **L**oosely = **L**uxurious, **L**iberated (parallel bus with 8289 arbiter)."

**Common KTU 2024 Question Patterns to Expect:**
1. "Explain the closely coupled configuration with diagram." (7 marks)
2. "Differentiate closely and loosely coupled configurations." (3–7 marks)
3. "Describe the function of the 8089 bus arbiter." (7 marks)
4. "Explain the RQ\ / GT\ handshake." (7 marks)
5. "Why is WAIT instruction used in coprocessor systems?" (3 marks)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Advanced Processors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCIT404 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Advanced Processors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are "Advanced Processors"?

In the context of the **Intel x86 family evolution**, the term *Advanced Processors* refers to the post-8086 generation of microprocessors that broke free from the 16-bit architectural limitations of the original 8086/8088. The canonical representative studied in the KTU syllabus is the **Intel 80386 (i386)**, along with brief extensions into the **80486** and the **Pentium** family. These are collectively classified as **32-bit CISC (Complex Instruction Set Computer)** processors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> An *Advanced Processor* is a microprocessor that integrates a **32-bit internal architecture** (general-purpose registers, ALU, address bus, and data bus), supports **demand-paged virtual memory**, **multitasking through hardware-supported protection rings**, and executes instructions through a **pipelined fetch-decode-execute** organization. The 80386 is the first Intel processor to combine a **segmentation unit** and a **paging unit** for full memory management.

## 1.2 The Evolution Lineage — From 8086 to Pentium

To understand the word *advanced*, we need to see what these processors advanced *from*:

| Processor | Year | Data Bus | Address Bus | Register Width | Physical Memory |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 8086 | 1978 | **16-bit** | 20-bit | 16-bit | **1 MB** |
| 80286 | 1982 | 16-bit | 24-bit | 16-bit | 16 MB |
| **80386 DX** | **1985** | **32-bit** | **32-bit** | **32-bit** | **4 GB** |
| 80486 DX | 1989 | 32-bit | 32-bit | 32-bit | 4 GB |
| Pentium (P5) | 1993 | 64-bit | 32-bit | 32-bit | 4 GB |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Office Building"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — From a Tiny Shop to a Skyscraper:**
> - The **8086** is like a **single-room office**: one door (16-bit data), one phone line (single bus), one clerk doing everything sequentially. Maximum occupancy: **1 MB** of files.
> - The **80386** is a **full corporate skyscraper**: a **32-bit wide front door** (buses), a **security desk that checks visitor passes** (protection rings), a **filing system with shortcuts** (paging + TLB), and **multiple elevators running in parallel** (pipelining). It can address **4 GB directly** and up to **64 TB virtually**.
> - The **Pentium** is that same skyscraper with **two front doors** (64-bit external data bus) so two streams of people can enter simultaneously (**superscalar dual-pipeline**).

The "advanced" label therefore means three concrete hardware capabilities:
1. **Wider datapath** → 32-bit internal processing.
2. **Virtual memory** → programs behave as if they have **64 TB** of RAM, even on a 4 GB machine.
3. **Hardware protection** → multiple programs (multitasking) can run safely without trampling each other.

## 1.4 Why Study the 80386 Specifically?

The 80386 is the **architectural foundation** of all modern x86 operating systems (Windows, Linux, macOS). Every 64-bit Intel/AMD CPU in your laptop today still **boots in 8086 real mode**, then switches to a **protected mode descendant of the 386**. Studying the 80386 is therefore not historical — it is the **conceptual root** of contemporary computing.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> The board examiner expects you to master (a) the **register set**, (b) **operating modes**, (c) **segmentation + paging** memory model, (d) **pipelining**, and (e) **protection mechanism** of the 80386.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 80386DX — Salient Features

The **Intel 80386 DX** (where DX = *Double-word eXternal*, meaning a true 32-bit external data bus) is the workhorse of Module 2. Its features can be grouped under four pillars:

### Pillar 1 — 32-Bit Architecture
- **32-bit data bus** → can fetch 4 bytes in one memory cycle.
- **32-bit address bus** → direct addressing of $2^{32} = \mathbf{4\,GB}$ physical memory.
- **32-bit registers and ALU** → native 32-bit arithmetic, no need to pair two 16-bit registers.

### Pillar 2 — Integrated Memory Management Unit (MMU)
The chip contains two cooperating units:

- **Segmentation Unit** — translates a **logical address** (segment:offset) into a **linear address**.
- **Paging Unit** — translates the **linear address** into a **physical address**.

This two-stage translation is what enables **virtual memory**.

### Pillar 3 — Operating Modes (Three of Them)
1. **Real Mode** — inherited from the 8086. 1 MB address space, no protection, no paging. Used only for booting (BIOS, bootloaders).
2. **Protected Mode** — the native 32-bit mode. Full 4 GB physical, virtual memory via paging, 4 privilege levels (rings 0–3).
3. **Virtual 8086 Mode** — a sub-mode of Protected Mode that lets 8086 programs run *as if* they owned the whole machine, while the hardware secretly multiplexes them. This is how DOS emulators work.

### Pillar 4 — Pipelining
The 80386 uses a **3-stage pipeline**:
1. **Bus Interface Unit (BIU)** — fetches instructions and data from memory.
2. **Instruction Decode Unit (IDU)** — decodes opcodes and computes effective addresses.
3. **Execution Unit (EU)** — performs the operation and writes results.

While the EU is executing instruction *n*, the IDU is decoding *n+1* and the BIU is fetching *n+2*. This is **instruction-level parallelism** at its simplest.

## 2.2 The 80386 Register Set — The Heart of the Topic

The 80386 has **eight categories** of registers. Mastering them is the single highest-yield activity for KTU exams.

### Category 1: General Purpose Registers (8 registers, 32-bit)

These are the **extended** (prefixed with `E`) versions of the 8086 registers. The lower 16 bits are still directly addressable for backward compatibility.

| 32-bit | 16-bit | 8-bit (low) | 8-bit (high) | Conventional Use |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| **EAX** | AX | AL | AH | Accumulator (arithmetic, I/O) |
| **EBX** | BX | BL | BH | Base register (memory pointer) |
| **ECX** | CX | CL | CH | Counter (loops, shifts) |
| **EDX** | DX | DL | DH | Data register (multiplication, I/O port) |
| **ESI** | SI | — | — | Source Index (string operations) |
| **EDI** | DI | — | — | Destination Index (string operations) |
| **EBP** | BP | — | — | Base Pointer (stack frame) |
| **ESP** | SP | — | — | Stack Pointer (top of stack) |

### Category 2: Segment Registers (6 registers, 16-bit)

In protected mode, these are no longer shifted left 4 times. They are **selectors** — indexes into a **descriptor table** (GDT or LDT).

| Register | Stands For | Typical Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **CS** | Code Segment | Where instructions are fetched from |
| **DS** | Data Segment | Default for data accesses |
| **SS** | Stack Segment | Default for stack operations |
| **ES** | Extra Segment | String instruction destination |
| **FS** | (extra) | Thread-local storage (modern OS) |
| **GS** | (extra) | Kernel data in modern OS |

### Category 3: Instruction Pointer and Flags
- **EIP** (32-bit) — points to the **next instruction** to be fetched. Replaces the 16-bit IP.
- **EFLAGS** (32-bit) — contains the condition code flags, control flags (IF, TF, IOPL, NT, RF, VM), and system flags. The **VM flag** (bit 17) is what enables **Virtual 8086 Mode**.

### Category 4: Control Registers (CR0, CR2, CR3) — 32-bit
These are the *master switches* of protected mode.

| Register | Key Bits | Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **CR0** | PE (bit 0) | Protection Enable — `1` = protected mode |
| **CR0** | PG (bit 31) | Paging Enable — `1` = paging on |
| **CR2** | — | **Page Fault Linear Address** (the address that caused the last page fault) |
| **CR3** | — | **Page Directory Base Register** (physical address of the page directory) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students write *CR2 = page directory base*. Wrong! **CR2 stores the offending address that triggered a page fault.** The **page directory base is in CR3.**

### Category 5: System Address Registers
- **GDTR** (48 bits) — base + limit of the **G**lobal **D**escriptor **T**able.
- **IDTR** (48 bits) — base + limit of the **I**nterrupt **D**escriptor **T**able.
- **LDTR** (16-bit selector) — points to the current **L**ocal **D**escriptor **T**able.
- **TR** (16-bit selector) — points to the current **T**ask **S**tate **S**egment (TSS).

### Category 6: Debug Registers (DR0–DR7)
Used to set up to **4 hardware breakpoints**. DR0–DR3 store breakpoint linear addresses; DR7 enables/disables them.

### Category 7: Test Registers (TR6, TR7)
Used internally to test the **Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB)**.

## 2.3 Address Translation — The Two-Stage MMU

### Stage A: Segmentation
In protected mode, the logical address is **not** segment × 16 + offset. Instead, the **segment selector** (16 bits) indexes a **segment descriptor** in the GDT/LDT, and the descriptor provides a **32-bit base address**.

$$\text{Linear Address} = \text{Segment Base (from descriptor)} + \text{Effective Offset}$$

### Stage B: Paging
The linear address is split into three fields that index a **two-level page table**:

$$\text{Linear Address} = \underbrace{\text{Dir}_{10}}_{\text{Page Dir Index}} \;\vert\; \underbrace{\text{Table}_{10}}_{\text{Page Table Index}} \;\vert\; \underbrace{\text{Offset}_{12}}_{\text{Byte within page}}$$

A 4 KB page has $2^{12} = 4096$ bytes, hence a 12-bit offset.

## 2.4 Pipelining — The 3-Stage 386 Pipeline

The 80386 splits work across three independent units that operate in parallel:

- **BIU** (Bus Interface Unit) — handles all external bus cycles.
- **IDU** (Instruction Decode Unit) — converts opcodes into micro-ops and computes addresses.
- **EU** (Execution Unit) — performs ALU, shifts, multiplications, etc.

Throughput improves because **N instructions can be in three different stages simultaneously**. The 80486 later extended this to a **5-stage** pipeline, and the Pentium to a **dual 5-stage superscalar** pipeline.

## 2.5 Protection Mechanism — The 4 Rings

The 80386 enforces hardware isolation through four privilege levels (rings):

- **Ring 0** — Most privileged. Kernel / OS core.
- **Ring 1** — Device drivers (rarely used).
- **Ring 2** — OS services (rarely used).
- **Ring 3** — Least privileged. User applications.

A user program in Ring 3 **cannot** access Ring 0 memory or execute privileged instructions. Any attempt triggers a **General Protection Fault (#GP)**.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Commit these formulas to memory — they are examiner favorites.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Relation | Notes |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Physical memory (bytes) | $N_{phys} = 2^{a}$ where $a$ = address bus bits | 80386: $2^{32} = 4\,\text{GB}$ |
| 2 | Virtual memory (bytes) | $N_{virt} = 2^{s} \times 2^{o}$ | 80386: $2^{32} \times 2^{32} = 64\,\text{TB}$ theoretically; practical $\approx 2^{46}$ |
| 3 | Page offset bits | $o = \log_2(\text{page size})$ | 4 KB page → $o = 12$ |
| 4 | Page directory index bits | $\text{Dir bits} = 10$ | For 4 KB pages, 32-bit linear address split as 10/10/12 |
| 5 | Page table entries | $2^{10} = 1024$ entries per table | One page table = 4 KB |
| 6 | Page directory entries | $2^{10} = 1024$ entries per directory | One page directory = 4 KB |
| 7 | Linear address from seg:off | $\text{LA} = \text{SegBase} + \text{Offset}$ | Protected mode, **no left shift by 4** |
| 8 | Physical address (when paging off) | $\text{PA} = \text{LA}$ | When CR0.PG = 0 |
| 9 | Number of rings | 4 (Rings 0–3) | Ring 0 = most privileged |
| 10 | Number of GDT entries | $\le 8192$ | Descriptor = 8 bytes, GDT limit $\le 65535$ |
| 11 | Selector format | 13-bit index $\vert$ 1-bit TI $\vert$ 2-bit RPL | Total 16 bits |
| 12 | Descriptor size | 8 bytes | Base(4) + Limit(2) + Access(1) + Flags+Base(1) |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

The 80386 architecture is the direct ancestor of every modern x86-64 CPU. Concepts like **rings of protection** still exist (kernel mode vs user mode), **page tables** still translate virtual to physical addresses, and **CR3 switching on context switch** is still the mechanism by which an OS swaps between processes. Engineers writing device drivers, hypervisors, or bootloaders must understand these primitives intimately. Even Android emulators and Docker containers rely on descendants of the 386 paging system.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maximum Physical Memory Addressable

**Problem:** Compute the maximum addressable physical memory of the 80386DX.

**Given:** Address bus width $a = 32$ bits.

**Step 1 — Address bus interpretation.**
A bus of $a$ bits can represent $2^{a}$ distinct binary patterns. Each pattern corresponds to exactly **one unique memory location** (one byte).

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$N_{phys} = 2^{a} = 2^{32}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the power of 2.**

$$2^{32} = (2^{10})^{3} \times 2^{2} = 1024^{3} \times 4$$

**Step 4 — Convert to engineering units.**

$$2^{32} = 4 \times 1024 \times 1024 \times 1024 = 4\,\text{GB}$$

$$\boxed{N_{phys} = 4\,\text{GB} = 4\,294\,967\,296\,\text{bytes}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maximum Virtual Address Space

**Problem:** A 32-bit processor with 32-bit offsets and 32-bit segment bases can in theory address what virtual space?

**Step 1 — Segments.**
With a 32-bit segment base and 32-bit segment limit, each segment can span up to $2^{32} = 4\,\text{GB}$.

**Step 2 — Number of segments.**
The selector index field is 13 bits wide, allowing up to $2^{13} = 8192$ selectors per descriptor table, and the OS can swap descriptor tables.

**Step 3 — Multiplicative bound (theoretical).**

$$N_{virt,\;theory} = 2^{32} \times 2^{32} = 2^{64} = 16\,\text{EB (exabytes)}$$

> The classic textbook number quoted for the 386 is **64 TB** ($2^{46}$), because practical limit and segment descriptor formats cap the effective reach. Both numbers are accepted in KTU answers, but **64 TB is the standard textbook answer**.

$$\boxed{N_{virt} = 64\,\text{TB}\;\text{(practical)} \;\;\text{or}\;\; 16\,\text{EB (theoretical)}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Linear Address Decomposition for 4 KB Paging

**Problem:** Show the bit-fields of a 32-bit linear address when paging is enabled with 4 KB pages.

**Step 1 — Page size determines offset bits.**

$$o = \log_2(4096) = \log_2(2^{12}) = 12\,\text{bits}$$

**Step 2 — Page table size constraint.**
A page table must fit inside one 4 KB page. Each entry (PTE) is 4 bytes.

$$\text{Entries per page table} = \frac{4\,\text{KB}}{4\,\text{B}} = 1024 = 2^{10}$$

So the **page table index** needs $10$ bits.

**Step 3 — Page directory index.**
The remaining bits of the 32-bit address form the directory index.

$$d = 32 - 10 - 12 = 10\,\text{bits}$$

**Step 4 — Verify the page directory also fits a 4 KB page.**

$$\text{PDEs per directory} = \frac{4\,\text{KB}}{4\,\text{B}} = 1024 = 2^{10} \;\; \checkmark$$

**Step 5 — Final bit-field formula.**

$$\text{Linear Address} = \underbrace{31\ldots22}_{10\,\text{bits},\;\text{Dir}} \;\vert\; \underbrace{21\ldots12}_{10\,\text{bits},\;\text{Table}} \;\vert\; \underbrace{11\ldots0}_{12\,\text{bits},\;\text{Offset}}$$

$$\boxed{32 = 10 + 10 + 12}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Selector Format

**Problem:** A 16-bit segment selector contains three fields. Derive their widths.

**Step 1 — Index field.** The descriptor table is at most 64 KB. With 8-byte descriptors:

$$N_{max} = \frac{2^{16}\,\text{bytes}}{8\,\text{bytes/descriptor}} = 8192 = 2^{13}\,\text{descriptors}$$

So the **index** needs $13$ bits.

**Step 2 — Table Indicator (TI).** One bit suffices: 0 = GDT, 1 = LDT.

**Step 3 — Requested Privilege Level (RPL).** Two bits encode rings 0–3.

**Step 4 — Total bits.**

$$13 + 1 + 2 = 16 \;\; \checkmark$$

$$\boxed{\text{Selector} = \underbrace{b_{15}\ldots b_{3}}_{13\,\text{index}} \;\vert\; \underbrace{b_{2}}_{\text{TI}} \;\vert\; \underbrace{b_{1}b_{0}}_{\text{RPL}}}$$

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Page Table Memory Footprint

**Problem:** How much physical memory is consumed by the page tables of one process whose entire 4 GB virtual space is mapped with 4 KB pages?

**Step 1 — Number of page tables.** Each page table covers $1024 \times 4\,\text{KB} = 4\,\text{MB}$ of virtual address space. For 4 GB:

$$N_{tables} = \frac{4\,\text{GB}}{4\,\text{MB}} = 1024 = 2^{10}$$

**Step 2 — Size per page table.** Each table is 4 KB.

**Step 3 — Total page table memory.**

$$M_{tables} = 1024 \times 4\,\text{KB} = 4\,\text{MB}$$

**Step 4 — Add the page directory.**

$$M_{directory} = 4\,\text{KB}$$

**Step 5 — Total paging overhead per fully-mapped 4 GB process.**

$$M_{total} = 4\,\text{MB} + 4\,\text{KB} \approx 4\,\text{MB}$$

$$\boxed{M_{paging} = 4\,\text{MB} + 4\,\text{KB}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** In real systems, only *used* pages are mapped. The OS allocates page tables *lazily* — on demand, when a page fault occurs. This is called **demand paging**.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Side-by-Side Mode Comparison

| Feature | Real Mode (8086) | Protected Mode (80386) | Virtual 8086 Mode |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Default bus width | 16-bit | 32-bit | 16-bit (emulated) |
| Max physical memory | **1 MB** | **4 GB** | 1 MB *per V86 task* |
| Address calculation | $\text{Seg}\times 16 + \text{Off}$ | SegBase + Off (via descriptor) | Same as real |
| Paging | Not supported | Optional (controlled by CR0.PG) | Available |
| Protection rings | None | **4 rings (0–3)** | 8086-like, but ring 3 |
| Multitasking | None | Full (via TSS + CR3 switch) | Each V86 task is separate |
| Used by | DOS, boot code | All modern OS kernels | DOS emulators, NTVDM |

## 3.7 Worked Example — Linear-to-Physical Walk

**Problem:** Given:
- CR3 = `0x00005000` (page directory base)
- PDE at directory index 5: PTE pointer = `0x00009000`
- PTE at table index 3: frame base = `0x00008000`
- Offset within page = `0x1A4`

**Find:** Final physical address.

**Step 1 — Read page directory entry at byte offset.**

$$\text{PD offset} = 5 \times 4 = 20 = 0x14$$

**Step 2 — From PTE, frame base** = `0x8000`.

**Step 3 — Compute physical address.**

$$\text{PA} = 0x8000 + 0x1A4 = 0x81A4$$

$$\boxed{\text{PA} = \mathbf{0x81A4}}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — 80386 Internal Architecture

This mermaid diagram captures the functional blocks, the buses, and the data flow between the BIU, IDU, EU, MMU, and registers.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[External Address Bus 32-bit] --> B
    Z[External Data Bus 32-bit] --> B
    B["Bus Interface Unit BIU"] --> C["Instruction Decode Unit IDU"]
    C --> D["Execution Unit EU ALU and Shifter"]
    C --> E["Segmentation Unit"]
    E --> F["Paging Unit"]
    F --> G[Physical Address to BIU]
    B <--> H["Prefetch Queue 16 bytes"]
    D --> I["General Registers EAX EBX ECX EDX ESI EDI EBP ESP"]
    D --> J["Segment Registers CS DS SS ES FS GS"]
    D --> K["EIP and EFLAGS"]
    D --> L["Control Registers CR0 CR2 CR3"]
    D --> M["System Address Registers GDTR IDTR LDTR TR"]
    D --> N["Debug and Test Registers"]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Two-Stage Address Translation

This shows how a **logical address** flows through the segmentation and paging units to become a **physical address**.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1["Program issues MOV EAX 0x12345678"]
    P2["Logical Address Selector 0x08 plus Offset 0x12345678"]
    P3["Segmentation Unit reads GDT or LDT descriptor"]
    P4["Descriptor returns Base 0x00400000 and Limit"]
    P5["Linear Address = Base plus Offset = 0x01445678"]
    P6{"CR0 PG bit = 1?"}
    P7["Paging Unit splits 0x01445678 into Dir 5 Table 1 Offset 0x678"]
    P8["CR3 points to Page Directory base"]
    P9["PDE 5 yields Page Table 0x00009000"]
    P10["PTE 1 yields Frame 0x00080000"]
    P11["Physical Address = 0x00080678"]
    P12["Linear Address used directly as Physical Address"]
    P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5 --> P6
    P6 -- yes --> P7 --> P8 --> P9 --> P10 --> P11
    P6 -- no --> P12
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Pipelined Execution Flow

This captures the 3-stage pipeline of the 80386 across four consecutive clock cycles.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph C1[Cycle 1]
        A1["BIU fetches Instruction I1"]
        B1["IDU idle"]
        C1e["EU idle"]
    end
    subgraph C2[Cycle 2]
        A2["BIU fetches Instruction I2"]
        B2["IDU decodes Instruction I1"]
        C2e["EU idle"]
    end
    subgraph C3[Cycle 3]
        A3["BIU fetches Instruction I3"]
        B3["IDU decodes Instruction I2"]
        C3e["EU executes Instruction I1"]
    end
    subgraph C4[Cycle 4]
        A4["BIU fetches Instruction I4"]
        B4["IDU decodes Instruction I3"]
        C4e["EU executes Instruction I2"]
    end
    C1 --> C2 --> C3 --> C4
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Protection Ring Hierarchy

A visual of the 4-level privilege hierarchy enforced by the 80386.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R0["Ring 0 Most privileged Kernel and HAL"]
    R1["Ring 1 Device drivers rarely used"]
    R2["Ring 2 OS services rarely used"]
    R3["Ring 3 Least privileged User applications"]
    R0 --- R1
    R1 --- R2
    R2 --- R3
    G["Calls from outer ring to inner ring go through controlled Gate Descriptors"]
    R3 -. gate .-> R0
    R3 -. gate .-> R1
    R3 -. gate .-> R2
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Linear Address Field Decomposition for 4 KB Paging

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["32-bit Linear Address"]
    A --> B["Bits 31 to 22 10 bits Page Directory Index"]
    A --> C["Bits 21 to 12 10 bits Page Table Index"]
    A --> D["Bits 11 to 0 12 bits Byte Offset within 4 KB page"]
    B --> E["CR3 plus Dir index 4 gives PDE address"]
    E --> F["PDE points to Page Table base"]
    F --> G["Page Table plus Table index 4 gives PTE address"]
    G --> H["PTE yields 4 KB Frame base address"]
    H --> I["Frame base plus Offset 12 bits yields final Physical Address"]
```

## 4.6 Block Diagram — 80386 Register Set Family Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    RS["80386 Register Set"]
    RS --> GPR["General Purpose 32-bit"]
    RS --> SEG["Segment Registers 16-bit selectors"]
    RS --> CTRL["Control and System Registers"]
    RS --> DBG["Debug and Test Registers"]
    GPR --> G1["EAX EBX ECX EDX"]
    GPR --> G2["ESI EDI EBP ESP"]
    SEG --> S1["CS DS SS ES FS GS"]
    CTRL --> C1["EIP and EFLAGS"]
    CTRL --> C2["CR0 CR2 CR3"]
    CTRL --> C3["GDTR IDTR LDTR TR"]
    DBG --> D1["DR0 DR1 DR2 DR3 DR6 DR7"]
    DBG --> T1["TR6 TR7 for TLB testing"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
