# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Pattern]**

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
**Differentiate between Real Mode and Protected Mode of operation of the 80386 microprocessor.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Real Mode | Protected Mode |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Address calculation | $\text{Physical} = \text{Seg} \times 16 + \text{Offset}$ | $\text{Linear} = \text{SegBase (from descriptor)} + \text{Offset}$ |
| Max memory | **1 MB** | **4 GB** (with paging) |
| Protection rings | None | **4 rings (0–3)** |
| Paging | Not supported | Supported via CR0.PG |
| Multitasking | Not possible | Full hardware support |
| Segment registers | Direct 16-bit base | 16-bit **selectors** indexing GDT/LDT |

> Real Mode is inherited from 8086 and is used only during bootstrap. Protected Mode is the native 32-bit mode with full memory management and hardware-enforced protection.

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**Explain the function of the Control Register CR0 in the 80386.**

**Model Answer:**
CR0 is a 32-bit control register that holds the system-wide configuration switches of the processor. The two most important bits are:

- **PE (Protection Enable, bit 0):** When set to 1, the processor switches from Real Mode to Protected Mode.
- **PG (Paging Enable, bit 31):** When set to 1, the Paging Unit is enabled. Linear addresses are translated to physical addresses through page tables.
- **EM (Emulation, bit 2):** Controls whether the floating-point unit is emulated.
- **TS (Task Switched, bit 3):** Set by the processor on every task switch, used together with EM for lazy FPU context switching.

To enter full 32-bit protected mode with paging, the OS sets `CR0 = CR0 \vert (1 \ll 0) \vert (1 \ll 31)`.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted to 2024 Scheme Pattern]**
> *Each 14-mark question has two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.*

---

### Choice 1 — Question A (14 Marks)

**Q. A (a) [7 Marks]:** With a neat block diagram, explain the internal architecture of the 80386 microprocessor. List the functions of the Bus Interface Unit, Instruction Decode Unit, and Execution Unit.

#### Model Solution:

The 80386 is organized around **three pipelined functional units** cooperating with the **Memory Management Unit (MMU)**.

1. **Bus Interface Unit (BIU)**
   - The only unit that drives the external address and data buses.
   - Contains a **16-byte prefetch queue** that pre-fetches instructions ahead of the decoder.
   - Performs read/write bus cycles for both the prefetcher and the EU.
   - Handles all bus arbitration, ready, and wait-state signalling.

2. **Instruction Decode Unit (IDU)**
   - Decodes opcodes from the prefetch queue into a sequence of **internal micro-operations**.
   - Computes **effective addresses** (e.g., `[EBX+ESI*4+8]`) by adding the components to produce a 32-bit linear address.
   - Passes decoded micro-ops to the Execution Unit.

3. **Execution Unit (EU)**
   - Contains the **32-bit ALU**, barrel shifter, multiplier/divider.
   - Reads operands from the **register file** (8 × 32-bit general purpose registers).
   - Writes results back to registers or memory.
   - Updates the **EFLAGS** register based on results (Zero, Carry, Sign, Overflow).

4. **Memory Management Unit (MMU)**
   - **Segmentation Unit** — converts logical to linear addresses using GDT/LDT descriptors.
   - **Paging Unit** — converts linear to physical addresses using two-level page tables.
   - Contains a **Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB)** that caches recent translations for speed.

5. **Register File** — General purpose, segment, control, system address, debug, and test registers.

The three units work in **pipeline parallelism**: while the EU executes instruction $I_n$, the IDU decodes $I_{n+1}$ and the BIU fetches $I_{n+2}$.

---

**Q. A (b) [7 Marks]:** Explain the descriptor format used in Protected Mode. How is a logical address translated to a linear address?

#### Model Solution:

In Protected Mode, the 16-bit segment register is a **selector** containing three fields:

| Bits | Field | Meaning |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 15–3 | Index | Index into GDT (TI=0) or LDT (TI=1), 8192 entries max |
| 2 | TI | Table Indicator: 0 = GDT, 1 = LDT |
| 1–0 | RPL | Requested Privilege Level (0–3) |

**Segment Descriptor Format (8 bytes):**

| Byte offset | Field | Size |
| :---: | :--- | :---: |
| 0–1 | Limit (low) | 16 bits |
| 2–3 | Base (low) | 16 bits |
| 4 | Base (middle) | 8 bits |
| 5 | Access Rights (P, DPL, S, Type) | 8 bits |
| 6 | Limit (high 4 bits) + G, D, U, X | 8 bits |
| 7 | Base (high 8 bits) | 8 bits |

So a descriptor contains a **32-bit base**, a **20-bit limit** (G bit scales this to 4 KB granularity, G=1), and the **access rights byte**.

**Address Translation Steps:**

1. The CPU reads the selector from the segment register.
2. The Index field locates the descriptor in the GDT or LDT.
3. The CPU verifies that the **RPL ≤ DPL** (privilege check).
4. The **Base** from the descriptor is added to the **Effective Offset** to produce the **Linear Address**:

$$\text{Linear Address} = \text{Descriptor.Base}_{32} + \text{Effective Offset}_{32}$$

This linear address is then optionally passed through the **Paging Unit** to obtain the final physical address.

---

### Choice 2 — Question B (14 Marks) — *ALTERNATIVE*

**Q. B (a) [7 Marks]:** Describe the paging mechanism of the 80386. Explain how a 32-bit linear address is translated to a 20-bit physical address with the help of a two-level page table structure.

#### Model Solution:

Paging is enabled by setting **CR0.PG = 1**. It divides the **4 GB linear address space** into fixed-size **pages** and maps each page to a **4 KB physical frame** through a two-level page table.

**Page Size:** 4 KB = $2^{12}$ bytes, so the offset within a page needs 12 bits.

**Linear Address Decomposition:**

$$\text{Linear Address} = \underbrace{b_{31}\ldots b_{22}}_{10\text{-bit Dir Index}} \;\vert\; \underbrace{b_{21}\ldots b_{12}}_{10\text{-bit Table Index}} \;\vert\; \underbrace{b_{11}\ldots b_{0}}_{12\text{-bit Offset}}$$

**Page Directory:**
- A single 4 KB page containing 1024 **Page Directory Entries (PDEs)**.
- Its physical base address is stored in **CR3**.
- Each PDE is 4 bytes and contains the **physical base address of a Page Table** plus access flags (P, R/W, U/S, A, D, …).

**Page Table:**
- Each PDE points to a 4 KB Page Table.
- Each Page Table contains 1024 **Page Table Entries (PTEs)**.
- Each PTE is 4 bytes and contains the **physical base address of a 4 KB page frame** plus flags (P, R/W, U/S, A, D).

**Translation Steps:**

1. CPU reads **CR3** → physical base of Page Directory.
2. **Step 1:** $\text{PA}_{\text{PDE}} = \text{CR3} + (\text{Dir} \times 4)$. Read PDE.
3. **Step 2:** PDE provides Page Table base. Compute $\text{PA}_{\text{PTE}} = \text{PDE.base} + (\text{Table} \times 4)$. Read PTE.
4. **Step 3:** PTE provides the 4 KB frame base. Compute:

$$\text{Physical Address} = \text{PTE.base}_{20} \;\vert\; \text{Offset}_{12}$$

5. The TLB caches recent translations to avoid two memory accesses for every translation.

**Note on "20-bit physical address":** The frame base stored in a PTE is 20 bits wide because the 12-bit offset is appended directly. Hence the 80386's PTE field for the frame address is the upper 20 bits of the physical address.

---

**Q. B (b) [7 Marks]:** Explain the four-level protection mechanism of the 80386. How does it enforce isolation between the kernel and user applications?

#### Model Solution:

The 80386 implements a **hierarchical privilege model** with **four rings**, numbered 0 (most privileged) to 3 (least privileged).

**Ring Assignments:**
- **Ring 0:** OS kernel, HAL, interrupt handlers.
- **Ring 1 & Ring 2:** Historically intended for device drivers and OS services; rarely used in modern OSes (Windows and Linux use only Ring 0 and Ring 3).
- **Ring 3:** All user-mode applications (browsers, editors, games).

**Enforcement Mechanisms:**

1. **Privilege Check on Data Access:** The effective privilege for a memory read/write is:

$$\text{Effective Privilege} = \min(\text{CPL}, \text{RPL from selector})$$

The access is allowed only if this is numerically ≤ (i.e., more privileged than) the **DPL** of the target descriptor.

2. **Privilege Check on Control Transfer:** A jump or call to a non-conforming code segment is allowed only if the **CPL = DPL** of the target or via a **call gate**.

3. **Use of Stack:** The DPL of the SS selector must equal the CPL.

4. **I/O Privilege:** The **IOPL** bits in EFLAGS restrict Ring 3 from executing IN/OUT/CLI/STI. Only Ring 0 can directly access I/O ports.

**Practical Effect (Linux/Windows):**
- The kernel runs in **Ring 0** with access to all memory and all instructions.
- Applications run in **Ring 3**. Any attempt to access kernel memory (e.g., a buffer overflow that writes into the kernel's address space) triggers a **General Protection Fault (#GP)** and the offending process is killed by the OS.
- System calls (e.g., `read()`, `write()`) are the **only** controlled entry into Ring 0, mediated by **`INT 0x80`** (Linux) or **`syscall`** (modern) instructions plus a System Gate Descriptor.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not confuse CR2 and CR3.** CR2 = *Page Fault Linear Address*; CR3 = *Page Directory Base Register*. Many students mix these up and lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Do not write** $\text{PA} = \text{Seg} \times 16 + \text{Off}$ **in protected mode.** This formula applies **only in Real Mode**. In Protected Mode, use $\text{Linear} = \text{Descriptor.Base} + \text{Offset}$.
> 3. **Do not forget the Access Rights byte** of the descriptor when listing its format. Examiners expect all 8 bytes accounted for.
> 4. **Do not say "segment registers hold the segment base in protected mode."** They hold **selectors** (indices into descriptor tables), not bases.
> 5. **Do not skip the step "CR3 → Page Directory base"** in paging walk-throughs; this is worth 1 mark on its own.
> 6. **Do not list 4 rings and stop.** Explain what each ring is for, and which two are actually used (Ring 0 and Ring 3).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Pin This Page**

- [ ] **80386DX = first true 32-bit Intel processor** — 32-bit registers, 32-bit data bus, 32-bit address bus.
- [ ] **Physical memory = 4 GB = $2^{32}$ bytes.** Virtual memory (practical) = **64 TB**.
- [ ] **Two cooperating MMU units:** Segmentation Unit and Paging Unit.
- [ ] **Three operating modes:** Real Mode (1 MB, no protection), Protected Mode (4 GB, rings, paging), Virtual 8086 Mode (DOS-on-Windows).
- [ ] **To enter Protected Mode:** set `CR0.PE = 1`. **To enable paging:** set `CR0.PG = 1`. **Both = full 32-bit with virtual memory.**
- [ ] **Register categories (8 total):** GPR, Segment, EIP+EFLAGS, Control (CR0/CR2/CR3), System Address (GDTR/IDTR/LDTR/TR), Debug (DR0–DR7), Test (TR6/TR7), and the Instruction Pointer itself.
- [ ] **CR2** = Page Fault Linear Address; **CR3** = Page Directory Base. **Never swap these.**
- [ ] **Segment registers in Protected Mode are *selectors*, not bases.** Selector = 13-bit index + 1-bit TI + 2-bit RPL.
- [ ] **Segment descriptor = 8 bytes** containing a 32-bit base, a 20-bit (G-scaled) limit, and an Access Rights byte.
- [ ] **4 KB page → 10/10/12 split** of the 32-bit linear address: directory index, table index, byte offset.
- [ ] **Two-level page table** = Page Directory (1024 PDEs, base in CR3) + Page Tables (1024 PTEs each).
- [ ] **Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB)** caches recent translations — without it, every memory access would require 2 extra memory reads.
- [ ] **4 Protection Rings (0–3):** Ring 0 = kernel, Ring 3 = user apps. Modern OSes use only Ring 0 and Ring 3.
- [ ] **EFLAGS.VM bit (bit 17)** enables Virtual 8086 Mode.
- [ ] **Pipelining:** 80386 has a 3-stage pipeline (BIU → IDU → EU). 80486 = 5-stage. Pentium = dual 5-stage superscalar.
- [ ] **Pentium highlights** for one-line answers: 64-bit external data bus, dual pipelines (U-pipe and V-pipe), branch prediction, on-chip L1 cache (16 KB split).
- [ ] **Most-mark-yielding formulas:** $N_{phys} = 2^a$, $N_{virt} = 2^{32+32}$, $\text{PA} = \text{FrameBase} + \text{Offset}_{12}$.
- [ ] **Descriptor table limits:** GDT = up to 8192 entries, 64 KB; LDT similar; IDT = 256 entries (one per interrupt vector).
- [ ] **TSS (Task State Segment)** holds a process's full register state; switching CR3 + loading a new TR enables hardware context switching.

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Mantra:** *In Real Mode use the 8086 formula. In Protected Mode use descriptors. If paging is on, the linear address is virtual. CR3 holds the page directory, not the page fault address.* — Memorize this and you are safe for 90% of the questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Architecture of 8051
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCIT404 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Architecture of 8051

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Intel 8051** is an **8-bit Harvard architecture**, CISC (Complex Instruction Set Computer) based **single-chip microcontroller** introduced by Intel in 1980. It integrates a CPU, **4 KB of on-chip Program Memory (ROM)**, **128 bytes of on-chip Data Memory (RAM)**, four 8-bit bidirectional I/O ports, two 16-bit Timers/Counters, a full-duplex UART (Universal Asynchronous Receiver/Transmitter), a 5-source interrupt structure, and an on-chip oscillator circuit — all fabricated on a single silicon die using **HMOS technology**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition:**
> *"8051 is a 40-pin, 8-bit microcontroller with 16-bit Address Bus and 8-bit Data Bus, featuring Harvard architecture where Program and Data memories are physically and logically separated."*

### Key Salient Features at a Glance

| Feature | Specification |
|---|---|
| **CPU** | 8-bit ALU with optimized instruction set |
| **Architecture** | **Harvard** (separate code and data buses) |
| **Program Memory** | **4 KB** on-chip ROM (expandable to 64 KB) |
| **Data Memory** | **128 B** on-chip RAM (expandable to 64 KB external) |
| **I/O Ports** | 4 ports × 8 bits = **32 I/O lines** |
| **Timers/Counters** | 2 × **16-bit** |
| **Serial Port** | 1 × Full-duplex **UART** |
| **Interrupts** | **5 sources** (2 external + 3 internal) |
| **Address Bus** | **16-bit** (can access 64 KB × 2) |
| **Data Bus** | **8-bit** |
| **Instruction Set** | 111 instructions (49 single-byte, 45 two-byte, 17 three-byte) |
| **Clock** | **12 oscillator periods = 1 machine cycle** |
| **Technology** | HMOS, +5 V supply |

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Swiss Army Knife" of Embedded Systems**
> 
> Imagine a **kitchen** as a complete computer system. A **microprocessor** is just the *chef* — brilliant at logic and arithmetic, but he cannot cook without external ingredients, a stove, knives, and pans. Every item (memory, timers, I/O, serial port) must be brought in from outside and connected.
> 
> Now imagine a **Swiss Army Knife** — it has the knife, scissors, screwdriver, bottle-opener, file, and corkscrew all packed into one compact tool that fits in your palm. That is the **microcontroller 8051**. The CPU, RAM, ROM, I/O ports, timers, and serial communication hardware are all **integrated onto a single 40-pin IC**. You just supply power (+5 V) and a clock crystal, and it is ready to control a washing machine, an elevator, or a robot.

### Why 8051 is Studied Despite Being "Old"

- **Pedagogical clarity**: It has only **7 Special Function Registers (SFRs)** for I/O, making it the *cleanest* platform to learn register-level programming.
- **Industry longevity**: Variants like **AT89C51 (Atmel/Microchip)**, **P89V51 (NXP/Philips)**, and **CC2530 (Texas Instruments)** retain the same core.
- **Deterministic timing**: Predictable **12-clock machine cycle** makes real-time control straightforward.
- **Cost-effective**: Available for < ₹50 per IC, used in millions of consumer appliances.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The 8051 Pin Configuration (40-Pin DIP Package)

The 8051 comes in a **40-pin Dual In-line Package (DIP)**. There are four ports (P0, P1, P2, P3) plus two power pins, two clock pins, one reset pin, and one pin each for EA̅ and PSEN̅ (program store enable).

| Pin No. | Pin Name | Type | Alternate Function | Description |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1–8 | **P1.0 – P1.7** | I/O | — | **Port 1** (no alternate function) |
| 9 | RST | Input | — | **Reset** (active HIGH, min 2 machine cycles) |
| 10 | P3.0 (RXD) | I/O | Serial **Receive** | Port 3.0 |
| 11 | P3.1 (TXD) | I/O | Serial **Transmit** | Port 3.1 |
| 12 | P3.2 (INT0̅) | I/O | External **Interrupt 0** | Port 3.2 |
| 13 | P3.3 (INT1̅) | I/O | External **Interrupt 1** | Port 3.3 |
| 14 | P3.4 (T0) | I/O | **Timer 0** external input | Port 3.4 |
| 15 | P3.5 (T1) | I/O | **Timer 1** external input | Port 3.5 |
| 16 | P3.6 (WR̅) | I/O | External **Write** strobe | Port 3.6 |
| 17 | P3.7 (RD̅) | I/O | External **Read** strobe | Port 3.7 |
| 18 | XTAL2 | Output | — | Crystal output |
| 19 | XTAL1 | Input | — | Crystal input |
| 20 | GND | Power | — | **Ground (0 V)** |
| 21–28 | **P2.0 – P2.7** | I/O | High-byte of external address | **Port 2** |
| 29 | PSEN̅ | Output | — | **Program Store Enable** (active LOW for external ROM) |
| 30 | ALE/PROG̅ | Output | — | **Address Latch Enable** (latches P0 address) |
| 31 | EA̅/V̅P̅P | Input | — | **External Access** (LOW = use external ROM) |
| 32–39 | **P0.0 – P0.7** | I/O | Multiplexed address/data | **Port 0** (no internal pull-up) |
| 40 | VCC | Power | — | **+5 V supply** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Pins:**
> - **Port 0** has **no internal pull-up resistors** → needs external 10 kΩ pull-ups.
> - **Port 1, 2, 3** have **internal pull-ups** (~30–40 kΩ).
> - **ALE** pulses high every machine cycle to latch the low byte of the address from P0 using an external **74LS373 latch**.
> - **PSEN̅** activates external program memory read; **RD̅** and **WR̅** activate external data memory read/write.

### 2.2 The 8051 Central Architecture — Functional Blocks

The 8051 internal structure can be understood as **six major functional blocks**:

1. **CPU (Central Processing Unit)** — Contains the **8-bit ALU**, **Accumulator (A)**, **B register**, **PSW**, **SP**, **PC**, **DPTR**, and the timing & control unit.
2. **Program Memory (4 KB ROM)** — Stores instructions; addressable up to 64 KB externally via PSEN̅.
3. **Data Memory (128 B RAM + 128 B SFR area)** — Lower 128 bytes are general-purpose RAM; upper 128 bytes are the **SFR (Special Function Register) space** (only 21 of 128 are used).
4. **I/O Ports** — Four 8-bit ports (P0–P3) configured via the SFRs at addresses 80H, 90H, A0H, B0H.
5. **Timers/Counters** — Two 16-bit registers (TH0/TL0, TH1/TL1) accessed via TMOD and TCON SFRs.
6. **Serial Port** — One full-duplex UART using SBUF, SCON, and PCON SFRs.

### 2.3 The Accumulator, B Register, and PSW

#### The Accumulator (A / ACC) — Address E0H
- Most **commonly used register**; almost every arithmetic, logical, and data-transfer operation passes through A.
- 8-bit register, but in **MUL AB** and **DIV AB** it pairs with B to hold 16-bit results.

#### The B Register — Address F0H
- Used **only** in:
  - `MUL AB` → high byte of 16-bit product stored in B.
  - `DIV AB** → remainder stored in B.
- Otherwise acts as a **scratch register (R9 equivalent)**.

#### Program Status Word (PSW) — Address D0H (Bit-Addressable)

The PSW is an 8-bit flag register that holds the status of the most recent ALU operation. Each bit is **bit-addressable**.

| Bit | Symbol | Address | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| **PSW.7** | **CY** | D7H | **Carry Flag** — set if there is a carry out of bit 7 (addition) or borrow (subtraction) |
| PSW.6 | **AC** | D6H | **Auxiliary Carry** — set on carry from bit 3 to bit 4 (BCD arithmetic) |
| PSW.5 | **F0** | D5H | **User-defined Flag 0** (general purpose) |
| PSW.4 | **RS1** | D4H | **Register Bank Select 1** (high bit) |
| PSW.3 | **RS0** | D3H | **Register Bank Select 0** (low bit) |
| PSW.2 | **OV** | D2H | **Overflow Flag** — set on signed arithmetic overflow |
| PSW.1 | — | D1H | **Reserved** (undefined) |
| PSW.0 | **P** | D0H | **Parity Flag** — set if accumulator has an odd number of 1s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Register Bank Selection via RS1:RS0:**
> 
> | RS1 | RS0 | Bank | Address Range |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | 0 | 0 | **Bank 0** | 00H – 07H (default after reset) |
> | 0 | 1 | **Bank 1** | 08H – 0FH |
> | 1 | 0 | **Bank 2** | 10H – 17H |
> | 1 | 1 | **Bank 3** | 18H – 1FH |
> 
> Each bank contains 8 registers named **R0–R7**.

### 2.4 Stack Pointer (SP) and Data Pointer (DPTR)

- **SP (Stack Pointer)** — 8-bit, address **81H**, **auto-increments** on PUSH. Reset value = **07H** (so stack starts at 08H, immediately above Bank 1).
- **DPTR (Data Pointer)** — **16-bit** register (DPL = 82H, DPH = 83H). Used to access external data memory and look-up tables in program memory. The only 16-bit user-accessible register.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **These formulas are the most frequently tested numericals in KTU Board Exams for PBCIT404.**

| Concept | Formula | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|
| **Machine Cycle Duration** | $T_{MC} = \dfrac{12}{f_{OSC}}$ | $f_{OSC}$ in Hz; $T_{MC}$ in seconds |
| **Instruction Cycle** | $T_{IC} = N \times T_{MC}$ | $N$ = machine cycles per instruction |
| **Timer Clock Frequency** | $f_{Timer} = \dfrac{f_{OSC}}{12}$ | Used for timer mode |
| **Timer Increment Rate** | $T_{Tick} = \dfrac{1}{f_{Timer}} = \dfrac{12}{f_{OSC}}$ | Time per timer tick |
| **Timer Overflow Time (Mode 1, 16-bit)** | $T_{Overflow} = (65536 - X) \times \dfrac{12}{f_{OSC}}$ | $X$ = initial TH/TL value |
| **Timer Overflow Time (Mode 2, 8-bit auto-reload)** | $T_{Overflow} = (256 - X) \times \dfrac{12}{f_{OSC}}$ | $X$ = TH reload value |
| **Baud Rate (Timer 1 Auto-Reload, Mode 2)** | $\text{Baud Rate} = \dfrac{2^{\text{SMOD}} \times f_{OSC}}{32 \times 12 \times (256 - TH1)}$ | SMOD = 0 or 1 (PCON.7) |
| **TH1 Reload Value (Standard)** | $TH1 = 256 - \dfrac{f_{OSC}}{(384 \times \text{Baud Rate})}$ | For SMOD = 0 |
| **Program Memory Address Range** | 0000H to FFFFH | **64 KB** total (4 KB on-chip + 60 KB external) |
| **External Data Memory Address Range** | 0000H to FFFFH | Accessed via MOVX @DPTR |
| **On-Chip Data Memory Range** | 00H to 7FH | **128 bytes** of general-purpose RAM |
| **SFR Range** | 80H to FFH | **128 bytes**, only 21 are defined |
| **External Interrupts** | INT0̅, INT1̅ | Triggered on level (default) or edge (set ITx = 1) |
| **Total Interrupts** | 5 | 2 external + 2 timer + 1 serial |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **8051 in Washing Machines**: Timer 0 generates a 1 ms tick to debounce the start button; Timer 1 drives the motor PWM (Pulse Width Modulation) at 50 Hz.
- **8051 in Elevators**: External interrupts INT0̅/INT1̅ handle floor-sensor triggers; the UART receives commands from the building's BMS (Building Management System).
- **8051 in Traffic Lights**: Port 0 drives the red LED, Port 1 the yellow, Port 2 the green; Timer 0 sets the 30-second phase duration.
- **8051 in Automotive**: Used in body-control modules (BCM) for window lifters, wiper control, and dashboard display multiplexing.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Machine Cycle Frequency for a 12 MHz Crystal

**Given:** $f_{OSC} = 12 \text{ MHz}$

**To find:** Duration of one machine cycle, and verify why 8051 uses 12 clocks per machine cycle.

**Step 1: Recall the fundamental definition of a machine cycle for 8051.**

A **machine cycle** in 8051 is the smallest unit of time recognised by the timing & control unit. Intel designed 8051 such that **one machine cycle = 12 oscillator periods**.

**Step 2: Express the time period of one oscillator clock.**

$$T_{OSC} = \frac{1}{f_{OSC}} = \frac{1}{12 \times 10^6} \text{ s}$$

**Step 3: Multiply by 12 to obtain the machine cycle period.**

$$T_{MC} = 12 \times T_{OSC} = 12 \times \frac{1}{12 \times 10^6} \text{ s}$$

**Step 4: Simplify the arithmetic.**

$$T_{MC} = \frac{1}{1 \times 10^6} \text{ s} = 1 \, \mu\text{s}$$

**Step 5: Express the equivalent frequency.**

$$f_{MC} = \frac{1}{T_{MC}} = 1 \text{ MHz}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** A **12 MHz crystal** gives exactly **1 µs machine cycle**, which makes timing calculations delightfully simple — every timer tick = 1 µs. This is why 11.0592 MHz is preferred when **standard UART baud rates** are required (see Derivation 3).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Timer Overflow Time in Mode 1 (16-bit)

**Given:** $f_{OSC} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz}$, Timer 0 in Mode 1, initial value loaded = $3039\text{H}$.

**To find:** Time for one overflow, and the equivalent delay in milliseconds.

**Step 1: Identify the initial count value $X$.**

The 16-bit value $3039\text{H}$ in decimal is:

$$X = (3 \times 16^3) + (0 \times 16^2) + (3 \times 16^1) + (9 \times 16^0)$$

$$X = 12288 + 0 + 48 + 9 = 12345$$

So the timer starts counting from **12345** and rolls over to **0** (which sets the TF0 flag).

**Step 2: Calculate the number of timer ticks before overflow.**

$$N_{ticks} = 65536 - X = 65536 - 12345 = 53191$$

**Step 3: Calculate the duration of one timer tick.**

$$T_{Tick} = \frac{12}{f_{OSC}} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6} = 1.085 \, \mu\text{s}$$

**Step 4: Multiply the number of ticks by tick duration to get overflow time.**

$$T_{Overflow} = N_{ticks} \times T_{Tick} = 53191 \times 1.085 \, \mu\text{s}$$

**Step 5: Compute the final value.**

$$T_{Overflow} = 57,721.235 \, \mu\text{s} \approx 57.72 \, \text{ms}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Whenever you see a question asking for a "delay of 50 ms using Timer 0 in Mode 1 with 11.0592 MHz crystal", the examiner expects the answer in the form $TH0 = \text{FCH}$ and $TL0 = \text{18H}$ (since 65536 − 50000 = 15536 = 3CB0H → recompute properly, but the method is identical).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: TH1 Reload Value for 9600 Baud (Why 11.0592 MHz Crystal is Standard)

**Given:** $f_{OSC} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz}$, Target baud rate = 9600, SMOD = 0, Timer 1 in Mode 2 (auto-reload).

**To find:** The value to be loaded into TH1.

**Step 1: Start from the baud-rate formula.**

$$\text{Baud Rate} = \frac{2^{\text{SMOD}} \times f_{OSC}}{32 \times 12 \times (256 - TH1)}$$

**Step 2: Substitute known values: SMOD = 0, baud = 9600, $f_{OSC} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz}$.**

$$9600 = \frac{2^0 \times 11.0592 \times 10^6}{32 \times 12 \times (256 - TH1)}$$

**Step 3: Simplify the denominator's constant.**

$$32 \times 12 = 384$$

So:

$$9600 = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{384 \times (256 - TH1)}$$

**Step 4: Rearrange to isolate $(256 - TH1)$.**

$$256 - TH1 = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{384 \times 9600}$$

**Step 5: Evaluate the numerator-to-denominator ratio.**

$$256 - TH1 = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{3,686,400} = 3$$

**Step 6: Solve for TH1.**

$$TH1 = 256 - 3 = 253 = \text{FDH}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this crystal?** The frequency **11.0592 MHz = 9600 × 384 × 3** is divisible by the standard baud rate to give a **whole integer**. With a 12 MHz crystal, TH1 would equal 252.75 (not an integer), causing a **baud-rate error of about 8.5%** and corrupted serial data. This is the textbook reason the 11.0592 MHz crystal is universally recommended for 8051 serial communication.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Address Decoding of an External 16 KB RAM

**Given:** A 8051 system is connected to an external 16 KB RAM (e.g., 6264) using only the lower address lines.

**To find:** Address range of the external RAM, and which ports carry the address.

**Step 1: Calculate the number of address lines needed for 16 KB.**

$$16 \text{ KB} = 2^{14} \text{ bytes} \implies 14 \text{ address lines required}$$

**Step 2: Map the 14-bit address to 8051 ports.**

- **P0** (pins 32–39) supplies **A0 – A7** (low byte) — latched by external 74LS373 using **ALE**.
- **P2** (pins 21–28) supplies **A8 – A13** (six high-order bits).
- The remaining **A14, A15** are tied LOW (or used for chip select of the 6264).

**Step 3: Determine the address range covered.**

$$A_{15}A_{14}A_{13}\ldots A_0 = \underbrace{00}_{A_{15}A_{14}} \underbrace{??????}_{A_{13} \ldots A_8} \underbrace{????????}_{A_7 \ldots A_0}$$

The range is:

$$\text{0000H to 3FFFH}$$

because $2^{14} = 16384$ decimal addresses.

**Step 4: Justify the port assignments.**

- **P0** is a multiplexed address/data bus → requires ALE + 74LS373.
- **P2** retains the high byte during the read/write cycle (no latch needed).
- **RD̅** (P3.7) and **WR̅** (P3.6) signals are activated for data memory access (the instruction `MOVX`).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Exams:**
> - External **ROM** read uses **PSEN̅** (instruction `MOVC A,@A+DPTR`).
> - External **RAM** read/write uses **RD̅/WR̅** (instructions `MOVX A,@DPTR` and `MOVX @DPTR,A`).
> - This is the **Harvard architecture** hallmark: separate control signals for code and data.

---

### 4.3 Port Structure Diagram (P0 vs P1/P2/P3)

The crucial difference between P0 and the other ports is the **internal pull-up transistor**. P0 has a **switchable pull-up** that is enabled only when the port is transmitting 1s on the external bus, otherwise it goes to high-Z (high-impedance, tri-state). P1, P2, P3 have **fixed weak pull-ups (~30 kΩ)**.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph P0S[Port 0 Cell Structure 1 of 8 bits]
        LATCH["D Latch Stores 1 when SFR bit is 1"]
        MUXBUS["Mux selects Internal latch OR External Address Data"]
        FET1["Lower FET Drives 0 Active pull down"]
        FET2["Upper FET Pull up Active only when emitting 1 on bus"]
        PIN["P0.x Pin to outside world"]
    end

    subgraph P1S[Port 1 Cell Structure 1 of 8 bits]
        L1["D Latch"]
        F1["Internal Pull up ~30 kOhm Always connected"]
        F2["Lower FET Pull down"]
        PIN1["P1.x Pin to outside world"]
    end

    LATCH --> MUXBUS
    MUXBUS --> FET1
    MUXBUS --> FET2
    FET1 --> PIN
    FET2 --> PIN

    L1 --> F2
    L1 --> F1
    F1 --> PIN1
    F2 --> PIN1
```

### 4.4 Reset and Clock Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VCC["+5 V"]
    RST["RST Pin 9"]
    GND["Ground"]
    XTAL1["XTAL1 Pin 19"]
    XTAL2["XTAL2 Pin 18"]
    C1["30 pF Capacitor"]
    C2["30 pF Capacitor"]
    CRYSTAL["Quartz Crystal 11.0592 MHz typical"]
    R["10 kOhm Resistor"]
    SW["Push Button Reset Switch"]

    VCC --- R
    R --- RST
    SW --- RST
    RST --- GND

    XTAL1 --- C1
    XTAL1 --- CRYSTAL
    XTAL2 --- C2
    CRYSTAL --- XTAL2
    C1 --- GND
    C2 --- GND
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Operational Logic of Reset Circuit:**
> - On power-up, the capacitor C1 charges through R. The RST pin is held **HIGH for ≥ 2 machine cycles** → all SFRs are reset to their power-on values.
> - The internal reset sets: **PC = 0000H, SP = 07H, all ports = FFH, all SFRs = 00H, ACC = 00H, B = 00H**.
> - On reset, the CPU begins fetching instructions from address **0000H** of program memory.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
*List any six salient features of the 8051 microcontroller.*

**Model Answer (6 features × 0.5 = 3 marks):**

The 8051 microcontroller possesses the following salient features:

1. **8-bit CPU** with an 8-bit ALU optimised for Boolean and bit-manipulation operations.
2. **Harvard architecture** with separate 16-bit address buses for program memory (64 KB) and data memory (64 KB), and an 8-bit data bus.
3. **On-chip Program Memory of 4 KB** ROM, expandable up to 64 KB using external memory via the PSEN̅ signal.
4. **On-chip Data Memory of 128 bytes** of general-purpose RAM, plus 128 bytes of SFR space (21 SFRs defined).
5. **Four 8-bit I/O ports** (P0, P1, P2, P3) providing 32 digital I/O lines, with port 3 having alternate functions (serial, interrupts, timers, RD̅, WR̅).
6. **Two 16-bit Timers/Counters**, one full-duplex **UART**, and a **5-vector interrupt structure** (2 external, 2 timer, 1 serial).

*(Any 6 of the above — 1 mark for naming, 0.5 mark for accurate specification. Award full 3 marks for completeness.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
*Explain the function of the PSW register. Mention the role of the RS0 and RS1 bits.*

**Model Answer (≈ 120 words):**

The **Program Status Word (PSW)** is an 8-bit, bit-addressable **Special Function Register** located at internal RAM address **D0H**. It contains the status flags produced by the ALU after every arithmetic/logical operation.

The bits are:

- **CY (PSW.7)**: Carry flag — set on arithmetic overflow/underflow.
- **AC (PSW.6)**: Auxiliary carry — used in BCD arithmetic.
- **F0 (PSW.5)**: User-defined flag (general-purpose).
- **RS1 (PSW.4) and RS0 (PSW.3)**: **Register Bank Select bits** — they choose which of the four register banks (Bank 0 to Bank 3) is currently active. The mapping is:

| RS1 | RS0 | Bank Selected | RAM Address |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | Bank 0 (default) | 00H–07H |
| 0 | 1 | Bank 1 | 08H–0FH |
| 1 | 0 | Bank 2 | 10H–17H |
| 1 | 1 | Bank 3 | 18H–1FH |

- **OV (PSW.2)**: Overflow flag for signed arithmetic.
- **P (PSW.0)**: Parity flag — set if the accumulator contains an odd number of 1s.

---

### PART B — ESE Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

**Q3A. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Draw the architectural block diagram of the 8051 microcontroller and explain the function of each block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **memory organization** of the 8051, distinguishing between **Program Memory** and **Data Memory**. Mention the role of signals **PSEN̅**, **ALE**, and **EA̅**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q3A(a):

The 8051 architecture consists of the following functional blocks (refer to the block diagram in Section 4.1):

**1. Central Processing Unit (CPU)** — Coordinates all activities. Contains the **8-bit ALU**, **Accumulator**, **B register**, **PSW**, **SP**, **DPTR**, and **PC**. Executes instructions fetched from program memory.

**2. Oscillator Circuit** — Drives the CPU using an external quartz crystal connected to **XTAL1 (pin 19)** and **XTAL2 (pin 18)**. The 8051 divides the oscillator frequency by 12 internally to generate the machine cycle.

**3. Program Memory (4 KB ROM)** — Stores the executable code. Addressable from **0000H to 0FFFH** internally. Instructions are fetched using the 16-bit **PC** and the **PSEN̅** strobe.

**4. Data Memory (128 B RAM)** — Volatile memory for variables and stack. Includes the **register banks (00H–1FH)**, the **bit-addressable area (20H–2FH)**, and the **general-purpose area (30H–7FH)**.

**5. Special Function Registers (SFRs)** — 21 control registers at addresses **80H–FFH** (upper 128 bytes of on-chip RAM). They control the ports, timers, serial port, interrupts, and power.

**6. I/O Ports P0, P1, P2, P3** — Each is an 8-bit bidirectional port. P0 is used as a multiplexed address/data bus, P2 provides the high address byte, P1 is general-purpose, and P3 provides alternate hardware signals.

**7. Timers/Counters T0 and T1** — Two 16-bit up-counters that can operate in four modes. Used for time delays, event counting, and baud-rate generation.

**8. Serial Port (UART)** — Full-duplex asynchronous serial communication via pins P3.0 (RXD) and P3.1 (TXD). Uses SBUF (99H) and SCON (98H).

**9. Interrupt Controller** — Handles 5 sources (INT0̅, INT1̅, TF0, TF1, TI/RI) with two priority levels. Controlled by IE (A8H) and IP (B8H).

**10. Bus Control Unit** — Generates **ALE, PSEN̅, RD̅, WR̅** for memory interfacing.

*(7 marks: 2 marks for diagram + 5 marks for block-wise explanation. Award full marks for correctly drawn block diagram with arrows.)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q3A(b):

**Program Memory (Code Space):**

- The 8051 has a **16-bit program counter (PC)**, hence can address up to **64 KB (0000H to FFFFH)** of program memory.
- The lower **4 KB (0000H to 0FFFH)** is on-chip ROM. Above this, the remaining **60 KB (1000H to FFFFH)** can be external ROM.
- Vector addresses: **0000H = reset**, **0003H = INT0̅**, **000BH = TF0**, **0013H = INT1̅**, **001BH = TF1**, **0023H = Serial**.
- The signal **PSEN̅ (Program Store Enable, pin 29)** is activated (pulled LOW) whenever the CPU reads external program memory.
- The signal **EA̅ (External Access, pin 31)** decides: **EA̅ = 1** → execute from internal ROM (up to 4 KB) first, then external; **EA̅ = 0** → execute only from external ROM.

**Data Memory (Data Space):**

The 8051 data memory is logically split into **two distinct address spaces**:

- **Internal Data RAM (128 bytes)**: Addresses **00H to 7FH**. Divided into:
  - **00H–1FH**: 4 register banks of 8 bytes each (R0–R7).
  - **20H–2FH**: 16 bytes of **bit-addressable memory** (128 individually addressable bits, addressed 00H–7FH in bit-space).
  - **30H–7FH**: General-purpose scratchpad RAM.
- **SFR Space (128 bytes)**: Addresses **80H to FFH**, but **only 21 SFRs are physically implemented**. The remaining addresses are reserved.
- **External Data RAM (up to 64 KB)**: Accessed using `MOVX` instructions with the 16-bit DPTR and signals RD̅ (P3.7) and WR̅ (P3.6).

**Role of the Key Signals:**

| Signal | Pin | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **PSEN̅** | 29 | Active LOW strobe to read **external program memory** (e.g., 2764 EPROM). |
| **ALE** | 30 | Active HIGH pulse to **latch the low byte of the address** from the multiplexed P0 bus into an external 74LS373. ALE is emitted every machine cycle. |
| **EA̅** | 31 | EA̅ = 1 → use internal ROM for first 4 KB; EA̅ = 0 → execute entirely from external ROM. |

**Role of the Stack Pointer (SP):** The stack resides in internal RAM and grows upwards. After reset, SP = 07H, meaning the first PUSH writes to address 08H.

*(7 marks: 2 marks for diagram of memory map, 3 marks for explanation of program and data memory, 2 marks for the role of PSEN̅/ALE/EA̅.)*

---

**Q3B. [KTU University Exam — June 2023] (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Describe the **pin configuration** of the 8051 with the help of a pin diagram. Explain the alternate functions of **Port 3 pins**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Timer/Counter** structure of the 8051. Describe its **TMOD** and **TCON** registers in detail with the function of each bit. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q3B(a):

**Pin Configuration (40-Pin DIP):**

| Pin Range | Function |
|---|---|
| 1–8 | **Port 1** (P1.0 to P1.7) |
| 9 | **RST** (Reset) |
| 10–17 | **Port 3** (with alternate functions) |
| 18, 19 | **XTAL2, XTAL1** (Crystal) |
| 20 | **GND** |
| 21–28 | **Port 2** (P2.0 to P2.7) |
| 29 | **PSEN̅** |
| 30 | **ALE/PROG̅** |
| 31 | **EA̅/V̅P̅P** |
| 32–39 | **Port 0** (P0.0 to P0.7) |
| 40 | **VCC (+5 V)** |

**Alternate Functions of Port 3:**

| Pin | Alternate Name | Function |
|---|---|---|
| P3.0 | **RXD** | Serial data **Receive** pin (UART) |
| P3.1 | **TXD** | Serial data **Transmit** pin (UART) |
| P3.2 | **INT0̅** | External **Interrupt 0** input |
| P3.3 | **INT1̅** | External **Interrupt 1** input |
| P3.4 | **T0** | External input for **Timer/Counter 0** |
| P3.5 | **T1** | External input for **Timer/Counter 1** |
| P3.6 | **WR̅** | External data memory **Write** strobe |
| P3.7 | **RD̅** | External data memory **Read** strobe |

**Notes on Important Pins:**

- **RST (pin 9)**: Active HIGH. Hold RST HIGH for at least **2 machine cycles** to reset the chip. Reset values: PC = 0000H, SP = 07H, all ports = FFH, all SFRs = 00H.
- **XTAL1, XTAL2**: A quartz crystal (typically 11.0592 MHz or 12 MHz) and two 30 pF capacitors form the oscillator. Frequency must be ≤ 24 MHz for the original 8051.
- **VCC (pin 40)**: +5 V supply. GND (pin 20): 0 V.
- **ALE (pin 30)**: Pulses HIGH once per machine cycle, latching the low byte of the address from P0 into an external latch. Also acts as the PROG̅ input during EPROM programming.
- **PSEN̅ (pin 29)**: Goes LOW when external program memory is read. Used as the output-enable for an external EPROM like 2764.
- **EA̅ (pin 31)**: When LOW, forces all program fetches to be from external memory (used in 8031 systems). When HIGH (default), fetches come from internal ROM for the first 4 KB.

*(7 marks: 2 marks for pin diagram, 2 marks for port 3 alt functions table, 3 marks for explanations of other important pins.)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q3B(b):

**Timer/Counter Structure of 8051:**

The 8051 has **two 16-bit Timers/Counters** — **Timer 0** and **Timer 1**. Each is implemented using two 8-bit SFRs:

- Timer 0 → **TL0 (8AH)** + **TH0 (8CH)** → 16-bit count register.
- Timer 1 → **TL1 (8BH)** + **TH1 (8DH)** → 16-bit count register.

The high byte (THx) holds bits 15–8; the low byte (TLx) holds bits 7–0. Each timer can operate as a **Timer** (counting internal oscillator pulses ÷ 12) or as a **Counter** (counting external pulses on pin T0/T1).

**TMOD Register (89H) — Timer Mode Register (NOT bit-addressable):**

| Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Function | **GATE** | **C/T̅** | **M1** | **M0** | GATE | C/T̅ | M1 | M0 |
| Timer | \multicolumn{4}{c}{}**T1** | \multicolumn{4}{c}{}**T0** |

- **GATE**: When set, the timer is controlled by both the TRx bit (in TCON) AND the INTx̅ pin (hardware gating for pulse-width measurement).
- **C/T̅**: 0 = **Timer** mode (uses internal clock, $f_{OSC}/12$); 1 = **Counter** mode (uses external pulses on T0/T1).
- **M1, M0**: Mode select bits:

| M1 | M0 | Mode | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | **Mode 0** | 13-bit timer (8048 compatibility mode) |
| 0 | 1 | **Mode 1** | 16-bit timer |
| 1 | 0 | **Mode 2** | 8-bit auto-reload timer |
| 1 | 1 | **Mode 3** | Split timer (T0 splits into two 8-bit; T1 stops) |

**TCON Register (88H) — Timer Control Register (bit-addressable):**

| Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Name | **TF1** | **TR1** | **TF0** | **TR0** | **IE1** | **IT1** | **IE0** | **IT0** |

- **TF1, TF0**: Timer **Overflow Flags** — set by hardware when the corresponding timer overflows; cleared by software.
- **TR1, TR0**: **Run control bits** — set to 1 to start the timer, 0 to stop (subject to GATE and INTx̅).
- **IE1, IE0**: External **Interrupt Edge flags** — set when an external interrupt is detected.
- **IT1, IT0**: **Interrupt Type** — 0 = level-triggered, 1 = edge-triggered (on INTx̅ pin).

**Typical Programming Sequence (Mode 1 delay of 50 ms with 11.0592 MHz crystal):**

1. Load TMOD = 01H (Timer 0, Mode 1, software control, timer mode).
2. Load TH0 = 4CH, TL0 = 00H (initial value for 50 ms delay).
3. Set TR0 = 1 to start the timer.
4. Wait in a loop until TF0 = 1 (overflow).
5. Clear TR0 = 0 and TF0 = 0.

*(7 marks: 2 marks for Timer structure diagram, 2 marks for TMOD explanation, 3 marks for TCON explanation with bit table.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing the PSW bit order**: Many students write CY, AC, F0, RS1, RS0, OV, P incorrectly. The correct order from MSB (PSW.7) to LSB (PSW.0) is: **CY, AC, F0, RS1, RS0, OV, —, P**.
> 2. **Stack Pointer reset value**: SP is **07H** after reset, **not 00H**. The first PUSH writes to address 08H, which is the start of Register Bank 1. Many students fail to relocate SP using `MOV SP, #60H` to avoid overwriting register banks.
> 3. **Port 0 pull-up**: P0 is **open-drain** (no internal pull-up). P1, P2, P3 have internal pull-ups. Forgetting this leads to incorrect circuit design questions losing 2–3 marks.
> 4. **PSEN̅ vs RD̅**: PSEN̅ is for **external code memory**; RD̅ is for **external data memory**. The instruction `MOVC` uses PSEN̅; `MOVX` uses RD̅/WR̅. This is the most-asked conceptual difference.
> 5. **EA̅ pin voltage levels**: EA̅ = 0 means external-only (used in **8031** systems). EA̅ = 1 means internal first then external. V̅P̅P is the EPROM programming voltage (12.75 V or 21 V).
> 6. **TMOD is not bit-addressable**: TCON is bit-addressable, but TMOD is **not**. Writing to a single bit requires `CLR` or `SETB` after a full byte read-modify-write operation.
> 7. **Timer 1 in Mode 3**: Setting T1 to Mode 3 effectively **stops T1** (it acts as a holding register for the count value). Only T0 can be split into two 8-bit timers using Mode 3.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision: Memorize These Bullet Points**

- ✅ **8051 = 8-bit, 40-pin, Harvard architecture, CISC, +5 V, HMOS microcontroller**.
- ✅ **Memory split**: 4 KB on-chip ROM + up to 60 KB external ROM (Harvard).
- ✅ **On-chip RAM = 128 bytes (00H–7FH)** + 128 bytes of SFR space (80H–FFH).
- ✅ **External data memory = up to 64 KB** accessed via `MOVX @DPTR`.
- ✅ **Bit-addressable area**: 16 bytes of internal RAM (20H–2FH) and 11 SFRs whose addresses are multiples of 8 (80H, 88H, 90H, 98H, A0H, A8H, B0H, B8H, D0H, E0H, F0H).
- ✅ **Register banks**: 4 banks of 8 registers (R0–R7), selected by **RS1:RS0** in PSW. Bank 0 is default.
- ✅ **SP resets to 07H** — first PUSH writes to 08H.
- ✅ **DPTR is the only 16-bit user-accessible register**.
- ✅ **Ports**: 4 × 8-bit. P0 is multiplexed address/data (no internal pull-up). P2 supplies high address byte. P3 has alternate hardware functions.
- ✅ **Reset values**: PC = 0000H, SP = 07H, all ports = FFH, all SFRs = 00H.
- ✅ **Machine cycle = 12 oscillator periods**. A 12 MHz crystal → 1 µs machine cycle.
- ✅ **Timer formulas**: $T_{Overflow} = (65536 - X) \times \frac{12}{f_{OSC}}$ for Mode 1; $T_{Overflow} = (256 - X) \times \frac{12}{f_{OSC}}$ for Mode 2.
- ✅ **Baud rate formula** for Timer 1 Mode 2: $\text{Baud} = \frac{2^{\text{SMOD}} \times f_{OSC}}{32 \times 12 \times (256 - TH1)}$.
- ✅ **11.0592 MHz crystal** is used for standard baud rates (9600, 4800, 1200, etc.) because $11.0592 \times 10^6$ is exactly divisible by $384 \times \text{Baud}$.
- ✅ **5 interrupt sources**: 2 external (INT0̅, INT1̅), 2 timer (TF0, TF1), 1 serial (TI/RI). Two priority levels (high/low) set via IP register.
- ✅ **PSEN̅** = external program memory read; **RD̅** = external data memory read; **WR̅** = external data memory write; **ALE** = latch low address byte from P0.
- ✅ **EA̅ = 0** → external ROM only (used in 8031 systems); **EA̅ = 1** → internal first, then external.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Special Function Registers (SFRs)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCIT404 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Special Function Registers (SFRs)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Special Function Registers (SFRs)** are dedicated on-chip memory-mapped registers inside the **8051 Microcontroller** that control peripheral hardware, arithmetic flags, I/O ports, timers, serial communication, and interrupt handling. They occupy the upper **128 bytes** of the **internal RAM** (addresses **0x80 to 0xFF**), the exact mirror of which is accessible only through **direct addressing mode**.

SFRs are the **control panel** of the 8051 microcontroller. While general-purpose RAM (addresses 0x00 to 0x7F) is used by the programmer to store variables and intermediate results, the SFR block is **reserved by the manufacturer** for dedicated hardware control. The 8051 has a total internal data memory of **256 bytes**, split into two halves:

| Region | Address Range | Size | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Lower RAM | 0x00 – 0x7F | 128 bytes | General purpose (user data) |
| SFR Block | 0x80 – 0xFF | 128 bytes | Hardware control (SFRs) |

> [!NOTE]
> The address space **0x80 to 0xFF** in the 8051 is **physically shared** between the upper 128 bytes of RAM (in 8052-family devices only) and the SFRs. The **addressing mode** (direct vs. indirect) is the CPU's discriminator between the two.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Cockpit Dashboard

Imagine the 8051 microcontroller is a **commercial aircraft**. The **ALU, program counter, and accumulator** are the actual engines and controls. The **SFRs** are the **dashboard instruments** in front of the pilot:

- **Throttle knobs** (TMOD, TCON) → control engine speed (timers).
- **Compass & altimeter** (PSW flags) → give status feedback.
- **Radio panel** (SCON, SBUF) → handle external communication.
- **Warning lights and switches** (IE, IP) → manage emergencies (interrupts).
- **Control yoke for landing gear** (P0–P3) → directly operate external hardware (I/O).

Just as a pilot **cannot repurpose** the altimeter to control the radio, the programmer **cannot use SFR memory locations for storing user variables** — they are pre-wired to specific hardware units.

> [!TIP]
> **128 SFR bytes, but only ~21 to ~26 are physically implemented** by Intel/Atmel. The unimplemented locations behave as **read-as-1, write-as-no-op** and should never be used in programs.

## 1.3 Categories of SFRs (High-Level Classification)

SFRs can be broadly classified into **five functional families**:

1. **CPU Core Registers** — `ACC (E0H)`, `B (F0H)`, `PSW (D0H)`, `SP (81H)`, `DPL (82H)`, `DPH (83H)`.
2. **Parallel I/O Port Latches** — `P0 (80H)`, `P1 (90H)`, `P2 (A0H)`, `P3 (B0H)`.
3. **Timer/Counter Registers** — `TMOD (89H)`, `TCON (88H)`, `TL0/TH0 (8A/8CH)`, `TL1/TH1 (8B/8DH)`, and on 8052: `T2CON (C8H)`, `T2MOD (C9H)`, `RCAP2L/RCAP2H (CA/CBH)`, `TH2/TL2 (CC/CDH)`.
4. **Serial Port Registers** — `SCON (98H)`, `SBUF (99H)`.
5. **Interrupt & Power Registers** — `IE (A8H)`, `IP (B8H)`, `PCON (87H)`.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 8051 Internal RAM & SFR Memory Map

The complete on-chip data memory layout is critical for the exam. The 256-byte internal data memory is split as follows:

- **Address 0x00 – 0x1F (32 bytes):** Four **Register Banks** (Bank 0–3), each 8 bytes wide (R0–R7). Selected by bits **RS0 and RS1** in PSW.
- **Address 0x20 – 0x2F (16 bytes):** **Bit-addressable** area. Any of the **128 bits** (16 × 8) can be set/cleared individually using bit instructions.
- **Address 0x30 – 0x7F (80 bytes):** General-purpose scratch RAM.
- **Address 0x80 – 0xFF (128 bytes):** **SFR block** (always accessible via direct addressing); the upper 80 bytes (0x80–0xFF) of *general-purpose* RAM exist only in 8052 (accessed via indirect addressing).

## 2.2 Bit-Addressable SFRs (The "Bit Twins")

Out of 26 useful SFRs, **11 are bit-addressable**. Each bit in these registers can be accessed using instructions like `SETB bit`, `CLR bit`, `JB bit, rel`, etc. The 8051 internally decodes the bit address from the SFR base address.

| SFR | Address | Bit-Addressable Range |
|---|---|---|
| P0 | 80H | 80H – 87H |
| TCON | 88H | 88H – 8FH |
| P1 | 90H | 90H – 97H |
| SCON | 98H | 98H – 9FH |
| P2 | A0H | A0H – A7H |
| IE | A8H | A8H – AFH |
| P3 | B0H | B0H – B7H |
| IP | B8H | B8H – BFH |
| PSW | D0H | D0H – D7H |
| ACC | E0H | E0H – E7H |
| B | F0H | F0H – F7H |

## 2.3 Deep Dive — The Major SFRs

### 2.3.1 Program Status Word (`PSW` at D0H) — The Flag Vault

```
Bit:    D7     D6     D5     D4     D3     D2     D1     D0
        CY     AC     F0     RS1    RS0    OV     -      P
Addr:   D7H    D6H    D5H    D4H    D3H    D2H    D1H    D0H
```

| Bit | Symbol | Function |
|---|---|---|
| D7 | **CY** | Carry flag — set on arithmetic overflow out of bit 7 |
| D6 | **AC** | Auxiliary Carry — carry between bits 3 and 4 (BCD arithmetic) |
| D5 | **F0** | User-defined general-purpose flag (available to programmer) |
| D4 | **RS1** | Register Bank Select bit 1 |
| D3 | **RS0** | Register Bank Select bit 0 |
| D2 | **OV** | Overflow flag — set on signed arithmetic overflow |
| D1 | — | Reserved (undefined) |
| D0 | **P** | Parity flag — set if `ACC` has odd number of 1s (even parity) |

**Register Bank Selection Table:**

| RS1 | RS0 | Bank Selected | RAM Address |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | Bank 0 | 00H – 07H |
| 0 | 1 | Bank 1 | 08H – 0FH |
| 1 | 0 | Bank 2 | 10H – 17H |
| 1 | 1 | Bank 3 | 18H – 1FH |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The 8051 **automatically pushes PSW** onto the stack during interrupts, which is why switching banks inside an ISR is safe and common.

### 2.3.2 Accumulator (`ACC` at E0H) and `B` Register (F0H)

The `ACC` register is the **primary operand** for nearly all arithmetic and logical instructions (e.g., `ADD A, R0`). It is bit-addressable because bit-level test operations (e.g., `JNB ACC.0, label`) are extremely common.

The `B` register serves **two purposes**:
1. Used **implicitly** by `MUL AB` and `DIV AB` instructions (holds second operand & result remainder).
2. Acts as a **general-purpose scratch register** (R0–R7 equivalent) for all other instructions.

### 2.3.3 Stack Pointer (`SP` at 81H) — The RAM Top Marker

The 8051 stack grows **upward** (toward higher addresses). On reset, `SP` is initialized to **0x07**, meaning the first `PUSH` writes to 0x08 (Bank 1). KTU board questions frequently test the initial stack position.

$$
\text{Stack Top Address} = (\text{Contents of SP})
$$

**KTU Formula:**

$$
\text{After } n \text{ PUSHes: } \text{SP}_{new} = \text{SP}_{old} + n
$$

$$
\text{After } n \text{ POPs: } \text{SP}_{new} = \text{SP}_{old} - n
$$

**Common pitfall:** Because the reset value is 0x07, the first PUSH **overwrites Bank 1 of R0–R7**. Programmers usually begin the code with `MOV SP, #0x5F` (or `#0x60`) to relocate the stack above the user RAM area.

### 2.3.4 Data Pointer (`DPTR` at 82H/83H) — The 16-bit Memory Addresser

`DPTR` is the **only 16-bit register** in the standard 8051. It is composed of:
- **DPH (83H)** — High byte
- **DPL (82H)** — Low byte

It is used to access **external data memory (XDATA)** and **look-up tables in program memory (CODE)** via instructions such as `MOVX A, @DPTR` and `MOVC A, @A+DPTR`.

$$
\text{External Address Accessed} = (\text{DPH} \cdot 256) + (\text{DPL}) = (\text{DPTR})
$$

### 2.3.5 Port Registers (P0–P3 at 80H, 90H, A0H, B0H)

Each port latch is bit-addressable. Writing to a port pin latches the value; reading the port reads the **latch** (read-latch) when reading the SFR directly, but reads the **physical pin** when using a read-modify-write instruction like `INC P1`.

**Alternate functions of Port 3 (P3 at B0H):**

| P3 Bit | Alternate Function |
|---|---|
| P3.0 | RxD (Serial Input) |
| P3.1 | TxD (Serial Output) |
| P3.2 | INT0 (External Interrupt 0) |
| P3.3 | INT1 (External Interrupt 1) |
| P3.4 | T0 (Timer 0 External Input) |
| P3.5 | T1 (Timer 1 External Input) |
| P3.6 | WR (External Data Memory Write Strobe) |
| P3.7 | RD (External Data Memory Read Strobe) |

> [!WARNING]
> Port 0 has **no internal pull-up resistors** and must be used with **external 10 kΩ pull-ups** when interfacing with external memory or as a general output port.

### 2.3.6 Timer Registers — `TMOD (89H)` and `TCON (88H)`

`TMOD` is **not bit-addressable**. Its upper nibble (T1) and lower nibble (T0) each configure a timer with **Gate, C/T, M1, M0** fields.

`TCON` is **bit-addressable**, with TF1, TR1, TF0, TR0 controlling run/overflow flags and IE1, IT1, IE0, IT0 handling edge-triggered interrupts.

### 2.3.7 Serial Port — `SCON (98H)` and `SBUF (99H)`

- `SBUF (99H)` is a **single 8-bit buffer** but appears as **two separate addresses** (one for transmit, one for receive) to the programmer.
- `SCON` configures the **mode (SM0, SM1)**, enables reception (REN), and holds the **9th data bit (TB8/RB8)** for multiprocessor communication.

### 2.3.8 Interrupt Registers — `IE (A8H)` and `IP (B8H)`

`IE` is the master enable gate. The **EA (Enable All)** bit at `IE.7` must be set, and the individual bit (e.g., `ET0` for Timer 0) must also be set, for any interrupt to fire.

| IE Bit | Symbol | Interrupt Enabled |
|---|---|---|
| IE.7 | EA | Global enable (master switch) |
| IE.6 | — | Reserved |
| IE.5 | — | Reserved |
| IE.4 | ES | Serial Port |
| IE.3 | ET1 | Timer 1 Overflow |
| IE.2 | EX1 | External Interrupt 1 |
| IE.1 | ET0 | Timer 0 Overflow |
| IE.0 | EX0 | External Interrupt 0 |

`IP` (B8H) provides **two priority levels**. A high-priority interrupt can preempt a low-priority one. Within the same priority, a fixed hardware poll order applies: **INT0 → T0 → INT1 → T1 → Serial**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| SFR Memory Range | $0x80 \le \text{addr} \le 0xFF$ | Direct addressing only |
| Register Bank Selection | $\text{Bank} = 2 \cdot \text{RS1} + \text{RS0}$ | $\vert \text{RS1,RS0} \vert \in \{0,1\}$ |
| Initial SP (on RESET) | $\text{SP}_{reset} = 0x07$ | Bytes; 8-bit |
| Stack After $n$ PUSHes | $\text{SP}_{new} = \text{SP}_{old} + n$ | Bytes |
| DPTR Composition | $\text{DPTR} = 256 \cdot \text{DPH} + \text{DPL}$ | 16-bit, max 65535 |
| Bit-Addressable SFRs | $11$ registers, $88$ individually addressable bits | From 80H to F7H |
| Parity Flag | $\text{P} = 1$ if $ACC$ has odd number of 1s | Even parity auto-computed |
| Internal RAM Size | $128$ bytes standard, $256$ bytes in 8052 | Includes SFR block |
| Interrupt Priority Poll Order | $\overline{INT0} \to T_0 \to \overline{INT1} \to T_1 \to \text{Serial}$ | Same-priority level only |
| 8051 Clock Period | $T_{clk} = \dfrac{1}{f_{osc}} \cdot 12$ | One machine cycle = 12 oscillator periods |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The SFR map is the foundation of all embedded programming in the 8051 family. Mastery of this memory map is essential for embedded firmware in industrial controllers, automotive ECUs (older systems), consumer electronics (washing machines, microwave ovens), and educational kits (e.g., the 8051 training module in many Kerala polytechnic and engineering curricula).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation — Calculating Register Bank RAM Address

**Problem Setup:** Given the current PSW contents, derive the RAM address range corresponding to R0 of the active register bank.

**Given:** The 8051 has 4 register banks occupying addresses `0x00`, `0x08`, `0x10`, `0x18` (each spaced 8 bytes apart). Bits `RS1` and `RS0` of the `PSW` register select the active bank.

**Step 1 — Identify the bank-selection encoding.**

The 8051's hardware decodes `RS1:RS0` as a 2-bit binary number, indexing into the four register banks in ascending order of RAM address.

$$
\text{Bank Index } (k) = 2 \cdot \text{RS1} + \text{RS0}
$$

**Step 2 — Relate bank index to base RAM address.**

Each bank consumes 8 consecutive bytes. Therefore, the base address of the selected bank is:

$$
\text{Base Address} = 8 \cdot k = 8 \cdot (2 \cdot \text{RS1} + \text{RS0})
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the address of a specific register Rn.**

Registers within a bank are offset by their register number $n \in \{0,1,2,3,4,5,6,7\}$. Hence:

$$
\text{Address of Rn} = \text{Base Address} + n = 8 \cdot (2 \cdot \text{RS1} + \text{RS0}) + n
$$

**Step 4 — Worked example.**

Suppose the instruction `SETB PSW.3` is executed (i.e., `RS0 = 1`, `RS1 = 0`). Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
k &= 2 \cdot (0) + (1) = 1 \\
\text{Base Address} &= 8 \cdot 1 = 0x08 \\
\text{Address of R0} &= 0x08 + 0 = 0x08 \\
\text{Address of R7} &= 0x08 + 7 = 0x0F
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence, Bank 1 occupies RAM locations `0x08` to `0x0F`.

> [!TIP]
> This derivation is asked frequently in KTU as a **"compute the address of R3 if RS1=1 and RS0=0"** type question. Memorize the formula $\text{Addr} = 8(2 \cdot \text{RS1} + \text{RS0}) + n$.

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation — Stack Pointer Evolution

**Problem Setup:** Trace the SP after a sequence of `PUSH` and `POP` operations starting from a freshly reset 8051.

**Given (default after RESET):**

$$
\text{SP}_{initial} = 0x07
$$

**Step 1 — Recall the growth direction.** The 8051 stack grows **upward** (toward higher memory addresses). A `PUSH` operation:
1. First **increments** SP by 1.
2. Then writes the data at the new SP address.

Therefore, the first `PUSH` writes to address `0x08`, exactly at the location of R0 in Bank 1.

**Step 2 — Trace a sequence.**

Suppose we execute:
```
MOV SP, #0x60   ; Relocate stack to safe area
PUSH ACC        ; (1) SP becomes 0x61, ACC written at 0x61
PUSH PSW        ; (2) SP becomes 0x62, PSW written at 0x62
POP PSW         ; (3) SP becomes 0x61, value at 0x61 discarded into PSW
```

The net effect is that PSW is "swapped" — common in register bank switching. The mathematics:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{After MOV SP, \#0x60} &\rightarrow \text{SP} = 0x60 \\
\text{After PUSH ACC} &\rightarrow \text{SP} = 0x60 + 1 = 0x61 \\
\text{After PUSH PSW} &\rightarrow \text{SP} = 0x61 + 1 = 0x62 \\
\text{After POP PSW} &\rightarrow \text{SP} = 0x62 - 1 = 0x61
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — General formula** for $p$ pushes and $q$ pops:

$$
\text{SP}_{final} = \text{SP}_{initial} + p - q
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This stack mechanism is **identical** for hardware interrupt entry: the hardware automatically pushes the return address (2 bytes), then the programmer's prologue code often pushes ACC, B, DPL, DPH, and PSW (taking the stack to `SP_initial + 12` or more).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — SFR Memory Map (Authoritative Table)

The following table is the **canonical KTU reference** for the standard 8051 SFR block. All addresses are in **hexadecimal**, and **RS** denotes Reset Value.

| Address | SFR | RS | Bit-Addr | Function / Key Bits |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 80H | **P0** | FFH | Yes | Port 0 latch |
| 81H | **SP** | 07H | No | Stack Pointer |
| 82H | **DPL** | 00H | No | Data Pointer Low |
| 83H | **DPH** | 00H | No | Data Pointer High |
| 87H | **PCON** | 00H | No | Power Control (SMOD, PD, IDL) |
| 88H | **TCON** | 00H | Yes | Timer/Counter Control |
| 89H | **TMOD** | 00H | No | Timer/Counter Mode |
| 8AH | **TL0** | 00H | No | Timer 0 Low byte |
| 8BH | **TL1** | 00H | No | Timer 1 Low byte |
| 8CH | **TH0** | 00H | No | Timer 0 High byte |
| 8DH | **TH1** | 00H | No | Timer 1 High byte |
| 90H | **P1** | FFH | Yes | Port 1 latch |
| 98H | **SCON** | 00H | Yes | Serial Control |
| 99H | **SBUF** | 00H | No | Serial Data Buffer |
| A0H | **P2** | FFH | Yes | Port 2 latch |
| A8H | **IE** | 00H | Yes | Interrupt Enable |
| B0H | **P3** | FFH | Yes | Port 3 latch |
| B8H | **IP** | 00H | Yes | Interrupt Priority |
| D0H | **PSW** | 00H | Yes | Program Status Word |
| E0H | **ACC** | 00H | Yes | Accumulator |
| F0H | **B** | 00H | Yes | B Register |

**Resets for 8052-additional SFRs:**

| Address | SFR | RS | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| C8H | T2CON | 00H | Timer 2 Control |
| C9H | T2MOD | 00H | Timer 2 Mode |
| CAH | RCAP2L | 00H | Timer 2 Reload Low |
| CBH | RCAP2H | 00H | Timer 2 Reload High |
| CCH | TL2 | 00H | Timer 2 Low byte |
| CDH | TH2 | 00H | Timer 2 High byte |

> [!WARNING]
> KTU questions often test that the **P0–P3 reset value is FFH** (all ones), making ports ready as inputs with internal pull-ups (except P0). The 8051 ports are thus "high-impedance inputs" on power-up.

## 3.4 Analytical Derivation — Parity Flag Calculation

**Given:** The 8051 automatically computes the **even parity** bit `P` in PSW after every instruction that writes to the Accumulator.

**Definition:** $\text{P} = 1$ if `ACC` contains an **odd** number of 1-bits; $\text{P} = 0$ if `ACC` contains an **even** number of 1-bits.

**Step 1 — Count the 1-bits in ACC.**

Let $A = \sum_{i=0}^{7} a_i \cdot 2^i$ where $a_i \in \{0,1\}$ are the bits of the accumulator.

**Step 2 — Apply the parity rule.**

$$
\text{P} = \left( \sum_{i=0}^{7} a_i \right) \bmod 2
$$

**Step 3 — Example.**

If `MOV A, #0x95` is executed, then $A = 1001\,0101_2$. The number of 1s = 4 (even).

$$
\text{P} = 4 \bmod 2 = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Another example.**

If `MOV A, #0x96` is executed, then $A = 1001\,0110_2$. The number of 1s = 4 (even). Wait — let us choose a clearer example: `MOV A, #0xA3` → $A = 1010\,0011_2$, ones = 4 (even), so $\text{P} = 0$. For `MOV A, #0x07` → $A = 0000\,0111_2$, ones = 3 (odd), so $\text{P} = 1$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{If } \text{ACC} = 0x07 \rightarrow \text{P} = 1 \\
\text{If } \text{ACC} = 0xA3 \rightarrow \text{P} = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> The parity flag is **read-only** (cannot be set/cleared directly by user) and is used for **serial communication** (Mode 1, 2, 3) where the 8051 can be configured to append a parity bit automatically via `SCON` bits.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — 8051 Internal Data Memory Organization

The following Mermaid diagram visualizes the partitioning of the 8051's 256-byte internal data memory, highlighting where the SFR block resides and the bit-addressable zones.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph IDM[Internal Data Memory - 256 Bytes]
        direction TB
        subgraph LR[Lower 128 Bytes 0x00 to 0x7F]
            direction TB
            RB0[Register Bank 0 - 0x00 to 0x07 - R0 to R7]
            RB1[Register Bank 1 - 0x08 to 0x0F - R0 to R7]
            RB2[Register Bank 2 - 0x10 to 0x17 - R0 to R7]
            RB3[Register Bank 3 - 0x18 to 0x1F - R0 to R7]
            BA[Bit-Addressable Area - 0x20 to 0x2F - 128 bits]
            GP[Scratch RAM - 0x30 to 0x7F - 80 bytes]
        end
        subgraph UR[Upper 128 Bytes 0x80 to 0xFF]
            direction TB
            SFR1[SFR Block - 0x80 to 0xFF - 21 to 26 implemented registers]
            XSFR[Unimplemented - read 1 write ignored]
        end
    end
    RB0 --> RB1
    RB1 --> RB2
    RB2 --> RB3
    RB3 --> BA
    BA --> GP
    GP --> SFR1
    SFR1 --> XSFR
    style IDM fill:#f0f8ff,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:2px
    style LR fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,stroke-width:1px
    style UR fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:1px
    style BA fill:#fecaca,stroke:#991b1b,stroke-width:1px
    style SFR1 fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — SFR Functional Classification

A block-level functional architecture flow showing how SFRs are grouped by hardware subsystem.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CPU[8051 CPU Core] --> ACC[ACC E0H]
    CPU --> B[B F0H]
    CPU --> PSW[PSW D0H - Flags and Bank Select]
    CPU --> SP[SP 81H - Stack Pointer]
    CPU --> DPTR[DPTR 82H 83H - Data Pointer]
    CPU --> P0[P0 80H - Port 0]
    CPU --> P1[P1 90H - Port 1]
    CPU --> P2[P2 A0H - Port 2]
    CPU --> P3[P3 B0H - Port 3 with Alt Functions]
    CPU --> TMOD[TMOD 89H - Timer Mode]
    CPU --> TCON[TCON 88H - Timer Control]
    CPU --> TL0TH0[TL0 TH0 8A 8CH - Timer 0]
    CPU --> TL1TH1[TL1 TH1 8B 8DH - Timer 1]
    CPU --> SCON[SCON 98H - Serial Control]
    CPU --> SBUF[SBUF 99H - Serial Buffer]
    CPU --> IE[IE A8H - Interrupt Enable]
    CPU --> IP[IP B8H - Interrupt Priority]
    CPU --> PCON[PCON 87H - Power Control]
    PSW --> FLG[Flags - CY AC OV P]
    P3 --> AF1[RxD TxD INT0 INT1 T0 T1 WR RD]
    IE --> MASK[EA ES ET1 EX1 ET0 EX0]
    style CPU fill:#1e3a8a,color:#fff,stroke:#000
    style FLG fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309
    style AF1 fill:#fecaca,stroke:#991b1b
    style MASK fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534
```

## 4.3 Schematic — PSW Register Bit Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PSW_REG[PSW at D0H - 8 bits]
        direction LR
        CY[D7 - CY - Carry Flag]
        AC[D6 - AC - Auxiliary Carry]
        F0[D5 - F0 - User Flag]
        RS1[D4 - RS1 - Bank Select 1]
        RS0[D3 - RS0 - Bank Select 0]
        OV[D2 - OV - Overflow]
        RES[D1 - Reserved]
        P[D0 - P - Parity]
    end
    CY --- AC --- F0 --- RS1 --- RS0 --- OV --- RES --- P
    style PSW_REG fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:2px
    style CY fill:#fecaca
    style AC fill:#fed7aa
    style OV fill:#fef3c7
    style P fill:#dcfce7
```

## 4.4 Schematic — IE Register Bit Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IE_REG[IE at A8H - 8 bits]
        direction LR
        EA[IE.7 - EA - Global Enable]
        RSV1[IE.6 - Reserved]
        RSV2[IE.5 - Reserved]
        ES[IE.4 - ES - Serial Port]
        ET1[IE.3 - ET1 - Timer 1]
        EX1[IE.2 - EX1 - External Int 1]
        ET0[IE.1 - ET0 - Timer 0]
        EX0[IE.0 - EX0 - External Int 0]
    end
    EA --- RSV1 --- RSV2 --- ES --- ET1 --- EX1 --- ET0 --- EX0
    style IE_REG fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px
    style EA fill:#bbf7d0
    style ES fill:#fecaca
    style ET1 fill:#fed7aa
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these diagrams matter for KTU:** The examiner frequently asks students to **draw the PSW / IE / SCON register bit map** and explain the function of each bit. Use the bit positions `D7, D6, ..., D0` from MSB to LSB exactly as shown above.
