<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define Special Function Registers (SFRs) in the 8051 microcontroller. List any six important SFRs with their addresses.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Special Function Registers (SFRs) are dedicated on-chip registers inside the 8051 that control the operation of the CPU, I/O ports, timers, serial port, and interrupt system. They occupy the upper internal RAM addresses from **0x80 to 0xFF** and are accessible only through **direct addressing mode**.
>
> **Six Important SFRs:**
>
> | SFR | Address | Function |
> |---|---|---|
> | ACC | E0H | Accumulator |
> | B | F0H | B register (used by MUL/DIV) |
> | PSW | D0H | Program Status Word (flags) |
> | SP | 81H | Stack Pointer |
> | DPTR | 82H/83H | Data Pointer (16-bit) |
> | P1 | 90H | Port 1 latch |
>
> Other valid answers: TCON (88H), SCON (98H), IE (A8H), IP (B8H), TMOD (89H), PCON (87H), SBUF (99H). **(3 marks)**

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between bit-addressable and non-bit-addressable SFRs. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Bit-Addressable SFRs | Non-Bit-Addressable SFRs |
|---|---|---|
| **Bit-level access** | Each of the 8 bits can be accessed individually | Only the full byte can be accessed |
| **Instructions used** | `SETB bit`, `CLR bit`, `JB`, `JNB`, `ANL C,bit` | `MOV dir, A`, `ANL dir, #data` |
| **Hardware benefit** | Used for fast flag manipulation, port pin control, interrupt enables | Used for configuration values, mode selection |
| **Examples** | `PSW (D0H)`, `P1 (90H)`, `ACC (E0H)`, `TCON (88H)`, `SCON (98H)`, `IE (A8H)`, `IP (B8H)`, `P0 (80H)`, `P2 (A0H)`, `P3 (B0H)`, `B (F0H)` | `SP (81H)`, `DPTR (82H/83H)`, `TMOD (89H)`, `PCON (87H)`, `SBUF (99H)`, `TL0/TH0/TL1/TH1` |

> The 11 bit-addressable SFRs each provide 8 bit addresses, giving **88 individually addressable bits** in the SFR region. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the PSW register of the 8051 microcontroller in detail, with a neat bit-format diagram. Describe the function of each flag bit and the role of RS0, RS1 bits in register bank selection. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, describe the internal RAM organization of the 8051. Explain the role of the Stack Pointer and show step-by-step what happens to the SP value after the execution of the following instructions (assume initial SP = 07H):

```
MOV SP, #5FH
PUSH ACC
PUSH B
POP 00H     ; (Invalid in 8051 — used illustratively)
POP 00H
```

Show the final SP value. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part A(a):**

The **Program Status Word (PSW)** at address **D0H** is an 8-bit bit-addressable SFR that contains the arithmetic flags and the active register bank selector.

| Bit | Symbol | Address | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| D7 | **CY** | D7H | Carry flag — set on carry out of bit 7 during addition; borrow in subtraction |
| D6 | **AC** | D6H | Auxiliary carry — carry between bits 3 and 4; used for BCD correction via `DA A` |
| D5 | **F0** | D5H | General-purpose user flag; freely available to the programmer |
| D4 | **RS1** | D4H | Register bank select bit 1 |
| D3 | **RS0** | D3H | Register bank select bit 0 |
| D2 | **OV** | D2H | Overflow flag — set on signed arithmetic overflow (e.g., 7F+01) |
| D1 | — | D1H | Reserved (undefined behavior) |
| D0 | **P** | D0H | Parity flag — auto-set to 1 if ACC has odd number of 1s |

**Register Bank Selection:**

| RS1 | RS0 | Bank | RAM Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | Bank 0 | 00H – 07H |
| 0 | 1 | Bank 1 | 08H – 0FH |
| 1 | 0 | Bank 2 | 10H – 17H |
| 1 | 1 | Bank 3 | 18H – 1FH |

The address of register $R_n$ in the active bank is calculated as:

$$
\text{Address of } R_n = 8 \cdot (2 \cdot \text{RS1} + \text{RS0}) + n
$$

For example, if RS1=1, RS0=0, the active bank is Bank 2 (0x10–0x17), and R3 resides at 0x10 + 3 = **0x13**. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part A(b):**

**Internal RAM Organization of 8051 (256 bytes):**

| Address Range | Size | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 0x00 – 0x1F | 32 bytes | 4 Register Banks (0–3), 8 bytes each |
| 0x20 – 0x2F | 16 bytes | Bit-addressable area (128 bits) |
| 0x30 – 0x7F | 80 bytes | General-purpose scratch RAM |
| 0x80 – 0xFF | 128 bytes | SFR block (direct addressing) |

**Stack Pointer Role:** SP is an 8-bit SFR (address 0x81) that holds the address of the **top of the stack**. The 8051 stack grows **upward**. A `PUSH` increments SP then writes; a `POP` reads then decrements SP.

**Step-by-step SP evolution:**

```
Initial:                  SP = 0x07
After  MOV SP, #5FH:      SP = 0x5F      ; explicit relocation
After  PUSH ACC:          SP = 0x5F + 1 = 0x60  ; ACC stored at 0x60
After  PUSH B:            SP = 0x60 + 1 = 0x61  ; B stored at 0x61
After  POP 00H:           SP = 0x61 - 1 = 0x60  ; value read from 0x61 (was B)
After  POP 00H:           SP = 0x60 - 1 = 0x5F  ; value read from 0x60 (was ACC)

Final SP value:           SP = 0x5F
```

**Stack memory state summary:**

| Address | Content |
|---|---|
| 0x5F | (unchanged, last restored spot) |
| 0x60 | ACC (saved earlier) |
| 0x61 | B (saved earlier) |

> The two POPs effectively restore the stack to its initial relocated value, demonstrating a "save-modify-restore" idiom common in ISRs. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the IE and IP registers of the 8051 with neat bit-format diagrams. Describe how the priority of interrupts is resolved when multiple interrupts occur simultaneously. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the function of the following SFRs: (i) TMOD and TCON, (ii) SCON and SBUF, (iii) DPTR, (iv) PCON. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part B(a):**

**IE Register (Interrupt Enable) — Address A8H, Bit-Addressable, Reset = 0x00**

| Bit | Symbol | Function |
|---|---|---|
| IE.7 | **EA** | Global enable — must be 1 to enable any interrupt |
| IE.6 | — | Reserved |
| IE.5 | — | Reserved |
| IE.4 | **ES** | Enable Serial Port interrupt |
| IE.3 | **ET1** | Enable Timer 1 overflow interrupt |
| IE.2 | **EX1** | Enable External Interrupt 1 |
| IE.1 | **ET0** | Enable Timer 0 overflow interrupt |
| IE.0 | **EX0** | Enable External Interrupt 0 |

**IP Register (Interrupt Priority) — Address B8H, Bit-Addressable, Reset = 0x00**

| Bit | Symbol | Function |
|---|---|---|
| IP.7 | — | Reserved |
| IP.6 | — | Reserved |
| IP.5 | — | Reserved |
| IP.4 | **PS** | Serial Port priority (1 = high) |
| IP.3 | **PT1** | Timer 1 priority (1 = high) |
| IP.2 | **PX1** | External Int 1 priority (1 = high) |
| IP.1 | **PT0** | Timer 0 priority (1 = high) |
| IP.0 | **PX0** | External Int 0 priority (1 = high) |

**Interrupt Priority Resolution:**

The 8051 supports **two priority levels**. A high-priority interrupt can interrupt a low-priority ISR, but not vice versa. Within the **same priority level**, when multiple interrupts occur simultaneously, the 8051 uses a **fixed hardware poll order**:

$$
\text{Poll Order} = \overline{INT0} \to T_0 \to \overline{INT1} \to T_1 \to \text{Serial Port}
$$

The vector addresses for these interrupts are:

| Interrupt | Vector Address |
|---|---|
| Reset | 0x0000 |
| External Int 0 | 0x0003 |
| Timer 0 | 0x000B |
| External Int 1 | 0x0013 |
| Timer 1 | 0x001B |
| Serial Port | 0x0023 |
| Timer 2 (8052) | 0x002B |

**Resolution algorithm:**

1. **High vs. low priority** is decided first. A high-priority interrupt preempts any low-priority ISR in progress.
2. **Within same priority**, the hardware checks the poll order.
3. The first eligible interrupt (one that is both `IE`-enabled and currently asserted) is serviced.
4. If two requests at the same priority arrive **truly simultaneously**, the one earlier in the poll order wins. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part B(b):**

**(i) TMOD (89H) and TCON (88H) — Timer Control:**

- **TMOD (89H)** is **not bit-addressable**; it configures the **mode and operation** of Timers 0 and 1. The upper nibble (bits 4–7) controls Timer 1, the lower nibble (bits 0–3) controls Timer 0. Each nibble is structured as: **GATE, C/T, M1, M0**.
  - **GATE**: 1 = hardware-controlled start (using INTx pin), 0 = software-controlled (using TRx).
  - **C/T**: 1 = counter (external pulses on Tx pin), 0 = timer (internal oscillator).
  - **M1, M0**: mode select — `00`=Mode 0 (13-bit), `01`=Mode 1 (16-bit), `10`=Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload), `11`=Mode 3 (split timer).

- **TCON (88H)** is **bit-addressable** and provides the **run-control and overflow flags**:
  - `TF1, TF0` (bits 7, 5): Timer 1/0 overflow flags, set by hardware, cleared by ISR.
  - `TR1, TR0` (bits 6, 4): Timer 1/0 run control bits, set/cleared by software.
  - `IE1, IE0` (bits 3, 1): External interrupt 1/0 edge flags.
  - `IT1, IT0` (bits 2, 0): External interrupt 1/0 trigger type — 0=low-level, 1=falling-edge.

**(ii) SCON (98H) and SBUF (99H) — Serial Port:**

- **SCON** is bit-addressable, reset to 0x00, and configures the **mode and status** of the on-chip UART.
  - `SM0, SM1` (bits 7, 6): Mode select — Mode 0 (8-bit shift register), Mode 1 (8-bit UART, variable baud), Mode 2 (9-bit UART, fixed baud), Mode 3 (9-bit UART, variable baud).
  - `SM2` (bit 5): Multiprocessor communication enable (Modes 2, 3).
  - `REN` (bit 4): Receive enable.
  - `TB8, RB8` (bits 3, 2): 9th transmitted/received data bit.
  - `TI, RI` (bits 1, 0): Transmit/Receive interrupt flags, cleared by software in ISR.

- **SBUF** is a single 8-bit register (address 99H) that serves as both the **transmit buffer** (writing to SBUF loads the next byte to send) and the **receive buffer** (reading SBUF retrieves the last byte received). They share an address but are physically separate latches.

**(iii) DPTR (82H/83H) — Data Pointer:**

DPTR is a **16-bit register** composed of DPH (high byte, 83H) and DPL (low byte, 82H). It is the only native 16-bit register in the standard 8051 and is used to:

1. **Address external data memory** (XDATA) via `MOVX A, @DPTR` and `MOVX @DPTR, A` — supports up to 64 KB of external RAM.
2. **Access code memory look-up tables** via `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` — useful for `JMP @A+DPTR` computed jumps.

For example, `MOV DPTR, #0x1234` sets DPH = 0x12 and DPL = 0x34, allowing a future external-memory access to 0x1234.

**(iv) PCON (87H) — Power Control:**

PCON is **not bit-addressable**. Its most significant bit **SMOD** (bit 7) **doubles the UART baud rate** in Modes 1, 2, 3 when set. The two lowest bits control power-saving modes:

- **PD (bit 1)** — Power-Down mode. The on-chip oscillator is stopped; only a hardware reset can recover.
- **IDL (bit 0)** — Idle mode. The CPU clock is gated off, but peripherals (timers, serial) keep running. Any enabled interrupt or reset wakes the CPU.

The remaining bits (2–6) are **reserved/undefined** on the standard 8051. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting the SP reset value of 0x07** — Marks deducted if the answer omits the initial value before tracing.
> 2. **Confusing bit addresses with byte addresses** — `P1.0` is at bit-address `90H`, while the byte P1 is also at `90H`. The CPU uses the same numerical value; the instruction mnemonic (e.g., `SETB 90H` vs. `MOV 90H, #data`) is the disambiguator.
> 3. **Calling P0–P3 as "ports" without distinguishing latch vs. pin** — The SFR is a **latch**; the physical pin is read only when the latch is bypassed (via `read-pin` instructions like `MOV A, P1` actually reads pin in some 8051 variants, while `INC P1` reads the latch).
> 4. **Stating the 8051 stack grows downward** — It grows **upward** (toward higher addresses). This is the opposite of the 8085/8086 stack convention.
> 5. **Forgetting `EA` in IE** — The global enable bit is the most commonly omitted detail.
> 6. **Confusing SBUF's transmit and receive buffers** — They share the same SFR address (99H) but are physically distinct latches.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **SFR memory range:** `0x80 – 0xFF` (direct addressing only).
- **Total on-chip data memory:** 256 bytes — split into lower 128 (user RAM) and upper 128 (SFRs).
- **SFR count:** 21 implemented in standard 8051; 26 in 8052 (with Timer 2).
- **Bit-addressable SFRs:** 11 registers, giving **88 individually accessible bits**.
- **ACC (E0H) and B (F0H)** are the CPU's primary arithmetic operands.
- **PSW (D0H)** holds **CY, AC, F0, RS1, RS0, OV, _, P** — note bit 1 is reserved.
- **Register bank selection:** 4 banks of 8 bytes each; selected by `RS1:RS0`. Default is Bank 0.
- **SP (81H) reset value = 0x07**; stack grows **upward** (PUSH increments, POP decrements).
- **DPTR (82H/83H)** is the **only 16-bit register**; used for XDATA and code look-ups.
- **P0 has no internal pull-up** — requires external 10 kΩ resistors when used as I/O.
- **P3 has dual functions** — RxD, TxD, INT0, INT1, T0, T1, WR, RD.
- **TMOD configures, TCON controls** timers; TMOD is byte-level, TCON is bit-level.
- **SBUF (99H) is dual** — separate transmit and receive latches at the same address.
- **SCON modes 0/1/2/3** — shift register, 8-bit UART, 9-bit fixed baud, 9-bit variable baud.
- **IE bit 7 (EA)** is the **global master switch**; without it, no interrupt fires.
- **IP provides 2 priority levels**; within a level, fixed poll order applies.
- **Interrupt vector addresses** (8 bytes apart): 0x03, 0x0B, 0x13, 0x001B, 0x0023 (and 0x002B for T2).
- **PCON's SMOD** bit doubles UART baud rate in modes 1, 2, 3.
- **Unimplemented SFR locations** (e.g., 84H, 85H, 86H, 8EH–96H, 97H, 9AH–9FH, A1H–A7H, A9H–AFH, B1H–B7H, B9H–BFH, C0H–CFH, D1H–DFH, E1H–EFH, F1H–FFH) **read as 1 and ignore writes** — must not be used.
- **Reset values of P0–P3 = 0xFF**, ready as input ports.
- **Reset value of all other SFRs = 0x00**, except SBUF which is also 0x00.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: I/O Pins Ports and Circuits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCIT404 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** I/O Pins Ports and Circuits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

In the **Intel 8051 Microcontroller Architecture**, an **I/O (Input/Output) Pin** is an individual physical terminal (leg) on the IC package through which the CPU exchanges digital data with the outside world. A **Port** is a logical grouping of **8 such pins** (an 8-bit wide data path) that is mapped to a **Special Function Register (SFR)** in the internal memory space, allowing the programmer to read or write the entire 8-bit port using a single instruction.

The 8051 Microcontroller has **four I/O ports — P0, P1, P2, and P3** — giving a total of **32 I/O pins**. Each pin is bidirectional (can act as input or output), but the internal hardware design of each port differs significantly, depending on whether it is used purely for I/O or whether it must also serve alternate functions like external memory addressing or serial communication.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Note (Must Memorize):**
> The 8051 has **4 ports × 8 pins = 32 I/O pins**.
> - **P0** → Open-drain, no internal pull-up, multiplexed with Address/Data bus (AD0–AD7).
> - **P1** → Quasi-bidirectional, general-purpose only, no alternate function.
> - **P2** → Quasi-bidirectional, multiplexed with high-byte address bus (A8–A15).
> - **P3** → Quasi-bidirectional, multiplexed with critical control signals (RxD, TxD, INT0, INT1, T0, T1, WR, RD).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Building with 32 Doors"

Imagine the 8051 chip as a **large office building** (the CPU), and the outside world as the **city street** (peripherals, sensors, LCD, motors). The building has **4 floors (P0, P1, P2, P3)**, and each floor has **8 doors (8 pins per port)**.

- **Doors can open inward (INPUT — read from outside)** or **outward (OUTPUT — write to outside)**.
- **Floor 1 (P0)** has doors that swing open freely to the outside but have **no springs** to close on their own — you must install external springs (pull-up resistors) to keep them in the "high" state. Moreover, this floor doubles as a **service entrance** for delivery trucks carrying extra memory (Address/Data bus).
- **Floor 2 (P1)** is a normal office floor with **spring-loaded doors** (internal pull-ups) — they swing back to "high" naturally.
- **Floor 3 (P2)** is similar to Floor 2, but during large deliveries, its doors also act as a **second service entrance** (high-byte address bus).
- **Floor 4 (P3)** has special-purpose doors: one is the **reception desk** (RxD, serial input), another is the **mail room** (TxD, serial output), others connect to **fire alarms** (INT0, INT1), **clocks** (T0, T1), and **control room signals** (WR, RD).

## 1.3 Port-to-SFR Address Mapping

| Port | SFR Name | SFR Address (Hex) | Bit Addressable | Reset Value |
|:----:|:--------:|:-----------------:|:---------------:|:-----------:|
| P0   | 80H      | Yes               | 80H–87H         | FFH         |
| P1   | 90H      | Yes               | 90H–97H         | FFH         |
| P2   | A0H      | Yes               | A0H–A7H         | FFH         |
| P3   | B0H      | Yes               | B0H–B7H         | FFH         |

> [!NOTE]
> After a **system reset**, all four ports are initialized to **FFH (all 1s)**, which configures them as **inputs** (ready to read external signals).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Generic 8051 Port Pin — Three Functional Blocks

Every 8051 I/O pin contains three internal sub-circuits:

1. **Latch (1-bit D flip-flop)** — Stores the logic value written by the CPU. Part of the port SFR.
2. **Output Driver** — A pair of transistors (one pull-up, one pull-down) that physically drives the pin voltage HIGH or LOW.
3. **Input Buffer** — A high-impedance read path that samples the voltage on the physical pin and feeds it back to the CPU's internal bus.

```
        +------------------+
CPU --> | Latch (D-FF)     | --+
        +------------------+   |
                               v
        +------------------+  Output Driver  ----> Pin (to outside world)
        | Control Logic    |   ^
        +------------------+   |
                               |
        +------------------+   |
        | Input Buffer     | <--
        +------------------+
```

## 2.2 Port 0 — The "True" Open-Drain Port

**Structure**: P0 has a unique dual-transistor output stage. A **multiplexer (MUX)** controlled by the internal signal **`/\\overline{ALE}\\,/ADDRESS_LATCH_ENABLE`** and the **Control** signal decides whether the pin is in *Address/Data mode* or *General I/O mode*.

- **In Address/Data mode** (during external memory access): The MUX connects the internal **Address/Data** line to the output driver, and an **internal pull-up FET** is activated. The pin becomes a strong push-pull driver.
- **In General I/O mode**: The internal pull-up is **deactivated**. Only the lower pull-down transistor remains active, making it an **open-drain** output. The pin can only actively pull LOW; to produce a HIGH, **external pull-up resistors (typically 10 kΩ)** must be connected to +5V.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why P0 is open-drain in I/O mode?**
> Because P0 serves as the multiplexed Address/Data bus, its internal pull-up is sacrificed to allow the address and data to be time-multiplexed on the same physical pins. When P0 is freed (not accessing external memory), the pull-up must be disabled to prevent contention with the external bus.

## 2.3 Ports 1, 2, 3 — Quasi-Bidirectional I/O

These three ports share an identical internal structure, often called the **"quasi-bidirectional"** configuration. The key insight is:

- A **"weak" pull-up FET** is **permanently connected** to Vcc through the latch.
- A **"strong" pull-down FET** is activated by the inverted latch output.

| Latch Bit | Strong Pull-Down | Weak Pull-Up | Effective Pin Voltage | Mode           |
|:---------:|:----------------:|:------------:|:---------------------:|:--------------:|
| 0         | ON               | OFF          | Strong LOW (0 V)      | Output LOW     |
| 1         | OFF              | ON           | Weak HIGH (~5 V)      | Input / Output HIGH |

**Why "Quasi"?**
- When the pin outputs a **HIGH (1)**, the weak pull-up can be **overridden** by an external device pulling the line LOW. This is a one-way transition: HIGH → LOW is fast, but LOW → HIGH is slow (controlled by the weak pull-up resistor charging any line capacitance).
- This allows a **single pin to function as both input and output without reconfiguration** — the external circuit can drive the pin low (input data) and the CPU can read it; later, the CPU writes 0 to latch to drive the pin low (output data).

## 2.4 Read Pin vs. Read Latch — The "Read-Modify-Write" Hazard

The 8051 has **two separate read paths** for each port:

- **Read Latch**: Reads the value stored in the internal D-FF (what the CPU last wrote).
- **Read Pin**: Reads the actual physical voltage on the external pin (used for genuine input operations).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Classic Pitfall**: Instructions like `ANL P1, A` or `INC P1` perform a **Read-Modify-Write (RMW)** operation. They **read the latch** (not the pin) to avoid the pin voltage being misinterpreted. However, instructions like `MOV A, P1` (read port to accumulator) **read the physical pin**, so a momentary external disturbance can corrupt the read.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|:-:|:--------|:--------------:|:-------------:|
| 1 | P0 external pull-up resistor | $R_{L} \approx 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ (typical) | Required when P0 used as I/O |
| 2 | DC Output Low Current (IOL) | $I_{OL} = 1.6\,\text{mA}$ per pin (max, V_OL = 0.45 V) | Sinks current for LED drive |
| 3 | DC Output High Current (IOH) | $I_{OH} = -60\,\mu\text{A}$ per pin (P1, P2, P3 weak pull-up) | Quasi-bidirectional: limited source current |
| 4 | P0 source current (I/O mode) | $\approx 0$ | No pull-up → cannot source current |
| 5 | P0 in address mode source current | $I_{OH} \approx -800\,\mu\text{A}$ | Strong push-pull during bus cycles |
| 6 | Input low voltage | $V_{IL} \leq 0.8\,\text{V}$ | Logic 0 threshold |
| 7 | Input high voltage | $V_{IH} \geq 2.0\,\text{V}$ | Logic 1 threshold |
| 8 | Number of I/O lines | $N_{IO} = 4 \times 8 = 32$ | Total bidirectional pins |
| 9 | SFR address formula | Base + bit number | e.g., P1.3 = 90H + 3 = 93H |
| 10 | Fan-out (DC) | $N = \dfrac{I_{OL(\text{OL max})}}{I_{IL(\text{IL max})}}$ | Pure numeric ratio, dimensionless |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The 8051's I/O structure has profound practical implications:

- **P0 is reserved** for connecting external memory/parallel bus devices (LCD in 8-bit mode, external ADC, RAM). Engineers use P0 with a **74HC573 octal latch** to demultiplex the Address/Data bus.
- **P1 is the "safe" general-purpose port** — easiest to use for LEDs, switches, relays, and digital I/O.
- **P2 carries the upper address byte** when external memory is used; in such systems, P2 is essentially consumed by the bus and is **not available for I/O**.
- **P3 pins are precious**: serial port, timers, and interrupts all use P3. Engineers must carefully plan pin allocation to avoid conflicts with alternate functions.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Quasi-Bidirectional Pin Behavior

Consider a single pin of P1 (logic equivalent). Let $Q$ be the latch output, and let $V_{pin}$ be the physical pin voltage.

**Step 1 — The latch drives two transistors.**

A pull-up FET (PMOS) is gated by $Q$, and a pull-down FET (NMOS) is gated by $\overline{Q}$.

**Step 2 — When the CPU writes 0 ($Q = 0$):**

$$
\overline{Q} = 1 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{NMOS ON, PMOS OFF}
$$

$$
V_{pin} \approx 0\,\text{V} \quad \text{(Strong LOW)}
$$

The pin actively sinks current from any connected load. The strong NMOS can pull down even if the load tries to hold the line high.

**Step 3 — When the CPU writes 1 ($Q = 1$):**

$$
\overline{Q} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{NMOS OFF, PMOS ON}
$$

$$
V_{pin} \approx V_{CC} \quad \text{(Weak HIGH)}
$$

The pin is held high by a high-value resistor (typically $\sim 40\,\text{k}\Omega$ internally), so the source current is limited to tens of microamperes.

**Step 4 — When an external device drives the pin LOW while Q = 1:**

The weak pull-up ($\sim 40\,\text{k}\Omega$) cannot overcome the external low-impedance driver, so:

$$
V_{pin} \approx 0\,\text{V} \quad \text{(External LOW wins)}
$$

The CPU can read this external value via the input buffer, achieving **input functionality without changing the latch**.

**Step 5 — Asymmetry analysis (why "quasi"):**

The transition $0 \to 1$ is **slow** (limited by the weak pull-up charging the line capacitance), but $1 \to 0$ is **fast** (strong NMOS discharges). This asymmetry defines the "quasi" behavior.

The RC rise time for a pin with load capacitance $C_L$ is:

$$
\tau_{rise} = R_{pullup} \cdot C_L
$$

For typical values $R_{pullup} = 40\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $C_L = 50\,\text{pF}$:

$$
\tau_{rise} = (40 \times 10^{3}) \cdot (50 \times 10^{-12}) = 2 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{s} = 2\,\mu\text{s}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Port 0 Address/Data Demultiplexing

When the 8051 accesses external memory, P0 carries both the **lower 8-bit address** and the **8-bit data** in two distinct time windows of the same bus cycle.

**Step 1 — Identify the two phases:**

- **Phase 1 (Address phase)**: $\text{ALE} = 1$ (HIGH). P0 outputs $\text{A0–A7}$ and the external latch (74HC573) captures them on the falling edge of ALE.
- **Phase 2 (Data phase)**: $\text{ALE} = 0$. P0 is now freed; it either **drives** the data byte (write cycle, with strong internal pull-up active) or **floats** to let the external memory drive the data (read cycle).

**Step 2 — The control signal equation:**

Let $C$ be the control line that activates the internal pull-up. The control logic yields:

$$
C = \overline{\text{ALE}} \cdot \overline{\text{READ}} \quad \text{(or similar, simplified)}
$$

The internal pull-up is **only enabled** when P0 is in the **data phase of a write cycle** to external memory.

**Step 3 — In the absence of external memory access:**

$$
C = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Internal pull-up OFF} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Open-drain behavior}
$$

Hence, the external **10 kΩ pull-up resistors are mandatory** when P0 is used as a general-purpose I/O port.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Pin Behavior Summary

| Port | Pin Mode | Internal Pull-Up | External Pull-Up Needed? | Source Current (HIGH) | Sink Current (LOW) | Alternate Function |
|:----:|:--------:|:----------------:|:------------------------:|:---------------------:|:------------------:|:------------------:|
| P0   | I/O (no ext mem) | None (open-drain) | **Yes (≈ 10 kΩ)** | $\approx 0$ | $\leq 1.6$ mA | — |
| P0   | Address/Data bus | Active during bus | No | $\approx 800\,\mu$A | $\leq 3.2$ mA | AD0–AD7 |
| P1   | I/O only         | Weak (~40 kΩ)     | No | $\approx 60\,\mu$A | $\leq 1.6$ mA | — |
| P2   | I/O (no ext mem) | Weak (~40 kΩ)     | No | $\approx 60\,\mu$A | $\leq 1.6$ mA | — |
| P2   | Address bus (A8–A15) | Weak (~40 kΩ) | No | $\approx 60\,\mu$A | $\leq 1.6$ mA | A8–A15 |
| P3   | I/O (alt. fn OFF) | Weak (~40 kΩ)    | No | $\approx 60\,\mu$A | $\leq 1.6$ mA | RxD, TxD, INT0/1, T0/1, WR, RD |

## 3.4 KTU 8051 Port 3 Alternate Function Table (Must Memorize)

| Bit   | Pin  | Alternate Function | Direction | SFR Bit | KTU Mnemonic |
|:-----:|:----:|:------------------:|:---------:|:-------:|:------------:|
| P3.0  | 10   | RxD (Serial Input) | Input     | B0H     | **R**eceive |
| P3.1  | 11   | TxD (Serial Output)| Output    | B1H     | **T**ransmit |
| P3.2  | 12   | $\overline{\text{INT0}}$ (External Interrupt 0) | Input | B2H | **I**nterrupt 0 |
| P3.3  | 13   | $\overline{\text{INT1}}$ (External Interrupt 1) | Input | B3H | **I**nterrupt 1 |
| P3.4  | 14   | T0 (Timer 0 Input) | Input     | B4H     | **T**imer 0 |
| P3.5  | 15   | T1 (Timer 1 Input) | Input     | B5H     | **T**imer 1 |
| P3.6  | 16   | $\overline{\text{WR}}$ (External Write strobe) | Output | B6H | **W**rite |
| P3.7  | 17   | $\overline{\text{RD}}$ (External Read strobe)  | Output | B7H | **R**ead |

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    pullupFET[Internal Pull up FET weak]
    pulldownFET[Strong Pull down FET NMOS]
    pinP0[Physical Pin P0x]
    extPU[External 10k Pull up to Vcc]
    inBufP0[Input Buffer to CPU]

    addrData --> ctrlMux
    latchP0 --> ctrlMux
    ctrlMux --> pullupFET
    latchP0 --> pulldownFET
    pullupFET --> pinP0

```

## 4.3 Quasi-Bidirectional Port (P1, P2, P3) — Internal Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    cpuLatch[Latch from SFR bit write]
    cpuRead[CPU read pin or read latch]
    inBuf[Input Buffer high Z]
    weakPU[Weak Pull up FET PMOS about 40k]
    strongPD[Strong Pull down FET NMOS]
    pinNode[Physical Pin Px.y]

    cpuLatch -->|Q direct| weakPU
    cpuLatch -->|Q inverted| strongPD
    weakPU --> pinNode

```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Dip40[8051 PDIP 40 Pin]
        p1a[P1.0 Pin 1]
        p1b[P1.7 Pin 8]

    end
    p1a --> p1b
    p3a --> p3b
    p0a --> p0b
    p2a --> p2b
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the standard **40-pin DIP package** of the 8051, the four ports are physically split across the chip. **P3 has 8 pins but is *not* physically contiguous** — pins 10–17. Likewise, **P0 is at pins 32–39** and **P2 is at pins 21–28**. This non-contiguity is a common source of wiring errors for KTU students building hardware projects.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is a quasi-bidirectional I/O port? Why is it used in the 8051 microcontroller?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A quasi-bidirectional I/O port is a port whose pin can function as both input and output **without explicit reconfiguration** of the data direction. Each pin has a **weak internal pull-up resistor** (always connected to Vcc) and a **strong pull-down transistor** controlled by the port latch.

- When the latch is **0**: the strong NMOS is ON, the pin is **strongly driven LOW**.
- When the latch is **1**: the strong NMOS is OFF, the weak PMOS holds the pin **HIGH**, but an external device can easily pull it LOW.

This design is used in the 8051 because it **eliminates the need for a separate Data Direction Register (DDR)**, reducing the instruction overhead and making single-pin I/O operations efficient.

---

Port 3 serves the following alternate functions:

| P3.1 | TxD      | Serial port transmit output          |
| P3.2 | INT0-bar | External interrupt 0 request         |
| P3.3 | INT1-bar | External interrupt 1 request         |
| P3.4 | T0       | Timer 0 external input               |
| P3.5 | T1       | Timer 1 external input               |
| P3.6 | WR-bar   | External data memory write strobe    |
| P3.7 | RD-bar   | External data memory read strobe     |

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3 Internal Choice Q1]**

**(a) Describe the internal structure of Port 0 of the 8051 with a neat diagram. Why does Port 0 require external pull-up resistors when used for general-purpose I/O? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit Description (4 marks):**

Port 0 of the 8051 is the most complex of the four ports. It has:
1. A **latch** (D-FF) that stores the output bit.
2. A **multiplexer (MUX)** controlled by an internal "Control" signal.
3. A **strong pull-down NMOS transistor** (active-low driver).
4. A **weak pull-up PMOS transistor** (optional, bus-mode only).
5. An **input buffer** for reading the physical pin.

```
        +-------+
   DFF Q| LATCH |----> to strong NMOS (pull-down)
        +-------+
            |
            +---> to MUX
                   |
   Internal <------+----> to weak PMOS (pull-up)
   Address/Data
        +-------+
        | INPUT |----> to CPU
        | BUFFER|
        +-------+
            ^
            |
   Physical Pin (P0.x)
```

**Operation in Two Modes (2 marks):**

- **Bus Mode (External Memory Access)**: The MUX connects the internal Address/Data line to the output stage. The weak PMOS pull-up is **activated**, giving a strong push-pull driver for address and data transfer.
- **General I/O Mode**: The MUX connects the latch output to the driver, and the **weak PMOS pull-up is DEACTIVATED**. The output stage becomes **open-drain** (only the NMOS is available).

**Why External Pull-up is Required (1 mark):**

In general I/O mode, when the latch is 1, the NMOS is OFF and there is **no internal path to Vcc**. Without an external pull-up resistor, the pin would **float** (undefined logic level). A typical **10 kΩ pull-up to +5V** restores a valid logic HIGH. This is also why P0 cannot source current to drive an LED directly in I/O mode — it can only sink current (drive LOW).

---

**(b) Explain the concept of "read-modify-write" hazard in 8051 ports with an example. How does the 8051 overcome it? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**The Problem (3 marks):**

The 8051 has **two physical read paths** for each port:
- **Read Pin**: samples the actual external voltage on the pin.
- **Read Latch**: reads the last value written by the CPU.

Instructions that involve a **Read-Modify-Write (RMW)** sequence (e.g., `ANL P1, A`, `ORL P1, A`, `XRL P1, A`, `INC P1`, `CPL P3.0`, bit-level SETB/CLR) operate as follows:
1. **Read** the current value of the port.
2. **Modify** the read value in the ALU.
3. **Write** the modified value back to the port.

If the CPU read the **physical pin** (which might be disturbed by an external device driving it LOW), the modification would corrupt the other bits that are currently outputting HIGH. This is the **read-modify-write hazard**.

**Example (2 marks):**
Suppose P1 = 1111 1111 (all outputs HIGH). An external switch pulls P1.3 to LOW. The CPU executes `ANL P1, #0F0H` (clear lower nibble).
- If the CPU **read the pin**, it would see `1111 0111` (bit 3 LOW), then `AND 0F0H` = `1111 0000`, then write back → **bits 4–7 still HIGH (correct)**, bits 0–3 forced LOW. The act of modification only affects intended bits.
- However, if the switch had been on **bit 7** instead, reading the pin would see `0111 1111`, AND with `0F0H` = `0111 0000`, written back → **bit 7 is wrongly cleared** even though the CPU never intended to touch it.

**How 8051 Solves It (2 marks):**

The 8051's internal bus design routes the **RMW instructions to read the LATCH** (not the pin). The instruction decoder generates a special "read latch" control signal for RMW opcodes. This ensures that modifications are based on the **last written value** (a known reference), and external disturbances on the pin do not corrupt the other bits. The "real" pin reading is only used for genuine input operations like `MOV A, P1`.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Module 3 Internal Choice Q2]**

**(a) With a neat diagram, explain the quasi-bidirectional I/O structure used by Ports 1, 2, and 3 of the 8051. List the conditions for each possible pin state. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit Diagram (3 marks):**

```
   Vcc
    |
   [R_weak ≈ 40 kΩ]    (Permanent internal pull-up)
    |
    +---> to physical pin
    |
   [PMOS_weak]
    |
   Q (latch output)
    |
   [NMOS_strong] <--- Q-bar (inverted latch)
    |
   GND
```

A read buffer also taps the pin and routes to the CPU for genuine input operations.

**Pin State Table (3 marks):**

| Latch Q | Internal NMOS (pull-down) | Internal PMOS (pull-up) | Pin Voltage | Logical Function |
|:-------:|:-------------------------:|:-----------------------:|:-----------:|:----------------:|
| 0       | ON                        | OFF                     | Strong 0 V  | Output LOW       |
| 1       | OFF                       | ON (weak)               | ~5 V (high impedance) | Input or Output HIGH |

**Engineering Implications (1 mark):**

- The weak pull-up allows external devices to **override** the HIGH state (used in matrix keypads, switch arrays, open-collector buses like I²C emulation).
- The 8051 thus implements **open-collector-like I/O** without the hardware complexity of an explicit DDR register.

---

**(b) Tabulate the alternate functions of all four ports of the 8051. Also explain the role of the EA-bar and ALE pins in enabling Port 0 and Port 2 as the external memory bus. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Alternate Function Table (4 marks):**

| Port | Pin Range | Alternate Function              | When Activated              |
|:----:|:---------:|:-------------------------------:|:---------------------------:|
| P0   | P0.0 – P0.7 | Multiplexed Address/Data bus (AD0–AD7) | When external memory is accessed |
| P1   | P1.0 – P1.7 | None (general-purpose I/O only) | Always I/O                   |
| P2   | P2.0 – P2.7 | High-byte address bus (A8–A15) | When external memory is accessed (EA-bar = 0, or > 4 KB code) |
| P3   | P3.0       | RxD (Serial Input)             | When SCON is configured     |
| P3   | P3.1       | TxD (Serial Output)            | When SCON is configured     |
| P3   | P3.2       | INT0-bar (Interrupt 0)         | When EX0 = 1 in IE register |
| P3   | P3.3       | INT1-bar (Interrupt 1)         | When EX1 = 1 in IE register |
| P3   | P3.4       | T0 (Timer 0 external input)    | When T0 is in counter mode  |
| P3   | P3.5       | T1 (Timer 1 external input)    | When T1 is in counter mode  |
| P3   | P3.6       | WR-bar (External Write strobe) | During MOVX @DPTR, A write  |
| P3   | P3.7       | RD-bar (External Read strobe)  | During MOVX A, @DPTR read   |

**Role of EA-bar and ALE (3 marks):**

- **$\overline{\text{EA}}$ (External Access) — Pin 31:**
  - When $\overline{\text{EA}} = 1$ (HIGH): The 8051 executes code from the **internal ROM** (up to 4 KB for the standard 8051, up to 8 KB for 8052). P0 and P2 function as **general I/O**.
  - When $\overline{\text{EA}} = 0$ (LOW): The 8051 fetches all code from **external ROM**. P0 and P2 are configured as the **address/data bus** automatically.

- **ALE (Address Latch Enable) — Pin 30:**
  - During external memory access, the lower 8 bits of the address and the 8-bit data are **time-multiplexed on P0**.
  - ALE emits a positive pulse in the first half of every bus cycle to **latch the address** (A0–A7) into an external 74HC573 latch.
  - In the second half of the cycle, P0 carries the actual data byte (either driven by the 8051 for write, or by the external device for read).
  - ALE also clocks the internal operations and emits a constant 1/6 of the oscillator frequency during normal mode (useful for external timing).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Losing Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting external pull-ups for P0**: Many students write code that sets P0 = 0xFF but fail to mention the 10 kΩ pull-up resistors. **No external pull-up → pin floats → undefined behavior.** Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this.
> 2. **Confusing P0's open-drain (I/O mode) with its push-pull (bus mode)**: When answering "why P0 needs pull-up," students often forget to specify **"only in general-purpose I/O mode."** Examiners want the condition stated explicitly.
> 3. **Mislabelling P2's role**: P2 is **NOT** the lower address bus; it carries the **upper 8 bits (A8–A15)**. P0 carries the lower 8 bits (A0–A7). Confusing these will cost full marks on a 7-mark sub-question.
> 4. **Forgetting the "quasi" property of P1/P2/P3**: Saying "they are bidirectional ports" is **incomplete**. You must mention the **weak pull-up + strong pull-down** asymmetry, and that the pin can be overridden externally.
> 5. **Bit addressability**: All four ports are **bit-addressable** SFRs. Writing `SETB P1.3` is valid. Forgetting this loses 1 mark in Part A questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- The 8051 has **32 I/O pins** organized as **4 ports of 8 bits each**: P0, P1, P2, P3.
- All ports are **bit-addressable**, SFR-mapped at **80H, 90H, A0H, B0H** respectively.
- After reset, all ports are at **FFH** (configured as input).
- **P0 is open-drain in I/O mode** — **external 10 kΩ pull-up resistors are mandatory** for proper HIGH output.
- **P0 also serves as the multiplexed Address/Data bus (AD0–AD7)** during external memory access; the lower address is latched externally using ALE.
- **P1 is purely general-purpose**, with no alternate function.
- **P2 serves as the high-byte address bus (A8–A15)** during external memory access.
- **P3 has 8 critical alternate functions**: RxD, TxD, INT0-bar, INT1-bar, T0, T1, WR-bar, RD-bar.
- **P1, P2, P3 are quasi-bidirectional**: weak internal pull-up (~40 kΩ) + strong NMOS pull-down.
- The **read-modify-write hazard** is resolved by routing RMW instructions to read the **latch** (not the pin).
- **ALE** is the address latch enable; **EA-bar** selects internal vs. external program memory.
- **P0 cannot source current** in I/O mode; it can only sink. Use P1/P2/P3 (with buffering) to drive LEDs in HIGH mode.
- For driving heavy loads, use external **driver ICs (ULN2003, 74LS244, 74LS245)** — never rely on the weak internal pull-up.
- The **pin numbering** in the 40-pin DIP is **non-contiguous** for P0, P2, P3 — always refer to the datasheet, not port order.
- **SFR bit addresses**: P1.3 = 93H, P3.7 = B7H, etc. (Base + bit position).
- **Trick question to expect**: "Why is P0's internal pull-up disabled in I/O mode?" → **Because P0's pull-up doubles as the bus driver when external memory is used; leaving it on would cause bus contention with external devices.** This is the most cited reason in 8051 textbooks (M. Mazidi, K. Ayala).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Instruction Set and Addressing Modes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCIT404 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Instruction Set and Addressing Modes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Instruction Set?

The **Instruction Set** of a microcontroller is the complete collection of machine-level commands (opcodes) that the CPU can decode, understand, and execute. For the **8051 microcontroller**, the instruction set is broadly classified into **5 functional groups** as mandated by the KTU 2024 syllabus.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PBCIT404 - Module 3):** The 8051 instruction set is a **CISC (Complex Instruction Set Computer)** based, 8-bit instruction set comprising **111 distinct instructions** (255 opcodes) that operate on the internal registers, SFRs, and program/data memory of the 8051 family.

### Functional Classification of 8051 Instructions
| Group | Category | Typical Mnemonic Examples | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Data Transfer** | `MOV`, `MOVX`, `MOVC`, `PUSH`, `POP`, `XCH` | Move data between registers, RAM, SFRs |
| 2 | **Arithmetic** | `ADD`, `ADDC`, `SUBB`, `MUL`, `DIV`, `INC`, `DEC`, `DA A` | Mathematical operations on 8-bit data |
| 3 | **Logical** | `ANL`, `ORL`, `XRL`, `CLR`, `CPL`, `RL`, `RR`, `SWAP` | Bitwise logic and bit manipulation |
| 4 | **Boolean (Bit)** | `SETB`, `CLR`, `ANL C`, `ORL C`, `MOV C`, `JC`, `JNC`, `JB`, `JNB` | Single-bit operations on bit-addressable space |
| 5 | **Program Branching** | `ACALL`, `LCALL`, `RET`, `RETI`, `AJMP`, `LJMP`, `SJMP`, `CJNE`, `DJNZ`, `JZ`, `JNZ` | Control flow: jumps, calls, loops |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of the instruction set as a **vocabulary of a language**. Just as you can express thousands of sentences using a fixed set of English words (~111 base instructions here), the 8051 executes complex programs using only these 111 instructions. The **opcode** is the verb (the action), and the **operands** are the nouns (the data the action works upon).

## 1.2 What are Addressing Modes?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition:** An **Addressing Mode** is a method by which the instruction specifies the location of its operand(s) — i.e., *where* to find the data on which the operation must be performed. The 8051 supports **5 distinct addressing modes**.

### Real-World Analogy (The "Where is the data?" Question)
Imagine you want a friend to fetch a book:

- *"Fetch **THIS** book"* → data is in hand → **Register / Immediate**
- *"Fetch the book **in my bag**"* → data is at a known location → **Direct**
- *"Fetch the book from the location **pointed to** by this note"* → note contains an address → **Register Indirect**
- *"Fetch the book on **shelf number 5 + offset 3**"* → base + index → **Indexed**

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Tip:** Always state the **mode name** AND the **effective memory location** for full marks. Writing only the mode loses 1 mark; the location calculation fetches the rest.

## 1.3 Opcode Format & Execution Time (Foundation)
Every 8051 instruction occupies **1, 2, or 3 bytes** in program memory, and consumes **1, 2, or 4 Machine Cycles**. Since the 8051 oscillator is divided by 12:

$$T_{machine\ cycle} = \frac{12}{f_{oscillator}}$$

- For a standard **11.0592 MHz** crystal: $T_{machine\ cycle} = 12 / 11.0592 \times 10^6 \approx \mathbf{1.085\ \mu s}$
- For a **12 MHz** crystal: $T_{machine\ cycle} = \mathbf{1\ \mu s}$ (often used in textbook examples)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 5 Addressing Modes of 8051 — Deep Dive

### Mode 1: Immediate Addressing (`#data`)
The data (operand) is **literally embedded inside the instruction** as a constant. The `#` symbol is the assembler directive that signals "this is a constant, not a memory address."

**Example:**
```assembly
MOV A, #25H      ; Load the literal value 25H into Accumulator
MOV DPTR, #0FE00H ; Load 16-bit constant into Data Pointer
```

**Key Engineering Insight:** The data is fetched along with the opcode from program memory, so this mode uses **2 or 3 bytes** of program memory but is the **fastest** in terms of CPU cycle consumption (no extra memory fetch for data).

---

### Mode 2: Register Addressing
The operand is one of the **internal CPU registers**: `R0–R7` (current bank), `A` (Accumulator), `B`, `DPTR`, or `AB` (as a pair).

**Example:**
```assembly
ADD A, R0        ; Add contents of R0 to A → result in A
MOV R3, A        ; Copy Accumulator to R3
```

**Bank Switching:** The active set of R0–R7 is selected by the **RS0 and RS1 bits** in the **PSW (Program Status Word)** register. There are **4 banks** (Bank 0–3) located at internal RAM addresses **00H–07H, 08H–0FH, 10H–17H, 18H–1FH**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why banks?** When an interrupt occurs, the 8051 can switch to a different R0–R7 bank in **1 machine cycle** instead of doing `PUSH` operations to save registers — a critical real-time feature for embedded systems.

---

### Mode 3: Direct Addressing
The instruction contains the **8-bit internal RAM address (00H–7FH)** or the **SFR address (80H–FFH)** where the data resides.

**Example:**
```assembly
MOV A, 30H       ; Copy contents of RAM[30H] into A
MOV 60H, A       ; Copy A into RAM[60H]
SETB P1.0        ; Set bit 0 of Port 1 (SFR at 90H)
```

**Address Space Used:**
- **00H–1FH** → 32 bytes of general-purpose RAM (4 banks × 8 registers)
- **20H–2FH** → 16 bytes of bit-addressable space (128 bits)
- **30H–7FH** → 80 bytes of general-purpose scratchpad RAM
- **80H–FFH** → SFR (Special Function Register) space

---

### Mode 4: Register Indirect Addressing
A register holds the **address of the data**, not the data itself. Only **R0, R1 (8-bit pointer for internal/external RAM)** and **DPTR (16-bit pointer for code/data)** are allowed. The `@" symbol denotes indirection.

**Example:**
```assembly
MOV A, @R0       ; A ← contents of RAM address pointed to by R0
MOVX A, @DPTR    ; A ← contents of external RAM at 16-bit address in DPTR
```

**Critical Distinction — `MOV` vs `MOVX` vs `MOVC`:**
| Mnemonic | Memory Accessed | Pointer | Address Space |
|---|---|---|---|
| `MOV` | Internal RAM / SFR | R0/R1 | 00H–FFH (8-bit) |
| `MOVX` | **External** data memory | R0/R1 (8-bit, P2 provides high byte) or DPTR (16-bit) | 0000H–FFFFH |
| `MOVC` | **Code memory** (Program ROM) | A + DPTR (or A + PC) | 0000H–FFFFH — used for **Lookup Tables** |

> [!TIP]
> **Board Trap:** `@R0` and `@R1` are the **only** registers usable for indirect addressing. You **cannot** write `@R2` or `@A` for general data — this is a common KTU viva question.

---

### Mode 5: Indexed Addressing
Used exclusively for **code memory lookups** (program ROM). The final address is computed as: **Base Register + Accumulator Offset**.

$$Address_{final} = DPTR + A \quad \text{or} \quad Address_{final} = PC + A$$

**Example:**
```assembly
MOVC A, @A+DPTR  ; A ← code memory at (DPTR + A)
```

**Real-World Engineering Use:** This is the canonical method for **implementing lookup tables (LUTs)** — e.g., retrieving the 7-segment code for a digit, computing `y = sin(x)` from a pre-computed sine table, or decoding ASCII to a custom font.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Limit |
|---|---|---|
| Machine Cycle Period | $T_{mc} = \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}}$ | seconds |
| For 11.0592 MHz | $T_{mc} \approx 1.085$ | μs |
| For 12 MHz (textbook) | $T_{mc} = 1$ | μs |
| Instruction Time | $T_{inst} = N \cdot T_{mc}$ where $N \in \{1, 2, 4\}$ | seconds |
| Indexed Address | $A_{eff} = (DPTR) + (A)$ | 16-bit |
| External RAM Page | $A_{eff} = (P2 \parallel R0/R1)$ | 16-bit (P2 high byte) |
| DPTR Reset Value | $0000\text{H}$ | — |
| Program Counter | Auto-increments after each byte fetch | 16-bit (64KB ROM) |
| Stack Growth Direction | **Upward** (toward higher address) | Starts at 07H (after `MOV SP, #07H`) |
| Maximum Stack | $SP$ can reach 7FH (internal RAM) | 128 bytes usable |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Hallucination Note:** The 8051 instruction set is officially **111 base instructions → 255 opcodes**. This is a verified Intel MCS-51 architectural fact. Do not confuse with the 8085 (246 instructions) or 8086 (~15000) instruction set sizes.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Execution Time of a 3-Byte, 2-Cycle Instruction

**Problem (KTU-style):** Compute the time taken to execute the instruction `MOV A, #55H` on an 8051 system using an **11.0592 MHz** crystal. Comment on the frequency of polling for a real-time sensor.

### Step 1 — Identify Instruction Properties
From the 8051 instruction set reference table:
- `MOV A, #data` is a **2-byte instruction** (1 byte opcode + 1 byte immediate data)
- It consumes **1 machine cycle** per byte fetched
- Total machine cycles = **1**

> For `MOV A, #55H`: opcode byte (74H) and immediate data byte (55H) are fetched in sequence, totaling **1 machine cycle** of execution.

### Step 2 — Compute Machine Cycle Period

$$
T_{mc} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}\ \text{Hz}}
$$

$$
T_{mc} = 1.085\ \mu s \quad (\text{exact: } 1.08506944...\ \mu s)
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Total Execution Time

$$
T_{inst} = N_{cycles} \times T_{mc} = 1 \times 1.085\ \mu s = 1.085\ \mu s
$$

### Step 4 — Engineering Interpretation
If a temperature sensor needs to be polled every **10 ms**, the number of `MOV` instructions that can fit in that polling window is:

$$
N_{instructions} = \frac{T_{poll}}{T_{inst}} = \frac{10 \times 10^{-3}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 9216\ \text{instructions}
$$

This means the 8051 has **massive headroom** for direct polling of slow sensors.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Indexed Addressing — Sine Wave Lookup Table

**Problem:** Compute the 8051 assembly to read `sin(θ)` for `θ` given in the Accumulator (0°–90° stored as 0–90 decimal in `A`).

**Mathematical Foundation:** The effective address is:

$$
A_{eff} = (DPTR) + (A)
$$

**Step-by-Step Code:**
```assembly
; Assume A contains 0..90 (degrees)
; DPTR must be pre-loaded with base address of sine table
MOV  DPTR, #SINE_TABLE   ; DPTR = 16-bit address of table in code memory
MOVC A, @A+DPTR          ; A ← CodeMem[DPTR + A]
SJMP $                   ; Halt / wait

SINE_TABLE:
    DB  0,   2,   3,   5,   7,   9,  10,  12,  14,  16
    DB 17,  19,  21,  22,  24,  26,  28,  29,  31,  33
    ; ... (scaled integer sine values for 0–90°)
```

**Analytical Walk-through:**
1. Assembler places `SINE_TABLE` at some address, e.g., `0x0400`.
2. Suppose `A = 30` (decimal, meaning 30°). The CPU computes `0x0400 + 0x001E = 0x041E`.
3. `MOVC` fetches the byte at program memory address `0x041E` and places it in `A`.
4. This is **read-only access** to program memory — the lookup table is permanent in ROM.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Addressing Mode Selection Guide

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is the **highest-yield summary** for KTU board exams. Memorize this grid.

| Mode | Operand Symbol | Typical Instruction | Bytes | Cycles | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Immediate** | `#data` | `MOV A, #25H` | 2 | 1 | Loading constants, initializing registers |
| **Register** | `Rn` (n=0–7) | `ADD A, R3` | 1 | 1 | Fast operations on the active bank |
| **Direct** | `direct` (00H–FFH) | `MOV 30H, A` | 2 | 2 | Accessing any RAM/SFR location by name |
| **Register Indirect** | `@R0`, `@R1`, `@DPTR` | `MOVX A, @DPTR` | 1 or 2 | 2 | Pointers, arrays, table traversal, external RAM |
| **Indexed** | `@A+DPTR`, `@A+PC` | `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` | 1 | 2 | Lookup tables in program ROM (e.g., 7-segment, sine) |

### Quick Derivation: Why `@A+DPTR` is the most powerful mode

The Accumulator can range 0–255, and DPTR can point anywhere in the **64 KB code space**. So a single instruction can access **any of 65,536 locations** based on a runtime variable — the foundation of **table-driven embedded algorithms**.

$$
A_{eff} = DPTR + A \quad \Rightarrow \quad A_{eff} \in [DPTR, DPTR + 255]
$$

This gives a sliding **256-byte window** into code memory, controlled entirely by the Accumulator's dynamic value.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture: How an Instruction is Fetched, Decoded, and Executed

This diagram maps the **data flow** of an 8051 instruction through the CPU, highlighting which **addressing mode** is invoked at the "Operands Resolution" stage.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Program Counter PC] -->|Holds next address| nodeB[Program Memory 64KB ROM]
    nodeB -->|Opcode Byte Fetch| nodeC[Instruction Register IR]
    nodeC -->|Decode Opcode| nodeD[Instruction Decoder]
    nodeD -->|Identify Addressing Mode| nodeE[Operand Resolution Unit]
    
    nodeE -->|Mode 1: Immediate| nodeF1[data is in instruction itself]
    nodeE -->|Mode 2: Register| nodeF2[Read from R0-R7, A, B, DPTR]
    nodeE -->|Mode 3: Direct| nodeF3[Fetch from RAM 00-7FH or SFR 80-FFH]
    nodeE -->|Mode 4: Register Indirect| nodeF4[Use R0 R1 or DPTR as pointer to RAM]
    nodeE -->|Mode 5: Indexed| nodeF5[Compute A plus DPTR or A plus PC]
    
    nodeF1 --> nodeG[ALU Arithmetic Logic Unit]
    nodeF2 --> nodeG
    nodeF3 --> nodeG
    nodeF4 --> nodeG
    nodeF5 --> nodeG
    
    nodeG -->|Result| nodeH[Store to Destination]
    nodeH -->|Update Flags in PSW| nodeI[Program Status Word]
    nodeH -->|PC plus plus| nodeA
    
    style nodeA fill:#FFE4B5
    style nodeB fill:#87CEEB
    style nodeC fill:#90EE90
    style nodeD fill:#DDA0DD
    style nodeE fill:#F0E68C
    style nodeG fill:#FFA07A
    style nodeI fill:#FFB6C1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Walkthrough:** The **Instruction Decoder** acts as a "traffic controller." It inspects the opcode, decides which of the **5 addressing paths** (Modes 1–5) to take, fetches the operand, and hands both opcode and operand to the **ALU**. The ALU then writes the result back and updates the **PSW flags** (Carry, Auxiliary Carry, Overflow, Parity, etc.).

---

## 4.2 Addressing Mode Resolution Tree (Decision Logic)

This flowchart mirrors the **mnemonic decoding logic** inside the 8051 silicon. When the decoder sees a specific opcode, it follows this tree:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Opcode Arrives] --> checkA{Operand starts with hash symbol?}
    checkA -->|Yes| modeA1[Mode 1: Immediate Addressing]
    checkA -->|No| checkB{Operand is R0 to R7?}
    checkB -->|Yes| modeA2[Mode 2: Register Addressing]
    checkB -->|No| checkC{Operand is at sign R0 or at sign R1 or at sign DPTR?}
    checkC -->|Yes| modeA4[Mode 4: Register Indirect Addressing]
    checkC -->|No| checkD{Opcode is MOVC type?}
    checkD -->|Yes| modeA5[Mode 5: Indexed Addressing at A plus DPTR]
    checkD -->|No| modeA3[Mode 3: Direct Addressing 8 bit address]
    
    style modeA1 fill:#FFD700
    style modeA2 fill:#FFD700
    style modeA3 fill:#FFD700
    style modeA4 fill:#FFD700
    style modeA5 fill:#FFD700
```

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Visual Aid:** Drawing this decision tree (or a similar one) in the exam answers **2 bonus marks** for "neat presentation" — a real KTU valuation habit.

---

## 4.3 Memory Map of 8051 — Showing Where Each Addressing Mode Operates

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CODE[Program Code Memory 64KB ROM]
        A0[0000H - Reset Vector]
        A1[0003H - External Int 0]
        A2[000BH - Timer 0 Overflow]
        A3[0013H - External Int 1]
        A4[001BH - Timer 1 Overflow]
        A5[0023H - Serial Port]
        A6[Lookup Tables - Mode 5 Indexed]
    end
    
    subgraph IDATA[Internal Data Memory 256 bytes]
        B0[00-1FH: 4 Register Banks R0-R7 - Mode 2]
        B1[20-2FH: Bit Addressable 128 bits - Boolean]
        B2[30-7FH: General Purpose RAM - Mode 3]
        B3[80-FFH: SFR Area - Mode 3 Direct]
    end
    
    subgraph XDATA[External Data Memory 64KB optional]
        C0[0000-FFFFH: External RAM - Mode 4 with MOVX]
    end
    
    style A0 fill:#FF6347
    style B0 fill:#7FFFD4
    style B1 fill:#FFE4B5
    style B2 fill:#87CEFA
    style B3 fill:#DDA0DD
    style C0 fill:#F0E68C
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Reading:** Mode 5 (Indexed) operates **exclusively on CODE memory** (top section). Mode 4 with `MOVX` operates on **XDATA** (bottom section). Modes 1, 2, 3, and 4-with-MOV operate on **IDATA** (middle section). This memory map is essential for KTU Module 3 viva questions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PBCIT404]`
**Differentiate between Immediate Addressing Mode and Direct Addressing Mode in 8051 with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Immediate | Direct |
|---|---|---|
| Symbol | `#data` | `direct` (8-bit address) |
| Data source | Constant embedded in instruction | Contents of RAM/SFR at given address |
| Byte size | 2 bytes (for `MOV A, #data`) | 2 or 3 bytes |
| Example | `MOV A, #25H` | `MOV A, 25H` |
| What `A` receives | Literal value `25H` | Value **stored at** RAM address `25H` |

**Key distinguisher:** In Immediate mode, the value is **inside the instruction itself**; in Direct mode, the instruction **points to a memory location** where the value resides. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PBCIT404]`
**List any three differences between `MOV`, `MOVX`, and `MOVC` instructions of 8051.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **`MOV`** → Accesses **internal RAM (00H–7FH) and SFRs (80H–FFH)**. Uses 8-bit addresses. Operands can be registers, direct, or indirect (@R0/@R1). *Example: `MOV A, @R0`.*
2. **`MOVX`** → Accesses **external data memory** (up to 64KB). Always uses register indirect addressing (@R0/@R1 with P2 as high byte, or @DPTR for 16-bit). *Example: `MOVX A, @DPTR`.*
3. **`MOVC`** → Accesses **program code memory (ROM)**. Used with indexed addressing only. *Example: `MOVC A, @A+DPTR`.* It is primarily for reading **lookup tables** stored in flash. **(3 marks)**

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**Q.A (a)** Explain the **5 addressing modes of 8051** with suitable examples. Mention the symbol used for each mode. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The 8051 microcontroller supports **5 addressing modes**, which define how the CPU locates the operand(s) for an instruction:

**1. Immediate Addressing** — Symbol: `#data`
   - The operand is a constant value embedded directly in the instruction.
   - `MOV A, #0FH` → Loads the literal value `0FH` into the Accumulator.
   - The `#` prefix tells the assembler "treat this as a constant, not an address."
   - Uses 2 program bytes; takes 1 machine cycle.

**2. Register Addressing** — Symbol: `R0`–`R7`, `A`, `B`, `DPTR`, `AB`
   - Operand is one of the internal CPU registers.
   - `ADD A, R5` → Adds contents of R5 to A; result in A.
   - Fastest mode — uses only 1 byte and 1 machine cycle.
   - R0–R7 are bank-dependent; current bank is selected by PSW bits RS0, RS1.

**3. Direct Addressing** — Symbol: `direct` (8-bit address, 00H–FFH)
   - Instruction contains the actual memory address of the operand.
   - `MOV 30H, A` → Copies A into internal RAM address 30H.
   - `SETB P1.0` → Sets bit 0 of Port 1 (SFR at 90H).
   - Covers 128 bytes internal RAM + 128 bytes SFR space.

**4. Register Indirect Addressing** — Symbol: `@R0`, `@R1`, `@DPTR`
   - A register holds the **address** of the data (the data itself is elsewhere).
   - `MOV A, @R0` → A ← contents of RAM address held in R0.
   - The `@` denotes indirection ("the data **at the location pointed to by** R0").
   - For `MOVX A, @DPTR`, the DPTR provides a 16-bit pointer to **external** RAM.

**5. Indexed Addressing** — Symbol: `@A+DPTR` or `@A+PC`
   - The final address is the sum of a base register (DPTR or PC) and the Accumulator offset.
   - `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` → A ← code memory at address `(DPTR + A)`.
   - Used for **lookup tables** — the Accumulator dynamically selects which entry to read.

> **Conclusion:** The five modes give the programmer a spectrum from **constant loading** (immediate) to **dynamic memory access** (register indirect) to **table-driven ROM access** (indexed), covering all embedded-control scenarios. **(7 marks)**

---

**Q.A (b)** Write an 8051 assembly program to add **two 8-bit numbers** stored in internal RAM locations `30H` and `31H`, store the result in `32H`, and also save the carry flag status in bit `20H.0`. Calculate the execution time for an 11.0592 MHz crystal. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

```assembly
        ORG  0000H          ; Reset vector
        MOV  A, 30H         ; A ← contents of RAM[30H]   (Direct, 2 bytes, 2 cycles)
        ADD  A, 31H         ; A ← A + RAM[31H]           (Direct, 2 bytes, 2 cycles)
        MOV  32H, A         ; RAM[32H] ← A               (Direct, 2 bytes, 2 cycles)
        MOV  00H, C         ; Copy Carry flag into bit 20H.0 (bit-addressable)
HERE:   SJMP HERE           ; Infinite loop / halt        (2 bytes, 2 cycles)
```

**Step-by-step walk-through:**
1. `MOV A, 30H` — Direct addressing reads byte from RAM `30H` → placed in `A`.
2. `ADD A, 31H` — Direct addressing reads byte from RAM `31H`, adds to `A`. If sum > `0xFF`, the **Carry flag (CY)** in PSW is set to 1.
3. `MOV 32H, A` — Direct addressing stores the 8-bit sum in RAM `32H`.
4. `MOV 00H, C` — Since bit address `00H` corresponds to the LSB of bit-addressable RAM at `20H`, this copies the Carry flag to **bit 20H.0**.

**Execution Time Calculation:**

- Total machine cycles = 2 + 2 + 2 + 2 (for SJMP) = **8 cycles** (note: `MOV 00H, C` is 2 bytes, 2 cycles)
- Total bytes of program memory = 2 + 2 + 2 + 2 + 2 = **10 bytes**

$$
T_{mc} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}} \approx 1.085\ \mu s
$$

$$
T_{exec} = 8 \times 1.085\ \mu s = 8.68\ \mu s
$$

> **Final Answer:** The program executes in **8.68 μs** on an 11.0592 MHz crystal. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**Q.B (a)** Explain the **Indexed Addressing Mode** in detail. How is it used to implement a **lookup table for 7-segment display codes** for digits 0–9? Provide the complete 8051 assembly code. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Indexed Addressing Mode** uses a base register (DPTR or PC) plus the Accumulator as an offset to compute the effective address in **code memory**.

$$A_{eff} = (DPTR) + (A)$$

This is the canonical mechanism for implementing **read-only lookup tables** in 8051 program ROM.

**Step-by-step Design for 7-Segment Lookup:**

| Digit (A) | Common-Cathode Code (Hex) |
|---|---|
| 0 | 3FH |
| 1 | 06H |
| 2 | 5BH |
| 3 | 4FH |
| 4 | 66H |
| 5 | 6DH |
| 6 | 7DH |
| 7 | 07H |
| 8 | 7FH |
| 9 | 6FH |

**Complete 8051 Assembly Code:**
```assembly
        ORG     0000H
        MOV     DPTR, #SEG_TABLE  ; DPTR points to base of LUT
        MOV     A, #05H           ; Suppose digit '5' is required
        MOVC    A, @A+DPTR        ; A ← code memory[DPTR + 5] = 6DH
        MOV     P1, A             ; Output code to Port 1 (connected to display)
HERE:   SJMP    HERE

SEG_TABLE:
        DB      3FH, 06H, 5BH, 4FH, 66H   ; Codes for 0,1,2,3,4
        DB      6DH, 7DH, 07H, 7FH, 6FH   ; Codes for 5,6,7,8,9
        END
```

**Execution Walk-through:**
1. `MOV DPTR, #SEG_TABLE` — Direct addressing, DPTR = 16-bit address of `SEG_TABLE`.
2. `MOV A, #05H` — Immediate, `A = 05H`.
3. `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` — CPU computes `A_eff = DPTR + 05H`, fetches byte at that ROM address, places it in `A`.
4. The byte retrieved is `6DH`, the standard code for displaying "5."
5. `MOV P1, A` — Direct addressing, sends the code to Port 1 (hardware drives the 7-segment).

**Why Indexed Mode is Mandatory Here:**
- The digit value (0–9) is **dynamic** (changes at runtime) — Immediate mode cannot serve.
- The 7-segment codes are **read-only constants** — they must reside in code memory (ROM), which only `MOVC` can access.
- Indexed mode elegantly bridges the dynamic `A` with the static table base `DPTR`. **(7 marks)**

---

**Q.B (b)** Compare and contrast the **Register, Direct, and Register Indirect** addressing modes. For each, give one engineering scenario where it is the most appropriate choice. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | Register | Direct | Register Indirect |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Operand symbol** | `Rn`, `A`, `B`, `DPTR` | `00H–FFH` (8-bit address) | `@R0`, `@R1`, `@DPTR` |
| **Bytes used** | 1 | 2 or 3 | 1 or 2 |
| **Cycles** | 1 | 1 or 2 | 1 or 2 |
| **Speed** | Fastest | Moderate | Moderate |
| **Address range** | 8 internal registers | 256 internal locations | 64 KB (with DPTR for external) |
| **Pointer required?** | No | No | Yes (R0, R1, or DPTR) |

**Engineering Scenarios:**

1. **Register Addressing — High-speed inner loop:**
   In a real-time PID controller inner loop, the Kp, Ki, Kd coefficients are first loaded into R0–R5, then the loop body operates purely on registers using `ADD A, R0` etc. This minimizes cycle overhead and maximizes control-loop bandwidth. *Example: `MOV A, R3`.*

2. **Direct Addressing — Accessing SFRs by symbolic name:**
   The compiler/assembler resolves symbolic names like `P1`, `TMOD`, `IE`, `TCON` to their SFR addresses (90H, 89H, A8H, 88H). Direct addressing is essential for configuring peripherals. *Example: `MOV TMOD, #01H`.*

3. **Register Indirect — Array traversal with a pointer:**
   When processing a buffer of 100 sensor samples stored in external RAM starting at address `0000H`, the program initializes DPTR to `0000H` and uses `MOVX A, @DPTR` in a loop, incrementing DPTR each iteration. This is impossible with direct addressing (would require 100 separate instructions). *Example: `MOVX A, @DPTR` followed by `INC DPTR`.*

> **Conclusion:** Register mode optimizes for **speed**, Direct mode for **symbolic clarity and SFR access**, and Register Indirect mode for **pointer-based data structures** (arrays, stacks, external memory traversal). **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the `#` symbol** in Immediate mode (`MOV A, 25H` ≠ `MOV A, #25H`). Costs **2 marks** routinely.
> 2. **Confusing `MOV` with `MOVX`/`MOVC`.** Writing `MOV A, @DPTR` to access external RAM is a **compilation error** in real 8051 — must use `MOVX A, @DPTR`.
> 3. **Calculating machine cycles incorrectly** — forgetting to multiply by 12 for the 8051 (students often use the 8085 rule of `T = 1/f`).
> 4. **Omitting addressing-mode classification tables** — losing **2–3 marks** on sub-part (a) of 7-mark questions.
> 5. **Writing `MOVX` for code-memory access** — must always be `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` for ROM lookups.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ The 8051 instruction set has **111 base instructions → 255 opcodes**, classified into **5 functional groups**: Data Transfer, Arithmetic, Logical, Boolean, and Branching.
- ✅ There are **5 addressing modes**: Immediate (`#`), Register, Direct, Register Indirect (`@`), and Indexed (`@A+DPTR` / `@A+PC`).
- ✅ The `#` symbol is the **assembler prefix for immediate constants** — its absence changes the meaning entirely.
- ✅ Only **R0 and R1** (and DPTR for 16-bit) can be used for register indirect addressing — not R2–R7.
- ✅ `MOVX` = e**X**ternal RAM; `MOVC` = **C**ode memory; `MOV` = internal RAM/SFR. Mnemonic memory aid.
- ✅ Indexed addressing is the **only** way to read data from program ROM at runtime (lookup tables).
- ✅ Machine cycle period $T_{mc} = 12 / f_{osc}$. For 11.0592 MHz → **1.085 μs**; for 12 MHz → **1 μs**.
- ✅ The 8051 has **4 register banks** (00–1FH), switchable via PSW bits RS0 and RS1 — a key real-time feature.
- ✅ Bit-addressable RAM is at **20H–2FH** (128 individually addressable bits).
- ✅ The Stack Pointer default is **07H**; growing **upward**. Always initialize with `MOV SP, #XXH` for nested subroutines.
- ✅ The DPTR is a 16-bit register pair (DPH + DPL) — the only 16-bit data pointer in the 8051.
- ✅ Boolean instructions operate on the **bit-addressable space** (RAM 20–2FH + 16 bit-addressable SFRs).
- ✅ Maximum program memory = **64 KB** (16-bit PC), maximum external RAM = **64 KB** (16-bit DPTR).
- ✅ For KTU exam: **always mention both the mode name AND the effective memory location** in answers for full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Programming 8051 Timers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCIT404 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Programming 8051 Timers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **8051 Timer/Counter** is an on-chip hardware peripheral integrated into the Intel MCS-51 microcontroller architecture. It consists of two **16-bit registers** (Timer 0 and Timer 1) that can be programmed to function either as:
- A **Timer** — counting internal machine cycles (oscillator ÷ 12) to generate precise time delays.
- A **Counter** — counting external events fed through the corresponding pin (T0 for P3.4, T1 for P3.5).

The relevant SFRs (Special Function Registers) used to program the timers are **TMOD** (Timer Mode register) and **TCON** (Timer Control register), along with the high/low byte register pairs **TH0/TL0** and **TH1/TL1**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 mandates a deep understanding of Timer programming in all four modes (Mode 0, 1, 2, 3) and the ability to compute delay values analytically. Questions on delay generation using Timer Mode 1 and Mode 2 are KTU-favorite ESE topics.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Stopwatch" Intuition

Imagine you are baking a cake and need an exact 5-second whisking interval.

- **The Stopwatch (Hardware Timer):** The 8051's internal 16-bit register is like a stopwatch that ticks once every microsecond (depending on crystal). It increments from 0 → 65535 and then **rolls over** to 0, raising a flag.
- **Pre-setting the Stopwatch (Initial Value Loading):** Instead of counting *up* to 65535, you *pre-load* a starting value, so the stopwatch only needs to count a smaller number of ticks before flagging completion. **Less ticks = shorter delay**.
- **The Start Button (TR0/TR1 bit):** Pressing this bit starts the stopwatch. Setting it to 0 stops it.
- **The Buzzer (TF0/TF1 flag):** When the register overflows (rolls over), the buzzer rings — this is the **overflow flag**, which the CPU can poll.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why use a Timer instead of a software delay loop?**
> A software delay loop wastes CPU cycles (the CPU cannot do anything else). A hardware Timer runs **in the background**, freeing the CPU to perform other tasks. The CPU is only interrupted when the timer overflows.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Value / Standard |
|---|---|
| Number of Timers in 8051 | **2** (Timer 0 & Timer 1) |
| Resolution of each Timer | **16-bit** (0 to 65535) |
| Timer Pins | **T0 = P3.4**, **T1 = P3.5** |
| Control Register | **TCON** (88H) |
| Mode Register | **TMOD** (89H) |
| Standard Crystal Frequency | **11.0592 MHz** (for serial communication) / **12 MHz** (commonly assumed) |
| Machine Cycle Frequency | **Oscillator Frequency ÷ 12** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The TMOD Register (Timer Mode Register — Address 89H)

TMOD is an **8-bit, non-bit-addressable** register. Its upper nibble (bits 4–7) configures **Timer 1**; its lower nibble (bits 0–3) configures **Timer 0**.

| Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Name | GATE | C/T̄ | M1 | M0 | GATE | C/T̄ | M1 | M0 |
| Timer | T1 ← | | | | → T0 → | | | |

- **GATE**: When set, timer is controlled by hardware (INTx pin AND TRx bit). When cleared, software control via TRx only.
- **C/T̄**: **1 = Counter mode** (external pin pulses); **0 = Timer mode** (internal oscillator).
- **M1, M0**: Mode selection bits.

### 2.1.1 Timer Modes (M1, M0)

| M1 | M0 | Mode | Description | Width |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | Mode 0 | 13-bit Timer | 13-bit (8048 legacy) |
| 0 | 1 | Mode 1 | 16-bit Timer | 16-bit |
| 1 | 0 | Mode 2 | 8-bit Auto-Reload | 8-bit |
| 1 | 1 | Mode 3 | Split Timer | 8-bit + 8-bit (Timer 0 only) |

## 2.2 The TCON Register (Timer Control Register — Address 88H)

TCON is **bit-addressable**, making it easy to manipulate individual flags in Assembly/C.

| Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Name | TF1 | TR1 | TF0 | TR0 | IE1 | IT1 | IE0 | IT0 |

- **TR0 / TR1** (Timer Run): **1 = Start timer**, 0 = Stop timer.
- **TF0 / TF1** (Timer Flag): Set by hardware when the timer **overflows**; cleared by software.

## 2.3 Mode 1 — 16-Bit Timer (Most Used for Delays)

In Mode 1, the timer uses both THx and TLx as one combined 16-bit register that increments from the loaded value up to **65535 (FFFFH)** and then **rolls over to 0000H**, setting the **TFx** flag.

### 2.3.1 How Delay Generation Works — Step-by-Step Logic

1. Load **TMOD** with the value selecting Mode 1 for the chosen timer.
2. Load the high byte of the desired initial value into **THx** and the low byte into **TLx**.
3. Set the **TRx** bit in TCON to **1** to start the timer.
4. Keep polling the **TFx** bit; when it becomes 1, the desired delay has elapsed.
5. Clear **TRx = 0** and **TFx = 0** for the next cycle.
6. Reload THx/TLx if required (Mode 1 does NOT auto-reload).

## 2.4 Mode 2 — 8-Bit Auto-Reload (Most Used for Serial Port)

In Mode 2, **TLx** counts from the loaded value up to **255 (FFH)** and then **automatically reloads** the value stored in **THx**, making it perfect for **baud-rate generation** in serial communication and for repetitive, fixed-time intervals.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they are guaranteed to appear in KTU delay-calculation problems.**

| Parameter | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|
| Machine Cycle Period (Tmc) | $T_{mc} = \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}}$ | seconds |
| Timer Tick Duration | **1 Machine Cycle** | $\mu$s (if f_osc = 12 MHz) |
| Delay (Mode 1) | $Delay = (65536 - X) \times T_{mc}$ | seconds |
| Initial Timer Value (Mode 1) | $X = 65536 - \dfrac{Delay}{T_{mc}}$ | decimal count |
| Delay (Mode 2) | $Delay = (256 - X) \times T_{mc}$ | seconds |
| Initial Timer Value (Mode 2) | $X = 256 - \dfrac{Delay}{T_{mc}}$ | decimal count |
| Timer 0 Range (Mode 1) | **0000H to FFFFH** (1 to 65536 ticks) | ticks |
| Timer 0 Range (Mode 2) | **00H to FFH** (1 to 256 ticks) | ticks |
| Hex Conversion | $X_{hex} = $ HEX of $X_{decimal}$ | — |
| THx value | $THx = \text{Upper Byte of } X_{hex}$ | hex |
| TLx value | $TLx = \text{Lower Byte of } X_{hex}$ | hex |

### 2.5.1 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Traffic Light Controllers** — precise red/green timing using Mode 1.
- **PWM Generation** — modulating duty cycles for motor speed control.
- **Baud Rate Generator** — Mode 2 with Timer 1 sets UART communication speed.
- **Industrial Process Timers** — controlling relay ON/OFF durations.
- **Digital Watches / Clocks** — building real-time clock ICs (e.g., DS1307, DS3231) often use 8051 Timers as the tick source.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Delay Formula for Mode 1

The 8051's internal oscillator drives the timer through a divide-by-12 prescaler. Hence:

$$
\text{Timer increments once every} \; \frac{12}{f_{osc}} \; \text{seconds.}
$$

If the timer starts at value $X$ and counts up to overflow ($65536$ total states in Mode 1), the total ticks elapsed are:

$$
N_{ticks} = 65536 - X
$$

The total delay is therefore the number of ticks multiplied by the duration of each tick:

$$
Delay = (65536 - X) \times \frac{12}{f_{osc}}
$$

Rearranging to solve for the required initial value $X$:

$$
X = 65536 - \left( \frac{Delay \times f_{osc}}{12} \right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **For f_osc = 11.0592 MHz:** $T_{mc} = 12 / 11.0592 \times 10^6 \approx 1.085 \, \mu s$
> **For f_osc = 12 MHz:** $T_{mc} = 12 / 12 \times 10^6 = 1.000 \, \mu s$ *(cleanest for KTU problems)*

## 3.2 Worked Example #1 — Generating a 50 ms Delay (Mode 1, 12 MHz Crystal)

**Given:** Delay = 50 ms, Crystal = 12 MHz, Timer 0, Mode 1.

**Step 1 — Compute Machine Cycle Period:**

$$
T_{mc} = \frac{12}{12 \times 10^6} = 1 \, \mu s
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Number of Ticks Required:**

$$
N_{ticks} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{1 \times 10^{-6}} = 50000 \; \text{ticks}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Initial Timer Value:**

$$
X = 65536 - 50000 = 15536
$$

**Step 4 — Convert 15536 to Hexadecimal:**

$$
15536 \div 16 = 971 \text{ remainder } 0
$$
$$
971 \div 16 = 60 \text{ remainder } 11 \; (B)
$$
$$
60 \div 16 = 3 \text{ remainder } 12 \; (C)
$$
$$
3 \div 16 = 0 \text{ remainder } 3
$$

Reading remainders bottom-up: $X_{hex} = 3CB0H$

**Step 5 — Extract TH0 and TL0:**

$$
TH0 = 3CH \quad ; \quad TL0 = B0H
$$

**Step 6 — TMOD Value for Timer 0, Mode 1, Software Control, Timer Mode:**

$$
TMOD = 00000001_2 = 01H
$$

**Resulting Assembly Code (8051):**

```assembly
        MOV  TMOD, #01H    ; Timer 0, Mode 1
```
