Mastering this logic is essential for understanding WHY the 8086 can read/write both 8-bit and 16-bit data efficiently. # 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP --- ## 5.1 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: 8086 Memory Banks **`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** **Q: Explain the concept of Even and Odd memory banks in 8086 with the help of the BHE' and A0 signals.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The 8086 has a 16-bit data bus, but the 1 MB memory space is divided into two **512 KB banks** to allow flexible 8-bit and 16-bit access: 1. **Even Bank (Low Bank):** Connected to data lines **D0–D7**. It contains all memory locations with **even addresses** (A0 = 0). It is selected when A0 = 0. 2. **Odd Bank (High Bank):** Connected to data lines **D8–D15**. It contains all memory locations with **odd addresses**. It is selected when **BHE' = 0** (BHE = Bus High Enable, active low). For a **16-bit word read** at an even address, both A0 and BHE' are LOW, so both banks are activated simultaneously, completing the operation in one memory cycle. For a word at an odd address, the operation requires two memory cycles. > [!NOTE] > **Mark Distribution:** Definition of banks (1 mark), explanation of BHE' and A0 (1 mark), 16-bit operation behavior (1 mark). --- ### Question 2: Stepper Motor Step Angle **`[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`** **Q: Define step angle. A stepper motor has 50 rotor teeth and operates in 2-phase mode. Calculate the step angle and the number of steps per revolution.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **step angle (β)** is the minimum angular displacement of the rotor per excitation pulse. $$\beta = \frac{360°}{N \cdot m} = \frac{360°}{50 \times 2} = \frac{360°}{100} = 3.6°$$ Number of steps per revolution: $$S = \frac{360°}{\beta} = \frac{360°}{3.6°} = 100 \text{ steps/rev}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Mark Distribution:** Definition of step angle (1 mark), formula and substitution (1 mark), final answer with steps/rev (1 mark). --- ## 5.2 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]`** **Q: (a)** Interface two 16 KB ROM chips and two 32 KB RAM chips to an 8086 system. The starting addresses are ROM1 = `F0000H`, ROM2 = `E8000H`, RAM1 = `00000H`, and RAM2 = `08000H`. Draw the memory map and explain the address decoding using a 74LS138. **(7 marks)** **Q: (b)** With a neat diagram, explain the interfacing of a 4-phase stepper motor with 8086. Write an 8086 ALP to rotate the stepper motor continuously in the clockwise direction. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for (a): 7 marks** **Step 1: Memory Map** (1 mark) | Chip | Start Address | End Address | Size | |------|---------------|-------------|------| | RAM1 | `00000H` | `07FFFH` | 32 KB | | RAM2 | `08000H` | `0FFFFH` | 32 KB | | ROM2 | `E8000H` | `EBFFFH` | 16 KB | | ROM1 | `F0000H` | `F3FFFH` | 16 KB | **Step 2: Address Line Requirements** (1 mark) - 16 KB → $2^{14}$ → needs A1–A14 - 32 KB → $2^{15}$ → needs A1–A15 - Remaining lines A15–A19 (5 bits) are used for decoding. **Step 3: 74LS138 Connections** (2 marks) The 74LS138's CBA inputs are connected to **A17, A16, A15**. G1 is tied to **M/IO' = 1** (memory operation), and G2A' is tied to RD' (only activate during read). | Chip | A19 A18 | A17 A16 A15 | CBA | Output | Extra Condition | |------|---------|-------------|-----|--------|-----------------| | RAM1 | 0 0 | 0 0 0 | 0 0 0 | Y0' | — | | RAM2 | 0 0 | 0 0 1 | 0 0 1 | Y1' | — | | ROM2 | 1 1 | 1 0 1 | 1 0 1 | Y5' | AND with NOT A15 | | ROM1 | 1 1 | 1 1 0 | 1 1 0 | Y6' | AND with NOT A15 | **Step 4: Justification** (1 mark) - The 74LS138 has 8 outputs; we use Y0', Y1', Y5', Y6' for our 4 chips. - A19 and A18 are used as additional gating signals to differentiate ROM (top 256 KB) from RAM (bottom 256 KB). **Step 5: Final Interfacing Diagram** (2 marks) (A neat diagram showing 8086 → Latch → Decoder → 4 memory chips with appropriate A0/BHE' connections to D0–D7 and D8–D15 lines. The data bus connections to upper and lower bytes of each chip should be clearly shown.) --- #### **Model Solution for (b): 7 marks** **Step 1: Block Diagram of Interface** (2 marks) ``` 8086 Data Bus (D0–D3) → 8255 Port A (or simple latch 74LS373) 8255 Port A → ULN2003 Driver → Stepper Motor Coils (A, B, C, D) ``` **Step 2: Explanation of Driver IC** (1 mark) The ULN2003 is a **Darlington transistor array** that provides high current drive (~500 mA per channel) to the motor coils. The 8086 port cannot supply this current directly, so the driver is essential. **Step 3: Program Logic** (2 marks) ``` ASSUME CS:CODE CODE SEGMENT START: MOV AL, 0BH ; Step 1: AB on (1011) MOV DX, PORT_ADDR ; Port address AGAIN: OUT DX, AL ; Send pattern to motor CALL DELAY ; Wait for step duration ROL AL, 1 ; Rotate left for next step JMP AGAIN ; Loop forever (continuous rotation) ; The sequence 0BH, 07H, 05H, 0DH repeats DELAY: ; Software delay subroutine MOV CX, COUNT DEL1: LOOP DEL1 RET CODE ENDS END START ``` **Step 4: Direction Control Note** (1 mark) For **clockwise rotation**, the sequence is `0BH → 07H → 05H → 0DH → 0BH ...` (two-phase-on excitation, stepping through phases AB → BC → CD → DA). For **counter-clockwise**, the sequence is reversed: `0BH → 0DH → 05H → 07H → 0BH ...` (using ROR instead of ROL). **Step 5: Speed Control** (1 mark) Speed is controlled by the **DELAY subroutine's count value**. Higher count = slower rotation. The count is calculated from the desired RPM and step angle as derived in Section 3.2. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **`[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question Paper]`** **Q: (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the address decoding scheme used in 8086-based systems. Differentiate between absolute and linear decoding. What is the "image problem" in linear decoding? **(7 marks)** **Q: (b)** A stepper motor has 200 steps per revolution. It is driven by an 8086 system running at 5 MHz clock frequency. Calculate the delay count required to rotate the motor at **(i) 10 RPM** and **(ii) 50 RPM**. Write the 8086 program structure for variable speed control. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for (a): 7 marks** **Step 1: Address Decoding Concept** (1 mark) Address decoding is the process of generating **chip select (CS')** signals for memory and I/O devices using the upper address bits of the 8086. **Step 2: Block Diagram** (2 marks) ``` 8086 Address Lines A0–A19 │ ├── A0–A13 (or A0–A14) → Directly to memory chip │ └── A14–A19 (or A15–A19) → 74LS138 Decoder │ ├── Y0' → Chip 1 CS' ├── Y1' → Chip 2 CS' ├── Y2' → Chip 3 CS' └── ... etc ``` **Step 3: Absolute Decoding** (1 mark) - ALL address bits are used in decoding. - Each memory location has a **unique address** — no overlap. - Disadvantage: Requires more hardware (multiple gates or decoder ICs). **Step 4: Linear Decoding** (1 mark) - Some upper address bits are **ignored** (treated as "don't care"). - A single NAND/AND gate often suffices for chip selection. - Disadvantage: The same physical memory location can be accessed via **multiple addresses** — the "image problem." **Step 5: Image Problem Explanation** (2 marks) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Image Problem (2 marks):** > > Suppose a 16 KB RAM is connected at the address range `00000H–03FFFH` using **linear decoding** where A19 is ignored. The chip select is generated only from A15. Then the same RAM will also appear at: > > - `80000H–83FFFH` (when A19 = 1, A15 = 0) > - `40000H–43FFFH` (when A19 = 0, A15 = 0)... and so on. > > In fact, the same RAM appears at **64 different locations** in the 1 MB space (because 6 address lines are floating). This wastes address space and can cause **software bugs** because the program might inadvertently access the "image" instead of the intended memory. Absolute decoding eliminates this problem. --- #### **Model Solution for (b): 7 marks** **Step 1: Given Data** (0.5 mark) - Steps per revolution $S = 200$ - Clock frequency = 5 MHz → T-state = 200 ns **Step 2: Case (i) — 10 RPM** (1.5 marks) Pulse frequency: $$f_{\text{pulse}} = \frac{N_{\text{rpm}} \cdot S}{60} = \frac{10 \times 200}{60} = 33.33 \text{ Hz}$$ Time delay: $$T_{\text{delay}} = \frac{1}{33.33} = 30 \text{ ms}$$ Number of T-states: $$N_T = \frac{30 \times 10^{-3}}{200 \times 10^{-9}} = 150{,}000 \text{ T-states}$$ Delay count (using a loop with 17 T-states per iteration including overhead): $$n = \frac{150{,}000 - 8}{17} \approx 8823 = 2277\text{H}$$ **Step 3: Case (ii) — 50 RPM** (1.5 marks) $$f_{\text{pulse}} = \frac{50 \times 200}{60} = 166.67 \text{ Hz}$$ $$T_{\text{delay}} = \frac{1}{166.67} = 6 \text{ ms}$$ $$N_T = \frac{6 \times 10^{-3}}{200 \times 10^{-9}} = 30{,}000 \text{ T-states}$$ $$n = \frac{30{,}000 - 8}{17} \approx 1764 = 6\text{E}4\text{H}$$ **Step 4: Program for Variable Speed Control** (3.5 marks) ```assembly ASSUME CS:CODE CODE SEGMENT START: MOV BX, SPEED_TABLE ; Point to speed lookup table MOV CL, COUNT_TOTAL ; Number of steps to rotate MOV DX, MOTOR_PORT ; Port address MAIN_LOOP: MOV AL, [BX] ; Load speed delay from table MOV SI, AX ; Save delay count ; Get next step pattern (assume sequential or indexed) MOV AL, [STEP_TABLE + OFFSET] OUT DX, AL ; Send to motor port ; Variable delay based on SI CALL VARIABLE_DELAY ; Update BX to next speed (for variable speed demo) INC BX DEC CL JNZ MAIN_LOOP HLT VARIABLE_DELAY: PUSH CX MOV CX, SI ; Load delay count VD_LOOP: NOP LOOP VD_LOOP POP CX RET STEP_TABLE: DB 0BH, 07H, 05H, 0DH ; Two-phase-on sequence SPEED_TABLE: DW 2277H, 1000H, 0700H, 0500H ; Different delays for varying RPM CODE ENDS END START ``` > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Don't forget to latched address lines** when drawing the 8086 memory interface. The 74LS373 latch and the ALE signal are **NOT optional** — drawing them shows understanding of the multiplexed bus (often 1 mark lost). > 2. **Forgetting the driver IC** between 8086 and stepper motor. Direct connection is **wrong** and shows lack of practical knowledge (-2 marks). > 3. **Direction reversal logic:** Students often forget to reverse the sequence table. CW and CCW are not the same code with a sign change — you must use ROR instead of ROL, or read the table backwards. > 4. **Decoding tables:** A19 and A18 are often ignored in exam diagrams, leading to an "image problem" by default. Always justify why you are ignoring (or using) each line. > 5. **Software delay calculation:** Many students forget the **loop overhead** (the JNZ instruction itself takes 16 T-states when taken). A delay of "n iterations × n T-states" without accounting for JNZ/LOOP is a common -1 mark error. --- ## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Section Before Exam!** ### 📌 8086 External Memory Interface - [ ] 8086 has a **20-bit address bus** → **1 MB** memory space (`00000H` to `FFFFFH`). - [ ] Memory split into **two 512 KB banks** (Even & Odd). - [ ] **A0** selects the Even Bank (D0–D7); **BHE'** selects the Odd Bank (D8–D15). - [ ] Aligned word access (BHE'=0, A0=0) is **fastest** — one memory cycle. - [ ] Misaligned word access (BHE'=0, A0=1) needs **two memory cycles** — performance penalty. - [ ] Multiplexed bus → **74LS373 latch** controlled by **ALE** is mandatory. - [ ] **74LS138** is the standard 3-to-8 decoder for address decoding. - [ ] **Absolute decoding** = unique addresses, more hardware, no image problem. - [ ] **Linear decoding** = less hardware, but **image problem** exists. - [ ] **Reset vector** = physical address **`FFFF0H`** (CS:IP = FFFF:0000H). - [ ] ROM is placed at the **top** of memory (around F0000H–FFFFFH). - [ ] RAM is placed at the **bottom** (starting at 00000H). ### 📌 Stepper Motor - [ ] Stepper motor is a **pulse-driven, open-loop** positioning device. - [ ] **Step angle** $\beta = 360° / (N \cdot m)$. - [ ] Standard hybrid stepper: **200 steps/rev** → **β = 1.8°**. - [ ] Half-step mode → **400 steps/rev** → smoother motion. - [ ] Three excitation modes: **Wave, Full-Step (Two-Phase-On), Half-Step**. - [ ] **Two-Phase-On** has higher torque than Wave excitation. - [ ] **Direction** = forward/reverse sequence of the 4-phase pattern. - [ ] **Speed** = controlled by delay between steps; calculated as $f = \text{RPM} \times S / 60$. - [ ] **Driver IC** (ULN2003/L293D) is **mandatory** between 8086 port and motor. - [ ] Stepper motor coils are **inductive loads** — flyback diodes in ULN2003 handle back-EMF. ### 📌 Key Numerical Conversions to Memorize | Quantity | Value | |----------|-------| | 1 MB | $2^{20}$ = 1,048,576 bytes | | 8086 Banks | $2 \times 2^{19}$ = $2 \times$ 512 KB | | 64 KB | $2^{16}$ bytes (16 address lines) | | 16 KB | $2^{14}$ bytes (14 address lines) | | 8 KB | $2^{13}$ bytes (13 address lines) | | 4 KB | $2^{12}$ bytes (12 address lines) | | 200 steps/rev | β = 1.8° (standard hybrid) | | 5 MHz clock | T = 200 ns | | 8086 reset vector | `FFFF0H` | ### 📌 High-Yield Exam Triggers - "Interface X KB of RAM/ROM to 8086 starting at address..." → **Calculate end address, design decoder, draw diagram.** - "Calculate delay for stepper motor at X RPM..." → **Use $T = 60 / (\text{RPM} \times S)$ then convert to T-states.** - "Explain bank selection in 8086..." → **Draw the truth table of BHE' and A0.** - "Differentiate absolute and linear decoding..." → **Mention image problem.** - "Write 8086 ALP to rotate stepper motor..." → **Include direction logic and delay loop.**

topics.title: ARM Processor Fundamentals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCIT404 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** ARM Processor Fundamentals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ARM (Advanced RISC Machine)** is a family of **32-bit Reduced Instruction Set Computer (RISC)** processor architectures developed by **ARM Holdings (Cambridge, UK)**. It is widely used in embedded systems, mobile devices, IoT, and real-time applications due to its **low power consumption**, **high performance per watt**, and **load-store architecture**.

The **ARM architecture** is based on the **RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computing)** design philosophy, which contrasts sharply with the **CISC (Complex Instruction Set Computing)** approach used by traditional x86 processors. The architecture defines a set of **registers**, **instruction set**, **execution model**, and **memory model** that licensees (e.g., Qualcomm, Samsung, Apple) implement in their System-on-Chip (SoC) designs.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Swiss Army Knife vs. The Specialist Chef's Knife"**
> 
> Imagine a **CISC processor** (like Intel x86) as a **Swiss Army Knife** — it has hundreds of specialized tools (complex instructions), each capable of doing a sophisticated job in one stroke, but it is heavy, power-hungry, and complex.
> 
> Now think of an **ARM processor** as a **Specialist Chef's Knife** — it has only a few, extremely sharp, highly optimized tools (simple instructions). Each does less per instruction, but it executes them **blazingly fast**, consumes **minimal power**, and the processor can be made incredibly small and efficient.
> 
> This is why your **smartphone** (ARM) lasts all day on a battery, while a laptop (x86 CISC) needs a fan and a big battery!

### 1.3 Key Characteristics of ARM Architecture

- **Load/Store Architecture**: Data must be loaded from memory into registers before any operation; results are stored back via explicit store instructions.
- **Fixed Instruction Length**: Most ARM instructions are exactly **32 bits** wide (Thumb instructions are **16 bits**).
- **Large Register File**: **37 physical registers** (31 general-purpose + 6 status registers) visible as **16 user-mode registers** through banking.
- **Conditional Execution**: Almost every instruction can be conditionally executed based on condition flags (unique to ARM).
- **Low Power Consumption**: Designed from the ground up for power efficiency.
- **Orthogonal Design**: Any register can be used for any operation (with few exceptions).
- **Orthogonal Barrel Shifter**: The second operand of every data-processing instruction can be shifted/rotated for free.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Instruction Width**: **32 bits** (ARM mode) / **16 bits** (Thumb mode)
- **Register Width**: **32 bits**
- **Address Bus Width**: **32 bits** → 4 GB addressable memory space
- **Data Bus Width**: **32 bits**
- **Pipeline Stages**: 3-stage (classic ARM7) → 5-stage (ARM9) → 13-stage (Cortex-A15)
- **Operating Voltage**: Typically **1.8 V – 3.3 V**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 ARM Architecture vs. CISC Architecture

| Feature | ARM (RISC) | CISC (e.g., x86) |
|---|---|---|
| **Instruction Set Size** | Small (~50–100) | Large (>500) |
| **Instruction Length** | Fixed (32-bit) | Variable (1–15 bytes) |
| **Execution Cycles** | Mostly 1 cycle/cycle | Multiple cycles/instruction |
| **Memory Access** | Load/Store only | Memory-to-memory operations |
| **Decoding** | Hardwired, fast | Microcoded, complex |
| **Pipelining** | Highly efficient | Difficult due to variable length |
| **Power Consumption** | Very low | High |
| **Transistor Count** | Lower | Higher |
| **Code Density** | Lower (fixed length) | Higher (variable length) |
| **Example Use** | Smartphones, IoT | Desktops, servers |

### 2.2 ARM Operating Modes

ARM processors operate in **7 different modes** (in classic ARM architecture). The mode determines which registers are visible and what privileges the processor has.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 7 ARM Operating Modes:**
> 
> | Mode | Abbrev | Privilege | Description |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | **User** | usr | Unprivileged | Normal user code execution |
> | **FIQ** | fiq | Privileged | Fast Interrupt handling |
> | **IRQ** | irq | Privileged | General Interrupt handling |
> | **Supervisor** | svc | Privileged | OS Kernel / SWI calls |
> | **Abort** | abt | Privileged | Memory access violations |
> | **Undefined** | und | Privileged | Undefined instruction trap |
> | **System** | sys | Privileged | Privileged user mode (uses User registers) |

### 2.3 ARM Register Set (37 Registers Total)

ARM has **37 physical registers** arranged in banks. The user sees only **16 general-purpose registers (R0–R15)** plus the **CPSR**. Other modes bank additional registers.

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Visible Registers in User Mode:                    │
│  R0, R1, R2, R3, R4, R5, R6, R7, R8, R9, R10,      │
│  R11, R12, R13 (SP), R14 (LR), R15 (PC)            │
│  + CPSR (Current Program Status Register)           │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Special-Purpose Registers in User Mode:**
> - **R13 — Stack Pointer (SP)**: Points to top of the active stack.
> - **R14 — Link Register (LR)**: Stores return address when a subroutine is called via `BL`.
> - **R15 — Program Counter (PC)**: Always holds address of current instruction + 8 (in ARM state) due to pipeline.

### 2.4 Banked Registers (for Privileged Modes)

In privileged modes, the higher-numbered registers (R8–R14) and SPSR (Saved Program Status Register) are **banked** — meaning each mode has its own physical copy.

| Mode | R13 (SP) | R14 (LR) | R8–R12 | SPSR |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| User | R13 | R14 | R8–R12 | — |
| FIQ | R13_fiq | R14_fiq | R8_fiq–R14_fiq | SPSR_fiq |
| IRQ | R13_irq | R14_irq | — | SPSR_irq |
| Supervisor | R13_svc | R14_svc | — | SPSR_svc |
| Abort | R13_abt | R14_abt | — | SPSR_abt |
| Undefined | R13_und | R14_und | — | SPSR_und |
| System | R13 | R14 | R8–R12 | — |

### 2.5 Program Status Register (CPSR / SPSR)

The **CPSR (Current Program Status Register)** is a 32-bit register with the following structure:

```
Bits:  31 30 29 28  27 24  23 16   15 8   7 6 5 4 0
       N  Z  C  V   Reserved  I F T   Reserved   Mode
       └── Condition Flags ─┘   └─Ctrl─┘           └─Mode─┘
```

**Condition Flags (bits 31–28):**
- **N (Negative)** = bit 31: Set if result is negative (MSB = 1)
- **Z (Zero)** = bit 30: Set if result is zero
- **C (Carry)** = bit 29: Set if unsigned overflow (carry out)
- **V (Overflow)** = bit 28: Set if signed overflow

**Control Bits:**
- **I (bit 7)**: IRQ disable (1 = disabled)
- **F (bit 6)**: FIQ disable (1 = disabled)
- **T (bit 5)**: Thumb state (0 = ARM, 1 = Thumb)

**Mode Bits (bits 4–0):**
- `0b10000` (0x10) = User
- `0b10001` (0x11) = FIQ
- `0b10010` (0x12) = IRQ
- `0b10011` (0x13) = Supervisor
- `0b10111` (0x17) = Abort
- `0b11011` (0x1B) = Undefined
- `0b11111` (0x1F) = System

### 2.6 ARM Instruction Set Categories

| Category | Examples | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Processing** | `ADD`, `SUB`, `AND`, `ORR`, `EOR` | ALU operations on registers |
| **Data Transfer** | `LDR`, `STR`, `LDM`, `STM` | Load/Store from memory |
| **Branch** | `B`, `BL`, `BX` | Conditional/unconditional jumps |
| **Multiply** | `MUL`, `MLA` | Integer multiplication |
| **Status Register** | `MRS`, `MSR` | Read/Write CPSR/SPSR |
| **Coprocessor** | `CDP`, `LDC`, `STC`, `MCR`, `MRC` | Interface to coprocessors |
| **Exception** | `SWI` (SVC) | Software interrupt |

### 2.7 ARM Pipeline (Classic 3-Stage)

$$T_{total} = N \times C + (S - 1)$$

Where:
- $N$ = number of instructions
- $C$ = cycles per instruction (≈ 1 for ARM)
- $S$ = number of pipeline stages

For the classic **ARM 3-stage pipeline**:

$$T_{pipeline} = N + 2 \text{ cycles}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The 3 Pipeline Stages:**
> 1. **Fetch (F)**: Instruction fetched from memory at PC address.
> 2. **Decode (D)**: Instruction decoded, operands read from register file.
> 3. **Execute (E)**: ALU operation, memory access, write-back to register.

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

ARM processors are the backbone of:
- **Mobile Computing**: Smartphones (Snapdragon, Apple A-series, Exynos)
- **Embedded Systems**: Automotive ECUs, industrial controllers
- **IoT Devices**: Smart sensors, wearables (Cortex-M series)
- **Single-Board Computers**: Raspberry Pi (Cortex-A series)
- **AI/ML Edge Devices**: Google Edge TPU, NVIDIA Jetson
- **Space & Aerospace**: Radiation-hardened ARM chips in satellites
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Pipeline Speedup Calculation

**Problem**: A program contains **N = 1000** instructions. Compare the execution time on a **non-pipelined ARM** vs. a **3-stage pipelined ARM**, assuming each stage takes **1 clock cycle** and the ALU stage requires **1 cycle**.

**Step 1: Non-Pipelined Execution**

In a non-pipelined design, each instruction must complete all stages sequentially.

$$T_{non-pipe} = N \times (S \times C_{stage})$$

$$T_{non-pipe} = 1000 \times (3 \times 1) = 3000 \text{ cycles}$$

**Step 2: Pipelined Execution**

After the pipeline is filled, ideally 1 instruction completes per cycle.

$$T_{pipe} = (N - 1) + S$$

$$T_{pipe} = (1000 - 1) + 3 = 1002 \text{ cycles}$$

**Step 3: Speedup Ratio**

$$S_{pipeline} = \frac{T_{non-pipe}}{T_{pipe}} = \frac{3000}{1002} \approx 2.99$$

**Step 4: Theoretical Maximum Speedup** (for ideal pipeline)

$$S_{max} = S = 3 \text{ (for 3-stage pipeline)}$$

**Step 5: Efficiency of Pipeline**

$$E_{pipeline} = \frac{S_{max}}{S_{actual}} = \frac{3}{2.99} \approx 1.003$$

As $N \to \infty$, the efficiency approaches **1.0** (100%), confirming the theoretical maximum.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Pipeline Filling and Draining Diagram

**Analytical Matrix of Pipeline Stages:**

| Cycle | Stage F | Stage D | Stage E |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | I1 | — | — |
| 2 | I2 | I1 | — |
| 3 | I3 | I2 | I1 |
| 4 | I4 | I3 | I2 |
| 5 | I5 | I4 | I3 |
| ... | ... | ... | ... |
| 1000 | I1000 | I999 | I998 |
| 1001 | — | I1000 | I999 |
| 1002 | — | — | I1000 |

**Mathematical Justification:**

The **pipeline depth** is $S = 3$. The first instruction completes at cycle **3**, the last at cycle **$N + S - 1 = 1002$**.

$$T_{total} = (N - 1) + S = (1000 - 1) + 3 = 1002 \text{ cycles}$$

This shows that for large $N$, **one instruction completes per cycle (CPI ≈ 1)**, which is the hallmark of a well-designed RISC pipeline.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: PC Value Offset in ARM

**Problem**: Show why `R15 (PC) = Current_Instruction_Address + 8` in ARM state.

**Step 1**: At the start of the **Fetch** stage of instruction $I_n$ at address $A_n$:

$$\text{PC} = A_n + 8$$

**Step 2**: In the **Execute** stage of $I_n$, the PC has been updated to $A_n + 8$ because:
- During Fetch: PC reads $A_n$
- During Decode: PC reads $A_n + 4$ (next instruction)
- During Execute: PC reads $A_n + 8$ (instruction after next)

**Step 3**: When a branch is taken (e.g., `B label`), the target address is computed as:

$$\text{PC}_{new} = \text{PC}_{old} + \text{offset}$$

Since PC is already 8 bytes ahead, the offset is a **signed 24-bit immediate** relative to the branch instruction's address + 8.

**Step 4**: Effective range of branch:

$$R_{branch} = \pm 2^{23} \text{ words} = \pm 32 \text{ MB}$$

This large range is sufficient for nearly all control-flow transfers within a program.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: CPSR Condition Flag Computation for ADD

For an `ADD R0, R1, R2` instruction where $R_0 = R_1 + R_2$:

$$\text{Result} = R_1 + R_2 = R_0$$

**Flag Computation:**

- **N flag**: $N = R_0[31]$ (sign bit of result)
- **Z flag**: $Z = 1$ if $R_0 = 0$, else $Z = 0$
- **C flag**: $C = 1$ if unsigned addition overflows (carry out of bit 31), else $C = 0$
- **V flag**: $V = 1$ if signed addition overflows, else $V = 0$

**Signed overflow condition**:

$$V = (R_1[31] \land R_2[31] \land \overline{R_0[31]}) \lor (\overline{R_1[31]} \land \overline{R_2[31]} \land R_0[31])$$

**Example**: Let $R_1 = 0x7FFFFFFF$ and $R_2 = 0x00000001$.

$$R_0 = 0x7FFFFFFF + 0x00000001 = 0x80000000$$

- $N = 1$ (result is negative in signed interpretation)
- $Z = 0$ (result is non-zero)
- $C = 0$ (no unsigned carry out)
- $V = 1$ (signed overflow: positive + positive = negative)
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: ARM Processor Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["ADDRESS BUS 32 bit"] --> B["INSTRUCTION DECODE UNIT"]
    C["DATA BUS 32 bit"] --> B
    B --> D["CONTROL UNIT"]
    D --> E["BARREL SHIFTER"]
    E --> F["ALU 32 bit"]
    F --> G["REGISTER FILE 37 Registers"]
    G --> H["WRITE BACK STAGE"]
    H --> I["OUTPUT TO MEMORY AND BUS"]
    J["CPSR SPSR FLAGS"] --> F
    F --> J
    G --> J
    style A fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#0891b2,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#0891b2,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#f59e0b,color:#000000
    style F fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff
    style H fill:#7c3aed,color:#ffffff
    style I fill:#475569,color:#ffffff
    style J fill:#9333ea,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: ARM Register Banking Across Modes

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R0["R0 to R7 SHARED across all modes"] --> USR["USER MODE"]
    R0 --> FIQ["FIQ MODE"]
    R0 --> IRQ["IRQ MODE"]
    R0 --> SVC["SUPERVISOR MODE"]
    R0 --> ABT["ABORT MODE"]
    R0 --> UND["UNDEFINED MODE"]
    R0 --> SYS["SYSTEM MODE"]
    R8USR["R8 to R12 USER"] --> USR
    R8FIQ["R8_fiq to R12_fiq BANKED"] --> FIQ
    R8SYS["R8 to R12 SYSTEM"] --> SYS
    R13USR["R13 SP USER"] --> USR
    R13FIQ["R13_fiq SP FIQ"] --> FIQ
    R13IRQ["R13_irq SP IRQ"] --> IRQ
    R13SVC["R13_svc SP SVC"] --> SVC
    R13ABT["R13_abt SP ABT"] --> ABT
    R13UND["R13_und SP UND"] --> UND
    R14USR["R14 LR USER"] --> USR
    R14FIQ["R14_fiq LR FIQ"] --> FIQ
    R14IRQ["R14_irq LR IRQ"] --> IRQ
    R14SVC["R14_svc LR SVC"] --> SVC
    R14ABT["R14_abt LR ABT"] --> ABT
    R14UND["R14_und LR UND"] --> UND
    PC15["R15 PC Program Counter SHARED"] --> USR
    PC15 --> FIQ
    PC15 --> IRQ
    PC15 --> SVC
    PC15 --> ABT
    PC15 --> UND
    PC15 --> SYS
    CPSR["CPSR Current PSR SHARED"] --> USR
    CPSR --> FIQ
    CPSR --> IRQ
    CPSR --> SVC
    CPSR --> ABT
    CPSR --> UND
    CPSR --> SYS
    SPSR["SPSR Saved PSR one per privileged mode"] --> FIQ
    SPSR --> IRQ
    SPSR --> SVC
    SPSR --> ABT
    SPSR --> UND
    style R0 fill:#0ea5e9,color:#ffffff
    style USR fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff
    style FIQ fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff
    style IRQ fill:#f59e0b,color:#000000
    style SVC fill:#7c3aed,color:#ffffff
    style ABT fill:#ec4899,color:#ffffff
    style UND fill:#06b6d4,color:#ffffff
    style SYS fill:#475569,color:#ffffff
    style PC15 fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff
    style CPSR fill:#9333ea,color:#ffffff
    style SPSR fill:#9333ea,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: 3-Stage ARM Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INST["INSTRUCTION STREAM"] --> F1["F1 Fetch I1"]
    F1 --> F2["F2 Fetch I2"]
    F2 --> F3["F3 Fetch I3"]
    F2 --> D1["D1 Decode I1"]
    F3 --> D2["D2 Decode I2"]
    D1 --> E1["E1 Execute I1"]
    D2 --> E2["E2 Execute I2"]
    E1 --> E2
    E2 --> E3["E3 Execute I3"]
    E3 --> OUT["OUTPUT RESULTS"]
    style INST fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style F1 fill:#0891b2,color:#ffffff
    style F2 fill:#0891b2,color:#ffffff
    style F3 fill:#0891b2,color:#ffffff
    style D1 fill:#f59e0b,color:#000000
    style D2 fill:#f59e0b,color:#000000
    style E1 fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff
    style E2 fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff
    style E3 fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff
    style OUT fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram: CPSR Bit Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B31["BIT 31 N NEGATIVE FLAG"] --> B30["BIT 30 Z ZERO FLAG"]
    B30 --> B29["BIT 29 C CARRY FLAG"]
    B29 --> B28["BIT 28 V OVERFLOW FLAG"]
    B28 --> B27["BITS 27 to 8 RESERVED"]
    B27 --> B7["BIT 7 I IRQ DISABLE"]
    B7 --> B6["BIT 6 F FIQ DISABLE"]
    B6 --> B5["BIT 5 T THUMB STATE"]
    B5 --> B4["BITS 4 to 0 MODE FIELD"]
    style B31 fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff
    style B30 fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff
    style B29 fill:#0891b2,color:#ffffff
    style B28 fill:#f59e0b,color:#000000
    style B27 fill:#6b7280,color:#ffffff
    style B7 fill:#9333ea,color:#ffffff
    style B6 fill:#7c3aed,color:#ffffff
    style B5 fill:#ec4899,color:#ffffff
    style B4 fill:#475569,color:#ffffff
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
**Explain the features of ARM processor. List the different operating modes of ARM processor.**

**Model Answer:**

ARM is a 32-bit **RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computer)** processor with the following features:
- **Load/Store architecture** — data processing operates only on registers
- **Fixed 32-bit instructions** for fast decoding
- **37 physical registers** with banking (16 visible in user mode)
- **Conditional execution** of almost all instructions
- **Orthogonal design** — any register usable in any operation
- **Low power consumption** — ideal for mobile/embedded use
- **Built-in barrel shifter** — second operand can be shifted/rotated for free

**The 7 operating modes of ARM are:**
1. **User (usr)** — Normal unprivileged mode
2. **FIQ (fiq)** — Fast Interrupt handling
3. **IRQ (irq)** — Normal Interrupt handling
4. **Supervisor (svc)** — Protected OS kernel mode
5. **Abort (abt)** — Memory access violation handling
6. **Undefined (und)** — Undefined instruction trap
7. **System (sys)** — Privileged mode using User registers

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]**
**With a neat diagram, explain the CPSR (Current Program Status Register) of ARM processor.**

**Model Answer:**

The **CPSR (Current Program Status Register)** is a **32-bit register** that holds:
- **Condition flags** (bits 31–28): N, Z, C, V
- **Control bits** (bits 7–5): I, F, T
- **Mode bits** (bits 4–0): current operating mode

```
Bits:  31 30 29 28  27 8   7 6 5   4 0
       N  Z  C  V   --  -- I F T   Mode
```

| Bit | Name | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 31 | N | Set if result is negative |
| 30 | Z | Set if result is zero |
| 29 | C | Carry/borrow flag |
| 28 | V | Signed overflow flag |
| 7  | I | 1 = IRQ disabled |
| 6  | F | 1 = FIQ disabled |
| 5  | T | 0 = ARM state, 1 = Thumb state |
| 4–0 | Mode | 0b10000 = User, 0b10011 = Supervisor, etc. |

Each privileged mode also has its own **SPSR (Saved PSR)** to preserve CPSR during exception handling.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified Pattern]**

### **OR** Choice — Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the **ARM processor architecture** with a neat block diagram. List any **six features** of ARM processor. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **ARM register organization**. How are registers banked in different operating modes? **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) ARM Processor Architecture (7 Marks)**

The ARM processor uses a **RISC load-store architecture** with the following major blocks:

1. **Instruction Fetch Unit** — Fetches instructions from memory using the program counter.
2. **Instruction Decode Unit** — Decodes the 32-bit opcode and reads operands from the register file.
3. **Barrel Shifter** — Performs shift/rotate operations on the second operand in a single cycle (free operation).
4. **ALU (Arithmetic Logic Unit)** — Executes arithmetic (ADD, SUB), logic (AND, OR, XOR), and comparison operations.
5. **Register File** — Contains 37 physical 32-bit registers, organized in banks for fast mode switching.
6. **Multiplier** — Performs integer multiplication (MUL, MLA) using a dedicated hardware unit.
7. **Control Unit** — Generates control signals for all data paths; manages the pipeline.
8. **Memory Interface** — Load/Store engine for data transfer between registers and memory.
9. **Coprocessor Interface** — Allows extension with FPU, MMU, DSP, etc.

**Six Key Features of ARM:**
1. Load/Store RISC architecture
2. Fixed 32-bit instruction length
3. 37-register file with banking
4. Conditional execution of instructions
5. Built-in barrel shifter
6. Low power consumption

**(b) ARM Register Organization (7 Marks)**

ARM has **37 physical 32-bit registers**, of which **16 are user-visible** in User mode. The user-visible registers are **R0–R15** plus the **CPSR**.

**Special-purpose registers:**
- **R13 (SP)**: Stack Pointer
- **R14 (LR)**: Link Register — stores return address
- **R15 (PC)**: Program Counter

**Banking in Privileged Modes:**

In privileged modes (FIQ, IRQ, SVC, ABT, UND), certain registers are **banked** (replaced by mode-specific physical copies). This eliminates the need to save/restore registers on every exception.

| Mode | Banked Registers |
|---|---|
| FIQ | R8_fiq–R14_fiq, SPSR_fiq (7 banked GPRs) |
| IRQ | R13_irq, R14_irq, SPSR_irq |
| Supervisor | R13_svc, R14_svc, SPSR_svc |
| Abort | R13_abt, R14_abt, SPSR_abt |
| Undefined | R13_und, R14_und, SPSR_und |
| System | Uses User registers (no banking) |

**Advantages of Banking:**
- **Fast exception entry** — no need to push registers to stack immediately
- **Mode isolation** — interrupt service routines cannot corrupt user registers
- **Deterministic latency** — critical for real-time systems

---

### **OR** Choice — Question B (14 Marks)

**(a)** Compare **RISC and CISC** architectures. Why is ARM classified as RISC? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **3-stage pipeline** of ARM processor. Compute the **speedup** for executing **500 instructions** in a non-pipelined vs. pipelined ARM (each stage = 1 cycle). **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) RISC vs CISC (7 Marks)**

| Parameter | RISC (ARM) | CISC (x86) |
|---|---|---|
| **Full Form** | Reduced Instruction Set Computer | Complex Instruction Set Computer |
| **Instruction Set Size** | Small (≤ 100) | Large (> 200) |
| **Instruction Length** | Fixed (32-bit) | Variable (1–15 bytes) |
| **Memory Access** | Load/Store only | Memory-to-memory |
| **Clock Cycles/Instruction** | Mostly 1 | Multiple |
| **Decoding** | Hardwired logic | Microcode |
| **Pipelining** | Easy & efficient | Complex & hard |
| **Register Count** | Many (37) | Few (8–16) |
| **Power** | Low | High |
| **Code Size** | Larger | Smaller |
| **Compiler Complexity** | Higher | Lower |
| **Examples** | ARM, MIPS, SPARC | Intel x86, Motorola 68k |

**Why ARM is RISC:**
1. **Fixed-length 32-bit instructions** allow simple, fast decoding.
2. **Load/Store architecture** — ALU never accesses memory directly.
3. **Large register file** (37 registers) reduces memory access.
4. **Hardwired control logic** instead of microcode.
5. **One instruction per cycle (CPI ≈ 1)** execution model.

**(b) 3-Stage Pipeline (7 Marks)**

The classic ARM7 uses a **3-stage pipeline**:
1. **Fetch (F)**: Instruction read from memory at PC.
2. **Decode (D)**: Instruction decoded, source operands read from registers, barrel shifter applied.
3. **Execute (E)**: ALU operation performed, result written back, memory accessed if required.

**Pipeline Diagram (5-instruction snippet):**

| Cycle | F | D | E |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | I1 | — | — |
| 2 | I2 | I1 | — |
| 3 | I3 | I2 | I1 |
| 4 | I4 | I3 | I2 |
| 5 | I5 | I4 | I3 |
| 6 | I6 | I5 | I4 |

**Speedup Calculation for N = 500 instructions:**

**Non-Pipelined:**

$$T_{np} = N \times 3 = 500 \times 3 = 1500 \text{ cycles}$$

**Pipelined:**

$$T_{p} = (N - 1) + 3 = 499 + 3 = 502 \text{ cycles}$$

**Speedup:**

$$S = \frac{T_{np}}{T_{p}} = \frac{1500}{502} \approx 2.99$$

**Efficiency:**

$$E = \frac{S_{ideal}}{S_{actual}} = \frac{3}{2.99} = 1.003$$

This shows the pipeline achieves near-ideal performance for large N.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to mention "37 physical registers"** — Many students write "16 registers" only, losing 1–2 marks. KTU expects the **37-register** architecture with banking.
> 
> 2. **Confusing CPSR with SPSR** — CPSR is the *current* status; SPSR is *saved* status for each privileged mode. Each privileged mode has its **own** SPSR.
> 
> 3. **PC offset confusion** — Many students say `PC = current_address` but in ARM state, `PC = current_address + 8` due to the 3-stage pipeline.
> 
> 4. **Mode bits confusion** — User mode is `0b10000` (decimal 16), NOT 0. Supervisor is `0b10011` (decimal 19).
> 
> 5. **Forgetting System mode** — Some students list only 6 modes; the correct answer is **7 modes** including System mode.
> 
> 6. **RISC feature mismatch** — Writing "variable-length instructions" for RISC will cost marks; it is a **CISC** feature.
> 
> 7. **Pipeline speedup formula** — Use $(N-1)+S$, not $N \times S$ for pipelined execution. The latter is non-pipelined.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — ARM Processor Fundamentals**
> 
> ✅ **Architecture**: ARM is a **32-bit RISC Load/Store** processor by **ARM Holdings, Cambridge**.
> 
> ✅ **Instruction Size**: **32 bits** (ARM state) / **16 bits** (Thumb state)
> 
> ✅ **Registers**: **37 physical** 32-bit registers, **16 visible** to user (R0–R15)
> 
> ✅ **R15 = PC** = current instruction address + **8** (due to 3-stage pipeline)
> 
> ✅ **R13 = SP** (Stack Pointer), **R14 = LR** (Link Register)
> 
> ✅ **7 Operating Modes**: User, FIQ, IRQ, Supervisor, Abort, Undefined, System
> 
> ✅ **User mode** is the **only unprivileged** mode
> 
> ✅ **CPSR** holds condition flags (N, Z, C, V), control bits (I, F, T), and mode bits
> 
> ✅ **SPSR** is one per privileged mode — saves CPSR on exception entry
> 
> ✅ **Condition Flags** (in CPSR): N=bit31, Z=bit30, C=bit29, V=bit28
> 
> ✅ **Control Bits** (in CPSR): I=bit7, F=bit6, T=bit5
> 
> ✅ **Mode Bits** are in bits **4–0** of CPSR
> 
> ✅ **Banked Registers**: FIQ banks R8–R14 (7 registers), others bank only R13–R14
> 
> ✅ **Pipeline (ARM7)**: **3 stages** — Fetch, Decode, Execute
> 
> ✅ **Pipeline Time**: $T_p = (N - 1) + S$ cycles (ideal, no hazards)
> 
> ✅ **CPI for ARM** ≈ **1** (one instruction per cycle in ideal conditions)
> 
> ✅ **Load/Store**: Only `LDR`/`STR` (and `LDM`/`STM`) access memory; ALU operates on registers
> 
> ✅ **Barrel Shifter**: Second operand can be shifted/rotated for **free** (in the same cycle)
> 
> ✅ **RISC vs CISC**: RISC = small set, fixed length, load-store, pipelined, low power
> 
> ✅ **Branch Range**: ± **32 MB** (signed 24-bit offset × 4 bytes)
> 
> ✅ **No `INC`/`DEC`** in ARM — only `ADD` and `SUB`
> 
> ✅ **ARM Ltd. designs the architecture**; licensees (Qualcomm, Apple, Samsung) manufacture chips
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ARM Architecture - Register
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCIT404 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** ARM Architecture - Register

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🧠 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **ARM Register Architecture Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)**
> 
> The **ARM (Advanced RISC Machine) processor register file** is a **Load/Store architecture** register bank consisting of **37 registers** in total, physically arranged as **31 general-purpose 32-bit registers** and **6 status registers**. These registers are dynamically banked (physically duplicated) across the **7 privileged processor modes** to provide fast context switching, exception handling, and rapid interrupt response.

The ARM core is a **RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computer)** based processor, and its **register set is the heart of its performance**. Unlike accumulator-based processors (e.g., 8085) where most operations implicitly target the Accumulator, ARM uses a **flat, orthogonal register file** where nearly any operation can use any register as a source or destination.

### The 37 Registers — The Complete ARM Register Set

| Register Group | Count | Width | Purpose |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| General-Purpose Registers (R0–R12) | **13** | **32-bit** | Data manipulation, ALU operations |
| Stack Pointer (R13 / SP) | **1 (6 banked)** | **32-bit** | Stack management |
| Link Register (R14 / LR) | **1 (6 banked)** | **32-bit** | Subroutine return address storage |
| Program Counter (R15 / PC) | **1** | **32-bit** | Address of next instruction |
| Current Program Status Register (CPSR) | **1** | **32-bit** | Flags, mode bits, interrupt masks |
| Saved Program Status Register (SPSR) | **1 (5 banked)** | **32-bit** | CPSR backup during exceptions |
| **Total Physical Registers** | **37** | **32-bit** | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Although the ARM logical name space is **R0–R15** (16 registers visible to the programmer at any time), the **physical register file actually contains 37 registers** because R13, R14, and SPSR are physically duplicated (banked) for fast mode-switching.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Workbench" View

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy: A Carpenter's Workbench** 🛠️
> 
> Imagine a **highly skilled carpenter's workbench**:
> - **General-Purpose Registers (R0–R12)** = The **13 open tool slots** on the bench. Any tool (data) can be placed in any slot, and the carpenter (ALU) can grab any tool for any job. There is **no designated "hammer slot"** — flexibility is total.
> - **Stack Pointer (R13 / SP)** = A **clipboard holding a "current shelf number"**. As the carpenter places cut wood on shelves, the clipboard tracks *which shelf is the topmost*, so he always knows where to put the next piece.
> - **Link Register (R14 / LR)** = A **Post-it Note** stuck to the workbench that says *"go back to the kitchen when done"*. When a helper is called (subroutine invoked), he jots down the boss's (caller's) location so he can return there when the task is finished.
> - **Program Counter (R15 / PC)** = The **carpenter's eyes** — always fixed on the **next instruction to execute** on the blueprint.
> - **CPSR / SPSR** = The **boss's clipboard with status flags** — recording whether the previous cut resulted in a "carry-over piece" (Carry flag), a "negative remainder" (Negative flag), or a "zero leftover" (Zero flag).

This analogy makes it clear *why* ARM has **so many registers** — they eliminate slow memory accesses, just as a well-stocked workbench eliminates trips to the tool shed.

---

## 1.3 The Logical View vs Physical View (Critical Distinction)

From the **programmer's logical view**, the ARM core always exposes **R0–R15** (16 registers). However, in **privileged modes** (FIQ, IRQ, Supervisor, Abort, Undefined), the registers R13, R14, and R15 are **physically different registers** with the same logical name — a concept called **register banking**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Banking** is a hardware-level feature where multiple physical registers share the same logical name. Only one is visible at a time, depending on the current processor mode. This is the **key to ARM's nanosecond-fast exception handling** — no memory save/restore is needed on mode entry.

The **System mode** is unique: it uses the **User mode registers** but retains **privileged access** — it is the only privileged mode that does *not* bank its own R13/R14/SPSR.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Architectural Metrics (Bold Highlights)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key ARM Register Metrics:**
> 
> - **Register Width:** **32 bits** (= **4 bytes** = **1 word** in ARM terminology)
> - **Total Physical Registers:** **37**
> - **Visible Logical Registers:** **16** (R0–R15)
> - **Banked Pairs (R13, R14):** **6 physical pairs** for User/FIQ/IRQ/Supervisor/Abort/Undefined modes
> - **Banked SPSR:** **5 physical SPSRs** (excluded for User/System mode)
> - **Maximum Modes:** **7** (User, FIQ, IRQ, Supervisor, Abort, Undefined, System)
> - **Pipeline Fetch Offset:** **PC = Current Instruction Address + 8 bytes** in ARM state (2-stage pipeline)
> - **ARM7TDMI Internal Bus Width:** **32-bit data bus**

---

## 1.5 Why Registers Matter in KTU Examinations

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Note:** The ARM register set is a **high-weightage topic** in KTU Module 4. Questions frequently appear on:
> 1. Listing the 37 registers and their groupings
> 2. Explaining **banked registers** with diagrams
> 3. Differences between **ARM state and Thumb state** register usage
> 4. The role of **CPSR condition flags** (N, Z, C, V)
> 5. The unique **PC behavior** (PC = address + 8)
> 
> Memorize the **mode-to-bank mapping table** — it is the single most-tested concept.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The General-Purpose Registers (R0 – R12)

The first **13 registers**, namely **R0 to R12**, are **identical 32-bit general-purpose registers**. They have **no special hardware function** assigned by the architecture — they are pure data containers used by the programmer and compiler.

### 2.1.1 Operational Rules for R0–R12

- **Unbanked:** There is only **one physical copy** of R0–R12, shared by **all processor modes**.
- **Usage Restrictions:**
  - In **ARM state**, **all 13 registers** are freely usable.
  - In **Thumb state**, **only R0–R7 are fully accessible** for most instructions. Registers **R8–R12 are restricted** — they can only be accessed by a small subset of Thumb instructions (MOV, CMP, ADD, SUB).

> [!NOTE]
> **Compiler Calling Convention (AAPCS — ARM Architecture Procedure Call Standard):**
> - **R0–R3:** Used to pass **function arguments** and hold **return values** (caller-saved / scratch registers).
> - **R4–R11:** Used to hold **local variables** across function calls (callee-saved).
> - **R12 (IP):** The **Intra-Procedure call scratch register** — used by the linker for veneers and trampolines.
> - **R11 (FP):** Conventionally used as the **Frame Pointer** in stack-frame-based debuggers.
> - **R9:** Has a dual role in some ARM variants (e.g., position-independent code on Linux uses it as a static base).

### 2.1.2 Step-by-Step: Why 13 General-Purpose Registers?

The number 13 is not arbitrary. It is the result of a 4-bit register address field (giving 16 logical registers) minus the 3 special-purpose registers (SP, LR, PC). This allows the **entire register set to be encoded in a single 4-bit field** within the 32-bit ARM instruction, making register access **single-cycle and direct**.

---

## 2.2 Stack Pointer — R13 (SP)

> [!NOTE]
> **R13 is the Stack Pointer (SP).** It holds the **memory address of the topmost element** in the currently active stack.

### 2.2.1 Banking of R13

R13 is **banked** in **6 privileged modes**:

- `SP_usr` / `SP_sys` (User and System share the same physical SP)
- `SP_fiq`
- `SP_irq`
- `SP_svc`
- `SP_abt`
- `SP_und`

When the processor switches to a new mode, the **corresponding banked SP becomes active**, giving each mode its **own private stack**. This prevents stack corruption between User tasks and exception handlers.

### 2.2.2 Stack Models in ARM

ARM supports **two stack growth models** selected via configuration:

| Stack Type | Full-Descending (FD) | Full-Ascending (FA) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Direction** | Grows toward **lower** addresses | Grows toward **higher** addresses |
| **SP points to** | **Last pushed item** (full) | **Last pushed item** (full) |
| **KTU Default** | ✅ **Yes (most common)** | Less common |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** In **Full-Descending** stacks (used by virtually all modern ARM software), `PUSH` decrements SP first, then stores; `POP` loads first, then increments SP. The SP always points to the **last valid item on the stack**.

---

## 2.3 Link Register — R14 (LR)

> [!NOTE]
> **R14 is the Link Register (LR).** When a **Branch-and-Link (`BL`)** instruction executes, the **return address** is automatically saved into R14.

### 2.3.1 How `BL` Works (Step-by-Step)

1. The CPU encounters a `BL subroutine_label` instruction.
2. The address of the **instruction after the BL** is computed: `LR = PC`.
3. Since the ARM pipeline has already fetched `PC + 8` at the time of decode, **LR = address of BL + 4** (the next sequential instruction).
4. Program flow jumps to `subroutine_label`.
5. Inside the subroutine, the instruction `MOV PC, LR` (or `BX LR`) returns control to the caller.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Utility:** The LR enables **zero-overhead subroutine calls** — no memory access is needed to save the return address. This is **orders of magnitude faster** than the 8085's stack-based `CALL/RET`, which requires explicit memory writes/reads.

### 2.3.2 LR Banking

Like R13, R14 is banked across the **6 privileged modes**. Each exception mode has its **own private LR**, allowing the exception handler to itself invoke subroutines without corrupting the User-mode LR.

---

## 2.4 Program Counter — R15 (PC)

> [!NOTE]
> **R15 is the Program Counter (PC).** It is **not banked** — it is the **single physical PC** shared by all modes.

### 2.4.1 The Famous "PC = Current + 8" Rule

In **ARM state**, due to the **classic 3-stage pipeline** (Fetch → Decode → Execute), the value read from R15 is always **2 instructions ahead** of the currently executing instruction:

$$ \text{Value of R15 read} = \text{Address of current instruction} + 8 \text{ bytes} $$

This is because the PC has been **incremented during fetch and decode stages**, even though execution is still on the original instruction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Fact:**
> - In **ARM state**: `PC = Current + 8`
> - In **Thumb state**: `PC = Current + 4` (due to the 2-stage pipeline)
> - Reading R15 in `STR` / `LDR` returns `address + 8` (or `+12` for some implementations)
> - Writing R15 causes a **branch** (the lowest bit is forced to 0 in ARM state, to 1 in Thumb state)

### 2.4.2 Step-by-Step: PC Pipeline Behavior

Let the instruction stream be at addresses `A`, `A+4`, `A+8`...

- **Cycle 1:** `A` is fetched; PC internally points to `A+4`.
- **Cycle 2:** `A+4` is fetched, `A` is decoded; PC internally points to `A+8`.
- **Cycle 3:** `A+8` is fetched, `A+4` is decoded, `A` is executed; PC internally points to `A+12`.
- **When the instruction at A executes** and reads PC, it sees **`A+8`**.

---

## 2.5 The CPSR — Current Program Status Register

> [!NOTE]
> **CPSR (Current Program Status Register)** is a **32-bit register** holding the **current ALU flags**, **interrupt masks**, **processor mode**, and **ARM/Thumb state**.

### 2.5.1 CPSR Bit Layout (32 bits)

The CPSR is **not banked** — there is exactly **one physical CPSR** that all modes share. The bit allocation is:

| Bits | Field | Symbol | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| [31:28] | **Condition Flags** | N, Z, C, V | Result of last ALU operation |
| [27:25] | **IT [1:0], J** | — | Thumb-2 IT block state, Jazelle |
| [24] | **J** | J | Jazelle state (Java bytecode) |
| [23:20] | **Reserved** | — | Should be `1` (SBZ — Should Be Zero) |
| [19:16] | **GE [3:0]** | — | Greater-than-or-Equal flags (SIMD) |
| [15:10] | **IT [7:2]** | — | Thumb-2 IT block state |
| [9] | **Endianness** | E | **0 = Big-endian, 1 = Little-endian** |
| [8] | **Async abort mask** | A | Mask asynchronous aborts (1 = masked) |
| [7] | **IRQ mask** | I | Mask IRQ interrupts (1 = masked) |
| [6] | **FIQ mask** | F | Mask FIQ interrupts (1 = masked) |
| [5] | **Thumb state** | T | **0 = ARM state, 1 = Thumb state** |
| [4:0] | **Mode bits** | M[4:0] | Current processor mode (see Section 2.6) |

### 2.5.2 The Four Condition Flags (Bits 31–28)

| Flag | Bit | Set When | Cleared When |
| :---: | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| **N — Negative** | 31 | ALU result bit 31 is **1** | ALU result bit 31 is **0** |
| **Z — Zero** | 30 | ALU result equals **zero** | ALU result is non-zero |
| **C — Carry** | 29 | Carry-out from bit 31 is **1** (unsigned overflow) | Carry-out is **0** |
| **V — Overflow** | 28 | Signed overflow occurred (two's-complement) | No signed overflow |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid — "NZCV":** Think of the four flags as **"Negative, Zero, Carry, oVerflow"** — a common trick question in KTU asks to identify which bit corresponds to which flag.

---

## 2.6 Processor Modes and Mode Bits

ARM has **7 processor modes**, encoded in the **lower 5 bits (M[4:0])** of the CPSR:

| Mode | M[4:0] | Privileged? | Banked SP? | Banked LR? | Banked SPSR? | Use |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| **User (usr)** | `10000` | ❌ | ✅ (usr) | ✅ (usr) | ❌ | Normal application code |
| **FIQ (fiq)** | `10001` | ✅ | ✅ (fiq) | ✅ (fiq) | ✅ (fiq) | Fast Interrupt |
| **IRQ (irq)** | `10010` | ✅ | ✅ (irq) | ✅ (irq) | ✅ (irq) | General Interrupt |
| **Supervisor (svc)** | `10011` | ✅ | ✅ (svc) | ✅ (svc) | ✅ (svc) | OS kernel, SWI |
| **Abort (abt)** | `10111` | ✅ | ✅ (abt) | ✅ (abt) | ✅ (abt) | Memory access faults |
| **Undefined (und)** | `11011` | ✅ | ✅ (und) | ✅ (und) | ✅ (und) | Undefined instructions |
| **System (sys)** | `11111` | ✅ | ❌ (uses usr) | ❌ (uses usr) | ❌ | Privileged OS tasks |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Critical Fact:** User mode and System mode **share the same physical R13 and R14** (no banking). All other 5 privileged modes have their **own dedicated R13, R14, and SPSR**.

---

## 2.7 SPSR — Saved Program Status Register

> [!NOTE]
> **SPSR (Saved Program Status Register)** is a **shadow copy of the CPSR** that is **automatically loaded** by the CPU when an **exception** is taken. The SPSR of the *exception* mode captures the CPSR of the *previous* mode.

### 2.7.1 SPSR Banking Rules

- **5 physical SPSRs** exist: `SPSR_fiq`, `SPSR_irq`, `SPSR_svc`, `SPSR_abt`, `SPSR_und`.
- **User mode and System mode do NOT have an SPSR** — it is undefined in these modes.
- On exception return (`MOVS PC, LR` or `SUBS PC, LR, #4`), the SPSR is **atomically restored to the CPSR**.

### 2.7.2 The Step-by-Step Exception Mechanism

1. An exception occurs (e.g., IRQ).
2. CPU switches to `irq` mode (M[4:0] ← `10010`).
3. CPU **saves** the old CPSR into `SPSR_irq`.
4. CPU saves the return address in `LR_irq` (with adjustments for the exception type).
5. CPU sets I-flag = 1 (masks further IRQs) and branches to the **IRQ vector** at `0x00000018`.
6. The ISR executes, reading its banked `R13_irq` (SP).
7. On exit, `SUBS PC, LR, #4` returns — restoring CPSR from SPSR_irq in the same cycle.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The atomic CPSR/SPSR swap enables **interrupt latency as low as ~13 clock cycles** on ARM7TDMI — far better than the hundreds of cycles required by 8085-style stack-based context save/restore.

---

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula & Boundary Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Formula Sheet — ARM Register Architecture**

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Logical visible registers** | `R0` to `R15` | 16 registers (always) |
| **Physical registers** | **37** | 31 general + 6 status |
| **PC read value (ARM state)** | $PC = \text{CurrentInstrAddr} + 8$ | 3-stage pipeline offset |
| **PC read value (Thumb state)** | $PC = \text{CurrentInstrAddr} + 4$ | 2-stage pipeline offset |
| **Stack growth (Full Descending)** | $\text{New SP} = \text{Old SP} - 4$ | After each PUSH |
| **Stack pop (Full Descending)** | $\text{New SP} = \text{Old SP} + 4$ | After each POP |
| **Mode bits in CPSR** | $M[4:0]$ | Lower 5 bits |
| **Condition flags in CPSR** | $N, Z, C, V$ | Upper 4 bits (bits 31–28) |
| **Endianness bit** | $E = 0 \rightarrow$ Big, $E = 1 \rightarrow$ Little | Bit 9 of CPSR |
| **Thumb state bit** | $T = 0 \rightarrow$ ARM, $T = 1 \rightarrow$ Thumb | Bit 5 of CPSR |
| **IRQ mask bit** | $I = 1 \rightarrow$ Masked | Bit 7 of CPSR |
| **FIQ mask bit** | $F = 1 \rightarrow$ Masked | Bit 6 of CPSR |
| **LR update on `BL`** | $LR = \text{Address of BL} + 4$ | Returns to next instruction |
| **Carry flag (ADD)** | $C = 1$ if unsigned overflow | $C = \overline{\text{borrow}}$ for SUB |
| **Overflow flag (ADD)** | $V = 1$ if signed overflow | $V = C_{31} \oplus C_{32}$ |

---

## 2.9 ARM State vs Thumb State Register Differences

| Feature | ARM State | Thumb State |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Instruction width** | 32-bit | 16-bit |
| **Fully accessible GP regs** | R0–R12 | R0–R7 |
| **Restricted regs** | None | R8–R12 (limited) |
| **PC pipeline offset** | $+8$ | $+4$ |
| **CPSR read/write** | Yes (via `MRS`/`MSR`) | Yes (same instructions) |
| **CPSR T-bit** | 0 | 1 |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often state *"Thumb has only 8 registers."* This is wrong. Thumb has the **same 16 logical registers**, but the **instruction encoding** restricts which ones most instructions can use.

---

## 2.10 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Where ARM Register Architecture Shines in Industry:**
> 
> 1. **Mobile SoCs (Qualcomm Snapdragon, Apple A-series):** The 37-register bank enables **microsecond-level context switches** between Android apps, made possible by hardware register banking rather than slow stack saves.
> 2. **Automotive ECUs (ARM Cortex-R):** Fast FIQ banking allows **deterministic interrupt response** within microseconds — critical for airbag and brake-by-wire systems.
> 3. **IoT (ARM Cortex-M):** The automatically-pushed R0–R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR on exception entry (Cortex-M3/M4) is an **evolution of the same banking philosophy**.
> 4. **Embedded RTOS (FreeRTOS, Zephyr):** The AAPCS calling convention (R0–R3 args, R4–R11 saved) is **directly derived** from the ARM register semantics.

# 📐 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The "PC = Current + 8" Rule from Pipeline Mechanics

This is a **classic KTU 14-mark derivation** — examiners frequently ask to *prove* why PC reads as `current + 8` in ARM state. We will derive it from first principles.

### 3.1.1 Setup: The 3-Stage ARM7TDMI Pipeline

The ARM7TDMI (the canonical KTU reference processor) uses a **3-stage pipeline**:

| Stage | Operation | Registers Used |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| **F (Fetch)** | Read instruction from memory at address `A` | PC holds `A` here |
| **D (Decode)** | Decode the fetched instruction | PC incremented to `A+4` |
| **E (Execute)** | Execute the instruction (ALU op, etc.) | PC incremented to `A+8` |

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Pipeline Walk-Through

Let us trace a small instruction sequence starting at memory address `0x00000000`:

| Cycle | Instruction at PC value | Stage | Physical PC counter (internal) |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | `0x00000000` (Instr I1) | Fetch | `0x00000000` |
| 2 | `0x00000004` (Instr I2) | Fetch | `0x00000004` |
|   | `0x00000000` (Instr I1) | Decode | `0x00000004` |
| 3 | `0x00000008` (Instr I3) | Fetch | `0x00000008` |
|   | `0x00000004` (Instr I2) | Decode | `0x00000008` |
|   | `0x00000000` (Instr I1) | Execute | `0x00000008` |

### 3.1.3 The Derivation

During **Cycle 3**, instruction I1 is in the **Execute stage**, but the **physical PC counter** has already advanced through Fetch and Decode, sitting at `0x00000008`.

Therefore, when the instruction I1 reads R15 (PC) during execution, it observes:

$$
\text{R15 read by I1} = \text{Address of I1} + 2 \times \text{Instruction Size}
$$

$$
\text{R15 read by I1} = A + 2 \times 4 = A + 8
$$

Thus:

$$
\boxed{\text{PC} = \text{Current Instruction Address} + 8 \text{ bytes (in ARM state)}}
$$

### 3.1.4 The Same Derivation for Thumb State

In Thumb state, instructions are **2 bytes (16 bits)**, and the pipeline effectively behaves as **2-stage** for simple instructions:

$$
\text{PC}_{\text{Thumb}} = A + 2 \times 2 = A + 4
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{PC} = \text{Current Instruction Address} + 4 \text{ bytes (in Thumb state)}}
$$

### 3.1.5 Numerical Worked Example

**Problem:** An ARM instruction is located at address `0x40001000`. The instruction `ADD R0, R1, R2` reads R15 to compute a PC-relative offset.

**Solution:**

$$
\text{PC value read} = 0x40001000 + 8 = 0x40001008
$$

The **offset of 8 bytes** must be subtracted if you want the address of the current instruction:

$$
\text{Address of current instruction} = \text{PC} - 8
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Carry Flag (C) Behaviour in Arithmetic Operations

### 3.2.1 Carry in ADD

For an unsigned addition `R_dest = R_a + R_b + C_{in}`:

$$
C_{\text{out}} = 1 \iff (R_a + R_b + C_{\text{in}}) > 2^{32} - 1
$$

In **KTU exam problems**, you must show:

$$
C = \text{carry out of bit 31}
$$

### 3.2.2 Carry in SUB (Inverse Logic)

For `SUBS` (which sets flags), the **C flag is set to 1 if NO borrow occurred**:

$$
C = 1 \iff R_a \geq R_b
$$

This is **inverse of the 8085 carry convention** — a frequent KTU pitfall.

### 3.2.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** Compute the CPSR flags after `ADDS R0, R1, R2` where `R1 = 0x7FFFFFF0` and `R2 = 0x00000020`.

**Step 1 — Perform the addition:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
0x7FFFFFF0 + 0x00000020 &= 0x80000010
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Determine the flags:**

- **N (bit 31):** Result = `0x80000010`, MSB = 1 → $\mathbf{N = 1}$
- **Z (bit 30):** Result is non-zero → $\mathbf{Z = 0}$
- **C (bit 29):** No unsigned overflow ($0x7FFFFFF0 + 0x20 = 0x80000010$, fits in 32 bits unsigned) → $\mathbf{C = 1}$
- **V (bit 28):** Signed overflow (positive + positive = negative) → $\mathbf{V = 1}$

$$
\text{Final CPSR flags: } N=1,\ Z=0,\ C=1,\ V=1
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: CPSR Encoding for Mode "Supervisor"

### 3.3.1 Mode Encoding Rule

ARM mode bits are **5 bits (M[4:0])** located in CPSR[4:0]. The encoding is **not sequential** — there are gaps to prevent accidental mode changes from random `MSR` operations.

| Mode | Binary (M[4:0]) | Hex |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| User | `10000` | `0x10` |
| FIQ | `10001` | `0x11` |
| IRQ | `10010` | `0x12` |
| **Supervisor** | **`10011`** | **`0x13`** |
| Abort | `10111` | `0x17` |
| Undefined | `11011` | `0x1B` |
| System | `11111` | `0x1F` |

### 3.3.2 Numerical Worked Example

**Problem:** Write the lower 5 bits of CPSR to enter Supervisor mode with **all interrupts enabled** and **ARM state**.

**Step 1 — Set mode bits:** Supervisor = `10011`

**Step 2 — Set T = 0** (ARM state)

**Step 3 — Set I = 0, F = 0** (enable IRQ and FIQ)

**Step 4 — Combine into lower byte of CPSR:**

$$
\text{CPSR}[7:0] = \underbrace{0}_{I} \cdot 2^7 + \underbrace{0}_{F} \cdot 2^6 + \underbrace{0}_{T} \cdot 2^5 + \underbrace{10011}_{\text{Mode bits}}
$$

$$
\text{CPSR}[7:0] = 0 \times 80 + 0 \times 40 + 0 \times 20 + 0 \times 13 = 0 \times 13
$$

Thus, the lower byte of CPSR is **`0x13`** (binary `0001 0011`).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: ARM Register File Summary Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Master KTU Comparison Table** — Memorize this for Part A and Part B questions.

| Register | Logical Name | Banked? | Number of Physical Copies | Width | Primary Function |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| R0 | R0 | No | 1 | 32 | GP / argument 1 / return value |
| R1 | R1 | No | 1 | 32 | GP / argument 2 |
| R2 | R2 | No | 1 | 32 | GP / argument 3 |
| R3 | R3 | No | 1 | 32 | GP / argument 4 |
| R4 | R4 | No | 1 | 32 | GP / callee-saved variable |
| R5 | R5 | No | 1 | 32 | GP / callee-saved variable |
| R6 | R6 | No | 1 | 32 | GP / callee-saved variable |
| R7 | R7 | No | 1 | 32 | GP / VFP frame pointer (in some ABIs) |
| R8 | R8 | No | 1 | 32 | GP |
| R9 | R9 | No | 1 | 32 | GP / platform-specific |
| R10 | R10 | No | 1 | 32 | GP / platform-specific |
| R11 | R11 | No | 1 | 32 | GP / Frame Pointer (FP) |
| R12 | R12 | No | 1 | 32 | IP — Intra-Procedure scratch |
| R13 | SP | **Yes** | **6** | 32 | Stack Pointer |
| R14 | LR | **Yes** | **6** | 32 | Link Register |
| R15 | PC | No | 1 | 32 | Program Counter |
| — | CPSR | No | 1 | 32 | Current Program Status |
| — | SPSR | **Yes** | **5** | 32 | Saved Program Status |

**Totals:** 13 (unbanked GP) + 6×2 (banked R13/R14) + 1 (PC) + 1 (CPSR) + 5 (SPSR) = **37 physical registers**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Mode-to-Banked-Register Mapping

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Most Tested Table in KTU Module 4** — this appears in nearly every ESE paper.

| Processor Mode | M[4:0] | Banked R13 (SP) | Banked R14 (LR) | Banked SPSR |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **User (usr)** | 10000 | R13_usr | R14_usr | None |
| **System (sys)** | 11111 | R13_usr (shared) | R14_usr (shared) | None |
| **Supervisor (svc)** | 10011 | R13_svc | R14_svc | SPSR_svc |
| **IRQ** | 10010 | R13_irq | R14_irq | SPSR_irq |
| **FIQ** | 10001 | R13_fiq | R14_fiq | SPSR_fiq |
| **Abort (abt)** | 10111 | R13_abt | R14_abt | SPSR_abt |
| **Undefined (und)** | 11011 | R13_und | R14_und | SPSR_und |

**Total banked R13:** 6 (usr + fiq + irq + svc + abt + und)
**Total banked R14:** 6
**Total banked SPSR:** 5 (all except usr/sys)

---

## 3.6 Derivation: Physical Register Count Verification

We now verify the **"37 registers"** count, which is a frequently asked KTU question.

**Step 1 — General-purpose registers (unbanked):**

$$
N_{\text{unbanked}} = 13 \quad (\text{R0} - \text{R12})
$$

**Step 2 — Special registers:**

- R13 (SP): **6 physical copies** (1 for User, 1 shared with System, + 4 other modes)
- R14 (LR): **6 physical copies** (same distribution)
- R15 (PC): **1 physical copy** (unbanked)

$$
N_{\text{special}} = 6 + 6 + 1 = 13
$$

**Step 3 — Status registers:**

- CPSR: **1 physical copy** (unbanked)
- SPSR: **5 physical copies** (User and System have none)

$$
N_{\text{status}} = 1 + 5 = 6
$$

**Step 4 — Total:**

$$
\boxed{N_{\text{total}} = 13 + 13 + 6 = 37 \text{ physical registers}}
$$

**Step 5 — Sanity check — total bits stored:**

$$
N_{\text{bits}} = 37 \times 32 = 1184 \text{ bits} \approx 148 \text{ bytes}
$$

This is small enough to fit on-chip with negligible die area — a key design decision that makes ARM register banking economically viable.

---

## 3.7 Quick-Reference Decision Tree: When PC Reads as What

```
┌─────────────────────────────────┐
│  Is the processor in ARM state? │
└────────────┬────────────────────┘
             │
      ┌──────┴──────┐
      ▼             ▼
    YES            NO (Thumb)
      │             │
      ▼             ▼
   PC = A + 8    PC = A + 4
```

Where `A` is the address of the currently executing instruction.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** If a question says *"during execution, the PC value is observed as 0x80001014, and the instruction is in ARM state,"* the address of that instruction is `0x80001014 - 8 = 0x8000100C`.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Exception Entry Flow (CPSR/SPSR Swap)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant UserCode as User Mode Code
    participant CPU as ARM CPU Core
    participant NewMode as Exception Mode (e.g. IRQ)
    participant Handler as ISR Handler

    UserCode->>CPU: Normal execution (User mode)
    Note over CPU: IRQ signal arrives

    CPU->>CPU: 1. Save CPSR into SPSR_irq
    CPU->>CPU: 2. Set CPSR M bits to 10010 (IRQ)
    CPU->>CPU: 3. Set CPSR I bit = 1 (mask further IRQ)
    CPU->>CPU: 4. Set CPSR T bit = 0 (force ARM state)
    CPU->>CPU: 5. Save return addr into LR_irq
    CPU->>CPU: 6. PC = 0x00000018 (IRQ vector)

    CPU->>NewMode: Mode switch complete
    NewMode->>Handler: Begin ISR execution
    Handler->>Handler: Push {R0-R3, R12, LR} onto SP_irq
    Handler->>Handler: Process interrupt
    Handler->>Handler: Pop saved registers from SP_irq
    Handler->>CPU: SUBS PC, LR, #4  atomic CPSR restore

    CPU->>CPU: SPSR_irq copied back into CPSR
    CPU->>CPU: Mode returns to User
    CPU->>UserCode: Resume execution after interrupted instruction
```

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> UserMode: Reset vector entry
    UserMode --> FIQMode: FIQ interrupt
    UserMode --> IRQMode: IRQ interrupt
    UserMode --> SupervisorMode: SWI instruction
    UserMode --> AbortMode: Data/Pre-fetch abort
    UserMode --> UndefinedMode: Undefined instruction
    SupervisorMode --> UserMode: MOVS PC, LR (return)
    IRQMode --> UserMode: SUBS PC, LR, #4
    FIQMode --> UserMode: SUBS PC, LR, #4
    AbortMode --> UserMode: SUBS PC, LR, #8
    UndefinedMode --> UserMode: MOVS PC, LR
    UserMode --> SystemMode: Write to CPSR M bits
    SystemMode --> UserMode: Write to CPSR M bits
    SupervisorMode --> [*]: Reset

    note right of UserMode: M = 10000\nNo SPSR\nBanked R13/R14 usr
    note right of FIQMode: M = 10001\nR8-R14 banked\nSPSR_fiq
    note right of SupervisorMode: M = 10011\nSPSR_svc\nFor OS kernel
```
