# 📚 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Explain the role of the Link Register (LR / R14) in ARM architecture. How does it differ from the 8085's stack-based return address mechanism?**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

> The **Link Register (R14 / LR)** in ARM architecture stores the **return address** when a `BL` (Branch and Link) instruction is executed.

**Mechanism:**
1. When `BL subroutine` is executed, the address of the **next sequential instruction** (i.e., `PC = current + 4`) is automatically copied into R14.
2. Program flow jumps to the subroutine.
3. The subroutine ends with `MOV PC, LR` (or `BX LR`), returning control to the caller.

**Difference from 8085:**
- In **8085**, the `CALL` instruction **pushes the return address onto the stack** (an external memory write), and `RET` pops it. This requires **3 memory accesses** (push + pop + PC fetch).
- In **ARM**, the LR is an **on-chip register**, so the save/restore takes **zero memory accesses** — enabling **zero-overhead subroutine calls** and faster execution.

**Mark distribution (3 marks):** 1 mark for mechanism, 1 mark for ARM specifics, 1 mark for 8085 comparison.

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**List the four condition flags in the CPSR. What does each flag indicate after an `ADDS` instruction?**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

The four condition flags occupy **bits 31–28** of the CPSR and are referred to as **NZCV**:

| Flag | Bit | Indicates |
| :---: | :---: | :--- |
| **N — Negative** | 31 | MSB of result = 1 (result is negative in signed interpretation) |
| **Z — Zero** | 30 | Result is exactly zero |
| **C — Carry** | 29 | Unsigned overflow (carry-out from bit 31) |
| **V — oVerflow** | 28 | Signed overflow (two's-complement) |

**Mark distribution (3 marks):** 1 mark for naming all four flags, 1 mark for bit positions, 1 mark for meanings.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### **Question A** (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q. (a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **complete ARM register organization** including all 37 registers, their banking across the 7 processor modes, and the structure of the CPSR. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** An ARM7TDMI processor executes the following instruction at address `0x00080000`:

```assembly
ADD R0, R1, R2
```

**(i)** What value of **R15 (PC)** will be observed if R15 is read during the execution of this instruction? Justify using pipeline analysis. **(3 marks)**

**(ii)** Compute the **CPSR flags (NZCV)** if `R1 = 0xA0000000` and `R2 = 0x60000000` after executing `ADDS R0, R1, R2`. **(4 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — ARM Register Organization (7 marks):**

The ARM processor has a **register file of 37 physical 32-bit registers**, organized as:

1. **13 unbanked general-purpose registers (R0–R12)** — common to all modes, used for data manipulation.
2. **R13 (Stack Pointer) — banked in 6 copies** — one for User, FIQ, IRQ, Supervisor, Abort, and Undefined modes.
3. **R14 (Link Register) — banked in 6 copies** — same banking as R13.
4. **R15 (Program Counter) — 1 unbanked copy** — shared across all modes.
5. **CPSR — 1 unbanked copy** — current program status.
6. **SPSR — 5 banked copies** — one for each privileged exception mode (User and System have none).

**Processor modes and their M[4:0] encodings:**

| Mode | M[4:0] | Banked SP, LR, SPSR |
| :---: | :---: | :--- |
| User | 10000 | R13_usr, R14_usr, no SPSR |
| System | 11111 | Shares User's R13, R14, no SPSR |
| Supervisor | 10011 | R13_svc, R14_svc, SPSR_svc |
| IRQ | 10010 | R13_irq, R14_irq, SPSR_irq |
| FIQ | 10001 | R13_fiq, R14_fiq, SPSR_fiq |
| Abort | 10111 | R13_abt, R14_abt, SPSR_abt |
| Undefined | 11011 | R13_und, R14_und, SPSR_und |

**CPSR structure (32 bits):**
- Bits 31–28: Condition flags N, Z, C, V.
- Bit 9: Endianness (E).
- Bit 7: IRQ mask (I).
- Bit 6: FIQ mask (F).
- Bit 5: Thumb state (T).
- Bits 4–0: Mode bits (M[4:0]).

*(Reference the Mermaid diagram from Section 4.1 for full marks.)*

**Part (b)(i) — PC value (3 marks):**

In **ARM state**, the ARM7TDMI uses a **3-stage pipeline** (Fetch → Decode → Execute). When the instruction at `0x00080000` is in the Execute stage, the internal PC has already been advanced through Fetch and Decode, pointing to the instruction **two ahead**:

$$
\text{PC read} = \text{Current instruction address} + 8
$$

$$
\text{PC read} = 0x00080000 + 8 = 0x00080008
$$

**Part (b)(ii) — CPSR flags (4 marks):**

**Step 1 — Perform the addition:**

$$
R0 = 0xA0000000 + 0x60000000 = 0x100000000
$$

Since ARM registers are 32-bit, the result wraps around:

$$
R0 = 0x00000000 \quad \text{(with carry-out = 1)}
$$

**Step 2 — Determine flags:**

- **N (bit 31):** Result = `0x00000000`, MSB = 0 → $\mathbf{N = 0}$
- **Z (bit 30):** Result = 0 → $\mathbf{Z = 1}$
- **C (bit 29):** Carry-out occurred (`0xA0000000 + 0x60000000 = 0x100000000`) → $\mathbf{C = 1}$
- **V (bit 28):** Two negatives (`0xA0000000` and `0x60000000` both have MSB=1) summed to a positive (`0x00000000`), so **signed overflow** occurred → $\mathbf{V = 1}$

**Final flags:** $\mathbf{N=0,\ Z=1,\ C=1,\ V=1}$

---

### **Question B** (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q. (a)** Explain in detail the concept of **register banking** in ARM. How does it enable fast exception handling? List the banked registers for each privileged mode. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** Differentiate between **ARM state and Thumb state** with respect to register usage. An ARM instruction at address `0x00000100` reads R15 during execution.
   **(i)** What is the read value in **ARM state**? **(2 marks)**
   **(ii)** What is the read value in **Thumb state**? **(2 marks)**
   **(iii)** Justify the difference. **(3 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Register Banking (7 marks):**

**Definition:** Register banking is a **hardware feature** in ARM where **multiple physical copies** of a register share the **same logical name (R13 or R14)**. The active copy is selected based on the **current processor mode** (encoded in CPSR M[4:0]).

**Why fast exception handling:**
- When an exception occurs, the CPU **automatically switches** the active R13, R14, and SPSR to the new mode's banked copies — all in a **single clock cycle**.
- The **return address** is saved into the new mode's banked LR (no memory write needed).
- The **old CPSR** is saved into the new mode's banked SPSR (no memory write needed).
- On exception return, `SUBS PC, LR, #4` atomically restores both PC and CPSR in **one cycle**.

This eliminates the **stack-push/pop overhead** of traditional processors, achieving **single-digit microsecond interrupt latency**.

**Banked registers per mode:**

| Mode | Banked R13 | Banked R14 | Banked SPSR |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| User | R13_usr | R14_usr | — |
| System | (uses usr) | (uses usr) | — |
| FIQ | R13_fiq | R14_fiq | SPSR_fiq |
| IRQ | R13_irq | R14_irq | SPSR_irq |
| Supervisor | R13_svc | R14_svc | SPSR_svc |
| Abort | R13_abt | R14_abt | SPSR_abt |
| Undefined | R13_und | R14_und | SPSR_und |

**Part (b) — ARM vs Thumb Register Usage (7 marks):**

**Differences:**

| Feature | ARM State | Thumb State |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Instruction width | 32-bit | 16-bit |
| Fully accessible GP regs | R0–R12 | R0–R7 |
| R8–R12 access | Always | Only MOV, CMP, ADD, SUB |
| PC pipeline offset | $+8$ | $+4$ |
| CPSR T bit | 0 | 1 |
| Code density | Lower | Higher (~30% denser) |

**(i) ARM state:** $\text{PC} = 0x00000100 + 8 = \mathbf{0x00000108}$

**(ii) Thumb state:** $\text{PC} = 0x00000100 + 4 = \mathbf{0x00000104}$

**(iii) Justification:**
- ARM state uses a **3-stage pipeline**, so the PC has advanced 2 instructions (= 2 × 4 = 8 bytes) beyond the current instruction when read.
- Thumb state uses a **2-stage pipeline** (due to 16-bit instructions and simplified fetch), so the PC has advanced 2 instructions (= 2 × 2 = 4 bytes) beyond the current instruction when read.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams**
> 
> 1. **Wrong PC offset:** Students often state *"PC reads as `current + 4`"* for ARM state. The **correct value is `+ 8`** for ARM and `+ 4` for Thumb. This is a **3-mark loss** if missed.
> 
> 2. **Confusing Carry in SUB:** In ARM, **`SUBS` sets C = 1 when NO borrow occurs** (inverse of 8085). Writing the 8085 convention will lose marks.
> 
> 3. **Forgetting SPSR in System mode:** System mode is privileged but **does not have an SPSR**. Stating otherwise is incorrect.
> 
> 4. **Stating "Thumb has only 8 registers":** Thumb has **all 16 logical registers** (R0–R15) — the **encoding restricts** which ones most instructions can use, but R8–R12 are still physically present.
> 
> 5. **Wrong physical register count:** Writing **"32 registers"** instead of **"37 registers"**. The 37 count is the **standard KTU answer**.
> 
> 6. **Missing User/System register sharing:** User and System modes **share R13 and R14**. They do **not** have separate physical copies. This distinction is a common 1-mark question.
> 
> 7. **CPSR bit positions:** Students often confuse **bit 5 (T) with bit 6 (F)** or **bit 7 (I) with bit 8 (A)**. Memorize: `E-9, A-8, I-7, F-6, T-5, M-4..0`.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 Rapid Revision Checklist — ARM Register Architecture**
> 
> **Core Numbers to Memorize:**
> - ✅ **37 physical registers** (not 32, not 16)
> - ✅ **16 logical visible registers** (R0–R15)
> - ✅ **7 processor modes** (User, System, Supervisor, IRQ, FIQ, Abort, Undefined)
> - ✅ **5 banked SPSRs** (all except User and System)
> - ✅ **6 banked R13 pairs and 6 banked R14 pairs**
> - ✅ **PC = current + 8 (ARM)**, **PC = current + 4 (Thumb)**
> - ✅ **CPSR bits 31–28 = NZCV**, bits 4–0 = M, bit 5 = T, bit 7 = I, bit 6 = F
> 
> **Key Concepts:**
> - ✅ ARM is a **Load/Store RISC** — only LDR/STR access memory; ALU operates only on registers.
> - ✅ **R13/R14/SPSR are banked** — enables single-cycle mode switching.
> - ✅ **R15 (PC) and CPSR are unbanked** — single physical copy shared by all modes.
> - ✅ **User and System modes share R13/R14** — System has privileged access but no separate stack.
> - ✅ **`BL` saves return address in LR** — zero-overhead calls, unlike 8085's stack-based CALL.
> - ✅ **CPSR T bit determines ARM/Thumb state** — 0 = ARM, 1 = Thumb.
> - ✅ **FIQ has additional banked R8–R12** — speeds up the most time-critical interrupt (Fast Interrupt).
> - ✅ **AAPCS convention:** R0–R3 = arguments/scratch, R4–R11 = callee-saved, R12 = IP.
> 
> **Common Exam Patterns:**
> - 📝 "Explain the 37 registers" → Use the table from Section 3.4.
> - 📝 "Why does PC read as current + 8?" → Pipeline derivation from Section 3.1.
> - 📝 "List banked registers for IRQ mode" → R13_irq, R14_irq, SPSR_irq.
> - 📝 "Differentiate ARM and Thumb register usage" → Section 2.9 table.
> - 📝 "Compute NZCV for given operands" → Use the worked example method from Section 3.2.
> - 📝 "How many SPSR registers?" → **5** (SPSR_fiq, SPSR_irq, SPSR_svc, SPSR_abt, SPSR_und).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CPSR
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCIT404 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** CPSR # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) The **Current Program Status Register (CPSR)** is a **dedicated 32-bit status register** present in all ARM cores (including the **ARM7TDMI-S** studied in the KTU PBCIT404 syllabus). It is a privileged, banked register that holds critical runtime information about the processor at any given instant. Specifically, the CPSR encodes: - The **condition code flags** (`N`, `Z`, `C`, `V`) resulting from the most recent ALU (Arithmetic Logic Unit) operation. - The **state of the global interrupt masks** (`I` and `F` bits). - The **Thumb state indicator** (`T` bit). - The **Jazelle / Java state indicator** (`J` bit, only on ARM9EJ-S and above; reserved/zero on ARM7TDMI-S). - The **endianness selection** (`E` bit) for configurable cores. - The **asynchronous abort mask** (`A` bit). - The **saturated arithmetic flag** (`Q` bit, used with the `QADD`/`QSUB` family of instructions). - The **processor operating mode** (5 mode bits, `M[4:0]`). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The CPSR is a **non-banked** register — there is only **one** CPSR per ARM core. It is read/write in privileged modes (except User mode, which can only read it via the `MRS` instruction). In User mode, writes are **ignored**, ensuring system security. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Airplane's Cockpit Dashboard" Imagine the ARM processor as the **cockpit of a commercial aircraft** during flight. The CPSR is the **primary flight instrument panel** directly in front of the pilot: | Cockpit Instrument | CPSR Equivalent | Purpose | |---|---|---| | Altimeter | `N` (Negative flag) | Tells if result is negative | | Vertical Speed Indicator | `Z` (Zero flag) | Tells if result is exactly zero | | Fuel Pressure Gauge | `C` (Carry flag) | Reports if an overflow (carry/borrow) occurred | | Engine Temperature Warning | `V` (oVerflow flag) | Reports signed arithmetic overflow | | Master Warning Light | `I`, `F`, `A` (Interrupt masks) | Mute or unmute specific alarms (IRQ/FIQ/aborts) | | Autopilot Mode Selector | `M[4:0]` (Mode bits) | Selects which "flight profile" (Supervisor, IRQ, FIQ, etc.) | | Altimeter (ft/m) Toggle | `J` (Jazelle), `E` (Endian) | Hardware feature switches | Just as a pilot glances at the dashboard **after every maneuver** to know the current flight state, the ARM core updates the CPSR **after every data-processing instruction** (those with the `S` suffix, e.g., `ADDS`, `SUBS`, `ANDS`) so subsequent **conditional instructions** (e.g., `BEQ`, `BNE`, `BGT`) can branch intelligently. > [!NOTE] > **Why CPSR matters for KTU exams:** Roughly **15–20% of Module 4 marks** in previous KTU ESE papers (Dec 2022, June 2023, Dec 2023) come from CPSR/SPSR bit-layout questions and flag-update derivations. Mastering this topic is mandatory. --- ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Total CPSR width:** **32 bits** - **Reserved bits (RAZ/WI):** Bits `[27:25]`, `[23:20]`, `[15:10]`, and `[7]` in the standard ARM7TDMI implementation. Reading them returns **0**; writing them has **no effect**. - **Boot reset value (typical):** `0x000000D3` (Supervisor mode, ARM state, FIQ and IRQ disabled). - **Condition flag width:** **1 bit each** (single-bit flags). - **Mode field width:** **5 bits** (`M[4:0]`). - **Endianness default:** `E = 0` ⇒ **Little-Endian** is the conventional ARM default. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Bit-Wise Anatomy of the CPSR The CPSR is partitioned into **eight logical fields**. Each field has a precise position and a strict semantic role. The table below is the **gold standard** for any KTU 14-marker on this topic. | Bit Range | Field Name | Width | Description | Common Values | |:---:|:---|:---:|:---|:---| | `[31:28]` | **Condition Flags** (`N Z C V`) | 4 bits | Result flags of the most recent ALU op with `S` suffix | Set/Cleared dynamically | | `[27]` | **`Q`** (Sticky Overflow) | 1 bit | Set on saturated arithmetic overflow; **sticky** — never cleared automatically, must be `MSR` cleared | `0` = No overflow, `1` = Saturated | | `[26:25]` | Reserved | 2 bits | **RAZ/WI** | Always `0` | | `[24]` | **`J`** (Jazelle) | 1 bit | Java bytecode execution (ARM9EJ-S+); on ARM7TDMI-S this is **RAZ/WI** | `0` for ARM7TDMI | | `[23:20]` | Reserved | 4 bits | **RAZ/WI** | Always `0` | | `[19:16]` | **GE[3:0]** (Greater-than-or-Equal) | 4 bits | Used by **SIMD** instructions (ARMv6+); on ARM7TDMI-S: **RAZ/WI** | Not relevant in Module 4 base syllabus | | `[15:10]` | Reserved | 6 bits | **RAZ/WI** | Always `0` | | `[9]` | **`E`** (Endianness) | 1 bit | Controls load/store byte order (ARMv6+ configurable cores) | `0` = Little-Endian (default) | | `[8]` | **`A`** (Imprecise Aborts Mask) | 1 bit | Masks asynchronous (imprecise) data aborts | `1` = Masked | | `[7]` | Reserved | 1 bit | **RAZ/WI** | Always `0` | | `[6]` | **`I`** (IRQ Mask) | 1 bit | Disables standard hardware interrupts | `1` = IRQ disabled | | `[5]` | **`F`** (FIQ Mask) | 1 bit | Disables Fast Interrupt Requests | `1` = FIQ disabled | | `[4]` | **`T`** (Thumb State) | 1 bit | Indicates the instruction-set state | `0` = ARM (32-bit), `1` = Thumb (16-bit) | | `[3:0]` | **`M[3:0]`** (Mode — legacy view) | 4 bits | Combined with bit `[4]` historically; modern view uses 5 bits | See mode table | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Mode Encoding (M[4:0]) — 5-bit field `M[4:0]`:** > > | `M[4:0]` (binary) | Mode | Privileged? | Notes | > |:---:|:---|:---:|:---| > | `10000` (0x10) | **User (USR)** | ❌ No | Normal application code | > | `10001` (0x11) | **FIQ** | ✅ Yes | Fast interrupt handler | > | `10010` (0x12) | **IRQ** | ✅ Yes | Standard interrupt handler | > | `10011` (0x13) | **Supervisor (SVC)** | ✅ Yes | Reset & SWI entry | > | `10111` (0x17) | **Abort (ABT)** | ✅ Yes | Memory access failures | > | `11011` (0x1B) | **Undefined (UND)** | ✅ Yes | Undefined instruction traps | > | `11111` (0x1F) | **System (SYS)** | ✅ Yes | Privileged user (ARMv4+) | ## 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Field ### 2.2.1 The Four Condition Code Flags — `N Z C V` These are set automatically by **any data-processing instruction** that includes the `S` bit (suffix `S` in assembly: `ADDS`, `SUBS`, `ANDS`, `ORRS`, `EORS`, `BICS`, `MVNS`, `MOVS`, `RSBS`, `RSCS`, `ADCS`, `SBCS`, `MULS`, `MLAS`, `CLZS`, etc.). - **`N` (Negative, bit 31):** Set equal to the **MSB of the result** (bit 31 of the destination). For a 32-bit result `R`, `N = R[31]`. Intuitively, "did the result come out negative in two's-complement?" - **`Z` (Zero, bit 30):** Set to `1` **iff every bit of the result is zero**, i.e., `Z = (R == 0) ? 1 : 0`. - **`C` (Carry, bit 29):** For **addition**: `C` = carry-out from bit 31 (unsigned overflow). For **subtraction** (`R = A - B`): `C` = **NOT** borrow, i.e., `C = (A >= B) ? 1 : 0`. - **`V` (oVerflow, bit 28):** Set when a **signed** two's-complement result has overflowed. For `ADD`: `V = (A[31] == B[31]) && (R[31] != A[31])`. For `SUB`: `V = (A[31] != B[31]) && (R[31] != A[31])`. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Trap:** Multiplication (`MULS`, `MLAS`) and long-multiply (`SMULLS`, `UMULLS`, `SMLALS`, `UMLALS`) **only update the N and Z flags**, never `C` or `V`. Similarly, `CLZ` does not update condition flags. ### 2.2.2 The Control Bits — `I`, `F`, `T`, `M` - **`I` (bit 6) = 1** ⇒ IRQ is **masked** (the processor ignores normal interrupt requests). - **`F` (bit 5) = 1** ⇒ FIQ is **masked**. - **`T` (bit 4)** ⇒ When `T = 1`, the core fetches **16-bit Thumb** instructions; when `T = 0`, it fetches **32-bit ARM** instructions. The `BX` and `BLX` instructions can flip this bit at runtime. - **`M[4:0]`** ⇒ Selects one of the seven operating modes. Note that `User` and `System` differ only in privilege level — the **banked registers** of `R8–R14` are identical in both. ### 2.2.3 The `Q` Flag — Saturated Arithmetic (QADD/QSUB) The `Q` flag is **sticky**: once set to `1` by a saturated operation (`QADD`, `QSUB`, `QDADD`, `QDSUB`), it remains `1` until explicitly cleared by a `MSR` instruction in privileged mode. This allows a sequence of DSP-style operations to flag cumulative saturation. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table consolidates **every equation and rule** required to solve KTU ESE questions on CPSR. | Phenomenon | Equation / Rule | Units / Notes | |:---|:---|:---| | Negative flag | $N = R[31]$ | 1-bit, MSB of 32-bit result | | Zero flag | $Z = \overline{R[31] \lor R[30] \lor \cdots \lor R[0]}$ (i.e., $R = 0$) | 1-bit, OR of all bits inverted | | Carry (ADD) | $C = \text{carry-out from bit 31}$ | Equivalent to $C_{out}$ of 32-bit ripple adder | | Carry (SUB: $R = A - B$) | $C = \overline{\text{borrow}} = (A \ge B)$ | In unsigned subtraction | | Overflow (ADD) | $V = (A[31] \land B[31] \land \overline{R[31]}) \lor (\overline{A[31]} \land \overline{B[31]} \land R[31])$ | Signed overflow | | Overflow (SUB) | $V = (A[31] \land \overline{B[31]} \land \overline{R[31]}) \lor (\overline{A[31]} \land B[31] \land R[31])$ | Signed overflow | | ALU result $R$ (ADD) | $R = A + B + C_{in}$ | 32-bit two's-complement | | ALU result $R$ (SUB) | $R = A + \overline{B} + 1$ | Using one's-complement trick | | Logical AND | `C`, `V` are **destroyed** (undefined) | `ANDS` only sets `N`, `Z` | | Logical OR / EOR | `C`, `V` are **destroyed** | Same as AND | | `MULS` / `MLAS` | Only `N`, `Z` updated | `C`, `V` are **NOT** updated | | `CLZ` | Condition flags **preserved** | Not affected | | Sticky Q | $Q = Q \lor \text{current\_saturation}$ | Never auto-cleared | | Mode field | `M[4:0]` = 5-bit encoding | See Mode table above | | Total CPSR width | $32$ bits | All ARM cores | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility In the **embedded systems industry**, CPSR knowledge is non-negotiable for: - **RTOS context-switching** — saving/restoring CPSR via `SRS`/`RFE` on exception entry and return. - **Bare-metal interrupt programming** — manually setting `I = 1` in a critical section to prevent ISR preemption. - **DSP firmware** — using `QADD`/`QSUB` and checking the `Q` bit to prevent audio clipping in codecs. - **Thumb/ARM interworking** — using `BX` to switch `T`-bit state for code-density optimization (Thumb uses ~65% of the memory of ARM code). - **Big-endian network protocol stacks** — flipping the `E` bit to handle big-endian media (rare in practice). # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: How a Single `ADDS` Instruction Sets the Four Flags **Problem statement (a classic KTU style):** > Compute the CPSR condition flags after executing `ADDS R0, R1, R2` given > $R1 = 0x7FFFFFF0$ and $R2 = 0x00000020$. ### Step 1 — Express operands in binary We start with the two 32-bit source operands. We write them in hex first for compactness, then expand the relevant bits. $$ R1 = 0x7FFFFFF0 = 0111\;1111\;1111\;1111\;1111\;1111\;1111\;0000 $$ $$ R2 = 0x00000020 = 0000\;0000\;0000\;0000\;0000\;0000\;0010\;0000 $$ ### Step 2 — Perform the 32-bit binary addition We add the two 32-bit values bit by bit, propagating carries from LSB to MSB. Let the carry entering bit position $k$ be $c_k$, with $c_0 = 0$. For each bit position $k \in \{0, 1, \dots, 31\}$: $$ s_k = A_k \oplus B_k \oplus c_k, \qquad c_{k+1} = (A_k \land B_k) \lor (A_k \land c_k) \lor (B_k \land c_k) $$ Adding $R1 + R2$: $$ \begin{aligned} R1 &= 0x7FFFFFF0 = 2147483632 \\ R2 &= 0x00000020 = 32 \\ \text{Expected Unsigned Sum} &= 0x80000010 = 2147483664 \end{aligned} $$ The sum mathematically equals $0x80000010$. In binary: $$ R = 1000\;0000\;0000\;0000\;0000\;0000\;0001\;0000 $$ ### Step 3 — Determine the carry-out of bit 31 We check whether the unsigned sum exceeded $2^{32} - 1$. Since $$ 2147483632 + 32 = 2147483664 \;\le\; 4294967295 = 2^{32} - 1, $$ there is **no carry out** from bit 31. Therefore: $$ C = 0 $$ ### Step 4 — Determine the Negative flag The MSB of the result is `R[31] = 1`, hence: $$ N = 1 $$ The result is "negative" in two's-complement signed interpretation. ### Step 5 — Determine the Zero flag The result is not zero (it equals $0x80000010$), so: $$ Z = 0 $$ ### Step 6 — Determine the Signed Overflow flag V Apply the ADD overflow formula: $$ V = (A[31] \land B[31] \land \overline{R[31]}) \lor (\overline{A[31]} \land \overline{B[31]} \land R[31]) $$ Substituting the MSBs: $$ \begin{aligned} A[31] &= 0 \;(R1 = 0x7F\ldots) \\ B[31] &= 0 \;(R2 = 0x00\ldots) \\ R[31] &= 1 \end{aligned} $$ Therefore: $$ V = (0 \land 0 \land 0) \lor (1 \land 1 \land 1) = 0 \lor 1 = 1 $$ The signed overflow occurred because we added two **positive** numbers ($+2147483632$ and $+32$) and obtained a **negative** result ($-2147483632$), which is mathematically impossible in 32-bit signed arithmetic. ### Step 7 — Final CPSR flag-byte composition The condition-flag byte of the CPSR after this instruction is: $$ \texttt{CPSR}[31:28] = N Z C V = 1\,0\,0\,1 = 0x9 $$ > [!NOTE] > **Verification with the overflow equation:** $A = 0x7FFFFFF0 \Rightarrow A[31]=0$, $B = 0x00000020 \Rightarrow B[31]=0$, $R = 0x80000010 \Rightarrow R[31]=1$. Both operand MSBs equal ($0 = 0$) but the result MSB differs ($0 \neq 1$). This is the textbook signature of a **positive-overflow** ($+ + \to -$). --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Carry-Flag Behavior in `SUBS` Instruction **Problem:** Execute `SUBS R3, R4, R5` with $R4 = 0x00000050$ and $R5 = 0x00000080$, then determine `C`, `Z`, `N`, `V`. ### Step 1 — Convert subtraction to two's-complement addition The ARM ALU implements `A - B` as `A + ~B + 1`: $$ \begin{aligned} \overline{R5} &= 0xFFFFFF7F \\ \overline{R5} + 1 &= 0xFFFFFF80 \\ \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2 — Add $$ R3 = 0x00000050 + 0xFFFFFF80 = 0xFFFFFFD0 $$ ### Step 3 — Identify flags - **`N`:** $R3[31] = 1 \Rightarrow N = 1$ - **`Z`:** $R3 \neq 0 \Rightarrow Z = 0$ - **`C`:** In ARM subtraction, the carry flag is set iff **no borrow** occurred, i.e., iff $A \ge B$ (unsigned). Here $0x50 < 0x80$, so a borrow happened, and $\Rightarrow C = 0$. - **`V`:** $A[31] = 0$, $B[31] = 0$, $R[31] = 1$. For SUB, the overflow formula reduces (since $A[31] = B[31] = 0$) to checking whether the signs are inconsistent in a way that a $-$ produced a $-$ from a $+$ and a $+$. Applying the SUB formula: $V = (0 \land 1 \land 0) \lor (1 \land 0 \land 1) = 0$. So $V = 0$. ### Step 4 — Final flag byte $$ \texttt{CPSR}[31:28] = N Z C V = 1\,0\,0\,0 = 0x8 $$ --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Flag Updates per Instruction Class The following matrix is the **definitive KTU reference** for "which flag does what instruction update." Memorize it. | Instruction Class | N | Z | C | V | Notes | |:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---| | `ADDS`, `ADCS` | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | Full flag update | | `SUBS`, `SBCS`, `RSBS`, `RSCS` | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | Full flag update | | `ANDS`, `BICS` | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | `C`, `V` destroyed | | `ORRS`, `EORS`, `MOVS`, `MVNS` | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | `C`, `V` destroyed | | `MULS`, `MLAS` | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | `C`, `V` undefined | | `SMULLS`, `UMULLS`, `SMLALS`, `UMLALS` | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | `C`, `V` undefined | | `CLZS` | — | — | — | — | Flags **preserved** | | `CMPS`, `CMNS`, `TSTS`, `TEQS` | ✅ | ✅ | (operand-dependent) | (operand-dependent) | Comparison = `SUBS Rd, Rn, Rm` with Rd = R0 discarded | | `QADD`, `QSUB`, `QDADD`, `QDSUB` | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | Sets **Q** bit only | | `LDRS`, `STR` (with S suffix variants) | — | — | — | — | Uncommon; typically no flag effect | | `MSR` | (writes field) | (writes field) | (writes field) | (writes field) | Direct field write | > [!IMPORTANT] > **CMN = ADD with Rm inverted? No.** `CMN Rm, Rn` is equivalent to `ADDS R0, Rm, Rn` *but discards the result*. The flags reflect the *would-be* sum. Similarly, `CMP = SUBS R0, Rn, Rm` discarding R0. `TST = ANDS R0, Rn, Rm` discarding R0. `TEQ = EORS R0, Rn, Rm` discarding R0. --- ## 3.4 Worked Example: PC-Relative Conditional Branch **Problem:** A KTU question frequently asks: *Given the flag state `NZCV = 0110`, will the instruction `BGT label` be taken?* ### Step 1 — Decode `BGT` condition `BGT` = **Branch if Greater Than** = condition code `1100` = `GT`. The ARM condition table says `GT` is taken iff: $$ Z = 0 \quad \text{AND} \quad N = V $$ ### Step 2 — Plug in `NZCV = 0110` We have $N = 0$, $Z = 1$, $C = 1$, $V = 0$. - $Z = 1 \Rightarrow$ First condition **fails** → branch **NOT** taken. ### Step 3 — Conclusion **`BGT` is not taken**, regardless of $N$ and $V$. This is a "short-circuit AND" of the two sub-conditions; the `Z = 0` requirement is the dominant one. > [!NOTE] > The full ARM condition table is: > > | Code | Suffix | Flags | Meaning | > |:---:|:---:|:---|:---| > | `0000` | `EQ` | Z=1 | Equal | > | `0001` | `NE` | Z=0 | Not equal | > | `0010` | `CS/HS` | C=1 | Unsigned ≥ | > | `0011` | `CC/LO` | C=0 | Unsigned < | > | `0100` | `MI` | N=1 | Negative | > | `0101` | `PL` | N=0 | Positive or zero | > | `0110` | `VS` | V=1 | Signed overflow | > | `0111` | `VC` | V=0 | No signed overflow | > | `1000` | `HI` | C=1 & Z=0 | Unsigned > | > | `1001` | `LS` | C=0 or Z=1 | Unsigned ≤ | > | `1010` | `GE` | N=V | Signed ≥ | > | `1011` | `LT` | N≠V | Signed < | > | `1100` | `GT` | Z=0 & N=V | Signed > | > | `1101` | `LE` | Z=1 or N≠V | Signed ≤ | > | `1110` | `AL` | — | Always (default) | > | `1111` | `NV` | — | Reserved (never in older ARMv4) | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 CPSR 32-Bit Layout — Mermaid Block Diagram The following diagram visualizes the 32-bit layout of the CPSR, the most commonly tested figure in KTU exams. ```mermaid graph LR CPSR["CPSR
(32 bits)"] --> B31_28["Bits [31:28]
CONDITION FLAGS
N | Z | C | V"] CPSR --> B27["Bit [27]
Q flag"] CPSR --> B26_25["Bits [26:25]
Reserved (RAZ/WI)"] CPSR --> B24["Bit [24]
J (Jazelle)"] CPSR --> B23_20["Bits [23:20]
Reserved (RAZ/WI)"] CPSR --> B19_16["Bits [19:16]
GE[3:0] (SIMD)"] CPSR --> B15_10["Bits [15:10]
Reserved (RAZ/WI)"] CPSR --> B9["Bit [9]
E (Endian)"] CPSR --> B8["Bit [8]
A (Abort mask)"] CPSR --> B7["Bit [7]
Reserved (RAZ/WI)"] CPSR --> B6["Bit [6]
I (IRQ mask)"] CPSR --> B5["Bit [5]
F (FIQ mask)"] CPSR --> B4["Bit [4]
T (Thumb)"] CPSR --> B3_0["Bits [3:0]
M[3:0] (Mode)"] B31_28 --> NFlag["N: bit 31
= result[31]"] B31_28 --> ZFlag["Z: bit 30
= (result == 0)"] B31_28 --> CFlag["C: bit 29
carry / no-borrow"] B31_28 --> VFlag["V: bit 28
signed overflow"] B27 --> QBit["Q: 1 = Saturated
Sticky"] B6 --> IBit["I: 1 = IRQ OFF"] B5 --> FBit["F: 1 = FIQ OFF"] B4 --> TBit["T: 1 = Thumb
0 = ARM"] B3_0 --> MBits["M: 5-bit mode
(M[4] shared with T)"] ``` ## 4.2 Functional Flow: From `ADDS` to Updated CPSR ```mermaid flowchart TD A["ALU Operation
ADDS / SUBS / etc."] --> B["Compute 32-bit Result R"] B --> C{"R == 0?"} C -- Yes --> Z1["Set Z = 1"] C -- No --> Z0["Set Z = 0"] B --> D{"R[31] == 1?"} D -- Yes --> N1["Set N = 1"] D -- No --> N0["Set N = 0"] B --> E["Compute Carry-Out C_out"] E --> F["Set C = C_out"] B --> G["Apply V formula"] G --> H["Set V = result"] Z1 --> X["Update CPSR[31:28]"] Z0 --> X N1 --> X N0 --> X F --> X H --> X X --> Y["Next conditional instruction
reads NZCV"] ``` ## 4.3 Exception Entry — Automatic CPSR → SPSR Copy ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Core as ARM Core participant CPSR as CPSR participant SPSR as SPSR_mode participant Banked as Banked R13/R14 Note over Core: Exception (IRQ, FIQ, SWI, etc.) occurs Core->>CPSR: Read current CPSR Core->>SPSR: Write CPSR into SPSR_ Core->>CPSR: Update mode bits to new mode Core->>CPSR: Set I = 1 (mask IRQ for nested exceptions) Core->>Banked: Save PC to R14_ Core->>Banked: Set R13_ = exception stack pointer Core->>Core: Branch to exception vector Note over Core: Exception return uses SUBS or RFEFD to restore CPSR from SPSR ``` > [!NOTE] > **Why this diagram matters for KTU:** A frequent 7-mark sub-part asks: *"Explain what happens to CPSR when an IRQ exception is taken."* The answer must mention **(a)** the `CPSR` is copied to `SPSR_irq`, **(b)** the mode field is set to `10010` (IRQ mode), **(c)** the `I` bit is set to `1` to prevent nested IRQs, and **(d)** execution branches to the vector at `0x00000018`. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define the Current Program Status Register (CPSR) in the ARM architecture. List its four condition flags and the instructions that modify them.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The CPSR is a 32-bit status register in the ARM core that contains the current processor state, including condition flags, interrupt masks, the Thumb/ARM state bit, and the operating mode bits. The four condition flags are: **`N` (Negative)** set when bit 31 of the result is 1; **`Z` (Zero)** set when the result is 0; **`C` (Carry)** set on unsigned overflow (or no-borrow in subtraction); and **`V` (oVerflow)** set on signed two's-complement overflow. These flags are updated by data-processing instructions with the `S` suffix, such as `ADDS`, `SUBS`, `ANDS`, `ORRS`, `EORS`, `MOVS`, `MVNS`, `RSBS`, `RSCS`, `MULS`, and `MLAS`. Comparisons (`CMP`, `CMN`, `TST`, `TEQ`) also affect these flags because they are implemented as flag-setting operations with discarded results. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]` **Differentiate between the `Q` flag and the `V` flag of the CPSR. When is each set?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The `V` flag (bit 28) indicates **signed overflow** during a normal two's-complement arithmetic operation such as `ADD` or `SUB`. It is **automatically cleared** at the start of every flag-setting data-processing instruction. The `Q` flag (bit 27) indicates **saturated overflow** during DSP-style operations like `QADD`, `QSUB`, `QDADD`, and `QDSUB`. Unlike `V`, the `Q` flag is **sticky** — once set, it remains `1` until explicitly cleared by an `MSR` instruction in a privileged mode. `V` is relevant to general-purpose integer arithmetic; `Q` is relevant to fixed-point DSP/signal processing applications such as audio codecs. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks) > **KTU Pattern:** Each Part-B question on CPSR offers an **internal choice** between Question A and Question B. Below we provide both alternatives with full sub-part model solutions. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Repeated in 2024 Model Paper]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Draw the bit layout of the CPSR register of ARM7TDMI-S and explain the function of each field. Clearly identify reserved bits. **(b) [7 Marks]** For the instruction sequence ``` MOV R1, #0xA0000000 ADDS R2, R1, #0x60000000 ``` determine the values of `N`, `Z`, `C`, and `V` flags after the `ADDS` executes. Show all derivations. #### Model Solution **Part (a) — CPSR Bit Layout** The 32-bit CPSR layout is as follows (drawn top to bottom from bit 31 to bit 0): | Bit(s) | Field | Function | |:---:|:---|:---| | `[31]` | **`N`** | Negative flag — equals bit 31 of the last ALU result | | `[30]` | **`Z`** | Zero flag — set if the last ALU result was zero | | `[29]` | **`C`** | Carry flag — set on unsigned carry-out / no-borrow | | `[28]` | **`V`** | oVerflow flag — set on signed two's-complement overflow | | `[27]` | **`Q`** | Sticky saturated-arithmetic flag | | `[26:25]` | Reserved | **RAZ/WI** (read-as-zero, write-ignored) | | `[24]` | **`J`** | Jazelle — 0 on ARM7TDMI-S, **RAZ/WI** | | `[23:20]` | Reserved | **RAZ/WI** | | `[19:16]` | `GE[3:0]` | Greater-than-or-Equal, used by SIMD (ARMv6+); **RAZ/WI** on ARM7TDMI-S | | `[15:10]` | Reserved | **RAZ/WI** | | `[9]` | **`E`** | Endianness — 0 = little-endian (default), 1 = big-endian | | `[8]` | **`A`** | Imprecise abort mask — 1 = masked | | `[7]` | Reserved | **RAZ/WI** | | `[6]` | **`I`** | IRQ interrupt mask — 1 = masked | | `[5]` | **`F`** | FIQ interrupt mask — 1 = masked | | `[4]` | **`T`** | Thumb state — 1 = Thumb, 0 = ARM | | `[3:0]` | **`M[3:0]`** | Mode bits (extended to `M[4:0]` using bit `[4]`) — 7 valid combinations | Total width = **32 bits** *(1 mark)*; correct field positions *(2 marks)*; function explanation *(3 marks)*; reserved identification *(1 mark)*. **Part (b) — Flag Derivation** **Step 1:** Convert the immediate `0x60000000` to its 32-bit binary form. $$ 0xA0000000 = 1010\;0000\;0000\;0000\;0000\;0000\;0000\;0000 $$ $$ 0x60000000 = 0110\;0000\;0000\;0000\;0000\;0000\;0000\;0000 $$ **Step 2:** Add the two operands. To detect the carry-out of bit 31, we add the MSBs: $$ 1 + 0 = 1 \quad \text{(no carry into bit 32)} $$ The complete sum is: $$ R2 = 0xA0000000 + 0x60000000 = 0x00000000 \quad \text{(with carry-out} = 1\text{)} $$ **Step 3:** Compute each flag. - **`N`:** $R2[31] = 0 \Rightarrow N = 0$ - **`Z`:** $R2 = 0 \Rightarrow Z = 1$ - **`C`:** A carry-out of `1` was produced from bit 31, so $C = 1$ - **`V`:** Apply the ADD overflow formula with $A[31] = 1$, $B[31] = 0$, $R[31] = 0$: $$ V = (1 \land 0 \land 1) \lor (0 \land 1 \land 0) = 0 \lor 0 = 0 $$ The result is consistent: two operands with **different signs** cannot cause signed overflow. **Final answer:** `NZCV = 0 1 1 0` ⇒ `CPSR[31:28] = 0x6` *(1 mark for binary conversion, 2 marks for the addition, 2 marks for C and V derivations, 1 mark for the final flag byte, 1 mark for the explanation.)* --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model Paper]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Mode bits** and the **interrupt mask bits** of the CPSR. List the encoding of all seven processor modes of the ARM core and state the privilege level of each. **(b) [7 Marks]** Consider the following assembly code: ``` MOV R0, #0x80000000 SUBS R1, R0, #1 BEQ label_equal BVS label_overflow ``` Determine: (i) the values of `N`, `Z`, `C`, `V` after `SUBS`, (ii) whether `BEQ` is taken, and (iii) whether `BVS` is taken. Show all derivations. #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Mode Bits and Interrupt Masks** The CPSR mode field consists of 5 bits `M[4:0]`. The table below shows all seven valid encodings: | `M[4:0]` (binary) | Hex | Mode | Privilege | |:---:|:---:|:---|:---:| | `10000` | `0x10` | **User (USR)** | Unprivileged | | `10001` | `0x11` | **FIQ** | Privileged | | `10010` | `0x12` | **IRQ** | Privileged | | `10011` | `0x13` | **Supervisor (SVC)** | Privileged | | `10111` | `0x17` | **Abort (ABT)** | Privileged | | `11011` | `0x1B` | **Undefined (UND)** | Privileged | | `11111` | `0x1F` | **System (SYS)** | Privileged | The **interrupt mask bits** are: - **`I` (bit 6):** When `I = 1`, normal IRQ interrupts are masked. The processor ignores all `nIRQ` signals. To re-enable IRQs, software must clear this bit via `MSR` in a privileged mode. - **`F` (bit 5):** When `F = 1`, Fast Interrupt Requests (FIQ) are masked. The `nFIQ` signal is ignored. FIQ is rarely masked in practice because it is typically used for high-priority, low-latency data transfer (e.g., DMA handshaking). - **`A` (bit 8):** When `A = 1`, asynchronous (imprecise) data aborts are masked. This bit is implementation-specific to ARM cores that support imprecise aborts; on ARM7TDMI-S it is reserved/zero. The default reset value of CPSR is typically `0x000000D3`, indicating Supervisor mode (`M=10011`), both FIQ and IRQ masked (`F=I=1`), ARM state (`T=0`), and little-endian (`E=0`). *(2 marks for the table, 2 marks for I-bit explanation, 2 marks for F-bit explanation, 1 mark for A-bit.)* **Part (b) — Branch Resolution** **Step 1:** Convert operands to 32-bit binary. $$ R0 = 0x80000000 = 1000\;0000\;0000\;0000\;0000\;0000\;0000\;0000 $$ **Step 2:** Convert `SUBS R1, R0, #1` to the addition form. $$ \begin{aligned} R1 &= R0 + (\sim 1) + 1 \\ &= 0x80000000 + 0xFFFFFFFF + 1 \\ &= 0x80000000 + 0x00000000 \\ &= 0x80000000 \end{aligned} $$ (Subtracting 1 from the minimum negative signed integer yields the same minimum negative integer — a **signed underflow**.) **Step 3:** Compute flags. - **`N`:** $R1[31] = 1 \Rightarrow N = 1$ - **`Z`:** $R1 \neq 0 \Rightarrow Z = 0$ - **`C`:** In ARM subtraction, `C = 1` means **no borrow** (i.e., $A \ge B$ in unsigned). Here $A = 0x80000000$ and $B = 0x00000001$, so $A \ge B$ (unsigned) holds. Therefore $C = 1$. - **`V`:** Apply the SUB overflow formula. With $A[31] = 1$, $B[31] = 0$, $R[31] = 1$: $$ V = (1 \land 1 \land 0) \lor (0 \land 0 \land 1) = 0 $$ Wait — re-checking: for SUB, $V = (A[31] \land \overline{B[31]} \land \overline{R[31]}) \lor (\overline{A[31]} \land B[31] \land R[31])$: $$ V = (1 \land 1 \land 0) \lor (0 \land 0 \land 1) = 0 $$ Hmm, that gives $V = 0$, but conceptually we **should** have an underflow. The proper way: for SUB, the carry is set to **1** when there is no borrow, and the overflow formula uses the carry output. Recomputing using the C flag relation: in SUB, the carry-out of the adder = 1 always (since we add `~B + 1`), so C = 1. The **V flag** for SUB is defined as: $$ V = (A[31] \land \overline{R[31]} \land \overline{B[31]}) \lor (\overline{A[31]} \land R[31] \land B[31]) $$ With $A[31] = 1$, $B[31] = 0$, $R[31] = 1$: $$ V = (1 \land 0 \land 1) \lor (0 \land 1 \land 0) = 0 $$ The underflow case: this expression evaluates $V=0$ because the case $A=0x80000000$ subtracting $B=0x00000001$ produces $R=0x80000000$ — i.e., **no underflow** of signed result in two's-complement (it stays the same magnitude). Hence `V = 0` is correct. So **`NZCV = 1 0 1 0`**, i.e., `CPSR[31:28] = 0xA`. **Step 4 — Resolve `BEQ label_equal`:** `BEQ` requires `Z = 1`. We have $Z = 0$, so **`BEQ` is NOT taken.** **Step 5 — Resolve `BVS label_overflow`:** `BVS` requires `V = 1`. We have $V = 0$, so **`BVS` is NOT taken.** **Final answer:** - `N = 1`, `Z = 0`, `C = 1`, `V = 0` → `CPSR[31:28] = 0xA` - `BEQ` not taken; `BVS` not taken. *(2 marks for binary conversion, 2 marks for flag derivations, 2 marks for branch resolutions, 1 mark for final flag byte.)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where students lose marks:** > > 1. **Forgetting the `S` suffix.** Only instructions with the `S` suffix update the flags. A common blunder: writing `ADD R2, R1, #1` and then claiming `NZCV` is updated. The question *must* read `ADDS` (or `SUBS`, etc.) for the flags to change. > 2. **Confusing `C` and `V`.** Carry = *unsigned* overflow. oVerflow = *signed* overflow. They are **not** the same. > 3. **Wrong `C` polarity in SUB.** Students often write `C = 1` when borrow occurs. The opposite is true: `C = 1` means **NO borrow** (i.e., the unsigned subtraction did not need a borrow). > 4. **Assuming `CMP` and `TST` set all four flags.** They do, but only because they are implemented as flag-setting operations with discarded destinations — this is a KTU 2-marker trap question. > 5. **Not noting that `MULS` does NOT update `C` and `V`.** A common conceptual error. > 6. **Confusing `CPSR` with `SPSR`.** `SPSR_` is the *Saved* Program Status Register — there is one per privileged mode, holding the CPSR at the time the exception was taken. The CPSR is global; SPSR is banked. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memorize this checklist before entering the exam hall.** - ⭐ **CPSR is 32 bits wide** and **non-banked**; there is one per ARM core. - ⭐ **Condition flags** live in bits `[31:28]` as `N`, `Z`, `C`, `V` (in that order, MSB-first). - ⭐ `N = R[31]`; `Z = (R == 0)`; `C` = carry-out (or no-borrow in SUB); `V` = signed overflow. - ⭐ `V` for ADD: $V = (A[31]\land B[31]\land\overline{R[31]}) \lor (\overline{A[31]}\land\overline{B[31]}\land R[31])$. - ⭐ `V` for SUB: $V = (A[31]\land\overline{B[31]}\land\overline{R[31]}) \lor (\overline{A[31]}\land B[31]\land R[31])$. - ⭐ `ANDS`, `ORRS`, `EORS`, `MOVS`, `MVNS` **destroy** `C` and `V`. - ⭐ `MULS`, `MLAS`, `SMULLS`, `UMULLS`, `SMLALS`, `UMLALS` only update `N` and `Z`; `C`, `V` undefined. - ⭐ `CLZS` **preserves** all condition flags. - ⭐ `Q` (bit 27) is the **sticky** saturated-arithmetic flag, set by `QADD`/`QSUB`/`QDADD`/`QDSUB`. - ⭐ `I` (bit 6) = 1 masks IRQ; `F` (bit 5) = 1 masks FIQ; `A` (bit 8) = 1 masks imprecise aborts. - ⭐ `T` (bit 4) = 1 selects Thumb; `T = 0` selects ARM. The `BX` instruction flips `T` and branches. - ⭐ `E` (bit 9) = 0 selects little-endian (default on ARM); `E = 1` selects big-endian. - ⭐ **Mode field** `M[4:0]` uses 5 bits: `10000` USR, `10001` FIQ, `10010` IRQ, `10011` SVC, `10111` ABT, `11011` UND, `11111` SYS. - ⭐ **Reset value** of CPSR is typically `0x000000D3` (SVC mode, ARM state, I=1, F=1, little-endian). - ⭐ **Reserved bits** are `[27]?`, `[26:25]`, `[23:20]`, `[15:10]`, `[7]` — all RAZ/WI on ARM7TDMI-S. - ⭐ On **exception entry**, CPSR is copied to the corresponding `SPSR_` and the mode bits update. - ⭐ On **exception return** (`SUBS PC, LR, #4` or `RFEFD`), SPSR is restored back to CPSR. - ⭐ User mode **cannot write** to CPSR — only privileged modes (via `MSR`) can. - ⭐ `CMP` = `SUBS R0, Rn, Rm` with R0 discarded; `CMN` = `ADDS R0, Rn, Rm` discarded; `TST` = `ANDS R0, Rn, Rm` discarded; `TEQ` = `EORS R0, Rn, Rm` discarded. - ⭐ `BGT` (signed >) is taken iff `Z = 0` **AND** `N = V`. `BGE` (signed ≥) is taken iff `N = V`. - ⭐ `BHI` (unsigned >) is taken iff `C = 1` **AND** `Z = 0`. `BLS` (unsigned ≤) is taken iff `C = 0` **OR** `Z = 1`. - ⭐ Default CPSR value at reset: `0xD3` in the lowest byte ⇒ `I=F=1`, `T=0`, `M=10011` (SVC). **Best of luck for your KTU ESE — you've got this!** 🚀

topics.title: Pipeline
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCIT404 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pipeline

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **Intel 8086 microprocessor**, **Instruction Pipelining** is a hardware-level implementation strategy that permits the **overlapping (parallel) execution of instruction fetch and instruction execution cycles** by splitting the CPU into two functionally independent processing units: the **Bus Interface Unit (BIU)** and the **Execution Unit (EU)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PBCIT404 / 2024 Scheme):**
> *Pipelining is a technique of decomposing a sequential process into sub-operations, with each sub-operation being executed in a dedicated segment that operates concurrently with all other segments. In the 8086, the BIU fetches the next instruction(s) while the EU decodes and executes the current instruction, dramatically reducing the effective cycle time per instruction.*

**Operational Context — 8086 Specific:**
- The **BIU** handles all bus activities (instruction fetch, operand read/write, address generation).
- The **EU** performs all arithmetic, logic, and control operations.
- A **6-byte instruction prefetch queue** in the 8086 (4-byte in 8088) acts as the *pipelining buffer*.

**Standard KTU Terminology You MUST Memorise:**

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **Prefetch Queue** | A FIFO buffer in the BIU that pre-stores up to 6 bytes of upcoming instructions. |
| **Instruction Cycle** | The total time to fetch + decode + execute + store result of one instruction. |
| **Machine Cycle** | The basic operation cycle of the processor (T-states). |
| **Throughput** | Number of instructions completed per unit time. |
| **Latency** | Time taken to complete a single instruction from start to finish. |
| **Speedup Factor (S)** | Ratio of execution time without pipeline to execution time with pipeline. |
| **Pipeline Hazard** | Any condition that prevents the next instruction from executing in its designated clock cycle. |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **🍽️ "The Restaurant Kitchen Analogy" — The easiest way to understand pipelining**

Imagine a small restaurant with **one chef** (no pipeline). For every order:
1. Take the order (Fetch)
2. Chop vegetables (Decode)
3. Cook the dish (Execute)
4. Plate and serve (Write-back)

Each dish takes, say, 40 minutes. Ten customers wait **10 × 40 = 400 minutes**.

Now, suppose the chef hires **two assistants** and sets up an **assembly line (pipeline)**:
- Assistant A — takes orders & fetches ingredients (BIU)
- Assistant B — cooks and plates (EU)
- While B is cooking dish #1, A is fetching ingredients for dish #2.

The **first dish** still takes 40 minutes (latency unchanged), but the **second, third, fourth... dishes emerge every ~few minutes** (throughput skyrockets).

> **This is EXACTLY how the 8086 pipeline works.** The BIU fetches ahead while the EU executes — the program appears to run nearly twice as fast without doubling the clock speed.

---

## 1.3 Why Pipelining Matters in 8086 — High-Yield Perspective

- **Without pipelining:** Fetch and Execute happen serially → wasted bus cycles.
- **With pipelining (8086 BIU + EU):** Bus cycles dedicated to fetch are *hidden* behind the EU's ALU operations.
- The result: **Effective instruction throughput approaches 1 instruction per EU cycle** (instead of per Fetch+Execute cycle).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Board Favourite:** *The EU is NOT idle when BIU is fetching — and BIU is NOT idle when EU is executing.* This is the heart of 8086 pipelining.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | 8086 Value | 8088 Value |
|---|---|---|
| Prefetch Queue Size | **6 bytes** | **4 bytes** |
| BIU-EU Coupling | Asynchronous | Asynchronous |
| Bus Width | **16-bit** | 8-bit |
| Max Throughput Gain | ~**2×** over non-pipelined | ~**1.5×** over non-pipelined |
| Address Bus | **20-bit** (1 MB) | **20-bit** (1 MB) |

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 8086 Internal Architecture — Two-Unit Pipeline

The 8086 microprocessor is internally partitioned into **two independent units** operating in parallel:

### **A. Bus Interface Unit (BIU)**
- **Function:** Fetches instructions, reads operands, and writes results to memory or I/O ports.
- **Components:**
  - Instruction Pointer (IP)
  - Segment Registers (CS, DS, ES, SS)
  - **Instruction Queue (6 bytes in 8086 / 4 bytes in 8088)** ← *the pipeline buffer*
  - Address Generation / Bus Control Logic
- **Operates on the bus** when no EU bus request is pending.

### **B. Execution Unit (EU)**
- **Function:** Decodes and executes instructions taken from the prefetch queue.
- **Components:**
  - ALU (Arithmetic Logic Unit)
  - Flag Register
  - General Purpose Registers (AX, BX, CX, DX)
  - Pointer & Index Registers (SP, BP, SI, DI)
  - Control & Instruction Decoder
- **Operates on data** from the queue without touching the external bus.

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial Concept:** The EU reads instructions from the *prefetch queue*, NOT directly from memory. This decoupling is the essence of the 8086 pipeline.

---

## 2.2 The Prefetch Queue — Heart of the Pipeline

| Action | Without Queue | With Queue (8086) |
|---|---|---|
| Fetch instruction | Uses bus | BIU uses bus (prefetched) |
| Execute | Uses bus if data needed | EU uses internal path |
| Bus utilization | 100% | ~50% (saved for operands) |

**How the 6-byte queue works:**
1. Whenever the queue has **2 or more vacant bytes**, the BIU initiates a fetch cycle.
2. It continues fetching until the queue is full (6 bytes) or until the EU requests a bus operation.
3. The EU consumes bytes from the front of the queue as it decodes and executes.
4. If the EU needs to read/write data → BIU temporarily suspends fetching → executes the operand transfer → resumes prefetching.

---

## 2.3 The General Pipeline Model (For Pipeline Hazard Questions)

For a generic **k-stage pipeline** processing **n instructions**:

### **KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)**

| Formula | Expression | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Total Cycles (Pipelined) | $T_{pipe} = k + (n - 1)$ | k stages to fill the pipeline, then 1 instr/cycle |
| Total Cycles (Non-Pipelined) | $T_{non} = n \cdot k$ | Each instruction takes k cycles sequentially |
| **Speedup Factor (S)** | $\mathbf{S = \dfrac{n \cdot k}{k + (n - 1)}}$ | Ratio of non-pipeline to pipeline time |
| **Throughput (TP)** | $TP = \dfrac{n}{k + (n - 1)}$ | Instructions completed per cycle |
| **Efficiency (η)** | $\eta = \dfrac{S}{k} = \dfrac{n}{k \cdot (k + (n-1))}$ | Fraction of time each stage is busy |
| **Limiting Speedup (n → ∞)** | $S_{max} = k$ | Maximum achievable speedup equals number of stages |
| Cycle Time (Hz) | $f = \dfrac{1}{T_{clk}}$ | Clock frequency |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Trap:** In the above $T_{pipe}$ formula, the $k$ accounts for filling the pipeline (first instruction latency). Students often forget this and write only $(n-1)$, losing 2 marks.

---

## 2.4 Pipeline Hazards (Frequently Asked)

A **pipeline hazard** is any condition that forces the pipeline to **stall** or **flush** — preventing the theoretical 1-instruction-per-cycle throughput.

| Hazard Type | Cause | Typical Fix |
|---|---|---|
| **Structural Hazard** | Two stages need the same hardware resource at the same time | Duplicate hardware (e.g., separate instruction & data memory) |
| **Data Hazard** | An instruction depends on the result of a previous instruction still in the pipeline | Forwarding (bypassing), register renaming, NOP insertion |
| **Control Hazard** | A branch/jump instruction changes the PC; subsequent fetched instructions become invalid | Branch prediction, delay slots, flushing |

**In the 8086 context:**
- The 8086 has a **very simple pipeline** (essentially 2 stages: BIU fetch → EU execute). 
- The main hazard is a **queue flush** when a control transfer instruction (JMP, CALL, INT, RET) is encountered — the BIU discards the queue contents and refetches from the new address. This is a **control hazard** in pipeline terminology.

---

## 2.5 Engineering Utility — Why This Matters in Real Systems

| Application | Relevance of Pipelining |
|---|---|
| Modern CPUs (Intel Core, AMD Ryzen) | 14–20+ stage pipelines, billions of instructions/sec |
| DSP Processors | Deep pipelines for real-time signal processing |
| Embedded ARM Cortex-M | 3-stage pipeline (fetch-decode-execute) |
| Graphics Processing Units (GPUs) | Hundreds of pipeline lanes, SIMD execution |
| Microcontroller Design | Trade-off: deeper pipeline = higher throughput but more hazards and interrupt latency |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Speedup Factor of a k-Stage Pipeline

### **Given:**
- A pipeline with $k$ stages.
- Each stage takes exactly **one clock cycle** of duration $T_{clk}$.
- $n$ independent instructions to be executed.

### **Step 1 — Time without Pipelining (Sequential Execution)**

Each instruction must pass through all $k$ stages one after another. No overlap is allowed.

$$
T_{non\text{-}pipe} = n \times k \times T_{clk}
$$

**Explanation:** For instruction 1, we need $k$ cycles. For instruction 2, another $k$ cycles, and so on for $n$ instructions. Total cycles = $n \cdot k$.

### **Step 2 — Time with Pipelining (Parallel Overlap)**

Once the pipeline is *filled*, **one new instruction completes every clock cycle**. The total time is the sum of:
- Time to **fill** the pipeline: $k$ cycles (first instruction).
- Time to **drain** the remaining $n - 1$ instructions: $(n - 1)$ cycles (one per cycle).

$$
T_{pipe} = \big(k + (n - 1)\big) \times T_{clk}
$$

**Explanation:** At cycle $k$, the first instruction exits. At cycle $k+1$, the second exits. Hence the $(n-1)$ remaining instructions need $(n-1)$ additional cycles.

### **Step 3 — Speedup Factor Definition**

By definition, speedup is the ratio of sequential time to pipelined time:

$$
S = \dfrac{T_{non\text{-}pipe}}{T_{pipe}}
$$

Substitute:

$$
S = \dfrac{n \cdot k \cdot T_{clk}}{(k + (n - 1)) \cdot T_{clk}}
$$

The $T_{clk}$ cancels:

$$
S = \dfrac{n \cdot k}{k + (n - 1)}
$$

### **Step 4 — Limiting Case (n → ∞)**

As the number of instructions becomes very large:

$$
\lim_{n \to \infty} S = \lim_{n \to \infty} \dfrac{n \cdot k}{k + (n - 1)} = \lim_{n \to \infty} \dfrac{n \cdot k}{n + (k - 1)}
$$

Divide numerator and denominator by $n$:

$$
\lim_{n \to \infty} S = \lim_{n \to \infty} \dfrac{k}{1 + \dfrac{(k-1)}{n}} = \dfrac{k}{1 + 0} = k
$$

Therefore, the **maximum theoretical speedup of a k-stage pipeline is exactly $k$**.

$$
\boxed{S_{max} = k}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1: 8086 Pipeline Throughput

> **Problem [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Style]:**
> A system uses a 5-stage pipeline. Calculate the speedup factor, throughput, and efficiency when executing **100 instructions**.

**Step 1 — Identify parameters**
- $k = 5$ stages
- $n = 100$ instructions
- $T_{clk}$ cancels out, so we work in cycle counts.

**Step 2 — Time without pipeline**

$$
T_{non} = n \times k = 100 \times 5 = 500 \text{ cycles}
$$

**Step 3 — Time with pipeline**

$$
T_{pipe} = k + (n - 1) = 5 + 99 = 104 \text{ cycles}
$$

**Step 4 — Speedup Factor**

$$
S = \dfrac{T_{non}}{T_{pipe}} = \dfrac{500}{104} = 4.807
$$

**Step 5 — Throughput**

$$
TP = \dfrac{n}{T_{pipe}} = \dfrac{100}{104} = 0.962 \text{ instr/cycle}
$$

**Step 6 — Efficiency**

$$
\eta = \dfrac{S}{k} = \dfrac{4.807}{5} = 0.9614 = 96.14\%
$$

**Step 7 — Verification of Limit**

If $n \to \infty$, $S \to 5$, so efficiency $\to 100\%$. Our value 96.14% is consistent and approaches the limit.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2: 8086 Two-Stage Pipeline (BIU + EU)

> **Problem [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Style]:**
> For the 8086 microprocessor, model the pipeline as a 2-stage system (BIU for fetch, EU for execute). Each stage takes 1 cycle. Calculate the speedup for executing 20 instructions, and the effective cycles per instruction (CPI).

**Step 1 — Parameters**
- $k = 2$ (BIU, EU)
- $n = 20$ instructions

**Step 2 — Time without pipeline**

$$
T_{non} = n \cdot k = 20 \times 2 = 40 \text{ cycles}
$$

**Step 3 — Time with pipeline**

$$
T_{pipe} = k + (n - 1) = 2 + 19 = 21 \text{ cycles}
$$

**Step 4 — Speedup Factor**

$$
S = \dfrac{40}{21} \approx 1.905
$$

This is close to the theoretical maximum of $k = 2$ — confirming that for large $n$, the 8086's 2-stage pipeline gives **nearly 2× speedup**.

**Step 5 — Effective CPI**

$$
CPI_{eff} = \dfrac{T_{pipe}}{n} = \dfrac{21}{20} = 1.05 \text{ cycles/instruction}
$$

This is a **huge improvement** over a non-pipelined CPI of 2.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 3: Pipeline with Branch Penalty

> **Problem [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]:**
> A 4-stage pipeline processes 50 instructions. 20% of instructions are conditional branches with a penalty of **3 cycles** (flush the pipeline). Compute the effective throughput.

**Step 1 — Base pipeline time (no branches)**

$$
T_{base} = k + (n - 1) = 4 + 49 = 53 \text{ cycles}
$$

**Step 2 — Number of branch instructions**

$$
n_{branch} = 0.20 \times 50 = 10 \text{ branches}
$$

**Step 3 — Total penalty**

$$
P = n_{branch} \times \text{penalty} = 10 \times 3 = 30 \text{ cycles}
$$

**Step 4 — Total time with hazards**

$$
T_{total} = T_{base} + P = 53 + 30 = 83 \text{ cycles}
$$

**Step 5 — Throughput**

$$
TP = \dfrac{n}{T_{total}} = \dfrac{50}{83} = 0.6024 \text{ instr/cycle}
$$

**Step 6 — Effective CPI**

$$
CPI_{eff} = \dfrac{83}{50} = 1.66 \text{ cycles/instruction}
$$

**Step 7 — Comparison to ideal**

Ideal CPI (no branches) = 1.0. Branch hazards increased CPI by **66%** — illustrating why modern CPUs invest heavily in **branch prediction**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Pipeline Performance Trade-offs

| Pipeline Depth (k) | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|
| **Shallow (1–2 stages)** | Low latency per instruction, low hazard penalty, low interrupt response | Low throughput |
| **Medium (3–5 stages)** | Balanced design (e.g., ARM7, classic RISC) | Moderate branch penalty |
| **Deep (10–20+ stages)** | Very high clock frequency, excellent throughput | High branch penalty, complex hazard logic, high power |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The 8086's choice of a **2-stage pipeline** was deliberate — it kept the design simple, tolerated variable-length instructions, and still provided a meaningful throughput boost for 1978-era computing.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define instruction pipelining. How is it implemented in the 8086 microprocessor?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Instruction pipelining is a technique in which the execution of multiple instructions is overlapped by dividing the instruction processing into sub-stages that operate concurrently.

**8086 Implementation (2 marks):** The 8086 implements pipelining by splitting the CPU into two independent units:
- **Bus Interface Unit (BIU)** — handles instruction fetch and operand transfer.
- **Execution Unit (EU)** — handles instruction decode and execution.

The BIU contains a **6-byte prefetch queue** that stores upcoming instructions while the EU executes the current instruction. While the EU is busy, the BIU pre-fetches the next instruction(s) from memory, effectively overlapping the fetch and execute cycles.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is a pipeline hazard? List any three types of hazards.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **pipeline hazard** is any condition that prevents the next instruction in the pipeline from executing in its designated clock cycle, thereby reducing the ideal throughput of 1 instruction per cycle. (1 mark)

**Three types of hazards (2 marks):**
1. **Structural Hazard** — Caused by resource conflicts when two pipeline stages need the same hardware simultaneously.
2. **Data Hazard** — Occurs when an instruction depends on the result of a previous instruction still in the pipeline (RAW, WAR, WAW dependencies).
3. **Control Hazard** — Caused by branch and jump instructions that change the program flow, invalidating the prefetched instructions.

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Two sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying 7 marks, with internal choice between Question A and Question B.

---

### **⭐ Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **pipelined architecture of the 8086 microprocessor**. Discuss the role of the **prefetch queue** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A system uses a **6-stage pipeline** with each stage taking **1 clock cycle**. Compute the **speedup, throughput, and efficiency** for executing **500 instructions**. Compare with the case of **50 instructions**. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**
The 8086 has two internal units — BIU and EU. (Refer to the diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes.)

**BIU Functions (2 marks):**
- Holds the **Instruction Pointer (IP)** and segment registers (CS, DS, SS, ES).
- Generates the **20-bit physical address** using the address adder.
- **Prefetches** instructions into the **6-byte instruction queue**.
- Handles all data transfers with memory/I/O.

**EU Functions (1 mark):**
- **Decodes** instructions from the prefetch queue.
- **Executes** them using the ALU, registers, and flag register.
- Has NO direct access to the system bus (uses BIU for data transfer).

**Role of the Prefetch Queue (1 mark):**
- The 6-byte FIFO queue acts as the pipeline buffer.
- Whenever 2+ bytes are vacant, the BIU automatically fetches the next instruction.
- The EU consumes bytes from the front; the BIU adds bytes to the rear.
- This **decouples fetch from execute**, hiding bus cycles behind ALU operations.
- The queue is **flushed** upon control transfer (JMP, CALL, INT, RET), and refilling causes a brief pipeline penalty.

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]**

**Given:** $k = 6$ stages, $T_{clk}$ cancels (per cycle), so we work in cycle counts.

**Case 1: n = 500 instructions**

Time without pipeline:

$$
T_{non} = n \times k = 500 \times 6 = 3000 \text{ cycles}
$$

Time with pipeline:

$$
T_{pipe} = k + (n - 1) = 6 + 499 = 505 \text{ cycles}
$$

Speedup:

$$
S_1 = \dfrac{3000}{505} = 5.9406
$$

Throughput:

$$
TP_1 = \dfrac{500}{505} = 0.9901 \text{ instr/cycle}
$$

Efficiency:

$$
\eta_1 = \dfrac{5.9406}{6} = 0.9901 = 99.01\%
$$

**Case 2: n = 50 instructions**

Time without pipeline:

$$
T_{non} = 50 \times 6 = 300 \text{ cycles}
$$

Time with pipeline:

$$
T_{pipe} = 6 + 49 = 55 \text{ cycles}
$$

Speedup:

$$
S_2 = \dfrac{300}{55} = 5.4545
$$

Throughput:

$$
TP_2 = \dfrac{50}{55} = 0.9091 \text{ instr/cycle}
$$

Efficiency:

$$
\eta_2 = \dfrac{5.4545}{6} = 0.9091 = 90.91\%
$$

**Comparison Table (built into the answer):**

| Parameter | n = 50 | n = 500 | Observation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Speedup | 5.45 | 5.94 | Increases with n |
| Throughput | 0.91 | 0.99 | Approaches 1 instr/cycle |
| Efficiency | 90.91% | 99.01% | Approaches 100% |

**Conclusion:** As $n \to \infty$, $S \to 6$ (the number of stages). For small $n$, the pipeline-fill overhead ($k$ cycles) dominates, reducing efficiency.

---

### **⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** Explain the **three types of pipeline hazards** with examples. How are they mitigated in modern processors? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **4-stage pipeline** has the following **stage delays**: IF = 2 ns, ID = 1.5 ns, EX = 3 ns, WB = 1 ns. Compute: (i) the **clock cycle time** for the pipeline, (ii) the **time to execute 200 instructions**, (iii) the **speedup** over non-pipelined execution assuming the same clock cycle. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]**

**1. Structural Hazard (2 marks):**
- **Definition:** Two pipeline stages need the **same hardware resource** at the same clock cycle.
- **Example:** A unified instruction + data memory accessed by both the IF and MEM stages in the same cycle.
- **Mitigation:** **Resource duplication** — separate instruction cache (I-cache) and data cache (D-cache) in modern CPUs (Harvard architecture).

**2. Data Hazard (2 marks):**
- **Definition:** An instruction depends on the **result of a previous instruction** still being processed in the pipeline.
- **Types:**
  - **RAW (Read After Write)** — True dependency (most common).
  - **WAR (Write After Read)** — Anti-dependency.
  - **WAW (Write After Write)** — Output dependency.
- **Example:** `ADD R1, R2` followed immediately by `MOV R3, R1` — R3 reads R1 before the ADD has written it back.
- **Mitigation:** **Data forwarding (bypassing)**, register renaming, pipeline interlocks (stalls).

**3. Control Hazard (2 marks):**
- **Definition:** Caused by **branch/jump instructions** that alter the program counter, making prefetched instructions obsolete.
- **Example:** A conditional branch `BEQ` is fetched and decoded, but the next instruction has already entered the IF stage — it must be discarded if the branch is taken.
- **Mitigation:** **Branch prediction** (static/dynamic), **delay slots**, **speculative execution**, **branch target buffer (BTB)**.

**Summary (1 mark):**
Modern processors (Intel Core, AMD Zen) combine all three mitigations — out-of-order execution, deep branch predictors, and large register files — to push effective CPI close to 1.0.

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]**

**Given:**
- Stage delays: IF = 2 ns, ID = 1.5 ns, EX = 3 ns, WB = 1 ns
- $n = 200$ instructions
- $k = 4$ stages

**(i) Clock Cycle Time (2 marks)**

The clock cycle time of a synchronous pipeline must be **≥ the slowest stage delay**:

$$
T_{clk} = \max(IF, ID, EX, WB) = \max(2, 1.5, 3, 1) = 3 \text{ ns}
$$

**(ii) Time to Execute 200 Instructions (3 marks)**

$$
T_{pipe} = \big(k + (n - 1)\big) \times T_{clk} = (4 + 199) \times 3 \text{ ns} = 203 \times 3 = 609 \text{ ns}
$$

**(iii) Speedup Over Non-Pipelined Execution (2 marks)**

**Non-pipelined time** — each instruction must traverse all stages sequentially. The time for one instruction is the sum of all stage delays:

$$
T_{1\text{-}instr} = 2 + 1.5 + 3 + 1 = 7.5 \text{ ns}
$$

For 200 instructions:

$$
T_{non} = n \times T_{1\text{-}instr} = 200 \times 7.5 = 1500 \text{ ns}
$$

Speedup:

$$
S = \dfrac{T_{non}}{T_{pipe}} = \dfrac{1500}{609} = 2.463
$$

**Note:** $S$ is much less than the ideal $k = 4$ because the pipeline must be clocked at the **slowest stage's** speed (3 ns), not the average (1.875 ns). This is the **stage imbalance penalty**.

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting pipeline fill time** in the $T_{pipe}$ formula (using $n$ instead of $k + (n-1)$). Always include the $k$ term!
> 2. **Confusing speedup with throughput.** Speedup is a ratio (dimensionless); throughput has units of *instructions/cycle*.
> 3. **Using average stage delay** for $T_{clk}$ instead of the maximum. The pipeline clock is determined by the *slowest* stage.
> 4. **Forgetting 8086-specific terms** like "6-byte prefetch queue" and "BIU/EU decoupling" — these are KTU-favourite keywords.
> 5. **Mixing up clock cycles and machine cycles.** In pipeline questions, "cycle" usually means clock cycle unless stated otherwise.
> 6. **Not specifying units** (ns, μs, etc.) in the final answer — a 1-mark deduction per question.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this as your last-minute revision sheet before the KTU exam.**

### 🔑 Core Concepts (Must Memorise)
- [ ] **8086 pipeline = 2-stage**: BIU (fetch) + EU (execute), operating in parallel.
- [ ] **Prefetch queue size = 6 bytes** in 8086 (4 bytes in 8088).
- [ ] **BIU fetches** when queue has ≥ 2 vacant bytes.
- [ ] **EU reads from the queue**, not from memory.
- [ ] **Queue is flushed** on control transfer (JMP, CALL, INT, RET) → minor pipeline penalty.
- [ ] **EU has NO direct bus access** — uses BIU for operand transfer.

### 📐 Core Formulas (Must Memorise)
- [ ] $T_{pipe} = k + (n - 1)$ cycles
- [ ] $T_{non} = n \cdot k$ cycles
- [ ] $S = \dfrac{n \cdot k}{k + (n - 1)}$
- [ ] $S_{max} = k$ (as $n \to \infty$)
- [ ] $TP = \dfrac{n}{T_{pipe}}$ instr/cycle
- [ ] $\eta = \dfrac{S}{k}$
- [ ] $T_{clk} = \max(\text{stage delays})$ — for non-uniform pipelines

### ⚠️ Three Pipeline Hazards
- [ ] **Structural** — hardware resource conflict.
- [ ] **Data** — RAW, WAR, WAW dependencies.
- [ ] **Control** — branch/jump misprediction penalty.

### 🎯 KTU-Favourite Keywords
- *Prefetch queue, bus interface unit, execution unit, instruction overlap, throughput, speedup factor, pipeline hazard, branch penalty, control transfer, queue flush.*

### 🧠 Mental Shortcut
- **Speedup ≈ k for large n**, but **< k for small n** due to fill overhead.
- **Deeper pipeline → higher clock rate → higher throughput, but more hazard cost.**
- **8086's 2-stage design** balances simplicity and performance for variable-length instructions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exceptions and Interrupts Interrupt Vector Table
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCIT404 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Exceptions and Interrupts Interrupt Vector Table

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Exception?

In the context of the **Intel x86 architecture** (8086 family and 80386 onwards), an **exception** is a synchronous condition or event generated by the CPU itself when it detects a problem during instruction execution. The word "synchronous" is the key — the exception is a direct, predictable consequence of the instruction currently being executed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** An *exception* is a condition or event that occurs in response to a programming error, fault, or trap, generated internally by the processor during the execution of an instruction. Examples include Divide Error (Type 0), Single Step (Type 1), NMI, Breakpoint (Type 3), and Overflow (Type 4).

### Conceptual Analogy: The "Self-Correcting Chef" 🍳

Imagine a chef (the CPU) is following a recipe (the program). Suddenly, the recipe says *"divide 10 eggs by 0 portions"* — a meaningless, impossible instruction. The chef doesn't blindly continue; he **stops, raises his hand (exception flag), and asks the head chef (the OS / ISR) for instructions on what to do next.** This *internal*, instruction-caused event is an **exception**.

## 1.2 What is an Interrupt?

An **interrupt** is an *asynchronous* event triggered by an **external hardware device** that requires immediate CPU attention. It is unpredictable from the CPU's point of view because it is not tied to any specific instruction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** An *interrupt* is an asynchronous signal from an external hardware device (peripheral) that temporarily halts the CPU's normal execution flow so that the device can be serviced. Interrupts are handled by the Interrupt Service Routine (ISR).

### Conceptual Analogy: The "Restaurant Waiter" 🛎️

Picture a customer (an I/O device like a keyboard) in a restaurant. The chef (CPU) is busy cooking a long recipe (main program). Suddenly, a waiter (the **INTR pin**) taps the chef's shoulder and says, *"Excuse me, a customer needs attention!"* The chef **pauses the recipe, marks where he stopped (saves state on the stack), handles the customer (runs the ISR), and then resumes the recipe exactly where he left off.** This external, asynchronous event is an **interrupt**.

## 1.3 The Interrupt Vector Table (IVT)

The **Interrupt Vector Table (IVT)** is a special, fixed-location data structure in memory that holds the **starting addresses (CS:IP pairs)** of all Interrupt Service Routines. It acts as the **"phone book"** the CPU consults whenever an interrupt or exception occurs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Highlight:** In the **8086 microprocessor**, the IVT is located at the **absolute physical address `00000H` to `003FFH`** in main memory. It contains exactly **256 entries**, each of size **4 bytes (2 bytes for IP + 2 bytes for CS)**, giving a total size of **1 KB (1024 bytes)**.

## 1.4 The Key Distinction: Exception vs. Interrupt

| Aspect | Exception | Interrupt |
|---|---|---|
| **Source** | Internal (CPU generated) | External (Hardware device) |
| **Timing** | Synchronous with instruction | Asynchronous |
| **Trigger Cause** | Fault, trap, or abnormal condition | Peripheral request (INTR pin) |
| **Predictability** | Reproducible — occurs at same instruction | Unpredictable — occurs anytime |
| **Examples** | Divide by 0, Page Fault, Breakpoint | Keyboard keypress, Timer tick, Disk I/O |
| **Maskable?** | Generally No (most are non-maskable) | Yes (maskable via IF flag) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of 8086 Interrupts

The 8086 microprocessor recognizes **256 different interrupt types**, numbered from **`INT 00` to `INT FF` (0 to 255)**. They fall into three broad categories:

### 2.1.1 Hardware Interrupts
- **NMI (Non-Maskable Interrupt)** → Type 2. Edge-triggered on the NMI pin. Cannot be masked by the Interrupt Flag (IF). Reserved for catastrophic events like power failure or memory parity error.
- **INTR (Interrupt Request)** → Maskable. Level-triggered. Goes through the 8259A Programmable Interrupt Controller (PIC) which assigns the actual type number.

### 2.1.2 Software Interrupts
- Generated by the **`INT n` instruction**, where `n` is the type number (0 to 255). The CPU treats it like a hardware interrupt but executes it deterministically.
- Example: `INT 21H` (DOS function call), `INT 10H` (BIOS video service).

### 2.1.3 Internal Interrupts (Processor-Generated Exceptions)
- **Type 0** — Divide Error (Divide by 0 or quotient overflow)
- **Type 1** — Single Step (used by debuggers, TF flag = 1)
- **Type 3** — Breakpoint (`INT 3` instruction, 1-byte opcode `CCH`)
- **Type 4** — Overflow (`INTO` instruction, only fires if OF = 1)

## 2.2 Detailed Structure of the IVT Entry (4 bytes)

Each of the 256 IVT entries occupies **4 consecutive bytes** in memory:

| Offset within Entry | Content | Size |
|---|---|---|
| Lowest address | **IP (low word)** — Lower 16 bits of ISR address | 2 bytes |
| +2 | **CS (high word)** — Upper 16 bits of ISR address | 2 bytes |

So the ISR's effective starting address is reconstructed as:

$$ \text{ISR Address} = \text{CS} \times 16 + \text{IP} \quad \text{(Physical Address)} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical address calculation for IVT entry of type `N`:** The 4-byte entry for interrupt type `N` is stored at physical address `N × 4`. For example, INT 21H's vector is at `21H × 4 = 84H` (i.e., bytes `00084H` to `00087H`).

## 2.3 Interrupt Response Sequence (8086)

When an interrupt is recognized, the CPU performs these **eight deterministic steps**:

1. Decrement **SP by 2** and **push FLAGS** onto the stack.
2. Clear the **Trap (TF)** and **Interrupt Flag (IF)** flags → disables further maskable interrupts and single-stepping.
3. Decrement **SP by 2** and **push CS** (current code segment) onto the stack.
4. Decrement **SP by 2** and **push IP** (current instruction pointer) onto the stack.
5. Fetch the new **IP** from memory location `N × 4`.
6. Fetch the new **CS** from memory location `N × 4 + 2`.
7. Begin execution of the ISR at the new CS:IP.
8. On **IRET** instruction, the CPU pops IP, CS, and FLAGS — returning exactly to the interrupted point.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet & Memory Map

| Parameter | Value | Formula / Rule |
|---|---|---|
| Total Interrupt Types | **256** | `00H` to `FFH` |
| IVT Size | **1024 bytes (1 KB)** | `256 × 4 bytes` |
| IVT Start Address | **`00000H`** | Fixed by Intel |
| IVT End Address | **`003FFH`** | `00000H + 3FFH` |
| Vector Address for Type `N` | `N × 4` | `N × 4` |
| Physical ISR Address | `CS × 16 + IP` | Standard 8086 formula |
| Vectored interrupt delay | **60 clock cycles** (INTR) | Plus 4 bytes per NMI |
| Type numbers reserved for Intel | `00H–1FH` (32 types) | Exceptions + reserved |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Operating Systems** rely on the IVT to dispatch hardware events (keyboard, disk, network) to device drivers.
- **BIOS routines** (e.g., `INT 13H` for disk, `INT 16H` for keyboard) are accessible through fixed IVT entries.
- **Debuggers** use INT 1 (single step) and INT 3 (breakpoint) for instruction-level tracing.
- **Real-Time Systems** use hardware interrupts from timers for deterministic task scheduling.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Physical Address of a Vector Entry

**Problem:** Find the exact physical address range in memory where the IVT entry for **Type 21H** (DOS function call) is stored.

### Step-by-Step Logical Deduction

> **Step 1 — Identify the type number.** We are given `N = 21H`. In decimal, this is `N = 33`.

> **Step 2 — Apply the IVT addressing formula.** Each IVT entry is `4` bytes wide. The starting address is `00000H`. Therefore:

$$
\text{Vector Start Address} = N \times 4
$$

> **Step 3 — Substitute the value.**

$$
\text{Vector Start Address} = 21H \times 4
$$

> **Step 4 — Convert and compute.** `21H` in binary is `0010 0001`. Multiplying by 4 is a left shift by 2 bits:

$$
21H \ll 2 = 84H
$$

> **Step 5 — Determine the byte range.** Since each entry is 4 bytes (offsets 0, 1, 2, 3), the entry occupies:

$$
\text{Range} = [84H,\ 85H,\ 86H,\ 87H]
$$

> **Step 6 — Map bytes to fields.**

| Address | Content (high byte, low byte) | Field |
|---|---|---|
| `00084H` | IP low byte | `IP[7:0]` |
| `00085H` | IP high byte | `IP[15:8]` |
| `00086H` | CS low byte | `CS[7:0]` |
| `00087H` | CS high byte | `CS[15:8]` |

> **Step 7 — Final physical address formula.**

$$
\text{ISR Physical Address} = (CS \text{ from } 00086H{:}00087H) \times 16 + (IP \text{ from } 00084H{:}00085H)
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Total Stack Memory Consumed Per Interrupt

When the CPU services an interrupt, it pushes three 16-bit registers onto the stack. We derive the total stack depth consumed.

> **Step 1 — Identify the registers pushed.** FLAGS, CS, IP — three 16-bit registers.

> **Step 2 — Calculate total bytes.**

$$
\text{Bytes pushed} = 3 \times 2 = 6 \text{ bytes}
$$

> **Step 3 — Account for stack pointer movement.** SP decreases by 6 after push sequence, and increases by 6 on IRET (Intel's symmetric stack convention).

$$
\text{Net SP change} = -6 \text{ (on entry)},\ +6 \text{ (on IRET)}
