$$

> **Step 4 — Nested interrupt depth limit.** If a user ISR allows further interrupts, each nesting consumes 6 bytes. For a 1 KB stack segment, the maximum nesting depth is:

$$
\text{Max nested interrupts} = \left\lfloor \frac{1024}{6} \right\rfloor = 170 \text{ levels}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: 8086 vs. 80386 Interrupt Architecture

| Feature | 8086 (Real Mode) | 80386+ (Protected Mode) |
|---|---|---|
| **Table Name** | Interrupt Vector Table (IVT) | Interrupt Descriptor Table (IDT) |
| **Table Size** | 1 KB (256 × 4 bytes) | 2 KB (256 × 8 bytes) |
| **Table Location** | Fixed at `00000H` | Anywhere in physical memory (set via `LIDT`) |
| **Entry Content** | CS:IP (4 bytes) | Gate descriptor (8 bytes): offset, selector, type, DPL |
| **Gate Types** | None (all entries are direct pointers) | Task Gate, Interrupt Gate, Trap Gate |
| **Types Reserved** | `00H–1FH` (CPU), `20H–FFH` (user) | `00H–1FH` (CPU-defined exceptions), rest available |
| **Privilege Check** | None (real mode) | Yes, checked via DPL & CPL |

## 3.4 Worked Example: Programming the IVT

> **Problem:** Install an ISR at `CS = 4000H`, `IP = 0100H` for interrupt type `80H` using 8086 assembly.

**Solution Steps:**

> **Step 1 — Compute the vector address.**

$$
N \times 4 = 80H \times 4 = 200H
$$

> **Step 2 — Compute each byte address.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{IP low byte} &\rightarrow 00200H \\
\text{IP high byte} &\rightarrow 00201H \\
\text{CS low byte} &\rightarrow 00202H \\
\text{CS high byte} &\rightarrow 00203H
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Step 3 — Write the 8086 assembly fragment.**

```asm
; Assuming DS points to segment 0000H
CLI                       ; Disable interrupts
SUB  AX, AX
MOV  DS, AX               ; DS = 0000H (point to IVT)
MOV  AX, 0100H
MOV  [0200H], AX          ; Store IP low
MOV  [0201H+1]... etc.
; (or use MOV WORD PTR [0200H], AX form)
MOV  AX, 4000H
MOV  [0202H], AX          ; Store CS
STI                       ; Re-enable interrupts
```

> **Step 4 — Validate physical address.**

$$
\text{Physical ISR address} = 4000H \times 16 + 0100H = 40000H + 100H = 40100H
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 IVT Memory Layout Diagram

```mermaid
graph TB
    A[Physical Address 00000H]:::startNode --> B[INT 00H Vector - 4 bytes]
    B --> C[INT 01H Vector - 4 bytes]
    C --> D[INT 02H Vector NMI - 4 bytes]
    D --> E[INT 03H Vector Breakpoint - 4 bytes]
    E --> F[INT 04H Vector Overflow - 4 bytes]
    F --> G[INT 05H to INT 1FH - Reserved by Intel]
    G --> H[INT 20H to INT FFH - User Available 224 entries]
    H --> I[Physical Address 003FFH - End of IVT]

    J[IP Low Byte]:::fieldNode
    K[IP High Byte]:::fieldNode
    L[CS Low Byte]:::fieldNode
    M[CS High Byte]:::fieldNode

    B -.contains.-> J
    B -.contains.-> K
    B -.contains.-> L
    B -.contains.-> M

    classDef startNode fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef fieldNode fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Interrupt Processing Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CPU Executes Instruction]:::normalNode --> B{Interrupt or Exception Pending?}:::decideNode
    B -- No --> A
    B -- Yes --> C[Push FLAGS onto Stack]:::actionNode
    C --> D[Clear TF and IF Flags]:::actionNode
    D --> E[Push CS onto Stack]:::actionNode
    E --> F[Push IP onto Stack]:::actionNode
    F --> G[Fetch New IP from Vector N x 4]:::actionNode
    G --> H[Fetch New CS from Vector N x 4 + 2]:::actionNode
    H --> I[Begin ISR Execution at New CS:IP]:::isrNode
    I --> J{More Interrupts to Service?}:::decideNode
    J -- Yes --> I
    J -- No --> K[Execute IRET Instruction]:::iretNode
    K --> L[Pop IP from Stack]:::actionNode
    L --> M[Pop CS from Stack]:::actionNode
    M --> N[Pop FLAGS from Stack]:::actionNode
    N --> A

    classDef normalNode fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef decideNode fill:#d62728,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef actionNode fill:#9467bd,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef isrNode fill:#8c564b,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef iretNode fill:#e377c2,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 8086 Exception/Interrupt Classification Tree

```mermaid
graph TB
    Root[8086 Interrupts Total 256 Types]:::rootNode
    Root --> A1[Hardware Interrupts]:::hwNode
    Root --> A2[Software Interrupts]:::swNode
    Root --> A3[Internal Exceptions]:::exNode

    A1 --> B1[NMI Non Maskable Pin Type 02H]:::hwNode
    A1 --> B2[INTR Maskable Via 8259A PIC Type 08H to 0FH]:::hwNode

    A2 --> C1[INT n Instruction Type 00H to FFH]:::swNode
    A2 --> C2[INT 3 Breakpoint 1 byte opcode]:::swNode
    A2 --> C3[INTO Overflow Trap if OF=1]:::swNode

    A3 --> D1[Type 0 Divide Error]:::exNode
    A3 --> D2[Type 1 Single Step TF=1]:::exNode
    A3 --> D3[Type 3 Breakpoint]:::exNode
    A3 --> D4[Type 4 Overflow]:::exNode

    classDef rootNode fill:#000000,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff
    classDef hwNode fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef swNode fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef exNode fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.4 8086 with 8259A PIC Interface Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    CPU[8086 CPU]:::cpuNode
    PIC[8259A Programmable Interrupt Controller]:::picNode
    DEV1[Keyboard Controller]:::devNode
    DEV2[Timer 8253]:::devNode
    DEV3[Disk Controller]:::devNode
    DEV4[Serial Port]:::devNode

    CPU -- INTR pin --> PIC
    PIC -- IR0 --> DEV1
    PIC -- IR1 --> DEV2
    PIC -- IR2 --> DEV3
    PIC -- IR3 --> DEV4
    CPU -- CS IP to Memory Bus --> MEM[IVT in Main Memory 00000H to 003FFH]:::memNode

    classDef cpuNode fill:#d62728,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef picNode fill:#9467bd,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef devNode fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef memNode fill:#8c564b,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** What is an Interrupt Vector Table (IVT)? Mention its location and size in an 8086 system.

**Model Answer:**
The Interrupt Vector Table (IVT) is a 1 KB data structure in the 8086 microprocessor's memory that stores the **starting addresses (CS:IP)** of all 256 interrupt service routines. It is located at the **fixed physical address range `00000H` to `003FFH`** in main memory. It contains **256 entries**, each of **4 bytes** (2 bytes for the IP value followed by 2 bytes for the CS value), giving a total of `256 × 4 = 1024 bytes (1 KB)`. The CPU uses the formula `N × 4` to locate the vector for interrupt type `N`.

*(3 marks — keywords: location, size, formula, byte layout)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** Differentiate between an exception and an interrupt with two examples for each.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Exception | Interrupt |
|---|---|---|
| Origin | Generated internally by the CPU | Generated by external hardware |
| Synchrony | Synchronous with instruction | Asynchronous |
| Example 1 | Divide by zero (Type 0) | NMI from power failure (Type 2) |
| Example 2 | Breakpoint (Type 3) | Keyboard INTR via 8259A |

*(3 marks — comparison + examples must both be correct)*

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the structure of the 8086 Interrupt Vector Table. Show how the CPU computes the vector address for a given interrupt type `N`. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the complete sequence of operations performed by the 8086 CPU when it responds to a hardware INTR interrupt. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

1. The IVT is a lookup table of 256 entries, occupying addresses `00000H`–`003FFH`.
2. Each entry stores the CS:IP of an ISR in the format `[IP-low][IP-high][CS-low][CS-high]`.
3. The vector address for type `N` is given by:

$$
\text{Vector Address} = N \times 4
$$

4. For example, **Type 80H** → address `200H` (i.e., `80H × 4 = 200H`).
5. The CPU first fetches IP from `200H` and `201H`, then CS from `202H` and `203H`, then jumps to that CS:IP.

#### Model Solution for (b):

1. The peripheral raises an INTR signal (active HIGH).
2. The 8259A PIC resolves the request and places the interrupt type on the data bus.
3. The CPU checks the **IF flag** — if `IF = 0`, INTR is masked and ignored.
4. The CPU completes the current instruction, then acknowledges with `INTA` (one or two pulses).
5. The CPU reads the type number `N` from the data bus during the second `INTA`.
6. The CPU pushes **FLAGS**, then **CS**, then **IP** onto the stack.
7. The CPU clears the **IF** and **TF** flags.
8. The CPU fetches the new IP and CS from IVT at `N × 4`.
9. Execution transfers to the ISR.
10. The ISR ends with **IRET**, which pops IP, CS, and FLAGS, restoring the prior state.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Describe the different types of interrupts supported by the 8086 microprocessor with appropriate type numbers. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Interface an 8259A Programmable Interrupt Controller with the 8086 and explain its role in expanding the interrupt handling capability. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

The 8086 supports three categories:

1. **Hardware Interrupts (External, Asynchronous)**
   - **NMI** — Type `02H`, non-maskable, edge-triggered, used for critical hardware failures.
   - **INTR** — Maskable, level-triggered, processed via the 8259A PIC.

2. **Software Interrupts (INT n)**
   - User-triggered via the `INT n` instruction.
   - **Type 3** — Special 1-byte `INT 3` (breakpoint).
   - **Type 4** — `INTO` (overflow trap) fires only if `OF = 1`.

3. **Internal Exceptions (Processor-Generated)**
   - **Type 0** — Divide error (division by zero or overflow).
   - **Type 1** — Single step (TF = 1, used for debugging).
   - **Type 3** — Breakpoint.
   - **Type 4** — Overflow trap.

#### Model Solution for (b):

- The 8086 has only **two hardware interrupt pins** (NMI and INTR), but real systems need many more I/O devices.
- The **8259A PIC** cascades 8 input lines (IR0–IR7) into the single INTR pin of the 8086.
- Each IR line can be assigned a unique **type number** (e.g., IR0 → `08H`, IR1 → `09H`, ..., IR7 → `0FH`).
- Up to **64 interrupts** can be handled by cascading 8 PICs in a master-slave configuration.
- The 8259A is programmed via two I/O ports (typically `20H` and `21H`) using Initialization Command Words (ICWs) and Operation Command Words (OCWs).
- The 8259A also supports **masking** of individual IR lines via the OCW1 register and **priority modes** (fully nested, rotating, etc.).
- The interrupt acknowledge (`INTA`) signal from the 8086 triggers the 8259A to place the resolved type number on the data bus.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the IVT start address**: Students often write `0000:0000` (segment:offset) instead of the **physical address `00000H`**. The IVT is always addressed physically, not by segment:offset.
> 2. **Confusing entry byte order**: The IVT stores **IP FIRST, then CS** (low-to-high memory). Reversing this loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Misquoting the formula**: The vector address is `N × 4`, NOT `N × 2` or `N × 8`. The `× 4` is non-negotiable for 8086.
> 4. **Skipping flag clear**: Many students omit the **clearing of IF and TF** during the interrupt response sequence, losing marks on part (b) of 14-mark questions.
> 5. **Forgetting IRET symmetry**: Always mention that IRET pops in the order **IP → CS → FLAGS** to match the push order.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌

- ✅ The **IVT in 8086** is at physical address `00000H` to `003FFH` and is exactly **1 KB** in size.
- ✅ It contains **256 entries × 4 bytes each** = **2 bytes IP + 2 bytes CS**.
- ✅ **Vector address formula**: `N × 4` where `N` is the interrupt type number.
- ✅ The 8086 supports **256 interrupt types** (`00H` to `FFH`).
- ✅ **Three categories**: Hardware (NMI, INTR), Software (`INT n`), Internal (Types 0, 1, 3, 4).
- ✅ **NMI** is non-maskable; **INTR** is maskable via the **IF flag** in the FLAGS register.
- ✅ The CPU pushes **FLAGS, CS, IP** (in that order) and clears **IF & TF** before fetching the new CS:IP.
- ✅ The **8259A PIC** expands the single INTR pin to handle up to **8 (or 64 cascaded)** external interrupt sources.
- ✅ **IRET** is the symmetric return instruction that pops **IP, CS, FLAGS** to resume the interrupted program.
- ✅ In **80386 protected mode**, the IVT is replaced by the **IDT (Interrupt Descriptor Table)**, which uses **8-byte gate descriptors** and can be located anywhere in memory via the `LIDT` instruction.
- ✅ The first **32 type numbers (`00H`–`1FH`)** are reserved by Intel for CPU-defined exceptions; the remaining `20H`–`FFH` are user-definable.
- ✅ A single interrupt response on the 8086 consumes exactly **6 bytes of stack space** (3 pushes × 2 bytes each).
- ✅ The 8086 takes **61 clock cycles** to respond to an INTR interrupt (in minimum mode).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ARM Instruction Set - Data Processing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCIT404 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** ARM Instruction Set - Data Processing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the **ARM (Advanced RISC Machine)** architecture, **Data Processing Instructions** constitute the core class of computational operations that operate exclusively on values held in the **General Purpose Registers (GPRs) R0–R12**, the **Link Register (R14)**, and the **Stack Pointer (R13)**. They perform arithmetic, logical, comparison, and data-movement operations on **32-bit word operands**.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, ARM data processing instructions are characterized by three distinguishing features:

1. **Operand2 (the "flexible second operand")** – a unique composite operand that may be an 8-bit rotated immediate or a register combined with a barrel-shifter operation.
2. **Conditional Execution** – every instruction carries a 4-bit condition code in bits [31:28], allowing predicated execution.
3. **Optional Flag Update (`S` bit)** – the instruction may optionally update the **CPSR (Current Program Status Register)** condition flags: **N, Z, C, V**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The 32-bit data processing instruction is the most fundamental instruction class in the ARM ISA. Mastering the encoding format and operand2 structure is essential for both University Examination and Laboratory evaluation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine a **scientific calculator with a tiny rotating wheel** before its number pad. Before you press "+", you can choose whether the second number is:

- A **plain number** (immediate constant), or
- A **stored number pulled from a memory register**, but twisted/rotated by a small mechanical wheel (the **barrel shifter**) before reaching the adder.

That is precisely what the ARM **Operand2** does — it lets the second operand be either a small rotated constant or a shifted register, **all without an extra clock cycle** because the shift happens inside the datapath. This is why ARM is called a **"load-store, register-register"** architecture: all real work is done *inside* the CPU, while memory is only touched by `LDR`/`STR`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Summary:** ARM Data Processing = "Operate on R0–R14 using flexible second operand + optional condition + optional flag update, **never touching memory**."

## 1.3 Classification of ARM Data Processing Instructions

The instructions fall into **three logical groups**, distinguished by the **opcode (bits [24:21])**:

| Group | Opcode (Binary) | Mnemonic Examples | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Arithmetic** | `0010`, `0011`, `0100`, `0101`, `0110`, `0111` | `ADD, ADC, SUB, SBC, RSB, RSC` | Signed/unsigned addition & subtraction with/without carry |
| **Logical / Bitwise** | `0000`, `0001`, `1100`, `1101`, `1110` | `AND, ORR, EOR, BIC, MOV, MVN` | AND, OR, XOR, bit-clear, move, move-not |
| **Comparison** | `1000`, `1001`, `1010`, `1011` | `TST, TEQ, CMP, CMN` | Test bits, compare, set flags only (Rd ignored) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Data Processing Instruction (32-bit Encoding)

A single ARM data processing instruction is encoded into 32 bits, partitioned as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{bits }[31:28] &\rightarrow \texttt{cond} \;\;(\text{4-bit condition code}) \\
\text{bits }[27:26] &\rightarrow \texttt{00}   \;\;(\text{type identifier for data processing}) \\
\text{bit  }[25]    &\rightarrow \texttt{I}    \;\;(\text{Immediate operand flag}) \\
\text{bits }[24:21] &\rightarrow \texttt{opcode} \\
\text{bit  }[20]    &\rightarrow \texttt{S}    \;\;(\text{Set condition codes flag}) \\
\text{bits }[19:16] &\rightarrow \texttt{Rn}   \;\;(\text{First source register}) \\
\text{bits }[15:12] &\rightarrow \texttt{Rd}   \;\;(\text{Destination register}) \\
\text{bits }[11:0]  &\rightarrow \texttt{Operand2}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The `I` bit is the **gatekeeper**: `I=0` → Operand2 is a *register with shift*; `I=1` → Operand2 is an *8-bit rotated immediate*.

## 2.2 Operand2 – The "Flexible Second Operand"

### 2.2.1 Immediate Constant Form (`I = 1`)

The 12 bits of Operand2 are split into an **8-bit value** and a **4-bit rotation**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{bits }[11:8]  &\rightarrow \texttt{rotate} \in [0..15] \\
\text{bits }[7:0]   &\rightarrow \texttt{imm8}   \in [0..255]
\end{aligned}
$$

The actual 32-bit constant generated is computed as:

$$
\boxed{\;\; \text{imm32} \;=\; \text{imm8} \;\text{rotated right by}\; (2 \times \text{rotate}) \;\text{bits} \;\;}
$$

This clever encoding produces a **repeating 8-bit pattern** of length **1, 2, 4, or 8** (e.g., `0x01010101`, `0x00FF00FF`, `0x40404040`).

### 2.2.2 Register Form (`I = 0`)

The 12 bits encode a source register `Rm` and an optional shift:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{bits }[11:7]  &\rightarrow \texttt{shift amount} \;\;(\text{5-bit unsigned, or } Rs) \\
\text{bits }[6:5]   &\rightarrow \texttt{shift type} \\
\text{bit  }[4]     &\rightarrow \texttt{0} \\
\text{bits }[3:0]   &\rightarrow \texttt{Rm}
\end{aligned}
$$

| `shift` (bits 6:5) | Mnemonic | Operation |
|---|---|---|
| `00` | `LSL` | Logical Shift Left |
| `01` | `LSR` | Logical Shift Right |
| `10` | `ASR` | Arithmetic Shift Right |
| `11` | `ROR` / `RRX` | Rotate Right / Rotate Right Extended by carry |

The shifter produces:

$$
\boxed{\;\; \text{shifter\_operand} \;=\; \text{Op2}(Rm,\, \text{shift},\, \text{amount}) \;\;}
$$

and feeds it into the ALU in the same cycle (no extra latency — the **barrel shifter** is a parallel hardware unit).

## 2.3 General Instruction Operation

Most arithmetic/logical instructions perform:

$$
\boxed{\;\; \text{Rd} \;=\; \texttt{op}(Rn,\, \text{Operand2}) \;\;}
$$

For `MOV` and `MVN`, **Rn is ignored** (treated as R0):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MOV } &: \text{Rd} = \text{Operand2} \\
\text{MVN } &: \text{Rd} = \lnot \text{Operand2}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 2.4 The `S` Bit and the CPSR Flags

When the `S` bit is set, four condition flags in the **CPSR** are updated:

| Flag | Bit | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **N** | 31 | **N**egative — set if MSB of result is 1 |
| **Z** | 30 | **Z**ero — set if result equals 0 |
| **C** | 29 | **C**arry — set on unsigned overflow/out-borrow |
| **V** | 28 | o**V**erflow — set on signed overflow |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Comparisons (`CMP`, `CMN`, `TST`, `TEQ`) are implemented as data-processing instructions with `S=1` and `Rd=R0` (result discarded). This is why they *only* affect flags and *never* write to a destination register.

## 2.5 Condition Code Table (Bits [31:28])

| Code | Mnemonic | Meaning (Flag test) |
|---|---|---|
| `0000` | `EQ` | Equal (Z=1) |
| `0001` | `NE` | Not Equal (Z=0) |
| `0010` | `CS/HS` | Carry Set / Unsigned ≥ |
| `0011` | `CC/LO` | Carry Clear / Unsigned < |
| `0100` | `MI` | Minus (N=1) |
| `0101` | `PL` | Plus (N=0) |
| `0110` | `VS` | Overflow Set (V=1) |
| `0111` | `VC` | Overflow Clear (V=0) |
| `1000` | `HI` | Unsigned Higher (C=1 AND Z=0) |
| `1001` | `LS` | Unsigned Lower or Same |
| `1010` | `GE` | Signed ≥ (N==V) |
| `1011` | `LT` | Signed < (N≠V) |
| `1100` | `GT` | Signed > (Z=0 AND N==V) |
| `1101` | `LE` | Signed ≤ |
| `1110` | `AL` | Always execute |
| `1111` | `NV` | Never (deprecated) |

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

$$
\begin{array}{|l|l|l|}
\hline
\textbf{Concept} & \textbf{Equation} & \textbf{Units / Notes} \\
\hline
\text{Immediate constant} & \text{imm32} = \text{imm8 ROR } (2 \times \text{rotate}) & \text{rotate} \in [0..15] \\
\hline
\text{Logical Shift Left} & x \ll n = x \cdot 2^{n} & n \in [0..31] \\
\hline
\text{Logical Shift Right} & x \gg_{L} n = \lfloor x / 2^{n} \rfloor & n \in [1..32] \\
\hline
\text{ASR (sign-preserving)} & x \gg_{A} n & \text{MSB replicated} \\
\hline
\text{ADD} & \text{Rd} = Rn + \text{Op2} & \text{writes to Rd} \\
\hline
\text{SUB} & \text{Rd} = Rn - \text{Op2} & C = NOT borrow \\
\hline
\text{RSB} & \text{Rd} = \text{Op2} - Rn & \text{operand swap} \\
\hline
\text{ADC} & \text{Rd} = Rn + \text{Op2} + C_{\text{in}} & \text{for multi-word add} \\
\hline
\text{SBC} & \text{Rd} = Rn - \text{Op2} - (1 - C_{\text{in}}) & \\
\hline
\text{Carry from ADD} & C = (R_n + \text{Op2} + C_{in}) \ge 2^{32} & \text{unsigned overflow} \\
\hline
\text{Overflow} & V = (R_n \,\&\, \overline{\text{Op2}} \,\&\, \overline{R_d}) \mid (\overline{R_n} \,\&\, \text{Op2} \,\&\, R_d) & \text{signed} \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

## 2.7 Engineering Utility

Data processing instructions are the **backbone of every embedded algorithm** — from PID controllers in motor drives (used a thousand times per second) to FIR filter inner loops in DSP firmware. The **barrel shifter + Operand2** design lets C compilers emit extremely tight shift-and-add code (e.g., `R0 = R0 + (R1 LSL #2)`) in a single cycle, which is why ARM dominates power-efficient real-time systems: **mobile phones, automotive ECUs, IoT sensors, and drones**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Encoding a Data Processing Instruction

**Problem:** Encode the instruction `ADD R3, R2, R1, LSL #2` into its 32-bit binary form.

**Step 1 — Identify fields**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\texttt{cond}    &= \texttt{AL} \;\; (1110) \\
\texttt{type}    &= 00 \;\; (\text{data processing}) \\
\texttt{I}       &= 0  \;\; (\text{register form}) \\
\texttt{opcode}  &= 0100 \;\; (\text{ADD}) \\
\texttt{S}       &= 0  \;\; (\text{do not set flags}) \\
\texttt{Rn}      &= 0010 \;\; (R2) \\
\texttt{Rd}      &= 0011 \;\; (R3) \\
\texttt{Rm}      &= 0001 \;\; (R1) \\
\texttt{shift}   &= 00  \;\; (\text{LSL}) \\
\texttt{amount}  &= 00010 \;\; (2) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Concatenate fields from MSB to LSB**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\texttt{cond} \vert \texttt{00} \vert \texttt{I} \vert \texttt{opcode} \vert \texttt{S} \vert \texttt{Rn} \vert \texttt{Rd} \vert \texttt{shift} \vert 0 \vert \texttt{amount} \vert \texttt{Rm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Write the 32-bit binary string**

$$
1110\,0010\,1000\,0010\,0011\,0000\,1000\,0001
$$

**Step 4 — Group into hex nibbles**

$$
\begin{aligned}
1110 \; 0010 \; 1000 \; 0010 \; 0011 \; 0000 \; 1000 \; 0001 \\
= \texttt{E2 82 30 81}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Encoding (Little-Endian storage):** The word is stored in memory as bytes `0x81, 0x30, 0x82, 0xE2`.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Trick:** Always verify by checking that the test `Rd` matches the *expected* destination and that the `cond` is `1110` for unconditional.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Decoding an Immediate Constant

**Problem:** The instruction contains the immediate bits `rotate=1010` (binary, =10) and `imm8=0x4F`. Find the 32-bit constant.

**Step 1 — Apply the rotation formula**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{shift\_count} &= 2 \times \text{rotate} = 2 \times 10 = 20 \text{ bits} \\
\text{imm32} &= 0\text{x}4F \;\text{ROR}\; 20 \\
            &= 0\text{x}4F00\,0000 \;\; \text{(since rotating 0x4F right by 20 bits places it at bits 24..31)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Verify**

$$
\begin{aligned}
0\text{x}4F &\rightarrow \texttt{0000 0000 0000 0000 0000 0000 0100 1111} \\
\text{ROR 20} &\rightarrow \texttt{0100 1111 0000 0000 0000 0000 0000 0000} \\
            &= 0\text{x}4F00\,0000
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Barrel Shifter Behaviour

**Problem:** `R0 = 0x00000080` and the shifter is `R0 ASR #2`. Compute the result and the C flag.

**Step 1 — Convert to binary**

$$
R0 = \texttt{0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,1000\,0000}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Arithmetic Shift Right by 2**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ASR 2} &\rightarrow \text{MSB (0) replicated, then bits shift right} \\
&= \texttt{0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0010\,0000} \\
&= 0\text{x}00000020
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Determine Carry flag**

The last bit shifted out (from bit 1) was `0`, so **C = 0**.

$$
\boxed{ \text{shifter\_output} = 0\text{x}20, \quad C = 0 }
$$

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Multi-Word 64-bit Addition Using `ADC`

A common KTU question asks how ARM performs 64-bit addition of `{R1:R0} + {R3:R2}`.

**Step 1 — Add the lower words using `ADD`**

$$
\texttt{ADDS} \;\; R0, \; R0, \; R2 \quad (\text{S = 1 to record carry})
$$

**Step 2 — Add the upper words with `ADC`**

$$
\texttt{ADC} \;\; R1, \; R1, \; R3 \quad (\text{carry from step 1 is added automatically})
$$

**Step 3 — Algebraic representation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R0' &= (R0 + R2) \bmod 2^{32} \\
C_{\text{out}} &= (R0 + R2) \geq 2^{32} \\
R1' &= (R1 + R3 + C_{\text{out}}) \bmod 2^{32}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The `S` bit is **mandatory** on the lower `ADD`; otherwise the carry is lost and the 64-bit result is wrong. This is a classic **KTU valuation pitfall**.

## 3.5 Worked Example 5 — Computing Condition Codes for `SUBS`

**Problem:** Compute CPSR flags after `SUBS R4, R4, #1` given R4 = 0x00000000.

**Step 1 — Compute result**

$$
R4' = 0 - 1 = 0\text{x}FFFFFFFF \;\; (\text{in 32-bit wrap-around})
$$

**Step 2 — Compute N**

$$
N = \text{MSB}(R4') = 1
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Z**

$$
Z = (R4' = 0) \;\; ? \;\; 1 : 0 = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Compute C (no borrow)**

$$
C = (R4 \geq 1) \;\; ? \;\; 1 : 0 = 0
$$

**Step 5 — Compute V (signed overflow: 0 − 1 = +∞ is impossible)**

$$
V = 1
$$

**Final CPSR flags:** N=1, Z=0, C=0, V=1.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Common Pitfalls & Their Solutions

$$
\begin{array}{|p{5cm}|p{5cm}|p{4cm}|}
\hline
\textbf{Pitfall} & \textbf{Why it Fails} & \textbf{Correct Approach} \\
\hline
\text{Forgetting S bit on `CMP`} & \text{Compiler may emit `CMP` but assemble as `CMP` is always S=1 — confirmed by ARM spec} & \text{Remember CMP/CMN/TST/TEQ implicitly have S=1} \\
\hline
\text{Using immediate > 0xFF} & \text{Out of range — assembler rotates only} & \text{Use rotation: 0x100 = 0x01 ROR 24} \\
\hline
\text{Using RSB instead of SUB} & \text{Mnemonic confusion} & \text{SUB: Rd = Rn - Op2; RSB: Rd = Op2 - Rn} \\
\hline
\text{Overflow flag miscomputation} & \text{Confusing signed vs unsigned} & \text{V = signed overflow only; C = unsigned} \\
\hline
\text{Writing result of CMP to a register} & \text{Comparison discards Rd} & \text{Use Bxx (branch) after CMP} \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — ARM Data Processing Instruction Format

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    instWord["32-bit Data Processing Instruction Word"]
    condField["bits 31:28 - cond (Condition Code)"]
    typeField["bits 27:26 - 00 (Type Identifier)"]
    iBit["bit 25 - I (Immediate Flag)"]
    opField["bits 24:21 - Opcode"]
    sBit["bit 20 - S (Set Flags)"]
    rnField["bits 19:16 - Rn (First Operand Register)"]
    rdField["bits 15:12 - Rd (Destination Register)"]
    op2Field["bits 11:0 - Operand2"]
    immForm["I = 1 path: imm8 + 4-bit rotate"]
    regForm["I = 0 path: Rm + shift amount + shift type"]
    barrel["Barrel Shifter Hardware Unit"]
    alu["32-bit ALU Core"]
    cpsr["CPSR Flags N Z C V"]
    regFile["Register File R0 to R14"]

    instWord --> condField
    instWord --> typeField
    instWord --> iBit
    instWord --> opField
    instWord --> sBit
    instWord --> rnField
    instWord --> rdField
    instWord --> op2Field

    iBit -->|"I = 1"| immForm
    iBit -->|"I = 0"| regForm

    immForm --> barrel
    regForm --> barrel
    rnField --> alu
    barrel --> alu
    opField --> alu
    alu --> rdField
    sBit -->|"S = 1"| cpsr
    rdField --> regFile
    alu -->|"flag updates"| cpsr
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Operand2 Decoding Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    startOp2["Operand2 Field 12 bits"]
    checkI["Inspect I bit from instruction"]
    branchImm["Path A: I equals 1"]
    branchReg["Path B: I equals 0"]
    immDecode["Split into rotate and imm8"]
    rorCompute["imm32 equals imm8 ROR 2 times rotate"]
    regDecode["Split into shift and Rm and amount"]
    barrelShift["Apply LSL or LSR or ASR or ROR or RRX on Rm"]
    finalOp["Final 32-bit shifter operand to ALU"]

    startOp2 --> checkI
    checkI -->|"Immediate"| branchImm
    checkI -->|"Register"| branchReg
    branchImm --> immDecode --> rorCompute --> finalOp
    branchReg --> regDecode --> barrelShift --> finalOp
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Functional Architecture of ARM Data Path

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    regBank["Register Bank R0 to R14"]
    readA["Read Port A: Rn"]
    readB["Read Port B: Rm"]
    shifter["Barrel Shifter (5 shift modes)"]
    immEnc["Immediate Encoder (8-bit + rotate)"]
    muxA["Mux selecting Operand2 source"]
    aluCore["ALU Core (performs op)"]
    muxRes["Mux selecting ALU result or shifter bypass"]
    writeBack["Write Port: Rd"]
    cpsrReg["CPSR Register (N Z C V)"]
    pcReg["PC (Program Counter) R15"]
    incPC["PC plus 4 plus 8 pipeline offset"]

    regBank --> readA --> aluCore
    regBank --> readB --> shifter
    immEnc --> muxA
    shifter --> muxA
    muxA --> aluCore
    aluCore -->|"if S=1"| cpsrReg
    aluCore --> muxRes --> writeBack --> regBank
    pcReg --> incPC
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Architecture Insight:** Because `R15` is the **Program Counter** and is also physically in the register file, reading it from port A returns `PC + 8` (two-stage pipeline offset). This is a frequently asked KTU viva question.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Explain the significance of the `S` bit and condition codes in ARM data processing instructions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The `S` bit (bit 20) controls whether a data processing instruction updates the four condition flags — **N, Z, C, V** — in the CPSR register. When `S=1`, the result of the ALU operation is automatically written into the corresponding flag bits; when `S=0`, the CPSR remains unchanged. The condition code field (bits 31:28) allows *every* instruction to be **conditionally executed**: the CPU evaluates the specified flag test (e.g., `EQ` checks Z=1) before execution, and the instruction is treated as a NOP if the condition fails. This eliminates the need for many short branches and reduces pipeline flushes, giving ARM its characteristic code density. (3 marks)

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between `SUB` and `RSB` instructions with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Both are arithmetic data processing instructions that perform subtraction and set the carry flag based on the **unsigned underflow**.

- `SUB Rd, Rn, Operand2` performs `Rd = Rn − Operand2`.
- `RSB Rd, Rn, Operand2` performs `Rd = Operand2 − Rn` (operands are **reversed**).

**Example:**
`SUB R0, R1, R2` ⇒ `R0 = R1 − R2`
`RSB R0, R1, R2` ⇒ `R0 = R2 − R1`

`RSB` is particularly useful for computing **negation** (e.g., `RSB R0, R0, #0` → `R0 = 0 − R0 = −R0`). (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Choice Questions

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **encoding format of an ARM data processing instruction**. Discuss the role of the `I` bit and the **barrel shifter** in generating Operand2. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write the **32-bit encoding** of the following ARM instructions. Identify the condition, opcode, registers, and Operand2 in each case. **(7 marks)**
  (i) `ADD R5, R6, R7`
  (ii) `SUB R4, R4, #1`
  (iii) `AND R2, R3, #0x80`
  (iv) `MOV R0, R1, LSR #4`

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Instruction Format (7 marks):**

A 32-bit ARM data processing instruction has the following fields (refer to Section 2.1 diagram):

| Field | Bits | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `cond` | 31–28 | Condition code (e.g., `EQ`, `NE`, `AL`) |
| `00` | 27–26 | Identifier for data processing type |
| `I` | 25 | Immediate operand flag |
| `opcode` | 24–21 | ADD, SUB, AND, etc. |
| `S` | 20 | Set condition codes |
| `Rn` | 19–16 | First source register |
| `Rd` | 15–12 | Destination register |
| `Operand2` | 11–0 | Flexible second operand |

**Role of the `I` bit:**
- `I = 0` → Operand2 is **Rm** combined with a barrel-shifter operation.
- `I = 1` → Operand2 is an **8-bit immediate constant** rotated right by an even amount.

**Role of the Barrel Shifter:**
The barrel shifter is a dedicated combinational hardware unit that performs `LSL, LSR, ASR, ROR, RRX` in a *single clock cycle* with no extra latency. It receives the value of `Rm` and the shift amount and produces a 32-bit shifted result that feeds directly into the ALU. This is what gives ARM data processing its characteristic **single-cycle shift-and-operate** capability. (7 marks)

---

**(b) Encoding Solutions (7 marks):**

**(i) `ADD R5, R6, R7`** — register-register ADD
$$
\begin{aligned}
\texttt{cond}   &= 1110 \;(\texttt{AL}) \\
\texttt{type}   &= 00 \\
\texttt{I}      &= 0 \\
\texttt{opcode} &= 0100 \;(\texttt{ADD}) \\
\texttt{S}      &= 0 \\
\texttt{Rn}     &= 0110 \;(R6) \\
\texttt{Rd}     &= 0101 \;(R5) \\
\texttt{Rm}     &= 0111 \;(R7) \\
\texttt{shift}  &= 00 \;(\texttt{LSL}) \\
\texttt{amount} &= 00000 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Concatenated: `1110 000 0 0100 0 0110 0101 00 0 00000 0111`
= **`0xE0865007`**

---

**(ii) `SUB R4, R4, #1`** — register-immediate SUB
$$
\texttt{imm32} = 0\text{x}01 \;\;(\text{rotate}=0, \text{imm8}=1)
$$
$$
\texttt{cond}   = 1110,\; \texttt{opcode} = 0010,\; \texttt{S}=0,\; \texttt{Rn}=0100,\; \texttt{Rd}=0100,\; \texttt{I}=1
$$

Binary: `1110 0010 0100 0100 0100 0000 0000 0001`
= **`0xE2440001`**

---

**(iii) `AND R2, R3, #0x80`** — register-immediate AND
`0x80 = 0x80 ROR 0`, so `imm8 = 0x80, rotate = 0`.

Binary: `1110 001 0 0000 0 0011 0010 0000 1000 0000`
= **`0xE2030080`**

---

**(iv) `MOV R0, R1, LSR #4`** — register shift
- `cond=1110, I=0, opcode=1101 (MOV), S=0, Rn=0000 (ignored), Rd=0000, Rm=0001, shift=01 (LSR), amount=00100`

Binary: `1110 000 1 1011 0 0000 0000 01 0 00100 0001`
= **`0xE1A01081`** *(R0 = R1 >> 4)* (7 marks)

---

### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **16 condition codes** available in the ARM instruction set with their mnemonics and flag-test interpretations. How does this feature improve performance over traditional branch-based control flow? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable example, demonstrate how **multi-word (64-bit) addition** is implemented using `ADD` and `ADC` instructions. Why is the `S` bit mandatory on the lower `ADD`? Compute the condition flags generated by `SUBS R3, R3, #1` when R3 = 0x00000000. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Condition Codes (7 marks):**
Refer to the table in **Section 2.5** for the complete list.

**Performance benefit:**
In traditional CISC/RISC ISAs, conditional logic requires a `CMP` followed by a `Bxx` (branch) instruction. Branches cause **pipeline flushes** (3–5 cycle penalty in classic ARM). In ARM, almost every instruction can be *predicated*:

> Example: convert `if (a==b) x++;` to a single predicated instruction:
> `CMP R0, R1; ADDEQ R2, R2, #1`

The `EQ` predicate evaluates the flags and the `ADD` is executed only if Z=1. **No branch, no flush, no branch predictor needed.** This dramatically reduces dynamic instruction count and energy consumption in tight loops. (7 marks)

---

**(b) 64-bit Addition (7 marks):**

**Example:** Add `R1:R0` (64-bit) to `R3:R2` (64-bit), store in `R5:R4`.

```text
ADDS  R4, R0, R2     ; lower word, S=1 to record carry
ADC   R5, R1, R3     ; upper word, carry-in added automatically
```

**Why `S` bit is mandatory on lower `ADD`:**
The carry output of the lower-word addition must be visible to the upper-word `ADC`. If `S=0` on the lower `ADD`, the carry flag is not updated, and `ADC` would add `0` (the old C value) instead of the actual carry. This silently corrupts the 64-bit result.

**CPSR flags after `SUBS R3, R3, #1` with R3 = 0x00000000:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Result} &= 0 - 1 = 0\text{x}FFFFFFFF \\
N &= \text{MSB(result)} = 1 \\
Z &= (\text{result} = 0) = 0 \\
C &= (0 \geq 1) = 0 \;\; \text{(borrow occurred, C cleared)} \\
V &= 1 \;\; \text{(signed overflow: 0 - 1 cannot be represented in 32-bit signed)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**CPSR after execution: N=1, Z=0, C=0, V=1.** (7 marks)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Reasons Students Lose Marks in ARM Data Processing Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the pipeline offset**: Reading R15 (PC) returns `PC+8`, not `PC+4`. Many students compute branch targets incorrectly. **Always** add 8 for PC-relative addressing in pre-ARMv7.
> 2. **Confusing `SUB` carry with borrow**: In ARM, the C flag is **set on no-borrow** (C=1 means unsigned underflow did *not* occur). Students often invert this. Remember: `C = (Rn ≥ Op2)` for `SUB`.
> 3. **Confusing signed vs unsigned overflow**: `V` is for *signed* overflow only; `C` is for *unsigned*. For `SUBS` on 0, V=1 (signed), C=0 (unsigned borrow).
> 4. **Comparing with shifted registers**: `CMP R0, R1, LSL #2` compares R0 with R1×4 — students frequently forget the shift semantics.
> 5. **Misusing the `I` bit**: An immediate larger than 0xFF cannot be used directly; the assembler uses rotation. `MOV R0, #0x100` is encoded as `0x01 ROR 24`, not as a 9-bit literal.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ARM data processing instructions are **32 bits long** and operate **only on registers** (no memory access).
- The 32-bit format is: `[cond 00 I opcode S Rn Rd Operand2]`.
- **Operand2** is the *flexible second operand*: an 8-bit rotated immediate (`I=1`) or a shifted register (`I=0`).
- The **barrel shifter** executes `LSL, LSR, ASR, ROR, RRX` in a single cycle with zero latency.
- The `S` bit (bit 20) controls whether the **CPSR flags N, Z, C, V** are updated.
- The `cond` field (bits 31:28) implements **conditional execution** for all 16 conditions — a major ARM code-density feature.
- **Arithmetic opcodes**: ADD, ADC, SUB, SBC, RSB, RSC.
- **Logical opcodes**: AND, ORR, EOR, BIC.
- **Move opcodes**: MOV (Rd = Op2), MVN (Rd = ~Op2) — both ignore Rn.
- **Comparison opcodes**: CMP, CMN, TST, TEQ — set flags only, discard Rd.
- **Carry flag** in `SUB`: C=1 if `Rn ≥ Op2` (no borrow), C=0 otherwise.
- **Overflow flag** V: signed two's-complement overflow only.
- **Multi-word arithmetic** requires `S=1` on the lower `ADD`/`SUB` so the carry propagates to the `ADC`/`SBC` of the next word.
- `R15` (PC) reads as `PC+8` due to the two-stage pipeline.
- Immediate constants larger than 0xFF are constructed by rotating an 8-bit value by an **even** number of bits: `imm32 = imm8 ROR (2×rotate)`.
- ARM data processing instructions are the **backbone of real-time embedded firmware**, used in motor control, signal processing, IoT, automotive, and mobile platforms.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Branch Instructions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCIT404 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Branch Instructions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🎯 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Branch Instructions** are a class of program control transfer instructions in the **8086 microprocessor** that modify the default sequential flow of execution by loading a new value into the **Instruction Pointer (IP)** or by simultaneously loading values into both **Code Segment (CS)** and **IP**. These instructions are essential for implementing decision-making logic, iteration, and modular programming in assembly language.

In the 8086 architecture, the **Instruction Pointer (IP)** holds the **16-bit offset** of the next instruction to be fetched. A branch instruction alters this pointer — either directly (near jump), indirectly (through a register/memory), or via the stack (CALL/RET) — thereby changing the address from which the next instruction is fetched.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Address Generation Rule (KTU 2024 High-Yield Concept):**
> $$\text{Physical Address} = (\text{CS} \times 10\text{H}) + \text{IP}$$
> When a far branch is executed, **both** `CS` and `IP` are reloaded. For a near branch, only `IP` is modified (CS remains unchanged).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "GPS Navigation" Analogy 🗺️

Imagine you are driving on a **one-way highway** following a pre-planned route (sequential execution):

| 8086 Concept | Driving Analogy | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Sequential Flow** | Driving straight on the highway | Default behavior — `IP` auto-increments |
| **JMP (Unconditional Jump)** | Taking a U-turn / diversion sign "Go to City B" | Forcibly redirects the bus to a new stop |
| **Conditional Jump (JZ, JNZ, etc.)** | Traffic signal — "Go only if light is GREEN" | Decision based on a condition (flag) |
| **LOOP** | A bus running a fixed route N times | Repeats a block until a counter hits zero |
| **CALL / RET** | Subcontracting work to a specialist workshop | Branch to a subroutine and come back |
| **INT (Interrupt)** | Emergency siren — emergency services dispatched | Hardware/software triggered branch |

> [!TIP]
> Just as a **GPS recalculates the route** when you miss a turn, the 8086 **recalculates the next fetch address** when a branch instruction is decoded.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Branch Instructions (KTU Syllabus)

The KTU 2024 scheme classifies branch instructions into **four major categories**:

1. **Unconditional Branch Instructions** — `JMP`
2. **Conditional Branch Instructions** — `JE/JZ`, `JNE/JNZ`, `JG`, `JL`, `JC`, `JNC`, `JO`, `JNO`, `JS`, `JNS`, etc.
3. **Iteration (Loop) Instructions** — `LOOP`, `LOOPE/LOOPZ`, `LOOPNE/LOOPNZ`
4. **Subroutine (Call) Instructions** — `CALL` (near/far), `RET` (near/far), `IRET`

> [!NOTE]
> The **Mnemonic Boundary**: Branch instructions in 8086 can jump a **maximum of ±127 bytes** (signed 8-bit displacement) or anywhere within the **current segment** (16-bit displacement for NEAR), or anywhere in the **1 MB memory** (FAR jump with new CS:IP).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The CPU Execution Cycle & Why Branching is Necessary

The 8086 executes instructions through the **Fetch–Decode–Execute** cycle. During sequential execution, after every byte of an instruction is fetched, the **BIU (Bus Interface Unit)** automatically increments `IP`. To redirect this flow, branch instructions must:

1. Compute the **target address** (either absolute or relative to `IP`).
2. **Load** the target into `IP` (and `CS` if required).
3. **Flush the prefetch queue**, because the bytes already fetched belong to the old path.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pipeline Penalty:** A branch instruction causes the 8086 to **flush the 6-byte prefetch queue**, resulting in a performance penalty of 2–6 clock cycles. This is why branch instructions are placed strategically, not in tight inner loops.

---

## 2.2 Category 1: Unconditional Jump (`JMP`)

The `JMP` instruction transfers control to a target address **without checking any condition**.

### 2.2.1 Near (Intrasegment) Jump

Only `IP` is modified. `CS` remains unchanged. The jump stays within the **current code segment**.

| Syntax | Operand Size | Effective Operation |
|---|---|---|
| `JMP rel16` | 16-bit relative | $\text{IP}_{\text{new}} = \text{IP}_{\text{current}} + \text{displacement} + 2$ |
| `JMP rel8` | 8-bit relative (Short) | $\text{IP}_{\text{new}} = \text{IP}_{\text{current}} + \text{disp8} + 2$ |
| `JMP reg16` | Indirect via register | $\text{IP} \leftarrow [\text{reg16}]$ |
| `JMP mem16` | Indirect via memory | $\text{IP} \leftarrow [\text{mem16}]$ |

### 2.2.2 Far (Intersegment) Jump

Both `CS` and `IP` are reloaded. Control can transfer to **any address in the 1 MB memory**.

| Syntax | Operand | Operation |
|---|---|---|
| `JMP rel32` (in 80386+) | 32-bit ptr | $\text{CS} \leftarrow \text{segment}, \quad \text{IP} \leftarrow \text{offset}$ |
| `JMP mem32` (FAR PTR) | 32-bit memory | Loads CS from `[mem32+2]`, IP from `[mem32]` |
| `JMP FAR PTR label` | Direct far | $\text{CS:IP} \leftarrow \text{target}$ |

---

## 2.3 Category 2: Conditional Branch Instructions

These instructions check the **state of the Flag Register** and branch only if the condition is **TRUE**. They are all **near (short) jumps** with an **8-bit signed displacement** of range **−128 to +127 bytes**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Conditional jumps are **always SHORT** in 8086 (not NEAR). If the target is more than 127 bytes away, you must insert an **intermediate unconditional `JMP`** to bypass the limit.

### 2.3.1 Flag Status After CMP / SUB / TEST

Conditional jumps are typically used **after** an instruction that sets the flags:

$$\text{CMP A, B} \implies \text{computes } A - B \text{ (discards result, sets flags only)}$$

| Flag | Bit | Symbol | Condition Set By |
|---|---|---|---|
| Carry | 0 | CF | Arithmetic overflow / borrow |
| Parity | 2 | PF | Even parity of LSB result |
| Auxiliary | 4 | AF | BCD carry between bits 3–4 |
| Zero | 6 | ZF | Result = 0 |
| Sign | 7 | SF | MSB of result = 1 (negative) |
| Overflow | 11 | OF | Signed overflow |

### 2.3.2 Conditional Jump Mnemonics — Complete KTU Reference Table

#### 2.3.2.1 Unsigned Conditional Jumps

| Mnemonic | Condition Checked | Branch If | Typical Companion |
|---|---|---|---|
| `JE` / `JZ` | ZF = 1 | Equal / Zero | After `CMP` of equal values |
| `JNE` / `JNZ` | ZF = 0 | Not Equal / Not Zero | Default for inequality |
| `JA` / `JNBE` | CF = 0 AND ZF = 0 | Above (>) | Strictly greater unsigned |
| `JAE` / `JNB` / `JNC` | CF = 0 | Above or Equal (≥) | No carry / no borrow |
| `JB` / `JNAE` / `JC` | CF = 1 | Below (<) | Carry / borrow occurred |
| `JBE` / `JNA` | CF = 1 OR ZF = 1 | Below or Equal (≤) | |

#### 2.3.2.2 Signed Conditional Jumps

| Mnemonic | Condition Checked | Branch If |
|---|---|---|
| `JE` / `JZ` | ZF = 1 | Equal |
| `JNE` / `JNZ` | ZF = 0 | Not Equal |
| `JG` / `JNLE` | ZF = 0 AND SF = OF | Greater (signed, >) |
| `JGE` / `JNL` | SF = OF | Greater or Equal (≥) |
| `JL` / `JNGE` | SF ≠ OF | Less (<) |
| `JLE` / `JNG` | ZF = 1 OR SF ≠ OF | Less or Equal (≤) |

#### 2.3.2.3 Single-Flag Conditional Jumps

| Mnemonic | Condition | Use |
|---|---|---|
| `JC` | CF = 1 | Check for unsigned overflow |
| `JNC` | CF = 0 | No carry |
| `JO` | OF = 1 | Signed overflow detected |
| `JNO` | OF = 0 | No signed overflow |
| `JS` | SF = 1 | Result is negative |
| `JNS` | SF = 0 | Result is non-negative |
| `JP` / `JPE` | PF = 1 | Even parity |
| `JNP` / `JPO` | PF = 0 | Odd parity |

---

## 2.4 Category 3: Loop Instructions (Iteration)

The 8086 provides three loop instructions, all of which use the **CX register** as an implicit counter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pre-decrement rule for CX:** Before each branch decision, the 8086 **decrements CX first**, then checks the exit condition. This is a critical KTU concept often tested.

### 2.4.1 `LOOP` Instruction

$$\text{LOOP label} \iff \text{Decrement CX; jump to label if } CX \neq 0$$

| Step | Action |
|---|---|
| 1 | $CX \leftarrow CX - 1$ |
| 2 | If $CX \neq 0$, then $IP \leftarrow IP + \text{disp8}$ |
| 3 | Else fall through (exit loop) |

### 2.4.2 `LOOPE` / `LOOPZ`

$$\text{LOOPE label} \iff \text{Decrement CX; jump to label if } CX \neq 0 \text{ AND } ZF = 1$$

Loop continues **only if** count is not exhausted **AND** the previous comparison set the zero flag (i.e., the compared values are still equal).

### 2.4.3 `LOOPNE` / `LOOPNZ`

$$\text{LOOPNE label} \iff \text{Decrement CX; jump to label if } CX \neq 0 \text{ AND } ZF = 0$$

Loop continues **only if** count is not exhausted **AND** values are still different.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU High-Yield Trap:** `LOOP` decrements CX **first**. So if you load `CX = 0001H`, the loop body executes **exactly once**, not zero times. Setting `CX = 0` results in **65,536 iterations** (because it decrements to `FFFFH` ≠ 0).

---

## 2.5 Category 4: Subroutine Instructions (`CALL` and `RET`)

### 2.5.1 `CALL` — Branch to Subroutine

`CALL` pushes the **return address** onto the **stack**, then transfers control to the target.

| Type | Bytes Pushed to Stack | Stack Order | Operation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Near CALL (direct)** | 2 bytes (IP) | IP first, then SP updated | $\text{IP} \leftarrow \text{IP} + \text{disp16}$ |
| **Near CALL (indirect)** | 2 bytes (IP) | IP from reg/mem | $\text{IP} \leftarrow [\text{reg16}]/[\text{mem16}]$ |
| **Far CALL (direct)** | 4 bytes (CS:IP) | CS first, then IP | $\text{CS:IP} \leftarrow \text{target}$ |
| **Far CALL (indirect)** | 4 bytes (CS:IP) | CS first, then IP | $\text{CS} \leftarrow [\text{mem32+2}], \text{IP} \leftarrow [\text{mem32}]$ |

### 2.5.2 `RET` — Return from Subroutine

`RET` pops the return address from the stack in **reverse order** (IP first, then CS) and resumes execution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Stack discipline during CALL/RET:** For NEAR: `[SP-1:SP-2] ← IP`, then `SP ← SP − 2`. For FAR: `[SP-1:SP-2] ← CS`, then `[SP-3:SP-4] ← IP`, then `SP ← SP − 4`.

### 2.5.3 Interrupt Return — `IRET`

`IRET` is a special 3-byte instruction that pops **IP, CS, and FLAGS** off the stack (in that order) and returns from an interrupt service routine. Total bytes popped: **6 bytes**.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick-Reference Master Table** (use this for last-minute KTU revision):

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Range / Magnitude |
|---|---|---|
| Physical Address | $PA = (CS \times 10\text{H}) + IP$ | 00000H to FFFFFH (1 MB) |
| Short Jump Range | $\text{IP}_{\text{new}} - \text{IP}_{\text{old}} = \text{disp8}$ | **−128 to +127 bytes** |
| Near Jump Range | $\text{IP}_{\text{new}} - \text{IP}_{\text{old}} = \text{disp16}$ | Entire code segment (64 KB) |
| LOOP Decrement | $CX \leftarrow CX - 1$ **first**, then test | Decrement is **unconditional** |
| NEAR CALL Push | Pushes **2 bytes** (IP only) | Stack grows down by 2 |
| FAR CALL Push | Pushes **4 bytes** (CS:IP) | CS pushed first, then IP |
| NEAR RET Pop | Pops **2 bytes** (IP) | SP increments up by 2 |
| FAR RET Pop | Pops **4 bytes** (IP, then CS) | IP popped first, then CS |
| IRET Pop | Pops **6 bytes** (IP, CS, Flags) | IP → CS → Flags order |
| Branch Inst. Size | 2 bytes (short) / 3 bytes (near) / 5 bytes (far) | Variable based on type |
| Queue Flush Penalty | 6 clock cycles (full flush) to 0 (best case) | Prefetch queue = 6 bytes |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Why does industry care about branch instructions?**
> 1. **Compiler Optimization:** Modern compilers (gcc, MSVC) generate assembly using `JE`, `JNE`, `JL`, etc. for `if-else` blocks.
> 2. **Operating Systems:** The OS scheduler uses `IRET` to return from interrupt handlers; system calls use `INT` followed by `IRET`.
> 3. **Real-Time Embedded Systems:** In 8051 / ARM Cortex controllers, branch prediction accuracy directly impacts worst-case execution time (WCET) — critical for ISO 26262 (automotive) and DO-178C (aerospace).
> 4. **Vulnerability Research:** Buffer overflow exploits overwrite the saved return address on the stack — understanding `CALL`/`RET` stack mechanics is fundamental to cybersecurity.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Problem 1: Calculating the Target Address of a Relative Jump

> **Problem Statement (KTU Pattern):**
> In an 8086 program, the instruction `JMP SHORT AHEAD` is located at physical address `20100H`. The instruction at the label `AHEAD` is located at `2014AH`. Verify whether the jump is possible. If not, suggest a fix. (IP after fetching the JMP = `20102H`)

### 3.1.1 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the components of the SHORT jump instruction.**

A `JMP SHORT label` is a **2-byte instruction**: opcode (1 byte) + 8-bit signed displacement (1 byte).

**Step 2 — Compute the required displacement.**

The 8086 uses **IP-relative** addressing. After fetching the entire 2-byte instruction, IP has already advanced to:

$$IP_{\text{after fetch}} = 20100\text{H} + 2 = 20102\text{H}$$

The displacement formula is:

$$\text{disp8} = IP_{\text{target}} - IP_{\text{after fetch}}$$

$$\text{disp8} = 2014\text{AH} - 20102\text{H}$$

**Step 3 — Perform the hexadecimal subtraction.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{disp8} &= 2014\text{AH} - 20102\text{H} \\
&= 0\text{x}014\text{A} - 0\text{x}0102 \\
&= 0\text{x}0048 \\
&= +72 \text{ (decimal)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify against the SHORT jump range.**

The signed 8-bit range is **−128 to +127**.

$$-128 \le +72 \le +127 \quad \checkmark$$

> **[A] Conclusion:** The jump is **valid**. The assembler will encode it as `EB 48H` (EB = SHORT JMP opcode, 48H = +72 signed).

**Step 5 — Suppose the target had been at `20200H` instead.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{disp8} &= 20200\text{H} - 20102\text{H} \\
&= 0\text{x}00\text{FE} = +254
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $+254 > +127$, the SHORT jump **fails** ❌.

**Step 6 — Apply the KTU fix: use NEAR jump or trampoline.**

> [!TIP]
> **Solution A (preferred):** Use `JMP NEAR PTR AHEAD` (3-byte instruction with 16-bit displacement).
>
> **Solution B (trampoline):** Insert an intermediate short jump:
>
> ```assembly
>         JMP SHORT SKIP
>         ; ... unreachable code ...
> SKIP:   JMP NEAR PTR AHEAD
> ```

---

## 3.2 Analytical Problem 2: Tracing the Stack During a FAR CALL / FAR RET

> **Problem Statement:**
> Initially, `SP = 2000H`, `CS = 3000H`, `IP = 1050H`. A `CALL FAR PTR SUBR` instruction (opcode `9A`) is executed. The operand `SUBR` is stored in memory as `4500H : 0200H` (segment:offset). Show the contents of the stack and the new values of CS, IP, and SP.

### 3.2.1 Step-by-Step Memory Trace

**Step 1 — Identify the target address.**

$$\text{Target CS:IP} = 4500\text{H} : 0200\text{H}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the return address (CS:IP of the instruction *after* the CALL).**

A `CALL FAR PTR` is a **5-byte instruction**: 1 (opcode) + 2 (offset operand) + 2 (segment operand).

$$IP_{\text{return}} = 1050\text{H} + 5 = 1055\text{H}$$

$$CS_{\text{return}} = 3000\text{H} \quad (\text{unchanged by this calculation})$$

**Step 3 — Push CS onto the stack first, then push IP.**

The 8086 pushes in the order: **CS first (high byte first), then IP**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Stack push sequence:} \quad & [SP-1 : SP-2] \leftarrow CS_{\text{return}} = 30\text{H}, 00\text{H} \\
& [SP-3 : SP-4] \leftarrow IP_{\text{return}} = 10\text{H}, 55\text{H} \\
& SP \leftarrow SP - 4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the new SP value.**

$$SP_{\text{new}} = 2000\text{H} - 4 = 1FF\text{CH}$$

**Step 5 — Populate the stack memory map.**

| Memory Address | Byte | Note |
|---|---|---|
| `1FFD H` | `55 H` | IP low byte (pushed last → highest addr) |
| `1FFC H` | `10 H` | IP high byte |
| `1FFB H` | `00 H` | CS low byte |
| `1FFA H` | `30 H` | CS high byte (pushed first → lowest addr) |

**Step 6 — Update CS and IP to the subroutine address.**

$$CS_{\text{new}} = 4500\text{H}, \quad IP_{\text{new}} = 0200\text{H}$$

> **Visual stack diagram (growing downward ↓):**
>
> ```
>   SP →  1FFC  [55 10]   ← IP (10:55)
>         1FFA  [00 30]   ← CS (30:00)
>         1FF8  ........   ← (old data)
> ```

---

## 3.3 Analytical Problem 3: LOOP Instruction Trace with Decrement

> **Problem Statement:**
> ```assembly
>     MOV CX, 0003H
> BACK:   ADD AL, BL
>         LOOP BACK
>         HLT
> ```
> Trace the value of CX and the execution path for each pass.

### 3.3.1 Detailed Iteration Table

| Iteration | CX (before decrement) | CX (after decrement) | ZF | Branch Taken? | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `0003H` | `0002H` | — | Yes (CX ≠ 0) | Execute `ADD AL, BL`, jump back |
| 2 | `0002H` | `0001H` | — | Yes (CX ≠ 0) | Execute `ADD AL, BL`, jump back |
| 3 | `0001H` | `0000H` | — | Yes (CX ≠ 0) | Execute `ADD AL, BL`, jump back |
| 4 | `0000H` | `FFFFH` | — | Yes (CX ≠ 0) | Execute `ADD AL, BL`, jump back |
| 5 | `FFFFH` | `FFFEH` | — | Yes (CX ≠ 0) | ... |
| ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... |
| 65536 | `0001H` | `0000H` | — | No (CX = 0) | Fall through to `HLT` |

> [!WARNING]
> **Total iterations = 3.** The loop body runs 3 times (CX = 3, 2, 1 → branch; CX = 0 → exit). The 8086 decrements **before** testing, so the loop body is executed for `CX = 3, 2, 1` only.

**Mathematical general formula:**

$$\text{Number of iterations} = \text{Initial value of } CX \quad (\text{when } CX \neq 0)$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Problem 4: Conditional Jump after CMP

> **Problem Statement:**
> Determine the branch outcome of the following:
> ```assembly
>     MOV AL, 0F5H       ; AL = 1111 0101 (signed = -11)
>     CMP AL, 0AH        ; Compare with 10
>     JL  LESS            ; Jump if AL < 0AH (signed)
>     JG  GREATER         ; Jump if AL > 0AH (signed)
>     JMP EQUAL
> LESS:
>     ...
> GREATER:
>     ...
> EQUAL:
>     ...
> ```

### 3.4.1 Step-by-Step Flag Analysis

**Step 1 — Compute AL − 0AH (discarding the result).**

$$0\text{F}5\text{H} - 0\text{AH} = 0\text{EBH}$$

In **8-bit signed** interpretation:

$$-11 - (+10) = -21 \rightarrow \text{Result} = 0\text{EBH} = -21 \text{ (signed)}$$

**Step 2 — Determine each flag's state.**

| Flag | Calculation | Value |
|---|---|---|
| CF (Carry) | Borrow occurred in unsigned subtraction | **1** (borrow) |
| ZF (Zero) | Result is not zero | **0** |
| SF (Sign) | MSB of `0EBH` = 1 | **1** |
| OF (Overflow) | −11 − (+10) is valid signed, no overflow | **0** |

**Step 3 — Test each conditional jump.**

- **`JL LESS`** (Jump if SF ≠ OF): `1 ≠ 0` → **TRUE** ✓ (Branch taken)
- **`JG GREATER`** (would be tested only if JL failed): Not tested
- **`JMP EQUAL`**: Not reached

> **[A] Conclusion:** Control transfers to `LESS`.

---

## 3.5 Comprehensive Analytical Matrix — All 8086 Branch Instructions

| Instruction | Type | Bytes | Addressing Mode | Flags Affected | Stack Operation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `JMP rel8` | Unconditional Short | 2 | IP-relative | None | None |
| `JMP rel16` | Unconditional Near | 3 | IP-relative | None | None |
| `JMP reg16` | Unconditional Near | 2 | Register indirect | None | None |
| `JMP mem16` | Unconditional Near | 2–4 | Memory indirect | None | None |
| `JMP mem32` | Unconditional Far | 5–6 | Memory indirect | None | None |
| `Jcc rel8` | Conditional Short | 2 | IP-relative | None (only checked) | None |
| `LOOP rel8` | Iteration | 2 | IP-relative | None | None (CX dec) |
| `LOOPE/LOOPZ` | Iteration | 2 | IP-relative | None (ZF checked) | None (CX dec) |
| `LOOPNE/LOOPNZ` | Iteration | 2 | IP-relative | None (ZF checked) | None (CX dec) |
| `CALL rel16` | Near Call (direct) | 3 | IP-relative | None | Push 2 bytes (IP) |
| `CALL reg16` | Near Call (indirect) | 2 | Register indirect | None | Push 2 bytes (IP) |
| `CALL mem16` | Near Call (indirect) | 2–4 | Memory indirect | None | Push 2 bytes (IP) |
| `CALL label (far)` | Far Call (direct) | 5 | Direct | None | Push 4 bytes (CS:IP) |
| `CALL mem32` | Far Call (indirect) | 5–6 | Memory indirect | None | Push 4 bytes (CS:IP) |
| `RET` | Near Return | 1 | — | None | Pop 2 bytes (IP) |
| `RET n` | Near Return with add | 3 | Immediate | None | Pop 2 bytes + SP+n |
| `RET (far)` | Far Return | 1 | — | None | Pop 4 bytes (IP, CS) |
| `IRET` | Interrupt Return | 1 | — | All restored | Pop 6 bytes (IP, CS, F) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Displacement Encoding for Forward Jumps:** A forward jump of `+N` bytes is encoded as `2's complement of N` only when the assembler is in 8086 mode. For most assemblers (MASM/TASM), the displacement is stored as a **signed integer** (e.g., +5 = `05H`, −5 = `FBH`).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🖼️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Branch Instruction Execution Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Instruction Fetch Stage] --> B{Decode Opcode}
    B -->|JMP Detected| C[Compute Target Address]
    B -->|Jcc Detected| D[Read Flag Register]
    B -->|LOOP Detected| E[Decrement CX]
    B -->|CALL Detected| F[Push CS:IP to Stack]
    D --> G{Condition True?}
    C --> H[Load New IP or CS:IP]
    G -->|Yes| H
    G -->|No| I[Continue Sequential Flow]
    E --> J{CX != 0?}
    J -->|Yes| H
    J -->|No| I
    F --> K[Load Subroutine CS:IP]
    H --> L[Flush Prefetch Queue]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Begin Fetch from Target Address]
    I --> N[Fetch Next Sequential Instruction]
    M --> O[Resume Execution]
    N --> O
```

---

## 4.2 Flowchart — Conditional Jump Decision Logic (Generic `Jcc`)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Decode Jcc Instruction]) --> GetDisp[Read 8-bit Signed Displacement]
    GetDisp --> ReadFlags[Sample Flag Register<br/>CF ZF SF OF PF AF]
    ReadFlags --> EvalCond{Condition Met?}
    EvalCond -->|TRUE| AddDisp[IP new = IP + disp8 + 2]
    EvalCond -->|FALSE| SeqFlow[IP auto-increment<br/>Sequential execution]
    AddDisp --> Flush[Flush Prefetch Queue]
    SeqFlow --> Continue[Fetch Next Instruction]
    Flush --> Continue
```

---

## 4.3 Stack Operation Diagram — Near CALL and Near RET

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph STACK_BEFORE[Stack Before CALL]
        SB1[SP → 1FFE H]
        SB2[1FFC H]
        SB3[1FFA H]
    end

    subgraph STACK_AFTER_CALL[Stack After CALL]
        SA1[SP → 1FFC H]
        SA2[1FFA H = Return IP Low Byte]
        SA3[1FF8 H = Return IP High Byte]
    end

    subgraph STACK_AFTER_RET[Stack After RET]
        SR1[SP → 1FFE H restored]
        SR2[1FFC H]
        SR3[1FFA H]
    end

    STACK_BEFORE -->|PUSH IP| STACK_AFTER_CALL
    STACK_AFTER_CALL -->|POP IP| STACK_AFTER_RET
```

---

## 4.4 Decision Tree — Choosing the Correct Conditional Jump

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start{What Comparison?}
    Start -->|Unsigned| Unsigned[Use JA JAE JB JBE JE JNE]
    Start -->|Signed| Signed[Use JG JGE JL JLE JE JNE]
    Start -->|Single Flag| Single[Use JC JNC JO JNO JS JNS JP JNP]

    Unsigned --> U1{Need Strictly Greater?}
    U1 -->|Yes| U2[Use JA JNBE]
    U1 -->|No, Greater or Equal| U3[Use JAE JNB JNC]

    Signed --> S1{Need Strictly Greater?}
    S1 -->|Yes| S2[Use JG JNLE]
    S1 -->|No, Greater or Equal| S3[Use JGE JNL]
```

---

## 4.5 Memory Layout Diagram — Far CALL in 1 MB Address Space

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MEM1[Memory Segment 1 - Main Code CS = 3000 H]
        M1Addr1[3000:1050 H] --> M1Call[CALL FAR PTR SUBR<br/>Opcode 9A + Offset 0200 H + Segment 4500 H]
        M1Call --> M1Ret[3000:1055 H<br/>Return Address stored on stack]
    end

    subgraph MEM2[Memory Segment 2 - Subroutine CS = 4500 H]
        M2Entry[4500:0200 H<br/>Subroutine Entry Point]
        M2Entry --> M2Body[Subroutine Body]
        M2Body --> M2Ret[RET FAR<br/>Pops CS:IP from stack]
    end

    M1Ret -.Push 4 bytes.-> Stack[(Stack Memory<br/>1FFA-1FFD H)]
    M2Ret -.Pop 4 bytes.-> Stack
    Stack -.Restores.-> M1Ret
```

---

## 4.6 Block-Level Architecture — Branch Instruction in 8086 BIU/EU

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BIU[Bus Interface Unit]
        B1[Instruction Queue<br/>6 bytes]
        B2[IP Register]
        B3[CS Register]
        B4[Address Adder<br/>PA = CS x 10H + IP]
    end

    subgraph EU[Execution Unit]
        E1[Instruction Decoder]
        E2[Flag Register<br/>CF ZF SF OF PF AF]
        E3[Control Logic]
        E4[General Registers<br/>AX BX CX DX]
    end

    B1 -->|Fetched bytes| E1
    E1 -->|Decoded opcode| E3
    E2 -->|Flag status| E3
    E4 -->|Register values| E3
    E3 -->|New CS:IP target| B2
    E3 -->|New CS value| B3
    B3 --> B4
    B2 --> B4
    B4 -->|Physical Address| Bus[20-bit Address Bus]
```
