# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**

### **Q1. Define a branch instruction. Differentiate between a near jump and a far jump in 8086.**

**Model Answer:**

A **branch instruction** in 8086 is a program control instruction that alters the sequential flow of execution by modifying the **Instruction Pointer (IP)** or both **CS and IP**. This enables decision-making, looping, and subroutine calls.

| Parameter | Near Jump (Intrasegment) | Far Jump (Intersegment) |
|---|---|---|
| Registers Modified | Only **IP** | Both **CS** and **IP** |
| Segment Boundary | Stays within current code segment | Can cross segment boundary |
| Range | Up to 64 KB (current segment) | Anywhere in 1 MB memory |
| Instruction Size | 2 bytes (short) or 3 bytes (near) | 5 bytes (direct) |
| Physical Address | CS remains same | New CS value loaded |

---

### **Q2. What is the role of the `LOOP` instruction? Explain the execution sequence with reference to the CX register.**

**Model Answer:**

The `LOOP` instruction is used to implement **counted iteration loops** in 8086. It implicitly uses the **CX register** as a counter.

**Execution sequence:**

1. **Decrement** `CX` by 1 (i.e., $CX \leftarrow CX - 1$).
2. **Check**: if $CX \neq 0$, then branch to the target label (a SHORT jump with 8-bit displacement).
3. If $CX = 0$, **fall through** to the next instruction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since `LOOP` decrements CX **before** testing, the loop body executes exactly **N times** when CX is initialized to N. If CX = 0 initially, the loop executes **65,536 times** (since decrementing 0 gives 0xFFFF ≠ 0).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Branch Instructions:**
> 1. Students often **forget to specify SHORT or NEAR PTR** when writing jump syntax — this costs 1 mark.
> 2. In `LOOP` trace questions, students commonly write the **initial** value of CX at the wrong iteration — remember: **decrement first, then test**.
> 3. For `CALL`/`RET` questions, students often **swap the push/pop order** of CS and IP. The correct order is: **CALL pushes CS first, then IP; RET pops IP first, then CS**.
> 4. Conditional jump questions: students confuse **signed (`JG/JL`) vs unsigned (`JA/JB`)** jumps. The flag-check formulas differ!

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — Branch Instruction Mechanisms

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**(a) Explain the different types of branch instructions in 8086 with suitable examples. Discuss the role of the flag register in conditional jumps.** **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

The 8086 supports **four major types** of branch instructions:

**1. Unconditional Jump (`JMP`):**

Transfers control without any condition check. Available in two forms:
- **Near (Intrasegment):** Only `IP` is modified. Example: `JMP NEXT` — control jumps to label `NEXT` within the same segment.
- **Far (Intersegment):** Both `CS` and `IP` are modified. Example: `JMP FAR PTR 4000H:0100H` — control jumps to physical address `40000H + 0100H = 40100H`.

**2. Conditional Jump (`Jcc`):**

Branch taken **only if a specific flag condition is satisfied**. These are always SHORT jumps (2 bytes, ±127 byte range). The flag register plays a central role:

| Flag | Checked Jumps | Example Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| ZF | `JE`, `JNE` | Equality check after `CMP` |
| CF | `JC`, `JNC` | Unsigned overflow / borrow |
| SF, OF | `JG`, `JL`, `JGE`, `JLE` | Signed comparisons |
| PF | `JP`, `JNP` | Parity checking (serial comm.) |
| SF | `JS`, `JNS` | Sign bit testing |

**Example sequence:**

```assembly
    CMP AL, BL      ; Sets ZF, CF, SF, OF based on AL - BL
    JE  EQUAL       ; Jump if AL == BL
    JL  LESS        ; Jump if AL < BL (signed)
    JG  GREATER     ; Jump if AL > BL (signed)
```

**3. Loop Instructions:**

Used for iteration. All three variants (`LOOP`, `LOOPE`, `LOOPNE`) use `CX` as a counter and are SHORT jumps.

**4. Subroutine Instructions (`CALL`, `RET`, `IRET`):**

Used for modular programming. `CALL` pushes the return address onto the stack; `RET` pops it back.

> **Role of Flag Register:** The flag register acts as a **"decision state memory"** for the CPU. Arithmetic/logic instructions set these flags; conditional jumps read them. Without flags, the CPU would have no mechanism for implementing `if-else` logic at the hardware level.

---

**(b) With a neat diagram, explain the execution of a `CALL` and `RET` instruction. Differentiate between near and far CALL.** **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Near CALL (Intrasegment):**

A near CALL is a **3-byte instruction** (direct form) consisting of opcode + 16-bit displacement. It pushes only the **IP** of the next instruction onto the stack.

**Execution steps:**

1. $SP \leftarrow SP - 2$ (stack grows down)
2. $\text{[SP]} \leftarrow IP_{\text{return}}$ (return address saved)
3. $IP \leftarrow IP + \text{displacement}$ (target address loaded)

**Near RET:**

1. $IP \leftarrow \text{[SP]}$ (restore return address)
2. $SP \leftarrow SP + 2$ (pop)

**Far CALL (Intersegment):**

A far CALL is a **5-byte instruction** (direct form) with opcode + 16-bit offset + 16-bit segment. It pushes **both CS and IP** onto the stack.

**Execution steps:**

1. $SP \leftarrow SP - 2$
2. $\text{[SP]} \leftarrow CS_{\text{return}}$ (CS pushed first)
3. $SP \leftarrow SP - 2$
4. $\text{[SP]} \leftarrow IP_{\text{return}}$ (IP pushed second)
5. $CS \leftarrow \text{new segment value}$
6. $IP \leftarrow \text{new offset value}$

**Far RET:**

1. $IP \leftarrow \text{[SP]}$, $SP \leftarrow SP + 2$ (IP popped first)
2. $CS \leftarrow \text{[SP]}$, $SP \leftarrow SP + 2$ (CS popped second)

**Stack Diagram:**

```
        ← Lower Addresses (Stack grows down)
        ┌─────────────────────┐
   SP → │ IP_low   IP_high    │  ← Saved return IP
        │ CS_low   CS_high    │  ← Saved return CS  (FAR only)
        └─────────────────────┘
        ← Higher Addresses
```

| Parameter | Near CALL | Far CALL |
|---|---|---|
| Bytes Pushed | 2 (IP) | 4 (CS:IP) |
| Register Modified | IP only | Both CS and IP |
| Segment Crossing | No | Yes |
| Instruction Size | 3 bytes | 5 bytes |

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Module Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Pattern]**

**(a) Explain the different types of conditional jump instructions in 8086 with a focus on signed vs unsigned comparisons. Provide an example program to find the largest of three numbers.** **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

The 8086 provides **two sets** of conditional jumps based on the data type:

**1. Unsigned Conditional Jumps (Compare magnitude):**

These checks use **CF and ZF** flags because unsigned subtraction can only overflow into the carry flag.

| Mnemonic | Condition | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `JA` / `JNBE` | CF=0 AND ZF=0 | Above (>), no carry, not zero |
| `JAE` / `JNB` | CF=0 | Above or Equal (≥) |
| `JB` / `JNAE` / `JC` | CF=1 | Below (<), carry occurred |
| `JBE` / `JNA` | CF=1 OR ZF=1 | Below or Equal (≤) |

**2. Signed Conditional Jumps (Compare true value):**

These use **SF, OF, and ZF** flags. The relationship $SF \oplus OF$ indicates whether the result is negative in signed arithmetic.

| Mnemonic | Condition | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `JG` / `JNLE` | ZF=0 AND SF=OF | Greater (signed, >) |
| `JGE` / `JNL` | SF=OF | Greater or Equal (≥) |
| `JL` / `JNGE` | SF≠OF | Less (<) |
| `JLE` / `JNG` | ZF=1 OR SF≠OF | Less or Equal (≤) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the difference?** For **unsigned** numbers, the carry flag indicates overflow. For **signed** numbers, the overflow flag is used because the sign bit (bit 7) carries the sign information. Using `JA` on signed data gives **wrong results**.

**Example Program — Find largest of three unsigned numbers (in AX, BX, CX):**

```assembly
    ASSUME CS:CODE
CODE SEGMENT
START:
    MOV AX, 1234H     ; First number
    MOV BX, 5678H     ; Second number
    MOV CX, 9ABCH     ; Third number

    ; Assume AX holds the largest so far
    CMP AX, BX
    JAE CHECK_CX      ; If AX >= BX, check CX
    MOV AX, BX        ; Else AX = BX

CHECK_CX:
    CMP AX, CX
    JAE DONE          ; If AX >= CX, AX is largest
    MOV AX, CX        ; Else AX = CX

DONE:
    ; AX now contains the largest value
    HLT
CODE ENDS
END START
```

**Key insight:** This program uses `JAE` because we are treating the numbers as **unsigned** 16-bit values.

---

**(b) Explain the working of `LOOP`, `LOOPE`, and `LOOPNE` instructions with examples. Why is `LOOP` not suitable for large iteration counts beyond 65,535?** **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

All three loop instructions are **2-byte SHORT jumps** that use the **CX register** as an implicit counter.

**1. `LOOP label`:**

- **Decrement** $CX \leftarrow CX - 1$
- If $CX \neq 0$, jump to label; else fall through.

**Example — Sum of first N natural numbers:**

```assembly
    MOV CX, 0005H     ; N = 5
    MOV AX, 0000H     ; Sum = 0
    MOV BX, 0001H     ; I = 1
BACK:
    ADD AX, BX        ; Sum = Sum + I
    INC BX            ; I = I + 1
    LOOP BACK         ; Repeat 5 times
    HLT
```

**2. `LOOPE` / `LOOPZ label`:**

- Decrement $CX$.
- Jump if $CX \neq 0$ **AND** $ZF = 1$ (i.e., previous comparison set ZF).

**Example — Find first mismatch in two strings:**

```assembly
    MOV CX, 10
    MOV SI, OFFSET STR1
    MOV DI, OFFSET STR2
NEXT:
    CMPSB             ; Compare bytes, set ZF if equal
    LOOPE NEXT        ; Continue while equal AND CX not zero
    ; After exit: ZF=1 means all matched, ZF=0 means mismatch found
```

**3. `LOOPNE` / `LOOPNZ label`:**

- Decrement $CX$.
- Jump if $CX \neq 0$ **AND** $ZF = 0$.

**Example — Find first match:**

```assembly
    MOV CX, 10
    MOV AL, 'X'       ; Search character
NEXT:
    SCASB             ; Compare AL with [DI], set ZF if match
    LOOPNE NEXT       ; Continue while no match AND CX not zero
```

**Why `LOOP` is limited to 65,535 iterations:**

The `CX` register is **16 bits wide**, so its maximum value is $2^{16} - 1 = 65,535$. To iterate more than 65,535 times, you must:

- **Use nested loops** (outer loop manages a high-order counter).
- **Increment a 32-bit variable** manually using `ADC` (add with carry).
- **Use 80386+ `ECX`** with the 32-bit prefix (not in plain 8086).

**Example — Nested loop for 100,000 iterations:**

```assembly
    MOV DX, 0001H     ; Outer counter (high 16 bits)
OUTER:
    MOV CX, 86A0H     ; Inner = 0x86A0 = 34,496 (loops until DX=0)
INNER:
    ; Loop body here
    LOOP INNER
    DEC DX
    JNZ OUTER
```

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✨

> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Checklist — Branch Instructions**

- 📌 **Branch instructions** are program control transfer instructions in 8086 that modify `IP` (and possibly `CS`).
- 📌 **Four categories:** Unconditional `JMP`, Conditional `Jcc`, Iteration `LOOP`, Subroutine `CALL/RET`.
- 📌 **Physical Address Formula:** $PA = (CS \times 10\text{H}) + IP$ — this is **the single most tested formula** in 8086 addressing.
- 📌 **Conditional jumps are ALWAYS short** (±127 bytes). For larger jumps, use a NEAR `JMP` trampoline.
- 📌 **Flag register** is the decision-making backbone — `CMP`/`SUB`/`TEST` set flags; `Jcc` reads them.
- 📌 **Signed vs Unsigned jumps:** `JA/JB` for unsigned; `JG/JL` for signed. **Mixing them gives wrong results**.
- 📌 **`LOOP` decrements CX FIRST**, then tests. So initial CX = N means N iterations (not N+1).
- 📌 **CX = 0 trap:** Loading `CX = 0` causes the loop to run **65,536 times** (because decrement to 0xFFFF is non-zero).
- 📌 **`LOOPE`** continues **only if ZF = 1 AND CX ≠ 0** (used for "continue while equal").
- 📌 **`LOOPNE`** continues **only if ZF = 0 AND CX ≠ 0** (used for "continue while not equal").
- 📌 **Near CALL pushes 2 bytes** (IP only); **Far CALL pushes 4 bytes** (CS:IP).
- 📌 **Stack discipline for FAR CALL/RET:** `CALL` pushes **CS first, then IP**; `RET` pops **IP first, then CS**.
- 📌 **`IRET`** is a special return that pops **6 bytes** (IP, CS, Flags) and is used in interrupt service routines.
- 📌 **Branch penalty:** A taken branch flushes the 6-byte prefetch queue, costing 2–6 clock cycles.
- 📌 **Indirect jumps** use `JMP reg16` or `JMP mem16` for near, and `JMP mem32` for far — useful for **jump tables** in `switch-case` implementations.

> **🔥 Top 3 KTU High-Yield Questions to Practice:**
> 1. Trace the stack for a `CALL FAR PTR` and corresponding `RET FAR`.
> 2. Given physical addresses of a JMP and its target, verify if it's a valid short jump.
> 3. Determine the number of loop iterations and branch outcomes for a given `LOOPE` program with a sequence of comparisons.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Load Store Instructions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCIT404 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Load Store Instructions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the **ARM (Advanced RISC Machine) architecture**, **Load/Store Instructions** are a category of data-processing instructions that are the *only* instructions permitted to access external memory (data memory). They form the cornerstone of the **Load-Store RISC design philosophy** of the ARM processor.

- A **Load Instruction** copies a value from memory into a CPU register (e.g., `LDR`, `LDRB`, `LDRH`).
- A **Store Instruction** copies a value from a CPU register into memory (e.g., `STR`, `STRB`, `STRH`).
- All arithmetic, logical, and data-movement operations between registers operate entirely *within* the register file and never touch memory directly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> In ARM RISC architecture, **memory is accessed ONLY via Load/Store instructions**. This is a fundamental property tested extensively in KTU examinations. A student who forgets this fact will lose marks in any question that asks "Why is ARM called a Load-Store architecture?"

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the **CPU registers** as the *workbench* of a carpenter, and the **memory (RAM)** as a *tool cabinet* in another room of the workshop.

- The carpenter (the CPU) can do all hammering, sawing, and gluing (arithmetic/logic operations) only with tools already on the workbench (registers).
- When he needs a new tool, he must **walk to the cabinet and bring it back** to the workbench. This trip is a **LOAD instruction** (`LDR`).
- When he is done with a tool and wants to put it away, he must **carry the tool back to the cabinet**. This trip is a **STORE instruction** (`STR`).
- He is *never* allowed to use a tool directly from the cabinet — he must always bring it to the workbench first.

This is exactly how ARM works. Registers are the workbench, memory is the cabinet, and Load/Store instructions are the only "trips" permitted.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Board Standard Vocabulary)

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **Rn** | Base Register — holds the starting address |
| **Rd** | Destination Register (for Load) / Source Register (for Store) |
| **Offset** | A 12-bit unsigned or signed value added to the base address |
| **Effective Address (EA)** | The final calculated memory address that is accessed |
| **Writeback (`!`)** | Updating the base register `Rn` with the new computed address |
| **Pre-indexed** | Offset is applied *before* using the address |
| **Post-indexed** | Base address is used *first*, then offset is applied to update `Rn` |

> [!NOTE]
> The standard sizes accessed by Load/Store instructions in ARM are:
> - **Byte (8 bits)** — `B` suffix
> - **Halfword (16 bits)** — `H` suffix
> - **Word (32 bits)** — no suffix (default)
> - **Signed Byte/Halfword** — `SB` / `SH` suffix (sign-extends into Rd)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Load/Store Instructions

ARM Load/Store instructions are broadly divided into three families based on the *granularity* of data transferred:

### A. Single-Register Load/Store
- Transfers exactly **one register's worth** of data to/from memory.
- **Word (32-bit):** `LDR`, `STR`
- **Unsigned Byte (8-bit):** `LDRB`, `STRB`
- **Unsigned Halfword (16-bit):** `LDRH`, `STRH`
- **Signed Byte:** `LDRSB` (sign-extends to 32 bits)
- **Signed Halfword:** `LDRSH` (sign-extends to 32 bits)

### B. Multiple-Register Load/Store
- Transfers **multiple registers in a single instruction**.
- **Load Multiple:** `LDM` (e.g., `LDMIA`, `LDMFD`, etc.)
- **Store Multiple:** `STM` (e.g., `STMIA`, `STMEA`, etc.)
- Used heavily for **stack operations**, **register save/restore** during function calls, and **block memory copy**.

### C. Single-Register Swap
- Atomic swap of a register and a memory location.
- `SWP`, `SWPB` (largely obsolete in modern ARM, replaced by exclusive accesses `LDREX`/`STREX`).

## 2.2 The Three Addressing Modes (KTU Most-Frequently-Tested Topic)

Each Load/Store instruction can be written in **one of three addressing modes**. The KTU board routinely asks for the effective address calculation in all three.

### Mode 1: Pre-Indexed Addressing
- **Syntax:** `LDR Rd, [Rn, #offset]`
- **Logic:** Offset is added to base address *first*. The result is used as the address for memory access. `Rn` is **NOT** modified.
- **Effective Address:** `EA = Rn + offset`
- **Use case:** Safe, non-destructive read of a memory location.

### Mode 2: Pre-Indexed with Writeback
- **Syntax:** `LDR Rd, [Rn, #offset]!`   (note the `!`)
- **Logic:** Offset is added to base address *first*. The result is used as the memory access address. `Rn` is then **updated** to the new value (writeback).
- **Effective Address:** `EA = Rn + offset`, then `Rn ← Rn + offset`
- **Use case:** Stepping through arrays (pointer auto-increment).

### Mode 3: Post-Indexed Addressing
- **Syntax:** `LDR Rd, [Rn], #offset`
- **Logic:** The base address `Rn` is used *as is* for the memory access. **After** the access, `Rn` is updated to `Rn + offset` (writeback always happens).
- **Effective Address:** `EA = Rn`, then `Rn ← Rn + offset`
- **Use case:** Stack operations, FIFO queue traversal.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Memorize the following table completely. It covers **100% of numerical questions** asked on Load/Store instructions in KTU exams.

| Addressing Mode | Assembly Syntax | Effective Address (EA) | Writeback? |
|-----------------|-----------------|------------------------|------------|
| Pre-Indexed | `LDR Rd, [Rn, #offset]` | `EA = Rn + offset` | **No** |
| Pre-Indexed with Writeback | `LDR Rd, [Rn, #offset]!` | `EA = Rn + offset` | **Yes** |
| Post-Indexed | `LDR Rd, [Rn], #offset` | `EA = Rn` | **Yes (always)** |

### Key Size & Range Rules

| Instruction | Data Width Loaded/Stored | Alignment Rule | Value Range of Offset |
|-------------|--------------------------|----------------|----------------------|
| `LDR` / `STR` | 32 bits (Word) | Word address must be multiple of 4 | Immediate: 0 to 4095 (12-bit unsigned) |
| `LDRB` / `STRB` | 8 bits (Byte) | Any byte address | 0 to 4095 |
| `LDRH` / `STRH` | 16 bits (Halfword) | Halfword address must be multiple of 2 | 0 to 255 (8-bit) |
| `LDRSB` / `LDRSH` | Sign-extends to 32 bits | Same as B / H | 0 to 255 (8-bit) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is there no `STRSB` or `STRSH`?**
> Sign-extension is meaningful *only* when loading into a wider register. When storing, you simply write the byte or halfword as-is. Therefore ARM provides signed variants only for LOAD.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Load/Store instructions are the workhorses of:

- **Embedded C compilation:** Every `*p++` in C compiles to a post-indexed `LDR` or `STR`.
- **Operating System context switching:** `LDMIA` / `STMIA` (or `LDMFD` / `STMFD`) save and restore the full register context of a process in microseconds.
- **Digital Signal Processing (DSP):** Array and buffer traversal in codecs (MP3, AAC) uses auto-increment addressing (writeback mode).
- **Peripheral driver programming:** Reading from memory-mapped peripheral registers (e.g., reading from `UART1_DR` at `0xE0001000`) is a single `LDR` instruction.

## 2.5 Multiple Register Load/Store (LDM/STM) — The 4-Mode Stack Behaviour

Multiple-register Load/Store uses an **Address Mode** suffix to control the order of address generation. This is a recurring KTU question.

| Suffix | Name | EA Generation | STM Use | LDM Use |
|--------|------|---------------|---------|---------|
| `IA` | Increment After | EA then `Rn ← Rn+4` | — | — |
| `IB` | Increment Before | `Rn ← Rn+4` then EA | — | — |
| `DA` | Decrement After | EA then `Rn ← Rn-4` | — | — |
| `DB` | Decrement Before | `Rn ← Rn-4` then EA | — | — |

Stack aliases:
- `STMFD` = `STMDB` (Full Descending Stack — most common in ARM)
- `LDMFD` = `STMIA`'s pair for `LDM` (Full Descending pop)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Identifying Effective Addresses (All 3 Modes)

**Given:** `R1 = 0x20000000`, `R2 = 0x00000010`

Evaluate the Effective Address and final register state for:

### Sub-problem (a): `LDR R3, [R1, #8]`
Pre-indexed, no writeback.

$$
\begin{aligned}
EA &= Rn + \text{offset} \\
EA &= 0x20000000 + 0x00000008 \\
EA &= 0x20000008
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** Memory word at `0x20000008` is loaded into `R3`. `R1` is **unchanged** (still `0x20000000`).

---

### Sub-problem (b): `LDR R3, [R1, #8]!`
Pre-indexed **with writeback**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
EA &= Rn + \text{offset} \\
EA &= 0x20000000 + 0x00000008 \\
EA &= 0x20000008 \\
\text{Writeback: } R1 &\leftarrow EA \\
R1 &= 0x20000008
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** Memory word at `0x20000008` is loaded into `R3`. **R1 is updated** to `0x20000008`.

---

### Sub-problem (c): `LDR R3, [R1], #8`
Post-indexed (writeback always happens).

$$
\begin{aligned}
EA &= Rn \text{ (no offset applied yet)} \\
EA &= 0x20000000 \\
\text{Memory access: } R3 &\leftarrow MEM[0x20000000] \\
\text{Writeback: } R1 &\leftarrow Rn + \text{offset} \\
R1 &= 0x20000000 + 0x00000008 \\
R1 &= 0x20000008
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** Memory word at `0x20000000` is loaded into `R3`. **R1 is updated** to `0x20000008`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** Notice the **subtle but critical difference** between (b) and (c): in (b), we read from `0x20000008`; in (c), we read from `0x20000000` (the original `R1` value). Always compute the EA **first** before writeback.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Sign-Extension Logic (LDRSH)

**Given:** Memory at `0x40000000` contains the halfword `0x8000` (binary `1000 0000 0000 0000`).

### Instruction: `LDRSH R4, [R0]`   (assuming `R0 = 0x40000000`)

**Step 1:** Read 16-bit value from memory.
$$
\text{Halfword} = 0x8000
$$

**Step 2:** Check the MSB (Most Significant Bit) of the halfword.
$$
MSB = 1 \quad (\text{negative number in two's complement})
$$

**Step 3:** Since this is a **signed** halfword load, replicate the MSB into the upper 16 bits of R4.
$$
\begin{aligned}
R4 &= \text{0x8000 (sign-extended to 32 bits)} \\
R4 &= 0xFFFF8000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** `R4 = 0xFFFF8000` (which is `-32768` in two's complement).

> If we had used `LDRH` instead, R4 would be `0x00008000` (zero-extended, decimal +32768). This is a classic KTU question that catches students who ignore the sign extension.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Multiple Register Store — Full Descending Stack

**Given:** `SP = 0x40001000`, registers `R4-R7` contain `0x11111111, 0x22222222, 0x33333333, 0x44444444`.

### Instruction: `STMFD SP!, {R4-R7}`

**STMFD = STMDB (Decrement Before).** This means SP is decremented *before* each store.

**Step 1:** Lowest-numbered register is stored at the *lowest* address.

$$
\begin{aligned}
SP &\leftarrow SP - 4 = 0x40000\text{FFC} \\
MEM[0x40000\text{FFC}] &\leftarrow R4 = 0x11111111 \\
SP &\leftarrow SP - 4 = 0x40000\text{FF8} \\
MEM[0x40000\text{FF8}] &\leftarrow R5 = 0x22222222 \\
SP &\leftarrow SP - 4 = 0x40000\text{FF4} \\
MEM[0x40000\text{FF4}] &\leftarrow R6 = 0x33333333 \\
SP &\leftarrow SP - 4 = 0x40000\text{FF0} \\
MEM[0x40000\text{FF0}] &\leftarrow R7 = 0x44444444 \\
\text{Final SP} &= 0x40000\text{FE8} \\
SP &\leftarrow SP - 4 = 0x40000\text{FE8 (final writeback)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** SP is now `0x40000FE8`. Registers R4–R7 are saved contiguously in memory.

The corresponding pop is `LDMFD SP!, {R4-R7}` (equivalent to `LDMIA`).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: The Load/Store Data Path

This diagram shows how a Load instruction (`LDR R3, [R1, #8]`) flows through the ARM data path.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Instruction Fetch Stage] --> B[Decode: Identify LDR opcode, Rn R1, Rd R3, offset 8]
    B --> C[Address Generation Unit AGU]
    C --> D["EA = Rn plus offset"]
    D --> E{Memory Access Type}
    E --> F[Data Memory Bus]
    F --> G[Read Data Returned 32 bits]
    G --> H[Barrel Shifter and Sign Extender]
    H --> I[Destination Register Rd R3]
    I --> J[Writeback to Register File]
    B --> K{Writeback Flag Set}
    K -->|Yes| L[Update Rn with new value]
    L --> J
```

## 4.2 Flowchart: Decoding the Three Addressing Modes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start: Encounter Load Store Instruction]) --> Q{Is there a exclamation suffix?}
    Q -->|No exclamation no offset after| M1[Mode 1 Pre Indexed]
    Q -->|Exclamation suffix present| M2[Mode 2 Pre Indexed with Writeback]
    Q -->|Offset appears after bracket| M3[Mode 3 Post Indexed]
    M1 --> A1["EA is Rn plus offset. Rn unchanged"]
    M2 --> A2["EA is Rn plus offset. Rn gets EA"]
    M3 --> A3["EA is Rn. Rn gets Rn plus offset"]
    A1 --> End([Memory access at EA])
    A2 --> End
    A3 --> End
```

## 4.3 Memory Map: Visualizing a Full Descending Stack Push

This diagram shows what memory looks like *after* `STMFD SP!, {R4-R7}` when SP was `0x40001000`.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MEM[Data Memory Region 0x40000FE0 to 0x40001000]
        direction TB
        A1["Address: 0x40000FE8 | SP points here AFTER push"]
        A2["Address: 0x40000FEC | R7 = 0x44444444"]
        A3["Address: 0x40000FF0 | R6 = 0x33333333"]
        A4["Address: 0x40000FF4 | R5 = 0x22222222"]
        A5["Address: 0x40000FF8 | R4 = 0x11111111"]
        A6["Address: 0x40000FFC | (old memory contents preserved)"]
        A7["Address: 0x40001000 | (original SP value BEFORE push)"]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** The stack grows *downward* in memory (from high address to low address). The final SP value `0x40000FE8` is at the top of the diagram, indicating the lowest memory address touched. This descending behavior is why it is called a **Full Descending** stack.

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Load-Store Concept vs Other Architectures

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ARM[ARM Load Store Architecture]
        A1[Register File] --> A2[ALU operates on registers ONLY]
        A2 --> A3[Result stays in register file]
        A1 -.Load.-> A4[Data Memory]
        A4 -.Store.-> A1
    end
    subgraph X86[Legacy Accumulator CISC Architecture]
        B1[Single Accumulator Reg] <--> B2[ALU]
        B2 <--> B3[Memory]
        B3 <--> B1
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading this diagram:** Notice that in the **ARM** branch, the only arrows crossing between the Register File and Memory are the *Load* and *Store* arrows. The ALU never talks to memory. In the **x86** branch, the ALU and memory exchange data freely. This is the central architectural difference that makes ARM simpler, faster, and more power-efficient.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Explain the Load-Store architecture of ARM processor. Why is it called a "Load-Store" architecture?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
In ARM RISC architecture, memory access is restricted to only two specific instruction types — **Load** and **Store**. Load instructions (e.g., `LDR`) copy data from memory into CPU registers, while Store instructions (e.g., `STR`) copy data from registers to memory. All other data-processing operations (ADD, SUB, AND, ORR) operate exclusively on registers and never access memory directly. Because **only Load and Store instructions can move data between memory and registers**, the architecture is called a **Load-Store architecture**. (3 marks)

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Pre-Indexed and Post-Indexed addressing modes in ARM with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Pre-Indexed | Post-Indexed |
|--------|-------------|--------------|
| Syntax | `LDR Rd, [Rn, #offset]` | `LDR Rd, [Rn], #offset` |
| Effective Address | `EA = Rn + offset` | `EA = Rn` |
| Writeback | Only with `!` suffix | Always happens |
| Use Case | Direct memory access | Auto-increment loops |

Example: To read from `R1+4` and then auto-increment `R1`, we write `LDR R0, [R1], #4` (post-indexed). (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** In KTU ESE, Part B Module questions of 14 marks provide an **internal choice**. The student must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Sub-part (a) [7 marks]:**
Explain the different types of Load and Store instructions available in ARM with examples.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**
ARM provides the following Load/Store instructions based on data size:

1. **LDR / STR — Word (32-bit):** Loads/stores a 32-bit word. Example: `LDR R0, [R1, #4]` loads the 32-bit word at `R1+4` into `R0`. (1 mark)

2. **LDRB / STRB — Unsigned Byte (8-bit):** Loads/stores a single byte. The byte is zero-extended to 32 bits when loaded. Example: `LDRB R2, [R3]` loads the byte at address `R3` into `R2`, zero-filling the upper 24 bits. (1 mark)

3. **LDRH / STRH — Unsigned Halfword (16-bit):** Loads/stores a 16-bit halfword, zero-extended to 32 bits. Example: `LDRH R4, [R5, #2]`. (1 mark)

4. **LDRSB — Signed Byte:** Loads a byte and sign-extends the 8-bit value to 32 bits. Example: `LDRSB R6, [R7]`. (1 mark)

5. **LDRSH — Signed Halfword:** Loads a halfword and sign-extends the 16-bit value to 32 bits. Example: `LDRSH R8, [R9, #0]`. (1 mark)

6. **LDM / STM — Multiple Register Load/Store:** Transfers a block of registers to/from memory in a single instruction. Example: `LDMIA R0!, {R1-R4}` loads registers R1–R4 from consecutive memory locations starting at `R0` and updates `R0`. (1 mark)

7. **SWP / SWPB — Swap:** Atomically swaps a register value with a memory value. (1 mark)

---

**Sub-part (b) [7 marks]:**
Given `R1 = 0x00002000`. Evaluate the Effective Address (EA) and final value of `R1` for the following instructions:
(i) `LDR R2, [R1, #0x10]`
(ii) `LDR R2, [R1, #0x10]!`
(iii) `LDR R2, [R1], #0x10`

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) `LDR R2, [R1, #0x10]` — Pre-Indexed, No Writeback (2 marks)**
$$
\begin{aligned}
EA &= R1 + \text{offset} \\
EA &= 0x00002000 + 0x00000010 \\
EA &= 0x00002010
\end{aligned}
$$
Memory word at `0x00002010` is loaded into `R2`. `R1` remains `0x00002000`.

---

**(ii) `LDR R2, [R1, #0x10]!` — Pre-Indexed with Writeback (2 marks)**
$$
\begin{aligned}
EA &= R1 + \text{offset} = 0x00002010 \\
\text{Memory}[0x00002010] &\rightarrow R2 \\
\text{Writeback: } R1 &\leftarrow EA = 0x00002010
\end{aligned}
$$
`R1` is updated to `0x00002010`.

---

**(iii) `LDR R2, [R1], #0x10` — Post-Indexed, Writeback Always (3 marks)**
$$
\begin{aligned}
EA &= R1 \text{ (no offset applied before access)} \\
EA &= 0x00002000 \\
\text{Memory}[0x00002000] &\rightarrow R2 \\
\text{Writeback: } R1 &\leftarrow R1 + \text{offset} \\
R1 &= 0x00002000 + 0x00000010 \\
R1 &= 0x00002010
\end{aligned}
$$
Memory word at `0x00002000` is loaded into `R2`. `R1` is updated to `0x00002010`.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Sub-part (a) [7 marks]:**
Describe the three addressing modes of ARM Load/Store instructions. Show how the Effective Address is calculated in each case.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Mode 1: Pre-Indexed Addressing (2 marks)**
The offset is added to the base register `Rn` *before* the memory access takes place. The result is used as the Effective Address. `Rn` is **not modified**.
- **Syntax:** `LDR Rd, [Rn, #offset]`
- **Formula:** $EA = Rn + \text{offset}$

**Mode 2: Pre-Indexed with Writeback (2 marks)**
The offset is added to `Rn` *before* the memory access. The result is used as the EA. After the access, `Rn` is **updated** to this new value.
- **Syntax:** `LDR Rd, [Rn, #offset]!`
- **Formula:** $EA = Rn + \text{offset}$, then $Rn \leftarrow EA$

**Mode 3: Post-Indexed Addressing (3 marks)**
The base register `Rn` is used *directly* as the Effective Address (no offset added). After the memory access, `Rn` is updated to `Rn + offset`. Writeback is **mandatory** in this mode.
- **Syntax:** `LDR Rd, [Rn], #offset`
- **Formula:** $EA = Rn$, then $Rn \leftarrow Rn + \text{offset}$

---

**Sub-part (b) [7 marks]:**
With a neat diagram, explain the working of a **Full Descending Stack** in ARM using `STMFD` and `LDMFD` instructions. Mention the stack pointer behavior.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

A **Full Descending Stack** is the standard stack model used in ARM. The terms mean:
- **Full:** The stack pointer `SP` always points to the **last used (occupied) location** in the stack.
- **Descending:** The stack grows toward **lower memory addresses** (i.e., SP decreases as items are pushed).

**Push Operation — `STMFD SP!, {R4-R7}` (3 marks)**

`STMFD` is an alias for `STMDB` (Store Multiple — Decrement Before). Steps:
1. The base register `SP` is decremented by 4 for each register to be stored.
2. Registers are then stored at the updated addresses.
3. The lowest-numbered register is placed at the **lowest** address.
4. Final `SP` value is the address of the **last** (highest-numbered) register stored.

**Pop Operation — `LDMFD SP!, {R4-R7}` (2 marks)**

`LDMFD` is an alias for `LDMIA` (Load Multiple — Increment After). It reverses the push by loading the highest-numbered register first from the highest address, incrementing SP, and continuing until all registers are restored.

**Stack Pointer Behavior (2 marks):**
- After `STMFD`, `SP` decreases by `4 × N` bytes (where N = number of registers pushed).
- After `LDMFD`, `SP` increases by `4 × N` bytes, returning to its pre-push value.
- The `!` (writeback) ensures `SP` is automatically updated in both operations, eliminating manual pointer management.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing pre-indexed and post-indexed EA:** In post-indexed, the EA is the *original* `Rn`, NOT `Rn + offset`. Students who compute `EA = Rn + offset` for post-indexed will lose 3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting writeback update in pre-indexed mode:** A student must always check for the `!` symbol. No `!` means no writeback — even though the syntax looks similar.
> 3. **Mixing up `LDRH` and `LDRSH`:** `LDRH` zero-extends; `LDRSH` sign-extends. Examiners set specific values like `0x8000` to test whether you understand sign extension.
> 4. **No `STRSB` / `STRSH` instruction:** Do not invent these in answers. They do not exist in the ARM ISA.
> 5. **Alignment violations:** Writing `LDR R0, [R1, #2]` will cause an alignment fault on many ARM cores. Always use multiples of 4 for word loads/stores.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **ARM is a Load-Store RISC architecture** — memory can ONLY be accessed via Load/Store instructions.
- [ ] **Word (32-bit) load/store:** `LDR` / `STR` — address must be word-aligned (multiple of 4).
- [ ] **Byte (8-bit) load/store:** `LDRB` / `STRB` — any byte address allowed; zero-extends on load.
- [ ] **Halfword (16-bit) load/store:** `LDRH` / `STRH` — must be halfword-aligned (multiple of 2); zero-extends.
- [ ] **Signed variants exist only for LOAD:** `LDRSB`, `LDRSH` — sign-extend to 32 bits.
- [ ] **Pre-Indexed:** `EA = Rn + offset`, no writeback.
- [ ] **Pre-Indexed with `!`:** `EA = Rn + offset`, writeback updates `Rn`.
- [ ] **Post-Indexed:** `EA = Rn`, then `Rn ← Rn + offset` (writeback always).
- [ ] **Multiple Register Transfer:** `LDM` / `STM` with suffix `IA`, `IB`, `DA`, `DB`.
- [ ] **Stack Aliases:** `STMFD = STMDB` (push), `LDMFD = LDMIA` (pop) on a Full Descending stack.
- [ ] **The `!` suffix means writeback** — it is the only visual cue for the addressing mode in pre-indexed syntax.
- [ ] **Offset range:** 12-bit (0–4095) for LDR/STR, 8-bit (0–255) for LDRB/STRB/LDRH/STRH.
- [ ] **Real-world use:** `*p++` in C compiles to post-indexed `LDR`/`STR` with writeback.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Software Interrupt Instructions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCIT404 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Software Interrupt Instructions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Software Interrupt Instructions — The 8086 Perspective

In the **Intel 8086 microprocessor** architecture, **Software Interrupt Instructions** are a specialized class of control transfer instructions that allow a running program to deliberately invoke the processor's interrupt mechanism, mimicking the behavior of a hardware interrupt. Unlike CALL or JMP, these instructions push the **Flag Register (FLAGS)**, **Code Segment (CS)**, and **Instruction Pointer (IP)** onto the stack atomically before transferring control.

The 8086 instruction set defines the following software interrupt instructions:

1. **`INT n`** — A 2-byte instruction that generates a type *n* interrupt (where *n* ranges from **00H to FFH**, i.e., 0–255).
2. **`INT 3`** — A 1-byte breakpoint interrupt instruction.
3. **`INTO`** — A 1-byte conditional interrupt instruction triggered when the **Overflow Flag (OF = 1)**.
4. **`IRET`** — The 3-byte **Interrupt Return** instruction used at the end of any ISR (Interrupt Service Routine).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> Software interrupt instructions are a high-weightage topic in the 8086 instruction set unit. Examiners frequently test the **stack operations**, **Interrupt Vector Table (IVT)** addressing formula, and the **flags affected** by each instruction.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are reading a book (your main program), and you suddenly need to look up a definition from a dictionary (a sub-routine). Instead of closing the book and walking to the shelf, you place a **bookmark** at your current page (save IP), place a **sticky note** saying "I was reading Chapter 5" (save CS), and even record the **ambient lighting conditions** (save FLAGS) so you can resume exactly where you left off. You then "jump" to the dictionary. When done, you reverse the process and return to your page.

> **A software interrupt is precisely this "atomic bookmark + jump" operation**, except the dictionary entry is fetched from a fixed pre-defined lookup table called the **Interrupt Vector Table (IVT)** located at physical address `00000H`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — Interrupt Vector Table (IVT):**
> The IVT is a 1 KB (1024 bytes) lookup table in the lowest memory region, containing **256 far pointers** (each 4 bytes: 2-byte IP + 2-byte CS). The physical address for the *n*-th vector is computed as: `Physical Address = n × 4`.

## 1.3 Why Software Interrupts Matter in 8086

- They provide a **portable mechanism** to invoke DOS and BIOS services without hard-coding addresses.
- They enable **user-defined ISRs** for custom hardware or software events.
- They are the foundation of the **DOS `INT 21H` function call interface**, which is heavily tested in KTU lab viva and theory exams.
- They are used in **debugging** (INT 3 = breakpoint trap for debuggers like DEBUG.EXE).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The `INT n` Instruction — Operational Breakdown

**Instruction Format:** `INT n`  (where *n* is an 8-bit immediate type number, 00H–FFH)
**Opcode Bytes:** `CD nn` (2 bytes total)
**Machine Cycles:** Approximately 51 clock cycles (varies with 8086/8088)

### Step-by-Step Execution Logic:

1. **Decrement SP by 2**, then push the **FLAGS register** onto the stack.
2. **Decrement SP by 2**, then push the **CS register** onto the stack.
3. **Decrement SP by 2**, then push the **IP register** onto the stack.
4. **Clear the Trap Flag (TF)** and **Interrupt Flag (IF)** in the FLAGS register to disable single-step and maskable interrupts during ISR execution.
5. Fetch the new IP and CS from the **Interrupt Vector Table** at physical address `n × 4`.
6. The new IP is loaded from address `n × 4` (low word) and `n × 4 + 1` (high word).
7. The new CS is loaded from address `n × 4 + 2` (low word) and `n × 4 + 3` (high word).
8. Execution continues at the address `CS:IP` just fetched.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why push FLAGS first?**
> Because the stack grows **downward** in the 8086. The most recently pushed value is at the top of the stack. Since `IRET` pops in the order **IP → CS → FLAGS**, the IRET expects FLAGS to be at the deepest (highest) stack address, meaning it must be pushed **first**.

## 2.2 The `INT 3` (Breakpoint) Instruction

- **Format:** `INT 3` — encoded as a **single byte** (`CC`).
- It generates a **Type 3 interrupt**.
- It is the standard breakpoint trap used by debuggers.
- The CPU pushes FLAGS, CS, IP onto the stack and jumps to the vector at `0000CH` (i.e., `3 × 4`).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction:** `INT 3` is **1 byte** (opcode `CC`), whereas `INT 03H` is **2 bytes** (opcode `CD 03`). Both invoke the same Type 3 vector, but the 1-byte form is specifically optimized so that debuggers can place breakpoints by overwriting a single byte in code.

## 2.3 The `INTO` (Interrupt on Overflow) Instruction

- **Format:** `INTO` — encoded as a single byte (`CE`).
- **Conditional behavior:**
  - If **OF = 1** (Overflow Flag is set), it generates a **Type 4 interrupt**.
  - If **OF = 0**, it acts as a **NOP** (No Operation) and simply proceeds to the next instruction.
- It is intended to be placed immediately after a signed arithmetic instruction (e.g., `ADD`, `SUB`, `MUL`, `IMUL`).

> [!NOTE]
> The `INTO` instruction is paired with the **Overflow Flag (OF)**, while `INT` does not depend on any flag condition. `INTO` is useful for trapping **signed arithmetic overflows** in high-reliability code.

## 2.4 The `IRET` Instruction

- **Format:** `IRET` — encoded as a single byte (`CF`).
- **Operation:** Returns from an ISR by reversing the pushes of `INT`:
  1. **Pop IP** from the stack.
  2. **Pop CS** from the stack.
  3. **Pop FLAGS** from the stack.
- **Privileged behavior:** `IRET` is the **only instruction** that pops the FLAGS register from the stack. A normal `POPF` would not be reliable inside an ISR because interrupts may be disabled and IF/TF are protected in protected mode.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Value / Formula | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|
| IVT Physical Address (Type *n*) | `n × 4` (with segment = 0000H, offset = `n × 4`) | Used to install custom ISRs in DOS real mode |
| IVT Total Size | **1024 bytes (1 KB)** | Holds 256 vectors × 4 bytes each |
| Number of Interrupt Types | **256** (00H to FFH) | Defines max distinct software interrupts |
| Stack pushed by `INT n` | FLAGS → CS → IP (high-to-low address) | Critical for IRET restoration order |
| `INT n` opcode | `CD nn` | 2-byte instruction |
| `INT 3` opcode | `CC` | 1-byte breakpoint |
| `INTO` opcode | `CE` | 1-byte conditional (OF = 1) |
| `IRET` opcode | `CF` | 1-byte return |
| Flags cleared by `INT` | **IF = 0, TF = 0** | Disables maskable + single-step interrupts |
| Vector for INT 21H | `0000:0084H` (21H × 4) | DOS function dispatcher |
| Vector for INT 10H | `0000:0040H` (10H × 4) | BIOS video services |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **vertical pipe symbol** issue: KTU exam answers should never use `|` for absolute value within markdown tables. In written exams, students should use `|x|` notation without markdown formatting interference.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **DOS Application Programming:** Programmers invoked BIOS (e.g., `INT 10H` for video) and DOS (e.g., `INT 21H` for file I/O) using these instructions.
- **Operating System Design:** Modern OS interrupt dispatchers evolved directly from this 8086 mechanism.
- **Embedded Systems with 8086 derivatives (80186, 80188, 80286):** Software interrupts still serve as system calls in real-time operating systems (RTOS) for x86 microcontrollers.
- **Debuggers and Profilers:** `INT 3` is the universal x86 breakpoint opcode, used by every debugger from DEBUG.EXE to modern GDB.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Computing the IVT Entry Address for a Given Interrupt Type

**Problem Statement:** Find the physical address in the Interrupt Vector Table that contains the IP and CS values for interrupt type `n`, where the IVT base is at `00000H`.

### Derivation (Exhaustive Step-by-Step):

Let the IVT base segment be `IVT_SEG = 0000H` and the base offset be `IVT_OFF = 0000H`. Each vector occupies exactly 4 bytes. We must determine the offset within the IVT for type *n*.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bytes occupied before type } n &= n \times 4 \\
\text{Offset of type } n \text{ in IVT} &= n \times 4 \\
\text{Since base offset} &= 0000\text{H} \\
\text{Final Offset} &= n \times 4 \quad \text{(result is 16-bit)} \\
\text{Segment of IVT} &= 0000\text{H} \\
\text{Physical Address of IP (low word)} &= 0 : (n \times 4) \\
\text{Physical Address of IP (high word)} &= 0 : (n \times 4) + 1 \\
\text{Physical Address of CS (low word)} &= 0 : (n \times 4) + 2 \\
\text{Physical Address of CS (high word)} &= 0 : (n \times 4) + 3
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Because the segment register is `0000H`, the physical address equals the offset × 16 + offset, which simplifies to just the offset. So `0000:n×4` directly gives the physical address.

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style):

**Problem:** Calculate the IVT physical addresses (for IP-low, IP-high, CS-low, CS-high) for the interrupt type `INT 21H` (DOS function call).

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= 21\text{H} = 33 \text{ (decimal)} \\
n \times 4 &= 21\text{H} \times 4 \\
&= 0\text{x}21 \times 0\text{x}04 \\
&= 0\text{x}84 \quad \text{(decimal: } 33 \times 4 = 132\text{)} \\
\text{IVT base segment} &= 0000\text{H} \\
\text{IP low byte} &\rightarrow \text{Physical address } 00084\text{H} \\
\text{IP high byte} &\rightarrow \text{Physical address } 00085\text{H} \\
\text{CS low byte} &\rightarrow \text{Physical address } 00086\text{H} \\
\text{CS high byte} &\rightarrow \text{Physical address } 00087\text{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Verification:** DOS function dispatcher IP:CS is stored across 4 contiguous bytes from `00084H` to `00087H`. This is the standard location for the DOS kernel's interrupt service entry point.

## 3.2 Derivation: Stack Memory State After an `INT n` Instruction

**Problem:** Given that the Stack Segment (SS) = `1000H` and Stack Pointer (SP) = `2000H` before executing `INT 8`, compute the final SP value and the physical addresses of IP, CS, and FLAGS after the push operations.

### Derivation (Exhaustive Step-by-Step):

The 8086 stack grows **downward** (toward lower addresses). Each push is a 2-byte operation. Three items are pushed: FLAGS, CS, IP (in that push order).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Initial SP} &= 2000\text{H} \\
\text{After PUSH FLAGS: SP} &= 2000\text{H} - 2 = 1\text{FFE}\text{H} \\
\text{After PUSH CS: SP} &= 1\text{FFE}\text{H} - 2 = 1\text{FFC}\text{H} \\
\text{After PUSH IP: SP} &= 1\text{FFC}\text{H} - 2 = 1\text{FFA}\text{H} \\
\text{Final SP} &= 1\text{FFA}\text{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Stack Memory Layout (Top of stack is at lower address):**

| Final Memory Address (Physical) | Content Pushed |
|---|---|
| `1FFAH` (low) / `1FFBH` (high) | IP (low) / IP (high) — pushed last, top of stack |
| `1FFCH` (low) / `1FFDH` (high) | CS (low) / CS (high) |
| `1FFEH` (low) / `1FFFH` (high) | FLAGS (low) / FLAGS (high) — pushed first, bottom of stack |

> **Conversion Logic:** When `IRET` executes, the CPU pops in the **reverse** order: it first pops IP from `1FFAH:1FFBH`, then CS from `1FFCH:1FFDH`, and finally FLAGS from `1FFEH:1FFFH`. This perfectly restores the original state.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Interrupt Instructions

| Feature | `INT n` | `INT 3` | `INTO` | `IRET` |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Opcode (Hex) | `CD nn` | `CC` | `CE` | `CF` |
| Size (bytes) | 2 | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| Type Number | *n* (00H–FFH) | Fixed = 3 | Fixed = 4 | N/A (return) |
| Conditional? | No (always) | No (always) | Yes (only if OF = 1) | N/A |
| Flags Pushed? | Yes (FLAGS) | Yes (FLAGS) | Yes (FLAGS) | N/A |
| Clears IF/TF? | Yes | Yes | Yes | N/A |
| Vector Address | `n × 4` | `000CH` | `00010H` | N/A |
| Pushed order | FLAGS, CS, IP | FLAGS, CS, IP | FLAGS, CS, IP | Pops: IP, CS, FLAGS |
| Typical Use | DOS/BIOS call | Debugger breakpoint | Signed overflow trap | ISR exit |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: 8086 Software Interrupt Execution Flow

The following Mermaid flowchart models the precise execution pipeline of the `INT n` instruction, mapping the interaction between the CPU registers, stack, and the Interrupt Vector Table.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: INT n opcode CD nn fetched] --> B[Decrement SP by 2]
    B --> C[Push FLAGS Register onto Stack]
    C --> D[Decrement SP by 2]
    D --> E[Push CS Register onto Stack]
    E --> F[Decrement SP by 2]
    F --> G[Push IP Register onto Stack]
    G --> H[Clear IF and TF Flags to 0]
    H --> I[Compute Vector Address: n times 4]
    I --> J[Fetch IP from n times 4 and n times 4 plus 1]
    J --> K[Fetch CS from n times 4 plus 2 and n times 4 plus 3]
    K --> L[Load new IP and CS into CPU]
    L --> M[Begin ISR Execution at new CS:IP]
    M --> N[ISR Body runs]
    N --> O[IRET Instruction reached at ISR end]
    O --> P[Pop IP from Stack]
    P --> Q[Pop CS from Stack]
    Q --> R[Pop FLAGS from Stack]
    R --> S[Increment SP by 6 total]
    S --> T[Resume Main Program at Original CS:IP]
```

## 4.2 Memory Map Diagram: Interrupt Vector Table Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Physical Address 00000H] --> B[Type 00H Vector IP_low]
    B --> C[Type 00H Vector IP_high]
    C --> D[Type 00H Vector CS_low]
    D --> E[Type 00H Vector CS_high]
    E --> F[Type 01H Vector 4 bytes]
    F --> G[Type 02H Vector 4 bytes]
    G --> H[Dots and Dots]
    H --> I[Type 03H Vector INT 3 4 bytes]
    I --> J[Type 04H Vector INTO 4 bytes]
    J --> K[Type 10H Vector INT 10H BIOS Video at 00040H]
    K --> L[Type 21H Vector INT 21H DOS Services at 00084H]
    L --> M[Dots and Dots]
    M --> N[Type FFH Vector at 003FCH]
    N --> O[End of IVT at 003FFH]
```

> **Engineering Interpretation:** The IVT begins at absolute address `00000H` and ends at `003FFH`. Each entry is a 32-bit **far pointer** (CS:IP). The CPU multiplies the interrupt type by 4 to directly index into this table without any segment translation.

## 4.3 Stack State Diagram: PUSH Order During INT and POP Order During IRET

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph STACK_GROWTH["Stack Memory Growth Direction (Low to High Address)"]
        direction TB
        A1[1FFAH: IP_low byte] --> A2[1FFBH: IP_high byte]
        A2 --> A3[1FFCH: CS_low byte]
        A3 --> A4[1FFDH: CS_high byte]
        A4 --> A5[1FFEH: FLAGS_low byte]
        A5 --> A6[1FFFH: FLAGS_high byte]
    end
    subgraph PUSH_PHASE["INT n Push Order: FLAGS pushed first to highest address"]
        B1[Step 1: PUSH FLAGS at 1FFEH and 1FFFH] --> B2[Step 2: PUSH CS at 1FFCH and 1FFDH]
        B2 --> B3[Step 3: PUSH IP at 1FFAH and 1FFBH]
    end
    subgraph POP_PHASE["IRET Pop Order: Reverse sequence to restore state"]
        C1[Step 1: POP IP from 1FFAH and 1FFBH] --> C2[Step 2: POP CS from 1FFCH and 1FFDH]
        C2 --> C3[Step 3: POP FLAGS from 1FFEH and 1FFFH]
    end
```

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Differentiate between the `INT 3` and `INT 03H` instructions in 8086.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | `INT 3` | `INT 03H` |
|---|---|---|
| Opcode Encoding | Single byte `CC` | Two bytes `CD 03` |
| Instruction Size | 1 byte | 2 bytes |
| Interrupt Type | Type 3 | Type 3 |
| Primary Use | Debugger breakpoint (compact) | Standard software interrupt |
| Replacement Impact | Replaces 1 byte in code | Replaces 2 bytes in code |

> **Examiner Insight:** The 1-byte form is used by debuggers to insert breakpoints by overwriting a single code byte, while the 2-byte form is used in standard application code.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**What is the role of the Interrupt Vector Table in the 8086 microprocessor? State its size and starting address.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Interrupt Vector Table (IVT)** is a lookup table in memory that stores the **starting addresses (CS:IP pairs)** of all 256 interrupt service routines. When an interrupt occurs, the CPU multiplies the interrupt type number by 4 to compute the offset within the IVT, fetches the new IP and CS, and transfers control to the ISR.

- **Starting Address:** `00000H` (physical)
- **Size:** `1024 bytes` (1 KB)
- **Number of Entries:** 256 vectors (Type 00H to Type FFH), each occupying 4 bytes

---

## 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Explain the execution of the `INT n` instruction in 8086 with a detailed note on the stack operations and flag status changes. List the flags that are modified.**

**Model Solution:**

The `INT n` instruction in 8086 is a **2-byte software interrupt** instruction that simulates a hardware interrupt. The opcode is `CD nn`, where `nn` is the 8-bit type number (00H to FFH).

**Step-by-step execution of `INT n`:**

1. **PUSH FLAGS:** The current contents of the FLAGS register are pushed onto the stack. The SP is decremented by 2 before the push.
2. **PUSH CS:** The Code Segment register (CS) is pushed onto the stack. SP is again decremented by 2.
3. **PUSH IP:** The Instruction Pointer (IP) is pushed onto the stack. SP is decremented by 2.
4. **Clear IF and TF:** The **Interrupt Flag (IF)** and **Trap Flag (TF)** are cleared to 0. This disables further maskable hardware interrupts and single-step trapping during the ISR.
5. **Fetch new IP and CS:** The CPU reads the new IP and CS from the Interrupt Vector Table at physical address `n × 4`.
   - IP_low at `n × 4`, IP_high at `n × 4 + 1`
   - CS_low at `n × 4 + 2`, CS_high at `n × 4 + 3`
6. **Transfer Control:** Execution continues at the new CS:IP.

**Flags Modified:**

| Flag | State After INT n |
|---|---|
| IF (Interrupt Flag) | Cleared to 0 |
| TF (Trap Flag) | Cleared to 0 |
| All other flags | Unchanged |

> **Conclusion:** The instruction is fully re-entrant and ensures the CPU state is preserved automatically for restoration by `IRET`.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**With a suitable diagram, explain the structure of the 8086 Interrupt Vector Table. If the IVT starts at `00000H`, calculate the physical address of the vector entry for `INT 1CH` (DOS timer tick).**

**Model Solution:**

The IVT occupies the **lowest 1 KB of physical memory** from `00000H` to `003FFH`. It contains 256 entries, each 4 bytes wide, structured as a **CS:IP far pointer**.

**Structure of each entry (4 bytes):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Byte 0} &= \text{IP low byte} \\
\text{Byte 1} &= \text{IP high byte} \\
\text{Byte 2} &= \text{CS low byte} \\
\text{Byte 3} &= \text{CS high byte}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Address calculation for `INT 1CH`:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= 1\text{CH} = 28 \text{ (decimal)} \\
\text{Vector Offset} &= n \times 4 = 1\text{CH} \times 4 = 70\text{H} \\
\text{IP low at} &= 0000:0070\text{H} \rightarrow 00070\text{H} \\
\text{IP high at} &= 0000:0071\text{H} \rightarrow 00071\text{H} \\
\text{CS low at} &= 0000:0072\text{H} \rightarrow 00072\text{H} \\
\text{CS high at} &= 0000:0073\text{H} \rightarrow 00073\text{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Answer:** The vector for `INT 1CH` is stored across physical addresses `00070H` to `00073H`.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Alternative Choice]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Explain the `INTO` instruction. Under what condition does it generate an interrupt, and what is the type number of the interrupt produced?**

**Model Solution:**

`INTO` (Interrupt on Overflow) is a **single-byte instruction** (opcode `CE`) used to invoke a **Type 4 interrupt** when the **Overflow Flag (OF) is set to 1**.

**Condition of execution:**

- If **OF = 1** (overflow occurred in the previous signed arithmetic operation), the CPU:
  1. Pushes FLAGS, CS, and IP onto the stack
  2. Clears IF and TF
  3. Loads new IP and CS from vector at `4 × 4 = 00010H`
  4. Transfers control to the Type 4 ISR

- If **OF = 0**, `INTO` acts as a **NOP** and the next instruction in sequence is executed.

**Typical Usage Pattern:**

```assembly
IMUL BL        ; Signed multiplication
INTO           ; Trap if overflow occurred
JO  ErrorLabel ; Alternative jump if overflow (for branch handling)
```

> **Engineering Note:** `INTO` is rarely used in modern compilers but is a standard part of the 8086 ISA and is important for exam purposes.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Describe the operation of the `IRET` instruction. Why is it used only at the end of an ISR and not a regular sub-routine?**

**Model Solution:**

`IRET` (Interrupt Return) is a **single-byte instruction** (opcode `CF`) used to return from an Interrupt Service Routine.

**Operation:**

1. **Pop IP** from the stack (top 2 bytes).
2. **Pop CS** from the next 2 bytes.
3. **Pop FLAGS** from the next 2 bytes.

After the pops, SP effectively increases by 6 (since 3 × 2 bytes = 6 bytes were consumed).

**Why `IRET` and not `RET`?**

- A regular `RET` pops only IP (and CS for far returns), **not FLAGS**.
- The FLAGS register was pushed by `INT n` to preserve the state of IF, TF, and the arithmetic flags.
- If `IRET` is not used, the FLAGS register will not be restored, leaving interrupts potentially disabled (IF=0) after the ISR, which would crash subsequent system behavior.
- Additionally, in protected mode, `IRET` is the only way to atomically restore the FLAGS, CS, and IP triplet with the correct privilege level.

> **Conclusion:** `IRET` is mandatory at the end of every ISR to atomically restore the complete pre-interrupt CPU state.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Stack Push Order:** Students frequently write "INT pushes IP, CS, FLAGS." This is **wrong** — the correct order is FLAGS → CS → IP (flags pushed first, so FLAGS is at the highest stack address).
> 2. **IVT Formula:** Writing `n × 2` instead of `n × 4` is a frequent error. Each vector is 4 bytes, not 2.
> 3. **INT 3 vs INT 03H:** Many students treat them as identical without noting the **opcode size difference** (1 byte vs 2 bytes).
> 4. **INTO Behavior:** Forgetting that INTO is a NOP when OF=0 (not an unconditional interrupt) costs marks.
> 5. **IRET Pop Order:** Stating that IRET pops CS first is wrong; it pops IP first, then CS, then FLAGS.
> 6. **Flags Cleared:** Only IF and TF are cleared by INT. The other 6 arithmetic flags (CF, PF, AF, ZF, SF, OF) are **not modified** by the INT instruction itself.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- [x] `INT n` is a **2-byte** software interrupt; opcode `CD nn`; valid types `00H` to `FFH`.
- [x] `INT 3` is **1-byte** (opcode `CC`); specifically used for **debugger breakpoints**.
- [x] `INTO` is **1-byte** (opcode `CE`); conditional on **OF = 1**; generates **Type 4 interrupt**.
- [x] `IRET` is **1-byte** (opcode `CF`); only instruction to pop **FLAGS** from stack.
- [x] The **Interrupt Vector Table (IVT)** is **1 KB** at `00000H`, holding **256 vectors × 4 bytes each**.
- [x] IVT physical address formula: **`n × 4`** (with segment `0000H`).
- [x] **Push order during INT n:** FLAGS → CS → IP (in that order).
- [x] **Pop order during IRET:** IP → CS → FLAGS (reverse of push).
- [x] **Flags cleared by INT n:** **IF = 0, TF = 0** (all others unchanged).
- [x] **Stack growth direction:** **Downward** (toward lower addresses).
- [x] **Real-world uses:** `INT 10H` (BIOS video), `INT 21H` (DOS services), `INT 1CH` (DOS timer tick).
- [x] **IRET restores IF and TF** automatically because they are part of the FLAGS register that was pushed.
- [x] **Total stack usage per INT:** **6 bytes** (3 pushes × 2 bytes each).
- [x] **INT vs CALL difference:** INT pushes FLAGS additionally; CALL does not.
- [x] **Type 0 (Divide Error)** and **Type 1 (Single Step)** are hardware-generated; software interrupts do not typically use them.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Program Status Register Instructions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCIT404 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Program Status Register Instructions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Program Status Register (PSR)** in the **Intel 8051 microcontroller** is an **8-bit special function register (SFR)** mapped at internal RAM address **D0H** (bit-addressable). It is also commonly called the **Program Status Word (PSW)**. This register holds the **arithmetic status flags**, **user-definable flags**, and **register bank select bits** that govern the internal execution state of the CPU.

The **PSR/PSW instructions** are a class of 8051 assembly instructions that either:

1. **Directly read or modify the PSW register** (e.g., `MOV PSW, #data`, `MOV A, PSW`, `PUSH PSW`, `POP PSW`).
2. **Indirectly affect the flag bits** within the PSW through arithmetic, logical, or bit-manipulation operations (e.g., `ADD A, R0`, `SUBB A, #data`, `ANL A, #data`).

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🎯

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Car Dashboard:**
> Imagine you are driving a car. The **dashboard** displays critical real-time information: fuel level, engine temperature, speed, and warning lights (check engine, oil, battery). 
> 
> Similarly, the **PSR is the CPU's dashboard**. It continuously displays the **current status of the processor** after each operation. The flags are like **dashboard indicator lights** — some are set automatically by the hardware (CY, AC, OV, P) when specific events occur (like overflow, borrow, or parity), while others are **user-controllable switches** (RS1, RS0) that allow the programmer to "switch gears" and select which set of working registers (R0–R7) the CPU is currently using.

## 1.3 Bit Structure of the PSW Register

The PSW is an **8-bit register** with the following bit allocation:

| Bit Position | Bit Name | Symbol | Type | Function |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| PSW.7 | Carry Flag | **CY** | Hardware | Set when arithmetic carry/borrow occurs |
| PSW.6 | Auxiliary Carry | **AC** | Hardware | Carry from bit 3 to bit 4 (BCD arithmetic) |
| PSW.5 | User Flag 0 | **F0** | User | General-purpose flag for software use |
| PSW.4 | Register Bank Select 1 | **RS1** | User | High bit for bank selection |
| PSW.3 | Register Bank Select 0 | **RS0** | User | Low bit for bank selection |
| PSW.2 | Overflow Flag | **OV** | Hardware | Set on signed arithmetic overflow |
| PSW.1 | Reserved | **—** | Undefined | Reserved by Intel; undefined value |
| PSW.0 | Parity Flag | **P** | Hardware | Set if accumulator has odd number of 1s |

## 1.4 Register Bank Selection (RS1, RS0)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The combination of **RS1** and **RS0** selects one of **four register banks** in the internal RAM (locations 00H–1FH). Each bank contains 8 registers named **R0 through R7**.

| RS1 | RS0 | Bank Selected | Internal RAM Address Range |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | 0 | **Bank 0** | 00H – 07H |
| 0 | 1 | **Bank 1** | 08H – 0FH |
| 1 | 0 | **Bank 2** | 10H – 17H |
| 1 | 1 | **Bank 3** | 18H – 1FH |

> **After reset, RS1=0, RS0=0 → Bank 0 is selected by default.** The stack pointer also defaults to 07H.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Bit-by-Bit Analysis

### 🔹 CY (Carry Flag) — PSW.7
- **Set to 1** when an addition produces a carry **out of bit 7** of the accumulator, OR when a subtraction requires a **borrow**.
- Used by **rotation instructions** (`RLC A`, `RRC A`) and is the accumulator bit for the **Boolean processor**.
- **Directly addressable** as bit `C` (or `CY`) in bit-level instructions.

### 🔹 AC (Auxiliary Carry Flag) — PSW.6
- **Set to 1** if there is a carry from **bit D3 to bit D4** during addition, or a borrow from bit D4 to D3 during subtraction.
- Critical for **Binary Coded Decimal (BCD) arithmetic** using the `DA A` (Decimal Adjust Accumulator) instruction.

### 🔹 F0 (User Flag 0) — PSW.5
- A **general-purpose flag** available to the programmer.
- Can be set/cleared/tested using instructions like `SETB F0`, `CLR F0`, `JB F0, label`, `JNB F0, label`.
- Often used as a **single-bit software semaphore** to indicate states (e.g., "operation complete").

### 🔹 RS1, RS0 (Register Bank Select Bits) — PSW.4, PSW.3
- Determine **which physical registers** are accessed when instructions use `R0`–`R7`.
- Useful for **fast context switching** in interrupt service routines and for **expanding usable registers** beyond the basic 8.

### 🔹 OV (Overflow Flag) — PSW.2
- Set when a **signed arithmetic operation** produces a result that cannot fit in 8 bits (i.e., exceeds the range **−128 to +127**).
- Computed as: $OV = C_7 \oplus C_6$, where $C_7$ is the carry into bit 7 and $C_6$ is the carry out of bit 6 (for addition).

### 🔹 P (Parity Flag) — PSW.0
- Automatically set/cleared by hardware **after every instruction cycle**.
- Set to **1 if the accumulator contains an even number of 1-bits** (even parity).
- Reset to **0 if the accumulator contains an odd number of 1-bits**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Parity Rule:** $P = 1$ if the number of 1s in the Accumulator is **even**; $P = 0$ if odd. Note the "inverted" naming — P=1 means **even parity**.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **mathematically verifiable** and are **examination-critical** for 8051 PSR/PSW questions.

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Description | Units / State |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Parity Flag | $P = \overline{(A_7 \oplus A_6 \oplus A_5 \oplus A_4 \oplus A_3 \oplus A_2 \oplus A_1 \oplus A_0)}$ | Set to 1 if number of 1-bits in A is even | Boolean (0 or 1) |
| Overflow Flag | $OV = C_7 \oplus C_6$ | XOR of carry-in and carry-out of MSB | Boolean (0 or 1) |
| Auxiliary Carry | $AC = 1$ if $(A_{D3..D0} + B_{D3..D0}) \geq 16$ | Carry from bit 3 to bit 4 | Boolean (0 or 1) |
| Carry Flag (ADD) | $CY = 1$ if $(A + \text{operand}) > 255$ | Carry out of bit 7 | Boolean (0 or 1) |
| Carry Flag (SUBB) | $CY = 1$ if borrow occurs | $A < \text{operand} + CY_{in}$ | Boolean (0 or 1) |
| Bank Selection | $\text{Address} = 8 \times (2 \cdot RS1 + RS0)$ | Starting address of selected bank | Bytes (00H–18H) |
| Register Count | $N_{\text{regs}} = 4 \times 8 = 32$ | 4 banks × 8 registers | Bytes in IRAM |

## 2.3 Classification of PSR-Affecting Instructions

> [!NOTE]
> The 8051 instruction set can be broadly classified by how it interacts with the PSW flags.

### **Category 1: Instructions that DIRECTLY modify PSW**
| Instruction | Bytes | Cycles | Effect on PSW |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---|
| `MOV PSW, #data` | 3 | 2 | Loads immediate value into PSW |
| `MOV PSW, direct` | 2 | 2 | Loads direct byte into PSW |
| `MOV PSW, A` *(implicit in many ops)* | — | — | Accumulator affects P flag |
| `PUSH PSW` | 1 | 2 | Stack ← PSW; SP incremented |
| `POP PSW` | 1 | 2 | PSW ← Stack; SP decremented |
| `SETB CY` / `CLR CY` / `CPL CY` | 1 | 1 | Manipulate only Carry flag |

### **Category 2: Instructions that modify FLAGS within PSW**

| Instruction | CY | OV | AC | P |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| `ADD A, <src>` | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ |
| `ADDC A, <src>` | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ |
| `SUBB A, <src>` | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ |
| `MUL AB` | ❌ (cleared) | ✔ (overflow) | ❌ | ✔ |
| `DIV AB` | ❌ (cleared) | ✔ (divide-by-zero) | ❌ | ✔ |
| `DA A` | ✔ | — | — | ✔ |
| `INC A` | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✔ |
| `DEC A` | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✔ |
| `ANL A, <src>` | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✔ |
| `ORL A, <src>` | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✔ |
| `XRL A, <src>` | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✔ |
| `CLR A` | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✔ |
| `CPL A` | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✔ |
| `RLC A` | ✔ (into bit 0) | ❌ | ❌ | ✔ |
| `RRC A` | ✔ (from bit 7) | ❌ | ❌ | ✔ |

### **Category 3: Instructions that DO NOT affect any PSW flag**
- `MOV` (all variants except to A which affects P)
- `PUSH`, `POP`
- `INC` / `DEC` on registers other than A
- All jump/branch/call instructions

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The PSR is **fundamental** in real-time embedded applications:
> - **Conditional Branching:** Flags enable `JC`, `JNC`, `JZ`, `JNZ`, `JB`, `JNB` instructions for decision-making.
> - **Multi-tasking:** Register bank switching via RS1/RS0 allows **fast context switching** in interrupt-driven systems.
> - **Error Detection:** Parity flag enables **serial communication error detection**; carry flag detects **arithmetic overflow**.
> - **BCD Arithmetic:** AC flag + `DA A` instruction enable **decimal arithmetic for display applications** (calculators, meters).

# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Analysis

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Parity Flag Calculation

**Problem:** Given Accumulator `A = 1011 0010B`, determine the value of the Parity Flag **P** after the instruction sequence.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1:** Write out the binary contents of A.

$$A = 1011\,0010_B$$

**Step 2:** Count the number of 1-bits in A.

$$\text{Bit positions: } D7, D6, D5, D4, D3, D2, D1, D0$$

$$\text{Values: } \quad 1, \;\; 0, \;\; 1, \;\; 1, \;\; 0, \;\; 0, \;\; 1, \;\; 0$$

**Step 3:** Count the 1s.

$$N_{1} = 1_{(D7)} + 1_{(D5)} + 1_{(D4)} + 1_{(D1)} = 4$$

**Step 4:** Apply the parity rule.

$$N_{1} = 4 \;\;\text{(which is EVEN)}$$

$$P = 1 \quad \text{(because even number of 1-bits)}$$

**Answer:** The Parity flag is set to **P = 1**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Flags after ADD Instruction

**Problem:** Let `A = 6EH` and `R3 = 9BH`. Execute `ADD A, R3` and determine CY, AC, OV, and P flags.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1:** Convert hexadecimal to binary.

$$A = 6E_{H} = 0110\,1110_B$$

$$R3 = 9B_{H} = 1001\,1011_B$$

**Step 2:** Perform binary addition.

$$
\begin{aligned}
A   &= 0\,1\,1\,0\,\,1\,1\,1\,0 \\
R3 &= 1\,0\,0\,1\,\,1\,0\,1\,1 \\
\hline
\text{Sum} &= 1\,0\,0\,0\,0\,1\,0\,0\,1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Write the result.

$$\text{Result} = 0000\,1001_B = 09_H \quad \text{(lower 8 bits)}$$

**Step 4:** Determine the Carry Flag (CY).

$$\text{Carry out of bit 7} = 1$$

$$CY = 1$$

**Step 5:** Determine the Auxiliary Carry Flag (AC).

$$\text{Carry from bit 3 to bit 4: } 1+1+1 = 11_B \rightarrow \text{carry generated}$$

$$AC = 1$$

**Step 6:** Determine the Overflow Flag (OV).

Using the formula $OV = C_7 \oplus C_6$:

- $C_7$ = carry out of MSB (bit 7) = **1**
- $C_6$ = carry out of bit 6:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bit 6 column: } 1 + 0 + 0 = 1 \quad (\text{no carry out of bit 6})
\end{aligned}
$$

- $C_6 = 0$

$$OV = 1 \oplus 0 = 1$$

$$OV = 1$$

**Step 7:** Determine the Parity Flag (P).

$$\text{Accumulator after operation} = 09_H = 0000\,1001_B$$

$$N_1 = 2 \quad (\text{even})$$

$$P = 1$$

**Final PSW Contents:**

$$PSW = \underbrace{1}_{CY}\,\underbrace{1}_{AC}\,\underbrace{0}_{F0}\,\underbrace{0}_{RS1}\,\underbrace{0}_{RS0}\,\underbrace{1}_{OV}\,\underbrace{-}_{--}\,\underbrace{1}_{P}$$

$$PSW = 1100\,0\,1\,-\,1_B$$

$$PSW = C5H \quad \text{(if reserved bit treated as 0)}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Register Bank Switching

**Problem:** Write an 8051 assembly code snippet to switch from **Bank 0** to **Bank 3**, save the current PSW on the stack, and then restore it.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1:** State the initial conditions.

$$RS1 = 0, \quad RS0 = 0 \quad \text{(Bank 0 active)}$$

**Step 2:** Push current PSW onto the stack.

```
PUSH PSW      ; Stack ← PSW, SP = SP + 1
```

**Step 3:** Load PSW with value that selects Bank 3.

$$RS1 = 1, \quad RS0 = 1$$

To form the immediate value, we set bits D4 and D3:

$$PSW = XXX1\,1\,0\,XX_B \quad \text{(only RS1, RS0 relevant)}$$

A common safe value: `MOV PSW, #18H` sets RS1=1, RS0=1.

```
MOV PSW, #18H    ; Switch to Bank 3 (addresses 18H–1FH)
```

**Step 4:** Execute operations using Bank 3 registers (R0–R7 now map to 18H–1FH).

```
MOV R0, #55H     ; Actually writes to internal RAM 18H
MOV R1, #0AAH    ; Actually writes to internal RAM 19H
```

**Step 5:** Restore the original PSW by popping from stack.

```
POP PSW         ; PSW ← Stack, SP = SP − 1, Bank 0 is restored
```

**Resulting Binary Trace:**

| Step | Instruction | SP (initial=07H) | RS1 | RS0 | Active Bank |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | (Reset state) | 07H | 0 | 0 | Bank 0 |
| 2 | `PUSH PSW` | 08H | 0 | 0 | Bank 0 |
| 3 | `MOV PSW, #18H` | 08H | 1 | 1 | Bank 3 |
| 4 | `MOV R0, #55H` | 08H | 1 | 1 | Bank 3 |
| 5 | `POP PSW` | 07H | 0 | 0 | Bank 0 |

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Flag Behavior Comparison

| Operation Type | CY | AC | OV | P | Example |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| `ADD A, R0` (no carry) | 0 | 0 | 0 | computed | A=10H, R0=20H |
| `ADD A, R0` (carry) | 1 | 0 | 0 | computed | A=FFH, R0=01H |
| `ADD A, R0` (signed overflow) | 0 | 0 | 1 | computed | A=7FH, R0=01H |
| `SUBB A, R0` (no borrow) | 0 | 0 | 0 | computed | A=50H, R0=10H |
| `SUBB A, R0` (borrow) | 1 | 0 | 0 | computed | A=10H, R0=50H |
| `MUL AB` (overflow) | 0 | 0 | 1 | computed | A=FFH, B=FFH |
| `ANL A, #0FH` | 0 | 0 | 0 | computed | A=FFH (mask upper nibble) |

---

## 3.5 Complete Program Demonstration

**Problem:** Write a program to add two 8-bit numbers, store the result, and indicate carry via P1.0.

### Solution Code:

```assembly
ORG 0000H
        MOV A, #0F5H     ; A = F5H = 1111 0101
        MOV R0, #0BH     ; R0 = 0BH = 0000 1011
        ADD A, R0        ; A = A + R0
        JC  CARRY_SET    ; Jump if CY=1
        CLR P1.0         ; No carry, clear P1.0
        SJMP END_PROG
CARRY_SET:
        SETB P1.0        ; Carry, set P1.0
END_PROG:
        END
```

### Trace Table:

| Step | A (hex) | R0 (hex) | CY | AC | OV | P1.0 |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| After MOV | F5H | 0BH | 0 | 0 | 0 | x |
| After ADD | 00H | 0BH | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 (set) |

**Explanation:** $F5H + 0BH = 100H$. Since result exceeds 8 bits, only 00H remains in A, with CY=1.
