# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Explain the function of the Program Status Register (PSW) in the 8051 microcontroller. List all its bits with their symbols.**

**Model Answer:**

> The **Program Status Register (PSW)** is an **8-bit Special Function Register (SFR)** located at internal RAM address **D0H** in the 8051 microcontroller. It is **bit-addressable** and contains status flags that reflect the result of the most recent arithmetic or logical operation, as well as control bits for register bank selection.

**Bit Allocation:**

| Bit | Symbol | Name | Function |
|:---:|:---:|:---|:---|
| PSW.7 | CY | Carry Flag | Set on arithmetic carry/borrow |
| PSW.6 | AC | Auxiliary Carry | Carry from bit 3 to bit 4 (used in BCD) |
| PSW.5 | F0 | User Flag 0 | General-purpose software flag |
| PSW.4 | RS1 | Register Bank Select 1 | Bank selection high bit |
| PSW.3 | RS0 | Register Bank Select 0 | Bank selection low bit |
| PSW.2 | OV | Overflow Flag | Signed arithmetic overflow |
| PSW.1 | — | Reserved | Undefined; reserved by Intel |
| PSW.0 | P | Parity Flag | Set if accumulator has even number of 1s |

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: What is the Parity Flag? How is it computed for the Accumulator value A = A3H?**

**Model Answer:**

> The **Parity Flag (P)** is bit PSW.0 in the 8051. It is **automatically set/cleared by hardware** after every instruction that writes to the Accumulator.
> 
> **Rule:** P = 1 if the Accumulator contains an **even** number of 1-bits; P = 0 if **odd**.

**Computation for A = A3H:**

$$A3_{H} = 1010\,0011_B$$

Counting 1-bits: positions D7, D5, D1, D0 → **4 ones (even)**.

$$P = 1$$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Q: With a neat diagram, explain the PSW register of 8051. How are the register banks selected using PSW bits?**

**Model Answer:**

**PSW Register Diagram:**

| PSW.7 | PSW.6 | PSW.5 | PSW.4 | PSW.3 | PSW.2 | PSW.1 | PSW.0 |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| CY | AC | F0 | RS1 | RS0 | OV | — | P |

- **CY (PSW.7):** Carry flag — set when ADD/SUBB produces a carry/borrow.
- **AC (PSW.6):** Auxiliary carry — set when carry propagates from bit 3 to bit 4.
- **F0 (PSW.5):** User-defined flag, available for general software use.
- **RS1, RS0 (PSW.4, PSW.3):** Select one of four register banks.
- **OV (PSW.2):** Overflow flag — set on signed arithmetic overflow.
- **PSW.1:** Reserved (undefined).
- **P (PSW.0):** Parity flag — set if A has even number of 1s.

**Register Bank Selection:**

| RS1 | RS0 | Bank | Address Range |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | 0 | Bank 0 | 00H–07H |
| 0 | 1 | Bank 1 | 08H–0FH |
| 1 | 0 | Bank 2 | 10H–17H |
| 1 | 1 | Bank 3 | 18H–1FH |

The combination of RS1 and RS0 forms a 2-bit address that selects one of four banks, each containing R0–R7. **Default after reset: Bank 0.**

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Q: Explain how the Carry (CY), Auxiliary Carry (AC), and Overflow (OV) flags are set during the execution of the instruction `ADD A, R0` when A = 5AH and R0 = 8CH. Show all flag values.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Convert to binary.**

$$A = 5A_{H} = 0101\,1010_B$$

$$R0 = 8C_{H} = 1000\,1100_B$$

**Step 2: Perform binary addition.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& 0\,1\,0\,1\,1\,0\,1\,0 \\
+& 1\,0\,0\,0\,1\,1\,0\,0 \\
\hline
& 1\,1\,1\,0\,0\,1\,1\,0
\end{aligned}
$$

Result in A = $E6_{H}$ with carry out of bit 7.

**Step 3: Determine CY.**

Carry out of MSB (bit 7) = **1** → $CY = 1$

**Step 4: Determine AC.**

Lower nibble: $1010 + 1100 = 1\,0110_B$ → carry from bit 3 to bit 4 = **1** → $AC = 1$

**Step 5: Determine OV.**

$C_7 = 1$ (carry out of bit 7), $C_6 = 0$ (bit 6 sum: $1+0+0=1$, no carry out).

$$OV = C_7 \oplus C_6 = 1 \oplus 0 = 1 \quad \Rightarrow OV = 1$$

**Step 6: Determine P.**

A = $E6_{H} = 1110\,0110_B$ → number of 1s = 5 (odd) → $P = 0$

**Final PSW = 1100 0100 (with reserved bit = 0) = C4H**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Q: List and explain the different instructions that directly access or modify the Program Status Register in 8051. Give one example for each.**

**Model Answer:**

The 8051 provides the following instructions that directly operate on the PSW register:

**1. `MOV PSW, #data`** — Load immediate value into PSW.
- Example: `MOV PSW, #18H` → Sets RS1=1, RS0=1, selects Bank 3.
- Bytes: 3, Cycles: 2.

**2. `MOV direct, PSW`** — Copy PSW to internal RAM location.
- Example: `MOV 30H, PSW` → Saves current PSW flags to RAM address 30H.
- Bytes: 3, Cycles: 2.

**3. `PUSH PSW`** — Push PSW onto stack.
- Example: `PUSH PSW` → SP increments, then PSW written to stack.
- Bytes: 1, Cycles: 2.

**4. `POP PSW`** — Pop PSW from stack.
- Example: `POP PSW` → PSW restored from stack, SP decrements.
- Bytes: 1, Cycles: 2.

**5. Carry flag bit instructions:**
- `SETB CY` — Sets carry flag to 1.
- `CLR CY` — Clears carry flag to 0.
- `CPL CY` — Complements carry flag.
- Bytes: 1 each, Cycles: 1.

**6. Carry flag logic instructions:**
- `ANL C, bit` — AND carry with bit.
- `ORL C, bit` — OR carry with bit.
- Example: `ANL C, P1.0` — CY = CY AND P1.0.

**Key Point:** Instructions like `ADD`, `SUBB`, `MUL`, `DIV` modify flags **automatically** without explicit PSW operand.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Q: Write an 8051 ALP to add two 8-bit numbers stored in internal RAM locations 30H and 31H. Store the result in 32H. If a carry is generated, set bit P1.0; otherwise clear it. Show the final flag status.**

**Model Answer:**

```assembly
ORG 0000H
        MOV A, 30H       ; Load first number into A
        ADD A, 31H       ; Add second number
        JC  CARRY_FLAG   ; Jump if CY = 1
        MOV 32H, A       ; Store result
        CLR P1.0         ; No carry, clear P1.0
        SJMP FINISH
CARRY_FLAG:
        MOV 32H, A       ; Store result
        SETB P1.0        ; Carry generated, set P1.0
FINISH:
        END
```

**Example Trace** (assume 30H = C5H, 31H = 4AH):

$$C5_{H} + 4A_{H} = 10F_{H}$$

Result: A = 0FH, CY = 1, AC = 0, OV = 0, P = 0

**Flag Status After Execution:**

| Flag | Value | Reason |
|:---:|:---:|:---|
| CY | 1 | Carry out of bit 7 (100H result) |
| AC | 0 | No carry from bit 3 to bit 4 |
| OV | 0 | Signed result is within range |
| P | 0 | A = 0FH = 0000 1111 has 4 ones (even)... wait: actually 0FH has 4 ones, which is even, so P = 1 |

**Corrected Final Flag Status:** CY=1, AC=0, OV=0, P=1, P1.0=1.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 
> 1. **Parity Flag Trap (Most Common Mistake):** Students often write P=1 for "odd number of 1s" — this is **WRONG**. P=1 means **EVEN** parity. Many students lose 1 mark here.
> 
> 2. **Overflow vs. Carry Confusion:** The **Carry Flag (CY)** indicates unsigned overflow (result > 255). The **Overflow Flag (OV)** indicates signed overflow (result outside −128 to +127). These are **completely different** — do not interchange them.
> 
> 3. **INC/DEC Register vs. INC/DEC A:** `INC A` affects the Parity flag, but `INC R0` does **NOT** affect any flag. This is a common KTU trick question.
> 
> 4. **MUL/DIV Special Behavior:** `MUL AB` always **clears CY** but sets OV if the product exceeds 255. `DIV AB` clears CY and sets OV only on **division by zero**. Students forget that CY is always 0 after MUL/DIV.
> 
> 5. **RS1, RS0 Default:** After 8051 reset, PSW = 00H, meaning **Bank 0 is active** and **SP = 07H**. Writing to R0–R7 in default code modifies RAM addresses 00H–07H. If you forget this, your stack (which starts at 08H after first PUSH) will collide with R0!
> 
> 6. **Reserved Bit (PSW.1):** Never assume a value for PSW.1. In KTU exams, if asked for the complete PSW byte, state "PSW.1 is reserved and undefined."

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🚀

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Program Status Register Instructions**

### **Core Facts to Memorize:**
- ✅ PSW is at SFR address **D0H** and is **bit-addressable**.
- ✅ PSW has **8 bits**: CY, AC, F0, RS1, RS0, OV, (Reserved), P.
- ✅ **4 register banks** × **8 registers each** = 32 bytes (00H–1FH) of register space.
- ✅ Default bank after reset: **Bank 0** (RS1=0, RS0=0).
- ✅ Default SP after reset: **07H**.

### **Flag-Setting Rules:**
- ✅ **CY = 1** when unsigned addition exceeds 255 or subtraction needs borrow.
- ✅ **AC = 1** when carry propagates from bit D3 to D4 (BCD arithmetic indicator).
- ✅ **OV = 1** when signed result is outside [−128, +127]; formula: $OV = C_7 \oplus C_6$.
- ✅ **P = 1** when Accumulator has **EVEN** number of 1-bits (inverted naming convention).

### **Instruction Categories:**
- ✅ **Direct PSW access:** `MOV PSW, #data`, `PUSH PSW`, `POP PSW`.
- ✅ **Bit manipulation:** `SETB CY`, `CLR CY`, `CPL CY`, `ANL C,bit`, `ORL C,bit`.
- ✅ **Arithmetic affecting flags:** ADD, ADDC, SUBB, MUL, DIV, DA.
- ✅ **Logical affecting P only:** ANL, ORL, XRL, CLR, CPL, INC A, DEC A.

### **Special Behaviors:**
- ✅ `MUL AB` always clears CY.
- ✅ `DIV AB` sets OV only on divide-by-zero.
- ✅ `INC A` affects P; `INC Rn` does NOT affect any flag.
- ✅ `MOV` instructions do NOT affect any flag (except implicit P update when destination is A).

### **Bank Selection Quick Reference:**
- ✅ Bank 0: `MOV PSW, #00H` (or just `CLR RS1` / `CLR RS0`).
- ✅ Bank 1: `SETB RS0` / `CLR RS1`.
- ✅ Bank 2: `CLR RS0` / `SETB RS1`.
- ✅ Bank 3: `MOV PSW, #18H` or set both RS1 and RS0.

### **Common Exam Pitfalls to Avoid:**
- ❌ Confusing P=1 with "odd parity" (it means EVEN).
- ❌ Confusing CY (unsigned) with OV (signed).
- ❌ Forgetting that INC Rn does not affect flags.
- ❌ Assuming PSW.1 has a defined value.
- ❌ Not showing binary addition step-by-step in overflow/carry problems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Loading Constants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCIT404 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Loading Constants

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **Intel 8051 Microcontroller** assembly language programming, **"Loading Constants"** refers to the category of data transfer instructions that transfer a *known, fixed numerical value* (called an *immediate operand* or *literal*) into a CPU register (most commonly the **Accumulator `A`**, general-purpose registers `R0–R7`, or the **Data Pointer `DPTR`**) or into a specific **internal data memory (IRAM) location**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Exact Terminology):**
> "Loading constants is the process of initializing CPU registers and internal RAM locations with immediate 8-bit or 16-bit literal values using instructions of the **Immediate Addressing Mode** family. In the 8051, this operation is primarily performed by the `MOV` instruction using the hash (`#`) symbol prefix to denote an immediate source operand."

The mnemonic used is **`MOV`** (Move), and the syntax universally follows the pattern:

**Syntax:** `MOV Destination, #Source_Constant`

The `#` symbol is the **mandatory assembler directive** that tells the assembler: *"The data following is a literal number, not a memory address."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **🧠 Real-World Analogy — The Whiteboard & Marker**
> 
> Imagine you are a **factory supervisor** (the CPU), and you have several **empty boxes** (the registers) on your workbench labeled `A`, `R0`, `R1`, ... `R7`, and `DPTR`. 
> 
> - **"Loading a constant"** is equivalent to you picking up a **marker pen** and writing a *specific, hard-coded number* (e.g., `25`, `#0x3F`, `#1000`) directly onto a label and sticking it on a box. 
> - The number is **embedded inside the instruction itself** (printed on the pen label), it does **not** need to be fetched from another shelf (memory).
> - The `#` sign is the **assembler's signal** that "what follows is the value to write, not a shelf number to look up."

### 1.3 Why Constants Must Be "Loaded"

In any embedded program, the very first thing the CPU does is **initialize** its working environment. Without loaded constants:
- Counters cannot start at zero.
- Ports cannot be configured (e.g., `#0FFh` to make a port an input).
- Lookup tables cannot be pointed to (e.g., `MOV DPTR, #TABLE_BASE`).
- Mathematical computations cannot begin (`MOV A, #data` to provide the first operand).

### 1.4 Physical/Architectural Constants of the 8051 Relevant to Loading

| Architectural Parameter | Value | Implication for Constant Loading |
|-------------------------|-------|----------------------------------|
| Accumulator width | **8 bits** | Can hold a max immediate value of **255 decimal (`#0FFH`)** |
| General-purpose registers (R0–R7) | **8 bits each** | Each can hold max **255** |
| DPTR width | **16 bits** | Can hold up to **65,535 (`#FFFFH`)** for code/data ROM addresses |
| Internal RAM (IRAM) | **128 bytes** (00H–7FH) | Direct-address loading target is within this range |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule of 8051 Constant Loading:** You **CANNOT** load a 16-bit constant directly into the Accumulator or any R-register in a single instruction. You must use `DPTR` for 16-bit literals, or split the value into two 8-bit halves.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Families of Constant-Loading Instructions

The 8051 ISA groups constant-loading instructions into **two primary families** based on the data width of the literal operand.

#### Family A: 8-Bit Constant Loading (Most Common)
These instructions load a single byte (range: `#00H` to `#FFH`).

**1. Load immediate to Accumulator:**
- Mnemonic: `MOV A, #data`
- Opcode bytes: 2 (1 opcode + 1 immediate byte)
- Machine cycles: 1
- Operation: `A ← data`
- Example: `MOV A, #25H` → Loads hexadecimal 25 into the accumulator.

**2. Load immediate to Register Rn (n = 0 to 7):**
- Mnemonic: `MOV Rn, #data`
- Opcode bytes: 2
- Machine cycles: 1
- Operation: `Rn ← data`
- Example: `MOV R3, #0` → Initializes R3 as a counter starting at zero.

**3. Load immediate to Direct Internal RAM:**
- Mnemonic: `MOV direct, #data` (where direct is an address 00H–7FH, e.g., `30H`)
- Opcode bytes: 3 (1 opcode + 1 address byte + 1 data byte)
- Machine cycles: 2
- Operation: `(direct) ← data`
- Example: `MOV 30H, #55H` → Stores `55H` into IRAM address `30H`.

#### Family B: 16-Bit Constant Loading
Only the **Data Pointer (DPTR)** can be loaded with a 16-bit immediate in a single instruction.

**4. Load 16-bit immediate to DPTR:**
- Mnemonic: `MOV DPTR, #data16`
- Opcode bytes: 3 (1 opcode + 2 immediate bytes — high byte first, then low byte)
- Machine cycles: 2
- Operation: `DPH ← high_byte(data16)`, `DPL ← low_byte(data16)`
- Example: `MOV DPTR, #2000H` → `DPH = 20H`, `DPL = 00H`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Internal Architecture Note (KTU Hot Topic):** The 8051's `MOV DPTR, #data16` is the **only single instruction** in the entire ISA that handles a 16-bit immediate. This is why it is the cornerstone of lookup-table programming, where the base address of a table in code ROM must be specified.

### 2.2 The "Why" — Mechanics Inside the CPU

When the Program Counter (PC) fetches an instruction like `MOV A, #3FH`:

1. **Fetch Phase (T1 state):** The 8-bit opcode `74H` is fetched from code ROM and placed in the Instruction Register (IR).
2. **Decode Phase (T2):** The Instruction Decoder recognizes opcode `74H` and signals that **one more byte** must be fetched (immediate operand).
3. **Operand Fetch (T3, T4):** The PC is incremented, and the byte `3FH` is fetched from code ROM and temporarily held in a buffer.
4. **Execute Phase:** The internal data bus transfers the byte `3FH` from the operand buffer into the Accumulator's internal flip-flops. The PSW flags (especially **P — Parity flag**) are updated automatically.

### 2.3 Flag Register (PSW) Behavior During Constant Loading

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often assume *all* `MOV` instructions affect flags. **They do not.**
> 
> The only flag that can be **modified** by a constant-loading `MOV` (specifically `MOV A, #data`) is the **Parity Flag (P)**. The Carry (C), Auxiliary Carry (AC), and Overflow (OV) flags are **never affected** by `MOV` instructions. The `MOV` instruction only sets P=1 if the number of 1-bits in the Accumulator is odd.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Instruction | Operand Size | Opcode (Hex) | Bytes | Machine Cycles | Destination | Affected Flags |
|-------------|-------------|--------------|-------|----------------|-------------|----------------|
| `MOV A, #data` | 8-bit | **74H** | 2 | 1 | Accumulator | **P only** |
| `MOV R0, #data` | 8-bit | **78H** | 2 | 1 | R0 | None |
| `MOV R1, #data` | 8-bit | **79H** | 2 | 1 | R1 | None |
| `MOV R2, #data` | 8-bit | **7AH** | 2 | 1 | R2 | None |
| `MOV R3, #data` | 8-bit | **7BH** | 2 | 1 | R3 | None |
| `MOV R4, #data` | 8-bit | **7CH** | 2 | 1 | R4 | None |
| `MOV R5, #data` | 8-bit | **7DH** | 2 | 1 | R5 | None |
| `MOV R6, #data` | 8-bit | **7EH** | 2 | 1 | R6 | None |
| `MOV R7, #data` | 8-bit | **7FH** | 2 | 1 | R7 | None |
| `MOV direct, #data` | 8-bit | **75H** | 3 | 2 | IRAM (00H–7FH) | None |
| `MOV DPTR, #data16` | 16-bit | **90H** | 3 | 2 | DPTR (DPH+DPL) | None |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In industrial embedded systems (the field the 8051 dominates — washing machines, microwave ovens, automotive dashboards, elevator controllers), constant loading is the **first stage of every firmware**:

- **Initializing watchdog timer registers** → `MOV TMOD, #01H`
- **Setting up serial baud rates** → `MOV TH1, #0FDH` (for 9600 baud)
- **Defining message strings in ROM** → `MOV DPTR, #MY_STRING`
- **Resetting event counters** → `CLR A` is internally `MOV A, #00H` (opcode `E4H`)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** The instruction `CLR A` is essentially a *special-case constant load* — it loads the value `00H` into the Accumulator using a 1-byte opcode (`E4H`) instead of the 2-byte `MOV A, #0` (`74H 00H`). This is a classic "size optimization" trick examined in KTU tests.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Examples & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Loading an 8-bit Constant into the Accumulator

**Problem Statement:** 
Write an 8051 assembly program to load the value `3FH` into the Accumulator. Identify the opcode, the bytes stored in code memory, and the final status of the Parity flag.

**Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough:**

**Step 1 — Identify the Instruction:**
We use the instruction `MOV A, #3FH`.

**Step 2 — Look Up the Opcode:**
From the cheat sheet, the opcode for `MOV A, #data` is **`74H`**.

**Step 3 — Construct the Machine Code:**
The assembler translates `MOV A, #3FH` into two consecutive bytes stored in code ROM:
- Byte 1 (Address, say, 2000H): `74H` (the opcode)
- Byte 2 (Address 2001H): `3FH` (the immediate data)

**Step 4 — Execute and Track Internal State:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Before Execution:} \quad & A = XXH \text{ (don't care, old value)} \\
& P = x \text{ (unknown)} \\
\text{After CPU Fetches Opcode } 74H \text{ at } 2000H: \quad & \text{IR} = 74H \\
\text{After Operand Fetch at } 2001H: \quad & \text{Operand Buffer} = 3FH \\
\text{After Execute Phase:} \quad & A = 3FH \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Parity Flag (P):**
The value `3FH` in binary is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
3F_{16} &= 0011\;1111_2 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Counting the 1-bits: there are **six** 1-bits. Six is an **even** number, so the Parity flag `P = 0` (the 8051 convention is P=1 for *odd* parity).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final State:** `A = 3FH`, `P = 0`, all other PSW flags unchanged.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Loading a 16-bit Constant into DPTR

**Problem Statement:**
Load the value `1234H` into the DPTR. Show the byte arrangement.

**Step-by-Step Walkthrough:**

**Step 1 — Instruction:** `MOV DPTR, #1234H`

**Step 2 — Opcode:** `90H` (the only 3-byte instruction in the 8051 with 16-bit immediate)

**Step 3 — Machine Code Storage (in code ROM, little-endian convention):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Address 2000H:} \quad & 90H \quad \text{(opcode)} \\
\text{Address 2001H:} \quad & 12H \quad \text{(high byte of constant — DPH destination)} \\
\text{Address 2002H:} \quad & 34H \quad \text{(low byte of constant — DPL destination)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Execution Outcome:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DPH &= 12H \\
DPL &= 34H \\
DPTR &= 1234H \quad \text{(as a logical 16-bit register)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Loading Constants into Multiple Registers (Initialization Sequence)

**Problem Statement:** 
Write a code snippet to initialize the following state for a counter routine:
- `R0` ← `#09H` (loop count)
- `R1` ← `#00H` (sum accumulator)
- Internal RAM address `40H` ← `#0FFH` (status flag)
- `DPTR` ← `#2000H` (lookup table base)

**The Complete Initialization Block:**

```assembly
        MOV R0,  #09H      ; 2 bytes, 1 cycle
        MOV R1,  #00H      ; 2 bytes, 1 cycle
        MOV 40H, #0FFH     ; 3 bytes, 2 cycles
        MOV DPTR,#2000H    ; 3 bytes, 2 cycles
```

**Memory Footprint Analysis (Analytical Matrix):**

| Line | Instruction | Opcode | Byte 1 | Byte 2 | Byte 3 | Total Bytes | Cycles |
|------|-------------|--------|--------|--------|--------|-------------|--------|
| 1 | `MOV R0, #09H` | `78H` | `78H` | `09H` | — | **2** | **1** |
| 2 | `MOV R1, #00H` | `79H` | `79H` | `00H` | — | **2** | **1** |
| 3 | `MOV 40H, #0FFH` | `75H` | `75H` | `40H` | `FFH` | **3** | **2** |
| 4 | `MOV DPTR, #2000H` | `90H` | `90H` | `20H` | `00H` | **3** | **2** |
| | | | | | **TOTAL** | **10 bytes** | **6 cycles** |

**Total Execution Time at 11.0592 MHz Crystal:**
Each machine cycle = 12 oscillator periods.
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{cycle} &= \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6} = 1.085\;\mu s \\
T_{total} &= 6 \times 1.085\;\mu s = 6.51\;\mu s \\
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Illegal Loading Scenario (Common KTU Pitfall)

**Problem:** A student writes `MOV A, #1234H`.

**Analysis — Why this is INVALID:**

The 8051's `MOV A, #data` instruction is hard-wired (by the control logic of the `74H` opcode) to fetch **only one** additional byte from code memory. When the assembler encounters `#1234H` (a 16-bit literal), it cannot fit it into a single 8-bit immediate slot, so the assembler will throw the error: **`Error 22: Operand size mismatch`** or **`Symbol not defined`**.

**The Correct Two-Step Equivalent:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: } & MOV A, \#12H \quad ; \text{Load high byte first} \\
\text{Step 2: } & MOV B, \#34H \quad ; \text{Load low byte (in B register)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Or, if the goal was to load DPTR with `1234H`:
$$
MOV \; DPTR, \#1234H \quad \text{— This is the only legal one-instruction fix.}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Trap:** Questions like *"Load the value `0x1234` into the Accumulator"* are designed to test whether you know that **A is 8-bit only**. You must split it into two instructions.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Decision Tree for Choosing the Right Instruction

| If the destination is... | And the constant is... | Use this instruction | Opcode |
|--------------------------|------------------------|----------------------|--------|
| Accumulator `A` | 8-bit (`#00`–`#FF`) | `MOV A, #data` | `74H` |
| Register `Rn` | 8-bit | `MOV Rn, #data` | `78H`–`7FH` |
| IRAM `direct` (00H–7FH) | 8-bit | `MOV direct, #data` | `75H` |
| Data Pointer `DPTR` | 16-bit | `MOV DPTR, #data16` | `90H` |
| Any 16-bit value into `A` | 16-bit | **Not possible in one instruction** — split or use DPTR | N/A |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Flow Diagram — How `MOV A, #data` Executes Internally

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[PC points to instruction at 2000H] --> B[Fetch Opcode byte from Code ROM]
    B --> C{Opcode = 74H?}
    C -- No --> D[Decode as different instruction]
    C -- Yes --> E[PC increments to 2001H]
    E --> F[Fetch immediate operand byte]
    F --> G[Place operand in Operand Buffer]
    G --> H[Activate internal data bus path: Buffer to Accumulator]
    H --> I[Transfer 8 bits to A register flip-flops]
    I --> J[Update Parity flag P based on 1-bit count in A]
    J --> K[Instruction complete: A holds constant]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — The "Load Path" Inside the 8051

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CodeROM[Code ROM]
        instr1[Opcode 74H]
        instr2[Operand 3FH]
    end
    
    subgraph CPU[8051 CPU Core]
        PCblock[Program Counter PC]
        IRblock[Instruction Register]
        Decode[Instruction Decoder]
        OperandBuf[Operand Buffer]
        Datapath[Internal Data Bus 8-bit]
        AccumA[Accumulator A]
        PSWflags[PSW Flags P, C, AC, OV]
    end
    
    PCblock -->|fetches| instr1
    instr1 --> IRblock
    IRblock --> Decode
    Decode -->|control signals| OperandBuf
    PCblock -->|fetches| instr2
    instr2 --> OperandBuf
    OperandBuf --> Datapath
    Datapath --> AccumA
    AccumA -->|parity check| PSWflags
```

### 4.3 Decision Flowchart — Choosing the Correct Constant-Load Instruction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Need to load a constant]) --> Q1{What is the size of the constant?}
    Q1 -- 8-bit 00H to FFH --> Q2{Where to store it?}
    Q1 -- 16-bit 0000H to FFFFH --> R1[Use MOV DPTR, data16]
    Q1 -- More than 16 bits --> Err1[Not possible with single MOV]
    
    Q2 -- Into Accumulator A --> P1[MOV A, data8 - opcode 74H]
    Q2 -- Into R0 to R7 --> P2[MOV Rn, data8 - opcode 78H to 7FH]
    Q2 -- Into IRAM 00H to 7FH --> P3[MOV direct, data8 - opcode 75H]
    Q2 -- Into SFRs like P1, TMOD --> P4[MOV SFR_name, data8 - opcode 75H]
    
    P1 --> Done([Constant loaded])
    P2 --> Done
    P3 --> Done
    P4 --> Done
    R1 --> Done
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Explain how an 8-bit immediate constant is loaded into the Accumulator of the 8051 microcontroller. Mention the opcode, instruction size, and machine cycles.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

To load an 8-bit constant into the Accumulator, the instruction used is **`MOV A, #data`**, for example, `MOV A, #25H`.

- **Opcode:** `74H` (universal opcode for "load immediate to A")
- **Instruction Size:** 2 bytes (1 opcode byte + 1 immediate data byte)
- **Machine Cycles:** 1 cycle
- **Working:** The CPU fetches the opcode `74H` from code ROM, decodes it, fetches the next byte (the immediate data), and transfers it through the internal data bus into the Accumulator's flip-flops. The Parity flag `P` of the PSW is updated based on the number of 1-bits in the loaded value. No other flags (C, AC, OV) are affected.

*(Examiner's note: Award 1 mark for the instruction + example, 1 mark for opcode/size/cycles, 1 mark for the working description and flag behavior.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Why is the instruction `MOV A, #1234H` invalid in 8051 assembly language? Suggest the correct alternative.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The instruction `MOV A, #1234H` is **invalid** because the Accumulator of the 8051 is an **8-bit register** and can therefore hold a maximum value of `FFH` (255 decimal). The `MOV A, #data` instruction (opcode `74H`) is hard-wired to fetch only **one additional byte** (8 bits) from code memory. A 16-bit literal like `1234H` cannot fit into this 8-bit slot, causing an assembler error.

**Correct Alternatives:**

(a) If the intent was to point to a 16-bit address (e.g., a lookup table), use:
`MOV DPTR, #1234H` — This is the only 8051 instruction that accepts a 16-bit immediate operand in a single step.

(b) If the data must reside in the CPU, split it into two 8-bit loads:
`MOV A, #12H` (high byte) and `MOV B, #34H` (low byte) — using Register B as a temporary holder.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for "8-bit accumulator" reasoning, 1 mark for assembler behavior, 1 mark for the correct alternative.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 — Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**Question A (14 Marks):**
*(a)* Explain the different ways of loading constants in 8051 assembly language programming. Discuss the instructions `MOV A, #data`, `MOV Rn, #data`, `MOV direct, #data`, and `MOV DPTR, #data16` with their opcodes, sizes, and machine cycles. (7 Marks)

*(b)* Write an 8051 assembly program to initialize the system as follows: Load `#55H` into the Accumulator, `#0AH` into `R3`, `#0FFH` into internal RAM location `30H`, and `#2000H` into the DPTR. Show the complete machine code generated and calculate the total memory footprint and execution time assuming a crystal frequency of 12 MHz. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer — Part (a) [7 Marks]:**

| Instruction | Purpose | Opcode | Bytes | Cycles |
|-------------|---------|--------|-------|--------|
| `MOV A, #data` | Load 8-bit constant to Accumulator | `74H` | 2 | 1 |
| `MOV Rn, #data` | Load 8-bit constant to Register Rn (R0–R7) | `78H`–`7FH` | 2 | 1 |
| `MOV direct, #data` | Load 8-bit constant to IRAM 00H–7FH | `75H` | 3 | 2 |
| `MOV DPTR, #data16` | Load 16-bit constant to Data Pointer | `90H` | 3 | 2 |

**Key Explanations:**
- All four use **Immediate Addressing Mode** (signified by `#`).
- The first three are 8-bit and differ only in destination.
- The fourth is uniquely 16-bit, and is the **only instruction** in the 8051 ISA that handles a 16-bit immediate operand.
- The Accumulator is the only register whose loading affects the **Parity (P) flag**; others do not affect any flag.
- `MOV direct, #data` is also used to configure **Special Function Registers (SFRs)** such as `TMOD`, `IE`, `P1`, etc., as they overlap the upper IRAM range (80H–FFH).

**Model Answer — Part (b) [7 Marks]:**

The assembly program:
```assembly
        MOV A,   #55H     ; 2 bytes
        MOV R3,  #0AH     ; 2 bytes
        MOV 30H, #0FFH    ; 3 bytes
        MOV DPTR,#2000H   ; 3 bytes
```

**Generated Machine Code Table (assuming code starts at 0000H):**

| Address | Byte 1 | Byte 2 | Byte 3 | Instruction | Cycles |
|---------|--------|--------|--------|-------------|--------|
| `0000H` | `74H` | `55H` | — | `MOV A, #55H` | 1 |
| `0002H` | `7BH` | `0AH` | — | `MOV R3, #0AH` | 1 |
| `0004H` | `75H` | `30H` | `FFH` | `MOV 30H, #0FFH` | 2 |
| `0007H` | `90H` | `20H` | `00H` | `MOV DPTR, #2000H` | 2 |

**Total Memory Footprint:**

$$
\text{Total Bytes} = 2 + 2 + 3 + 3 = 10 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Total Machine Cycles:**

$$
\text{Total Cycles} = 1 + 1 + 2 + 2 = 6 \text{ cycles}
$$

**Execution Time at 12 MHz Crystal:**

The 8051 divides the oscillator frequency by 12 to get the machine cycle frequency.
$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{cycle} &= \frac{12 \text{ MHz}}{12} = 1 \text{ MHz} \\
T_{cycle} &= \frac{1}{1 \text{ MHz}} = 1\;\mu s \\
T_{total} &= 6 \text{ cycles} \times 1\;\mu s = 6\;\mu s \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final State of CPU Registers and Memory:**
- `A = 55H`
- `R3 = 0AH`
- Internal RAM `30H = FFH`
- `DPH = 20H`, `DPL = 00H` (so `DPTR = 2000H`)
- `P flag = 0` (since `55H = 0101 0101₂` has four 1-bits → even parity).

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**
*(a)* With a neat diagram, explain the internal execution steps of the instruction `MOV A, #3FH` in the 8051. Show the timing states and the data path. (7 Marks)

*(b)* Compare and contrast the instructions `MOV A, #data`, `MOV R0, #data`, and `MOV DPTR, #data16` in terms of: (i) addressing mode, (ii) operand size, (iii) opcode length, (iv) machine cycles required, and (v) flag register effects. Construct a comparison table. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer — Part (a) [7 Marks]:**

The instruction `MOV A, #3FH` is a 2-byte, 1-cycle instruction belonging to the Immediate Addressing family. Its execution spans **two machine cycles (4 oscillator states)**.

**Step-by-Step Execution with Timing States:**

| State | Activity | Bus Activity |
|-------|----------|--------------|
| **S1 of Cycle 1** | Opcode Fetch | PC outputs `0000H`; code ROM outputs `74H` on the bus; CPU reads opcode. |
| **S2 of Cycle 1** | Opcode Fetch (latch) | Opcode `74H` is latched into the Instruction Register (IR). |
| **S3 of Cycle 1** | Operand Fetch | PC incremented to `0001H`; code ROM outputs `3FH`. |
| **S4 of Cycle 1** | Operand Latch + Execute | Byte `3FH` is transferred to the internal data bus and written into the Accumulator. Parity logic checks bit count. |
| **S5, S6 (none)** | — | Instruction complete in 1 machine cycle; no Cycle 2 needed. |

**Internal Block Diagram (textual representation):**

```
   Code ROM                      8051 CPU
 ┌──────────┐    bus     ┌──────────────────────────┐
 │ 74H   3FH│ ─────────► │  PC → IR → Decoder       │
 └──────────┘            │           ↓              │
                         │  Operand Buffer ← 3FH    │
                         │           ↓              │
                         │  Internal Data Bus       │
                         │           ↓              │
                         │  Accumulator A ← 3FH     │
                         │           ↓              │
                         │  Parity Logic → PSW.P    │
                         └──────────────────────────┘
```

**Key Points:**
- The opcode `74H` is the *enabler*: it tells the decoder "fetch 1 more byte as immediate data."
- Execution completes within **a single machine cycle (1 µs at 12 MHz)**, unlike `MOV direct, #data` which takes 2 cycles.
- The internal data bus width (8 bits) limits the immediate operand to one byte.

**Model Answer — Part (b) [7 Marks]:**

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | `MOV A, #data` | `MOV R0, #data` | `MOV DPTR, #data16` |
|-----------|----------------|------------------|----------------------|
| (i) Addressing Mode | Immediate | Immediate | Immediate (16-bit variant) |
| (ii) Operand Size | 8-bit | 8-bit | 16-bit |
| (iii) Opcode Length | 2 bytes | 2 bytes | 3 bytes |
| (iv) Machine Cycles | 1 | 1 | 2 |
| (v) Flag Effects | P flag only | None | None |
| Opcode (Hex) | 74H | 78H | 90H |
| Destination | Accumulator | R0 register | DPTR (DPH + DPL) |
| Range of Value | 00H–FFH | 00H–FFH | 0000H–FFFFH |

**Narrative Comparison:**
- All three instructions share the **Immediate Addressing Mode**, distinguished by the `#` prefix.
- The first two are 8-bit operations and are **single-cycle**; the third is a 16-bit operation and requires **two machine cycles** because two operand bytes must be fetched and stored sequentially into DPH and DPL.
- Only `MOV A, #data` affects the Parity flag, because only the Accumulator is connected to the PSW parity-generation logic.
- `MOV DPTR, #data16` is unique — it is the only 8051 instruction that handles a 16-bit immediate operand in one line. It cannot be replicated by two 8-bit `MOV` instructions targeting DPH and DPL individually using immediate addressing (those instructions do not exist; DPH/DPL can only be loaded via `MOV direct, #data` for SFRs).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 Common Marks-Deduction Traps in "Loading Constants" Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the `#` symbol** — Writing `MOV A, 25H` is interpreted by the assembler as "load the *contents of memory location 25H* into A" (Direct Addressing), NOT "load the value 25H." This is a **silent semantic error** that loses 1–2 marks.
> 
> 2. **Trying to load a 16-bit constant into A or Rn** — As shown above, this is illegal. Students who write `MOV A, #1234H` and then "explain" it lose full marks.
> 
> 3. **Confusing `MOV direct, #data` with `MOV @Rn, #data`** — The latter does **not exist** in the 8051 ISA. You cannot load an immediate value into an indirectly-addressed location; you must first load A or Rn, then use a second `MOV` to transfer.
> 
> 4. **Incorrect PSW Flag Analysis** — Many students write that `MOV` affects the Carry flag. **It does not.** Only the Parity flag (and only for `MOV A, #data`) is affected.
> 
> 5. **Little-Endian Storage Error** — When showing the machine code of `MOV DPTR, #2000H`, students sometimes write `90H 00H 20H`. The correct order is `90H 20H 00H` (high byte first, because that is what gets loaded into DPH).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **"Loading Constants"** in 8051 = transferring a literal (immediate) value to a register or IRAM location using `MOV` with the `#` prefix.
- ✅ The four primary constant-loading instructions are: `MOV A, #data`, `MOV Rn, #data`, `MOV direct, #data`, and `MOV DPTR, #data16`.
- ✅ The `#` symbol is the **assembler's signal** for an *immediate* (literal) operand — without it, the assembler treats the operand as a *memory address*.
- ✅ Opcodes: `MOV A, #data` = **74H**; `MOV Rn, #data` = **78H–7FH**; `MOV direct, #data` = **75H**; `MOV DPTR, #data16` = **90H**.
- ✅ `MOV DPTR, #data16` is the **only 8051 instruction** that accepts a 16-bit immediate operand in a single instruction.
- ✅ **Only `MOV A, #data` affects a flag** — the Parity (P) flag. All other `MOV` constant-load instructions leave PSW unchanged.
- ✅ Instruction sizes: 2 bytes for 8-bit register/A loads; 3 bytes for `MOV direct, #data` and `MOV DPTR, #data16`.
- ✅ Machine cycles: 1 cycle for register/A loads; 2 cycles for direct and DPTR loads.
- ✅ 16-bit constants **cannot** be loaded into the Accumulator or any R-register in one instruction — they must be split or handled via DPTR.
- ✅ `CLR A` (opcode `E4H`) is a size-optimized special case of `MOV A, #00H` (opcode `74H 00H`) — both load 00H into A, but `CLR A` is 1 byte vs 2 bytes.
- ✅ `MOV @Rn, #data` **does not exist** in 8051 ISA; indirect-addressed targets must be loaded in two steps.
- ✅ Execution time at 12 MHz = machine cycles × 1 µs; at 11.0592 MHz ≈ machine cycles × 1.085 µs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Conditional Execution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCIT404 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Conditional Execution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conditional Execution** in ARM architecture is a distinctive feature wherein *every instruction* in the ARM instruction set is conditionally executed based on the state of the **Current Program Status Register (CPSR)** condition flags. The condition is encoded in the **upper 4 bits (bits [31:28])** of every 32-bit ARM instruction, allowing the processor to evaluate a predicate *before* the instruction executes. If the condition evaluates to *false*, the instruction is treated as a **No Operation (NOP)** and the pipeline simply advances, wasting only one cycle.

This is a defining hallmark of the ARM RISC philosophy — instead of using dedicated branch instructions to skip blocks of code, ARM permits *postfixing* any standard instruction with a **condition code suffix** (e.g., `ADDEQ`, `SUBNE`, `MOVGT`), thereby eliminating branch overhead in many short code sequences.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine a **smart elevator** in a building:
- You press a button (the **condition check**).
- If the elevator is *available* (condition **true**), it moves to your floor (the **instruction executes**).
- If the elevator is *busy/maintenance* (condition **false**), the request is simply **ignored** — the system silently does nothing and moves to the next request (a **NOP cycle**).

Conditional execution in ARM works identically:
- The condition field acts as a "gatekeeper".
- If the condition flag state matches, the instruction runs.
- If not, the instruction silently passes through the pipeline, taking only **1 cycle** to skip.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this important for KTU?**
> The ARM7TDMI core (used in **LPC2148**, a standard KTU microcontroller study platform) supports conditional execution on *all* data-processing, load/store, and branch instructions. This drastically reduces code size and branch penalties — a key exam point.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Boundary Conditions

- **Default condition code**: `AL` (Always) — encoded as `1110₂`. This is the implicit condition when no suffix is written.
- **Encoding width**: **4 bits** (16 possible condition codes).
- **Flag bits used**: Four condition flags — **N (Negative)**, **Z (Zero)**, **C (Carry)**, **V (Overflow)**.
- **Performance penalty when skipped**: Exactly **1 clock cycle** (single-cycle NOP).
- **Condition code `1111₂` (NV)**: Reserved/deprecated in ARMv4T and later (it was originally used to indicate "Never" — i.e., unconditional NOP).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of Conditional Execution

The ARM processor executes every instruction through this **micro-sequenced decision flow**:

- **Step 1 — Instruction Fetch (IF)**: The 32-bit instruction is fetched from memory.
- **Step 2 — Condition Decode (ID part 1)**: The 4-bit condition field `[31:28]` is extracted.
- **Step 3 — Flag Evaluation (EX part 1)**: The condition code is tested against the current CPSR flags $\{N, Z, C, V\}$. The logic produces a single boolean: **TRUE** or **FALSE**.
- **Step 4 — Conditional Commit (EX part 2)**:
   - If **TRUE** → ALU/load-store/branch operates normally.
   - If **FALSE** → the instruction's result is *discarded*; write-back to registers is suppressed; pipeline advances.
- **Step 5 — Update PC (if branch)**: Only conditional branches update `R15` (PC) if the condition is met.

### 2.2 The ARM Condition Code Table (KTU High-Yield Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for KTU exam questions on this topic. Memorize the **suffix**, the **flag test**, and the **mnemonic meaning**.

| Opcode (Binary) | Suffix | Flags Tested | Logical Condition | Meaning |
|----------------:|:------:|:-------------|:------------------|:--------|
| 0000 | **EQ** | Z = 1 | $Z$ | **Equal** (zero flag set) |
| 0001 | **NE** | Z = 0 | $\bar{Z}$ | **Not Equal** |
| 0010 | **CS / HS** | C = 1 | $C$ | **Carry Set** / **Unsigned Higher or Same** |
| 0011 | **CC / LO** | C = 0 | $\bar{C}$ | **Carry Clear** / **Unsigned Lower** |
| 0100 | **MI** | N = 1 | $N$ | **Minus / Negative** |
| 0101 | **PL** | N = 0 | $\bar{N}$ | **Plus / Positive or Zero** |
| 0110 | **VS** | V = 1 | $V$ | **Overflow Set** |
| 0111 | **VC** | V = 0 | $\bar{V}$ | **Overflow Clear** |
| 1000 | **HI** | C = 1, Z = 0 | $C \cdot \bar{Z}$ | **Unsigned Higher** |
| 1001 | **LS** | C = 0, Z = 1 | $\bar{C} + Z$ | **Unsigned Lower or Same** |
| 1010 | **GE** | N = V | $N \odot V$ | **Signed Greater or Equal** |
| 1011 | **LT** | N ≠ V | $N \oplus V$ | **Signed Less Than** |
| 1100 | **GT** | Z = 0, N = V | $\bar{Z} \cdot (N \odot V)$ | **Signed Greater Than** |
| 1101 | **LE** | Z = 1, N ≠ V | $Z + (N \oplus V)$ | **Signed Less or Equal** |
| 1110 | **AL** | (ignored) | 1 | **Always** (default) |
| 1111 | **NV** | — | 0 | **Never** (deprecated) |

### 2.3 How Flags Are Set

Flags in CPSR are **NOT** set automatically by every instruction. They are updated only when:
1. The instruction has the **S-bit** set in its encoding (e.g., `ADDS R0, R1, R2`).
2. A **compare-class** instruction is used (`CMP`, `CMN`, `TST`, `TEQ`) — these implicitly set flags.
3. The **MSR** (Move to Status Register) instruction is used to write directly to CPSR.

> [!NOTE]
> **Note for LPC2148 / ARM7 students:** In ARM, the comparison instructions (`CMP`, `TST`, etc.) are written *without* the `S` suffix but still set flags because of their dedicated opcodes.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Utility of Conditional Execution |
|:-------------------|:----------------------------------|
| **Digital Signal Processing** | Saturating arithmetic saturations implemented in 2–3 instructions without branches. |
| **Realtime Control Systems** | Sensor limit checks (`if temp > threshold`) execute in zero branch penalty time. |
| **Embedded Firmware** | Smaller code footprint (no skip-branches), faster interrupt response. |
| **Compiler Optimization** | GCC's `-O2` flag for ARM emits `IT`-block-free conditional code; compilers exploit `ADDS`+conditional to replace branch chains. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 The Algebraic Definition of Each Compound Condition

Each compound condition in the table can be derived as a Boolean expression over the four flag bits. We treat flags as logical propositions: $N, Z, C, V \in \{0, 1\}$.

**Derivation for `GT` (Signed Greater Than) — Example:**

A signed number $A > B$ (where $A - B$ is the implicit comparison) is true when:
- The result is **not zero** ($\bar{Z}$), AND
- The result has the **correct sign** (i.e., $N = V$).

$$ GT \iff \bar{Z} \cdot (N \odot V) $$

where $\odot$ denotes logical **XNOR** ($N \odot V \iff N \oplus \bar{V}$).

**Derivation for `LE` (Signed Less or Equal) — Example:**

$$ LE \iff \overline{GT} \iff Z + \overline{(N \odot V)} \iff Z + (N \oplus V) $$

by De Morgan's law:

$$ \overline{\bar{Z} \cdot (N \odot V)} = Z + \overline{(N \odot V)} = Z + (N \oplus V) $$

**Derivation for `HI` (Unsigned Higher) — Example:**

$$ HI \iff (A > B)_{\text{unsigned}} \iff C \cdot \bar{Z} $$

(If the subtraction produced no borrow and result is non-zero.)

**Derivation for `LS` (Unsigned Lower or Same) — Example:**

$$ LS \iff \overline{HI} \iff \bar{C} + Z $$

### 3.2 Worked Example: Tracing Conditional Execution in a Subtraction

> **Problem:** Execute the instruction `SUBS R4, R0, R1` with the initial state:
> $R0 = 0x0000000A$ (decimal 10), $R1 = 0x00000007$ (decimal 7).
> Find the resulting CPSR flags.

**Step-by-step solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Operation:} \quad & R4 = R0 - R1 \\
R4 & = 0x0000000A - 0x00000007 \\
R4 & = 0x00000003 \quad (\text{decimal } 3)
\end{aligned}$$

**Flag evaluation:**

- **Z (Zero) flag:** Set if result = 0. Since $R4 = 0x00000003 \neq 0$:
$$ Z = 0 $$

- **N (Negative) flag:** Set if bit 31 of result is 1. Since $0x00000003$ has bit 31 = 0:
$$ N = 0 $$

- **C (Carry) flag:** For subtraction, ARM uses **NOT Borrow** (i.e., $C = 1$ if no borrow occurred). Since $R0 > R1$ (unsigned), no borrow:
$$ C = 1 $$

- **V (Overflow) flag:** Set if signed overflow occurred. $10 - 7 = 3$ fits in 32-bit signed range with same sign as operands:
$$ V = 0 $$

**Resulting CPSR flag state: `{N, Z, C, V}` = `{0, 0, 1, 0}`**

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Behaviour of the Next Conditional Instruction

> **Now, immediately execute the next instruction:** `ADDGT R5, R2, R3`

This `ADDGT` will execute **only if the `GT` condition is true** for the flags above.

| Flag | Value |
|:----:|:-----:|
| Z    | 0     |
| N    | 0     |
| V    | 0     |
| C    | 1     |

Apply the `GT` predicate: $\bar{Z} \cdot (N \odot V) = \bar{0} \cdot (0 \odot 0) = 1 \cdot 1 = 1$

$$ \therefore \text{GT} = \text{TRUE} \implies \text{ADDGT executes}, \quad R5 = R2 + R3 $$

**Counter-example:** If the previous `SUBS` had produced $R4 = 0$ (e.g., $R0 = R1$), then $Z = 1$, and:

$$ \bar{Z} \cdot (N \odot V) = 0 \cdot 1 = 0 \implies \text{GT} = \text{FALSE} $$

The `ADDGT` becomes a **NOP** (1 cycle wasted, R5 unchanged).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of Conditional Execution

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **hardware decision flow** inside the ARM7TDMI pipeline when an instruction is decoded:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Instruction Fetch from Memory] --> B[Decode Condition Field bits 31 to 28]
    B --> C{Read CPSR Flags N Z C V}
    C --> D[Evaluate 4-bit Condition Code against Flag State]
    D --> E{Condition True?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Proceed to Normal Execution ALU or Branch or Load Store]
    F --> G[Write-Back Result to Register File]
    E -- No --> H[Suppress Execution Instruction becomes NOP]
    H --> I[Advance PC by 4 bytes]
    G --> I
    I --> J[Fetch Next Instruction]
```

### 4.2 CPSR Register Bit Map and Flag Position

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CPSR [CPSR 32-bit Register]
        direction LR
        B31[Bit 31 N Negative] --> B30[Bit 30 Z Zero] --> B29[Bit 29 C Carry] --> B28[Bit 28 V Overflow] --> B27[Bits 27 to 8 Reserved and Control Bits] --> B7[Bits 7 to 0 I F T M4 M3 M2 M1 M0 Mode]
    end
    subgraph Flags [Condition Flags Cluster]
        direction TB
        N2[N - Set if MSB of result is 1]
        Z2[Z - Set if result is 0]
        C2[C - Set if carry out from ALU or no borrow in subtract]
        V2[V - Set if signed overflow occurred]
    end
    B31 --- N2
    B30 --- Z2
    B29 --- C2
    B28 --- V2
```

### 4.3 Decision Flow for the GT Condition Code

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Evaluate GT condition] --> ZCheck{Z = 0?}
    ZCheck -- No Z = 1 --> False1[Result is zero - not greater - GT FALSE - NOP]
    ZCheck -- Yes Z = 0 --> NVCheck{N = V?}
    NVCheck -- Yes N equals V --> True[Both conditions satisfied - GT TRUE - execute]
    NVCheck -- No N different from V --> False2[Sign mismatch - GT FALSE - NOP]
```

### 4.4 Comparison: Branch vs. Conditional Execution

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Traditional [Traditional Branch Approach]
        direction TB
        T1[Compare operands] --> T2{Result zero?}
        T2 -- Yes --> T3[Branch to skip block]
        T2 -- No --> T4[Execute operation]
    end
    subgraph ARM [ARM Conditional Execution]
        direction TB
        A1[Execute conditional OPcond instruction] --> A2{Condition true?}
        A2 -- Yes --> A3[Execute operation directly]
        A2 -- No --> A4[NOP - 1 cycle penalty only]
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Q1]**
> **Q: Define conditional execution in ARM. Why is it considered more efficient than traditional branch-based conditional logic?**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> **Conditional execution** is an ARM feature where every instruction contains a 4-bit condition field `[31:28]` that is evaluated against the CPSR flags $\{N, Z, C, V\}$ before execution. If the condition is *false*, the instruction is treated as a NOP, otherwise it executes normally. **(1 mark)**
>
> It is more efficient because:
> 1. It **eliminates explicit branch instructions** (e.g., `BEQ skip`) for short conditional blocks, saving both code memory and branch-pipeline penalty. **(1 mark)**
> 2. It keeps the instruction stream **linear and predictable**, allowing the pipeline to remain full and avoiding branch misprediction stalls. **(1 mark)**
> *(Total: 3 marks)*

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Q2]**
> **Q: List any six condition codes of the ARM instruction set and state the flag test for each.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | # | Suffix | Flag Test |
> |:-:|:------:|:----------|
> | 1 | **EQ** | $Z = 1$ |
> | 2 | **NE** | $Z = 0$ |
> | 3 | **MI** | $N = 1$ |
> | 4 | **PL** | $N = 0$ |
> | 5 | **HI** | $C = 1 \text{ AND } Z = 0$ |
> | 6 | **LT** | $N \neq V$ |
>
> *(Any six correct entries with proper flag tests = 0.5 marks each; Total: 3 marks)*

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout**
> - Students frequently **confuse signed and unsigned conditions** (`GT` vs `HI`, `LT` vs `LO`). A single wrong flag test can cost up to 4 marks.
> - Do **not** write `SUBS` thinking it always sets flags when no `S` is present in newer ARM variants (ARMv6-M etc.). For **ARM7TDMI (LPC2148)**, all arithmetic ops *without* `S` *do not* update CPSR.
> - When asked to "write a program using conditional execution," examiners expect you to use `MOVcc`, `ADDcc`, `CMP`, etc. — *not* to fall back on `BEQ`/`BNE` branches. Misreading the question costs full marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024 Model Paper]*

**(a)** Explain the complete set of 16 ARM condition codes with their binary encoding, mnemonic suffix, flag test, and signed/unsigned interpretation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Opcode | Suffix | Flag Test | Interpretation | Signed/Unsigned |
|:------:|:------:|:----------|:---------------|:----------------|
| 0000 | EQ | $Z=1$ | Equal | Both |
| 0001 | NE | $Z=0$ | Not Equal | Both |
| 0010 | CS / HS | $C=1$ | Carry Set / ≥ | Unsigned |
| 0011 | CC / LO | $C=0$ | Carry Clear / < | Unsigned |
| 0100 | MI | $N=1$ | Negative | Both |
| 0101 | PL | $N=0$ | Positive or Zero | Both |
| 0110 | VS | $V=1$ | Overflow | Both |
| 0111 | VC | $V=0$ | No Overflow | Both |
| 1000 | HI | $C=1 \text{ AND } Z=0$ | Higher | Unsigned |
| 1001 | LS | $C=0 \text{ OR } Z=1$ | Lower or Same | Unsigned |
| 1010 | GE | $N=V$ | ≥ | Signed |
| 1011 | LT | $N \neq V$ | < | Signed |
| 1100 | GT | $Z=0 \text{ AND } N=V$ | > | Signed |
| 1101 | LE | $Z=1 \text{ OR } N \neq V$ | ≤ | Signed |
| 1110 | AL | always | Always | Default |
| 1111 | NV | never | Never | Reserved |

*(1 mark for table structure, 1 mark each for correctly identifying signed vs unsigned groups, 1 mark for the GE/GT boolean derivations, 1 mark for noting the default `AL`, 1 mark for the NV reservation, 1 mark for stating that these reside in bits [31:28], 1 mark for an example. Total: 7 marks)*

**(b)** Write an ARM7 assembly program using **conditional execution** (no branches) to compute $Y = \max(A, B)$ where $A$ and $B$ are 32-bit signed integers. Show the resulting CPSR flags when $A = 5$ and $B = -3$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

```assembly
        MOV   R0, #5          ; R0 = A = 5
        MOV   R1, #-3         ; R1 = B = -3
        CMP   R0, R1          ; Compute A - B, set flags
        MOVGT R2, R0          ; If A > B (signed), Y = A
        MOVLE R2, R1          ; If A <= B (signed), Y = B
        ; R2 now contains the maximum value
        END
```

**Flag trace for $A = 5$, $B = -3$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
A - B & = 0x00000005 - 0xFFFFFFFD \\
& = 0x00000005 + 0x00000003 \\
& = 0x00000008 \quad (\text{no borrow, no overflow})
\end{aligned}$$

- $Z = 0$ (result $\neq 0$)
- $N = 0$ (MSB = 0)
- $C = 1$ (no borrow, since $5 \geq -3$ treated as unsigned huge)
- $V = 0$ (no signed overflow)

**Evaluating `GT`:** $\bar{Z} \cdot (N \odot V) = 1 \cdot (0 \odot 0) = 1$ → **TRUE** → `MOVGT` executes, $R2 = R0 = 5$.

*(2 marks for correct program logic, 1 mark for using CMP correctly, 1 mark for MOVGT/MOVLE choice, 2 marks for the flag derivation, 1 mark for stating final R2 value. Total: 7 marks)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024 Model Paper, Alternative Choice]*

**(a)** Describe the role of the **Current Program Status Register (CPSR)** in ARM conditional execution. With a neat diagram, explain how the condition field `[31:28]` of an ARM instruction interacts with CPSR flags. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **CPSR** is a 32-bit status register holding:
- **Bits [31:28]**: Condition flags $\{N, Z, C, V\}$
- **Bits [27:8]**: Reserved / IT / control bits
- **Bits [7:0]**: Control bits — I (IRQ disable), F (FIQ disable), T (Thumb state), and Mode bits `M[4:0]`

**The interaction flow:**

1. A data-processing or compare instruction with the `S` bit set performs the operation and updates the **condition flags** in CPSR.
2. The *subsequent* instruction's **condition field `[31:28]`** is decoded in the Instruction Decode stage.
3. The processor checks the condition against the *current* flag state. If true, the instruction executes; if false, the pipeline advances by 1 cycle without write-back.

**Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[SUBS R0, R1, R2 with S-bit set] -->|Updates| F[(CPSR Flags N Z C V)]
    F -->|Read by| D[Decoder checks condition field 31 to 28]
    D -->|TRUE| X[Execute]
    D -->|FALSE| N[NOP]
```

*(2 marks for CPSR structure, 2 marks for the interaction flow, 2 marks for the diagram, 1 mark for explicit mention of the `S` bit. Total: 7 marks)*

**(b)** Write an ARM7 assembly program to determine whether a 32-bit signed number in `R0` is **positive, negative, or zero** using only **conditional instructions** (no `B`-class branches). Explain the flag transitions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

```assembly
        ; Assume R0 holds the test number
        CMP   R0, #0          ; Compare R0 with 0, sets flags
        MOVEQ R1, #0          ; If Z=1 (R0 == 0), R1 = 0
        MOVMI R1, #-1         ; If N=1 (R0 < 0),  R1 = -1
        MOVPL R1, #1          ; If N=0 (R0 > 0),  R1 = 1
        END
```

**Flag transition analysis:**

- After `CMP R0, #0`: This computes $R0 - 0 = R0$, setting flags based on the *value* of $R0$.
- If $R0 = 0$: $Z = 1$ → `MOVEQ` runs.
- If $R0 < 0$ (e.g., $R0 = 0xFFFFFFFF$): $N = 1$ → `MOVMI` runs.
- If $R0 > 0$ (e.g., $R0 = 0x00000005$): $N = 0, Z = 0$ → `MOVPL` runs (since `PL` is true when $N=0$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Note that `PL` (Plus) is true whenever $N = 0$, which includes both *positive* and *zero* values. To avoid both `MOVEQ` and `MOVPL` firing for $R0 = 0$, we rely on the fact that for $R0 = 0$, the `MOVEQ` runs *first* in program order — but ARM does NOT skip subsequent instructions. Therefore, the proper code uses the **`ELSE`-style pairing** by checking `PL` only for strictly positive via `GT`:

```assembly
        CMP   R0, #0
        MOVEQ R1, #0
        MOVLT R1, #-1
        MOVGT R1, #1
```

*(2 marks for using CMP correctly, 2 marks for the three-way conditional dispatch, 1 mark for explaining the N and Z flag transitions, 1 mark for choosing `EQ`/`LT`/`GT` correctly, 1 mark for the final R1 value definition. Total: 7 marks)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Conditional Execution:**

- ☐ **Every** ARM instruction has a 4-bit condition field in bits `[31:28]` — the **default** is `AL` (Always, `1110₂`).
- ☐ Flags updated **only** when the `S` bit is set (e.g., `ADDS`, `SUBS`) or via `CMP`/`TST`/`TEQ`/`CMN`.
- ☐ CPSR flags: **N** (bit 31), **Z** (bit 30), **C** (bit 29), **V** (bit 28).
- ☐ Use **signed** condition codes (`GT`, `LT`, `GE`, `LE`) for *signed* comparisons; use **unsigned** (`HI`, `LS`, `HS`, `LO`) for *unsigned*.
- ☐ When condition is **false**, the instruction is a single-cycle NOP — no pipeline flush, no write-back.
- ☐ ARM7TDMI (LPC2148) supports full conditional execution; **Thumb mode restricts** it to branches only — but Thumb-2 (Cortex-M) re-introduces it via `IT` blocks.
- ☐ `NV` (Never) is reserved/deprecated — do NOT use it in KTU answers.
- ☐ Boolean expression for `GT`: $\bar{Z} \cdot (N \odot V)$ — a frequent sub-part derivation question.
- ☐ The condition field interacts with the **preceding** flag-setting instruction, not the current one.
- ☐ Avoid common confusion: `CS = HS` and `CC = LO` are **two names** for the same condition code.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Thumb Instructions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCIT404 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Thumb Instructions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)**
> **Thumb Instructions** are a **16-bit compressed instruction set** of the ARM architecture that provides a subset of the standard **32-bit ARM instruction set**. They are designed to achieve **higher code density** (typically around **65% of ARM code size**) while retaining most of the performance benefits of the ARM processor core.

The Thumb instruction set was introduced by **ARM Holdings** in **1995** with the **ARM7TDMI** processor. The suffix **"T"** in ARM7TDMI specifically denotes hardware support for **Thumb instructions**, allowing the processor to dynamically decompress 16-bit Thumb instructions into equivalent 32-bit ARM instructions for execution.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: "The Suitcase Packing Problem"** 🧳
>
> Imagine you are packing a suitcase for a long trip:
> - **ARM Instructions (32-bit)** are like a **large, full-sized luxury suitcase** — you can fit everything you need (large operands, full addressing modes, conditional execution), but the suitcase itself takes up a lot of space in the cargo hold (program memory).
> - **Thumb Instructions (16-bit)** are like a **compact, foldable travel bag** — you trade away some features (no conditional execution on every instruction, fewer bits for immediate values, restricted register access), but you fit **twice as much functionality** into the same memory space.
>
> The processor, at runtime, is like a **custom officer** who automatically "unfolds" the compact Thumb bag back into its full-sized equivalent (decompression into ARM instructions) before letting it pass through (execution).

## 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Meaning | Bit Width |
|---|---|---|
| **ARM State** | Processor executes standard **32-bit ARM instructions** | 32-bit |
| **Thumb State** | Processor executes compressed **16-bit Thumb instructions** | 16-bit |
| **BX / BLX** | Branch and Exchange instructions used to **switch states** | 32-bit |
| **Thumb-2** | ARM's **mixed 16/32-bit** instruction set (introduced 2003) | 16/32-bit mix |
| **Cortex-M** | Microcontroller family that uses **Thumb-2 only** (no ARM state) | 16/32-bit mix |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In the **Cortex-M family** (e.g., Cortex-M3, M4, M7 used in STM32, LPC1768), the processor executes **only Thumb-2 instructions** — the 32-bit ARM state is **not available**. This is a frequently tested point.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Metrics

- **Code Density Gain:** Thumb code is typically **~65% the size** of equivalent ARM code
- **Performance Penalty:** Approximately **~15% slower** than ARM in pure Thumb code (older ARM7TDMI era)
- **Memory Savings:** Roughly **30–35% reduction** in program memory footprint
- **Instruction Ratio:** 1 ARM instruction (32 bits) ≈ **2 Thumb instructions** (16 + 16 bits)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Were Thumb Instructions Introduced?

The motivation for Thumb can be broken down into three engineering drivers:

### 🔹 Driver 1 — Memory Cost (1990s Era)
In the mid-1990s, **ROM/Flash memory was extremely expensive**. Embedded systems had tight memory budgets (often **32 KB–512 KB** of program memory). A 32-bit instruction occupies **4 bytes**, but a 16-bit instruction occupies only **2 bytes** — a **2× density advantage**.

### 🔹 Driver 2 — Bus Width Considerations
Many low-cost embedded systems used **16-bit external memory buses**. Fetching 32-bit ARM instructions across a 16-bit bus required **two memory cycles**, negating the performance advantage. 16-bit Thumb instructions could be fetched in **a single memory cycle** on a 16-bit bus.

### 🔹 Driver 3 — Power Consumption
Smaller instructions mean **fewer memory fetches**, which means the memory bus and external buffers toggle less — directly reducing **dynamic power consumption**, critical for battery-powered devices.

## 2.2 Architectural Working Principle

> [!NOTE]
> **How Thumb Instructions Work Internally (Step-by-Step Logic):**
> 1. The processor fetches a **16-bit instruction** from memory (Thumb state).
> 2. The instruction is **decoded** by the Thumb decoder.
> 3. The decoder **expands (decompresses) the 16-bit Thumb instruction into an equivalent 32-bit ARM instruction** internally.
> 4. The ARM core executes the **uncompressed 32-bit instruction** exactly as it would in ARM state.
> 5. **Key insight:** The execution pipeline never actually sees a 16-bit instruction — it always operates on 32-bit internal representations.

This is why performance penalty is small — the execution path is identical to ARM.

## 2.3 State Switching Mechanism

The ARM core uses a special bit in the **CPSR (Current Program Status Register)**, called the **T-bit**, to track the current execution state.

| CPSR T-bit Value | Processor State | Instruction Width |
|---|---|---|
| **T = 0** | **ARM State** | 32-bit |
| **T = 1** | **Thumb State** | 16-bit |

The T-bit is modified only by:
- **`BX` (Branch and Exchange)** — unconditional state change
- **`BLX` (Branch with Link and Exchange)** — state change + subroutine call
- **Exception entry/exit** — automatically saves and restores the T-bit

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Footprint Formula:**
>
> $$\text{Code Size Reduction (\%)} = \left(1 - \frac{\text{Thumb Code Size}}{\text{ARM Code Size}}\right) \times 100$$
>
> For typical C code compiled to Thumb:
>
> $$\text{Thumb Code Size} \approx 0.65 \times \text{ARM Code Size}$$
>
> $$\text{Memory Saved} = \text{ARM Size} - \text{Thumb Size} = 0.35 \times \text{ARM Size}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Instruction Density Formula:**
>
> $$\text{Instructions per KB} = \frac{1024 \text{ bytes} \times 8 \text{ bits}}{I_w}$$
>
> where $I_w$ = instruction width in bits.
>
> - For ARM: $\frac{8192}{32} = 256$ instructions/KB
> - For Thumb: $\frac{8192}{16} = 512$ instructions/KB
>
> $$\text{Thumb Density Ratio} = \frac{512}{256} = 2.0 \text{ (theoretical maximum)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Effective CPI (Cycles Per Instruction) Comparison:**
>
> $$\text{Effective Throughput} = \frac{\text{Clock Frequency}}{\text{CPI} \times I_w}$$
>
> Since Thumb has $\approx 1.15 \times$ CPI compared to ARM, but $0.5 \times$ instruction width, the **net memory bus utilization is improved** for memory-bound code.

## 2.5 ARM vs Thumb — Key Architectural Differences

| Feature | ARM (32-bit) | Thumb (16-bit) |
|---|---|---|
| **Instruction Width** | **32 bits** | **16 bits** |
| **Code Density** | Lower (1.0× baseline) | **Higher (~1.65×)** |
| **Conditional Execution** | **All instructions** (via condition field) | **Only branch instructions** |
| **Register Access** | All **16 registers** (R0–R15) | Limited subset (Hi/Lo registers) |
| **Immediate Values** | **8-bit rotated** (large range) | **5-bit** (small range) |
| **Barrel Shifter** | **Free shift** on every data op | **No free shift** (separate instructions) |
| **Branch Range** | ±32 MB | **±2 KB** (limited offset) |
| **Performance** | **Baseline (1.0×)** | **~0.85×** to 0.95× |
| **Best Suited For** | Performance-critical code | Memory-constrained, size-critical code |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Mobile Phones (1998–2010):** Early smartphones used ARM7TDMI / ARM9 with mixed ARM/Thumb code to fit large baseband stacks into small Flash memory.
- **IoT Sensor Nodes:** Modern Cortex-M0+ microcontrollers use **Thumb-2 only** to minimize Flash footprint and power.
- **Automotive ECUs:** Mixed ARM/Thumb code in older ARM7-based engine control units.
- **Firmware Boot Loaders:** Often compiled in Thumb for size optimization.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Code Size Reduction Calculation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example 1: Code Size Analysis**
>
> **Given:** An embedded C program compiled to ARM assembly produces **40,000 bytes** of code. When recompiled with the `-mthumb` flag (Thumb mode), the size reduces to **26,000 bytes**.
>
> **Find:** (a) Code size reduction in %, (b) Number of bytes saved, (c) Theoretical maximum number of Thumb instructions possible in that memory.

### Step 1 — Apply the Code Size Reduction Formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Reduction (\%)} &= \left(1 - \frac{\text{Thumb Size}}{\text{ARM Size}}\right) \times 100 \\[6pt]
&= \left(1 - \frac{26{,}000}{40{,}000}\right) \times 100 \\[6pt]
&= \left(1 - 0.65\right) \times 100 \\[6pt]
&= 0.35 \times 100 \\[6pt]
&= 35\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Calculate Bytes Saved

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bytes Saved} &= \text{ARM Size} - \text{Thumb Size} \\[6pt]
&= 40{,}000 - 26{,}000 \\[6pt]
&= 14{,}000 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Theoretical Maximum Thumb Instructions

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Max Thumb Instructions} &= \frac{\text{Thumb Size (bytes)} \times 8 \text{ bits/byte}}{16 \text{ bits/instruction}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{26{,}000 \times 8}{16} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{208{,}000}{16} \\[6pt]
&= 13{,}000 \text{ instructions}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Performance Trade-off Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example 2: CPI and Execution Time**
>
> **Given:** A processor runs at **72 MHz**. A benchmark loop takes:
> - **ARM mode:** 1000 ARM instructions at average **CPI = 1.2**
> - **Thumb mode:** 1500 Thumb instructions at average **CPI = 1.4**
>
> **Find:** Which mode executes faster for this loop?

### Step 1 — Calculate Execution Time in ARM Mode

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{ARM}} &= \frac{\text{Instruction Count} \times \text{CPI}}{\text{Clock Frequency}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1000 \times 1.2}{72 \times 10^6} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1200}{72 \times 10^6} \\[6pt]
&= 16.667~\mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Calculate Execution Time in Thumb Mode

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{Thumb}} &= \frac{1500 \times 1.4}{72 \times 10^6} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{2100}{72 \times 10^6} \\[6pt]
&= 29.167~\mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Compare and Interpret

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Thumb is slower by} &= \frac{29.167 - 16.667}{16.667} \times 100 \\[6pt]
&= \frac{12.5}{16.667} \times 100 \\[6pt]
&\approx 75\% \text{ slower in this case}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Although Thumb saves memory, **CPU-bound loops** can be significantly slower due to more instructions and slightly higher CPI. This is why **mixed ARM/Thumb code** is optimal — performance-critical inner loops in ARM, size-critical outer code in Thumb.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | ARM State (T=0) | Thumb State (T=1) | Winner |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Instruction Width** | 32 bits | 16 bits | **Thumb** (2× density) |
| **Code Size for 1M Instructions** | **4 MB** | **2 MB** | **Thumb** |
| **Avg. CPI (typical)** | 1.0 – 1.3 | 1.2 – 1.5 | **ARM** |
| **Conditional Execution** | Yes (all) | Limited (branches only) | **ARM** |
| **Immediate Operand Range** | 8-bit rotated (effectively 32-bit) | 5-bit (0–31) | **ARM** |
| **Memory Bandwidth Needed** | Higher (4 bytes/fetch) | Lower (2 bytes/fetch) | **Thumb** |
| **Power Consumption** | Higher | **Lower (~30%)** | **Thumb** |
| **Use Case** | DSP kernels, ISRs | Boot code, glue logic, UI code | **Context-dependent** |

## 3.4 Derivation: State Transition Cost

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example 3: Cost of Switching States**
>
> **Given:** A `BX` (Branch and Exchange) instruction takes **3 clock cycles** to switch from ARM to Thumb state. The system clock is **50 MHz**.
>
> **Find:** Time overhead of a single state switch.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{switch}} &= \frac{\text{Cycles}}{\text{Clock Frequency}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{3}{50 \times 10^6} \\[6pt]
&= 60 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Rule:** To keep state-switching overhead below **1%** of total execution time, a function called in Thumb mode from ARM code should execute **at least 300 cycles** in Thumb before returning. This is why **state switching is done at function boundaries**, not within tight loops.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Thumb Instruction Decode & Execute Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Program Memory Flash or ROM"] --> B["16-bit Instruction Fetch"]
    B --> C{"CPSR T-bit = 1"}
    C -- Yes --> D["Thumb Decoder 16 to 32 bit Expansion"]
    C -- No --> E["ARM Decoder 32 bit Direct Execution"]
    D --> F["Internal 32 bit ARM Equivalent Instruction"]
    F --> G["ARM Core Execution Unit ALU Shifter MAC"]
    E --> G
    G --> H["Register Bank R0 to R15"]
    G --> I["CPSR Update Flags NZCV"]
    I --> J["Next Instruction Fetch"]
    J --> B
    H --> K["BX or BLX Instruction"]
    K --> L{"Target Address Bit 0"}
    L -- Bit 0 is 0 --> M["Switch to ARM State T equals 0"]
    L -- Bit 0 is 1 --> N["Switch to Thumb State T equals 1"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Mixed ARM/Thumb Code Organization in Memory

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Reset Vector ARM Mode"] --> B["ARM Boot Code Startup"]
    B --> C["Initialize Stack and PLL"]
    C --> D["BLX to Thumb Function T equals 1"]
    D --> E["Thumb Main Application Code"]
    E --> F["BLX to ARM ISR Handler T equals 0"]
    F --> G["ARM Interrupt Service Routine"]
    G --> H["BX Return to Thumb T equals 1"]
    H --> E
    E --> I["Thumb Peripheral Driver Code"]
    I --> J["End of Program"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: ARM vs Thumb Instruction Format Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph ARM32["ARM 32-bit Instruction Format"]
        A1["Condition Field 4 bits"] --> A2["Opcode 3 bits"]
        A2 --> A3["Operand 1 4 bits"]
        A3 --> A4["Destination 4 bits"]
        A4 --> A5["Shift 2 bits"]
        A5 --> A6["Operand 2 12 bits"]
    end
    subgraph THUMB16["Thumb 16-bit Instruction Format"]
        B1["Opcode 5 bits"]
        B2["Source Register 3 bits"]
        B3["Destination Register 3 bits"]
        B4["Remainder 5 bits"]
        B1 --> B2
        B2 --> B3
        B3 --> B4
    end
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Thumb Instruction Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stage1["Stage 1: FETCH Read 16 bits from memory bus"] --> stage2["Stage 2: DECODE Thumb decoder identifies opcode class"]
    stage2 --> stage3["Stage 3: EXPAND Translate 16 bits to internal 32 bits"]
    stage3 --> stage4["Stage 4: REGISTER READ Read R0 to R7 or Hi registers"]
    stage4 --> stage5["Stage 5: EXECUTE ALU operation or branch evaluation"]
    stage5 --> stage6["Stage 6: WRITE BACK Update destination register"]
    stage6 --> stage7["Stage 7: CPSR UPDATE Set NZCV flags if applicable"]
    stage7 --> stage1
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram: CPSR T-bit State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> ARM_State: Reset Vector Loaded T equals 0
    ARM_State --> Thumb_State: BX with Bit0 equals 1
    Thumb_State --> ARM_State: BX with Bit0 equals 0
    ARM_State --> ARM_State: Normal ARM branch
    Thumb_State --> Thumb_State: Normal Thumb branch
    ARM_State --> Exception_Mode: IRQ or FIQ Triggered
    Thumb_State --> Exception_Mode: IRQ or FIQ Triggered
    Exception_Mode --> ARM_State: Exception Return T equals 0 default
    Exception_Mode --> Thumb_State: Exception Return with T equals 1 saved
```

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**What are Thumb instructions? Why are they used in ARM-based embedded systems?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Thumb instructions are a **16-bit compressed instruction set** of the ARM architecture that provides a subset of the functionality of the standard **32-bit ARM instruction set**. They are dynamically decompressed by the processor into equivalent 32-bit ARM instructions for execution.

**Reasons for use:**
1. **Improved code density** — Thumb code occupies approximately **65% of the memory** required by equivalent ARM code, reducing Flash/RAM cost. **(1 mark)**
2. **Better external memory utilization** — 16-bit Thumb instructions can be fetched in a single cycle on a 16-bit memory bus, whereas 32-bit ARM instructions require two cycles. **(1 mark)**
3. **Lower power consumption** — Reduced memory fetches and toggling lower dynamic power, critical for battery-powered embedded devices. **(1 mark)**

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**Explain the role of the T-bit in the CPSR register. How is the ARM/Thumb state switched?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **T-bit (bit 5)** of the **CPSR (Current Program Status Register)** indicates the current execution state of the ARM processor: **(1 mark)**
- **T = 0** → Processor is in **ARM state** (executes 32-bit instructions)
- **T = 1** → Processor is in **Thumb state** (executes 16-bit instructions)

**State Switching Mechanism:** The state is changed using the **`BX (Branch and Exchange)`** or **`BLX (Branch with Link and Exchange)`** instruction. The LSB (bit 0) of the target address determines the new state: **(1.5 marks)**
- If LSB = **0** → switches to ARM state
- If LSB = **1** → switches to Thumb state

Exception entry automatically saves the T-bit, and exception return restores it. **(0.5 marks)**
