## 📕 PART B — Full ESE Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **Thumb instruction set** of ARM in detail. Discuss the architectural features, instruction format, and register accessibility differences compared to the standard ARM instruction set. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, describe the **Thumb instruction decoding and execution pipeline**. How does the ARM processor internally handle 16-bit Thumb instructions to produce equivalent 32-bit execution? **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

### **Part (a) — Thumb Instruction Set Architecture (7 marks)**

The **Thumb instruction set** is a **16-bit compressed encoding** of the most commonly used ARM instructions. It was introduced to improve **code density** in cost-sensitive embedded applications.

**Key Architectural Features:** **(3 marks)**

1. **16-bit fixed instruction width** — every Thumb instruction is exactly 2 bytes, providing a 2× theoretical density advantage over 32-bit ARM instructions.
2. **Unified register file** — Thumb instructions can access all 16 registers (R0–R15), but most instructions are restricted to **low registers (R0–R7)**. Only a few operations (MOV, ADD, CMP) can access high registers (R8–R15).
3. **Limited conditional execution** — unlike ARM where *every* instruction can be conditionally executed using the 4-bit condition code, in Thumb, only **branch instructions** support conditional execution.
4. **Smaller immediate values** — 5-bit immediate (0–31) in Thumb vs 8-bit rotated (effectively 32-bit range) in ARM.
5. **No barrel shifter** — Thumb data-processing instructions do not support the free shift/rotate feature; separate shift instructions must be used.

**Instruction Format (general):** **(2 marks)**

- **Format 1 (Move/Compare/Add/Subtract Immediate):**
  `Opcode (5 bits) | Rd (3 bits) | Rn (3 bits) | Offset (5 bits)` = 16 bits total

- **Format 2 (Data Processing):**
  `Opcode (5 bits) | Op (2 bits) | Rm (3 bits) | Rn (3 bits) | Rd (3 bits)` = 16 bits

**Register Accessibility Differences:** **(2 marks)**

| Operation Type | Registers Accessible |
|---|---|
| Most ALU operations | R0–R7 (low) only |
| MOV, ADD, CMP with high reg | R8–R15 (high) allowed |
| Branch (BX, BLX) | R0–R15 (all) |
| Load/Store | R0–R7 base, R0–R15 for offset |

---

### **Part (b) — Decoding and Execution Pipeline (7 marks)**

The ARM processor performs a **transparent decompression** of Thumb instructions before execution.

**Pipeline Stages (refer to Section 4.4 diagram):** **(3.5 marks)**

1. **Fetch Stage:** The instruction cache/memory interface reads **16 bits** from program memory in a single cycle.
2. **Decode Stage:** The **Thumb decoder** identifies the instruction class (data processing, load/store, branch, etc.) by examining the opcode bits.
3. **Expansion Stage:** The decoder **expands the 16-bit instruction into a full 32-bit equivalent ARM instruction** internally — this is essentially a hardware multiplexer that maps the compact Thumb fields to the wider ARM fields.
4. **Execute Stage:** The ARM execution unit processes the instruction **identically to ARM state** — same ALU, same shifter, same register file access.
5. **Write-Back Stage:** Result is written to the destination register; CPSR flags (N, Z, C, V) are updated for relevant instructions.

**Key Internal Handling Concept:** **(3.5 marks)**

The **CPU pipeline does not know** whether the original instruction was Thumb or ARM — by the time it reaches the execution unit, all instructions are 32-bit. The T-bit in CPSR only affects the **fetch and decode** stages; the **execution backbone is unified**.

**Advantages of this approach:**
- **Minimal performance penalty** (~15% in worst case)
- **Single hardware execution engine** — no separate Thumb-specific ALU
- **Consistent interrupt handling** — state is preserved via the T-bit in SPSR
- **Easier compiler optimization** — toolchain can freely mix ARM/Thumb functions

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Compare and contrast **ARM and Thumb instruction sets** with respect to instruction size, code density, conditional execution, register accessibility, and performance. List any **four advantages and two disadvantages** of using Thumb instructions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **state switching mechanism** between ARM and Thumb using the `BX` instruction with a clear example. How does the **CPSR T-bit** govern this transition? What is the role of **Thumb-2 technology** in modern Cortex-M microcontrollers? **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

### **Part (a) — Comparison and Trade-offs (7 marks)**

**Detailed Comparison Table:** **(4 marks)**

| Parameter | ARM | Thumb |
|---|---|---|
| Instruction Width | 32 bits | 16 bits |
| Code Density | Baseline (1.0×) | ~1.65× denser |
| Conditional Execution | All instructions | Branch instructions only |
| Register Access | All R0–R15 | Mostly R0–R7 |
| Immediate Range | 8-bit rotated (32-bit effective) | 5-bit (0–31) |
| Barrel Shifter | Free in data ops | Not available |
| Branch Range | ±32 MB | ±2 KB |
| Performance | 1.0× baseline | ~0.85× to 0.95× |
| Power | Higher | Lower (~30% less) |

**Four Advantages of Thumb Instructions:** **(2 marks)**
1. **Higher code density** → less Flash memory required, lower BOM cost.
2. **Lower memory bandwidth** → 16-bit fetches suit narrow buses and reduce power.
3. **Better cache utilization** → more instructions fit per cache line.
4. **Reduced dynamic power** → fewer bus transitions in memory-bound code.

**Two Disadvantages:** **(1 mark)**
1. **Performance penalty** (~15%) due to extra decode and higher CPI.
2. **Limited expressiveness** — small immediate range and restricted register access require more instructions for complex operations.

---

### **Part (b) — State Switching & Thumb-2 (7 marks)**

**State Switching Using `BX`:** **(3 marks)**

The `BX (Branch and Exchange)` instruction has the syntax:
`BX <Rm>`, where Rm holds the **target address**. The **least significant bit (LSB = bit 0)** of the target address determines the new state:
- If `Rm[0] = 0` → Branch to **ARM state** (T-bit cleared)
- If `Rm[0] = 1` → Branch to **Thumb state** (T-bit set)

The processor ignores bit 0 during the actual branch (since ARM instructions are word-aligned) and uses it only to update the T-bit.

**Example Code Sequence:**

```assembly
        ; --- Currently in ARM state ---
        LDR   R0, =thumb_function + 1   ; Set bit 0 = 1 for Thumb
        BX    R0                         ; Switch to Thumb state
thumb_function:
        ; --- Now in Thumb state ---
        MOV   R1, #10                   ; Thumb MOV immediate
        ADD   R2, R1, #5                ; Thumb ADD
        ...
        ; Return to ARM caller
        LDR   R0, =arm_caller           ; Bit 0 = 0 for ARM
        BX    R0                        ; Switch back to ARM
```

**CPSR T-bit Governance:** **(1.5 marks)**
- The **T-bit (bit 5 of CPSR)** is the **sole hardware indicator** of the current instruction set state.
- It is **read at every instruction fetch** to determine the decoder behavior.
- It is **modified only** by BX, BLX, and exception return sequences — software cannot directly write to it.

**Role of Thumb-2 Technology in Cortex-M:** **(2.5 marks)**

**Thumb-2** (introduced in 2003 with ARM1156T2) extends the original Thumb set with:
- **32-bit Thumb instructions** mixed with **16-bit Thumb instructions** in a single stream
- **No ARM state required** — Cortex-M processors (M0, M3, M4, M7) execute **only Thumb-2**
- **Near-ARM performance** with **Thumb-level code density**
- **New instructions:** `CBNZ`, `CBZ` (compare and branch), `IT` (if-then conditional blocks), `TBB`, `TBH` (table branches), hardware multiply, bit-banding support

> [!NOTE]
> **Significance:** Thumb-2 eliminated the need to switch states, providing the **best of both worlds** — high density + high performance — and became the **universal instruction set** for the entire ARM microcontroller ecosystem.

---

> [!WARNING]
> ⚠️ **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> - **Mistake 1:** Many students write that Thumb instructions are executed directly as 16-bit operations. **Incorrect** — they are always **decompressed to 32-bit internally** before execution.
> - **Mistake 2:** Confusing **Thumb-2** (a 16/32-bit mixed set) with **original Thumb** (pure 16-bit). Cortex-M uses **Thumb-2**, not original Thumb.
> - **Mistake 3:** Forgetting that the **T-bit is in CPSR bit 5** (not SPSR). SPSR holds the *saved* T-bit during exceptions.
> - **Mistake 4:** Writing that `BX` uses a separate register field for state — the **LSB of the target address** itself encodes the state, not a separate operand.
> - **Mistake 5:** Claiming Thumb is "always faster" — it is **smaller**, not faster. Performance is typically **lower** than ARM by 10–15%.
> - **Marks Lost:** Typically **2–3 marks** are lost per question for these conceptual errors.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ Thumb instructions are **16-bit compressed** encodings of a subset of ARM instructions.
- ✅ They are **dynamically decompressed** to 32-bit internally for execution.
- ✅ The **CPSR T-bit (bit 5)** determines the current state: 0 = ARM, 1 = Thumb.
- ✅ State switching is performed only by **`BX` and `BLX`** using the LSB of the target address.
- ✅ Thumb provides **~65% code size** of ARM → significant **Flash memory savings**.
- ✅ Performance penalty is typically **10–15%** compared to ARM state.
- ✅ Thumb restricts most operations to **low registers (R0–R7)**; high registers (R8–R15) have limited use.
- ✅ Thumb does **not support free barrel shifting** or full conditional execution on all instructions.
- ✅ **Thumb-2** is a 16/32-bit mixed instruction set used **exclusively** in Cortex-M microcontrollers.
- ✅ Cortex-M processors **do not support ARM state** — Thumb-2 only.
- ✅ Thumb instructions have a **5-bit immediate field** (0–31) vs ARM's 8-bit rotated immediate.
- ✅ Branch range in Thumb is **±2 KB** (PC-relative, word-aligned) vs ±32 MB in ARM.
- ✅ Best practice: use **mixed ARM/Thumb code** — performance-critical inner loops in ARM, size-critical code in Thumb.
- ✅ State switching cost: ~3 cycles for `BX`; switch at **function boundaries**, not inside loops.
- ✅ KTU 2024 emphasis areas: **definition, state switching, CPSR T-bit, Thumb-2 in Cortex-M**.
